name: ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS

topics.title: Components of Electrical System on Board Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Components of Electrical System on Board Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Electrical System on Board Ships** refers to the integrated network of generation, distribution, control, and protection equipment that provides electrical power for propulsion auxiliaries, navigation, communication, lighting, cargo handling, safety, and habitability services on a marine vessel. The system is designed to comply with **classification society rules** (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, ABS, BV) and the **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) Convention** of the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the shipboard electrical system is classified as a **mission-critical, isolated micro-grid** that operates autonomously at sea and interfaces with shore supplies while in harbor. The system is engineered to maintain **uninterrupted power supply (UPS)** under extreme environmental stresses including salt-laden humidity, vibration, rolling/pitching motion, and ambient temperature variation from **$-25^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+45^{\circ}\text{C}$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The shipboard electrical system is essentially a **self-contained power plant** that combines the functions of a thermal power station, an industrial distribution grid, and a building management system—all within a floating, space-constrained, vibration-prone environment.

## 1.2 Hierarchical Functional Block Architecture

The complete shipboard electrical ecosystem can be decomposed into **seven hierarchical tiers**:

1. **Prime Movers** – Diesel engines, steam turbines, or gas turbines that convert fuel energy to mechanical rotation.
2. **Generators** – Synchronous AC machines converting mechanical energy to electrical energy.
3. **Switchboards** – Centralized power collection, switching, and protection nodes.
4. **Distribution Network** – Cables, bus ducts, transformers, and motor control centers.
5. **Loads** – Motors, lighting, navigation electronics, HVAC, cargo systems.
6. **Emergency & Standby Systems** – Emergency generators, batteries, UPS, blackout prevention.
7. **Auxiliary Control & Monitoring** – Integrated Automation System (IAS), Power Management System (PMS), alarm panels.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ship as a Floating City"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a modern cruise ship or aircraft carrier as a **floating self-sufficient city**. Just as a city has its own power plant (the **main generators**), substations (the **switchboards**), distribution lines (the **ship's cables**), and backup generators for hospitals (the **emergency generator**), the ship replicates this entire infrastructure in a steel hull. The only difference is that everything must withstand rolling, pitching, flooding, fire, and corrosion—conditions no land-based city ever faces.

The ship's electrical system can be visualized as the **circulatory system of the vessel**:

- **Generators = Heart** – pumps (electrons) into the network.
- **Switchboards = Aorta and major arteries** – distribute and regulate flow.
- **Cables = Capillaries** – reach every organ (load).
- **Emergency systems = Emergency blood reserve** – take over when the heart fails.
- **PMS = Brain** – decides when to start/stop generators based on load demand.

## 1.4 Standard Voltage & Frequency Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Governing Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Primary generation voltage | **440 V / 6.6 kV / 11 kV** | IEC 60092 / Classification Rules |
| Secondary distribution | 220 V single-phase, 380–440 V three-phase | IEC 60092-201 |
| Emergency voltage | 220 V / 24 V DC | SOLAS Chapter II-1 |
| Frequency | **50 Hz or 60 Hz** | Customer/Grid preference |
| Battery voltage | 24 V DC (typical) | SOLAS Reg. II-1/42 |
| Shore supply | 380–440 V, 3-phase | IEC 80005-1 (HVSC) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOLAS Mandate (Chapter II-1, Part D):** Every merchant ship above 500 GT must have **at least two independent sources of electrical power**, with the emergency source capable of automatically starting and supplying critical services within **45 seconds** of main power failure.

## 1.5 Key Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

- **Total installed power**: Up to **80 MW** for large container vessels (Ultra Large Container Vessels, ULCV).
- **Normal voltage regulation**: **$\pm 2.5\%$** (steady state), $\pm 20\%$ (transient recovery within 1.5 s).
- **Frequency regulation**: **$\pm 5\%$ steady state, $\pm 10\%$ transient**.
- **Insulation resistance**: Minimum **1 M$\Omega$ per kV** of rated voltage.
- **Short-circuit withstand**: 50 kA for 1 second at main bus bars.
- **Degree of protection**: IP 44 for generators, IP 56 for deck equipment, IP 67 for submerged equipment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Prime Movers — The Mechanical Heart

The prime mover converts stored chemical energy (diesel, HFO, LNG) or thermal energy (steam) into **rotational mechanical energy** to drive the ship's electrical generators.

### 2.1.1 Types of Prime Movers Used On Board

| Prime Mover | Application | Power Range | Advantages |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Diesel Engine** (HSD/MDO) | Most common; auxiliary & emergency | 500 kW – 8 MW | High efficiency, quick start, modular |
| **Steam Turbine** | LNG carriers, very large vessels | 5 MW – 25 MW | Smooth operation, no gear box |
| **Gas Turbine** | Naval combatants, fast ferries | 10 MW – 30 MW | High power-to-weight, fast start |
| **Dual-Fuel Engine** (LNG/MDO) | Modern eco-ships (LNG carriers) | 1 MW – 10 MW | IMO Tier III emission compliant |

### 2.1.2 Governing Equation for Mechanical Power Output

The mechanical power delivered by the prime mover to the generator shaft is given by:

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60}$$

Where:
- $P_{\text{shaft}}$ = Shaft power in **Watts**
- $N$ = Rotational speed in **RPM**
- $T$ = Torque in **N·m**

For a 4-stroke marine diesel generator with a brake-specific fuel consumption (BSFC) of **200 g/kWh**, the fuel consumption rate becomes:

$$m_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{P_{\text{shaft}} \times \text{BSFC}}{1000}$$

## 2.2 Generators — Conversion to Electrical Energy

### 2.2.1 Synchronous AC Generators (Dominant Type)

Nearly all shipboard generators are **brushless, self-excited, 3-phase synchronous alternators**. The key relationship is:

$$f = \frac{p \times N}{120}$$

Where:
- $f$ = electrical frequency (**50 Hz** or **60 Hz**)
- $p$ = number of poles
- $N$ = rotor speed in RPM

For 50 Hz operation with a 4-pole machine, $N = 1500$ RPM. For 60 Hz, $N = 1800$ RPM.

### 2.2.2 Internal Generated EMF

The EMF generated per phase in a synchronous generator is:

$$E_{\text{ph}} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{\text{ph}} \, \Phi \, k_w$$

Where:
- $N_{\text{ph}}$ = turns per phase
- $\Phi$ = flux per pole in **Weber**
- $k_w$ = winding factor (typically 0.95–0.98)

### 2.2.3 Real and Reactive Power Output

The power delivered by a synchronous generator to the bus is:

$$S = 3 V_{\text{ph}} I_{\text{ph}}^{*}$$

$$P = 3 V_{\text{ph}} I_{\text{ph}} \cos \phi$$

$$Q = 3 V_{\text{ph}} I_{\text{ph}} \sin \phi$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Synchronous Generators Dominate Ships:** They can supply **both real power (kW) and reactive power (kVAR)** independently. By adjusting the **DC field excitation current**, the generator's power factor and terminal voltage are precisely controlled—essential for stable parallel operation.

### 2.2.4 Governor and AVR — The Two Loops

- **Governor (Mechanical/Hydraulic Loop):** Controls fuel input → controls **P (active power)** and **frequency**.
- **Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR) (Electrical Loop):** Controls field current → controls **Q (reactive power)** and **terminal voltage**.

The droop characteristics are:

$$\Delta f = -\frac{P \cdot R_f}{f_0} \quad \text{(governor droop)}$$

$$\Delta V = -\frac{Q \cdot R_v}{V_0} \quad \text{(AVR droop)}$$

Typical droop settings: **4–5% for frequency**, **2.5–5% for voltage**.

## 2.3 Switchboards — The Central Nervous System

A **Marine Switchboard** is a free-standing, dead-front, drip-proof, IP 44 assembly containing:

- **Generator panels** – Circuit breakers, instruments, synchronizing gear.
- **Bus-tie breakers** – Interconnect switchboard sections.
- **Feeder panels** – Supply distribution to sub-boards.
- **Motor starters** – DOL, star-delta, soft starters, VFDs.
- **Protective relays** – Overcurrent, earth fault, reverse power, under-voltage.
- **Metering** – kW, kVAR, kWh, V, I, Hz, PF, MWh.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** A typical 14-mark question may ask: *"Explain the construction and features of a marine main switchboard with a neat sketch."* — Students must draw the **single-line diagram** and label at least 8 components.

## 2.4 Distribution Network

### 2.4.1 Power Distribution Topology

```
Generators → Main Switchboard → Emergency Switchboard
                  ↓                        ↓
            Sub-Boards (Zone)        Emergency Loads
                  ↓
            Motor Control Centers (MCC)
                  ↓
        Final Sub-Circuits (Loads)
```

The distribution is **radial** in most ships for **selectivity** and ease of fault isolation, although some naval vessels use **ring main** distribution for redundancy.

### 2.4.2 Voltage Drop in Cables

$$V_{\text{drop}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (R \cos \phi + X \sin \phi)$$

Maximum permissible drop: **2.5% for power circuits, 5% for lighting circuits** (per IEC 60092-201).

## 2.5 Transformers

Step-down transformers (typically **440/220 V** or **6.6 kV/440 V**) supply lighting and small power. Special **EPC (Earthed Phase-Compensated)** transformers are used to feed **single-phase loads** from a 3-phase ship system without creating third-harmonic issues.

## 2.6 Emergency Power System

Per **SOLAS Chapter II-1 Regulation 43**, the emergency source must supply for **at least 18 hours** the following:

- Emergency lighting at every muster station, alleyway, stairway.
- Navigation lights, alarms, and signals.
- Fire detection and fire-fighting systems.
- Steering gear (where electrically driven).
- Communication systems.
- Watertight doors.

The emergency generator is automatically started within **45 seconds** of blackout.

## 2.7 Batteries

Batteries are **DC power reservoirs** providing:

1. **Starting power** for emergency diesel generator.
2. **Backup for critical DC loads** (control circuits, alarm systems).
3. **Bridge navigation backup** (gyrocompass, GPS, GMDSS).

Common types: **Lead-acid (vented/VRLA)**, **NiCd (alkaline)**, and increasingly **Lithium-ion (LiFePO$_4$)**.

Battery capacity in ampere-hours is selected using:

$$C = \frac{I \cdot t}{k}$$

Where $k$ is a derating factor (typically 0.8 for temperature and aging).

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{\text{shaft}} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Mechanical power from torque | W, RPM, N·m |
| 2 | $f = \dfrac{p N}{120}$ | Frequency vs poles and speed | Hz, RPM |
| 3 | $E_{\text{ph}} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{\text{ph}} \, \Phi \, k_w$ | Generated EMF per phase | V |
| 4 | $P = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L \cos \phi$ | Three-phase real power | W |
| 5 | $Q = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L \sin \phi$ | Three-phase reactive power | VAR |
| 6 | $S = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L$ | Apparent power | VA |
| 7 | $V_{\text{drop}} = \sqrt{3} \, I \, L (R \cos \phi + X \sin \phi)$ | Cable voltage drop | V |
| 8 | $\% \text{Droop} = \dfrac{\Delta f / f_0}{P / P_{\text{rated}}} \times 100$ | Governor droop characteristic | % |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | Generator efficiency | % |
| 10 | $I_{\text{sc}} = \dfrac{V}{Z_{\text{sync}}}$ | Short-circuit current | A |
| 11 | $C_{\text{bat}} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{k}$ | Battery sizing | Ah |
| 12 | $I_{\text{starting}} = 6 \text{ to } 8 \times I_{\text{FLC}}$ | Motor starting current (DOL) | A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Application:** In ship design offices (e.g., Wärtsilä, MAN Energy Solutions, Siemens Marine), engineers use these exact equations in software tools like **ETAP**, **DIgSILENT PowerFactory**, and **AVEVA Electrical** to perform **short-circuit, load-flow, harmonic, and protection coordination studies** before the ship is built.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Component Specifications

The following analytical matrix provides a deep-dive into the **electrical ratings, design parameters, and KTU-relevant facts** for each major component of the shipboard electrical system.

| Component | Type | Typical Rating | Key Design Parameter | KTU Point |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Main Generator | 3-$\phi$ Sync. | 1–8 MW, 440 V / 6.6 kV | $X_d^{\prime\prime} = 0.15$ pu | AVR, governor |
| Emergency Gen. | 3-$\phi$ Sync. | 100–500 kW, 440 V | Auto-start in **$\leq$ 45 s** | SOLAS Reg. 43 |
| Shore Gen. | Diesel | 250 kW – 1 MW | Harbor operation | Cold Ironing prep |
| Main Switchboard | IP 44, dead-front | Up to 6300 A bus | Short-time **50 kA / 1 s** | 1L diagram |
| Bus Tie Breaker | ACB | 4000–6300 A | Selective tripping | Load sharing |
| Power Transformer | Oil/Dry type | 500–2500 kVA | $\Delta$-Y or Y-$\Delta$ | Harmonics |
| Propulsion Motor | Synchronous / Induction | 5–30 MW | Soft-start via VFD | POD, azimuth |
| Bow Thruster Motor | Slipring/Async | 1–4 MW | LRS, FFA, FPP | High inrush |
| Compressor Motor | Squirrel cage | 50–200 kW | Star-Delta / DOL | Reefer plant |
| Battery (VRLA) | DC | 24 V, 200–600 Ah | Cyclic vs float | GMDSS backup |
| UPS | Double conv. | 5–50 kVA | $<5$% THD | Bridge, ECDIS |
| Shore Connection | HVSC | 6.6/11 kV, 5–20 MVA | IEC 80005-1 | Cold Ironing |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Synchronization of Generators

The synchronization of an incoming generator to a live bus is one of the most critical shipboard operations. KTU frequently tests this concept.

### 3.2.1 Four Conditions for Synchronization

1. **Equal voltage magnitude**: $V_{\text{incoming}} = V_{\text{bus}}$
2. **Equal frequency**: $f_{\text{incoming}} = f_{\text{bus}}$
3. **Same phase sequence**: RYB matches RYB
4. **Phase angle coincident**: $\theta_{\text{incoming}} = \theta_{\text{bus}}$

### 3.2.2 Derivation of the Synchronizing Power Torque

When two generators are connected out of phase by an angle $\delta$, the synchronizing power transfer between them is:

$$P_{\text{sync}} = \frac{V_1 V_2}{X_s} \sin \delta$$

Derivation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Generator 1 internal EMF: } & E_1 \angle 0^\circ \\
\text{Generator 2 internal EMF: } & E_2 \angle \delta \\
\text{Per-phase voltage equation: } & E_1 - E_2 = jX_s \cdot I \\
\text{Current: } & I = \frac{E_1 \angle 0^\circ - E_2 \angle \delta}{j X_s} \\
\text{Complex power of Gen 1: } & S_1 = E_1 \cdot I^* \\
S_1 & = E_1 \cdot \left[\frac{E_1 \angle 0^\circ - E_2 \angle \delta}{j X_s}\right]^* \\
S_1 & = \frac{E_1 (E_1 \angle 0^\circ - E_2 \angle -\delta)}{-j X_s} \\
S_1 & = \frac{j}{X_s}\left[E_1^2 - E_1 E_2 (\cos \delta - j \sin \delta)\right] \\
S_1 & = \frac{E_1 E_2 \sin \delta}{X_s} + j\frac{E_1^2 - E_1 E_2 \cos \delta}{X_s} \\
\therefore P_{\text{sync}} & = \frac{E_1 E_2}{X_s} \sin \delta
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters on board:** If $\delta = 0$, no synchronizing torque exists — the generators are "in step". If $\delta$ deviates due to load changes, a restoring torque develops that pulls them back. Maximum stable power transfer occurs at $\delta = 90^\circ$, but operation is limited to $\delta = 20^\circ\text{–}30^\circ$ for safety.

## 3.3 Derivation: Short-Circuit Current at Ship's Busbar

Per IEC 61363, the sub-transient short-circuit current at the main bus is:

$$I_k^{\prime\prime} = \frac{c \cdot U_n}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{\text{eq}}}$$

Where $c = 1.05$ (voltage factor per IEC 60909), and $Z_{\text{eq}}$ is the Thevenin equivalent impedance.

For a single generator feeding a fault:

$$Z_{\text{eq}} = Z_k^{\prime\prime} = \sqrt{(R_a)^2 + (X_d^{\prime\prime})^2}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
R_a & = \text{armature resistance (pu)} \\
X_d^{\prime\prime} & = \text{sub-transient reactance} \approx 0.10 \text{–} 0.20 \text{ pu}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3.1 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A 3 MVA, 6.6 kV, 50 Hz, 3-phase ship generator has $X_d^{\prime\prime} = 0.15$ pu and $R_a = 0.01$ pu. Two such generators operate in parallel. A 3-phase bolted fault occurs at the main bus. Calculate the fault current in kA.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Base current calculation**

$$I_{\text{base}} = \frac{S_{\text{base}}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{\text{base}}} = \frac{3 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 6600}$$

$$I_{\text{base}} = \frac{3 \times 10^6}{11431.5} = 262.4 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Impedance per generator in pu**

$$Z_k^{\prime\prime} = \sqrt{(0.01)^2 + (0.15)^2} = \sqrt{0.0001 + 0.0225} = \sqrt{0.0226}$$

$$Z_k^{\prime\prime} = 0.1503 \text{ pu}$$

**Step 3 — Parallel combination of two generators**

Two identical impedances in parallel:

$$Z_{\text{eq}} = \frac{0.1503}{2} = 0.0752 \text{ pu}$$

**Step 4 — Initial symmetrical short-circuit current**

$$I_k^{\prime\prime} = \frac{c \cdot I_{\text{base}}}{Z_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{1.05}{0.0752}$$

$$I_k^{\prime\prime} = 13.96 \text{ pu} = 13.96 \times 262.4$$

$$I_k^{\prime\prime} = 3664 \text{ A} \approx 3.66 \text{ kA}$$

**Step 5 — Peak (making) current**

$$i_p = \kappa \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot I_k^{\prime\prime}$$

For a shipboard generator, $\kappa \approx 1.8$ (per IEC 61363):

$$i_p = 1.8 \times \sqrt{2} \times 3.66 = 9.32 \text{ kA (peak)}$$

**Step 6 — Verification against switchgear rating**

The main ACB must have a short-time withstand $\geq 9.32$ kA peak and $\geq 3.66$ kA rms for 1 second. A typical 6.6 kV marine switchboard is rated at **40 kA / 1 s** — so this system is safe.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always state the **assumed $\kappa$ factor** explicitly. Also, remember that the **X/R ratio** determines the DC decay component. For a marine system with predominantly inductive impedances, $X/R \approx 15$, giving $\kappa \approx 1.80$. For purely inductive circuits (theoretical), $\kappa = 2.0$.

## 3.4 Derivation: Battery Sizing for Emergency DC Loads

**Problem (typical KTU 14-marker):** A ship's emergency battery must supply the following loads continuously for 18 hours:
- 30 emergency lights @ 40 W each
- 5 alarm panels @ 60 W each
- 1 VHF radio @ 200 W
- 1 gyrocompass @ 150 W

Determine the required battery capacity (Ah) for a 24 V system, assuming 80% derating.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total power load**

$$P_{\text{lights}} = 30 \times 40 = 1200 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{\text{alarms}} = 5 \times 60 = 300 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{\text{VHF}} = 200 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{\text{gyro}} = 150 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{\text{total}} = 1200 + 300 + 200 + 150 = 1850 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — DC current draw**

$$I_{\text{DC}} = \frac{P_{\text{total}}}{V_{\text{DC}}} = \frac{1850}{24} = 77.08 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Ampere-hour consumption over 18 hours**

$$\text{Energy} = I_{\text{DC}} \times t = 77.08 \times 18 = 1387.5 \text{ Ah}$$

**Step 4 — Apply derating factor**

$$C_{\text{required}} = \frac{1387.5}{0.80} = 1734.4 \text{ Ah}$$

**Step 5 — Practical selection**

Nearest standard battery bank: **2 × 12 V, 1800 Ah** (connected in series for 24 V) or **4 × 6 V, 1800 Ah** strings.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Shipboard Electrical System — Single Line Diagram

The following Mermaid block represents the complete electrical power flow architecture of a typical merchant ship.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DG1[Diesel Gen 1<br/>3 MVA 6.6 kV]
    DG2[Diesel Gen 2<br/>3 MVA 6.6 kV]
    DG3[Diesel Gen 3<br/>Harbor / Emergency<br/>500 kVA 440 V]
    MSB[Main Switchboard<br/>6.6 kV 4000 A]
    ET1[EPC Transformer<br/>6.6 kV to 440 V<br/>2 MVA]
    ET2[EPC Transformer<br/>6.6 kV to 220 V<br/>500 kVA]
    ESB[Emergency Switchboard<br/>440 V]
    ESSB[Emergency Sub Board]
    BCB[Battery Charger Board<br/>24 V DC]
    BAT[Battery Bank<br/>24 V 1800 Ah]
    PMS[Power Management System]
    MCC1[MCC Zone 1<br/>Engine Room]
    MCC2[MCC Zone 2<br/>Cargo Hold]
    MCC3[MCC Zone 3<br/>Bridge]
    SHORE[Shore Power<br/>Connection 6.6 kV]
    BUSBREAK[Bus Tie Breaker<br/>ACB 4000 A]
    LOADA[Steering Gear Motor<br/>75 kW]
    LOADB[Bow Thruster Motor<br/>2 MW]
    LOADC[Compressor Motor<br/>100 kW]
    LOADD[Navigation Lights<br/>10 kW]
    LOADE[VHF GPS Radar<br/>5 kW]
    LOADF[Emergency Lighting<br/>2 kW]

    DG1 --> MSB
    DG2 --> MSB
    DG3 --> ESB
    MSB --> BUSBREAK
    BUSBREAK --> MSB
    MSB --> ET1
    MSB --> ET2
    ET1 --> MCC1
    ET1 --> MCC2
    ET1 --> MCC3
    ET2 --> LOADD
    MSB --> ESB
    ESB --> ESSB
    ESB --> BCB
    BCB --> BAT
    PMS --- MSB
    PMS --- ESB
    SHORE --> MSB
    MCC1 --> LOADA
    MCC1 --> LOADC
    MCC2 --> LOADB
    MCC3 --> LOADE
    ESSB --> LOADF
```

## 4.2 Main Switchboard Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GEN1[Generator 1<br/>Panel]
    GEN2[Generator 2<br/>Panel]
    GEN3[Generator 3<br/>Panel]
    BUSBAR[Main Bus Bar<br/>6.6 kV]
    SYNC[Syncroscope<br/>and Check Sync Relay]
    BTB[Bus Tie Breaker<br/>Panel]
    FEED1[Feeder to<br/>Transformer 1]
    FEED2[Feeder to<br/>Transformer 2]
    FEED3[Feeder to<br/>Emergency Board]
    FEED4[Feeder to<br/>Bow Thruster]
    METER[Centralized<br/>Metering Panel]
    PROT[Protection Relays<br/>OC EF RP UV]
    AMF[Auto Mains<br/>Failure Panel]
    PMSINT[PMS Interface<br/>and SCADA]

    GEN1 --> BUSBAR
    GEN2 --> BUSBAR
    GEN3 --> BUSBAR
    BUSBAR --> SYNC
    BUSBAR --> BTB
    BUSBAR --> FEED1
    BUSBAR --> FEED2
    BUSBAR --> FEED3
    BUSBAR --> FEED4
    BUSBAR --> METER
    BUSBAR --> PROT
    BUSBAR --> AMF
    BUSBAR --> PMSINT
```

## 4.3 Emergency Power System Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MAINSUP[Main Bus Failure<br/>Detected by UV Relay]
    EGD[Emergency Diesel<br/>Generator Starts<br/>within 45 sec]
    ESB2[Emergency Switchboard<br/>Energized]
    TD[Time Delay 0 to 30 s<br/>for load transfer]
    LOADT1[Steering Gear]
    LOADT2[Fire Pumps]
    LOADT3[Navigation Lights]
    LOADT4[Bridge Electronics]
    LOADT5[Internal Communication]
    BAT2[24 V Battery Bank<br/>Powers Loads<br/>during 45 s gap]
    BCB2[Battery Charger]
    TR3[Transformer<br/>440 V to 220 V]

    MAINSUP --> EGD
    EGD --> ESB2
    ESB2 --> TD
    TD --> LOADT1
    TD --> LOADT2
    TD --> LOADT3
    TD --> LOADT4
    TD --> LOADT5
    BAT2 --> LOADT4
    BCB2 --> BAT2
    ESB2 --> TR3
    TR3 --> LOADT5
```

## 4.4 Battery and UPS Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ACDC[AC to DC<br/>Rectifier]
    BATBANK[VRLA Battery Bank<br/>24 V 1800 Ah]
    INV[DC to AC<br/>Inverter]
    BYPASS[Static Bypass<br/>Switch]
    UPSOUT[Clean AC Output<br/>to Bridge Loads]
    CHARGE[Float / Boost<br/>Charge Controller]
    LOADV[ECDIS Radar]
    LOADW[GMDSS Console]
    LOADX[Gyrocompass]

    ACDC --> BATBANK
    BATBANK --> INV
    INV --> BYPASS
    BYPASS --> UPSOUT
    ACDC --> BYPASS
    UPSOUT --> LOADV
    UPSOUT --> LOADW
    UPSOUT --> LOADX
    CHARGE --- ACDC
    CHARGE --- BATBANK
```

## 4.5 Propulsion Motor Drive Architecture (Diesel-Electric)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DG4A[Diesel Engine 1]
    DG4B[Diesel Engine 2]
    SG1[Synchronous Generator 1<br/>3 MVA]
    SG2[Synchronous Generator 2<br/>3 MVA]
    CONV[AC-DC-AC Converter<br/>6-pulse / 12-pulse]
    PROP1[Propulsion Motor 1<br/>Synchronous 10 MW]
    PROP2[Propulsion Motor 2<br/>Synchronous 10 MW]
    CPP[Controllable Pitch<br/>Propeller]
    THROTTLE[Bridge Throttle<br/>Command]

    DG4A --> SG1
    DG4B --> SG2
    SG1 --> CONV
    SG2 --> CONV
    CONV --> PROP1
    CONV --> PROP2
    PROP1 --> CPP
    PROP2 --> CPP
    THROTTLE --- CONV
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** These block diagrams correspond to real engineering deliverables called **"Single Line Diagrams (SLD)"** or **"Key One-Line Diagrams"** that ship electrical designers must produce and submit to classification societies for approval (e.g., to IRS/Lloyd's) before construction begins.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** — *"List any six essential components of the shipboard electrical system."*

**Model Answer:**

The six essential components of the shipboard electrical system are:

1. **Prime Movers** (Diesel engines, steam/gas turbines) – convert fuel energy to mechanical rotation.
2. **Generators** (Synchronous alternators) – convert mechanical to electrical energy at 440 V / 6.6 kV, 50/60 Hz.
3. **Main Switchboard** – central collection, control, switching, and protection node.
4. **Distribution Network** (Cables, bus ducts, transformers, motor control centers) – delivers power to all ship areas.
5. **Loads** (Propulsion motors, pumps, compressors, lighting, navigation) – consume electrical power for ship operation.
6. **Emergency Power System** (Emergency generator, batteries, UPS) – provides backup as per SOLAS Chapter II-1.

> [!TIP]
> For full 3 marks, always write at least **one sentence describing the function** of each component, not just the name.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** — *"What are the functions of the Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR) and the Governor in a marine generator?"*

**Model Answer:**

| Control Element | Function | Variable Controlled |
|---|---|---|
| **AVR (Automatic Voltage Regulator)** | Maintains constant terminal voltage by varying the DC field excitation of the generator. Provides reactive power (kVAR) sharing in parallel operation. | $V_{\text{terminal}}$, $Q$ |
| **Governor (Speed Governor)** | Maintains constant frequency (and hence constant speed) by adjusting the fuel input to the prime mover. Provides real power (kW) sharing in parallel operation. | $f$, $P$ (kW) |

Together, AVR and Governor form a **dual-loop control system** that ensures the generator delivers stable voltage and frequency to the bus, conforming to classification rules (typically $\pm 2.5\%$ voltage, $\pm 5\%$ frequency variation).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions — Full Internal Choice (2 × 14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Each Module in the End Semester Examination carries **one 14-mark question with internal choice** (i.e., either Question A OR Question B must be answered). Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
>
> **(a)** Explain with a neat single-line diagram the **complete layout of the shipboard electrical system**, covering generation, main and emergency switchboards, distribution, and essential loads.  
> **(b)** Describe the **construction, types, and rating of marine switchboards** as per IEC 60092 / SOLAS standards.

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Layout of Shipboard Electrical System:**

A typical merchant ship employs **three or four diesel-generator sets** feeding a **main switchboard** at 440 V or 6.6 kV.

**Generation Stage:**
- 2–4 main diesel generator sets, each 1–8 MW, with prime mover speed **750 RPM (4-pole at 50 Hz) or 900 RPM (4-pole at 60 Hz)**.
- One emergency diesel generator, 100–500 kW, separately housed in the emergency generator room above the bulkhead deck.

**Main Switchboard Stage:**
- Located in the **engine control room (ECR)**.
- Contains generator panels, bus-tie breaker, feeder panels, synchronizing panel, and protection relays.
- The main bus is divided into **two sections** connected by a normally-closed or open bus-tie breaker for redundancy.

**Distribution Stage:**
- Step-down transformers feed **440 V sub-boards** for auxiliary motors and **220 V lighting transformers** for accommodation and corridors.
- **Motor control centers (MCCs)** are provided in each zone (engine room, cargo hold, bow, stern).
- **Cabling** runs through **cable trays** in dedicated routes with fire-resistant (Fire-Resistant type, FR) insulation per IEC 60332-3.

**Essential/Emergency Loads:**
- Emergency switchboard is fed from the emergency generator and supplies **steering gear, fire pumps, navigation lights, bridge equipment, and fire detection** for at least **18 hours**.

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Marine Switchboard Construction & Standards:**

A marine switchboard is a **dead-front, free-standing, IP 44 rated, drip-proof** panel assembly built per **IEC 60092-302**.

**Constructional Features:**
- **Enclosure**: 2–3 mm powder-coated steel, self-supporting.
- **Bus bars**: Tin-plated copper, sized for **125% of full-load current**, color-coded (R-Y-B for phases, grey for neutral, green-yellow for earth).
- **Circuit breakers**: Air Circuit Breakers (ACB) for generators and bus-ties; Moulded Case Circuit Breakers (MCCB) for feeders; Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCB) for sub-circuits.
- **Instruments**: Analogue and digital meters for V, I, Hz, kW, kVAR, PF, kWh.
- **Protective relays**: Static or numerical — overcurrent (51), earth fault (51N), reverse power (32), under-voltage (27), over-voltage (59), differential (87), loss of excitation (40).
- **Synchronizing gear**: Synchroscope, lamps, check-sync relay, auto-sync unit.
- **Interlocking**: Mechanical and electrical interlocks for safe operation.

**Types of Marine Switchboards:**
1. **Main Switchboard (MSB)**: Receives main generators.
2. **Emergency Switchboard (ESB)**: Receives emergency generator.
3. **Sub-Boards / Section Boards**: Feed specific zones.
4. **Motor Control Center (MCC)**: Houses starters for motors.
5. **Lighting Distribution Board (LDB)**: For 220 V lighting circuits.
6. **Battery Distribution Board (BDB)**: For 24 V DC circuits.

**Rating:** Up to 6300 A bus, short-time withstand 50 kA / 1 s, rated voltage up to 11 kV.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **classification, ratings, and applications of batteries** used on board ships.  
> **(b)** Describe the **procedure for paralleling (synchronizing) two ship generators** with the necessary instruments and the four synchronization conditions.

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Marine Batteries — Classification, Ratings, and Applications:**

**Classification (by Electrolyte Type):**

| Type | Voltage/Cell | Energy Density | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lead-Acid (Vented)** | 2 V | 30–40 Wh/kg | Main starting battery, UPS |
| **Lead-Acid (VRLA)** | 2 V | 35–45 Wh/kg | Maintenance-free, bridge |
| **NiCd (Alkaline)** | 1.2 V | 50–60 Wh/kg | Emergency, tropical climates |
| **Lithium-ion (LiFePO$_4$)** | 3.2 V | 90–160 Wh/kg | Modern eco-ships, hybrid |

**Rating:**
- **Capacity in Ah** (e.g., 600 Ah @ 10-hour rate).
- **Cold Cranking Amps (CCA)** for starting duty.
- **Cyclic vs Float** duty (cyclic for UPS, float for backup).

**Applications on Board:**
1. **Starting the emergency diesel generator**.
2. **Supplying GMDSS equipment** (Global Maritime Distress and Safety System) for 1 hour minimum.
3. **Backup for bridge navigation instruments** (gyro, radar, GPS, ECDIS).
4. **Engine control and alarm systems** (24 V DC).
5. **Reefer container monitoring** on reefer ships.

The battery room must be **ventilated** (H$_2$ gas explosion risk) and located **above the bulkhead deck**.

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Paralleling (Synchronizing) Procedure for Ship Generators:**

Before closing the incoming generator's circuit breaker, four conditions must be satisfied:

| Condition | Required Action |
|---|---|
| 1. **Equal Voltage** | Adjust field current via AVR until $V_{\text{incoming}} = V_{\text{bus}}$ |
| 2. **Equal Frequency** | Adjust governor speed until $f_{\text{incoming}} = f_{\text{bus}}$ (within $\pm 0.1$ Hz) |
| 3. **Same Phase Sequence** | Pre-verified during commissioning by phase-sequence indicator |
| 4. **In-Phase (Coincident Angle)** | Adjust speed slightly higher so the synchroscope rotates slowly clockwise |

**Instruments Used:**
- **Synchroscope**: Pointer rotates; at 12 o'clock position, voltages are in phase.
- **Lamp dark-bright method** (Lamps connected across the open breaker): when all lamps go dark simultaneously, voltages are in phase.
- **Check-sync relay** (ANSI 25): Permits breaker closure only when all four conditions are met.
- **Voltmeter & Frequency meter** on the synchronizing panel.

**Procedure Steps:**
1. Start the incoming diesel engine; bring it to rated speed.
2. Excite the generator to match bus voltage.
3. Adjust frequency to within $\pm 0.2$ Hz of bus frequency.
4. Observe synchroscope; allow incoming generator to run at slightly higher frequency.
5. Wait for synchroscope pointer to approach 12 o'clock.
6. At the correct instant (breaker closing time compensated), close the breaker.
7. Transfer load using governor (raise kW) and AVR (lower kVAR) controls to share load proportionally.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Forgetting the X/R ratio** while calculating short-circuit current — always state the assumption clearly.
> 2. **Confusing phase voltage and line voltage** — remember: $V_L = \sqrt{3} V_{\text{ph}}$.
> 3. **Not stating the SOLAS reference** for emergency systems — always cite **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 43** for the 45-second auto-start and 18-hour endurance.
> 4. **Skipping the droop percentage** when explaining parallel operation — droop of 4–5% must be mentioned.
> 5. **Drawing wrong switchboard layouts** — use **standard symbols** as per **IEC 60617**, not freehand drawings.
> 6. **Wrong direction of syncroscope rotation** — incoming should rotate **clockwise (slow)** meaning it is slightly **faster** than bus.
> 7. **Ignoring reactive load sharing** — load sharing is **two-dimensional** (kW via governor, kVAR via AVR).
> 8. **Forgetting IP ratings** — write **IP 44** for switchboards, **IP 56** for deck equipment, **IP 67** for bilge areas.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ☐ Shipboard electrical system is an **isolated micro-grid** — no external connection at sea.
- ☐ Standard voltages: **440 V** (small ships), **6.6 kV** (medium), **11 kV** (large vessels).
- ☐ Standard frequency: **50 Hz** (most of world) or **60 Hz** (Americas, Japan).
- ☐ Prime mover speed for 50 Hz, 4-pole: **1500 RPM**; for 60 Hz: **1800 RPM**.
- ☐ **Synchronous generators** dominate ships due to independent P and Q control.
- ☐ **AVR controls voltage/reactive power**; **Governor controls frequency/active power**.
- ☐ **Droop setting**: 4–5% (frequency), 2.5–5% (voltage) for load sharing.
- ☐ **4 synchronization conditions**: equal V, equal f, same phase sequence, in-phase.
- ☐ **SOLAS Reg. 43**: Emergency generator must auto-start within **45 seconds**; supply for **18 hours**.
- ☐ **Battery types**: Lead-acid (Vented & VRLA), NiCd, LiFePO$_4$. Voltage: **24 V DC** typical.
- ☐ **DOL starting current** = **6 to 8 times** Full Load Current (FLC).
- ☐ **IP 44** for switchboards; **IP 56** for deck; **IP 67** for bilge/submerged.
- ☐ **Maximum voltage drop**: 2.5% (power), 5% (lighting) per IEC 60092-201.
- ☐ **Switchboard short-time withstand**: 50 kA / 1 s typical.
- ☐ **Short-circuit current factor** $\kappa \approx 1.8$ for marine alternators.
- ☐ **Shore power (Cold Ironing)** standard: IEC 80005-1 for HVSC.
- ☐ **Classification societies**: IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, ABS, BV — must approve design.
- ☐ **Diesel-electric propulsion** uses AC–DC–AC converters (Cycloconverter or PWM VFD).
- ☐ **$f = \frac{pN}{120}$** — most-tested formula on KTU board for Module 1.
- ☐ **EPC (Earthed Phase-Compensated) transformers** are ship-specific for single-phase loads.
- ☐ **Power Management System (PMS)**: Auto-start/stop of generators based on load.
- ☐ **Auto Mains Failure (AMF)**: Detects blackout, starts EDG, transfers emergency loads.
- ☐ **$P_{\text{sync}} = \frac{E_1 E_2}{X_s} \sin\delta$** — synchronizing power, max at $\delta = 90^\circ$.
- ☐ **Three independent sources**: Main generators, Emergency generator, Shore supply.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Merits and Demerits of AC and DC on Board
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Merits and Demerits of AC and DC on Board # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In marine electrical engineering, the choice between **Alternating Current (AC)** and **Direct Current (DC)** distribution systems represents a fundamental design decision in shipboard power architecture. Historically, the **DC system** was the original choice for ship propulsion and auxiliary power (early 1900s), but modern commercial and naval vessels have overwhelmingly transitioned to **AC systems** due to superior power density, ease of voltage transformation, and compatibility with modern high-power drives. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** > **AC Distribution on Board:** A power distribution architecture in which the main generation, distribution, and load-side supply operate at sinusoidal alternating voltage (typically **440 V, 3-phase, 60 Hz** or **690 V, 3-phase, 60 Hz** for modern vessels). > **DC Distribution on Board:** A power distribution architecture in which the main supply maintains constant polarity (typically **24 V DC** for control circuits, **110 V DC** for emergency services, and up to **1000 V DC** for modern integrated electric propulsion systems). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine two highways connecting a ship’s engine room (the power source) to its various consumers (deck machinery, navigation, lighting): - **AC Highway** is like a **modern, multi-lane expressway with on-ramps and off-ramps at every city**. You can step up the voltage to cross long distances efficiently (like merging onto a fast highway), then step it down easily at every neighborhood (substation) using **transformers** — a simple iron-core device. However, traffic signals (switchgear) are slightly more complex because the current keeps reversing direction. - **DC Highway** is like an **old, single-direction railway track**. It only goes one way, so speed control and battery charging are wonderfully simple. But to run a high-power motor at high speed, you need **bulky rotating machinery with commutators** (think of an old steam locomotive needing constant maintenance of its carbon brushes). On a modern ship, you use the **AC highway for the long-distance, high-power routes (main propulsion)** and keep **DC tracks for specific short-distance services (batteries, emergency lighting, electronics)**. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants - **Standard Shipboard AC Voltages (per IEC 60092-201):** 440 V, 690 V (LV); 3.3 kV, 6.6 kV, 11 kV (HV) - **Standard Shipboard DC Voltages:** 24 V DC, 110 V DC, 220 V DC - **Standard Shipboard Frequency:** **60 Hz** (preferred in modern vessels to reduce motor weight and avoid resonance with propeller blade frequency) - **Insulation Class (typical):** Class F (155 °C) or Class H (180 °C) > [!NOTE] > **Why 60 Hz and not 50 Hz?** > At 60 Hz, transformers and motors can be made **15-20% smaller and lighter** for the same power rating — a critical advantage where every kilogram matters for ship displacement and fuel efficiency. ## 1.4 Historical Context | Era | Dominant System | Reason | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1880s – 1920s | **DC** | Only practical generator; storage batteries were the only way to retain power | | 1920s – 1960s | **Mixed (AC gen + DC aux)** | AC introduced for lighting, DC retained for auxiliaries and controls | | 1960s – 2000s | **AC dominant** | Reliable static transformers, AC motors matured, power electronics absent | | 2000s – Present | **Hybrid AC + DC (MVDC)** | Modern warships, cruise liners, and DP vessels use Integrated Electric Propulsion (IEP) with DC links | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Merits of AC on Board ### A. Easy Voltage Transformation AC voltage can be **stepped up or down** using a static **transformer**, which has no moving parts and requires minimal maintenance. This allows high voltage transmission through the ship’s cable trays (reducing current and therefore **I²R losses**), then stepping down at the load. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Voltage Transformation Equation:** > $$\frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{I_2}{I_1}$$ > where $N_1, N_2$ are the primary and secondary turns. ### B. Higher Power-to-Weight Ratio AC induction motors (especially **squirrel-cage type**) are **rugged, brushless, and explosion-proof** — ideal for the hazardous, salt-laden marine environment. They are 25-30% lighter than equivalent DC motors of the same rating. ### C. No Commutator DC machines require **commutators and carbon brushes** that wear out, spark, and need frequent replacement. AC machines (induction or synchronous) eliminate this entire maintenance burden. ### D. Efficient for High-Power Propulsion Modern **AC drives (VFDs)** using IGBTs can precisely control shaft speed for **diesel-electric** and **CODLAG** propulsion plants, achieving fuel savings of 15-20%. ### E. Natural Suitability for Parallel Operation Synchronous generators can be **synchronized and paralleled** using frequency, phase, and voltage matching — a well-established, reliable procedure. --- ## 2.2 Demerits of AC on Board ### A. Complex Switchgear AC breakers must **interrupt current at natural zero crossings** of the waveform. For high inductive loads, this requires **arc-quenching chambers**, making AC circuit breakers more elaborate than DC breakers. ### B. Skin Effect Losses At high frequencies, AC current tends to flow near the surface of conductors, effectively **reducing the cross-sectional area** available. This causes higher copper losses for the same cable size. > [!NOTE] > **Skin Depth Formula:** > $$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2\rho}{\omega\mu}}$$ > where $\rho$ is resistivity, $\omega = 2\pi f$ is angular frequency, $\mu$ is permeability. ### C. Harmonics from Power Electronics Modern **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** and **thyristor-controlled rectifiers** inject harmonic currents (5th, 7th, 11th, 13th…) into the distribution network. These can cause: - Overheating of transformers and generators - Malfunction of sensitive electronics - Resonance in power factor correction capacitors ### D. Single-Phase Issues in Three-Phase Systems **Unbalanced three-phase loading** (common on ships due to single-phase galley, lighting, and accommodation loads) can cause neutral currents, overheating, and vibration in alternators. ### E. Insulation Stress Peak AC voltage is $\sqrt{2}$ times the RMS value, demanding **higher insulation ratings** for cables and equipment. --- ## 2.3 Merits of DC on Board ### A. Simplicity of Speed Control DC motors (especially **shunt and separately excited**) offer **inherent and linear speed control** over a wide range by varying armature voltage or field current. > [!IMPORTANT] > **DC Shunt Motor Speed Equation:** > $$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k\phi}$$ > where $V$ is supply voltage, $I_a$ is armature current, $R_a$ is armature resistance, $\phi$ is field flux, and $k$ is a machine constant. ### B. Battery Compatibility Ships must provide **emergency power** (per SOLAS) for at least 3 hours. Batteries are inherently DC, making DC systems ideal for **emergency lighting, fire detection, and steering gear backup**. ### C. No Harmonics, No Reactive Power DC systems have **no frequency-dependent issues**, no skin effect, no reactive power (VARS), and no synchronization requirements for paralleling batteries. ### D. Simpler Parallel Operation DC generators can be paralleled by matching **voltage and polarity** — much simpler than AC synchronizing. ### E. Lower Peak Voltage DC peak voltage equals RMS voltage (no $\sqrt{2}$ factor), allowing **thinner insulation** for the same working voltage. --- ## 2.4 Demerits of DC on Board ### A. Heavy and Bulky Machines DC machines need commutators, brush gear, and field windings. For the same kW rating, a DC machine is **30-50% heavier** than an AC machine. ### B. High Maintenance Brushes wear out (typical life: 2000-4000 hours), commutators need turning/skimming, and sparking in flammable atmospheres is a major safety hazard. ### C. Voltage Drop in Long Cables For the same power, DC current is **constant** and **cannot be transformed** to a higher voltage. Long cable runs cause significant **I²R losses**. ### D. Sparking in Hazardous Areas Open commutator sparking is unacceptable in oil tanker pump rooms or chemical carriers — making **flameproof enclosures mandatory**, adding cost. ### E. Limited Power for Modern Propulsion The single largest demerit: pure DC systems **cannot economically handle the 20-100 MW** demanded by modern main propulsion. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Use Case on Board | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Three-Phase Power | $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | AC generator/motor rating | | 2 | DC Power | $P = V I$ | DC circuit calculations | | 3 | Transformer Ratio | $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | Voltage step-up/down | | 4 | Skin Depth | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\rho}{\omega\mu}}$ | AC cable sizing | | 5 | Peak/RMS Factor | $V_{peak} = \sqrt{2}\,V_{rms}$ | Insulation design | | 6 | DC Motor Speed | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k\phi}$ | DC propulsion control | | 7 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | AC motor selection | | 8 | Voltage Drop (DC) | $V_{drop} = 2 I R$ | Battery cable sizing | | 9 | Voltage Drop (3-φ AC) | $V_{drop} = \sqrt{3}\,I L (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ | AC feeder design | | 10 | Power Loss Ratio | $\dfrac{P_{loss,DC}}{P_{loss,AC}} = \left(\dfrac{V_{AC}}{V_{DC}}\right)^2$ | Transmission efficiency | > [!NOTE] > **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** For the same power, increasing transmission voltage by factor $k$ reduces line losses by factor $k^2$. This is why **AC at 6.6 kV or 11 kV** is preferred for large cruise ships and naval combatants. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why AC Wins for Long-Distance Power Transmission on Board ### Problem Context A bulk carrier has its main switchboard (MSB) located 80 m from a bow thruster rated at **1 MW, 690 V, 3-phase, 60 Hz, 0.85 pf lagging**. Compare the cable losses and required copper cross-section for: - **Option A:** Direct 690 V AC supply - **Option B:** 6.6 kV AC stepped up at MSB and stepped down at thruster ### Step 1: Calculate the Full-Load Current at Load End **At 690 V (Option A):** $$I_L = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L \cos\phi} = \frac{1 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 690 \times 0.85}$$ $$I_L = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1015.6} \approx 984.6 \text{ A}$$ **At 6600 V (Option B):** $$I_L = \frac{1 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 6600 \times 0.85} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{9714.6} \approx 102.95 \text{ A}$$ ### Step 2: Calculate Copper Cross-Section (Assuming 2 V% Drop) Maximum allowable voltage drop = 2% of 690 V = 13.8 V (Option A) Maximum allowable voltage drop = 2% of 6600 V = 132 V (Option B) **Resistance of cable (per phase):** $$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$ where $\rho = 0.0172 \,\Omega\text{-mm}^2/\text{m}$ for copper, $L = 80$ m, $A$ = cross-section in mm². Voltage drop per phase (3-φ): $V_{drop} = \sqrt{3}\,I L R_{per\,m}$ (resistance per km approach) Using a simpler approach — permissible voltage drop per conductor: **Option A (690 V):** $$R_{max} = \frac{V_{drop,phase}}{I} = \frac{13.8/\sqrt{3}}{984.6} = \frac{7.97}{984.6} \approx 0.00809 \,\Omega$$ $$A = \frac{\rho L}{R} = \frac{0.0172 \times 80}{0.00809} = \frac{1.376}{0.00809} \approx 170.1 \text{ mm}^2$$ Standard size: **185 mm²** copper cable. **Option B (6.6 kV):** $$R_{max} = \frac{132/\sqrt{3}}{102.95} = \frac{76.21}{102.95} \approx 0.7403 \,\Omega$$ $$A = \frac{0.0172 \times 80}{0.7403} = \frac{1.376}{0.7403} \approx 1.86 \text{ mm}^2$$ Standard size: **2.5 mm²** copper cable. ### Step 3: Calculate I²R Losses (3-Phase, Full Load) **Option A (185 mm², R per conductor ≈ 0.00743 Ω for 80 m):** $$P_{loss,A} = 3 I^2 R = 3 \times (984.6)^2 \times 0.00743$$ $$P_{loss,A} = 3 \times 969{,}437 \times 0.00743 \approx 21{,}610 \text{ W} \approx 21.61 \text{ kW}$$ **Option B (2.5 mm², R per conductor ≈ 0.5504 Ω for 80 m):** $$P_{loss,B} = 3 \times (102.95)^2 \times 0.5504$$ $$P_{loss,B} = 3 \times 10{,}599 \times 0.5504 \approx 17{,}500 \text{ W} \approx 17.50 \text{ kW}$$ ### Step 4: Compare Total Copper Weight **Option A:** 3 conductors × 80 m × 185 mm² × 8.89 g/cm³ (Cu density) $$W_A = 3 \times 80 \times 185 \times 10^{-6} \times 8890 \approx 394.7 \text{ kg}$$ **Option B:** 3 conductors × 80 m × 2.5 mm² × 8.89 g/cm³ $$W_B = 3 \times 80 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8890 \approx 5.33 \text{ kg}$$ ### Step 5: Analytical Conclusion $$\frac{W_A}{W_B} = \frac{394.7}{5.33} \approx 74.0$$ $$\frac{P_{loss,A}}{P_{loss,B}} = \frac{21.61}{17.50} \approx 1.23$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** Using a step-up/step-down transformer (Option B) at higher voltage reduces **copper weight by ~74×** while keeping losses comparable. The **transformer cost and weight** (≈150 kg for a 1.25 MVA unit) is far outweighed by the cable savings. **This is the primary merit of AC on board — easy voltage transformation for efficient power distribution.** --- ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: AC vs DC on Board | Parameter | AC System | DC System | Winner | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Voltage transformation** | Easy (transformer) | Difficult (DC-DC converter) | **AC** | | **Machine weight (1 MW)** | ~2,000 kg | ~3,500 kg | **AC** | | **Speed control range** | Limited (with VFD) | Wide, linear | **DC** | | **Maintenance** | Low (no brushes) | High (brushes, commutator) | **AC** | | **Emergency backup** | Needs UPS/Inverter | Direct battery | **DC** | | **Switchgear complexity** | High (arc chutes) | Moderate | **DC** | | **Power capacity** | Up to 100+ MW | Typically < 5 MW | **AC** | | **Hazardous area safety** | Better (enclosed) | Sparking risk | **AC** | | **Harmonics** | Significant problem | None | **DC** | | **Insulation thickness** | Thicker (√2 peak) | Thinner | **DC** | | **Modern IEP (Integrated Electric Propulsion)** | Standard | MVDC link emerging | **AC/MVDC** | | **Battery charging** | Needs rectifier | Direct | **DC** | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked to justify the dominance of AC on modern ships, always cite **four pillars:** > 1. Easy voltage transformation → low I²R loss > 2. Brushless, rugged motors > 3. Compatibility with VFDs for propulsion > 4. Standardization per IEC 60092 / SOLAS --- ## 3.3 Quantitative Proof: Why DC Survives in Specific Shipboard Roles **Battery-fed DC Emergency System Calculation:** Required emergency duration = 3 hours (per SOLAS Chapter II-1) Emergency load = 50 kW (lighting, steering gear motors, radio, navigation) Battery bank voltage = **110 V DC** $$I_{battery} = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{50{,}000}{110} \approx 454.5 \text{ A}$$ Energy required: $$E = P \times t = 50 \times 3 = 150 \text{ kWh}$$ Assuming battery efficiency 0.80, capacity needed: $$C = \frac{150}{0.80} = 187.5 \text{ kWh}$$ This calculation is **inherently DC** — no AC system can be backed up this directly. The **DC emergency system is therefore retained by maritime regulation** even on fully AC ships. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Modern Shipboard AC Power Distribution Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB DG1[Diesel Generator 1
3-Phase 690V 60Hz] --> MSB DG2[Diesel Generator 2
3-Phase 690V 60Hz] --> MSB SG1[ Shaft Generator
3-Phase 690V] --> MSB MSB[Main Switchboard MSB
690V 60Hz] --> EMERG MSB --> ESB MSB --> TR1 MSB --> TR2 TR1[Step-Down Transformer
690V to 440V] --> AC440[440V AC
Distribution Board] TR2[Step-Down Transformer
690V to 220V] --> AC220[220V AC
Accommodation] AC440 --> MOT1[Induction Motor
Bow Thruster] AC440 --> MOT2[Induction Motor
Cargo Winch] AC440 --> VFD[Variable Frequency Drive
Pump Motor] EMERG[Emergency Generator
or Battery via Inverter] --> ESB[Emergency Switchboard
440V AC] MSB --> RECT[AC-DC Rectifier
110V DC] RECT --> DC110[110V DC Emergency Board] DC110 --> BAT[Battery Bank
110V DC] DC110 --> DC24[24V DC
Control and Electronics] MSB --> HV[Step-Up Transformer
690V to 6.6kV] --> HVBD[HV Board 6.6kV] HVBD --> PROP[Electric Propulsion Motor
20MW] ``` ## 4.2 Decision Flow: When to Choose AC vs DC on Board ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Shipboard Load Type?] --> Q1{Power > 100 kW?} Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Variable speed needed?} Q1 -->|No| Q3{Emergency / Battery backed?} Q2 -->|Yes, smooth| AC_DRIVE[AC with VFD
Preferred for modern ships] Q2 -->|No, fixed speed| AC_DIRECT[Direct-on-line AC
Induction Motor] Q3 -->|Yes| DC_EMERG[DC System
110V or 24V] Q3 -->|No| Q4{Electronics / Control?} Q4 -->|Yes| DC_CTRL[DC Supply
24V regulated] Q4 -->|No| AC_LIGHT[AC for lighting
and small loads] AC_DRIVE --> NOTE1[Uses transformer for
voltage step up/down] AC_DIRECT --> NOTE1 DC_EMERG --> NOTE2[Direct battery connection
Simple and reliable] DC_CTRL --> NOTE2 ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Merits-Demerits Wheel ```mermaid flowchart LR AC_CENTER[AC SYSTEM ON BOARD] DC_CENTER[DC SYSTEM ON BOARD] AC_CENTER --> M1[M1: Easy voltage
transformation] AC_CENTER --> M2[M2: Lightweight
motors] AC_CENTER --> M3[M3: No commutator
maintenance] AC_CENTER --> M4[M4: VFD compatibility] AC_CENTER --> D1[D1: Complex
switchgear] AC_CENTER --> D2[D2: Harmonics
from drives] AC_CENTER --> D3[D3: Skin effect
losses] AC_CENTER --> D4[D4: Synchronization
needed for paralleling] DC_CENTER --> M5[M5: Simple speed
control] DC_CENTER --> M6[M6: Battery
compatible] DC_CENTER --> M7[M7: No harmonics
or reactive power] DC_CENTER --> M8[M8: Simple parallel
operation] DC_CENTER --> D5[D5: Heavy machines
with commutator] DC_CENTER --> D6[D6: High maintenance

hazardous areas] ``` ## 4.4 Evolution of Shipboard Power Systems ```mermaid flowchart LR E1[1880s-1920s
