topics.title: Electrical Power Generation on Board Ships - Comparison of Diesel, Thermal and Nuclear Power Plants as Prime Movers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electrical Power Generation on Board Ships - Comparison of Diesel, Thermal and Nuclear Power Plants as Prime Movers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of shipboard electrical engineering, a **Prime Mover** is the primary mechanical energy conversion device (engine or turbine) that drives the electrical generator (alternator) to produce electrical power for the vessel's propulsion auxiliaries, navigation, communication, lighting, and hotel loads. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme for *Electrical Systems in Ships and Shipyards* (PENST524), prime movers on board ships are categorized into three principal families based on their thermodynamic working cycle and energy source:

1. **Internal Combustion (IC) Prime Movers** — Marine Diesel Engines (slow-speed, medium-speed, high-speed)
2. **Thermal Prime Movers** — Steam Turbines (Rankine cycle) and Gas Turbines (Brayton cycle)
3. **Nuclear Prime Movers** — Nuclear Steam Supply System (NSSS) coupled to turbo-alternators

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The learner must be able to **compare and contrast** the three prime mover topologies on the basis of specific fuel consumption, thermal efficiency, weight-to-power ratio, maintenance intervals, starting time, capital cost, and emissions profile. Comparison questions historically carry **7–14 marks** in End Semester Evaluations (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Heart of the Ship" Model

Imagine a ship as a floating human body:

- The **hull** is the skeleton.
- The **propulsion shaft** is the spine, delivering locomotion.
- The **electrical generator** is the heart, pumping electrical "blood" (current) to every organ (motor, light, sensor).
- The **prime mover** is the **lungs-and-stomach system** that feeds the heart — it takes in fuel (food/air) and converts chemical energy into the rotational mechanical energy that the heart needs.

Just as a human can have different "lung" systems (healthy lungs, smoker's lungs, iron lung), a ship can derive its mechanical "breath" from diesel pistons, steam turbines, gas turbines, or even a nuclear reactor. The choice depends on the ship's mission profile — much like choosing a vehicle engine depends on whether you are hauling cargo, racing, or crossing an ocean.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **bold constants** are universally cited in KTU board papers when comparing prime movers. Memorize them verbatim.

- **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** of marine diesel engines: **155–220 g/kWh** (≈ 0.40–0.55 lb/hp·h)
- **Thermal efficiency** of a modern marine diesel: **45–52%**
- **Thermal efficiency** of a marine steam plant: **25–35%**
- **Thermal efficiency** of a marine gas turbine (simple cycle): **28–38%**
- **Thermal efficiency** of naval nuclear plant (Pressurized Water Reactor): **≈ 30–35%** (overall plant)
- **Heat value of marine HFO (Heavy Fuel Oil)**: **≈ 40,200 kJ/kg** (≈ 10,300 kcal/kg)
- **Heat value of marine diesel/gas oil**: **≈ 42,700 kJ/kg** (≈ 10,200 kcal/kg)
- **Carnot (ideal) efficiency formula**: **η_Carnot = 1 − T_cold/T_hot** (absolute temperatures in Kelvin)
- **Power output of alternator**: **P = √3 × V_L × I_L × cos φ × η_gen** (three-phase)
- **Specific weight of diesel generator set**: **15–25 kg/kW**
- **Specific weight of nuclear plant** (shielded): **≈ 100–200 kg/kW** (excluding fuel)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Prime Mover — Operational Logic Decomposition

Every prime mover, regardless of its energy source, executes the same five-step thermodynamic transformation:

1. **Energy Input (Chemical/Nuclear → Thermal)**: Fuel (diesel, HFO, LNG, or U-235) is oxidized or fissioned, releasing stored energy as heat.
2. **Heat-to-Mechanical Conversion (Thermal → Mechanical)**: The heat raises a working fluid (air, combustion gas, or steam) to high pressure and temperature, which then expands against turbine blades or pushes pistons.
3. **Rotational Transmission (Mechanical → Mechanical)**: The reciprocating motion of pistons (or the linear impulse on turbine blades) is converted to rotational shaft motion via crankshafts or reduction gearing.
4. **Electrical Conversion (Mechanical → Electrical)**: The shaft drives an alternator (synchronous generator), where Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction produces AC EMF.
5. **Power Conditioning & Distribution**: The generated AC is stepped up/down by transformers, rectified if needed, and routed through switchboards to the ship's consumers.

> The **Why** behind these five steps: ships require a self-contained, mobile energy conversion chain that operates reliably in a salt-laden, vibration-heavy, confined environment. The prime mover is the *bottleneck component* — its efficiency, reliability, and footprint determine the entire electrical plant's performance.

## 2.2 Type-by-Type Technical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Marine Diesel Engine (Dominant Prime Mover — 95%+ of global merchant fleet)

- **Cycle**: Diesel (modified Brayton for gas, or dual for liquid) — Compression Ignition.
- **Construction**: 2-stroke (slow-speed, directly coupled to propeller) or 4-stroke (medium/high-speed, coupled to generator).
- **Why chosen**: High thermal efficiency, low fuel cost, fast starting, modular maintenance, and the ability to burn cheap residual HFO.
- **Limitation**: Speed is limited; emissions (NOx, SOx, CO2) are subject to IMO MARPOL Annex VI regulations.

### 2.2.2 Marine Steam Turbine (Thermal — Rankine Cycle)

- **Cycle**: Rankine (water → steam → turbine → condenser → pump).
- **Construction**: Water-tube boiler, superheater, multi-stage impulse/reaction turbine, condenser, feedwater pump.
- **Why chosen historically**: High power output (up to 100+ MW per unit), smooth torque, capable of using any fuel including nuclear.
- **Limitation**: Low thermal efficiency (≈ 30%), huge boiler warm-up time (4–8 hours), high crew requirement, large footprint.

### 2.2.3 Marine Gas Turbine (Thermal — Brayton Cycle)

- **Cycle**: Brayton (open or closed).
- **Construction**: Compressor → Combustion chamber → Turbine → Exhaust.
- **Why chosen**: Extremely high power-to-weight ratio, rapid start (< 2 min), ideal for naval combatants and fast ferries.
- **Limitation**: Poor part-load efficiency, high specific fuel consumption, high exhaust temperature.

### 2.2.4 Nuclear Prime Mover (Submarines, Aircraft Carriers, Icebreakers)

- **Cycle**: Rankine (indirect) — nuclear fission heats a primary loop, which generates steam in a secondary loop (PWR) or boils water directly (BWR).
- **Why chosen**: Virtually unlimited range, no air required for combustion, no exhaust emissions, very high energy density of fuel.
- **Limitation**: Massive capital cost, complex shielding, regulatory burden, political sensitivity, low cold-start capability.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE TO AVOID MARKDOWN TABLE BREAKAGE:** The vertical pipe symbol `\vert` is used below instead of `|` to prevent table column misalignment.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Boundary / Typical Value | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Carnot ideal efficiency | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{c}}{T_{h}}$ | $T_{h} \le 1800\ \text{K}$, $T_{c} \ge 288\ \text{K}$ | Theoretical maximum for any heat engine |
| 2 | Diesel cycle (air-standard) efficiency | $\eta_{\text{Diesel}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | $r$ = compression ratio (12–22), $\rho$ = cut-off ratio | Quick comparison vs Carnot |
| 3 | Brayton cycle efficiency | $\eta_{\text{Brayton}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_{p}^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | $r_{p}$ = pressure ratio (5–30) | Gas turbine plant design |
| 4 | Rankine cycle efficiency | $\eta_{\text{Rankine}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{(h_1 - h_2) - (h_3 - h_4)}{h_1 - h_4}$ | Steam: $T_1 = 500\!-\!600^\circ\text{C}$, $P_1 = 60\!-\!250\ \text{bar}$ | Steam plant thermal audit |
| 5 | Specific Fuel Consumption | $\text{SFC} = \dfrac{m_{\text{fuel}}}{P_{\text{out}}}$ | Diesel: 155–220 g/kWh; GT: 280–400 g/kWh; ST: 250–330 g/kWh | Fuel-cost estimation |
| 6 | Generator electrical output | $P = \sqrt{3}\ V_{L} I_{L} \cos\phi \cdot \eta_{g}$ | $\cos\phi = 0.8$ (lag), $\eta_g = 0.95\!-\!0.98$ | Alternator rating selection |
| 7 | Plant overall efficiency | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{thermal}} \times \eta_{\text{mech}} \times \eta_{\text{gen}}$ | Diesel: $\approx 0.45 \times 0.95 \times 0.96 \approx 0.41$ | Whole-plant benchmarking |
| 8 | Heat rate | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta}\ \text{kJ/kWh}$ | $1/\eta$ convention: $3600/0.45 = 8000\ \text{kJ/kWh}$ | Power-station comparison |
| 9 | Power-to-weight ratio | $\text{PWR} = \dfrac{P_{\text{rated}}}{m_{\text{plant}}}$ | Diesel: 40–70 W/kg; GT: 200–600 W/kg; Nuclear: 5–10 W/kg | Warship design driver |
| 10 | Energy density of fuel | $\text{LHV}_{\text{HFO}} \approx 40{,}200\ \text{kJ/kg}$ | U-235: $\approx 8.0 \times 10^{13}\ \text{kJ/kg}$ | Range/endurance estimate |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not academic curiosities — they are the **daily tools** of:

- **Marine Superintendents** who decide between retrofitting a ship with a diesel-gen-set or a gas-turbine-gen-set.
- **Naval Architects** who compute the *K-values* (specific weights) of machinery to position the ship's center of gravity.
- **Power-System Engineers** who size the emergency generator (typically a fast-starting diesel of 100–500 kW) to satisfy SOLAS Chapter II-1 redundancy rules.
- **Cost Analysts** who compute the **Net Present Cost (NPC)** of fuel over a 25-year ship life.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why Diesel Outperforms Steam Thermodynamically (Carnot vs Diesel Cycle)

The thermal efficiency of any heat engine is bounded by the Carnot limit. The marine diesel approaches this limit more closely than the steam turbine because of its higher mean effective temperature of heat addition.

**Step 1 — Statement of Carnot's theorem:**
For an engine operating between a hot reservoir at $T_h$ and a cold reservoir at $T_c$ (both in Kelvin), the maximum possible thermal efficiency is:

$$
\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_c}{T_h}
$$

**Step 2 — Typical marine diesel hot-source temperature:**
During combustion in a large 2-stroke marine diesel, peak gas temperatures reach approximately $T_h \approx 2000\ \text{K}$ (≈ 1727 °C), while the cold source (charge air / cooling water) is at $T_c \approx 350\ \text{K}$ (≈ 77 °C).

**Step 3 — Substituting the values:**

$$
\eta_{\text{Carnot, diesel}} = 1 - \frac{350}{2000} = 1 - 0.175 = 0.825
$$

This represents an absolute upper bound of **82.5%** for any heat engine operating between these two reservoirs.

**Step 4 — Actual marine diesel efficiency:**
Real marine diesel engines achieve approximately **45–52%** thermal efficiency. The gap between 82.5% and 50% is explained by mechanical friction, heat loss to walls, incomplete combustion, and pumping losses.

**Step 5 — Steam turbine comparison:**
A marine steam plant operates with $T_h \approx 800\ \text{K}$ (superheated steam at 60 bar / 500 °C) and $T_c \approx 300\ \text{K}$ (sea-water cooled condenser).

$$
\eta_{\text{Carnot, steam}} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625
$$

**Step 6 — Concluding the comparison:**
The Carnot ceiling for a steam plant (62.5%) is fundamentally **lower** than that of a diesel plant (82.5%) because steam temperatures are limited by metallurgical creep strength of boiler tubes. This is the **thermodynamic root cause** of why diesel prime movers are dominant on ships.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Intuition:** Think of a high jumper (diesel) versus a pole-vaulter (steam). The high jumper has a higher theoretical ceiling (2.45 m vs 6.0 m) but more importantly, the *natural* ceiling of steam is metallurgically capped — the metals simply *melt or creep* above 650 °C in pressurized boiler tubes.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Fuel Consumption from First Principles

Given that 1 kg of marine HFO releases approximately $Q_{\text{HFO}} = 40{,}200\ \text{kJ}$ of heat on complete combustion, derive the minimum possible SFC for any plant of efficiency $\eta$.

**Step 1 — Define SFC:**
SFC is the mass of fuel required per unit of useful electrical work output:

$$
\text{SFC} = \frac{m_{\text{fuel}}}{W_{\text{out}}} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{kWh}} \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Express fuel mass in terms of heat input:**

$$
m_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{Q_{\text{in}}}{Q_{\text{HFO}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Relate heat input to work output via efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{W_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_{\text{in}} = \frac{W_{\text{out}}}{\eta}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into SFC:**

$$
\text{SFC} = \frac{W_{\text{out}} / \eta}{Q_{\text{HFO}} \cdot W_{\text{out}}} = \frac{1}{\eta \cdot Q_{\text{HFO}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert units (kJ → kWh):**
Since $1\ \text{kWh} = 3600\ \text{kJ}$, divide numerator and denominator:

$$
\text{SFC} = \frac{3600}{\eta \cdot Q_{\text{HFO}}} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{kWh}} \right]
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical evaluation for a marine diesel ($\eta = 0.48$):**

$$
\text{SFC}_{\text{diesel}} = \frac{3600}{0.48 \times 40{,}200} = \frac{3600}{19{,}296} \approx 0.1866\ \text{kg/kWh} = 186.6\ \text{g/kWh}
$$

This matches the **180–200 g/kWh** industry-quoted range — the derivation is self-consistent.

**Step 7 — Numerical evaluation for a marine gas turbine ($\eta = 0.35$):**

$$
\text{SFC}_{\text{GT}} = \frac{3600}{0.35 \times 40{,}200} \approx 0.2558\ \text{kg/kWh} = 255.8\ \text{g/kWh}
$$

(Real values are higher at 300+ g/kWh because turbines often burn distillate, not HFO.)

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Performance Parameter | Marine Diesel | Marine Steam Turbine | Marine Gas Turbine | Nuclear (PWR) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cycle | Diesel (CI) | Rankine | Brayton (open) | Rankine (indirect) |
| Thermal efficiency | **45–52 %** | **25–35 %** | **28–38 %** | **30–35 %** |
| Specific Fuel Consumption | **180–220 g/kWh** | **320–400 g/kWh** | **280–400 g/kWh** | Negligible (fuel mass) |
| Power-to-weight ratio (kW/tonne) | 40–70 | 10–20 | 200–600 | 5–10 |
| Starting time | 30 s – 5 min | 4 – 8 hours (boiler warm-up) | 30 s – 2 min | 12 – 24 hours (cold) |
| Capital cost per kW | Low–Medium (USD 400–800) | High (USD 1500–3000) | Medium (USD 700–1500) | Very High (USD 5000+) |
| Maintenance interval | 8,000–30,000 hrs (overhaul) | 20,000–40,000 hrs | 4,000–10,000 hrs | Refueling: 5–25 years |
| Air required for combustion | Yes | Yes (boiler) | Yes | **No** (inertially enclosed) |
| Underwater operation | No | No | No | **Yes** (submarines) |
| Emissions (NOx/SOx/CO2) | Moderate–High | Moderate | High | **None** |
| Crew skill required | Medium | High (boiler watch) | Medium–High | Very High (nuclear) |
| Typical application | Cargo, tankers, naval auxiliaries | LNG carriers, very large crude carriers | Frigates, fast ferries, CODOG warships | Submarines, carriers, icebreakers |
| Fuel storage for 30 days @ 10 MW | 1300–1500 t HFO | 2200–2800 t HFO | 1700–2400 t distillate | ≈ 50–100 kg U-235 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Prime Mover Family Tree (Classification)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Shipboard Prime Movers] --> B[Internal Combustion - Diesel]
    A --> C[Thermal]
    A --> D[Nuclear]

    B --> B1[2-Stroke Slow Speed]
    B --> B2[4-Stroke Medium Speed]
    B --> B3[4-Stroke High Speed]

    C --> C1[Rankine Steam Turbine]
    C --> C2[Brayton Gas Turbine]

    C1 --> C1a[Oil-fired boiler]
    C1 --> C1b[Exhaust-gas boiler - COGAS]
    C1 --> C1c[Nuclear Steam Supply]

    C2 --> C2a[Simple cycle GT]
    C2 --> C2b[Intercooled-Regenerative]
    C2 --> C2c[CODOG - Combined Diesel or Gas]
    C2 --> C2d[COGAG - Combined Gas and Gas]

    D --> D1[Pressurized Water Reactor - PWR]
    D --> D2[Boiling Water Reactor - BWR]
    D --> D3[Gas-cooled Reactor - GCR]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Energy Conversion Chain (Diesel Generator Set)

```mermaid

    FuelRes[(HFO / Diesel Tank)] -->|Feed pump| EngMan[Marine Diesel Engine]

    AltGen -->|3-phase AC| SwBoard[Main Switchboard]

    EngMan -.Heat loss.-> CoolSys[Sea-water Cooling]

```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Nuclear Steam Supply System (NSSS) Block Architecture

```mermaid

    ReactorCore[Reactor Core - U-235 Fission] -->|Heat 1| PriLoop[Primary Coolant Loop - Pressurized Water]
    PriLoop -->|Flow 1| SteamGen[Steam Generator - Heat Exchanger]
    SteamGen -->|Steam 2| SecLoop[Secondary Steam Loop]
    SecLoop -->|High pressure steam| Turbine[Steam Turbine HP-LP Stages]
    Turbine -->|Shaft| AltGen2[Turbo-Alternator]
    AltGen2 -->|AC power| DistNuc[Naval Switchboard]
    SteamGen -.Feedwater.-> SecLoop
    Turbine -.Exhaust steam.-> CondNuc[Condenser]
    CondNuc -.Cooling.-> SeaWater[Sea-water Cooling]

    PriLoop -.Pump.-> ReactorCore
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Decision Logic for Selecting a Prime Mover

```mermaid

    StartNew([New Ship Design]) --> Q1{Endurance required?}
    Q1 -->|Unlimited / Months| Q2{Submerge capability?}
```
