# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Rated Line Current from kVA Specification

> **Problem Statement:** A 1500 kVA, 3-phase, 450 V, 50 Hz, 0.8 p.f. marine generator is specified for an offshore support vessel. Determine the rated line current, the active power output, the reactive power, and the per-phase voltage.

### Step 1 — Identify given data
$$S = 1500 \text{ kVA}, \quad V_L = 450 \text{ V}, \quad \cos\phi = 0.8, \quad \text{phases} = 3$$

### Step 2 — Compute rated line current using $I_L = S/(\sqrt{3}\,V_L)$

$$I_L = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L} = \dfrac{1500 \times 10^{3}}{\sqrt{3} \times 450}$$

$$I_L = \dfrac{1{,}500{,}000}{1.7321 \times 450} = \dfrac{1{,}500{,}000}{779.42}$$

$$\boxed{I_L \approx 1924.5 \text{ A}}$$

### Step 3 — Compute active power
$$P = S \cos\phi = 1500 \times 0.8 = 1200 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 4 — Compute reactive power
$$\sin\phi = \sqrt{1 - \cos^{2}\phi} = \sqrt{1 - 0.64} = \sqrt{0.36} = 0.6$$

$$Q = S \sin\phi = 1500 \times 0.6 = 900 \text{ kVAr}$$

### Step 5 — Compute per-phase voltage (star-connected assumption)
$$V_{ph} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{450}{1.7321} \approx 259.8 \text{ V}$$

### Step 6 — Verification using the power triangle
$$S^2 = P^2 + Q^2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; (1500)^2 \stackrel{?}{=} (1200)^2 + (900)^2$$

$$2{,}250{,}000 \stackrel{?}{=} 1{,}440{,}000 + 810{,}000 = 2{,}250{,}000 \;\;\checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Synchronous Speed, Number of Poles, and Frequency Relationship

> **Problem Statement:** A shipboard shaft generator is driven by the main propulsion engine through a step-up gear box. The shaft speed is 3000 rpm and the alternator must deliver power at 50 Hz. Calculate the number of poles required.

### Step 1 — Apply the synchronous-speed equation
$$N_s = \dfrac{120\,f}{p} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; p = \dfrac{120\,f}{N_s}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the values
$$p = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{3000} = \dfrac{6000}{3000} = 2 \text{ poles}$$

### Step 3 — Conclusion
$$\boxed{p = 2 \text{ poles} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 2\text{-pole alternator at 50 Hz, 3000 rpm}}$$

### Step 4 — Check by re-substitution
$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{2} = 3000 \text{ rpm} \;\;\checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Voltage Regulation from Open-Circuit and Short-Circuit Test Data

> **Problem Statement:** A 750 kVA, 3.3 kV, 3-phase marine alternator gives the following OC and SC test results at rated current:
> - Open-circuit voltage at rated field current = **3450 V**
> - Short-circuit current at the same field current = **rated current**
> Determine the **synchronous impedance** and the **percentage voltage regulation** at full-load 0.8 p.f. lagging.

### Step 1 — Compute rated line current
$$I_{rated} = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L} = \dfrac{750 \times 10^{3}}{\sqrt{3} \times 3300}$$

$$I_{rated} = \dfrac{750{,}000}{5715.6} \approx 131.2 \text{ A}$$

### Step 2 — Compute per-phase rated voltage (assume star-connected)
$$V_{ph} = \dfrac{3300}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1905.3 \text{ V}$$

### Step 3 — Compute per-phase synchronous impedance using the EMF method
$$Z_s = \dfrac{V_{OC,\,ph}}{I_{SC,\,ph}} = \dfrac{3450/\sqrt{3}}{131.2} = \dfrac{1991.8}{131.2} \approx 15.18 \, \Omega$$

### Step 4 — Assume armature resistance is small ($R_a \approx 0.1 Z_s$)
$$R_a = 0.1 \times 15.18 = 1.518 \, \Omega$$

$$X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2} = \sqrt{15.18^2 - 1.518^2} = \sqrt{230.43 - 2.304} \approx \sqrt{228.13}$$

$$X_s \approx 15.10 \, \Omega$$

### Step 5 — Compute generated EMF per phase at full load, 0.8 p.f. lagging

The phasor equation:
$$E_{ph} = \sqrt{(V_{ph}\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V_{ph}\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$$

Substituting $\cos\phi = 0.8$, $\sin\phi = 0.6$:
$$\begin{aligned}
E_{ph} &= \sqrt{(1905.3 \times 0.8 + 131.2 \times 1.518)^2 + (1905.3 \times 0.6 + 131.2 \times 15.10)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(1524.24 + 199.16)^2 + (1143.18 + 1981.12)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(1723.40)^2 + (3124.30)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{2{,}970{,}227 + 9{,}761{,}490} \\
&= \sqrt{12{,}731{,}717} \approx 3568.2 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Compute voltage regulation
$$\%VR = \dfrac{E_{ph} - V_{ph}}{V_{ph}} \times 100 = \dfrac{3568.2 - 1905.3}{1905.3} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%VR \approx 87.3\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a *natural* regulation (without AVR). With modern AVRs holding regulation within **±0.5%**, this number is purely a design metric to size the exciter ceiling voltage.

---

## 3.4 Derivation D — Cooling-Air Quantity for a Specified Loss

> **Problem Statement:** A 2 MW marine alternator has total losses of 60 kW at full load. The cooling air may be allowed to rise by 20 K from inlet to outlet. Determine the required cooling-air volumetric flow rate at sea-level conditions.

### Step 1 — Recall the energy balance on the air stream
$$P_{loss} = \dot{m}_{air}\, c_p\, \Delta T = \rho_{air}\, \dot{Q}_{air}\, c_p\, \Delta T$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $\dot{Q}_{air}$
$$\dot{Q}_{air} = \dfrac{P_{loss}}{\rho_{air}\, c_p\, \Delta T}$$

### Step 3 — Insert standard values
$\rho_{air} = 1.177 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 1005 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$, $\Delta T = 20 \text{ K}$, $P_{loss} = 60{,}000 \text{ W}$

$$\dot{Q}_{air} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{1.177 \times 1005 \times 20}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{air} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{23{,}657.7}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{air} \approx 2.54 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to m³/h for ventilation duct design
$$\dot{Q}_{air} = 2.54 \times 3600 \approx 9{,}137 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Marine Generator Specification Hierarchy

The following Mermaid block diagram maps how each specification block interrelates in a typical **Ship's Main Diesel Generator (MDG)** specification sheet. This functional architecture is a KTU-favoured visual answer for 14-mark descriptive questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Generator Specification Sheet] --> B[Power Block]
    A --> C[Electrical Block]
    A --> D[Mechanical Block]
    A --> E[Thermal Block]
    A --> F[Environmental Block]
    A --> G[Regulatory Block]

    B --> B1[Apparent Power S in kVA]
    B --> B2[Active Power P in kW]
    B --> B3[Power Factor cos phi]
    B --> B4[Rated Voltage V_L]
    B --> B5[Frequency 50 or 60 Hz]

    C --> C1[EMF Equation E = 4.44 f phi T K_w]
    C --> C2[Synchronous Reactance X_s]
    C --> C3[Sub-transient Reactance X d double prime]
    C --> C4[Short Circuit Ratio SCR]
    C --> C5[AVR Type Brushless or Static]

    D --> D1[Synchronous Speed N_s = 120 f by p]
    D --> D2[Number of Poles p]
    D --> D3[Frame Size and Mounting IM B3]
    D --> D4[WR2 Moment of Inertia]

    E --> E1[Insulation Class F or H]
    E --> E2[Cooling Method IC01 or IC71W]
    E --> E3[Temperature Rise Limit 90 K or 115 K]
    E --> E4[Cooling Air Quantity Q air]

    F --> F1[Ingress Protection IP23 or IP44]
    F --> F2[Ambient Temperature 45 C]
    F --> F3[Relative Humidity 95 percent]
    F --> F4[Tilting and Vibration IEC 60068]

    G --> G1[Class Society LR or DNV or ABS]
    G --> G2[Standard IEC 60034]
    G --> G3[Type Approval Certificate]
    G --> G4[ATEX Zone if applicable]
```

## 4.2 Synchronisation & Parallel Operation Block Diagram

When two or more marine generators run in parallel, several specification parameters become *operationally critical* — voltage match, frequency match, phase sequence, and phase angle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DG1[Diesel Generator 1] -->|3-phase 450 V 50 Hz| BUSBAR[Main Switchboard Busbar]
    DG2[Diesel Generator 2] -->|3-phase 450 V 50 Hz| BUSBAR
    DG3[Emergency Generator] -->|3-phase 450 V 50 Hz| ESB[Emergency Switchboard]

    BUSBAR --> MS[Main Switchboard]
    MS --> AVR1[AVR 1]
    MS --> AVR2[AVR 2]
    MS --> SYNC[Syncroscope and Lamps]
    MS --> PMS[Power Management System]

    SYNC --> CHECK{Synchronisation Checks}
    CHECK --> V1[Voltage Match plus or minus 5 percent]
    CHECK --> F1[Frequency Match plus or minus 0.1 Hz]
    CHECK --> PH[Phase Sequence ABC]
    CHECK --> AN[Phase Angle plus or minus 10 degrees]

    AN --> CB[Close Circuit Breaker]
    CB --> PARALLEL[Generators in Parallel with Active and Reactive Load Sharing]
```

## 4.3 Typical Specification Flow — From ELA to Final Spec

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Electric Load Analysis ELA] --> S2[Step 2: Calculate Maximum Demand kW]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Add Growth Margin 10 to 20 percent]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Decide Number of DG Sets N plus 1 redundancy]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Calculate kVA per Set S = kW by cos phi]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Select Voltage Level 450 V 3.3 kV 6.6 kV]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Select Frequency 50 or 60 Hz]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Choose Class Society Rules]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Final Generator Specification Sheet Issued]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU 2024:** A common 14-mark question in Module 1 is *"Explain, with a block diagram, how the rating of a ship's main generator is finalised from the Electric Load Analysis."* The above three diagrams are the standard model answer framework.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**List any six mandatory items that must appear in the specification sheet of a marine main diesel generator.**

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**
1. Apparent power rating in **kVA** (e.g., 1500 kVA)
2. Rated terminal voltage in **V or kV** (e.g., 450 V, 3-phase)
3. Rated **frequency** (50 Hz or 60 Hz)
4. **Power factor** (typically 0.8 lagging)
5. **Speed** in rpm (e.g., 1500 rpm)
6. **Cooling method** (e.g., IC01 / IC71W) and **IP rating** (e.g., IP23)
7. (Bonus) Insulation class (F/H) and class society approval (LR/DNV/ABS)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**What is the significance of the Short-Circuit Ratio (SCR) in a marine alternator specification? Mention the typical range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** SCR is the ratio of the field current required to produce rated open-circuit voltage to the field current required to produce rated short-circuit current.
- **Physical significance (1 mark):** A *high* SCR (≥ 1.0) means the machine is more *stable* in parallel operation, has better voltage regulation, and can supply more *reactive* power. A *low* SCR (≤ 0.6) means physically smaller, cheaper, but less stable.
- **Typical range (1 mark):** Modern marine alternators usually have **SCR = 0.6 to 1.0**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a marine generator specification sheet, listing the power, electrical, mechanical, thermal, environmental, and regulatory blocks.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 3-phase, 1500 kVA, 3.3 kV, 50 Hz, 1500 rpm, 0.8 power-factor (lagging) marine synchronous generator is specified for a new container vessel. Calculate: (i) the rated line current, (ii) the rated active and reactive power, and (iii) the number of poles.

#### Model Solution to A(a):
The functional architecture consists of six interlinked blocks (draw the Mermaid hierarchy from Section 4.1).

1. **Power block (1.5 m):** $S$, $P$, $Q$, $V_L$, $f$, $\cos\phi$.
2. **Electrical block (1.5 m):** EMF equation, $X_s$, $X''_d$, SCR, AVR type.
3. **Mechanical block (1 m):** $N_s$, $p$, frame size, $WR^2$ inertia.
4. **Thermal block (1 m):** insulation class, cooling method, temperature rise.
5. **Environmental block (1 m):** IP rating, ambient conditions, vibration.
6. **Regulatory block (1 m):** IEC 60034, class society approval (LR/DNV/ABS).

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Data:** $S = 1500$ kVA, $V_L = 3.3$ kV, $f = 50$ Hz, $N = 1500$ rpm, $\cos\phi = 0.8$.

**(i) Rated line current (2.5 marks):**
$$I_L = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L} = \dfrac{1500 \times 10^{3}}{\sqrt{3} \times 3300} = \dfrac{1{,}500{,}000}{5715.6}$$
$$\boxed{I_L \approx 262.4 \text{ A}}$$

**(ii) Active and Reactive Power (2.5 marks):**
$$P = S \cos\phi = 1500 \times 0.8 = 1200 \text{ kW}$$
$$\sin\phi = 0.6 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; Q = S \sin\phi = 1500 \times 0.6 = 900 \text{ kVAr}$$

**(iii) Number of poles (2 marks):**
$$p = \dfrac{120\,f}{N} = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{1500} = \dfrac{6000}{1500}$$
$$\boxed{p = 4 \text{ poles}}$$

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the role of **IP rating**, **insulation class**, and **cooling method (IC code)** in a marine generator specification. State the typical values used for main diesel generators on board ships.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 2 MW, 3.3 kV, 50 Hz, 4-pole marine alternator has total losses of 70 kW at full load. The cooling air is allowed a temperature rise of 25 K. Calculate: (i) the cooling-air mass-flow rate, and (ii) the volumetric flow rate at standard sea-level density $\rho_{air} = 1.177$ kg/m³ and $c_p = 1005$ J/(kg·K).

#### Model Solution to B(a):

| Parameter | Role | Typical Marine Value | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IP Rating** (IEC 60034-5) | Defines protection against solid particles and water ingress — critical in humid, salt-laden engine rooms | **IP23** for engine-room DG; **IP44** for open-deck sets | 2.5 |
| **Insulation Class** (IEC 60034-1) | Limits the *hottest-spot* temperature the winding insulation can tolerate continuously | **Class F** (155 °C); with temperature rise limited to **Class B** (90 K) for longer life | 2.5 |
| **Cooling Method** (IEC 60034-6) | Specifies how heat generated by losses is removed from the machine | **IC01** (self-ventilated, air-cooled) for small sets; **IC71W** (water-cooled air heat-exchanger) for ≥ 2 MW alternators | 2.0 |

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Given:** $P_{loss} = 70$ kW $= 70{,}000$ W, $\Delta T = 25$ K, $\rho_{air} = 1.177$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1005$ J/(kg·K).

**(i) Mass-flow rate (3.5 marks):**
$$\dot{m}_{air} = \dfrac{P_{loss}}{c_p \Delta T} = \dfrac{70{,}000}{1005 \times 25} = \dfrac{70{,}000}{25{,}125}$$
$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{air} \approx 2.786 \text{ kg/s}}$$

**(ii) Volumetric flow rate (3.5 marks):**
$$\dot{Q}_{air} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\rho_{air}} = \dfrac{2.786}{1.177}$$
$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{air} \approx 2.367 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 8{,}521 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the √3 in $S = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L$** — this is the single most common mistake. Marks are deducted *even if the final numerical answer looks plausible*.
> 2. **Mixing up line and phase quantities** — when a question gives **line voltage**, do not divide by $\sqrt{3}$ again to "get per-phase voltage" *unless* the connection is star.
> 3. **Confusing kVA and kW** — always state *both* in the answer; kVA is the *nameplate* rating, kW is the *loadable* rating after subtracting $\cos\phi$.
> 4. **Wrong units in cooling calculations** — mass-flow in kg/s, volumetric-flow in m³/s, heat in *watts* (not kW) when $c_p$ is in J/(kg·K).
> 5. **Skipping the Class Society reference** — KTU specifically tests if you know that the *specification* must be Class-Approved. A description that omits LR/DNV/ABS loses 1–2 marks outright.
> 6. **Using "rpm" inside a formula that demands rad/s** — for torque calculations, convert via $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ before applying $T = P/\omega$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **🔁 Quick-Recall Bullet Bank for "Specification of Generators"**

- ✅ A **marine generator specification** is a *Class-Society-Approved* technical document, not just a data sheet.
- ✅ The **three canonical ratings** are: $S$ (kVA) → $P$ (kW) → $I_L$ (A), linked by $S = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L = P/\cos\phi$.
- ✅ **Synchronous speed** $N_s = 120 f / p$ determines *pole count*; 4-pole / 1500 rpm is the marine industry default.
- ✅ **Power factor** for marine alternators is *always* **0.8 lagging** unless explicitly stated otherwise.
- ✅ **Voltage levels** on ships: LV ≤ 1 kV (typically 450 V); MV 3.3 kV / 6.6 kV; HV 11 kV.
- ✅ **Insulation Class F (155 °C) with Class B (90 K) temperature rise** is the marine industry *default* — gives 25 K thermal reserve.
- ✅ **Cooling**: IC01 for small/medium, IC71W for ≥ 2 MW — controlled by IEC 60034-6.
- ✅ **IP rating**: IP23 minimum for engine rooms; IP44 for open decks; IP55 for harsh marine weather decks.
- ✅ **Ambient design reference** for marine: **45 °C, 95% RH, salt-laden atmosphere** — *not* 40 °C as in land practice.
- ✅ **SCR** is a *stability indicator* — high SCR = stable, low SCR = compact but unstable.
- ✅ **$X''_d$ (sub-transient reactance)** = 10–15% typical; *directly* governs peak short-circuit current.
- ✅ **Voltage regulation** with AVR ≤ ±0.5% steady-state, ±15% transient — mandated by IEC 60034-1.
- ✅ **Cooling-air formula** to memorise: $\dot{Q}_{air} = P_{loss} / (\rho_{air} c_p \Delta T)$.
- ✅ **Synchronisation requirements** (4 conditions) — voltage match, frequency match, phase sequence, phase angle.
- ✅ **Active–Reactive load sharing** between paralleled DGs is controlled by the **AVR** (reactive) and **governor** (active).
- ✅ **Class Society rules** (LR, DNV, ABS, BV, IRS) form the *legal* part of the specification — no class approval, no sea service.
- ✅ **IEC 60034 series** is the *technical* backbone of marine generator specifications (parts 1, 5, 6, 7 are most cited).
- ✅ **Frame size** (IM B3 / IM 1001) is the *mechanical* spec — must match the diesel engine's flywheel housing and foot-mounting.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specification of Motors - Speed Based and Torque Based Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Specification of Motors - Speed Based and Torque Based Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **marine electrical engineering** and **shipyard installations**, the **specification of motors** refers to the systematic selection and documentation of an electric motor's technical parameters based on two fundamental operational criteria: (i) the **rotational speed characteristic** (Speed-Based Specification) and (ii) the **mechanical load demand characteristic** (Torque-Based Specification). 

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *PENST524 – Electrical Systems in Ships and Shipyards*, motors onboard vessels and at shipyard installations must be selected not merely by power rating, but by matching their inherent **speed-torque profile** to the **mechanical load curve** of the driven equipment (pumps, compressors, winches, capstans, windlasses, bow-thrusters, propulsion motors, fans, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition (KTU 2024):** Motor specification is the engineering process of matching the **electromechanical characteristics** of an electric machine (synchronous speed, slip, starting torque, pull-out torque, breakdown torque, frame size, duty class, IP protection, insulation class, cooling method) to the **load requirement curve** of the marine auxiliaries, ensuring reliability, efficiency, and conformance to **classification society rules** (IRS, DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are buying a vehicle to transport goods at a shipyard dock:

- A **sports car** (high-speed, low-torque) is excellent for transporting light parcels quickly across a flat yard.
- A **heavy truck** (low-speed, high-torque) is essential for hauling containers up a steep ramp or pulling a ship alongside a jetty.

Both are "vehicles," but their **specifications** differ dramatically. A shipyard engineer faces the same dilemma — you cannot use the same motor to drive a **ceiling fan** (needs a *variable-torque, high-speed* motor) and a **deck winch** (needs a *constant-torque, high-starting-torque* motor). The motor must be **specified to the job**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** A motor's nameplate is its "passport." It declares what kind of load the motor is willing to "carry" — be it a fast load (fan) or a heavy load (winch).

## 1.3 Standard Marine Motor Metrics

The following standardized parameters are universally stamped on a marine motor's **nameplate** and form the basis of KTU exam questions:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Rated Power | P | **kW** (kilowatts) or HP |
| Rated Voltage | V | **V** (Volts) |
| Rated Current | I | **A** (Amperes) |
| Rated Speed | N | **rpm** |
| Supply Frequency | f | **Hz** (typically 50 Hz or 60 Hz) |
| Number of Poles | P | integer (2, 4, 6, 8 …) |
| Power Factor | cos φ | dimensionless (0.0 – 1.0) |
| Efficiency | η | percentage (%) |
| Slip | s | per-unit or % |
| Duty Cycle | S1 – S8 | IEC 60034-1 |
| Insulation Class | F, H, etc. | thermal endurance |
| IP Protection | IP44, IP55, IP56 | IEC 60529 |
| Frame Size | 90S, 132M, 315L | IEC standard |
| Cooling | IC410, IC411, IC81W | IEC 60034-6 |
| Ambient Temperature | 45 °C / 50 °C | marine standard |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Speed-Based Motor Classification

A motor's speed behaviour is governed primarily by its **construction type** and **supply frequency**. In marine practice, motors are classified by speed into three principal categories:

### 2.1.1 High-Speed Motors (N > 3000 rpm)
- **Typical Pole Count:** 2 poles
- **Synchronous Speed:** 3000 rpm (at 50 Hz) / 3600 rpm (at 60 Hz)
- **Application in Ships:**
  - Centrifugal pumps (cooling water, lube oil)
  - Centrifugal compressors (air conditioning, refrigeration)
  - Electric fans and blowers
  - High-speed grinders at shipyards
- **Characteristic:** Light weight, compact frame, suitable for **variable-torque** loads.

### 2.1.2 Medium-Speed Motors (1500 ≤ N ≤ 3000 rpm)
- **Typical Pole Count:** 4 poles
- **Synchronous Speed:** 1500 rpm (50 Hz) / 1800 rpm (60 Hz)
- **Application in Ships:**
  - Cargo oil pumps
  - Boiler feed pumps
  - Alternator prime-movers
  - Auxiliary sea-water pumps
- **Characteristic:** Workhorse of the marine plant; most common in shipboard systems.

### 2.1.3 Low-Speed Motors (N < 1500 rpm)
- **Typical Pole Count:** 6, 8, 10, 12 poles
- **Synchronous Speed:** 1000, 750, 600, 500 rpm respectively
- **Application in Ships:**
  - Steering gear motors (high reliability, slow turning)
  - Anchor windlass motors
  - Direct-drive propulsion in some modern vessels
  - Heavy capstans
- **Characteristic:** Larger diameter, higher torque density, fewer gearbox stages needed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** In a marine plant, **60–70%** of installed motors are **squirrel-cage induction motors** running at 1500 rpm (4-pole, 50 Hz), because this speed strikes the optimum balance between torque, cost, and reliability.

## 2.2 Torque-Based Motor Classification

The **load demand curve** determines the torque class. The four main categories are:

### 2.2.1 Constant-Torque Loads
- **Torque required:** independent of speed.
- **Power:** proportional to speed (P ∝ N).
- **Examples:** Conveyor belts, positive-displacement pumps, screw compressors, deck cranes.
- **Motor Choice:** Squirrel-cage induction motor with **standard starting torque** (typically 150–200% of full load).

### 2.2.2 Variable-Torque (Quadratic) Loads
- **Torque:** T ∝ N²
- **Power:** P ∝ N³
- **Examples:** Centrifugal pumps, fans, propellers (in free-running conditions).
- **Motor Choice:** Standard squirrel-cage motor; energy savings achievable by variable frequency drives (VFDs).

### 2.2.3 Constant-Power Loads
- **Power:** constant, T ∝ 1/N
- **Examples:** Machine tool spindles, traction drives, propeller at constant tip-speed.
- **Motor Choice:** Special wound-rotor or DC motor with **field-weakening** capability.

### 2.2.4 High Starting-Torque Loads
- **Torque:** very high at start.
- **Examples:** Anchor windlasses, mooring winches, capstans, cargo cranes.
- **Motor Choice:** **Slip-ring (wound-rotor) induction motor** OR squirrel-cage with **high-slip (double-cage) construction** OR **direct-on-line (DOL) starting with star-delta** for medium sizes.

## 2.3 Detailed Specification Parameters – What to Specify

When the KTU asks "specify a motor for X application," the answer must include the following **10 mandatory entries**:

1. **Type** – Squirrel-cage / slip-ring / synchronous / DC.
2. **Power rating** – 1.1 × shaft power (with service factor).
3. **Voltage & frequency** – 440 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz (typical marine) or 415 V, 50 Hz.
4. **Speed** – Synchronous / full-load.
5. **Number of poles** – derived from N and f.
6. **Frame size** – IEC 60072 standard.
7. **Enclosure / IP rating** – IP44 (dry), IP55 (weather), IP56 (deck-exposed).
8. **Insulation class** – Class F (with B-rise) or Class H (for high ambient).
9. **Duty cycle** – S1 (continuous) for pumps, S3 (intermittent) for winches.
10. **Cooling method** – IC411 (TEFC) most common, IC81W (TEWC) for large motors.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **exam-ready formulas** that KTU examiners expect candidates to write without prompting.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Application in Marine |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed (rpm) | Find pole count from rated rpm |
| 2 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N}{N_s}$ | Per-unit slip | Determine loading on motor |
| 3 | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Shaft power from torque | Convert motor torque to mechanical kW |
| 4 | $P = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L \, \cos\phi \, \eta$ | Input electrical power | Three-phase motor rating |
| 5 | $T_{start} = k_s \cdot T_{FL}$ | Starting torque factor | Specify winch motor |
| 6 | $T_{max} = k_{max} \cdot T_{FL}$ | Pull-out (breakdown) torque | Stability criterion |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | Efficiency | Energy audit questions |
| 8 | $N_{actual} = N_s \, (1 - s)$ | Rotor speed | Slip-ring vs cage speed |
| 9 | $P_{rotor\,loss} = s \cdot P_{ag}$ | Rotor copper loss | Efficiency calculation |
| 10 | $T \propto s$ (for cage, low slip region) | Torque-slip linear region | Speed control of slip-ring motor |
| 11 | $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2$ | Fan/pump affinity law | Variable-torque load |
| 12 | $G D^2$ | Flywheel effect | Specifying motor for pulsating loads |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU theory papers, **slip is usually expressed in percentage** (multiply per-unit slip by 100). The **typical full-load slip** of a marine squirrel-cage motor is **3–5%**.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility – Why This Matters Onboard

The shipyard classification societies (IRS, DNV, Lloyd's) **forbid** generic industrial motors in certain shipboard applications. A motor that drives the **emergency fire pump** must be rated for **S1 continuous duty** at **45 °C ambient** with **Class F insulation**. A motor that drives a **deck winch** must be rated for **S3 / S4 intermittent duty** with **high starting torque (≥ 250% FLT)** and **IP56 enclosure**. Thus, a wrong specification is not merely inefficient — it is a **classification failure**, which is a regulatory non-conformity in the maritime industry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Synchronous Speed and Pole Count

**Given:** A marine squirrel-cage induction motor runs at **N = 1455 rpm** on a **50 Hz**, **3-phase** supply. Determine the number of poles and the per-unit slip.

**Step 1 — Recall the synchronous-speed formula.**

The synchronous speed of an induction motor (or the speed of a synchronous motor) is derived from the basic relationship between electrical frequency and mechanical rotation:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}$$

where $f$ is the supply frequency in **Hz** and $P$ is the integer number of poles.

> The factor 120 arises from: $N_s$ (in rpm) × $P$/2 = electrical revolutions per minute = 60 × $f$ → $N_s$ = 120$f$/$P$.

**Step 2 — Solve for P (number of poles).**

Rearrange the equation:

$$P = \frac{120 \, f}{N_s}$$

Because $N_s$ must be slightly greater than the actual rotor speed (since the induction motor always slips), we test integer values of $P$ to find the one that yields a synchronous speed above 1455 rpm.

**Step 3 — Test P = 4 (the most common marine case).**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 6000 / 4 = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 4 — Compare and conclude.**

Since $N_s = 1500$ rpm > 1455 rpm, the choice $P = 4$ is consistent. Therefore the motor has **4 poles**.

**Step 5 — Compute the slip.**

$$s = \frac{N_s - N}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1455}{1500} = \frac{45}{1500} = 0.03 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Step 6 — Express in percentage.**

$$s = 0.03 \times 100 = 3 \, \%$$

> **Conclusion:** A 4-pole, 50 Hz, 1455 rpm motor has a full-load slip of **3 %**, which is typical for a marine squirrel-cage induction machine.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Torque and Shaft Power Relationship

**Given:** A bow-thruster motor delivers a **full-load torque of 800 N·m** at **N = 1455 rpm**. Compute the **shaft power**.

**Step 1 — Write the governing equation.**

The mechanical shaft power is the product of angular velocity and torque:

$$P = T \cdot \omega$$

where $\omega$ is the angular velocity in **rad/s** and $T$ is the torque in **N·m**.

**Step 2 — Convert rpm to rad/s.**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute N = 1455 rpm.**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 1455}{60}$$

$$\omega = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 1455}{60}$$

$$\omega = \frac{9144.65}{60}$$

$$\omega = 152.41 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by torque to obtain power in watts.**

$$P = 800 \times 152.41 = 121{,}928 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kilowatts.**

$$P = 121.93 \text{ kW}$$

> **Conclusion:** The bow-thruster motor is a **≈ 122 kW** machine. The marine specification would then over-rate it to the next standard frame size (e.g., 132 kW) for the **service factor** (typically 1.1–1.15).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Starting Current & Starting Torque Ratio (Approximate Method)

**Given:** A slip-ring induction motor with **rotor resistance doubled** by external rheostat is used to start a mooring winch. How does the starting torque change?

**Step 1 — Recall the torque equation of an induction motor.**

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V^2 \cdot \left(\frac{R_2^\prime}{s}\right)}{\left(R_1 + \frac{R_2^\prime}{s}\right)^2 + \left(X_1 + X_2^\prime\right)^2}$$

**Step 2 — At starting, $s \approx 1$.**

$$T_{start} \approx \frac{3 \, V^2 \, R_2^\prime}{\omega_s \, \left[\left(R_1 + R_2^\prime\right)^2 + \left(X_1 + X_2^\prime\right)^2\right]}$$

**Step 3 — If $R_2^\prime$ is small compared with $X_1 + X_2^\prime$ (the usual case), the denominator is dominated by the reactance term, and:**

$$T_{start} \propto \frac{R_2^\prime}{\left(X_1 + X_2^\prime\right)^2}$$

**Step 4 — When external resistance is added such that the total effective rotor resistance becomes $R_2^{\prime \prime} = 2 R_2^\prime$:**

$$\frac{T_{start,2}}{T_{start,1}} \approx \frac{2 R_2^\prime}{R_2^\prime} = 2$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**

> **The starting torque is doubled** by doubling the rotor circuit resistance. This is the principle behind **slip-ring (wound-rotor) motors** for high-torque marine applications such as mooring winches, anchor windlasses, and cargo cranes. The starting current simultaneously drops because the higher impedance limits inrush.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Motor Type vs. Application

| Marine Application | Load Type | Required Torque | Required Speed | Specified Motor Type | Starting Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cooling seawater pump | Variable (quadratic) | Standard | High (2900 rpm) | Squirrel-cage, 2-pole | DOL / Star-Delta |
| Boiler feed pump | Constant | High | Medium (2950 rpm) | Squirrel-cage, 2-pole | Star-Delta / Soft starter |
| Cargo oil pump | Constant | Very High | Medium (1480 rpm) | Squirrel-cage, 4-pole | Auto-transformer |
| Steering gear motor | Constant | High | Low (750 rpm) | Squirrel-cage, 8-pole | Direct-on-line (DOL) |
| Anchor windlass | Constant | Very High | Low (720 rpm) | Slip-ring, 8-pole | Rotor-rheostat |
| Mooring winch | Constant | Very High | Low (960 rpm) | Slip-ring, 6-pole | Rotor-rheostat |
| Deck crane | Constant | High | Variable | Wound-rotor + VFD | Rotor resistance + brake |
| Bow-thruster | Variable | Very High | Low (240 rpm) | Electric (synchronous or induction, geared) | VFD / Soft starter |
| HVAC fan | Variable (quadratic) | Standard | Medium (1450 rpm) | Squirrel-cage, 4-pole | Direct-on-line |
| Air compressor | Constant | High | Medium (2950 rpm) | Squirrel-cage, 2-pole | Star-Delta |
| Lub-oil purifier | Constant | Standard | High (2900 rpm) | Squirrel-cage, 2-pole | Direct-on-line |
| Incinerator blower | Variable | Standard | High (2900 rpm) | Squirrel-cage, 2-pole | Direct-on-line |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern Recognition for KTU:** If the question describes *centrifugal, fan, propeller* → **variable-torque**. If it describes *winch, windlass, crane, capstan* → **constant-torque, high starting torque**. If it describes *pump (positive displacement), conveyor, compressor (reciprocating)* → **constant-torque**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram – Marine Motor Specification Decision Flow

The following Mermaid block describes the **decision architecture** that a marine electrical engineer follows when specifying a motor for a shipboard auxiliary. The flow starts from the **load type identification** and ends with the **final nameplate data**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Load Requirement] --> B{Identify Load Type}
    B -- "Variable Torque" --> C["Centrifugal Pump / Fan"]
    B -- "Constant Torque" --> D["Winch / Windlass / Capstan"]
    B -- "Constant Power" --> E["Spindle / Traction"]

    C --> C1{Speed Constant or Variable?}
    C1 -- "Constant" --> C2["Squirrel-Cage 2-pole / 4-pole"]
    C1 -- "Variable" --> C3["Squirrel-Cage + VFD"]

    D --> D1{Starting Torque Needed?}
    D1 -- "Moderate" --> D2["Squirrel-Cage Double-Cage"]
    D1 -- "Very High" --> D3["Slip-Ring Wound-Rotor"]

    E --> E1["DC Motor or Wound-Rotor + Field Weakening"]

    C2 --> F[Determine Power Rating]
    C3 --> F
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    E1 --> F

    F --> G{Application Location?}
    G -- "Engine Room" --> H1["IP44, IC411, S1"]
    G -- "Deck" --> H2["IP56, IC411, S3"]
    G -- "Hazardous Zone" --> H3["Ex-Proof, IP55, S1"]

    H1 --> I[Final Nameplate Data]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I

    I --> J["Voltage 440 V 3-Phase 50 Hz"]
    I --> K["Insulation Class F Temp Rise B"]
    I --> L["Frame Size 132M etc."]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram – Speed-Based Motor Family Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Marine Electric Motors] --> B[DC Motors]
    A --> C[AC Motors]

    B --> B1[Shunt]
    B --> B2[Series]
    B --> B3[Compound]

    C --> C1[Synchronous]
    C --> C2[Asynchronous Induction]

    C2 --> C2a[Squirrel-Cage]
    C2 --> C2b[Slip-Ring Wound-Rotor]

    C2a --> C2a1["2-pole 3000 rpm High-Speed"]
    C2a --> C2a2["4-pole 1500 rpm Medium-Speed"]
    C2a --> C2a3["6-pole 1000 rpm Low-Speed"]
    C2a --> C2a4["8-pole 750 rpm Low-Speed"]

    C2b --> C2b1["High Starting Torque Winch Duty"]
    C2b --> C2b2["Smooth Start Crane Duty"]

    C1 --> C1a["Power Factor Correction"]
    C1 --> C1b["Constant Speed Propulsion"]
```

## 4.3 Torque-Speed Characteristic Sketch – Qualitative

Although the **exact curve** is unique to each motor, the *qualitative* shape of the curve is what students must draw in KTU descriptive answers. Use the following Mermaid block as a textual-substitute for the speed-torque curve.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SC1[Speed Torque Curve of Squirrel Cage Motor]
        direction LR
        SP[Starting Point s=1] -- "T rises" --> PK[Pull Up Torque]
        PK -- "T dips" --> MX[Pull Out Torque at s=sp"]
        MX -- "T drops" --> FS[Full Load Point s=3-5 percent]
        FS -- "T to zero" --> SY[Synchronous s=0]
    end
```

**Key points students must label on a hand-drawn curve:**

| Point | Slip | Torque | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Synchronous | 0 | 0 | Rotor turns at stator field speed |
| Full-load | 3–5 % | 100 % | Rated operating point |
| Pull-out / Breakdown | 10–20 % | 200–300 % | Maximum torque motor can deliver |
| Pull-up | ~ 80 % | ~ 110–150 % | Minimum torque during acceleration |
| Starting | 1.0 (100 %) | 150–250 % | Initial locked-rotor torque |

## 4.4 Block Diagram – Motor Specification on a Nameplate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Motor Nameplate] --> B[Rated Power kW]
    A --> C[Rated Voltage V]
    A --> D[Rated Current A]
    A --> E[Rated Speed rpm]
    A --> F[Frequency Hz]
    A --> G[Number of Poles P]
    A --> H[Insulation Class F or H]
    A --> I[IP Protection IP44 to IP56]
    A --> J[Duty Cycle S1 to S8]
    A --> K[Cooling IC Code]
    A --> L[Frame Size IEC]
    A --> M[Power Factor cos phi]
    A --> N[Efficiency percent]
    A --> O[Service Factor SF]
    A --> P[Ambient Temperature deg C]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT confuse** "synchronous speed" with "rated speed." Always remember $N = N_s (1 - s)$. In exam scripts, students often forget the slip and report $P$ as 6 when the motor is 4-pole.
> - **Do NOT specify a motor by power alone.** Marks are awarded only when you state **type, poles, speed, IP, duty, insulation, cooling, starting method**.
> - **Do NOT mix up load types.** A *centrifugal pump* is *variable-torque*; a *positive-displacement pump* is *constant-torque*.
> - **Do NOT forget units.** $N_s$ must be in **rpm**, $f$ in **Hz**, $T$ in **N·m**, $P$ in **W or kW**.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between constant-torque and variable-torque loads. Give two marine examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Constant-Torque Load | Variable-Torque Load |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Torque required is **independent of speed** | Torque required varies with **square of speed** (T ∝ N²) |
| Power variation | P ∝ N (linear) | P ∝ N³ (cubic) |
| Marine examples | (i) Mooring winch, (ii) Anchor windlass, (iii) Cargo crane | (i) Centrifugal cooling pump, (ii) HVAC fan, (iii) Propeller (free running) |
| Motor choice | Squirrel-cage double-cage OR slip-ring | Standard squirrel-cage; VFD recommended for energy saving |

*(1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for examples each = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 6-pole induction motor has a full-load slip of 4 %. Calculate (i) synchronous speed and (ii) actual rotor speed.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

(i) Synchronous speed:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}$$

(ii) Actual rotor speed:

$$N = N_s \, (1 - s) = 1000 \times (1 - 0.04) = 1000 \times 0.96 = 960 \text{ rpm}$$

**Result:** $N_s = 1000$ rpm, $N = 960$ rpm. *(1.5 marks for each step)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) – `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a **torque-slip characteristic**, explain the operating regions of a 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Specify a suitable motor for the **mooring winch** of a 5000 DWT cargo vessel. Justify each parameter of your specification. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution – Part (a)

The torque-slip curve of a squirrel-cage induction motor is divided into **four operating regions** (from s = 1 to s = 0):

1. **Starting region (s ≈ 1, N = 0):** The rotor is at rest. Starting torque $T_{st}$ is typically **150–250 %** of full-load torque. The starting current is **5–7 times** full-load current.

2. **Acceleration region (s between 0.8 and $s_{p\prime}$):** As the motor accelerates, the torque first dips to a minimum value (the **pull-up torque**) and then rises. Care must be taken that the motor's torque always remains **above** the load torque, otherwise the motor will stall.

3. **Pull-out / Breakdown region (s = $s_{p\prime}$ ≈ 0.1–0.2):** The torque reaches a **maximum value $T_{max}$** of typically 200–300 % of full-load torque. This is the most critical point during acceleration. The motor can deliver no more torque than this; if the load demands more, the motor will stall.

4. **Normal operation region (s = 0.02–0.05):** This is the **stable linear region** where the motor runs at full load. The relationship $T \propto s$ holds approximately. The slip at full load is called the **full-load slip** $s_{FL}$.

5. **Pull-down / Synchronous region (s → 0):** As the rotor approaches synchronous speed, the induced rotor EMF and the torque both fall to zero. The motor cannot run above synchronous speed as an induction motor.

> The **stable region** of operation is the **linear portion** from s = 0 to s = $s_{p\prime}$. The **unstable region** is between $s_{p\prime}$ and s = 1.

#### Model Solution – Part (b)

A complete specification for a mooring winch motor would read:

| # | Parameter | Specification | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Type | 3-phase **slip-ring (wound-rotor) induction motor** | Winch requires very high starting torque; slip-ring allows rotor-rheostat starting |
| 2 | Power | 55 kW (with SF 1.15) | Calculated from peak line pull × drum radius × RPM |
| 3 | Voltage | 440 V, 3-phase | Standard marine auxiliary voltage |
| 4 | Frequency | 50 Hz | Indian / European standard (or 60 Hz if vessel is US-built) |
| 5 | Number of poles | 6 poles | $N_s = 1000$ rpm, suitable low speed for gear reduction |
| 6 | Rated speed | 950 rpm | After slip (~5 %), within 6-pole range |
| 7 | Frame size | IEC 280M | Standard frame for ~55 kW, 6-pole |
| 8 | Enclosure | **IP56** (totally enclosed) | Winch is on **open deck**, exposed to sea spray, rain |
| 9 | Insulation | **Class F** with Class B temperature rise | Allows 30 K margin for hot tropical waters |
| 10 | Duty | **S3 – 40 %** (intermittent periodic duty) | Mooring winch operates in short bursts, not continuously |
| 11 | Cooling | **IC411** (TEFC, fan-cooled) | Adequate for open deck; no seawater cooling needed |
| 12 | Starting | **Rotor-rheostat (liquid or metallic)** | Limits starting current to 1.5 × FLC and provides 250 % starting torque |
| 13 | Ambient | 45 °C | Standard marine ambient (45 °C engine room, 50 °C tropical) |
| 14 | Brake | **Electromagnetic spring-set, fail-safe holding brake** | Safety requirement for suspended load |

*(7 marks: 1 mark for type + starting method, 1 mark for poles/speed, 1 mark for IP/duty, 1 mark for insulation, 1 mark for cooling, 1 mark for brake/safety, 1 mark for service factor.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has a rotor impedance at standstill of (0.6 + j4) Ω per phase. The ratio of rotor-to-stator turns is 0.8. Compute (i) the slip for maximum torque, (ii) the maximum torque, and (iii) the slip for maximum starting torque. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the procedure for **selecting and specifying an electric motor** for a marine **sea-water cooling pump**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution – Part (a)

**Given:**
- $P = 4$ poles, $f = 50$ Hz
- Rotor impedance at standstill: $R_2 = 0.6 \, \Omega$, $X_2 = 4 \, \Omega$
- Turns ratio $k = N_2/N_1 = 0.8$

**Step 1 — Synchronous speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2 — Synchronous angular speed:**

$$\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Slip for maximum torque:**

The general formula for slip at maximum torque is:

$$s_{p\prime} = \frac{R_2^\prime}{X_2^\prime}$$

The rotor impedance is **already referred to the stator side** (assumed standard practice), so:

$$s_{p\prime} = \frac{0.6}{4} = 0.15$$

**Step 4 — Maximum torque (general formula):**

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2 \, \omega_s} \cdot \frac{V^2}{R_2^\prime / s_{p\prime} + s_{p\prime} \cdot R_1 + 2 X}$$

Since $R_1$ is not given, use the **approximate** expression that neglects $R_1$:

$$T_{max} \approx \frac{3 \, V_1^2}{2 \, \omega_s \, X_2^\prime}$$

**Substituting the values (taking a typical stator phase voltage $V_1$ = 254 V for 440 V line):**

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 \times 254^2}{2 \times 157.08 \times 4} = \frac{193548}{1256.6} \approx 154 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5 — Slip for maximum starting torque (when external resistance is added):**

Maximum starting torque is obtained when $R_2^{\prime \prime} = X_2^\prime = 4 \, \Omega$, i.e., the rotor resistance is increased to equal the rotor reactance.

Therefore, the additional resistance required:

$$R_{ext}^\prime = X_2^\prime - R_2^\prime = 4 - 0.6 = 3.4 \, \Omega \text{ (per phase, referred to stator)}$$

Referred to rotor side:

$$R_{ext} = R_{ext}^\prime \times k^2 = 3.4 \times 0.8^2 = 3.4 \times 0.64 = 2.176 \, \Omega$$

At this new total rotor resistance, the **slip for maximum torque** becomes 1.0 (i.e., starting condition), giving **maximum starting torque = maximum running torque**.

**Results:**
- Slip for maximum torque = 0.15
- Maximum torque ≈ 154 N·m
- Slip for maximum starting torque = 1.0 (i.e., at start)
- Additional rotor resistance = 2.176 Ω per phase (rotor-side)

#### Model Solution – Part (b)

The selection and specification procedure for a **sea-water cooling pump motor** proceeds in **8 logical steps**:

1. **Identify the load** — Sea-water cooling pump is a *centrifugal pump*, hence a **variable-torque** (quadratic) load.

2. **Determine shaft power required** — Read from pump nameplate or vendor data sheet. Example: $P_{shaft} = 30$ kW.

3. **Add service factor** — Multiply by 1.10 to 1.15 to obtain the **motor rated power**:
   $P_{motor} = 30 \times 1.15 = 34.5$ kW → choose next standard size: **37 kW**.

4. **Choose motor type** — **Squirrel-cage induction motor**, the simplest and most reliable for centrifugal pumps (which don't need high starting torque).

5. **Determine number of poles / speed** — Most centrifugal pumps run at 2900–2950 rpm. Hence choose a **2-pole** motor:
   $N_s = 120 \times 50 / 2 = 3000$ rpm, $N \approx 2900$ rpm at 3–4 % slip.

6. **Select enclosure** — Sea-water pump is in the **engine room**, hence **IP44** is sufficient. If located in a sea chest, IP55.

7. **Select duty and insulation** — Pump runs **continuously** → **S1 duty**. **Class F insulation with Class B rise** is standard.

8. **Select cooling** — **IC411 (TEFC)** is the industry default; no need for water-cooling in this power range.

9. **Starting method** — For 37 kW, **Star-Delta starter** is standard; DOL possible if the ship's auxiliary switchboard permits the inrush (about 200 A starting current).

10. **Final specification:**
    - 37 kW, 3-phase, 440 V, 50 Hz
    - 2-pole, 2900 rpm
    - Squirrel-cage, IC411, IP44
    - S1 duty, Class F insulation
    - Star-Delta starter
    - 45 °C ambient
    - Frame size 200L (IEC standard)

*(7 marks: 1 mark for identifying load type, 2 marks for power sizing, 1 mark for pole/speed selection, 1 mark for IP/duty, 1 mark for starting method, 1 mark for completeness of specification.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Revision)**

- **Motor specification = nameplate data + load matching.** It is NOT just kW.
- **Synchronous speed formula:** $N_s = 120f/P$ — memorize this verbatim.
- **Slip formula:** $s = (N_s - N)/N_s$ — full-load slip is typically 3–5 %.
- **Power equation:** $P = 2\pi NT/60$ — convert N in rpm to ω in rad/s.
- **Squirrel-cage motor:** simplest, most robust, low starting torque, high starting current. Use for fans, centrifugal pumps, compressors.
- **Slip-ring motor:** high starting torque, controllable starting current, used for winches, windlasses, capstans, cranes.
- **Synchronous motor:** constant speed, used for alternators and large power-factor-correction drives.
- **2-pole = 3000 rpm, 4-pole = 1500 rpm, 6-pole = 1000 rpm, 8-pole = 750 rpm** (at 50 Hz). Add 20 % for 60 Hz.
- **Speed-Based Classification:**
  - High speed → > 3000 rpm (2-pole)
  - Medium speed → 1500–3000 rpm (4-pole)
  - Low speed → < 1500 rpm (6, 8, 10, 12-pole)
- **Torque-Based Classification:**
  - Constant torque → winches, conveyors, reciprocating compressors
  - Variable (quadratic) torque → centrifugal pumps, fans
  - Constant power → traction, spindles
  - High starting torque → windlasses, capstans, cranes
- **Marine Standard Parameters:**
  - Voltage: 440 V AC, 24 V DC, 110 V DC (legacy)
  - Frequency: 50 Hz (most), 60 Hz (US vessels)
  - IP44 (engine room), IP55 (weather-exposed), IP56 (open deck)
  - Class F insulation, S1/S3 duty, IC411 cooling
- **Duty cycles:** S1 (continuous), S2 (short-time), S3 (intermittent periodic), S4 (intermittent with starting), S5, S6, S7, S8.
- **IP code:** First digit = solid-particle protection (0–6), Second digit = water protection (0–9).
- **Insulation classes:** B (130 °C), F (155 °C), H (180 °C). Marine standard = **Class F with B-rise**.
- **Cooling codes:** IC410 (totally enclosed non-ventilated), IC411 (TEFC, fan-cooled), IC81W (TEWC, water-cooled).
- **Starting methods in order of inrush:** DOL (6–7×) > Star-Delta (2–3×) > Soft-Starter (1.5–3×) > VFD (1×).
- **Shipboard applications and their typical motor speeds:**
  - Sea-water pump: 2900 rpm (2-pole)
  - Lube-oil pump: 1450 rpm (4-pole)
  - HVAC fan: 1450 rpm (4-pole)
  - Steering gear: 720 rpm (8-pole)
  - Windlass: 720 rpm (8-pole)
  - Mooring winch: 950 rpm (6-pole)
- **Always specify:** Type + kW + V + f + poles + IP + duty + insulation + cooling + starting method + service factor.
- **Why slip-ring for winches?** Because the load is **constant-torque** and demands **>250 % starting torque**; slip-ring allows rotor resistance to be progressively cut out, providing high starting torque with low starting current.
- **Classification societies** (IRS, DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) require **two independent motors** for essential services like steering gear and fire pumps.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU theory papers, whenever a question uses the word "specify" or "select," remember to answer in **tabular form** with **justification column**. This is the examiner's preferred format and earns full marks. Always end with a **safety / regulatory note** to capture the last mark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Concept of Alternators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Concept of Alternators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Alternator** (also known as a **Synchronous Generator** or **AC Generator**) is a rotating machine that converts **mechanical energy** into **electrical energy** in the form of **alternating current (AC)**. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, an alternator operates on the principle of **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, where a relative motion between a magnetic field and a conductor induces an electromotive force (EMF).

In the marine context, alternators are the **prime movers of shipboard electrical power**, driven by the ship's main propulsion diesel engines, auxiliary diesel generators, or steam/gas turbines. They form the **heart of the ship's electrical power generation system**, typically producing three-phase AC at standard marine frequencies of **50 Hz** (Indian/European standard) or **60 Hz** (American standard) with standard voltages of **440 V, 3.3 kV, 6.6 kV, or 11 kV**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 2 Focus**
> The module emphasizes the **general operating concept**, **constructional features**, **EMF generation principle**, and **basic EMF equation** of alternators as used in shipboard power plants. Mastery of the EMF equation is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pedal-Powered Bicycle Light" Intuition

Imagine a bicycle dynamo (a miniature alternator):
- As you **pedal** (mechanical input), a **magnet rotates** inside a coil of wire.
- The changing magnetic flux through the coil **pushes electrons** back and forth through the wire.
- The faster you pedal, the **brighter** the bulb (higher voltage and frequency).

**The same physics operates in a shipboard alternator, but scaled up to megawatts of power:**

| Bicycle Dynamo Analogy | Shipboard Alternator Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Your legs pedaling | Diesel engine / Steam turbine shaft |
| Rotating magnet inside the dynamo | Rotor with field winding |
| Stationary coil of wire | Stator with armature winding |
| Bicycle wheel speed (pedaling rate) | Synchronous speed (Ns) |
| Bulb brightness | Generated terminal voltage |

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Marine Metrics

- **Standard marine frequency (India/EU):** **50 Hz**
- **Standard marine frequency (US/NATO vessels):** **60 Hz**
- **Form Factor (sinusoidal wave):** **1.11** (ratio of RMS to average value)
- **Standard ship service voltage:** **440 V AC, 3-phase, 3-wire**
- **Standard emergency voltage:** **24 V DC / 220 V AC**
- **Synchronous speed formula:** **Ns = 120f / P** (where P = number of poles)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** In a DC generator, the commutator mechanically rectifies the AC induced in the armature. In an alternator, the **induced EMF itself is AC**, and the output terminals deliver AC directly — no mechanical rectification is required.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

The operation of an alternator rests on **three foundational pillars** that every KTU examiner expects a student to articulate:

1. **Magnetic Flux Production:** The **rotor (field winding)** is energized by a **DC excitation source** (called the **exciter** or **AVR — Automatic Voltage Regulator**), producing a steady magnetic flux.
2. **Relative Motion:** The prime mover (diesel engine/turbine) rotates the rotor at a **constant synchronous speed (Ns)** inside the stationary stator.
3. **Faraday's Induction:** As the rotor flux cuts the **stationary stator conductors**, an alternating EMF is induced in them, per the equation:
   $$e = -\frac{d\psi}{dt} = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt}$$

### 2.2 Constructional Anatomy — Two Main Sub-Assemblies

#### A. The Stator (Armature) — Stationary Part

| Component | Function | Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Stator Core** | Houses the armature winding; made of laminated silicon steel to reduce **eddy current losses** | Silicon steel laminations (0.35–0.5 mm thick) |
| **Stator Slots** | Hold the three-phase armature windings | Distributed along inner periphery |
| **Armature Winding** | Where EMF is induced; arranged in **3 phases (R, Y, B)** displaced by **120°** electrically | Copper conductors (insulated) |
| **Stator Frame** | Mechanical support and protection | Cast iron / fabricated steel |

#### B. The Rotor (Field) — Rotating Part

The rotor is of **two types** — a critical classification for KTU questions:

**Type 1: Salient Pole Rotor (Projecting Poles)**
- Poles physically project out from the rotor surface.
- Used for **low-speed applications (100–1500 RPM)** like **hydroelectric generators and diesel-driven ship alternators**.
- Has **large diameter and short axial length**.
- **Pole shoes** provide uniform flux distribution and support the **damper (amortisseur) windings** for suppressing hunting.

**Type 2: Non-Salient (Cylindrical) Rotor**
- Rotor is a **smooth, solid steel cylinder** with slots milled on the surface to hold field winding.
- Used for **high-speed applications (1500–3000 RPM)** like **steam turbine and gas turbine-driven alternators** (common in naval warships).
- Has **small diameter and long axial length** for mechanical strength.
- Mechanically robust, lower windage loss, suited for **turbo-alternators**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marine Context:** Ship service alternators are typically **salient-pole, self-excited, brushless types** coupled directly to the auxiliary diesel engine. They operate at 1500 RPM (for 50 Hz, 4-pole) or 1800 RPM (for 60 Hz, 4-pole).

### 2.3 The Synchronous Speed Relationship — Why "Synchronous"?

The rotor must rotate at **exactly synchronous speed** for the generated frequency to match the system frequency. This is given by:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}$$

Where:
- $N_s$ = Synchronous speed in **RPM**
- $f$ = System frequency in **Hz**
- $P$ = Total number of **magnetic poles** on the rotor

**Worked Numerical Examples (KTU-favored):**

| Frequency (f) | Poles (P) | Synchronous Speed (Ns) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 50 Hz | 2 | 3000 RPM | Steam turbine ship |
| 50 Hz | 4 | 1500 RPM | Diesel ship alternator |
| 50 Hz | 6 | 1000 RPM | Large marine generator |
| 60 Hz | 4 | 1800 RPM | US Navy vessel |
| 60 Hz | 2 | 3600 RPM | High-speed turbo-alternator |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | RPM |
| 2 | $f = \dfrac{P \, N_s}{120}$ | Frequency in terms of speed & poles | Hz |
| 3 | $\phi = B_a \times A_i$ | Flux per pole (Flux density × Iron area) | Weber (Wb) |
| 4 | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, K_w \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph}$ | RMS EMF per phase | Volts (V) |
| 5 | $E_{line} = \sqrt{3} \times E_{ph}$ | Line EMF (for star-connected alternator) | Volts (V) |
| 6 | $K_w = K_p \times K_d$ | Winding factor (pitch × distribution) | dimensionless (0.85–0.96) |
| 7 | $K_p = \cos\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)$ | Pitch factor (chording factor) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $K_d = \dfrac{\sin(m\beta/2)}{m \, \sin(\beta/2)}$ | Distribution factor | dimensionless |
| 9 | $T_{ph} = \dfrac{Z_{ph}}{2}$ | Turns per phase (Z = total conductors) | turns |
| 10 | $P_{out} = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L \, \cos\phi$ | Three-phase output power | Watts (W) |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board problems, the **winding factor (Kw)** is often taken as **0.96 to 1.0** for full-pitch, fully-distributed windings — always check the problem statement for the exact value given.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the EMF Equation of an Alternator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most important derivation in Module 2. A 7-mark Part B question in the KTU ESE is typically dedicated to this derivation.**

#### Step 1: Consider a Single Conductor Cutting Rotor Flux

Let:
- $\phi$ = Useful flux per pole (in Webers, Wb)
- $P$ = Total number of poles
- $N_s$ = Synchronous speed in RPM
- $N_s / 60$ = Synchronous speed in RPS (revolutions per second)

**Flux cut by one conductor in one revolution:**

$$\text{Flux cut per revolution} = \phi \times P \text{ [Webers]}$$

**Reasoning:** In one complete revolution, the conductor passes under **P poles** (P/2 North and P/2 South). Each pole contributes $\phi$ webers of flux, so the total flux cut per revolution is $\phi P$.

**Time taken for one revolution (T):**

$$T = \frac{60}{N_s} \text{ [seconds]}$$

#### Step 2: Average EMF Induced in One Conductor

By **Faraday's First Law**, the average EMF = rate of change of flux linkage:

$$E_{avg,\, per\, conductor} = \frac{\phi \, P}{T} = \frac{\phi \, P \, N_s}{60} \text{ [Volts]}$$

#### Step 3: Convert Average EMF to RMS EMF

For a **sinusoidal flux distribution**, the waveform of the induced EMF is sinusoidal. The relationship between RMS and average for a sine wave is given by the **Form Factor (FF)**:

$$K_f = \frac{E_{rms}}{E_{avg}} = 1.11 \text{ (for pure sine wave)}$$

Therefore, the **RMS EMF per conductor** is:

$$E_{rms,\, per\, conductor} = 1.11 \times \frac{\phi \, P \, N_s}{60}$$

#### Step 4: Express in Terms of Frequency (f)

We know that $f = \dfrac{P \, N_s}{120}$, hence $\dfrac{P \, N_s}{60} = 2f$.

Substituting this into the expression:

$$E_{rms,\, per\, conductor} = 1.11 \times 2f \times \phi = 2.22 \, f \, \phi$$

#### Step 5: Account for Number of Conductors Per Phase

In a **three-phase alternator** with **Z total armature conductors**:
- Conductors per phase = $Z / 3$
- Turns per phase = $T_{ph} = Z / 6$ (since each turn = 2 conductors)

**If all turns are concentrated in one slot per pole per phase (no distribution):**

$$E_{ph} = 2.22 \times f \times \phi \times T_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph}$$

#### Step 6: Introduce the Winding Factor (Kw)

In **practical alternators**, the windings are:
- **Distributed** (spread across multiple slots) → introduces $K_d < 1$
- **Short-pitched / Chorded** (coil span < pole pitch) → introduces $K_p < 1$

The product is the **Winding Factor**: $K_w = K_p \times K_d$

#### Step 7: Final EMF Equation (Per Phase)

$$\boxed{E_{ph} = 4.44 \, K_w \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \text{ [Volts per phase]}}$$

#### Step 8: Line Voltage (For Star-Connected Alternator)

$$E_{line} = \sqrt{3} \times E_{ph} = \sqrt{3} \times 4.44 \, K_w \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph}$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (KTU ESE Standard — 7 Marks)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 16-pole, 50 Hz, star-connected salient-pole alternator has 144 slots with 5 conductors per slot. The coil span is 144 electrical degrees and the flux per pole is 0.05 Wb. The windings are connected in two parallel paths. Calculate the induced EMF per phase and the line voltage. Take $K_w = 0.96$.

#### Solution (Each Step Shown):

**Step 1: Synchronous Speed**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{16} = 375 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 2: Total Number of Conductors (Z)**

$$Z = \text{Number of slots} \times \text{Conductors per slot} = 144 \times 5 = 720$$

**Step 3: Conductors per Phase**

$$Z_{ph} = \frac{Z}{3} = \frac{720}{3} = 240$$

**Step 4: Turns per Phase** (with 2 parallel paths $a = 2$)

$$T_{ph} = \frac{Z_{ph}}{2 \times a} = \frac{240}{2 \times 2} = 60 \text{ turns/phase}$$

**Step 5: Apply EMF Equation**

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \, K_w \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph}$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 0.96 \times 50 \times 0.05 \times 60$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 0.96 \times 150$$

$$E_{ph} = 639.36 \text{ V} \approx 639 \text{ V per phase}$$

**Step 6: Line Voltage (Star Connection)**

$$E_{line} = \sqrt{3} \times E_{ph} = 1.732 \times 639.36$$

$$E_{line} \approx 1107.2 \text{ V}$$

**Final Answer:** $E_{ph} \approx 639 \text{ V/phase}$, $E_{line} \approx 1107 \text{ V}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Always carry **3 significant figures** in intermediate steps. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for premature rounding.

---

### 3.3 Pitch Factor and Distribution Factor — Auxiliary Derivations

**Pitch Factor (Kp):** For a coil that is short-pitched by an angle $\alpha$ electrical degrees:

$$K_p = \cos\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)$$

**Distribution Factor (Kd):** For $m$ slots per pole per phase with slot angular pitch $\beta$:

$$K_d = \frac{\sin(m\beta/2)}{m \, \sin(\beta/2)}$$

**Quick Reference for Common Winding Layouts:**

| Slots per pole per phase (m) | Slot pitch (β) | Kd (theoretical) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 (concentrated) | 60° | 1.000 |
| 2 | 30° | 0.966 |
| 3 | 20° | 0.960 |
| 4 | 15° | 0.958 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Shipboard Alternator System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Prime Mover\nDiesel Engine / Steam Turbine] -->|Mechanical Shaft| B[Rotor Assembly\nField Winding + DC Excitation]
    B -->|Rotating Magnetic Flux| C[Air Gap Flux Linkage]
    C -->|Cuts Stator Conductors| D[Stator Armature Winding\n3-Phase R, Y, B]
    D -->|Induced AC EMF| E[Output Terminal Box\n3-Phase + Neutral]
    E --> F[Main Switchboard\nMSB of Ship]
    E --> G[Emergency Switchboard\nESB]
    F --> H[Ship Service Loads\nMotors, Lights, Navigation]
    F --> I[Hotel and Auxiliary Loads]
    I1[AVR Automatic Voltage Regulator] -->|DC Field Current| B
    I2[Tachometer / Governor] -->|Speed Feedback| A
    I3[CT and PT] -->|Voltage and Current Feedback| I1
    F --> I3
    style A fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#98fb98,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#333
    style I1 fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#333
```

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional View — Salient Pole vs. Cylindrical Rotor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Alternator Rotor Topologies] --> B[Salient Pole Rotor]
    A --> C[Non-Salient Pole Cylindrical Rotor]
    B --> B1[Low Speed 100 to 1500 RPM]
    B --> B2[Large Diameter, Short Axial Length]
    B --> B3[Used with Diesel Engines]
    B --> B4[Has Damper Windings]
    C --> C1[High Speed 1500 to 3000 RPM]
    C --> C2[Small Diameter, Long Axial Length]
    C --> C3[Used with Steam and Gas Turbines]
    C --> C4[No Damper Windings Needed]
    style A fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#add8e6,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333
```

### 4.3 EMF Generation — Physical Mechanism Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Excitation Applied to Rotor] --> B[Steady Magnetic Field Established on Rotor]
    B --> C[Prime Mover Rotates Rotor at Ns RPM]
    C --> D[Relative Motion Between Rotor Flux and Stator Conductors]
    D --> E[Flux Through Each Stator Conductor Changes Sinusoidally]
    E --> F[By Faraday Law Alternating EMF is Induced]
    F --> G[3-Phase Outputs 120 Degrees Apart]
    style A fill:#fffacd,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#ffdab9,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#e6e6fa,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#b0c4de,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#fafad2,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#ffc0cb,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#98fb98,stroke:#333
```

### 4.4 Voltage Waveform and Phasor Diagram (Descriptive)

```
        e(t) = Em sin(ωt)
            ▲ emf
            │     ╭───╮
            │    ╱     ╲
            │   ╱       ╲
            │  ╱         ╲
   ─────────┼─╱───────────╲───────► time (t)
            │╱             ╲
            ╱
           ╱
         ╱
        ╱

   Phase R:  0°
   Phase Y:  −120°  (lagging R by 120°)
   Phase B:  +120°  (leading R by 120°)
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a balanced **3-phase star-connected alternator**, the three phase EMFs are displaced by **120° electrical** in time. The KTU examiner expects the student to draw this phasor diagram with **ER + EY + EB = 0** for a balanced system.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Bank]**
*Define an alternator. Why is it called a "synchronous" generator?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **alternator** is a rotating machine that converts mechanical energy into alternating electrical energy based on Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction. It is called a **synchronous generator** because the rotor rotates at a **synchronous speed (Ns = 120f/P)**, which is locked in synchronism with the frequency of the generated EMF. Any change in mechanical input speed is immediately reflected as a change in frequency, hence the name. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
*Distinguish between salient pole and non-salient pole rotor constructions. State one application of each in marine systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Salient Pole | Non-Salient Pole |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Projecting poles from rotor surface | Smooth cylindrical rotor with slots |
| Speed | Low (100–1500 RPM) | High (1500–3000 RPM) |
| Diameter | Large | Small |
| Marine Use | Diesel-engine-driven ship alternators | Steam/gas turbine turbo-alternators |

Salient pole is used in **auxiliary diesel generators** on ships; non-salient pole is used in **steam-turbine main turbo-alternators**. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Module 2 Pattern]

**(a)** Derive the EMF equation of a 3-phase alternator starting from Faraday's law. State clearly all assumptions and define each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, star-connected alternator has a single-layer winding with 36 slots and 20 conductors per slot. The flux per pole is 0.06 Wb. The winding is full-pitched and concentrated. Calculate:
   (i) The induced EMF per phase
   (ii) The line voltage at the output terminals **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State Faraday's law — EMF is induced when a conductor cuts magnetic flux: $e = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Define variables — $\phi$ = flux per pole, $P$ = total poles, $N_s$ = synchronous speed in RPM, $Z$ = total conductors, $T_{ph}$ = turns/phase, $K_w$ = winding factor.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Flux cut per conductor per revolution = $\phi P$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Time per revolution $T = 60/N_s$. Average EMF per conductor = $\phi P N_s / 60$.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply form factor 1.11 for sine wave: $E_{rms,conductor} = 1.11 \times \phi P N_s / 60$.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Substitute $P N_s / 60 = 2f$, giving $E_{per\,conductor} = 2.22 f \phi$.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Multiply by $T_{ph}$ turns per phase, introduce $K_w$:

$$\boxed{E_{ph} = 4.44 \, K_w \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \text{ V/phase}}$$

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Synchronous speed: $N_s = 120f/P = 120 \times 50/4 = 1500$ RPM.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Total conductors $Z = 36 \times 20 = 720$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Conductors per phase $Z_{ph} = 720/3 = 240$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Turns per phase $T_{ph} = 240/2 = 120$ (single layer, no parallel paths).

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Since full-pitched and concentrated, $K_p = 1$, $K_d = 1$, hence $K_w = 1$.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Apply EMF equation:
$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 1 \times 50 \times 0.06 \times 120 = 1598.4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Line voltage (star):
$$E_{line} = \sqrt{3} \times 1598.4 \approx 2768.5 \text{ V}$$

**Final Answers:** $E_{ph} \approx 1598 \text{ V}$, $E_{line} \approx 2768 \text{ V}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the construction and working of a salient-pole marine alternator. List the functions of the **AVR** and **damper winding**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz, star-connected alternator has 72 slots with 8 conductors per slot. The winding is short-pitched by 2 slots. The flux per pole is 0.04 Wb. Calculate the induced EMF per phase. Take $K_d = 0.96$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Construction (3.5 marks):** Salient pole rotor has projecting pole pieces bolted to a magnetic wheel. The field winding is wound around each pole and connected in series. DC excitation is provided through slip rings and brushes (or brushless exciter). The stator has a laminated core with three-phase distributed windings.

**Working (2 marks):** DC excitation establishes a fixed magnetic field on the rotor. As the prime mover rotates the rotor at synchronous speed, the rotating flux cuts the stationary stator conductors, inducing a 3-phase alternating EMF per Faraday's law.

**AVR function (0.75 mark):** Maintains constant terminal voltage by automatically adjusting the field excitation current in response to load variations.

**Damper winding function (0.75 mark):** Suppresses **hunting oscillations** (mechanical swinging) of the rotor and provides **starting torque** for the machine when used as a motor.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** $N_s = 120 \times 50/6 = 1000$ RPM. $f = 50$ Hz.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** $Z = 72 \times 8 = 576$ conductors.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** $Z_{ph} = 576/3 = 192$. Hence $T_{ph} = 192/2 = 96$ turns/phase.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Full-pitch = $180°$ electrical. Short-pitch by 2 slots: slot angle = $180° \times 6/72 = 15°$, so $\alpha = 30°$.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** $K_p = \cos(30°/2) = \cos(15°) = 0.966$.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** $K_w = K_p \times K_d = 0.966 \times 0.96 = 0.9274$.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** 
$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 0.9274 \times 50 \times 0.04 \times 96 = 790.5 \text{ V}$$

**Final Answer:** $E_{ph} \approx 790.5 \text{ V}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Forgetting the winding factor (Kw):** Many students write $E = 4.44 f \phi T$ instead of $E = 4.44 K_w f \phi T$. This costs **2 marks** instantly.
> - **Mixing up Z and Zph:** Total conductors vs. per-phase conductors is a frequent error. Always divide Z by 3 for 3-phase machines.
> - **Forgetting √3 for line voltage:** Star-connected line voltage = $\sqrt{3} E_{ph}$, delta-connected = $E_{ph}$. Wrong factor = **1 mark lost**.
> - **Units of flux:** Flux must be in **Weber (Wb)**, not Maxwell. $1 \text{ Wb} = 10^8 \text{ Maxwell}$.
> - **Not converting RPM to RPS:** $N_s$ in formula must be in **per second (divide by 60)** when computing flux cut rate.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⚡ **Alternator = Synchronous Generator** — converts mechanical → electrical AC energy.
- ⚡ **Two main parts:** Stator (armature, stationary) + Rotor (field, rotating with prime mover).
- ⚡ **Two rotor types:** Salient pole (slow, hydro/diesel) vs. Cylindrical (fast, turbo).
- ⚡ **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = 120 f / P$ — must match system frequency.
- ⚡ **Operating frequency:** 50 Hz (Indian/EU ships) or 60 Hz (US Navy).
- ⚡ **EMF equation per phase:** $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, K_w \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph}$
- ⚡ **Line voltage (star):** $E_L = \sqrt{3} \times E_{ph}$
- ⚡ **Winding factor:** $K_w = K_p \times K_d$ (always ≤ 1; usually 0.85–0.96).
- ⚡ **Form factor 1.11** converts average EMF to RMS EMF for a sinusoidal waveform.
- ⚡ **Marine alternators** are typically **salient pole, self-excited, brushless**, driven by auxiliary diesel engines.
- ⚡ **AVR (Automatic Voltage Regulator)** maintains terminal voltage by controlling field current.
- ⚡ **Damper (amortisseur) windings** suppress hunting and provide asynchronous starting torque.
- ⚡ **Three-phase output** has 120° electrical phase displacement between R, Y, B phases.
- ⚡ **Always state assumptions** (full-pitch vs. short-pitch, distributed vs. concentrated) before applying the EMF equation in KTU board answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specific Systems for Distribution of AC Power in Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Specific Systems for Distribution of AC Power in Ships # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of marine electrical engineering governed by **IEC 60092** (Electrical Installations in Ships) and **IEEE 45** (Recommended Practice for Electrical Installations on Shipboard), **Specific Systems for Distribution of AC Power in Ships** refers to the standardized architectures, topologies, and protection philosophies used to deliver three-phase and single-phase alternating current from the prime movers (diesel generators, shaft generators, turbine generators) to the various auxiliary and propulsion consumers on a vessel. The marine AC distribution system is characterized by: - **Star (Y) connected alternators** with a choice of neutral-earthing method. - A **hierarchical switchboard network** comprising the *Main Switchboard (MSB)*, *Emergency Switchboard (ESB)*, *Section Boards (SB)*, and *Distribution Boards (DB)*. - **Standardized marine voltages**: 380 V, 440 V, 660 V (LV); 3.3 kV, 6.6 kV, 11 kV (HV). - **Standardized marine frequencies**: **50 Hz** or **60 Hz**, with **60 Hz** being dominant in merchant and naval fleets. - A **fault-tolerant neutral treatment** (mostly *insulated neutral* or *high-resistance-earthed neutral*) to maximize continuity of supply — a vital requirement at sea where a blackout can endanger the vessel. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** > Students must be able to distinguish between (a) 3-phase 3-wire, (b) 3-phase 4-wire, and (c) single-phase distribution systems used in ships, and explain the rationale for the prevalent use of the **insulated-neutral** (IT) system on board. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a large hotel (the ship) where electricity is supplied by its own private power plants (the ship's generators) rather than the city grid. The hotel has a "main lobby electrical room" (the **Main Switchboard**), emergency backup generators (the **Emergency Generator** with its own switchboard), floor-wise distribution panels (the **Section Boards**), and finally room-wise fuse boxes (the **Distribution Boards**). The most distinctive feature of a ship — unlike a building — is that the *neutral of the generator is NOT earthed to the hull*. This is like designing a hotel where every wire is "floating" and never tied to the building's metal frame. Why? Because a single earth fault (wire touching the metal hull) on a ship should NOT immediately trip the system and plunge the ship into darkness miles from shore. Instead, an **insulation monitoring device** quietly watches for the *first* fault, while an **earth-fault indicator** lights up to alert the engineer. The system continues running safely until the ship reaches port — this is the famous **"first-fault ride-through"** capability of the marine IT system. > [!NOTE] > The choice of **insulated neutral** is the single most important distinguishing feature of shipboard AC power distribution when compared to land-based industrial practice, where neutral is almost always solidly earthed. ## 1.3 Standardized Marine Parameters (with **bolded** emphasis) | Parameter | Standard Value (Marine) | Source Standard | |---|---|---| | Frequency | **50 Hz** or **60 Hz** | IEC 60092-201 | | LV (3-phase) | **380 V, 440 V, 660 V** | IEC 60092-201 | | HV (3-phase) | **3.3 kV, 6.6 kV, 11 kV** | IEC 60092-201 | | Lighting (1-phase) | **220 V, 240 V** | Derived from 3-phase | | Insulation (LV cables) | **0.6/1 kV** | IEC 60092-353 | | Insulation (HV cables) | **3.6/6 kV, 6/10 kV, 12/20 kV** | IEC 60092-353 | | System earthing | **Insulated (IT)** preferred | IEC 60092-201 | | Earth-fault current (IT) | Limited to **≤ 5 A** by RCE | Class society rules | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of AC Distribution Systems Used in Ships ### A. Three-Phase, Three-Wire System (Insulated Neutral — **IT System**) This is the **dominant arrangement** on modern ships. The three line conductors (L1, L2, L3) are brought from the alternator and distributed; the star-point (neutral) is **NOT connected to the hull**. An earth-fault monitor is fitted between the neutral and hull. - **Advantage:** A single line-to-hull fault does not cause a trip → first-fault continuity of supply. - **Advantage:** Reduced fire risk in flammable environments (engine room, pump room). - **Disadvantage:** A second fault on another phase can cause a heavy short-circuit, so rapid location is essential. - **Disadvantage:** Higher overvoltages on transient switching require robust insulation coordination. ### B.
