Three-Phase, Four-Wire System (Earthed Neutral — **TN System**) The neutral is brought out as a fourth conductor and is **solidly earthed (TN-S)** or **impedance-earthed** at the switchboard. The phase-to-neutral voltage (e.g., 230 V) is available for single-phase loads. - **Used when:** A large proportion of single-phase loads exist (e.g., accommodation, hotel loads). - **Used when:** Specific class rules (some naval auxiliaries) mandate earthed neutral. - **Disadvantage:** Earth fault → high fault current → immediate breaker trip → loss of that feeder. ### C. Single-Phase, Two-Wire System Derived from one phase + neutral of the three-phase system. Used for: - Lighting circuits (typically **220 V / 240 V**). - Small domestic loads in the accommodation block. - Navigation electronics (radar, gyro, GMDSS). > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 examiners frequently test whether the student can identify the type of system from a one-line diagram and justify why ships favour the IT system. ## 2.2 Architecture of a Typical Shipboard AC Distribution Network The distribution is **radial** with selective back-up. The chain of energy flow is: $$ \text{Diesel / Shaft / Turbine Generator} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Main Switchboard (MSB)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Emergency Switchboard (ESB)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Section Boards} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Distribution Boards} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Final Sub-Circuits} $$ ### 2.2.1 Main Switchboard (MSB) - Central node of the entire power plant. - Houses **Air Circuit Breakers (ACBs)** for each generator and each outgoing feeder. - Equipped with bus-bar **Bus Section Circuit Breaker (BSCB)** allowing parallel operation of generators. - Carries synchronizing, power management, and generator protection panels. - Located in the **Engine Control Room (ECR)**. ### 2.2.2 Emergency Switchboard (ESB) - Supplied normally from the MSB via an **Emergency Feeder**. - On blackout, **Emergency Generator** (diesel-driven) auto-starts and supplies it within **≤ 45 s**. - Supplies emergency services as per **SOLAS**: steering gear, navigation, fire pumps, communication. - On cargo ships the emergency generator capacity is typically **~ 200–500 kVA** at **440 V, 60 Hz**. ### 2.2.3 Section Boards (Sub-Switchboards) - Sub-divide the ship into zones (e.g., forward section, aft section, deck machinery). - Reduce cable runs and fault levels at consumers. - Each typically has an **incomer breaker** fed from the MSB and a number of **outgoing feeders**. ### 2.2.4 Distribution Boards (DBs) - Final distribution level — feed lighting, small motors, receptacles. - Equipped with **Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs)** and **Residual Current Devices (RCDs)** for the accommodation areas (where 4-wire TN system is used). - Located in cabins, galleys, alleyways, and workshops. ## 2.3 Neutral Earthing — The Heart of the System The choice of neutral treatment is the most important design decision. There are **three** options used on board: | Earthing Method | Earth-Fault Current | Continuity on First Fault | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Insulated (IT)** | Very small (capacitive) | **Maintained** | Most merchant ships, naval combat vessels | | **High-Resistance Earthing (HRG)** | Limited to **5 A – 10 A** | **Maintained** | Modern DP vessels, offshore platforms | | **Solidly Earthed (TN)** | Very high (kA) | **Lost** | Accommodation 4-wire sub-system, some auxiliaries | For HRG, the resistor value is given by: $$ R_{\text{N}} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{LN}}}{\sqrt{3}\,\cdot\, I_{\text{EF}}} $$ where $V_{\text{LN}}$ is the line-to-neutral voltage and $I_{\text{EF}}$ is the desired limited earth-fault current (typically chosen between 5 A and 10 A for shipboard systems). ## 2.4 Insulation Monitoring & Earth-Fault Detection In an **IT system** the neutral is floating, so a *zero-sequence* overcurrent relay is useless. Instead, ships use: 1. **Earth-Fault Indicator Lamps (Voltmeter Method):** - Three identical high-value resistors connect L1, L2, L3 to the hull. - Three lamps (or voltmeters) connect L1–hull, L2–hull, L3–hull. - When healthy, all lamps glow equally at phase voltage / 3. - On a fault on L1, the L1–hull lamp goes dark; the L2 and L3 lamps become brighter (≈ line voltage). 2. **Insulation Monitoring Relay (IMR):** - Continuously measures the system **insulation resistance** $R_{\text{iso}}$ with respect to hull. - Typical alarm threshold: $R_{\text{iso}} \le 50\,\text{k}\Omega$ (or 100 kΩ, per class rules). - Operates on the principle of superimposing a low-frequency measuring voltage and measuring leakage current. 3. **Earth-Fault Passage Indicator (Selective Earth-Fault Detection):** - Uses a **core-balance CT (CBCT)** on each outgoing feeder. - The secondary current is proportional to the sum of the capacitive charging currents of that cable. - Identifies the faulty feeder in an IT system with multiple parallel circuits. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Application | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_{\text{L}} = \sqrt{3}\,V_{\text{ph}}$ | Line-to-line voltage from phase voltage | V | | 2 | $I_{\text{L}} = I_{\text{ph}}$ (Star) | Line current equals phase current in Y | A | | 3 | $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_{\text{L}}\,I_{\text{L}}\,\cos\phi$ | Total 3-phase active power | W | | 4 | $Q = \sqrt{3}\,V_{\text{L}}\,I_{\text{L}}\,\sin\phi$ | Total 3-phase reactive power | VAR | | 5 | $S = \sqrt{3}\,V_{\text{L}}\,I_{\text{L}}$ | Total 3-phase apparent power | VA | | 6 | $R_{\text{N}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{LN}}}{\sqrt{3}\,I_{\text{EF}}}$ | High-resistance neutral earthing resistor | Ω | | 7 | $I_{\text{sc}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{L}}}{\sqrt{3}\,Z_{\text{sys}}}$ | Initial symmetrical short-circuit current | kA | | 8 | $V_{\text{drop}} = \sqrt{3}\,I_{\text{L}}\,(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)\,L$ | Voltage drop in a feeder (per phase) | V | | 9 | $R_{\text{iso(min)}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{L}}}{I_{\text{EF(max)}}}$ | Minimum acceptable insulation resistance | Ω | | 10 | $f_{\text{sync}} = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert \le 0.1\,\text{Hz}$ | Synchronization frequency condition | Hz | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board-Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** Every formula in the above table is examinable. The voltage-drop formula (Eq. 8) and the HRG resistor formula (Eq. 6) are the most frequently asked derivations. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility The marine IT system is not just an academic curiosity. It is the reason a warship can continue fighting after a missile strike, a tanker can keep its cargo pumps running after a hull breach, and an LNG carrier can maintain its gas-re-liquefaction plant in heavy weather. **Continuity of supply at sea is life-safety** — and the AC distribution system is the nervous system that delivers it. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sizing the High-Resistance Earthing Resistor (HRG) **Problem statement:** A 440 V, 60 Hz, 3-phase marine generator has its neutral to be earthed through a resistor such that the earth-fault current is limited to 5 A. Calculate the value of the resistor and the power it must dissipate. ### Step 1 — Identify the relevant voltage For HRG design, the resistor is connected between the **neutral point** of the generator and the **hull (earth)**. During a *single line-to-hull fault*, the full line-to-neutral voltage appears across this resistor. $$ V_{\text{ph}} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{L}}}{\sqrt{3}} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute the phase voltage for 440 V system $$ V_{\text{ph}} \;=\; \frac{440}{\sqrt{3}} \;\approx\; 254.034\,\text{V} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply Ohm's law across the earthing resistor During a bolted single-line-to-hull fault, the fault current is the phase voltage divided by the resistor: $$ I_{\text{EF}} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{ph}}}{R_{\text{N}}} $$ ### Step 4 — Rearrange for the resistor $$ R_{\text{N}} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{ph}}}{I_{\text{EF}}} $$ ### Step 5 — Substitute numerical values $$ R_{\text{N}} \;=\; \frac{254.034}{5} $$ $$ R_{\text{N}} \;\approx\; 50.81\,\Omega $$ ### Step 6 — Compute continuous power dissipation The resistor must be rated for the *full phase voltage continuously*, because in an IT system the fault may persist until it is manually located. $$ P_{\text{R}} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{ph}}^{2}}{R_{\text{N}}} $$ ### Step 7 — Numerical evaluation $$ P_{\text{R}} \;=\; \frac{(254.034)^{2}}{50.81} $$ $$ P_{\text{R}} \;=\; \frac{64\,533.27}{50.81} $$ $$ P_{\text{R}} \;\approx\; 1\,270.2\,\text{W} $$ ### Step 8 — Practical rating A safety factor of at least 1.5 is applied, so the chosen resistor should be rated for **≥ 2 kW continuous**, typically a stainless-steel grid resistor mounted in the switchboard. > **Final Answer:** $R_{\text{N}} \approx 50.8\,\Omega$, $P_{\text{R}} \approx 1.27\,\text{kW}$ (use 2 kW rated unit). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Voltage Drop in a 440 V Shipboard Feeder **Problem statement:** A 440 V, 60 Hz, 3-phase feeder from the MSB supplies a 75 kW bow-thruster motor at 0.85 p.f. lagging. The cable run is 120 m, with per-phase resistance 0.15 Ω/km and reactance 0.08 Ω/km. Compute the percentage voltage drop. ### Step 1 — Per-phase impedance $$ R \;=\; 0.15 \times \frac{120}{1000} \;=\; 0.018\,\Omega $$ $$ X \;=\; 0.08 \times \frac{120}{1000} \;=\; 0.0096\,\Omega $$ ### Step 2 — Line current drawn by the motor $$ I_{\text{L}} \;=\; \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}\,V_{\text{L}}\cos\phi} $$ ### Step 3 — Substitute values $$ I_{\text{L}} \;=\; \frac{75\,000}{\sqrt{3}\times 440 \times 0.85} $$ $$ I_{\text{L}} \;=\; \frac{75\,000}{647.74} $$ $$ I_{\text{L}} \;\approx\; 115.78\,\text{A} $$ ### Step 4 — Voltage drop per phase (approximate) $$ V_{\text{drop}} \;=\; I_{\text{L}}\,(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi) $$ ### Step 5 — Compute the trigonometric terms $$ \cos\phi \;=\; 0.85 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \sin\phi \;=\; \sqrt{1 - 0.85^{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{1 - 0.7225} \;=\; \sqrt{0.2775} \;\approx\; 0.5268 $$ ### Step 6 — Substitute and evaluate $$ V_{\text{drop}} \;=\; 115.78 \times (0.018 \times 0.85 + 0.0096 \times 0.5268) $$ $$ V_{\text{drop}} \;=\; 115.78 \times (0.0153 + 0.005057) $$ $$ V_{\text{drop}} \;=\; 115.78 \times 0.02036 $$ $$ V_{\text{drop}} \;\approx\; 2.358\,\text{V (per phase)} $$ ### Step 7 — Percentage voltage drop (line-to-line, factor $\sqrt{3}$) $$ \%V_{\text{drop}} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{3} \times V_{\text{drop}}}{V_{\text{L}}} \times 100 $$ $$ \%V_{\text{drop}} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{3} \times 2.358}{440} \times 100 $$ $$ \%V_{\text{drop}} \;=\; \frac{4.083}{440} \times 100 $$ $$ \%V_{\text{drop}} \;\approx\; 0.928\,\% $$ ### Step 8 — Compare with class rule limits Class rules (DNV, Lloyd's) require motor feeder voltage drop $\le 5\,\%$ (during starting) and $\le 2.5\,\%$ (running). The computed 0.93 % is well within limits. > **Final Answer:** $\%V_{\text{drop}} \approx 0.93\,\%$, which is acceptable for shipboard installation. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Symmetrical Short-Circuit Current at the MSB **Problem statement:** A 1500 kVA, 440 V, 60 Hz, 3-phase generator has a sub-transient reactance $X''_{\text{d}} = 0.12$ p.u. Compute the initial symmetrical short-circuit current at its terminals. ### Step 1 — Base quantities $$ I_{\text{base}} \;=\; \frac{S_{\text{base}}}{\sqrt{3}\,V_{\text{base}}} $$ ### Step 2 — Substitute $$ I_{\text{base}} \;=\; \frac{1\,500\,000}{\sqrt{3}\times 440} $$ $$ I_{\text{base}} \;=\; \frac{1\,500\,000}{762.10} $$ $$ I_{\text{base}} \;\approx\; 1\,968.3\,\text{A} $$ ### Step 3 — Per-unit short-circuit current $$ I_{\text{sc(pu)}} \;=\; \frac{1}{X''_{\text{d}}} $$ $$ I_{\text{sc(pu)}} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.12} \;\approx\; 8.333\,\text{p.u.} $$ ### Step 4 — Actual short-circuit current $$ I_{\text{sc}} \;=\; I_{\text{sc(pu)}} \times I_{\text{base}} $$ $$ I_{\text{sc}} \;=\; 8.333 \times 1\,968.3 $$ $$ I_{\text{sc}} \;\approx\; 16\,400\,\text{A} \;=\; 16.4\,\text{kA} $$ > **Final Answer:** $I_{\text{sc}} \approx 16.4\,\text{kA}$. This value is critical for selecting the *breaking capacity* of the MSB air circuit breakers. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Shipboard Earthing Philosophies | Criterion | Insulated (IT) | High-Resistance (HRG) | Solidly Earthed (TN) | |---|---|---|---| | Neutral conductor distributed | No (kept in MSB) | No | Yes (4-wire) | | First-fault current | < 1 A (capacitive) | 5 – 10 A | Several kA | | Continuity on first fault | **Maintained** | **Maintained** | **Lost** | | Touch voltage on fault | Low (≈ $V_{\text{ph}}$ on healthy phases) | Moderate | High | | Overvoltage on ground fault | Up to $\sqrt{3}\,V_{\text{ph}}$ on healthy phases | $\approx V_{\text{ph}}$ | $V_{\text{LN}}$ | | Insulation monitoring required | **Mandatory** | **Mandatory** | Optional | | Typical ship use | Most cargo, naval | DP, offshore, electric propulsion | Accommodation sub-circuits | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Shipboard AC Power Distribution ```mermaid graph TD A1[Main DG 1
1500 kVA 440V 60Hz] A2[Main DG 2
1500 kVA 440V 60Hz] A3[Shaft Generator
Optional] SP[Shore Power
Connection Box] MSB[Main Switchboard MSB
440V 3-phase IT] ESB[Emergency Switchboard ESB
440V 3-phase] EDG[Emergency Diesel Generator
300 kVA 440V] SB1[Section Board FWD
Forward Auxiliaries] SB2[Section Board AFT
Aft Auxiliaries] SB3[Section Board DECK
Deck Machinery] DB1[DB1 Lighting
220V 1-phase] DB2[DB2 Accommodation
220V 1-phase] DB3[DB3 Galley Workshop
220V 1-phase] L1[Bow Thruster Motor
75 kW] L2[Cargo Pump Motors
3 x 90 kW] L3[Steering Gear Motor
40 kW] L4[Navigation Radar
220V] L5[Main Propulsion Drive
HV 3.3 kV] A1 --> MSB A2 --> MSB A3 --> MSB SP --> MSB MSB --> ESB EDG --> ESB MSB --> SB1 MSB --> SB2 MSB --> SB3 MSB --> L5 SB1 --> L1 SB1 --> L4 SB2 --> L2 SB2 --> L3 SB1 --> DB1 SB2 --> DB2 SB3 --> DB3 ``` ## 4.2 Schematic — Earth-Fault Detection by Lamp Method (Insulated Neutral) ```mermaid graph TD G[Ship Generator
440V 3-phase Star
Neutral INSULATED] L1p[L1 Phase] L2p[L2 Phase] L3p[L3 Phase] H[Hull / Earth] R1[Indicator Resistor 1
High value] R2[Indicator Resistor 2
High value] R3[Indicator Resistor 3
High value] LP1[Lamp L1-E
Dim healthy] LP2[Lamp L2-E
Dim healthy] LP3[Lamp L3-E
Dim healthy] IMR[Insulation Monitoring
Relay IMR] CBCT[Core Balance CT
on outgoing feeder] EFPI[Earth Fault Passage
Indicator] L1p --- R1 L2p --- R2 L3p --- R3 R1 --- LP1 R2 --- LP2 R3 --- LP3 LP1 --- H LP2 --- H LP3 --- H G --- L1p G --- L2p G --- L3p IMR --- H IMR --- L1p IMR --- L2p IMR --- L3p CBCT --- EFPI ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Selective Earth-Fault Detection System ```mermaid graph TD BUS[Main Busbar 440V 3-phase
Insulated Neutral] F1[Feeder 1 to Bow Thruster] F2[Feeder 2 to Cargo Pump] F3[Feeder 3 to Steering Gear] CBCT1[CBCT on Feeder 1] CBCT2[CBCT on Feeder 2] CBCT3[CBCT on Feeder 3] REL1[Earth-Fault Relay 1] REL2[Earth-Fault Relay 2] REL3[Earth-Fault Relay 3] ANN[Annunciator Panel in ECR] BUS --> F1 BUS --> F2 BUS --> F3 F1 --> CBCT1 F2 --> CBCT2 F3 --> CBCT3 CBCT1 --> REL1 CBCT2 --> REL2 CBCT3 --> REL3 REL1 --> ANN REL2 --> ANN REL3 --> ANN ``` ## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Emergency Power Transfer Sequence ```mermaid graph TD S1[MSB Healthy
Supply from Main DG] S2[ESB Fed from MSB

This is a Mermaid-renderable substitute for a full single-line diagram and is acceptable in KTU 2024 answer scripts. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Why is the insulated-neutral (IT) system preferred for the main power distribution on board ships?** **Model Answer:** The insulated-neutral (IT) system is preferred for shipboard main distribution for the following reasons: 1. **First-fault ride-through:** A single line-to-hull earth fault does not create a low-impedance loop, so the fault current is limited to a few milliamperes (capacitive charging current). The system continues to operate normally, allowing the vessel to remain at sea safely until the fault is located and rectified at the next port. 2. **Reduced fire and explosion risk:** Because the fault current is very small, there is negligible arcing or heat at the fault point — critical in flammable compartments such as the engine room, pump room, and cargo oil tanks. 3. **Higher reliability for safety-critical services:** SOLAS-mandated services (steering gear, navigation, fire pumps) remain energized even with one earth fault, satisfying the maritime principle of *continuity of supply at all costs*. 4. **Compatibility with insulation monitoring:** A continuous Insulation Monitoring Relay (IMR) can detect the gradual degradation of cable insulation before a second fault occurs, enabling preventive maintenance. For these reasons, IEC 60092-201 and most class societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS) prescribe the IT system as the standard arrangement for the main and emergency switchboards of merchant and naval vessels. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Differentiate between 3-phase 3-wire and 3-phase 4-wire distribution systems used in ships.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | 3-Phase 3-Wire (IT) | 3-Phase 4-Wire (TN-S) | |---|---|---| | Number of conductors | 3 line conductors only | 3 line + 1 neutral | | Neutral earthing | Neutral isolated from hull | Neutral solidly earthed at MSB | | Phase-to-neutral voltage | Not available | Available (e.g., 230 V) | | Typical application | Main 440 V power, large motors, HV propulsion | Accommodation, lighting, hotel loads | | Behaviour on earth fault | No trip on first fault | Immediate trip on first fault | | Insulation monitoring | Mandatory | Optional | The 3-wire IT system is used for power distribution from the MSB to large consumers, while the 4-wire TN-S sub-system is used locally within the accommodation block to supply single-phase loads at 220–240 V. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper] **With neat single-line diagram, explain the architecture of a typical shipboard AC power distribution system starting from the main generators down to the final sub-circuits. Discuss the role of the main switchboard, emergency switchboard, section boards and distribution boards.** [14 Marks] #### (a) Architecture and Hierarchy — 7 Marks A modern shipboard AC distribution system follows a **radial, hierarchical architecture** designed to maximize segregation, selectivity, and continuity of supply. The energy chain is: - **Prime Movers → Generators → Main Switchboard (MSB) → Emergency Switchboard (ESB) and Section Boards → Distribution Boards (DBs) → Final Sub-Circuits.** **Main Switchboard (MSB):** The MSB is the heart of the electrical plant. It receives power from the main diesel generators (typically 2 or 3 units, e.g., 1500 kVA each at 440 V, 60 Hz), the shaft generator (where fitted), and the shore-power connection. It houses Air Circuit Breakers (ACBs) for each generator and for each outgoing feeder. A Bus-Section Circuit Breaker (BSCB) allows the bus to be split or paralleled. Synchronizing, power management, generator protection, and the Insulation Monitoring Relay (IMR) are all located here. **Emergency Switchboard (ESB):** Normally supplied from the MSB via an interconnector feeder, the ESB becomes the secondary source of power. On a total blackout, the Emergency Diesel Generator (EDG) auto-starts and reaches rated voltage and frequency within **45 seconds**, after which the emergency breaker closes and the ESB feeds the emergency loads as listed in SOLAS Chapter II-1: steering gear, navigation equipment, fire pumps, emergency lighting, and communication systems. **Section Boards:** The MSB feeder cables run to sub-switchboards distributed geographically throughout the ship (forward, aft, upper deck, engine room, etc.). Each section board contains an incoming ACB and a number of outgoing moulded-case circuit breakers (MCCBs) feeding motors and large loads in its zone. Section boards reduce the length of sub-main cables, localize faults to a zone, and provide easy isolation for maintenance. **Distribution Boards (DBs):** The final distribution level. DBs feed lighting circuits, receptacles, and small single-phase loads. In the accommodation block, the DBs operate on the **4-wire TN-S sub-system** (220–240 V single-phase + neutral + protective earth) to permit the use of standard MCBs and RCDs. Each cabin typically has a dedicated final sub-circuit fed from a corridor DB. #### (b) Single-Line Representation and Selectivity — 7 Marks In a single-line diagram, each generator is shown as a circle with a rating tag (e.g., 1500 kVA, 440 V, 3-phase, 60 Hz). A tie-line connects to the shore-power receptacle. The busbars are drawn as thick horizontal lines, and each feeder is shown as a single line representing all three phases. The ACBs are shown as rectangular boxes on the bus, and the feeders run vertically downward to the next switchboard level. **Selectivity (Discrimination):** The breakers are graded so that a fault on a small sub-circuit is cleared by the local MCB without tripping the upstream MCCB or ACB. This is achieved by **current-time grading** and by using **time-delay relays** on the upstream breakers. Modern ships also use **zone-selectivity** logic for fast discrimination. **Earth-fault detection:** The IMR measures insulation resistance continuously and gives an alarm at ≤ 50 kΩ. Core-balance CTs on each outgoing feeder identify the faulty zone. **Example of system voltages used:** - Main bus: **440 V, 3-phase, 60 Hz** - Lighting: **220 V, 1-phase** - HV propulsion (if fitted): **3.3 kV or 6.6 kV, 3-phase** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently (a) forget to mention the **shore-power connection**, (b) omit the **bus-section circuit breaker (BSCB)**, and (c) confuse the **emergency switchboard** with the **emergency generator** itself. The ESB is the *switchboard*, not the generator. Losing these three points can cost you up to **3 marks** in the 14-mark question. --- ### Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper] **Explain with sketches the principle of operation of (a) the earth-fault detection system by lamp method, and (b) the Insulation Monitoring Relay (IMR) used in shipboard insulated-neutral distribution systems.** [14 Marks] #### (a) Earth-Fault Detection by Lamp Method — 7 Marks In an **insulated-neutral (IT) system**, the star-point of the alternator is isolated from the hull. To detect any line-to-hull fault, three identical high-value resistors (typically 10 kΩ to 50 kΩ) are connected from L1, L2, and L3 to the hull. Across each resistor, a low-wattage indicator lamp (or a moving-coil voltmeter) is connected between the corresponding phase and the hull. **Healthy Condition (no fault):** Each phase-to-hull voltage is the *system neutral displacement voltage*, which for a perfectly balanced insulated system is theoretically zero. In practice, a small displacement exists due to unbalanced line-to-earth capacitance, and all three lamps glow with *equal, dim intensity* (corresponding to the small voltage across each resistor). **Fault on L1 (L1 touched to hull):** The L1-to-hull voltage collapses to nearly 0 V, so **lamp L1 goes dark**. The other two phases L2 and L3 are now at line-to-line voltage with respect to L1 (the new "neutral"), so lamps L2 and L3 become *brighter*, glowing at line-to-line voltage. This pattern is unmistakable and instantly locates the faulty phase. **Sketch:** (The schematic from Section 4.2 above may be redrawn by the student in the answer script.) **Limitations:** The lamp method only identifies the *faulty phase* — not the *faulty feeder*. It is therefore a *global* indicator, suitable for a single radial feeder or for the entire MSB. For multi-feeder selectivity, a Core-Balance CT and an Earth-Fault Passage Indicator are added (see schematic in Section 4.3). #### (b) Insulation Monitoring Relay (IMR) — 7 Marks The IMR continuously measures the **insulation resistance $R_{\text{iso}}$** of the entire network with respect to the hull, and raises an alarm (and optionally a trip) when $R_{\text{iso}}$ falls below a preset threshold (typically **50 kΩ** for LV marine systems, per IEC 61557-8). **Operating Principle:** The IMR superimposes a low-frequency (typically 2.5 Hz) low-voltage AC measuring signal between the three phases (joined together) and the hull. The current that flows is the leakage current $I_{\text{L}}$ through the insulation. From Ohm's law: $$ R_{\text{iso}} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{meas}}}{I_{\text{L}}} $$ Because the measuring frequency is different from the 50/60 Hz mains, the IMR can distinguish the measuring current from any load currents flowing in the system. **Functional Block Diagram of an IMR:** 1. **Measuring-voltage generator** (low-frequency, low-voltage source). 2. **Coupling network** (connected between all three phases and hull through a coupling impedance). 3. **Leakage-current sensing** (measures the magnitude of the 2.5 Hz component). 4. **Microprocessor / Evaluation unit** (computes $R_{\text{iso}}$ and compares with threshold). 5. **Output relays** (alarm contact and trip contact). 6. **Display** (shows actual $R_{\text{iso}}$ in kΩ, also history of worst recorded value). **Alarm & Trip Settings (typical):** - *Pre-alarm* at $R_{\text{iso}} \le 200\,\text{k}\Omega$ — yellow lamp, audible in ECR. - *Main alarm* at $R_{\text{iso}} \le 50\,\text{k}\Omega$ — red lamp, audible + printout. - *Trip* (optional, on non-essential feeders) at $R_{\text{iso}} \le 10\,\text{k}\Omega$. **Why it is critical in ships:** The IMR detects the *slow deterioration* of cable insulation due to salt, humidity, vibration, oil and heat — a slow degradation that would otherwise only manifest as a sudden blackout at the second fault. By giving early warning, the IMR enables preventive maintenance, which is a class-society requirement (e.g., Lloyd's Register Rules, Chapter 5). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **lamp method** (which uses the mains frequency and the line-to-hull voltage) with the **IMR** (which uses a superimposed low-frequency measuring voltage). In a 14-mark question, this conceptual mix-up will cost you at least **2 marks**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 2** - ☐ The **IT (insulated-neutral) system** is the marine standard for main distribution; the **TN-S 4-wire system** is used only for the accommodation sub-system. - ☐ Standardized marine voltages: **380 V, 440 V, 660 V (LV)**, **3.3 kV, 6.6 kV, 11 kV (HV)**; frequencies: **50 Hz / 60 Hz**. - ☐ Hierarchy: **Generators → MSB → ESB / Section Boards → Distribution Boards → Final Sub-Circuits**. - ☐ The **Emergency Generator** must auto-start and feed the **ESB** within **45 s** of a blackout. - ☐ **Insulation Monitoring Relay (IMR)** continuously measures $R_{\text{iso}}$; alarm at ≤ **50 kΩ** for LV systems. - ☐ **Lamp method** of earth-fault detection uses three high-value resistors + three lamps across L1, L2, L3 to hull. - ☐ **Core-Balance CT (CBCT)** is used for *selective* earth-fault detection on individual feeders. - ☐ **HRG resistor** value: $R_{\text{N}} = V_{\text{LN}} / (\sqrt{3}\,I_{\text{EF}})$; typical $I_{\text{EF}} = 5\,\text{A}$ to 10 A. - ☐ Voltage drop in a feeder: $V_{\text{drop}} = \sqrt{3}\,I\,(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)\,L$; permitted ≤ 2.5 % running, ≤ 5 % starting. - ☐ Symmetrical short-circuit current: $I_{\text{sc}} = V_{\text{L}} / (\sqrt{3}\,Z_{\text{sys}})$ — used to size the **breaking capacity** of ACBs. - ☐ Synchronization of alternators: $V$, $f$ and phase angle must match within tolerances ($f$ match within **0.1 Hz**, phase within **± 10°**). - ☐ A single earth fault on an IT system does **not** trip any breaker; this is the **first-fault ride-through** philosophy. - ☐ **SOLAS Chapter II-1** lists the emergency services that the ESB must feed. **Memory Hook — "NEVER":** - **N**eutral is **E**arthed? **V**ery **E**xceptional in **R**eal ships — i.e., normally **IT**.

topics.title: General Distribution Scheme Onboard a Ship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Distribution Scheme Onboard a Ship

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — GENERAL DISTRIBUTION SCHEME ONBOARD A SHIP

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — General Distribution Scheme (GDS)**
> The *General Distribution Scheme Onboard a Ship* refers to the complete network architecture — comprising generators, switchboards, bus-bars, transformers, feeder cables, sub-distribution panels, and load circuits — through which electrical power is generated, conditioned, protected, and delivered to every consumer on a merchant or naval vessel. The scheme is governed by the **Rules of Classification Societies** (IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, BV), **IEC 60092** (Electrical Installations in Ships), and **SOLAS Chapter II-1** (Regulations on Electrical Power).

The shipboard distribution system is functionally divided into **four hierarchical tiers**:

| Tier | Network Level | Typical Voltage | Function |
|------|---------------|-----------------|----------|
| 1 | **Generation** | 440 V / 6.6 kV / 11 kV | Prime movers (Diesel Generators) feed the Main Switchboard |
| 2 | **Main Distribution** | 440 V / 690 V | Main Switchboard (MSB) with bus-tie and interconnections |
| 3 | **Sub-Distribution** | 440 V / 230 V | Sub-Boards (Emergency, Group starters, Engine-room, Bridge) |
| 4 | **Load Level** | 230 V / 24 V DC | Final consumers (motors, lighting, navigation, control) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Shipboard Voltage & Frequency (per IEC 60092 / IEEE 45):**
> - **Main generation:** 3-phase, 440 V, 60 Hz (or 380 V, 50 Hz).
> - **High-voltage generation:** 3.3 kV, 6.6 kV, or 11 kV for ships above ~10 MW installed power.
> - **Secondary:** 230 V, 1-phase for lighting and small loads.
> - **Emergency & DC systems:** 24 V DC (battery banks), 110 V DC, 220 V DC.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: "The Ship is a Floating City"**
> Think of a ship as a **self-contained floating city** that cannot plug into the national grid. Just as a metropolitan city has power stations, high-voltage transmission lines, distribution substations, and finally household wiring, a ship has its own miniature version:
>
> - **Diesel Generators** = Power plants
> - **Main Switchboard** = High-voltage substation with circuit breakers
> - **Sub-Boards & Group Starters** = Local distribution transformers/panels feeding a neighborhood
> - **Cables & Bus-bars** = Transmission and distribution lines
> - **Emergency Generator & Battery Banks** = Standby backup-power infrastructure
> - **Shore-Supply Connection** = External grid interconnector used when docked
>
> The key difference is **fault tolerance**: a ship at sea has *no second chance* if its power fails — every consumer must be categorized, every fault must be isolated, and emergency systems must start within **45 seconds** automatically (SOLAS requirement).

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard frequency:** **60 Hz** (most merchant fleets) or **50 Hz** (European/Asian builds).
- **Allowable voltage variation:** **±10 %** steady-state, **±20 %** transient (IEC 60092-101).
- **Allowable frequency variation:** **±5 %** steady-state, **±10 %** transient.
- **Main bus fault level (LV):** typically **50 kA – 100 kA** for 440 V systems.
- **Degrees of protection (motors, panels):** **IP44** (deck), **IP56** (weather), **IP23** (engine-room internal).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Building Blocks of a Shipboard Distribution System

The General Distribution Scheme is built from six core functional blocks:

#### A. Generating Plant
- **Auxiliary Diesel Generators (DG sets):** 3 or 4 units, each typically **500 kW – 4 MW** at 440 V.
- For large vessels (LNG carriers, cruise ships, container ships), **High-Voltage (HV) generation** at 6.6 kV is used to limit current and cable size.
- Prime-mover: 4-stroke marine diesel engine coupled to a 3-phase synchronous alternator (typically with **brushless excitation** and **AVR**).

#### B. Main Switchboard (MSB)
The MSB is the **heart of the ship** — a large, self-supporting, arc-resistant, drip-proof, front-and-rear-access switchgear assembly. It contains:
- **Main bus-bars** (3-phase + neutral, copper, sized by **current density ≈ 1.5 A/mm²**).
- **Generator circuit breakers (ACBs)** — withdrawable, motorised, with **short-time withstand (Icw)** rating.
- **Bus-tie breaker (BTB)** — allows running generators in parallel, or splits the board into two halves for fault isolation.
- **Preferential (Non-Essential) Trips** — automatically shed non-vital loads if a generator fails.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOLAS Preferential Tripping Order:** Air-conditioning → Galley → Deck machinery → Bow thruster. **Essential services (steering, navigation, fire-pumps, communication) are NEVER tripped.**

#### C. Emergency Switchboard (ESB)
Fed from the **Emergency Generator** (independent, located above the bulkhead deck, self-starting within **45 s**) and the **Emergency Battery Bank** (typically Ni-Cd or lead-acid, sized for **30 minutes** of emergency loads + 18 hours of emergency lighting per SOLAS II-1/43).

#### D. Shore-Supply Connection (Shore Power / Cold-Ironing)
An **IEC 80005-3 compliant** shore-power receptacle allows the ship to draw power from the dockside grid, isolating the auxiliaries. Rated typically **440 V, 60 Hz, 200–1500 A** depending on vessel size.

#### E. Cable Network
- **Power cables:** IEC 60092-353 (XLPE/EPR insulated, halogen-free, flame-retardant).
- **Cables routed through EMC zones** (IEC 60533) — power and signal cables separated by **≥ 200 mm** or by EMC barriers.
- **Penetrations** through watertight bulkheads use **fire-rated gland boxes**.

#### F. Load Categories
1. **Vital / Essential services** — steering gear, fire-fighting, navigation, communication.
2. **Important services** — fuel & lube-oil pumps, cooling water pumps, air-compressors.
3. **Non-essential / Preferential loads** — accommodation, galley, deck-crane.
4. **Emergency services** — emergency fire-pump, sprinkler, watertight-door motors, emergency lighting, general alarm.

---

### 2.2 Distribution Topologies Used Onboard

There are three principal radial/loop configurations in marine practice:

**(i) Radial System (most common for LV)**
- Each consumer is fed by a **single feeder** from the MSB.
- Simple, but a single cable fault blackouts that consumer.
- *Used for*: lighting, small auxiliary motors, electronics.

**(ii) Ring-Loop / Closed-Bus System (preferred for HV & propulsion)**
- Two feeders per consumer (one from MSB port, one from MSB starboard), with a **bus-tie** and **sectionalising breakers**.
- A single cable fault does NOT blackout the consumer — power is fed from the other side.
- *Used for*: thrusters, cargo pumps, HV main bus.

**(iii) Dual-Fed (Changeover) System**
- Consumer has **two feeders** with an **Automatic Changeover Switch (ATS)**.
- *Used for*: navigation equipment, steering gear, emergency fire-pumps.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Typical Value / Unit | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|----------------------|---------|
| 1 | Generator apparent power | $S_g = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L$ | kVA / MVA | 3-phase AC |
| 2 | Three-phase active power | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | kW | $\cos\phi$ = power factor (0.8 lagging typical) |
| 3 | Bus-bar current density | $J = \dfrac{I_{bus}}{A_{cu}}$ | $\approx 1.5\ \text{A/mm}^2$ | For copper bus-bars in MSB |
| 4 | Required generator count (n-1 redundancy) | $N_{req} = \lceil P_{load} / P_{gen} \rceil + 1$ | — | n-1 rule per Class rules |
| 5 | Voltage drop (3-φ feeder) | $\Delta V\% = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)}{V_L} \times 100$ | $\leq 5\%$ steady, $\leq 10\%$ motor starting | IEC 60092-352 |
| 6 | Short-circuit current (LV) | $I_{sc}'' = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{eq}}$ | kA | Used for ACB breaking capacity |
| 7 | Battery autonomy | $t = \dfrac{C \cdot k}{I_{load}}$ | hours | $C$ = capacity (Ah), $k$ = ageing factor ≈ 0.8 |
| 8 | Earth-fault loop impedance | $Z_e = Z_s + Z_{cable} + Z_{electrodes}$ | mΩ | Determines protective-device discrimination |
| 9 | Emergency-load sizing | $P_{em} = \sum P_{ess\;loads}$ | kW | Sum of all emergency consumers |
| 10 | Insulation resistance (Class rule) | $R_{iso} \ge \dfrac{V_{rated}}{1000 + 0.01\,C}$ | MΩ | $C$ = cable capacitance (µF) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example: Voltage Drop in a 440 V, 100 m Motor Feeder**
> - Cable: $3 \times 70\ \text{mm}^2$ XLPE, $R = 0.32\ \Omega/\text{km}$, $X = 0.08\ \Omega/\text{km}$
> - Load: 200 A at $\cos\phi = 0.85$
> - $L = 0.1\ \text{km}$, so $R_c = 0.032\ \Omega$, $X_c = 0.008\ \Omega$
> - $\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \times 200 \times (0.032 \times 0.85 + 0.008 \times 0.527) = \sqrt{3} \times 200 \times 0.0314 = 10.88\ \text{V}$
> - $\Delta V\% = (10.88 / 440) \times 100 = 2.47\%$ ✔ (within 5%)

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The General Distribution Scheme is the **operational backbone** of every modern ship. Without it, no thruster, no navigation radar, no fuel pump, no fire-fighting pump, no radio could function. Class surveyors audit the GDS at design approval, during construction (FAT/SAT), and at every 5-yearly Special Survey. The **single-line diagram (SLD)** of the MSB is the single most scrutinised document in any shipbuilding project — it determines generator sizing, cable sizes, breaker ratings, selectivity (discrimination), and blackout-prevention logic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Generator Sizing for a Ship's Total Load (Analytical Method)

The total installed generator capacity must exceed the largest running load plus sufficient redundancy. The following derivation yields the **minimum number of generator sets** for a vessel with known load.

**Step 1 — Compile the load balance sheet.**
Divide the load list into:
- Continuous running load ($P_{cont}$)
- Intermittent / standby load ($P_{int}$)
- Peak / momentary load (e.g., bow thruster during arrival) ($P_{peak}$)

Each motor is multiplied by a **demand factor** ($DF$) — the fraction of nameplate power actually drawn. Lighting and heating use $DF = 1.0$, while auxiliaries use $DF = 0.7$–$0.85$.

$$P_{demand} = \sum_{i} DF_i \cdot P_{i,\text{nameplate}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the diversity factor.**
Not all consumers run simultaneously. The **diversity factor** $K_{div}$ accounts for the statistical coincidence:

$$P_{max,demand} = K_{div} \cdot P_{demand}, \quad 0.6 \le K_{div} \le 0.9$$

**Step 3 — Account for motor-starting transients.**
A large motor (e.g., 250 kW thruster) draws $6\times$ its rated current at DOL start. Generator voltage dips must stay within $\pm20\%$ transient. The **per-unit voltage dip** is:

$$\Delta V_{pu} = \frac{X_d''}{X_d'' + X_{line}} \quad \text{(simplified, for remote motors)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the (n-1) redundancy rule.**
The bus must remain operational with the **largest generator out of service**:

$$N_{gen} = \left\lceil \frac{P_{max,demand}}{P_{single\;gen} \cdot K_{margin}} \right\rceil + 1$$

where $K_{margin} = 0.8$ is the typical Class-rule loading margin (no generator should run above 80% of its MCR continuously).

**Step 5 — Check that the MSB can absorb the largest motor's starting current without under-voltage tripping:**

$$\Delta V_{\%} = \frac{I_{start}}{I_{sc,bus}} \times 100 \le 20\%$$

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: Generator Sizing for a Handysize Bulk Carrier

> **Given:** A Handysize bulk carrier has the following consumers:
> - Main engine auxiliaries (cooling pump, lube pump, fuel-oil purifier): $3 \times 30\ \text{kW}$, $DF = 0.85$
> - Steering gear: $2 \times 25\ \text{kW}$, $DF = 0.7$
> - Bow thruster: 1 × $250\ \text{kW}$ (intermittent, 30 min/day), $DF = 0.6$
> - Deck machinery (winches): $2 \times 80\ \text{kW}$, $DF = 0.4$
> - Galley, lighting, HVAC: $250\ \text{kW}$, $DF = 1.0$
> - Navigation/communication: $50\ \text{kW}$, $DF = 0.9$

**Step 1 — Sum the demand:**

$$P_{demand} = 3(30)(0.85) + 2(25)(0.7) + 1(250)(0.6) + 2(80)(0.4) + 250(1.0) + 50(0.9)$$

$$P_{demand} = 76.5 + 35 + 150 + 64 + 250 + 45 = 620.5\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 2 — Apply diversity factor** $K_{div} = 0.75$:

$$P_{max,demand} = 0.75 \times 620.5 = 465.4\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Class margin** $K_{margin} = 0.8$:

$$P_{per\;gen} = \dfrac{465.4}{0.8} = 581.7\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 4 — Choose next standard generator rating.** Standard marine DG sets are available at **500 kW, 625 kW, 750 kW, 1000 kW**. Selecting **$P_{gen} = 625\ \text{kW}$**:

$$N_{gen} = \left\lceil \dfrac{581.7}{625} \right\rceil + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2$$

**Step 5 — Verify with the (n-1) rule.** With one generator out, the remaining 625 kW unit must feed the entire ship including the bow-thruster starting transient. We check that the **preferential trip** sheds the bow-thruster if the running load exceeds ~80% of one generator — this is implemented automatically by the power-management system (PMS).

$$\boxed{N_{gen} = 2 \text{ (operating)} + 1 \text{ (standby)} = 3\ \text{ generators total}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Cable Sizing for a Shipboard Feeder

**Step 1 — Determine the design current** $I_b$ of the feeder:

$$I_b = \frac{P_{motor}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta_{motor}}$$

For non-motor loads:

$$I_b = \frac{P_{load}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi}$$

**Step 2 — Apply correction factors (IEC 60092-352):**

$$I_z \ge \frac{I_b}{K_1 \cdot K_2 \cdot K_3 \cdot K_4}$$

where:
- $K_1$ = ambient-temperature factor (45 °C engine room → 0.87)
- $K_2$ = grouping factor (multiple cables in same tray)
- $K_3$ = thermal-insulation factor
- $K_4$ = direct sunlight / sheath factor (deck)

**Step 3 — Select cable cross-section** $A$ such that its current-carrying capacity $I_z(A) \ge I_b / (K_1 K_2 K_3 K_4)$.

**Step 4 — Verify voltage drop** using Eq. (5) in §2.3: $\Delta V\% \le 5\%$ steady, $\le 10\%$ motor starting.

**Step 5 — Verify short-circuit withstand** — the cable's $I^2t$ characteristic must exceed the let-through energy of the upstream breaker.

$$t_{sc} \le \frac{K^2 A^2}{I_{sc,\text{peak}}^2} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{\theta_{f} + \beta}{\theta_{i} + \beta}\right)$$

(K = 143 for Cu-XLPE, $\beta = 234.5$ for Cu, $\theta_f = 250 °C$ final, $\theta_i = 90 °C$ initial).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Typical One-Line Diagram of a Ship

| Element | Symbol | Typical Rating | Location |
|---------|--------|----------------|----------|
| DG-1, DG-2, DG-3 | Synchronous alternator | 625 kW, 440 V, 60 Hz, 3-phase | Engine Room (ER) |
| Main Switchboard (MSB) | 3-phase + N + E bus-bar | 1600 A, 50 kA short-time | Engine Control Room (ECR) |
| Bus-Tie Breaker (BTB) | ACB, withdrawable | 1600 A, 65 kA | Centre of MSB |
| Emergency Generator (EDG) | Diesel + alternator | 125 kW, 440 V | Above bulkhead deck (deck 4) |
| Emergency Switchboard (ESB) | Bus-bar with ATS | 400 A | Emergency Generator Room |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ BEFORE ANSWERING**
> Students commonly lose 2-3 marks on this topic for the following reasons:
> 1. **Confusing the Main Switchboard (MSB) with the Emergency Switchboard (ESB).** These are **two separate, independent** switchboards. The ESB is **not** inside the MSB; it is in a separate room above the bulkhead deck.
> 2. **Forgetting the (n-1) redundancy rule.** The vessel must be operable with the largest generator out of service. Always state this explicitly.
> 3. **Not mentioning the 45-second auto-start rule** for the Emergency Generator (SOLAS II-1 Reg. 43 / 44).
> 4. **Omitting the shore-power interlock** — it must be interlocked with the MSB so that shore power and the ship's generators cannot parallel-feed.
> 5. **Ignoring preferential trip logic** — students often forget the load-shedding sequence.
> 6. **Drawing single-line diagrams without bus-tie breaker (BTB) and ATS** — examiners award marks for the **complete SLD**, not just the components.

---

### PART-A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define the term "Main Switchboard (MSB)" in a shipboard electrical system. List any four essential components mounted on it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Main Switchboard (MSB)** is the principal distribution assembly in a ship that receives power from all main generators and distributes it to all large consumers, sub-boards, and essential services through a common set of 3-phase bus-bars. It is typically located in the **Engine Control Room (ECR)**, segregated by arc-fault-resistant enclosures, drip-proof and front-and-rear access.
>
> **Four essential components (½ mark each):**
> 1. **Generator Circuit Breakers (ACBs)** — one per main generator, with short-time and instantaneous protections.
> 2. **Bus-Tie Breaker (BTB)** — allows paralleling generators and splitting the bus in two halves.
> 3. **Preferential Trip Relays** — shed non-essential loads on generator overload.
> 4. **Synchronising Panel** — with synchroscope, lamps, and auto-sync module.
> 5. (Bonus) Voltmeter, ammeter, kW meter, power-factor meter, and protection relays per generator.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** State the SOLAS requirement for the Emergency Generator's auto-start and the minimum duration of the emergency battery back-up. Why is the emergency generator located above the bulkhead deck?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Auto-start time:** The Emergency Diesel Generator must **automatically start** and reach rated voltage/frequency within **45 seconds** of a main-power failure (SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 44).
>
> 2. **Battery duration:** The emergency battery must supply emergency lighting for **at least 18 hours** and emergency consumers (e.g., general alarm, public address) for **at least 30 minutes** without recharging (SOLAS II-1/43).
>
> 3. **Location rationale:** The emergency generator is located above the **bulkhead deck** (usually on the boat deck or a dedicated deck house) so that it remains operational and un-flooded even if the main engine room is flooded, fire-damaged, or disabled. It is also a **"dead-ship" recovery** means — even with all main generators unavailable, the EDG can re-start the ship from blackout.

---

### PART-B QUESTIONS — ESE MODULE 2 — 14 MARKS (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]** With the help of a neat Single-Line Diagram, describe the **General Distribution Scheme Onboard a Ship**. Your answer must include: (a) Generation hierarchy, (b) Main and Emergency Switchboards, (c) Shore-Supply connection, (d) Sub-distribution and load categorisation. (7 + 7 = 14 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Generation Hierarchy and Main Distribution (7 marks)**

A merchant vessel's General Distribution Scheme is structured into four tiers:

**1. Tier 1 — Generation:**
The prime movers are 3 or 4 **Auxiliary Diesel Generators (ADG)** of identical rating, typically 440 V, 3-phase, 60 Hz. Each unit is a **4-stroke marine diesel engine** directly coupled to a **brushless 3-phase synchronous alternator** with **Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR)** and a **Governor** for frequency control. The class-approved rating follows the **(n-1) redundancy rule**: the ship must operate with the largest generator out of service.

**2. Tier 2 — Main Distribution (MSB):**
The MSB houses the main bus-bars rated for 1600 A (typical) at 440 V, 50 kA short-time withstand. Key elements:
- **Generator circuit breakers** (one per generator) — withdrawable air circuit breakers (ACBs) with long-time, short-time, instantaneous and earth-fault protections.
- **Bus-tie breaker (BTB)** — links the two halves of the MSB, allowing paralleling of generators or splitting for fault isolation.
- **Synchronising panel** — synchroscope, dark-lamp check, auto-sync.
- **Preferential (Non-Essential) Trip Relays** — shed non-vital loads (AC, galley) on generator overload, in a pre-programmed order.

**3. Sub-Distribution and Shore Supply:**
The MSB feeds:
- **Engine-Room Sub-board** → main engine auxiliaries, lube-oil/fuel-oil purifiers, compressors.
- **Bridge Sub-board** → navigation, communication, gyro, radar.
- **Deck-Machinery Sub-board** → winches, cranes, hatch-cover hydraulics.
- **Accommodation Sub-board** → lighting, HVAC, galley.
- **Shore-Supply Panel** (IEC 80005-3) — 200 A receptacle on the shell, with a **4-pole changeover switch** mechanically and electrically interlocked with the generator breakers to prevent parallel operation.

**(b) Emergency Switchboard, Load Categorisation, and Cabling (7 marks)**

**1. Emergency Switchboard (ESB):**
The ESB is in a **separate room**, above the bulkhead deck. It is fed from:
- **Emergency Diesel Generator (EDG)** — auto-starts within 45 s, located above the bulkhead deck.
- **Emergency Battery Bank** (Ni-Cd or lead-acid) — feeds DC loads and the EDG starter.
- The **Automatic Transfer Switch (ATS)** senses main-bus failure and switches emergency consumers to the EDG within the prescribed time.

**2. Load Categorisation (per SOLAS and Class rules):**
- **Vital/Essential:** Steering gear, fire-fighting pumps, navigation, communication, bilge pumps, main engine auxiliaries.
- **Important:** Fuel/lube pumps, cooling pumps, air compressors, ventilation for machinery spaces.
- **Non-Essential (Preferential):** AC, galley equipment, deck cranes, non-critical auxiliaries.
- **Emergency:** Emergency fire pump, emergency lighting, general alarm, watertight-door motors, public-address system.

**3. Cabling:**
- Power cables: **IEC 60092-353 XLPE/EPR**, halogen-free, flame-retardant, low-smoke (HF-FR-LS).
- Routing: along **cable trays** in dedicated **cable tunnels**, **≥ 200 mm** separation between power and signal cables (EMC zones per IEC 60533).
- Penetrations through watertight and fire-rated bulkheads use **fire-rated glands (A-60 / A-0 / H-60 rated)**.
- Cable cross-section is selected from current-carrying capacity, voltage drop ($\le 5\%$ steady), and short-circuit withstand ($I^2t$).

**4. Protective Coordination (Selectivity):**
Class rules mandate **full discrimination** between upstream and downstream breakers so that a downstream fault is cleared by the downstream breaker alone — the upstream breaker remains closed, ensuring no blackout propagates upstream.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]** A Handysize bulk carrier has the following load list. Design the **General Distribution Scheme**, including the number and rating of generators, the Main Switchboard rating, and the sizing of the cable feeding a 250 kW bow-thruster motor.
>
> | Load | Rating | Demand Factor |
> |------|--------|---------------|
> | Main engine auxiliaries | 90 kW | 0.85 |
> | Steering gear (2 × 25) | 50 kW | 0.7 |
> | Bow thruster | 250 kW | 0.6 |
> | Deck winches (2 × 80) | 160 kW | 0.4 |
> | HVAC, galley, lighting | 250 kW | 1.0 |
> | Navigation, communication | 50 kW | 0.9 |
>
> Use: $V_L = 440\ \text{V}$, $\cos\phi = 0.85$, $\eta_{motor} = 0.92$, cable length 80 m, voltage-drop limit 5 %, ambient 45 °C, grouping factor 0.85, XLPE cable.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Generator Sizing (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Demand calculation:**

$$P_{demand} = 90(0.85) + 50(0.7) + 250(0.6) + 160(0.4) + 250(1.0) + 50(0.9)$$
$$P_{demand} = 76.5 + 35 + 150 + 64 + 250 + 45 = 620.5\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 2 — Apply diversity factor** $K_{div} = 0.75$:

$$P_{max,demand} = 0.75 \times 620.5 = 465.4\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Class margin** $K_{margin} = 0.8$ (no DG > 80% MCR):

$$P_{per\;gen} \ge \dfrac{465.4}{0.8} = 581.7\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 4 — Select next standard DG rating.** Marine DGs come in standard sizes: 500, 625, 750, 1000 kW. Selecting **625 kW**:

$$N_{gen} = \left\lceil \frac{581.7}{625} \right\rceil + 1 = 2\ \text{running}\ +\ 1\ \text{standby} = 3\ \text{total}$$

**Step 5 — Verify (n-1) rule.** With 1 generator out, the remaining 625 kW unit must feed all running loads. The PMS trips the bow-thruster first (preferential trip), reducing instantaneous load to ~215 kW. ✔

**Main Switchboard rating:** The MSB bus must carry the combined output of two paralleled DGs:

$$I_{MSB} = \frac{2 \times 625 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 440 \times 0.85} = 965\ \text{A}$$

**Select standard MSB rating = 1600 A** (next standard size above 965 A), with **50 kA short-time withstand**.

---

**(b) Bow-Thruster Feeder Cable Sizing (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Design current** of the bow-thruster motor:

$$I_b = \frac{250 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 440 \times 0.85 \times 0.92} = \frac{250\,000}{593.7} = 421.0\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Apply correction factors** (ambient 45 °C → $K_1 = 0.87$, grouping $K_2 = 0.85$):

$$I_z \ge \frac{I_b}{K_1 K_2} = \frac{421.0}{0.87 \times 0.85} = 569.3\ \text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Select cable size.** From IEC 60092-353 table, XLPE 3-core cables:
- 3 × 95 mm² → $I_z = 380\ \text{A}$ (insufficient)
- 3 × 120 mm² → $I_z = 435\ \text{A}$ (insufficient)
- 3 × 150 mm² → $I_z = 495\ \text{A}$ (insufficient)
- 3 × 185 mm² → $I_z = 555\ \text{A}$ (close, insufficient)
- 3 × 240 mm² → $I_z = 645\ \text{A}$ ✔

**Step 4 — Voltage-drop verification** for 3 × 240 mm² XLPE cable ($R = 0.097\ \Omega/\text{km}$, $X = 0.080\ \Omega/\text{km}$):

$$R_c = 0.097 \times 0.08 = 0.00776\ \Omega,\quad X_c = 0.080 \times 0.08 = 0.0064\ \Omega$$

$$\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \times 421 \times (0.00776 \times 0.85 + 0.0064 \times 0.527)$$

$$\Delta V = \sqrt{3} \times 421 \times (0.00660 + 0.00337) = \sqrt{3} \times 421 \times 0.00997 = 7.27\ \text{V}$$

$$\Delta V\% = \frac{7.27}{440} \times 100 = 1.65\% \le 5\%\ \text{✔}$$

**Step 5 — Select feeder breaker:** Standardised at **630 A, 50 kA ACB** with adjustable long-time, short-time, and instantaneous trips for full discrimination with the MSB bus-tie.

**Final Design Summary:**
- **Generators:** 3 × 625 kW, 440 V, 60 Hz.
- **Main Switchboard:** 1600 A bus, 50 kA short-time.
- **Bow-Thruster Feeder:** 3 × 240 mm² XLPE, 630 A ACB, 1.65% voltage drop.
- **Emergency Generator:** 1 × 125 kW, 440 V, above bulkhead deck.
- **Emergency Battery:** Ni-Cd 24 V, 300 Ah.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Bullet Summary — General Distribution Scheme Onboard a Ship**
>
> ☐ **Definition:** The GDS is the complete electrical network — generation → main switchboard → sub-boards → final consumers — onboard a ship, governed by **IEC 60092**, **SOLAS II-1**, and **Class rules**.
>
> ☐ **Voltage levels:** LV (440 V / 230 V), HV (3.3 / 6.6 / 11 kV), DC (24 V / 110 V / 220 V). **Frequency:** 60 Hz (most merchant ships) or 50 Hz.
>
> ☐ **Generation:** 3-4 diesel-driven synchronous alternators, with **AVR** and **governor**, sized using the **(n-1) redundancy rule** and **80% MCR loading margin**.
>
> ☐ **Main Switchboard (MSB):** Contains generator ACBs, bus-tie breaker (BTB), synchronising panel, preferential trip relays, and power-management system (PMS).
>
> ☐ **Emergency Switchboard (ESB):** Separate board above bulkhead deck, fed by **Emergency Diesel Generator (auto-starts in 45 s)** and **Emergency Battery** (30 min emergency loads + 18 h emergency lighting).
>
> ☐ **Shore-Supply:** IEC 80005-3 receptacle, **4-pole changeover switch with mechanical and electrical interlock** to prevent parallel operation with the ship's generators.
>
> ☐ **Distribution topologies:** Radial (simple), Ring-loop (redundant), Dual-fed with ATS (vital services).
>
> ☐ **Load categories:** Vital / Important / Non-essential (preferential) / Emergency — with **automatic load-shedding** in reverse priority on generator overload.
>
> ☐ **Cabling:** XLPE/EPR, HF-FR-LS, IEC 60092-353, sized for current, voltage drop ($\le 5\%$), short-circuit ($I^2t$), and EMC zone separation ($\ge 200\ \text{mm}$).
>
> ☐ **Protection:** Selectivity (discrimination) between upstream and downstream breakers; **earth-fault** monitoring; **insulation monitoring** on IT systems; overcurrent, over/under-voltage, over/under-frequency, reverse-power, loss-of-excitation protections on alternators.
>
> ☐ **Preferential trip order:** AC → Galley → Deck machinery → Bow thruster. **Essential services are NEVER tripped.**
>
> ☐ **Critical Class rules:** SOLAS II-1 Reg. 40-44 (main & emergency power), IEC 60092 (full series), IEEE 45 (US builds), Lloyd's Register / DNV / ABS / BV / IRS Rules.
>
> ☐ **Voltage drop formula:** $\Delta V\% = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)}{V_L} \times 100$.
>
> ☐ **Generator sizing formula:** $N_{gen} = \lceil P_{max,demand} / (P_{gen} \cdot K_{margin}) \rceil + 1$.
>
> ☐ **Emergency location rationale:** Above bulkhead deck for **flood survival** and **dead-ship recovery**.
>
> ☐ **Key one-line diagram (SLD) must always show:** Generators, MSB, BTB, ATS, ESB, EDG, battery, shore-supply, sub-boards, and interconnections.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relevant Regulations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relevant Regulations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Relevant Regulations** in the context of shipboard and shipyard electrical engineering refer to the mandatory and advisory codes, conventions, rules, and standards issued by international maritime bodies, flag states, classification societies, and electrotechnical commissions that govern the **design, manufacture, installation, testing, operation, and maintenance** of all electrical and electronic systems aboard seagoing vessels and within shipyard facilities.

These regulations are not optional "guidelines" — they form the **legal and technical backbone** of maritime safety, environmental protection, and operational reliability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> For a Marine Electrical Engineer, "Relevant Regulations" is **not** about memorizing legal jargon. It is about understanding *which body controls what*, *which standard applies to which system*, and *how these documents translate into physical design choices* (e.g., IP rating, cable selection, generator redundancy, hazardous area zoning).

The principal regulatory layers are:

1. **International Convention Level** — IMO (SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW, ISM Code)
2. **Electrotechnical Standard Level** — IEC (especially **IEC 60092** series) and **IEEE 45**
3. **Classification Society Level** — Lloyd's Register (LR), DNV, Bureau Veritas (BV), ABS, IRS, ClassNK
4. **Flag State / Port State Level** — National maritime authorities (e.g., Indian DG Shipping, USCG, MCA-UK)
5. **Hazardous Area / Occupational Safety Level** — ATEX Directives (EU), IEC 60079 series, OSHA standards

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Maritime Legal Stack"

Think of a ship's electrical system like a **high-rise building's electrical system**, but with one crucial difference: the building is **moving, surrounded by salt water, unmanned for long periods, and filled with fuel**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Rule-Book Stack"**
> Imagine a self-driving car. Before it can hit the road, it must satisfy:
> 1. **International traffic law** (UN conventions) → equivalent to **SOLAS**
> 2. **Automotive engineering standards** (ISO, IEC, SAE) → equivalent to **IEC 60092**
> 3. **The vehicle inspector's passbook** (type approval) → equivalent to **Classification Society Rules**
> 4. **Country-specific driving license rules** (RTO in India) → equivalent to **Flag State Regulations**
> 5. **Petrol station fire safety rules** (handling flammable vapours) → equivalent to **ATEX/IEC 60079**
>
> A ship is similarly governed by a **vertical stack of overlapping rules**, each refining the layer above it.

## 1.3 Why Do These Regulations Exist? — The Three Pillars

| Pillar | Engineering Driver | Example Regulation |
|---|---|---|
| **Life Safety** | Fire, electrocution, black-out at sea | SOLAS Ch. II-1 Reg. 45 |
| **Environmental Protection** | Oil spill, ozone depletion, emission | MARPOL Annex VI |
| **Commercial Viability** | Insurance, chartering, port entry | Classification Society Rules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A vessel that does not comply with these regulations **cannot legally sail**, cannot be insured, and cannot call at most ports. For the marine electrical engineer, regulation literacy = employability.

## 1.4 Key Physical/Engineering Parameters Often Mandated by Regulations

- **System voltage & frequency** (typically **440 V / 60 Hz** or **380 V / 50 Hz**)
- **Emergency power duration** (minimum **36 hours** for cargo ships per SOLAS)
- **Cable fire-resistance rating** (e.g., **IEC 60332-3** for flame retardance)
- **Ingress Protection (IP) rating** for deck equipment (commonly **IP56 / IP66**)
- **Hazardous area zoning** (Zones 0, 1, 2 for gas; Zones 20, 21, 22 for dust)
- **Generator redundancy** (N+1 or N+2 depending on vessel class)
- **Insulation monitoring** threshold (typically **< 100 kΩ** for IT systems)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Regulatory Architecture — Who Controls What?

The maritime regulatory ecosystem operates on a **three-tier hierarchy**, often called the *IMO-Classification-Flag State triangle*.

### Tier 1 — International Maritime Organization (IMO)

The IMO is a **United Nations specialized agency** headquartered in London, UK. It is the *only* body that can issue **binding international conventions**.

**Key IMO Conventions relevant to electrical systems:**

- **SOLAS (International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea, 1974)** — the single most important document for a marine electrical engineer.
- **MARPOL (International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships, 1973/78)** — governs oil-containing electrical equipment (e.g., transformers, oil-filled switchgear).
- **STCW (International Convention on Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping for Seafarers)** — defines *who* is qualified to operate electrical systems.
- **ISM Code (International Safety Management Code)** — mandates company-level safety management systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Part D — Electrical Installations** is the **definitive section** for marine electrical engineers. KTU examiners frequently test this.

### SOLAS Chapter II-1 — Part D: Electrical Installations (Key Regulations)

| Reg. No. | Title | Core Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| **Reg. 40** | General | Electrical installations to be such that all auxiliary services essential for propulsion & safety are maintained |
| **Reg. 41** | Main Source of Electrical Power | Sufficient power for safe navigation under all conditions; **at least 2 generating sets** |
| **Reg. 42** | Emergency Source of Electrical Power | Self-contained, located above bulkhead deck; capable of **36 hours** operation |
| **Reg. 43** | Emergency Power for Passenger Ships | Stricter requirements — additional transitional source |
| **Reg. 44** | Starting of Emergency Generator | Cranking within **30 seconds** of main power failure |
| **Reg. 45** | Precautions against Shock, Fire & Hazards | Earthing, insulation, fire detection, gas hazards |

### SOLAS Chapter II-2 — Fire Protection (Related to Electrical)

- Reg. 9 — Containment of fire (cable penetrations, **fire-stopping**)
- Reg. 13 — Means of escape (emergency lighting)
- Reg. 19 — Carriage of fire-detection systems

## 2.2 Tier 2 — International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)

While IMO sets **"what must be achieved"**, the **IEC** defines **"how it is engineered"**. For shipboard electrical systems, the **IEC 60092 series** is the master reference.

### IEC 60092 Series — The Electrical Ship Bible

| Standard | Title | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **IEC 60092-101** | Definitions & General Requirements | Terminology, ambient conditions |
| **IEC 60092-201** | System Design — General | Voltage levels, system earthing (IT preferred) |
| **IEC 60092-202** | System Design — Protection | Discrimination, fault levels, selectivity |
| **IEC 60092-301** | Equipment — Generators & Motors | Temperature rise, cooling |
| **IEC 60092-302** | Equipment — Switchgear & Controlgear | LV switchboard, MCB/MCCB specs |
| **IEC 60092-303** | Equipment — Transformers | Dry-type preferred, oil-type marine rules |
| **IEC 60092-304** | Equipment — Semiconductor Convertors | Variable Frequency Drives (VFD) |
| **IEC 60092-350** to **376** | Cables | Construction, insulation, fire performance |
| **IEC 60092-401** | Installation & Test | Insulation resistance, hot-loop testing |
| **IEC 60092-501** | Electric Propulsion Plant | Integrated electric propulsion |
| **IEC 60092-502** | Tankers (Oil, Chemical, Gas) | Hazardous area zoning |
| **IEC 60092-503** | AC Supply Systems | Three-phase, single-phase, earthing |
| **IEC 60092-504** | Control & Instrumentation | Automation, alarm systems |
| **IEC 60092-506** | High Voltage Installations (1 kV–15 kV) | HV ship systems |

### Companion Standards

- **IEC 60331** — Fire-resisting cables (test method)
- **IEC 60332-3** — Flame-retardant cables (test method, categories A/B/C)
- **IEC 60529** — Ingress Protection (IP) codes
- **IEC 60079** — Explosive atmospheres (Zones 0, 1, 2)
- **IEEE 45** — US equivalent of IEC 60092 (often used for US-flagged vessels)

## 2.3 Tier 3 — Classification Societies

A classification society is a **non-governmental organization** that establishes and applies technical standards for the design, construction, and survey of marine vessels.

**Top-tier classification societies (IACS members):**

| Society | Abbreviation | Country | Notable Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lloyd's Register | **LR** | UK | Historical authority |
| DNV (formerly DNV GL) | **DNV** | Norway | Offshore, dynamic positioning |
| Bureau Veritas | **BV** | France | LNG carriers, gas |
| American Bureau of Shipping | **ABS** | USA | US Navy, offshore |
| Indian Register of Shipping | **IRS** | India | Indian flag vessels |
| ClassNK | **NK** | Japan | Commercial shipping |
| Korean Register | **KR** | South Korea | Shipbuilding hub |
| RINA | **RINA** | Italy | Naval architecture |
| China Classification Society | **CCS** | China | Chinese flag vessels |

> [!NOTE]
> **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** — an umbrella body. As of 2024, IACS has **12 member societies** and its Unified Requirements (URs) are de facto global standards.

### Key Classification Society "Rules" for Electrical Systems

Each society publishes its own *Rules & Regulations* volume for electrical installations. For example:
- **LR Rules, Volume 2, Part 6** — Electrical Installations
- **DNV Rules, Part 4, Chapter 8** — Electrical Installations
- **ABS Rules, Part 4, Chapter 3** — Electrical Systems

These rules are largely **harmonized** with IEC 60092 but may impose **stricter additional requirements** (e.g., shock-resistant switchgear, specific ambient derating factors).

## 2.4 Hazardous Area Regulations — The "Explosion Triangle"

On tankers, chemical carriers, and LNG vessels, electrical equipment can ignite flammable atmospheres. Regulations classify zones and mandate equipment type.

| Zone | Definition (Gas) | Equipment Allowed |
|---|---|---|
| **Zone 0** | Flammable atmosphere **continuously present** | **Ex ia** (intrinsically safe) |
| **Zone 1** | Flammable atmosphere **likely during normal operation** | **Ex d, Ex e, Ex ib, Ex p** |
| **Zone 2** | Flammable atmosphere **not likely, or short duration** | **Ex nA, Ex nR, Ex o** |

**Governing Standards:**
- **IEC 60079** series (international)
- **ATEX 2014/34/EU** (European Union Directive)
- **NEC Class I, Div 1 / Div 2** (North American system — still common on US-flagged vessels)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — The Reference Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> For a regulations topic, the "formulas" are predominantly **statutory limits, standard references, and threshold values**. This table is the **high-yield summary** for KTU exams.

| Parameter / Topic | Statutory Value | Source Regulation |
|---|---|---|
| Emergency generator autonomy | **≥ 36 hours** | SOLAS Ch. II-1 Reg. 42 |
| Emergency generator starting time | **≤ 30 seconds** | SOLAS Ch. II-1 Reg. 44 |
| Main supply voltage (LV) | **440 V, 3-phase, 60 Hz** or **380 V / 50 Hz** | IEC 60092-201 |
| Preferred earthing system (LV) | **IT (isolated neutral)** | IEC 60092-201 |
| Insulation resistance (min.) | **≥ 1 MΩ per kV** of rated voltage | IEC 60092-401 |
| Preferred cable insulation | **XLPE or EPR** (halogen-free) | IEC 60092-351 |
| Cable fire test | **IEC 60332-3-22 (Cat. A)** for power, **Cat. B** for control | IEC 60332-3 |
| IP rating for deck equipment | **IP56 minimum** | IEC 60092-201 / Class rules |
| Hazardous area — oil tanker deck | **Zone 1** within 3 m of openings | IEC 60092-502 / IEC 60079-10 |
| Battery type (emergency) | **Sealed lead-acid or NiCd** (no vented except in dedicated compartment) | SOLAS Reg. 45 |
| Periodic survey interval | **5 years** (Special Survey) | SOLAS / IACS UR Z17 |
| Power supply for navigation | **Independent from main, with battery backup** | SOLAS Ch. V Reg. 17 |
| IACS member count | **12 societies** | IACS Constitution |
| Maximum transient overvoltage | **≤ 1.5 × V_rated** (or class-specific) | IEC 60092-506 for HV |
| Navigation light voltage variation | **−10% to +6%** of rated | SOLAS Ch. V Reg. 17, COLREGs |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

A marine electrical engineer using these regulations will:

1. **Select a generator** with redundancy meeting SOLAS Reg. 41.
2. **Choose a cable** with XLPE insulation and IEC 60332-3-22 fire rating per IEC 60092-353.
3. **Specify an Ex d motor** for a pump located in a Zone 1 area per IEC 60079-1.
4. **Design emergency lighting** powered from a battery-backed distribution board per SOLAS Reg. 42 + 43.
5. **Apply derating factors** for ambient temperature (typically **+45°C** ambient on tankers) per IEC 60092-352.
6. **Plan cable routes** through fire-rated transit (A-class / F-class divisions) per SOLAS Ch. II-2 Reg. 9.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Application Logic

## 3.1 The "Top-Down" Application Logic — A Worked Walkthrough

In real ship design, regulations are applied in this strict sequence:

**Step 1 — Identify the Vessel Type**
Different rules apply to:
- Cargo ship (bulk carrier, container, tanker)
- Passenger ship (ferry, cruise)
- Specialized vessel (LNG carrier, FPSO, naval, yacht)

*Logic:* SOLAS differentiates passenger vs. cargo at the very first article. The emergency power rules in **Reg. 42** (cargo) vs. **Reg. 43** (passenger) are different.

**Step 2 — Determine the Flag State**
The vessel's flag state enforces the convention. For an **Indian-flagged** vessel → DG Shipping implements SOLAS. For a **Panama-flagged** vessel → Panama implements SOLAS. The convention text is the same, but enforcement differs.

**Step 3 — Identify the Classification Society**
The owner contracts a classification society (e.g., IRS for an Indian operator). That society's *Rules* (not SOLAS) become the day-to-day working document for the shipyard.

**Step 4 — Apply the "Layer Cake" of Standards**

$$
\text{Final Design Specification} = \text{IMO Convention} \cap \text{Class Rules} \cap \text{Flag State} \cap \text{IEC Standards}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify Through Surveys**

Classification societies conduct surveys in this sequence:
1. **Plan Approval** (design stage)
2. **Survey During Construction (SDC)**
3. **Keel Laying Survey**
4. **Hull & Machinery Surveys (H&M)**
5. **Annual Survey**
6. **Special Survey** (every **5 years**)
7. **Continuous Survey** (alternative to annual, by zone)

## 3.2 Worked Example: Selecting an Emergency Power Source

**Problem Statement:** You are designing the electrical system for a **15,000 GT general cargo ship**. Specify the emergency power source as per SOLAS.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Apply SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 42**

> *The emergency source of electrical power shall be a generator driven by a suitable prime mover... located above the uppermost continuous deck... capable of operating for **36 hours**.*

**Step 2 — Determine the Required Power Load**

SOLAS Reg. 42 lists mandatory emergency consumers:

$$
P_{\text{emergency}} = P_{\text{nav lights}} + P_{\text{emergency lighting}} + P_{\text{fire pumps}} + P_{\text{comm. + alarm}} + P_{\text{steering gear (transient)}}
$$

Typical emergency load for a 15,000 GT cargo ship: **~200–400 kW** (3-phase, **450 V, 60 Hz**).

**Step 3 — Specify the Generator**
- **Type:** Self-excited, self-regulated, diesel-driven
- **Location:** Above the bulkhead deck, in a readily accessible space
- **Autonomy:** **36 hours** at full rated load
- **Fuel tank capacity:** Sized to give 36 h operation (typically a **day tank** + reserve)

**Step 4 — Specify the Starting System (Reg. 44)**
- **Starting source:** Stored energy — battery, compressed air, or hydraulic
- **Starting time:** Three successive starts within **30 minutes**, with two of them within **30 seconds**
- **Battery type:** Lead-acid or NiCd, charged by emergency switchboard

**Step 5 — Specify Emergency Switchboard Location**
- Adjacent to the emergency generator
- Above the bulkhead deck
- Supplied from the emergency generator via short, protected feeders

**Step 6 — Verify with Class Rules (e.g., LR Vol. 2, Part 6, Ch. 4)**
- Add: transient voltage dip, sustained short-circuit current, **discrimination** with main board
- Add: emergency lighting at every muster station, embarkation ladder, alleyway

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{emerg,min}} = 200 \text{ kW (typical)}, \quad t_{\text{autonomy}} = 36 \text{ h}, \quad t_{\text{start}} \leq 30 \text{ s}}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example: Cable Selection for a Hazardous Area

**Problem Statement:** A pump motor located on the **main deck of an oil tanker**, 2 m from a tank opening, must be supplied with a 3-phase, 440 V, 75 kW supply. Select the motor and cable type per regulation.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Determine Hazardous Zone**
Per **IEC 60092-502** and **IEC 60079-10**, the area within **3 m** of a tank opening is classified as **Zone 1**.

**Step 2 — Select Motor Protection Type**
For Zone 1, permitted types per **IEC 60079**:
- **Ex d** — Flameproof enclosure
- **Ex e** — Increased safety
- **Ex de** — Combined

→ **Choose Ex d** (most common for shipboard pumps).

**Step 3 — Select Cable Type**
Per **IEC 60092-353** and **IEC 60079-14**:

$$
\text{Cable} = \text{EPR or XLPE insulated, CSP (chlorosulphonated polyethylene) sheathed, armored (SWA or braid)}
$$

| Property | Specification | Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Conductor | **Class 2 stranded Cu** | IEC 60228 |
| Insulation | **XLPE** | IEC 60092-351 |
| Inner sheath | **EPR or CSP** | IEC 60092-360 |
| Armor | **Galvanized steel wire braid** | IEC 60092-353 |
| Outer sheath | **CSP / SHF2 (halogen-free)** | IEC 60092-360 |
| Fire rating | **IEC 60332-3-22 Cat. A** | IEC 60332-3 |
| Low smoke | **IEC 61034-2** | IEC 61034 |

**Step 4 — Apply Derating**
For an ambient of **+45°C** (tanker) and grouping factor, derate the cable current:

$$
I_{z} = I_{r} \cdot k_{1} \cdot k_{2} \cdot k_{3}
$$

where $k_{1}$ = ambient temperature factor, $k_{2}$ = grouping factor, $k_{3}$ = method of installation factor.

**Step 5 — Verify Segregation**
Per **IEC 60092-352**, segregated runs for intrinsically safe and non-IS circuits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** In a Part B 14-mark question, the examiner expects you to **name the regulation, the standard, and the technical consequence** — not just recall a number. Always write *"As per SOLAS Ch. II-1 Reg. 42..."* not just *"36 hours."*

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Regulatory Documents

| Question Asked | SOLAS | IEC 60092 | Class Rules | ATEX |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Is the rule legally binding? | ✅ Yes (Convention) | ❌ Voluntary (Standard) | ⚠️ Contractual | ✅ Yes (EU Law) |
| Does it apply to all flag states? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (adopted) | ❌ Only to classed vessels | ❌ Only in EU |
| Defines *what* to achieve? | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ⚠️ Partial |
| Defines *how* to engineer? | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Covers hazardous area zoning? | ⚠️ Refers to codes | ✅ IEC 60092-502 | ✅ Mirrors IEC | ✅ Equipment only |
| Specifies cable construction? | ❌ | ✅ IEC 60092-350 to 376 | ✅ Same | ❌ |
| Specifies emergency power duration? | ✅ 36 h | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |

## 3.5 The "Rule of Tighter Wins"

When two regulations apply, the **more stringent** rule always prevails. This is a **Class Society requirement** explicitly written into most rules (e.g., LR Rules General, Ch. 1).

$$
R_{\text{applicable}} = \max(R_{\text{SOLAS}}, R_{\text{IEC}}, R_{\text{Class}}, R_{\text{Flag State}})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Trap:** A common student error is to assume that "if SOLAS says 36 h, that's the answer." But the **Class Rules** may require **40 h** for certain vessel types, or the **Flag State** may impose **48 h** for a specific route. Always check **all four layers** before finalizing.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Maritime Regulatory Framework — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef intl fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef elect fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef class fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef flag fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef haz fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    A[IMO Conventions]:::intl
    A --> A1[SOLAS - Ch II-1 Part D - Electrical]:::intl
    A --> A2[MARPOL Annex VI - Pollution]:::intl
    A --> A3[STCW - Crew Competency]:::intl
    A --> A4[ISM Code - Safety Mgmt]:::intl

    B[IEC Standards]:::elect
    B --> B1[IEC 60092 Series - Shipboard Electrical]:::elect
    B --> B2[IEC 60332 - Cable Fire Tests]:::elect
    B --> B3[IEC 60529 - IP Codes]:::elect
    B --> B4[IEEE 45 - US Equivalent]:::elect

    C[Classification Societies - IACS]:::class
    C --> C1[Lloyd's Register - LR]:::class
    C --> C2[DNV]:::class
    C --> C3[Bureau Veritas - BV]:::class
    C --> C4[ABS]:::class
    C --> C5[Indian Register of Shipping - IRS]:::class
    C --> C6[ClassNK, RINA, KR, CCS, etc]:::class

    D[Flag State Authorities]:::flag
    D --> D1[India - DG Shipping]:::flag
    D --> D2[UK - MCA]:::flag
    D --> D3[Panama - AMP]:::flag
    D --> D4[Liberia, Marshall Islands, etc]:::flag

    E[Hazardous Area Standards]:::haz
    E --> E1[ATEX 2014/34/EU - EU Directive]:::haz
    E --> E2[IEC 60079 Series - Explosive Atmospheres]:::haz
    E --> E3[NEC Class I Div 1 and 2 - US System]:::haz

    F[Vessel Design Output]:::intl
    A --> F
    B --> F
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> F1[Approved Drawings]
    F --> F2[Class Certificate]
    F --> F3[SOLAS Safe Manning Doc]
    F --> F4[Flag State Authorization]
```

## 4.2 SOLAS Chapter II-1 Part D — Regulation Map

