# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Ship Blackout" as per SOLAS regulations. List the four major categories of services that lose power during a total blackout.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** As per **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 43**, a *ship blackout* is a condition wherein the **main source of electrical power** fails to supply the **essential services** of the ship, leading to total loss of propulsion, navigation, and safety-critical electrical systems.

> **Four Categories of Services Lost (1 mark — ¼ mark each):**
> 1. **Propulsion services** — Main engine / shaftline / thruster motors
> 2. **Navigation & communication services** — RADAR, ECDIS, VHF, GMDSS
> 3. **Auxiliary machinery services** — Pumps, compressors, HVAC
> 4. **Habitability services** — Lighting, galley, freshwater, sanitation

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*State the SOLAS-mandated time limit for the emergency generator to become available after a blackout. Mention any two consumers that must be powered within 3 seconds.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Time Limit (2 marks):** Per **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Reg. 43.2.2**, the emergency generator must be capable of supplying the emergency services within **45 seconds** of a blackout.

> **Two Consumers Powered within 3 seconds (1 mark — ½ mark each):**
> 1. **Bridge emergency lighting** (battery-backed transitional supply)
> 2. **General alarm system and fire detection panel** (battery-backed)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 2, Q5]**

> **(a)** Describe in detail the **automatic actions** that occur in a ship's electrical system when a complete blackout is detected, starting from the moment the MSB undervoltage relay trips. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** As the **Chief Engineer on watch**, explain the **step-by-step manual actions** you would undertake to restore main power to the ship after the emergency generator has stabilized the essential services. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q5(a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Detection (1 mark)**
The **MSB undervoltage relay** detects that the phase-to-neutral voltage has fallen below **$0.85 \times U_n$** (i.e., below 215.9 V for a 440V system) for a sustained duration of **1.0–1.5 seconds**. This confirms a blackout, not a transient dip.

**Step 2: Alarm Generation (1 mark)**
A **general alarm** is sounded in the engine room, bridge, and accommodation. The **engineer's telegraph** and **power management system (PMS)** display indicate loss of all generators.

**Step 3: Emergency Generator Start Sequence (2 marks)**
- The MSB undervoltage relay sends a **start command** to the **Emergency Generator (EG)**.
- The EG performs **3 automatic starting attempts** at **10-second intervals** (per Class requirement).
- Each start attempt lasts approximately **10–15 seconds**.

**Step 4: Emergency Switchboard Energization (1.5 marks)**
- Upon successful start, the **EG breaker** automatically closes onto the **Emergency Switchboard (ESB)**.
- **Transitional loads** (bridge emergency lighting, GMDSS, fire detection) are powered **instantaneously** from the **battery bank** for the first 30 minutes.
- Within **45 seconds**, all emergency consumers are powered: emergency lighting, fire pumps, navigation lights, VHF radio, steering gear.

**Step 5: Load Shedding and Isolation (1.5 marks)**
- The **non-essential consumers** on the MSB remain disconnected (dead bus).
- The **main generator breakers** are locked out to prevent back-feed.
- The PMS prevents **auto-reclosing** until manual synchronization is performed.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q5(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Initial Assessment (1 mark)**
Don **EEBD (Emergency Escape Breathing Device)** if smoke is suspected. Verify personal safety. Put on appropriate **PPE** (helmet, safety shoes, gloves). Proceed to MSB.

**Step 2: Inform the Bridge (0.5 marks)**
Use the **engine order telegraph / telephone** to inform the Master: *"Blackout in progress, emergency generator is supplying, investigating cause. Will keep you informed."*

**Step 3: Visual Inspection of MSB (1.5 marks)**
- Check for **signs of fire, smoke, or burning smell** at the MSB.
- Look for **tripped breakers**, **blown fuses**, or **short-circuit indicators**.
- Verify **Governor, AVR, and Excitation** status on each generator.
- Check **fuel oil pressure** at the engine room manifold.

**Step 4: Identify and Isolate Fault (1.5 marks)**
Common root causes:
- **Reverse power trip** on the paralleled generator — reset and resynchronize.
- **Short circuit on a feeder** — isolate the affected section using the **section breaker**.
- **AVR failure** — manually adjust excitation or switch to **manual voltage regulation**.

**Step 5: Restart Auxiliary Generator (1.5 marks)**
- Start the **Auxiliary Diesel Generator** (port or starboard).
- Warm up for **5–10 minutes** at idle before applying load.
- Check **oil pressure, water temperature, frequency, voltage** — all within normal range.

**Step 6: Synchronize to Dead Bus (1 mark)**
Using the **manual synchronizing panel** (synchroscope + dark lamp method):
- Match **frequency** within ± 0.1 Hz
- Match **voltage** within ± 5%
- Match **phase sequence and angle**
- Close the breaker when synchroscope indicates **"in phase"**.

**Step 7: Load Transfer and Reporting (1 mark)**
- Gradually transfer essential loads from ESB to MSB (typically 10%, 25%, 50%, 100% over 15 minutes).
- Place EG in **standby mode**.
- Log the entire event in the **Engine Room Log Book** with timestamps, cause, and corrective action.
- Submit a written **incident report** to the Master and Class Society.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 — Module 2, Q6]**

> **(a)** Explain the **common causes of ship blackout** under the categories: mechanical, electrical, operational, and environmental. Give two examples for each category. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the **regulatory and statutory requirements** (SOLAS, Class Society, STCW) governing the engineer's response to a blackout situation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q6(a) — 7 marks:**

**Mechanical Causes (1.75 marks — 0.875 each):**
1. **Engine fuel supply failure** — Empty/closed fuel valve, fuel oil transfer pump failure, dirty fuel filters, fuel contamination.
2. **Lubricating oil pump failure** — Low LO pressure trip on the diesel engine.

**Electrical Causes (1.75 marks):**
1. **AVR (Automatic Voltage Regulator) failure** — Loss of excitation → undervoltage → blackout.
2. **Short circuit on MSB busbar** — Protective relays trip all generator breakers.

**Operational Causes (1.75 marks):**
1. **Wrong paralleling sequence** — Out-of-phase synchronizing causes massive circulating current → trip.
2. **Blackout-Preventer (BP) misconfigured** — BP fails to start standby generator in time.

**Environmental Causes (1.75 marks):**
1. **Heavy weather** — Shaft generator trip due to excessive propeller load variation.
2. **Fire or flooding in engine room** — Emergency shutdown of generators.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q6(b) — 7 marks:**

**SOLAS Requirements (3 marks):**
- **Chapter II-1, Regulation 43** — Emergency generator must start within **45 seconds** automatically.
- **Chapter II-1, Regulation 44** — Emergency generator must have its own fuel and cooling independent of main machinery.
- **Chapter II-1, Regulation 45** — Emergency switchboard must be located above the bulkhead deck and easily accessible.

**Class Society Rules (2 marks):**
- **DNV / Lloyd's Register / ABS** rules require:
  - Emergency generator **weekly testing** (running under load for 30 minutes).
  - **Dead ship recovery procedure** documented and tested annually.
  - **Load shedding** to prevent overloading of emergency generator.

**STCW Requirements (2 marks):**
- **Section A-VIII/2** — Engine room watchkeeping: Engineer must know blackout procedure and demonstrate competence in **emergency procedures**.
- **STCW Code Table A-III/1** — Competency in *operating emergency equipment and applying emergency procedures* is mandatory for Marine Engineering Officers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. **Common Mistake 1**: Writing "emergency generator starts in 45 seconds" — **WRONG**. The **45 seconds** is the time for **power to be available to consumers**, not just engine start. The actual cranking time is ~30 s; the remaining 15 s is for stabilization and breaker closing.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2**: Forgetting to mention **battery-backed transitional supply** for bridge lighting. Examiners specifically test this in Part A.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3**: Confusing **blackout** (loss of all generators) with **brownout** (voltage reduction under load). These are **different** conditions with **different** responses.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4**: In Part B, students often skip the **synchronization step** when restoring main power. The examiner awards 1–2 marks specifically for the **synchroscope/phase-matching** process.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5**: Failing to mention **regulatory references (SOLAS Reg. 43)** — examiners give "free" marks for citing the regulation number.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blackout Situation — One-Page Quick Reference**

✅ **Definition**: Total loss of main electrical power (SOLAS Reg. 43).

✅ **Detection Threshold**: Undervoltage relay trips at **$0.85 \times U_n$** for **1.0–1.5 s**.

✅ **Emergency Generator Start**: Must achieve power availability within **$\leq 45 \text{ seconds}$** (SOLAS).

✅ **Transitional Battery Backup**: **30 minutes** minimum for bridge emergency lighting, GMDSS, fire detection.

✅ **3-Second Loads**: Bridge emergency lighting, general alarm — powered by **battery**, not EG.

✅ **3 EG Start Attempts**: At **10-second intervals** (Class requirement).

✅ **Restoration Priority Order**:
1. Steering gear
2. LO pumps
3. Sea water cooling pumps
4. FO supply pumps
5. Compressed air system

✅ **Synchronization Criteria**:
- $\Delta f \leq 0.1 \text{ Hz}$
- $\Delta U \leq 5\%$
- Phase sequence matched
- Synchroscope at "in-phase" position

✅ **Logging Requirement**: Engine Room Log Book entry with **timestamp, cause, corrective action, signature**.

✅ **Incident Reporting**: If blackout > **30 min** → Class Society and Flag State notification mandatory.

✅ **Common Causes (Mnemonic — MEOE)**:
- **M**echanical (fuel, LO, governor)
- **E**lectrical (AVR, short circuit, reverse power)
- **O**perational (wrong paralleling, BP misconfig)
- **E**nvironmental (heavy weather, fire, flooding)

✅ **STCW Reference**: Section A-VIII/2 — Watchkeeping competence in emergency procedures.

✅ **SOLAS Reference**: Chapter II-1, Regulations 43, 44, 45.

✅ **IEC Reference**: IEC 60092-201 (Marine electrical installations — system design).

✅ **Key Personnel**: Chief Engineer (decision authority), Engine Room Watchkeeper (first responder), Master (notified authority).

✅ **Don't Forget**: **EEBD donning**, **PPE**, **bridge notification**, **logbook entry** — these are **mandatory** procedural steps examiners look for.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shore Supply
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shore Supply

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shore Supply (Shore Power / Cold Ironing):** Shore Supply is the external electrical power delivered to a ship while it is berthed at a port, substituting the operation of the ship's auxiliary diesel generators (commonly termed "Hotel Load Generators"). It is a vital, regulated interface covered under **IEC/IEEE 80005-1 (Utility Connections in Port)** and **IEC 80005-3 (High Voltage Shore Connection Systems)**.

The primary purpose of shore supply is to:
- Eliminate emissions of **NOx, SOx, CO₂, and Particulate Matter (PM)** in port areas.
- Reduce noise and vibration at berth.
- Comply with **IMO MARPOL Annex VI** and **EU Directive 2005/33/EC** (sulphur content limits in port).
- Enable crew maintenance of the main engines and auxiliaries during prolonged port stays.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Phone Charger Concept":** Imagine your smartphone battery is almost dead. You have two choices:
> 1. Use a portable power bank (analogous to running the ship's **diesel auxiliary generators** — noisy, polluting, but mobile).
> 2. Plug the phone into a **wall socket at home** (analogous to **Shore Supply** — silent, clean, energy-efficient, requires a physical cable).
>
> Shore Supply is the maritime equivalent of "plugging the ship into the city's power grid" through a heavy-duty marine connector at the pier. Just as the wall socket needs to match the phone's voltage (5V, 220V adapter), the shore supply must match the ship's **voltage, frequency, and phase sequence** — which makes standardization tricky across global ports.

### 1.3 Key Physical Standards & Metrics

- **Standard Frequencies:** **50 Hz** (Europe, India, most of Asia) vs **60 Hz** (USA, Japan, parts of South America). This is the biggest engineering challenge.
- **Standard Voltages (LV):** 380 V, 400 V, 440 V, 450 V, 690 V, 6.6 kV.
- **Standard Voltages (HV):** 11 kV (and above, typically 6.6 kV or 11 kV at 50/60 Hz).
- **Standard Connectors:** IEC 60309 (industrial plugs/sockets for LV), IEC 80005-3 standardized plug for HV.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Shore Supply System

The logical flow of a shore supply engagement can be broken into **seven structured steps**:

1. **Pre-Berthing Planning:** The ship's Chief Engineer and the Port Authority agree on voltage, frequency, and power demand *before* the vessel arrives.
2. **Cable Deployment:** The shore power cable reel is deployed from the shore-side cabinet (or the ship) to the ship's **shore connection box**, usually located on the main deck.
3. **Earthing & Bonding:** A dedicated **earth continuity conductor** is connected first — for safety against earth faults.
4. **Phase Sequence Verification:** A **phase-sequence indicator** confirms the shore phase sequence matches the ship's expected rotation (L1-L2-L3).
5. **Synchronization (for HV) or Direct Transfer (for LV):**
   - **HV systems:** The shore and ship busbars are *synchronized* (matching voltage, frequency, and phase angle) before paralleling.
   - **LV systems:** A "**Blackout-Transfer**" or "**Closed-Transition Transfer**" is performed — generators trip OFF, shore breaker closes, load shifts.
6. **Load Acceptance:** The shore power takes over the entire hotel load (lighting, galley, HVAC, reefer containers, navigation electronics).
7. **Monitoring & Disconnection:** Power quality, frequency, and voltage are continuously logged. On departure, the sequence is reversed.

### 2.2 Classification of Shore Supply Systems

| **Class** | **Voltage Level** | **Frequency** | **Typical Use** | **Standard** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Low Voltage (LV) | 380 V – 690 V | 50 / 60 Hz | Small vessels, yachts, navy auxiliaries | IEC 60309 |
| High Voltage (HV) | 6.6 kV / 11 kV | 50 / 60 Hz | Cruise ships, large container vessels | IEC 80005-3 |
| DC Shore Supply | 1000 V DC | — | Hybrid-electric ferries | IEC 80005-2 |

### 2.3 Why 50 Hz / 60 Hz is a Problem — The Frequency Conversion Challenge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A ship designed for 60 Hz (e.g., a US-built cruise ship) entering a 50 Hz European port cannot directly connect. Solutions:
>
> 1. **Dual-frequency ship design** — Generators and motors wound for 50/60 Hz.
> 2. **Static Frequency Converters (SFC)** — Onshore thyristor/IGBT-based converters (e.g., ABB Shore Connection).
> 3. **Rotary Frequency Converters** — Motor-Generator set at the port.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Three-Phase Real Power | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | Active power drawn by ship hotel load | **W (Watts)** |
| Three-Phase Reactive Power | $Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\phi$ | Reactive power (inductive/capacitive) | **VAR** |
| Three-Phase Apparent Power | $S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L$ | Total apparent power demand | **VA** |
| Power Factor | $\cos\phi = P / S$ | Ratio of real to apparent power | **dimensionless** |
| Line-to-Line Voltage | $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$ | Star-connected systems | **V** |
| Cable Current Capacity | $I = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi}$ | Cable sizing for shore connection | **A** |
| Synchronization Condition | $\Delta f \le 0.1$ Hz, $\Delta V \le 10\%$, $\Delta \phi \le 10^\circ$ | Paralleling criteria | **mixed** |
| Energy Saved (Annual) | $E_{saved} = P_{aux} \cdot t_{berth} \cdot (1 - \eta_{shore})$ | Compared to running auxiliaries | **kWh** |
| Cost of Conversion (CAPEX) | $C_{FC} = k \cdot S_{rated}$ | Static Frequency Converter | **USD** |

**⚠ CRITICAL LaTeX Note for Students:** When writing $\vert V_L \vert$ or absolute values, use `\vert` or `\mid` — never the raw pipe character `|`, which breaks markdown tables.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Shore supply reduces a single cruise ship's port emissions by approximately **30–40 tonnes of CO₂ per 24 hours** of berthing. In container ports like **Los Angeles (AMP)**, **Hamburg**, and **Rotterdam (Maasvlakte)**, shore power is mandatory for berthing privileges — making it an essential maritime infrastructure for the **decarbonization roadmap 2050** under IMO.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Examples

### 3.1 Derivation: Why $\sqrt{3}$ Appears in Three-Phase Power

For a balanced three-phase Y-connected (star) system, the line voltage $V_L$ and phase voltage $V_{ph}$ are related by phasor addition:

$$V_L = V_{ph1} - V_{ph2}$$

Magnitude-wise, since the three phase voltages are $120^\circ$ apart, the phasor difference yields:

$$V_L = 2 \cdot V_{ph} \cdot \cos(30^\circ)$$

Evaluating $\cos(30^\circ) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$:

$$V_L = 2 \cdot V_{ph} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$$

For power per phase: $P_{ph} = V_{ph} \cdot I_{ph} \cdot \cos\phi$. Since $I_L = I_{ph}$ in a star system and the total power is three times the per-phase value:

$$P_{total} = 3 \cdot V_{ph} \cdot I_{ph} \cdot \cos\phi = 3 \cdot \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$$

$$\boxed{P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi}$$

This is the **fundamental three-phase active power equation** used in every shore-supply load calculation.

### 3.2 Numerical Example 1: LV Shore Supply Cable Sizing

> **Problem:** A berthed cargo ship requires shore supply of **$P = 1200 \text{ kW}$** at **$V_L = 440 \text{ V}$**, **$f = 60 \text{ Hz}$**, and **$\cos\phi = 0.85$ lagging**. Calculate:
> (i) Line current
> (ii) Apparent power
> (iii) Reactive power
> (iv) Minimum cable cross-section if current density is $4 \text{ A/mm}^2$.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step (i) — Line Current:**
$$I_L = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi}$$

Substituting $P = 1{,}200{,}000 \text{ W}$, $V_L = 440 \text{ V}$, $\cos\phi = 0.85$:

$$I_L = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 440 \cdot 0.85}$$

Computing the denominator:
$$\sqrt{3} \cdot 440 \cdot 0.85 = 1.732 \cdot 440 \cdot 0.85 = 647.85 \text{ V}\cdot\text{A units}$$

$$I_L = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{647.85} \approx 1852.2 \text{ A}$$

**Step (ii) — Apparent Power:**
$$S = \frac{P}{\cos\phi} = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{0.85} \approx 1{,}411{,}765 \text{ VA} \approx 1.41 \text{ MVA}$$

**Step (iii) — Reactive Power:**
$$Q = \sqrt{S^2 - P^2}$$

$$S^2 = (1{,}411{,}765)^2 = 1.993 \times 10^{12}$$
$$P^2 = (1{,}200{,}000)^2 = 1.44 \times 10^{12}$$
$$Q = \sqrt{(1.993 - 1.44) \times 10^{12}} = \sqrt{0.553 \times 10^{12}} \approx 743{,}600 \text{ VAR} \approx 744 \text{ kVAR}$$

Alternatively, directly: $Q = S \sin\phi = 1{,}411{,}765 \cdot \sin(\cos^{-1}(0.85)) = 1{,}411{,}765 \cdot 0.527 \approx 744 \text{ kVAR}$.

**Step (iv) — Minimum Cable Cross-Section:**
$$A = \frac{I_L}{J} = \frac{1852.2}{4} \approx 463 \text{ mm}^2 \text{ per phase}$$

**Selection:** Use a standard **$500 \text{ mm}^2$** marine-grade XLPE cable per phase (with derating factors for ambient temperature, grouping, and soil/duct installation as per IEC 60092).

---

### 3.3 Numerical Example 2: High-Voltage Shore Connection (HVSC) — Frequency Conversion

> **Problem:** A 60 Hz cruise liner berths at a European 50 Hz port. The port's Static Frequency Converter (SFC) is rated at **$S_{rated} = 8 \text{ MVA}$**, and the ship requires **$P = 6 \text{ MW}$** at **$\cos\phi = 0.9$**. Determine:
> (i) Whether the SFC capacity is sufficient.
> (ii) The SFC efficiency if it consumes 200 kW internally.
> (iii) The ship's demand on the 50 Hz grid side.

**Solution:**

**Step (i):** Compute ship's apparent power requirement:
$$S_{ship} = \frac{P}{\cos\phi} = \frac{6{,}000{,}000}{0.9} = 6.67 \text{ MVA}$$

Since $S_{ship} = 6.67 \text{ MVA} < S_{rated} = 8 \text{ MVA}$, the converter capacity is **sufficient** (loading = $83.4\%$).

**Step (ii):** Efficiency of SFC:
$$\eta_{SFC} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss}} = \frac{6000}{6000 + 200} = \frac{6000}{6200} = 0.9677$$

So $\eta_{SFC} \approx 96.77\%$.

**Step (iii):** Power drawn from the 50 Hz grid side:
$$P_{grid} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta_{SFC}} = \frac{6000}{0.9677} \approx 6200 \text{ kW}$$

This 200 kW extra is the converter's loss — typically dissipated as heat and requires forced cooling at the converter substation.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Synchronization Criteria for HV Shore Connection

| **Parameter** | **Limit** | **Consequence if Violated** |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency difference ($\Delta f$) | $\le 0.1$ Hz | Inrush currents, mechanical stress on shaft |
| Voltage difference ($\Delta V$) | $\le 10\%$ of nominal | Circulating reactive currents |
| Phase angle ($\Delta \phi$) | $\le 10^\circ$ | Transient torque, blackout |
| Phase sequence | L1-L2-L3 must match | Reverse rotation of motors → catastrophic |
| Earth continuity | $< 1 \text{ }\Omega$ | Personnel safety hazard |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Full Shore Supply Architecture (LV System)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    gridA[Port 50/60 Hz Grid] --> stepA[Step Down Transformer]
    stepA --> stepB[Main Circuit Breaker MCB]
    stepB --> stepC[Phase Sequence Indicator]
    stepC --> stepD[Shore Connection Socket IEC 60309]
    stepD --> stepE[Shore Power Cable Flexible Marine Grade]
    stepE --> stepF[Ship Shore Connection Box]
    stepF --> stepG[Ship Main Switchboard MSB]
    stepG --> stepH[Hotel Load Distribution]
    stepH --> stepI[Lighting HVAC Galley Reefer Navigation]
    stepG -.Sync Check.-> stepJ[Synchronizer Panel]
    stepJ -.Generator Trip.-> stepK[Aux Diesel Generators OFF]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — High-Voltage Shore Connection (HVSC) with Frequency Conversion

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    gridB[Port HV Grid 11 kV 50 Hz] --> trafoB[Step Down to Converter Input]
    trafoB --> rectB[Rectifier Stage 6 Pulse or 12 Pulse]
    rectB --> dcLinkB[DC Link Capacitor Bank]
    dcLinkB --> invB[Inverter Stage IGBT PWM]
    invB --> filterB[LC Filter Sinusoidal Output]
    filterB --> syncB[Sync Panel for 60 Hz Ship]
    syncB --> breakerB[HV Circuit Breaker 11 kV]
    breakerB --> cableB[HV Flexible Cable IEC 80005-3]
    cableB --> shipB[Ship HV Switchboard 11 kV 60 Hz]
    shipB --> thrusterB[Propulsion Aux Transformers]
    shipB --> hotelB[Hotel Load Stepped Down to 440 V 60 Hz]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow — When to Use LV vs HV Shore Supply

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA{Vessel Power Demand}
    startA -->|Less than 1 MVA| lvA[Use LV Shore Supply 440 V 690 V]
    startA -->|Between 1 and 5 MVA| hybridA[Use LV with Multiple Cables]
    startA -->|Greater than 5 MVA| hvA[Use HV Shore Supply 6.6 kV 11 kV]
    lvA --> checkA{Frequency Match}
    hvA --> checkB{Frequency Match}
    checkA -->|Yes| directA[Direct Connection via IEC 60309]
    checkA -->|No| sfcA[Use Static Frequency Converter LV]
    checkB -->|Yes| directB[Direct Connection via IEC 80005-3]
    checkB -->|No| sfcB[Use Static Frequency Converter HV]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Safety Interlock Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    plugA[Plug Inserted] --> earthA[Earth Pin Connects First]
    earthA --> pilotA[Pilot Contact Closes]
    pilotA --> cbA[Shore CB Allowed to Close]
    cbA --> syncA[Sync Verifier Checks Delta f Delta V Delta phi]
    syncA --> shipA[Ship Generators Trip]
    shipA --> readyA[Load Transfer to Shore]
    readyA --> monitorA[Continuous Monitoring Active]
    monitorA --> faultA{Fault Detected}
    faultA -->|Yes| tripA[Instantaneous Trip both ends]
    faultA -->|No| monitorA
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Q1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1. Define shore supply. Mention any two advantages.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> Shore supply is the provision of electrical power from an onshore source to a berthed ship, replacing the ship's auxiliary diesel generators during port stay.
> **Advantages:**
> (i) Reduction in port air pollution (NOx, SOx, CO₂).
> (ii) Lower noise and vibration, improving port environment and crew working conditions.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Q2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2. What is the difference between LV and HV shore supply?**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> | **Aspect** | **LV Shore Supply** | **HV Shore Supply** |
> |---|---|---|
> | Voltage | 380–690 V | 6.6 kV / 11 kV |
> | Standard | IEC 60309 | IEC 80005-3 |
> | Application | Small vessels, yachts | Cruise ships, large container vessels |
> | Power transfer | Direct or via SFC | Via Static Frequency Converter |
> | Cable size | Thick, multiple cores | Thinner, fewer conductors |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern]**
> *Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the complete architecture of a **High-Voltage Shore Connection (HVSC)** system for a 60 Hz cruise ship berthing at a 50 Hz European port. Label all major components and standards.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A vessel requires **$P = 4.5 \text{ MVA}$** at **$11 \text{ kV}$**, **$60 \text{ Hz}$**, **$\cos\phi = 0.92$**. Calculate the line current, active power in kW, and minimum cable size if current density is $3 \text{ A/mm}^2$.

**Model Solution to (a):**

The HVSC system consists of:
1. **Port Grid Input (11 kV, 50 Hz):** Source from the utility.
2. **Step-Down Transformer:** Reduces 11 kV to converter input level (e.g., 6.6 kV or directly to converter DC link).
3. **Static Frequency Converter (SFC):** A two-stage power electronic system:
   - **Rectifier stage:** Converts 50 Hz AC → DC using thyristors or IGBTs (often 12-pulse for low harmonic distortion).
   - **DC Link:** Capacitor bank smooths the DC voltage.
   - **Inverter stage:** Converts DC → 60 Hz AC using **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)**.
4. **Output Filter (LC):** Removes switching harmonics to produce clean 60 Hz sine wave.
5. **Synchronization Panel:** Compares voltage, frequency, and phase of SFC output with ship's switchboard.
6. **HV Circuit Breaker:** Makes/breaks the HV connection (per IEC 62271).
7. **HV Flexible Cable (per IEC 80005-3):** Specialized marine connector with pilot contacts and earth continuity.
8. **Ship's HV Switchboard:** Receives 60 Hz power, distributes to propulsion transformers and hotel load.
9. **Earthing System:** Earth-fault monitoring and equipotential bonding per IEC 80005-3.

**Advantages of HVSC:** Smaller cable cross-section for the same power, fewer cables, reduced voltage drop over long distances.

**Model Solution to (b):**

Given: $S = 4.5 \text{ MVA}$, $V_L = 11{,}000 \text{ V}$, $\cos\phi = 0.92$, $J = 3 \text{ A/mm}^2$.

**Line Current:**
$$I_L = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 11{,}000}$$

$$\sqrt{3} \cdot 11{,}000 = 19{,}052.6$$
$$I_L = \frac{4{,}500{,}000}{19{,}052.6} \approx 236.2 \text{ A}$$

**Active Power:**
$$P = S \cdot \cos\phi = 4.5 \times 0.92 = 4.14 \text{ MW} = 4140 \text{ kW}$$

**Minimum Cable Cross-Section:**
$$A = \frac{I_L}{J} = \frac{236.2}{3} \approx 78.7 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Selected:** Use a standard **$95 \text{ mm}^2$** HV shore cable per phase (with proper derating for grouping and ambient temperature per IEC 60092-352).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **frequency incompatibility problem** in shore supply and explain the working of a **Static Frequency Converter (SFC)** with the help of a block diagram. Mention the role of the DC link.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A ship draws **$P = 2.5 \text{ MW}$** from shore supply at **$440 \text{ V}$**, **$60 \text{ Hz}$**, **$\cos\phi = 0.88$**. The shore grid is 50 Hz. If the SFC has an efficiency of $96\%$, calculate (i) the apparent power drawn, (ii) the reactive power, and (iii) the input power drawn from the 50 Hz grid.

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Frequency Incompatibility:** Global ships and ports operate at either 50 Hz or 60 Hz. A 60 Hz ship cannot directly connect to a 50 Hz shore grid because:
- All AC motors on the ship will run at $\frac{50}{60} = 83.3\%$ of rated speed.
- Transformers will saturate (60 Hz design has lower flux density tolerance; operating at 50 Hz increases flux by $20\%$, leading to core saturation and overheating).
- Auxiliary pumps and fans will lose performance.

**Static Frequency Converter (SFC) Working — Block Diagram Logic:**

$$ \text{50 Hz AC} \rightarrow \text{Rectifier (AC → DC)} \rightarrow \text{DC Link} \rightarrow \text{Inverter (DC → 60 Hz AC)} \rightarrow \text{Filter} \rightarrow \text{Ship} $$

- **Rectifier:** Uses 6-pulse or 12-pulse thyristor/IGBT bridge to convert AC to DC. 12-pulse configuration reduces input harmonic current from $THD_i \approx 30\%$ to $THD_i < 5\%$.
- **DC Link:** A large capacitor bank (often several thousand microfarads) that:
  (i) Stores energy to decouple input and output stages.
  (ii) Filters the rectified DC ripple.
  (iii) Provides fault ride-through capability (holds voltage during brief grid disturbances).
- **Inverter:** IGBT-based PWM inverter synthesizes a 60 Hz sinusoidal output by switching at high frequency (typically 2–5 kHz) and modulating the pulse width.
- **LC Output Filter:** Removes high-frequency switching components to deliver clean 60 Hz power to the ship.

**Model Solution to (b):**

Given: $P = 2.5 \text{ MW}$, $V_L = 440 \text{ V}$, $\cos\phi = 0.88$, $\eta_{SFC} = 0.96$.

**(i) Apparent Power Drawn by Ship:**
$$S = \frac{P}{\cos\phi} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^6}{0.88} = 2.84 \text{ MVA}$$

**(ii) Reactive Power:**
$$\sin\phi = \sin(\cos^{-1}(0.88)) = \sin(28.36^\circ) = 0.475$$
$$Q = P \cdot \tan\phi = 2.5 \times \tan(28.36^\circ) = 2.5 \times 0.5396 = 1.349 \text{ MVAR}$$

(Alternatively, $Q = \sqrt{S^2 - P^2} = \sqrt{2.84^2 - 2.5^2} = \sqrt{8.066 - 6.25} = \sqrt{1.816} = 1.348$ MVAR.)

**(iii) Input Power from 50 Hz Grid:**
$$P_{in} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta_{SFC}} = \frac{2500}{0.96} = 2604.2 \text{ kW}$$

The additional $104.2 \text{ kW}$ is lost as heat inside the SFC station, requiring forced-air or water cooling.

---

### 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Shore Supply Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ factor** in 3-phase calculations — leads to 2–3 mark loss immediately.
> 2. **Confusing line vs phase voltage** in star (Y) vs delta (Δ) systems — $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$ for star; $V_L = V_{ph}$ for delta.
> 3. **Not mentioning phase sequence verification** in synchronization answers — KTU examiners specifically test this; missing it costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Writing $P = V \cdot I$** without $\cos\phi$ — partial marking only.
> 5. **Not mentioning IEC 80005-3** when describing HV shore supply — standard names are mandatory in KTU theory answers.
> 6. **Conflating "Cold Ironing" with "Shore Supply"** — they are functionally the same, but cold ironing emphasizes the *de-energized main engine* state. Use both terms interchangeably but mention the nuance.
> 7. **Skipping the SFC efficiency in numerical problems** — if the question mentions a converter, account for $\eta$ in the input-side calculation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> 📋 **KTU Module 2 — Shore Supply Revision Sheet**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Onshore electrical power to a berthed ship, replacing auxiliary generators.
>
> ✅ **Alternative names:** Cold Ironing, Shore Power, HVSC, AMP (Alternative Maritime Power).
>
> ✅ **Standards to remember:**
>    - **IEC 80005-1** — General utility connections in port
>    - **IEC 80005-3** — High Voltage Shore Connection (HVSC)
>    - **IEC 60309** — Low-voltage shore plugs/sockets
>    - **IEC 62271** — HV circuit breakers
>
> ✅ **Master formula:** $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ (every numerical must start here).
>
> ✅ **Synchronization:** $\Delta f \le 0.1$ Hz, $\Delta V \le 10\%$, $\Delta \phi \le 10^\circ$.
>
> ✅ **Frequency fix:** 50 Hz ↔ 60 Hz solved by **Static Frequency Converter (SFC)** with rectifier → DC link → inverter.
>
> ✅ **Cable sizing:** $A = I_L / J$ with current density $J = 3$–$5 \text{ A/mm}^2$ for marine XLPE.
>
> ✅ **Earthing always first**, pilot contact enables breaker closure (safety interlock).
>
> ✅ **Environmental benefit:** Eliminates port emissions; complies with **MARPOL Annex VI** and **EU Directive 2005/33/EC**.
>
> ✅ **Key ports using shore power:** Los Angeles, Long Beach, Hamburg, Rotterdam, Oslo, Gothenburg, Seattle, Vancouver.
>
> ✅ **When answering numericals:** Always write units, show substitution step, and final answer with proper rounding (2–3 decimals max).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relevant Rules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relevant Rules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Relevant Rules**, in the context of shipboard electrical engineering, refers to the comprehensive set of international conventions, statutory regulations, classification society rules, and recognized industry standards that govern the **design, manufacture, installation, commissioning, operation, and maintenance** of electrical installations aboard seagoing vessels.

The principal framework is built upon:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — The Four Pillars of Marine Electrical Rules**
> 1. **International Convention Rules** — SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line, COLREG, STCW
> 2. **Classification Society Rules** — Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, Bureau Veritas, IRS, Class NK, KR, CCS
> 3. **Statutory Regulations** — Flag State administrations (e.g., MCA UK, USCG, IMO Resolutions)
> 4. **Recognized Standards** — IEC 60092 series, IEEE Std 45, IEC 60529 (IP Code), IEC 60079 (Ex), BS 8454

The acronym **SOLAS** stands for the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea**, first adopted in **1914** (post-Titanic) and currently maintained under the auspices of the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** headquartered in **London, United Kingdom**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Classification Society**
> A Classification Society is a non-governmental organization that establishes and applies technical standards relating to the design, construction, and operational maintenance of ships and offshore structures. Collectively, ten major societies form the **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)**, founded in **1969**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a house. You cannot simply wire it any way you like — you must follow the **National Electrical Code (NEC)** or the **IEC wiring regulations**, get it inspected, and obtain a certificate of occupancy. The wiring must survive rain (IP rating), not overheat, and safely carry the expected load.

A ship is essentially a **self-contained, floating power station, hotel, and industrial plant** combined. It is arguably the most demanding engineering environment on Earth because:

- It is **isolated** — no external grid backup
- It is **mobile** — subjected to motion, vibration, slamming, and sea spray
- It is **safety-critical** — passengers and cargo are remote from rescue services
- It is **environmentally sensitive** — pollution can devastate marine ecosystems

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Intuition**
> Think of "Relevant Rules" as the **maritime equivalent of traffic laws and vehicle inspection codes combined**. Just as cars must pass a fitness test (brakes, lights, exhaust) before being allowed on the road, ships must satisfy the *rules* of their classification society and flag state before being allowed to sail. The electrical system is the *engine, lights, and brake electronics* of the ship — and must therefore meet very specific marine-grade requirements that ordinary land-based standards do not fully address.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and standard metrics are used throughout marine electrical rule sets:

- **Standard ship power frequencies:** **60 Hz** (American/Japanese practice) and **50 Hz** (European/Asian practice)
- **Standard ship service voltages (IEC 60092-201):** see table below
- **Ambient design reference temperature:** **45 °C** for machinery spaces, **40 °C** for accommodation
- **Reference ambient humidity:** **95 %** at 45 °C (tropical conditions)
- **Ship motion parameters:** roll up to **± 30°**, pitch up to **± 10°**, heave acceleration up to **0.2 g**
- **Standardized IP rating minimum for marine equipment:** **IP 23** (machinery spaces) and **IP 44** (deck/weather decks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize This — Marine Standard Voltage Bands**
> The standard shipboard low-voltage AC bands as per IEC 60092-201 are:
> - **Single-phase:** 24 V, 110 V, 220 V, 240 V
> - **Three-phase:** 380 V, 400 V, 440 V, 690 V, 3.3 kV, 6.6 kV, 11 kV
>
> Note that **440 V / 60 Hz** is widely used in the USA-built fleet, while **400 V / 50 Hz** dominates European and Asian tonnage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of Marine Rules

Marine rules are **multi-tiered**. A single design must satisfy them simultaneously:

**Tier 1 — International Conventions (Universal, Treaty-based)**
- **SOLAS 1974** (as amended) — *Safety of Life at Sea*
- **MARPOL 73/78** (as amended) — *Marine Pollution* — Annex VI covers air pollution from machinery
- **Load Line 1966** — *Freeboard and stability*
- **COLREG 1972** — *International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea* (governs navigation lights)
- **STCW 1978** (as amended) — *Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping* for engine-room electrical officers
- **MLC 2006** — *Maritime Labour Convention* (crew accommodation electrical safety)
- **Ballast Water Management (BWM) Convention 2004**

**Tier 2 — Classification Society Rules (Mandatory if classed)**
- Lloyd's Register (LR)
- DNV (Det Norske Veritas, now DNV)
- American Bureau of Shipping (ABS)
- Bureau Veritas (BV)
- Indian Register of Shipping (IRS)
- Korean Register (KR)
- Class NK (Japan)
- China Classification Society (CCS)
- RINA (Italy)
- Polski Rejestr Statkow (PRS)

> [!NOTE]
> **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Part D** is the *electrical heart* of SOLAS. It contains:
> - **Regulation 40** — General electrical installations
> - **Regulation 41** — Main source of electrical power
> - **Regulation 42** — Emergency source of electrical power
> - **Regulation 43** — Emergency lighting
> - **Regulation 44** — Steering gear power
> - **Regulation 45** — Precautions against shock, fire, and other hazards

**Tier 3 — National / Flag State Regulations**
- Each ship's **flag state** (country of registration) enforces additional statutory rules.
- Example: A Liberian-flag ship carrying US cargo must satisfy both the Liberian Maritime Authority rules and the US Coast Guard (USCG) regulations under **46 CFR** (Code of Federal Regulations).

**Tier 4 — Industry Standards (Reference, not statutory)**
- **IEC 60092 series** — *Electrical installations in ships* (the primary marine standard)
- **IEEE Std 45** — *Recommended Practice for Electrical Installations on Shipboard* (predominant in USA)
- **IEC 60529** — *Degrees of protection provided by enclosures (IP Code)*
- **IEC 60079 series** — *Explosive atmospheres*
- **IEC 60331 / IEC 60332** — *Fire resistance of cables*
- **ISO 8468** — *Ships and marine technology — Ship's bridge layout and associated equipment*

### 2.2 The SOLAS Emergency Power Rules — Quantitative Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOLAS Reg. 42 (Emergency Source of Electrical Power)** is one of the most heavily tested regulations in KTU examinations. The key requirements are:

**Step-by-step logic of the regulation:**

1. A **self-contained emergency source of electrical power** must be located *outside* the machinery casings, above the bulkhead deck, and accessible from the open deck.
2. Its **location** must be such that a fire or other casualty in the main machinery space does not affect it.
3. The emergency source must be capable of operating for a minimum duration based on ship type:

| Ship Type | Minimum Duration of Emergency Power |
|---|---|
| Passenger ships (ro-ro) on short international voyages | **36 hours** |
| Passenger ships (≥ 65 m LOA) — long international voyage | **36 hours** |
| Passenger ships (< 65 m LOA) | **3 hours** (with transitional source) |
| Cargo ships of ≥ 500 GT | **18 hours** |
| Tankers, bulk carriers, etc. | **18 hours** |
| Ships with periodic unattended machinery spaces (UMS) — battery-powered transitional | **6 hours minimum battery for transitional source + 18 h emergency generator** |

4. The emergency power must supply, *simultaneously* and *for the specified duration*:
   - Emergency lighting at embarkation stations, alleyways, stairways, escape routes
   - Navigation lights and signals (COLREG compliance)
   - Communication systems, fire detection, fire alarms
   - Power-operated watertight doors and their indicators
   - Power for emergency fire pumps (one of them, if electrically driven)
   - Steering gear (if electrically driven) — for 30 minutes
   - All SOLAS Chapter II-2 fire safety services (e.g., sprinkler pumps, smoke extraction fans)

### 2.3 Hazardous Area Classification on Ships

Tanker ships and ships carrying flammable cargo have electrically hazardous zones. Classification follows **IEC 60092-502** and **IEC 60079-10**:

| Zone | Definition | Typical Location on a Tanker |
|---|---|---|
| **Zone 0** | Flammable atmosphere **continuously present** or present for **> 1000 hours/year** | Inside cargo tanks, cargo pipes |
| **Zone 1** | Flammable atmosphere **likely in normal operation** (10–1000 h/yr) | Cofferdams, cargo pump rooms, areas within 1.5 m of tank openings |
| **Zone 2** | Flammable atmosphere **not likely in normal operation** (< 10 h/yr) | Areas within 1.5 m of Zone 1 boundaries, weather decks within 3 m of tank vents |

> [!WARNING]
> **Equipment Selection Rule**
> - Zone 0 → **Ex ia** (intrinsically safe) — energy-limited to < 0.3 mJ
> - Zone 1 → **Ex d** (flameproof enclosure) or **Ex e** (increased safety) or **Ex p** (purged/pressurized)
> - Zone 2 → **Ex n** (non-sparking) or Ex d/e acceptable

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Relevant Rules & Shipboard Power Calculations**

| # | Formula / Rule | Statement | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{gen} \geq 1.1 \times P_{L,max}$ | Main generator capacity must exceed peak load by 10% reserve | kW |
| 2 | $E_{batt} = \dfrac{P_{L,emerg} \times t_{emerg}}{V_{bus} \times \eta_{batt}}$ | Emergency battery capacity | Ah |
| 3 | $I_{sc,rec} = \dfrac{V_{LL}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{eq}}$ | Symmetrical short-circuit current at receiving bus | A (kA) |
| 4 | $\Delta V\% = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot 2 \rho \cdot \cos\phi}{V_{LL} \cdot A} \times 100$ | Voltage drop in a 3-φ feeder (single-cable pair) | %, allowable ≤ 5 % motor, ≤ 2.5 % lighting |
| 5 | $P_{trans,emer} \geq 0.5 \times P_{emer,total}$ | Transitional power (battery) for UMS ships | kW |
| 6 | $t_{emer,duration} = f(\text{ship type})$ | 18 h cargo, 36 h passenger ro-ro | hours |
| 7 | $IP_{min} = f(\text{location})$ | IP 23 machinery, IP 44 deck, IP 56 submerged | — |
| 8 | $R_{earth,ship} \leq 10 \, \Omega$ | Hull-earth resistance (some class rules ≤ 1 Ω) | Ω |
| 9 | $f_{op} = 50$ or $60 \, \text{Hz}$ | Standard marine frequency | Hz |
| 10 | $V_{standard} \in \lbrace 24, 110, 220, 380, 400, 440, 690 \rbrace$ | Permissible shipboard low voltages | V |
| 11 | $V_{HV} \in \lbrace 3.3, 6.6, 11 \rbrace \, \text{kV}$ | Marine medium voltage | kV |
| 12 | $T_{amb,design} = 45^{\circ}\text{C}$ (machinery), $40^{\circ}\text{C}$ (accommodation) | Reference ambient | °C |
| 13 | $I_{sc,brk} \geq I_{sc,sym} \times 1.1$ | Circuit breaker rupturing capacity | kA |
| 14 | $t_{fire,resist} = 60 \, \text{min}$ (IEC 60331) | Fire-resistance of emergency cables | min |
| 15 | $A_{cable} \geq \dfrac{I_{load}}{\sigma_{cu} \cdot k_{temp} \cdot k_{group}}$ | Minimum cross-section (current-carrying) | mm² |

Where:
- $P_{gen}$ = generator rated power
- $P_{L,max}$ = maximum sustained load
- $E_{batt}$ = battery energy in Ah
- $P_{L,emerg}$ = emergency load
- $t_{emerg}$ = required emergency duration
- $V_{bus}$ = battery bus voltage
- $\eta_{batt}$ = battery round-trip efficiency (≈ 0.85 for lead-acid, 0.95 for Li-ion)
- $Z_{eq}$ = equivalent Thevenin impedance from generators + cables
- $L$ = cable run length (m)
- $\rho$ = conductor resistivity at operating temperature (Ω·m)
- $A$ = conductor cross-section (mm²)
- $k_{temp}, k_{group}$ = derating factors for ambient temperature and grouping

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

- **Shipyards** must demonstrate to the class surveyor that the design complies with the rules *before* the keel is laid; this drives the "approved drawings" workflow.
- **Port State Control (PSC)** officers can detain a ship that fails to comply with SOLAS or MARPOL — making rule-compliance a direct commercial issue.
- **Insurance** premiums (H&M and P&I) are reduced when class is maintained.
- **Crew certifications** under STCW require officers to *understand* the rules, not merely memorize them — this is why KTU tests the application of these rules.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Emergency Battery Capacity for a SOLAS-Compliant Vessel

**Problem Statement:**
A general cargo ship of **5,500 GT** is to be fitted with a transitional emergency battery source for a **UMS-classed** vessel. The connected transitional emergency loads are:

- Navigation lights: $P_1 = 1.5$ kW
- Bridge communication + GMDSS: $P_2 = 2.0$ kW
- Fire detection + alarm: $P_3 = 0.8$ kW
- Emergency lighting at muster station: $P_4 = 3.0$ kW
- Steering gear control: $P_5 = 1.2$ kW
- **Total transitional load:** $P_{total} = 8.5$ kW

The system bus voltage is **24 V DC**, the battery round-trip efficiency is **0.85**, and the SOLAS-required transitional duration for a UMS cargo ship is **6 hours**. Determine the required battery capacity in **Ah** and **kWh**.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation**

From the formula sheet (Entry 2):
$$E_{batt} = \dfrac{P_{L,emerg} \times t_{emerg}}{V_{bus} \times \eta_{batt}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values**

Substituting the known quantities:

$$E_{batt} = \dfrac{8.5 \times 10^3 \, \text{W} \times 6 \, \text{h}}{24 \, \text{V} \times 0.85}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator**

$$8.5 \times 10^3 \times 6 = 51{,}000 \, \text{Wh}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator**

$$24 \times 0.85 = 20.4 \, \text{V}_{\text{eff}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the final capacity in Ah**

$$E_{batt} = \dfrac{51{,}000}{20.4} = 2{,}500 \, \text{Ah}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the stored energy in kWh**

$$E_{stored} = V_{bus} \times E_{batt} = 24 \times 2{,}500 = 60{,}000 \, \text{Wh} = 60 \, \text{kWh}$$

**Step 7 — Apply design margin**

Class rules typically require a **20 % margin** above the calculated value to account for battery aging, temperature derating, and partial discharge. The final selected battery is therefore:

$$E_{selected} = 2{,}500 \times 1.20 = 3{,}000 \, \text{Ah at 24 V (C10 rating)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight**
> The unit of Ah represents capacity at a defined discharge rate (e.g., C10 = 10-hour rate). Always declare the **C-rate** when specifying a marine battery. Also note that for the *transitional* source, a lead-acid battery is the most common choice; for the *main emergency generator*, a diesel generator with its own fuel tank (sufficient for 18 h) is standard.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Voltage Drop in a 3-Phase Shipboard Feeder

**Problem Statement:**
A **400 V, 50 Hz, 3-phase** shipboard feeder supplies a **75 kW** induction motor (cos φ = 0.85, η = 0.93) located **85 m** from the main switchboard. Conductor material is **copper** at operating temperature (resistivity $\rho_{cu,90°C} = 0.0228$ Ω·mm²/m). Determine the required cable cross-section to keep the voltage drop **≤ 5 %** as required by class rules.

**Step 1 — Compute the line current drawn by the motor**

$$I = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL} \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$I = \dfrac{75{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 0.85 \times 0.93}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the denominator**

$$\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 0.85 \times 0.93 = 1.732 \times 400 \times 0.85 \times 0.93 = 547.5 \, \text{VA-per-ampere equivalent}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the current**

$$I = \dfrac{75{,}000}{547.5} = 137.0 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the voltage-drop formula**

$$\Delta V\% = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot 2 \rho \cdot \cos\phi}{V_{LL} \cdot A} \times 100$$

**Step 6 — Solve for A (cross-section)**

Rearranging:
$$A = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot 2 \rho \cdot \cos\phi \times 100}{V_{LL} \cdot \Delta V\%}$$

Substituting:
$$A = \dfrac{1.732 \times 137 \times 85 \times 2 \times 0.0228 \times 0.85 \times 100}{400 \times 5}$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the numerator**

$$1.732 \times 137 \times 85 \times 2 \times 0.0228 \times 0.85 \times 100$$

Compute stepwise:
- $1.732 \times 137 = 237.28$
- $237.28 \times 85 = 20{,}168.8$
- $20{,}168.8 \times 2 = 40{,}337.6$
- $40{,}337.6 \times 0.0228 = 919.7$
- $919.7 \times 0.85 = 781.7$
- $781.7 \times 100 = 78{,}170$

**Step 8 — Evaluate the denominator**

$$400 \times 5 = 2{,}000$$

**Step 9 — Compute the cross-section**

$$A = \dfrac{78{,}170}{2{,}000} = 39.08 \, \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 10 — Select the next standard size**

Standard marine cable sizes: 35, 50, 70, 95, 120 mm² …

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selected cross-section: 50 mm² copper** (to satisfy both voltage drop and current-carrying capacity, the latter being verified against IEC 60092-352 Table 2 with derating factors).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Class Society Emergency Power Rules at a Glance

| Parameter | Lloyd's Register (LR) | DNV | ABS | Bureau Veritas (BV) | IRS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Min. duration — passenger | 36 h | 36 h | 36 h | 36 h | 36 h |
| Min. duration — cargo | 18 h | 18 h | 18 h | 18 h | 18 h |
| Min. duration — transitional (UMS) | 6 h | 6 h | 6 h | 6 h | 6 h |
| Voltage tolerance (steady) | ± 10 % | ± 10 % | ± 10 % | ± 10 % | ± 10 % |
| Frequency tolerance (steady) | ± 5 % | ± 5 % | ± 5 % | ± 5 % | ± 5 % |
| Hull-earth resistance max | 10 Ω (or 0.5 Ω for clean earth) | 10 Ω | 10 Ω | 10 Ω | 10 Ω |
| Min. IP — weather deck | IP 56 | IP 56 | IP 56 | IP 56 | IP 56 |
| Min. IP — engine room | IP 23 | IP 23 | IP 23 | IP 23 | IP 23 |
| Min. IP — galley/accommodation | IP 20 | IP 20 | IP 20 | IP 20 | IP 20 |
| Fire-resistant cable (emergency circuits) | IEC 60331-21 (60 min) | IEC 60331-21 (60 min) | IEC 60331-21 (60 min) | IEC 60331-21 (60 min) | IEC 60331-21 (60 min) |
| Earth-fault relay max setting | 30 % of generator rated | 30 % | 30 % | 30 % | 30 % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these tolerances matter**
> The ± 10 % voltage and ± 5 % frequency tolerances are the same as those specified by **IEC 60092-201**. Motors are designed to deliver rated torque within this band; generators and governor/AVR setpoints are trimmed to maintain voltage and frequency within this band even at extreme load steps (e.g., starting a large bow thruster).

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Short-Circuit Current at the Main Busbar

**Problem Statement:**
A ship's main busbar is fed by **3 × 2,500 kVA** generators operating in parallel. Each generator has a **sub-transient reactance $X_d''$ = 0.18 pu** and **synchronous reactance $X_d$ = 1.8 pu** (per generator on its own base). Compute the **symmetrical short-circuit current** (in kA) at the bus, assuming a bolted 3-phase fault, system voltage **6.6 kV**, and a base of 100 MVA.

**Step 1 — Compute the per-unit reactances on the system base (100 MVA)**

For each generator, the per-unit reactance on the system base is:
$$X_{d,gen}'' = 0.18 \times \dfrac{100}{2.5} = 7.2 \, \text{pu}$$

**Step 2 — Three generators in parallel — equivalent reactance**

$$X_{eq} = \dfrac{7.2}{3} = 2.4 \, \text{pu}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the base current at 6.6 kV, 100 MVA**

$$I_{base} = \dfrac{S_{base}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{base}} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 6.6 \times 10^3} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{11.43} = 8{,}750 \, \text{A} = 8.75 \, \text{kA}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the short-circuit current in pu and in kA**

$$I_{sc,pu} = \dfrac{1.0}{X_{eq}} = \dfrac{1.0}{2.4} = 0.417 \, \text{pu}$$

$$I_{sc,kA} = 0.417 \times 8.75 = 3.65 \, \text{kA}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the asymmetrical peak factor**

The peak (asymmetrical) current is the symmetrical RMS multiplied by the peak factor $k_p$ (typically 1.8 to 2.5 depending on X/R):
$$I_{peak} = k_p \times \sqrt{2} \times I_{sc,rms} = 2.0 \times 1.414 \times 3.65 = 10.32 \, \text{kA}$$

**Step 6 — Specify the circuit breaker**

The breaker must have a **rated short-circuit breaking capacity** $\geq 1.1 \times I_{sc,rms}$ (per formula sheet entry 13):
$$I_{brk} \geq 1.1 \times 3.65 = 4.02 \, \text{kA}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for SOLAS / Class compliance**
> All switchgear on board must be **type-tested** to a short-circuit rating exceeding the maximum fault current at its terminals. This is one of the most heavily audited items during the *Sea Trials* and is a frequent cause of class-condition remarks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
