$R_a$ = Armature resistance - $R_{se}$ = Series field resistance - $I_a$ = Armature current - $k, k_T$ = Machine constants (with $k_T = 60k/2\pi$ in consistent SI units) ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Marine Application | Why Series Motor? | |---|---| | **Anchor Windlass** | Massive starting torque to lift 5–15 tonne anchor + chain | | **Mooring Winch** | Load-adaptive pull; handles shock loads during vessel movement | | **Cargo Crane / Davit** | Smooth heavy lift; tolerates 200% overload for short bursts | | **Capstan** | Continuous pulling with variable rope tension | | **Bow Thruster (older ships)** | High starting torque for rapid thrust reversal | | **Cargo Conveyor (auxiliary)** | Constant torque under varying load conditions | # 🟡 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Back EMF ($E_b$) in a Series Motor > **Starting Point:** Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction. **Step 1: Flux cut per revolution.** Each armature conductor cuts a flux of $P \Phi$ Webers in one complete revolution (one rotation under $P$ poles). $$ \text{Flux cut per conductor per revolution} = P \Phi \quad [\text{Webers}] $$ **Step 2: Flux cut per conductor per second (rate of change).** The armature rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, so the revolutions per second are $N/60$. $$ \text{Rate of flux cutting} = P \Phi \times \frac{N}{60} = \frac{P \Phi N}{60} \quad [\text{Wb/s}] $$ **Step 3: Total EMF in the armature.** With $Z$ total conductors arranged in $A$ parallel paths, the number of conductors in **series** per path is $Z/A$. These conductors are additive in EMF. $$ E_b = \left(\frac{Z}{A}\right) \times \left(\frac{P \Phi N}{60}\right) = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A} \quad [\text{Volts}] $$ **Step 4: Compact machine-constant form.** Defining the EMF constant $k = \frac{PZ}{60A}$: $$ E_b = k \, \Phi \, N \quad [\text{Volts}] $$ > ✅ **Verification:** For a 4-pole ($P=4$), 500-conductor ($Z=500$), wave-wound ($A=2$) motor with $\Phi = 0.05$ Wb and $N = 1000$ rpm: $E_b = \frac{4 \times 0.05 \times 1000 \times 500}{60 \times 2} = 833.3$ V. This matches textbook examples for the same design. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque Equation ($T_a$) > **Starting Point:** Force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field: $F = BIL$. **Step 1: Force on a single armature conductor.** The flux density at the pole is $B = \Phi / A_{pole}$. The length of the conductor is $L$ (active length on the armature). Current through each conductor is $I_a/A$ (current divides among $A$ parallel paths). $$ F_{\text{per conductor}} = B \cdot L \cdot \frac{I_a}{A} $$ **Step 2: Torque on a single conductor.** Each conductor sits at a radius $r$ from the shaft axis. Torque per conductor = $F \times r$. $$ T_{\text{per conductor}} = \frac{B L I_a r}{A} $$ **Step 3: Total armature torque from $Z$ conductors.** $$ T_a = Z \times \frac{B L I_a r}{A} = \frac{B L r Z I_a}{A} $$ **Step 4: Relate to flux and machine geometry.** The active surface area swept per pole is approximately $2\pi r L / P$, hence $B \cdot (2\pi r L / P) = \Phi$, giving $BLr = P\Phi/2\pi$. **Step 5: Substitute back.** $$ T_a = \frac{(P \Phi / 2\pi) \cdot Z \cdot I_a}{A} = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A} \quad [\text{N·m}] $$ **Step 6: Compact form with torque constant $k_T$.** $$ T_a = k_T \, \Phi \, I_a \quad \text{where} \quad k_T = \frac{PZ}{2\pi A} $$ > ✅ **Relation between constants:** $k_T = \frac{60}{2\pi} k \approx 9.549 \, k$ when $k$ is in V/(Wb·rpm). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Speed vs. Torque Characteristic of a Series Motor > **Goal:** Derive $N$ as a function of $T_a$ — the most important KTU exam relationship. **Step 1: Start with the voltage equation.** Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the series motor loop: $$ V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se}) $$ **Step 2: Substitute $E_b = k\Phi N$.** $$ V = k \Phi N + I_a(R_a + R_{se}) $$ **Step 3: Solve for $N$.** $$ N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k \Phi} \quad \text{[rpm]} $$ **Step 4: In the unsaturated region, $\Phi \propto I_a$.** Let $\Phi = c_1 I_a$, where $c_1$ is the field-winding constant. Also, $T_a = k_T \Phi I_a = k_T c_1 I_a^2$, so: $$ I_a = \sqrt{\frac{T_a}{k_T c_1}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Phi = c_1 \sqrt{\frac{T_a}{k_T c_1}} = \sqrt{\frac{c_1 T_a}{k_T}} $$ **Step 5: Substitute $\Phi$ into the speed equation.** $$ N = \frac{V - \sqrt{T_a / (k_T c_1)} \cdot (R_a + R_{se})}{k \sqrt{c_1 T_a / k_T}} $$ **Step 6: Simplify (dropping the small IR drop approximation for the ideal curve).** $$ N \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{T_a}} $$ > **Conclusion:** In the unsaturated operating region, **speed is inversely proportional to the square root of the torque** — this is the iconic **drooping hyperbolic curve** of a series motor. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power Flow & Efficiency in a Series Motor **Step 1: Electrical input.** $$ P_{in} = V \cdot I_a $$ **Step 2: Copper losses (armature + series field).** $$ P_{cu} = I_a^2 (R_a + R_{se}) $$ **Step 3: Iron (core) loss + friction & windage loss (rotational).** $$ P_{iron} + P_{mech} = \text{function of speed and flux (approx. constant at rated speed)} $$ **Step 4: Gross mechanical power developed.** $$ P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = T_a \cdot \omega = T_a \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} $$ **Step 5: Net shaft output power.** $$ P_{out} = P_m - (P_{friction} + P_{windage}) = \frac{2\pi N T_{sh}}{60} $$ **Step 6: Overall efficiency.** $$ \eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{V I_a - I_a^2(R_a+R_{se}) - P_{iron} - P_{mech}}{V I_a} \times 100 $$ --- ## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Series vs. Shunt vs. Compound Motors > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Comparison Table — High-Yield for Module 3:** | Feature | Series Motor | Shunt Motor | Compound Motor | |---|---|---|---| | **Field connection** | Series with armature | Parallel with armature | Series + Shunt | | **Field current** | $I_f = I_a$ (large) | $I_f = V/R_{sh}$ (small) | Combination | | **Field turns** | Few, thick | Many, thin | Both types | | **Field resistance** | Very low | Very high | Mixed | | **Flux $\Phi$** | $\Phi \propto I_a$ | Almost constant | Partial dependence | | **Speed regulation** | **Poor** (droops with load) | Good (nearly constant) | Moderate | | **Starting torque** | **Very high** ($T \propto I_a^2$) | Moderate | High | | **No-load speed** | **Dangerously high** | Safe (rated) | Safe | | **Torque-speed curve** | Inverse hyperbolic | Nearly straight line | Between series & shunt | | **Suitability for constant-speed load** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ⚠ Limited | | **Marine suitability** | ✅ **Winches, cranes** | ✅ Pumps, fans | ✅ Compressors | | **Reversal method** | Reverse either field or armature (not both) | Reverse armature | Reverse armature | # 🟣 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Circuit Topology of a DC Series Motor ```mermaid flowchart LR DC[DC Supply
V volts] --> P1[Positive Terminal] P1 --> SFC[Series Field

speed monitoring] TACH -.->|Signal| STR ``` # 🔴 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** **"Why is a DC series motor never started on no-load? What arrangement is used to couple it safely to a load?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A DC series motor develops flux $\Phi$ proportional to the armature current ($I_f = I_a$). On **no-load**, $I_a$ is very small, so $\Phi$ is small. From the speed equation $N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a+R_{se})}{k\Phi}$, with $\Phi$ very small, the denominator becomes tiny, and **N tends to infinity** — the motor "runs away," causing mechanical damage due to centrifugal forces. **Safe coupling:** The series motor is **always permanently coupled to its load** through a **gear train, chain drive, or direct shaft connection** (typical in marine winches and windlasses). The load provides the minimum $I_a$ to keep $\Phi$ above a safe threshold. In the laboratory, it is tested with a **rope-brake or eddy-current loading** arrangement. **(3 marks)** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]** **"Compare the starting torque of a DC series motor with that of a DC shunt motor of the same rating."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** For a **shunt motor**, flux $\Phi$ is constant (independent of $I_a$ at start), so starting torque $T_{st} \propto I_a$ only. Typically $T_{st} = 1.5$ to 2 times full-load torque. For a **series motor**, $\Phi \propto I_a$ at start (unsaturated), so $T_{st} \propto \Phi \cdot I_a \propto I_a^2$. The starting torque is **1.5 to 2 times the full-load current squared** relation, giving $T_{st} = 2$ to 3 times (and even up to 5×) full-load torque. **Conclusion:** A series motor delivers a **substantially higher starting torque** than a shunt motor of the same rating because of its **doubly-squared dependence** on armature current. This is why series motors are preferred for **anchor windlasses and cargo cranes** on ships. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern:** Each Module typically has an internal choice between two 14-mark questions. Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. --- ### **❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Adapted]** **A 220 V, 1000 rpm, 4-pole DC series motor has an armature resistance of 0.1 Ω and a series field resistance of 0.05 Ω. The armature has 500 conductors wave-wound ($A=2$). At a certain load, the motor takes 50 A and the flux per pole is 25 mWb.** **(a)** Calculate the **back EMF** developed and the **speed** of the motor at this load. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Calculate the **gross torque** developed, the **mechanical power** developed, and the **starting torque** if the starting current is limited to 1.5 times the full-load current (assume flux remains 25 mWb at start). **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution:** ### **Part (a) — Back EMF and Speed (7 marks)** **Given:** $V = 220$ V, $P = 4$, $Z = 500$, $A = 2$, $R_a = 0.1\ \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.05\ \Omega$, $I_a = 50$ A, $\Phi = 25$ mWb $= 25 \times 10^{-3}$ Wb. **Step 1: Calculate back EMF using KVL.** $$ E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se}) $$ $$ E_b = 220 - 50 \times (0.1 + 0.05) = 220 - 50 \times 0.15 = 220 - 7.5 $$ $$ E_b = 212.5 \ \text{V} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)} $$ **Step 2: Calculate EMF constant $k$.** $$ k = \frac{PZ}{60A} = \frac{4 \times 500}{60 \times 2} = \frac{2000}{120} = 16.667 \ \text{V/(Wb·rpm)} $$ **Step 3: Rearrange $E_b = k\Phi N$ to find $N$.** $$ N = \frac{E_b}{k \Phi} = \frac{212.5}{16.667 \times 25 \times 10^{-3}} $$ $$ N = \frac{212.5}{0.4167} = 510 \ \text{rpm} \quad \textbf{(4 marks)} $$ --- ### **Part (b) — Torque, Power & Starting Torque (7 marks)** **Step 4: Calculate torque constant $k_T$.** $$ k_T = \frac{PZ}{2\pi A} = \frac{4 \times 500}{2\pi \times 2} = \frac{2000}{12.566} = 159.15 \ \text{N·m/(Wb·A)} $$ **Step 5: Calculate gross developed torque.** $$ T_a = k_T \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a = 159.15 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 $$ $$ T_a = 159.15 \times 1.25 = 198.9 \ \text{N·m} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)} $$ **Step 6: Calculate mechanical power developed.** $$ P_m = E_b \times I_a = 212.5 \times 50 = 10{,}625 \ \text{W} = 10.625 \ \text{kW} $$ **Cross-check:** $P_m = \frac{2\pi N T_a}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 510 \times 198.9}{60} = 10{,}623 \ \text{W} \ \checkmark \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$ **Step 7: Starting torque calculation.** Starting current $I_{st} = 1.5 \times 50 = 75$ A. Flux at start = 25 mWb (assumed same). $$ T_{st} = k_T \cdot \Phi \cdot I_{st} = 159.15 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \times 75 $$ $$ T_{st} = 159.15 \times 1.875 = 298.4 \ \text{N·m} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)} $$ > **Key Insight:** $T_{st}/T_{FL} = 298.4/198.9 \approx 1.5$, confirming the **1.5× starting torque** for a 1.5× starting current (linear unsaturated region). In practice, with magnetic saturation, this ratio is even higher. --- ### **❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Adapted]** **With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a DC series motor. Derive the relationship between flux, armature current, and torque in a series motor. State three marine applications. State the speed-torque characteristic and explain why the motor must be permanently coupled to a load.** #### **Model Solution Outline:** ### **Part (a) — Construction, Working & Derivation (7 marks)** **Construction (3 marks):** - **Yoke** of cast steel — outer frame completing the magnetic circuit. - **Pole cores & pole shoes** of laminated steel — carry the series field winding. - **Series field winding** — few turns of heavy-gauge copper strip; connected in series with the armature. - **Armature** — slotted laminated cylinder carrying Z conductors wound in a wave/lap pattern. - **Commutator & brush gear** — copper segments and carbon brushes transferring current to the rotating armature. - **Shaft, bearings, end plates** — mechanical support. **Working (2 marks):** When DC supply is switched on, current flows through the series field (creating flux $\Phi$) and the armature (in which the conductors experience force $F = BIL$). The collective force produces torque $T = k_T \Phi I_a$, rotating the armature. The rotation induces a back EMF $E_b = k\Phi N$ which opposes the applied voltage, limiting the current at steady state. **Derivation of torque (2 marks):** $$ \Phi \propto I_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Phi = c \cdot I_a $$ $$ T_a = k_T \Phi I_a = k_T \cdot c \cdot I_a^2 = K \cdot I_a^2 $$ $$ \boxed{T_a \propto I_a^2 \quad \text{(unsaturated region)}} $$ ### **Part (b) — Characteristics, Applications & Load Coupling (7 marks)** **Speed-torque characteristic (3 marks):** From $N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k\Phi}$ and $\Phi \propto I_a$ (unsaturated): $$ N \approx \frac{V}{k c I_a} = \frac{V}{k c \cdot \sqrt{T_a / (K)}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{T_a}} $$ This is the iconic **inverse-hyperbolic curve** — speed falls sharply as torque increases. The curve flattens out at high loads due to magnetic saturation. **Three marine applications (2 marks):** 1. **Anchor windlass** — lifts the anchor and chain. 2. **Mooring winch** — handles mooring lines under tension. 3. **Cargo crane / davit** — lifts lifeboats and cargo. **Why permanent load coupling (2 marks):** On no-load, $I_a \to 0$, so $\Phi \to 0$, and $N = V/(k\Phi) \to \infty$. The motor "runs away" and may disintegrate from centrifugal force. Hence the series motor must **always remain coupled to a load** that draws sufficient current to maintain a safe flux. Marine winches achieve this inherently. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU 2024 — Where Students Lose Marks in Series Motor Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing $A$ (parallel paths) with $A$ (Amps):** Always state the winding type — lap winding gives $A = P$, wave winding gives $A = 2$. > 2. **Forgetting to convert mWb to Wb:** $\Phi$ must be in **Weber** in SI equations. Writing $\Phi = 25$ instead of $0.025$ leads to a 1000× error. > 3. **Mixing $k$ and $k_T$ constants:** $k$ is for EMF ($V$ vs. rpm), $k_T$ is for torque ($N\cdot m$). Show both, but use the correct one. > 4. **Using shunt-motor formulas on series motor:** The shunt equation $N = (V - I_a R_a)/(k\Phi)$ is **WRONG** for a series motor — you must add $R_{se}$ to $R_a$. > 5. **Ignoring saturation:** Beyond rated current, $\Phi$ does not rise linearly. The $N \propto 1/\sqrt{T}$ curve flattens out — examiners love this subtlety. > 6. **Not stating units explicitly:** KTU examiners deduct ½ mark per quantity without proper SI unit (e.g., writing $T = 198.9$ without **N·m**). > 7. **Skipping the sketch in 14-mark answers:** A labelled construction diagram carries **2 marks** by itself — never skip it. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 Quick-Recall Bullet Points — Read Before the Exam:** - ✅ A **DC series motor** has its **field winding in series** with the **armature** — same current flows through both. - ✅ The series field has **few turns of thick wire** (low resistance, ~0.1 Ω). - ✅ Field flux $\Phi \propto I_a$ **in the linear (unsaturated) region**. - ✅ **Torque** $T_a = k_T \Phi I_a$ — and in series motor, $T_a \propto I_a^2$ **(unsaturated)**. - ✅ **Speed** $N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k \Phi}$ — and in unsaturated region, $N \propto 1/\sqrt{T_a}$. - ✅ **Back EMF** $E_b = k \Phi N$ opposes the applied voltage. - ✅ **No-load operation is DANGEROUS** — motor "runs away" to destructive speeds. Always couple to a load. - ✅ **High starting torque** — ideal for **anchor windlasses, winches, capstans, cranes**. - ✅ **Speed regulation is poor** — speed drops significantly as load increases. - ✅ **Reversal of rotation** — reverse either the field or armature (NOT both). - ✅ **Wave-wound** armature: $A = 2$. **Lap-wound** armature: $A = P$. - ✅ **EMF constant** $k = \frac{PZ}{60A}$ and **Torque constant** $k_T = \frac{PZ}{2\pi A}$; relation $k_T = \frac{60}{2\pi} k \approx 9.549\,k$. - ✅ **Saturated region:** $\Phi$ ≈ constant → $T_a \propto I_a$ (linear, like a shunt motor). - ✅ Marine applications: **windlass, winch, capstan, davit, cargo crane, bow thruster (older ships)**. - ✅ Typical ratings on shipboard: **220 V DC** (older ships) or via **thyristor rectifier** from AC bus. - ✅ Standard insulation for marine motors: **Class F (155 °C)** or **Class H (180 °C)**. - ✅ Conversion: **1 HP = 746 W**; **1 N·m = 1 J** (per radian). - ✅ Always **show units** in the final answer for KTU marks. - ✅ Always **draw a labelled diagram** in 14-mark questions — it carries 2 marks minimum. --- > **🎓 End of Module 3 — Series Wound Motor Notes** > **Target Exam:** KTU 2024 Scheme — B.Tech (PENST524) > **Recommended Practice Time:** 6 hours (theory + 3 numerical problems) > **Expected Marks Distribution in ESE:** 1 × 14-mark question OR 1 × 7-mark + 1 × 3/4-mark from this module.

topics.title: Compound Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compound Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **DC Compound Motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that utilizes **two independent field windings** wound on the same pole structure — a **Shunt Field Winding** (consisting of many turns of fine wire connected directly across the armature supply) and a **Series Field Winding** (consisting of few turns of heavy-gauge wire connected in series with the armature circuit). The combination produces a **hybrid magnetic flux** that is jointly responsible for torque production, allowing the motor to inherit the desirable performance characteristics of both its parent topologies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Compound Motors are explicitly listed as a separate classification of DC machines. The student must demonstrate mastery of (a) construction topology, (b) the two operational variants (cumulative & differential), (c) speed–load and torque–current characteristic curves, and (d) marine application scenarios (deck cranes, capstans, windlasses, winches, steering gear motors).

## 1.2 The Two Sub-Topologies

| Variant | Field Flux Relation | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Cumulative Compound Motor** | $\Phi_{total} = \Phi_{sh} + \Phi_{se}$ | Series and shunt MMFs **aid** each other. Load increases → flux rises → high starting torque with stable no-load speed. |
| **Differential Compound Motor** | $\Phi_{total} = \Phi_{sh} - \Phi_{se}$ | Series and shunt MMFs **oppose** each other. Load increases → flux falls → extremely poor starting torque; **rarely used**. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Tug-of-War Team

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a rowing boat being pushed by two oarsmen.
> - The **Shunt Field** is like the **steady, predictable elderly oarsman** who always rows with the same calm rhythm — he ensures the boat has a constant cruising speed, never lets it run wild at no-load, and provides a stable magnetic anchor.
> - The **Series Field** is like the **burly young oarsman** who only rows hard when the boat is under heavy cargo — when the load is light, he barely pulls; when you add tons of cargo, he flexes his muscles and adds tremendous thrust.
> - In a **Cumulative** boat, both oarsmen pull in the **same direction** → smooth acceleration under load. In a **Differential** boat, they pull in **opposite directions** → the boat wobbles and barely moves.

## 1.4 Why Compound Motors Matter Onboard a Ship

On a ship, electrical drives are used for **intermittent heavy-load operations** — a deck crane lifting a 5-tonne anchor, a capstan pulling mooring ropes, a windlass hauling chain. These applications require:
- A **high starting torque** (to overcome stiction and inertia) — supplied by the series winding.
- A **safe no-load speed** (the motor must not "run away" if the load is suddenly removed) — supplied by the shunt winding.

The **Cumulative Compound Motor** uniquely satisfies both constraints, making it the **workhorse of marine auxiliary drives**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Architecture

A compound motor has the **same physical armature** as a series or shunt motor, but the **pole shoes** now carry **two distinct field coils**:
1. **Shunt Field Coil** — Many turns of thin enameled copper wire (gauge SWG 30–40), high resistance (~100–500 Ω), connected across the supply.
2. **Series Field Coil** — Few turns of thick copper strip or wire (gauge SWG 10–18), very low resistance (~0.01–0.1 Ω), connected in series with the armature.

The two coils are wound **concentrically** on the pole body, with the series coil placed closer to the armature (to reduce leakage flux) and the shunt coil on top.

## 2.2 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Reasoning — "Why does flux change with load?"**

- **Step 1 — Supply is applied.** The shunt field draws its rated current (essentially constant, because $R_{sh}$ is fixed and $V$ is constant). So $\Phi_{sh}$ is **approximately constant**.
- **Step 2 — Armature current begins to flow.** This same current passes through the series winding. So $\Phi_{se} \propto I_a$.
- **Step 3 — For a Cumulative Motor:** $\Phi_{total} = \Phi_{sh} + \Phi_{se}$. As load → $I_a \uparrow \Rightarrow \Phi_{se} \uparrow \Rightarrow \Phi_{total} \uparrow$.
- **Step 4 — Torque responds:** $T \propto \Phi_{total} \cdot I_a$. Since both $\Phi_{total}$ and $I_a$ grow with load, torque grows **faster than linearly** — excellent for heavy lifts.
- **Step 5 — Speed responds:** $N \propto \dfrac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{\Phi_{total}}$. The numerator decreases slightly (armature drop) and the denominator **increases** → speed drops with load, but **never uncontrollably** (the shunt field prevents runaway).

## 2.3 Speed–Load Characteristic (Qualitative Summary)

| Motor Type | No-Load Speed | Speed @ Full Load | Regulation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shunt | Moderate (defined) | Slight drop (~5–10%) | **Good** |
| Series | **DANGEROUS — Infinity** | Low | **Poor (unstable)** |
| **Cumulative Compound** | **Defined (safe)** | Moderate drop (~10–25%) | **Fair / Good** |
| Differential Compound | Slightly above rated | **Speed rises** with load | **Unstable — not used** |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Equation | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Flux (Cumulative) | $\Phi = \Phi_{sh} + \Phi_{se}$ | Weber (Wb) | MMFs add |
| 2 | Total Flux (Differential) | $\Phi = \Phi_{sh} - \Phi_{se}$ | Weber (Wb) | MMFs oppose |
| 3 | Back EMF | $E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | Volt (V) | $R_{se}$ included since series field carries $I_a$ |
| 4 | Speed Equation | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k \Phi}$ | RPM | Universal DC motor formula |
| 5 | Torque Equation | $T = k \Phi I_a$ | N·m | Flux varies with load → non-linear $T$–$I_a$ curve |
| 6 | Shaft Power | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Watt (W) | Mechanical output |
| 7 | Electrical Input | $P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$ | Watt (W) | $I_L = I_a$ in compound motor |
| 8 | Shunt Field Current | $I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}$ | Ampere (A) | Constant if $V$ constant |
| 9 | Armature Current | $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$ | Ampere (A) | $I_L$ = line current |
| 10 | Per-unit Regulation | $\%\,Reg = \dfrac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100$ | % | Indicator of speed stability |

> **KTU Pitfall — Pipe Symbol:** In KTU answer scripts, never write absolute value as `|x|` inside tables. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$. For example, "$\vert \Phi_{sh} \vert > \vert \Phi_{se} \vert$" at no-load.

## 2.5 Real-World Marine Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where you will find a Cumulative Compound DC Motor onboard a merchant vessel or in a shipyard:**
> - **Deck cranes and pedestal cranes** (lifting cargo, anchors, lifeboats).
> - **Mooring winches and capstans** (heavy intermittent duty cycle).
> - **Windlasses** (hauling the anchor chain — peak torque at break-out).
> - **Steering gear motors** (where redundant safe operation is mandated by SOLAS).
> - **Cargo elevators and conveyors** in the ship's stores or engine room.
> - **Shipyard transporters and Goliath cranes** (gantry drives).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: General Speed Equation of a Compound DC Motor

Starting from the **fundamental DC machine voltage equation**, applied to the complete armature circuit (which now includes both armature resistance $R_a$ and series field resistance $R_{se}$ in series, because the series winding carries the full armature current $I_a$):

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a + I_a R_{se}$$

This rearranges to give the back EMF:

$$E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$$

Using the **Faraday-induced EMF relation** for a DC machine:

$$E_b = k \Phi N$$

where $k$ is the machine construction constant, $\Phi$ is the **effective air-gap flux per pole** (which is the combined flux from shunt and series windings), and $N$ is the speed in RPM. Equating the two expressions for $E_b$:

$$k \Phi N = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$$

Solving explicitly for $N$:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k \Phi}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** The examiner expects you to clearly distinguish between the *numerator term* (which **decreases** with load) and the *denominator term* (which **increases** with load for cumulative compound). The net effect is a moderate drop in speed — neither as flat as a shunt motor nor as steep as a series motor.

## 3.2 Derivation: Torque Expression and Load Dependence

The electromagnetic torque developed by a DC motor is given by:

$$T = k \Phi I_a$$

For a **cumulative compound motor**, the flux is a linear function of armature current (to a first approximation, neglecting saturation):

$$\Phi = \Phi_{sh} + \Phi_{se} = \Phi_{sh} + k_{se} I_a$$

where $k_{se}$ is a constant that depends on the number of series turns and the magnetic circuit. Substituting:

$$T = k (\Phi_{sh} + k_{se} I_a) I_a$$

$$T = k \Phi_{sh} I_a + k k_{se} I_a^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The torque is a **parabolic (quadratic) function** of armature current. This is why cumulative compound motors develop **substantially more torque per ampere** at high loads than a pure shunt motor (which has only a linear $T$–$I_a$ relationship because $\Phi$ is constant). This quadratic behaviour is the **exact mathematical reason** that compound motors dominate marine heavy-lift applications.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A 10 kW, 220 V cumulative compound DC motor has $R_a = 0.1 \,\Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.05 \,\Omega$, and $R_{sh} = 110 \,\Omega$. The machine constant $k\Phi$ at full load is $0.05 \,\text{V·s/rad}$. At no-load, line current is 5 A. Calculate: (a) No-load speed, (b) Full-load speed, (c) Speed regulation as a percentage.

### Step (a) — No-Load Condition

The no-load line current is the sum of the shunt field current and a small armature current (used to overcome rotational losses). Compute the shunt field current:

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2 \, \text{A}$$

The armature current at no-load is therefore:

$$I_{a,\,NL} = I_{L,\,NL} - I_{sh} = 5 - 2 = 3 \, \text{A}$$

The back EMF at no-load (note: the series field is in the armature loop, so its drop is included):

$$E_{b,\,NL} = V - I_{a,\,NL}(R_a + R_{se}) = 220 - 3(0.1 + 0.05)$$

$$E_{b,\,NL} = 220 - 3(0.15) = 220 - 0.45 = 219.55 \, \text{V}$$

Using the speed equation with $k\Phi$ given (note: the problem states $k\Phi$ at full load; at no-load, the series flux is much smaller, so we approximate the no-load flux as essentially the shunt flux alone):

$$N_{NL} = \frac{E_{b,\,NL}}{k\Phi_{NL}} \approx \frac{219.55}{0.05 \times 0.96}$$

where $0.96$ is an approximate correction factor for the reduced no-load flux. For an exam-style estimation, we may take $k\Phi_{NL} \approx 0.048$:

$$N_{NL} \approx \frac{219.55}{0.048} \approx 4574 \, \text{RPM}$$

### Step (b) — Full-Load Condition

At full load, the motor delivers 10 kW. Compute the full-load line current:

$$I_{L,\,FL} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta \cdot V} \approx \frac{10000}{0.85 \times 220} \approx 53.5 \, \text{A}$$

(Assuming efficiency $\eta \approx 0.85$ as a typical KTU value.)

$$I_{a,\,FL} = I_{L,\,FL} - I_{sh} = 53.5 - 2 = 51.5 \, \text{A}$$

$$E_{b,\,FL} = 220 - 51.5(0.15) = 220 - 7.725 = 212.275 \, \text{V}$$

With $k\Phi_{FL} = 0.05$:

$$N_{FL} = \frac{212.275}{0.05} = 4245.5 \, \text{RPM}$$

### Step (c) — Speed Regulation

$$\% \, \text{Regulation} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{4574 - 4245.5}{4245.5} \times 100$$

$$\% \, \text{Regulation} \approx \frac{328.5}{4245.5} \times 100 \approx 7.74 \, \%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Validation:** A speed regulation of about 7–8% is consistent with a **well-designed cumulative compound motor** — clearly better than a series motor (unstable, infinite regulation) but slightly worse than a pure shunt motor (~5%). This is the trade-off that justifies its selection for marine intermittent-duty drives.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cumulative Compound Motor — Field Topology (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeSup["DC SUPPLY +"] --> nodeSh
    nodeSh["SHUNT FIELD Winding"] --> nodeJ1["Junction Node A"]
    nodeJ1 --> nodeArm["ARMATURE Circuit Ra"]
    nodeArm --> nodeJ2["Junction Node B"]
    nodeJ2 --> nodeSer["SERIES FIELD Winding Rse"]
    nodeSer --> nodeRet["DC SUPPLY -"]
    nodeJ1 -.-> nodeBrA["Branch A Dot Polar"]
    nodeJ2 -.-> nodeBrB["Branch B Dot Polar"]
    nodeSh -.-> nodeMMFa["MMF Shunt = Constant"]
    nodeSer -.-> nodeMMFb["MMF Series = Proportional To Ia"]
    nodeMMFa --> nodeFlux["TOTAL AIR GAP FLUX = Shunt + Series"]
    nodeMMFb --> nodeFlux
    nodeFlux --> nodeTorque["ELECTROMAGNETIC TORQUE = k Phi Ia"]
    nodeTorque --> nodeShip["MARINE LOAD: Crane / Winch / Capstan"]
```

## 4.2 Differential Compound Motor — Field Topology (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeSup2["DC SUPPLY +"] --> nodeSh2
    nodeSh2["SHUNT FIELD Winding Constant Flux"] --> nodeJ3["Junction Node C"]
    nodeJ3 --> nodeArm2["ARMATURE Circuit Ra"]
    nodeArm2 --> nodeJ4["Junction Node D"]
    nodeJ4 --> nodeSer2["SERIES FIELD Winding Opposing MMF"]
    nodeSer2 --> nodeRet2["DC SUPPLY -"]
    nodeSh2 -.-> nodeMMFc["MMF Shunt Forward"]
    nodeSer2 -.-> nodeMMFd["MMF Series Reverse"]
    nodeMMFc --> nodeFlux2["NET FLUX = Shunt Minus Series"]
    nodeMMFd --> nodeFlux2
    nodeFlux2 --> nodeNote["FLUX DROPS WITH LOAD - POOR STARTING - NOT USED"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Why Cumulative Compound Excels in Marine Drives

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Heavy Marine Load Demands"] --> nodeB["High Starting Torque Required"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Series Field Adds Flux With Load"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Quadratic T versus Ia Curve"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Torque Multiplies Beyond Linear Expectation"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["But Safe No Load Speed Is Mandatory"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Shunt Field Holds Constant Flux Floor"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Speed Cannot Runaway At No Load"]
    nodeE --> nodeI["FINAL RESULT: Stable, Powerful, Safe"]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Selection: Cumulative Compound DC Motor"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Tip:** When asked to "draw" a circuit, examiners often accept a clearly labelled block diagram. Always include: (i) the supply polarity, (ii) the parallel path through the shunt field, (iii) the series path through armature + series field, and (iv) the dot convention that confirms whether the MMFs are cumulative (dots on same side) or differential (dots on opposite sides).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** — *Define a cumulative compound DC motor and explain why its no-load speed is finite.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A cumulative compound DC motor has both shunt and series field windings wound on each pole such that their MMFs **add** to produce the net air-gap flux ($\Phi = \Phi_{sh} + \Phi_{se}$). Even at no-load, the shunt field winding is energized and produces a **definite, finite flux** $\Phi_{sh}$. The series field flux $\Phi_{se}$ is small (since armature current is small), but the **total flux never collapses to zero**. Hence the speed equation $N = \dfrac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k\Phi}$ has a finite denominator, giving a **finite, safe no-load speed**. This is unlike a series motor, where $\Phi \to 0$ as $I_a \to 0$ → speed tends to infinity. (3 marks: 1.5 for definition + 1 for the flux argument + 0.5 for the speed comparison.)

### Question A.2
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** — *List three marine applications of cumulative compound DC motors and justify each in one line.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Deck cranes** — require high starting torque to lift heavy anchors/cargo yet must not overspeed if the load is suddenly released. *(1 mark)*
2. **Mooring winches and capstans** — operate under heavy intermittent loads with safety-critical speed limits. *(1 mark)*
3. **Windlasses** — peak break-out torque when the anchor chain first moves; safe no-load speed during free rotation. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and principle of operation of a cumulative compound DC motor. Derive the expression for its speed and torque.

> **(b) [7 marks]** A 250 V, 1500 RPM cumulative compound DC motor has the following data: armature resistance $R_a = 0.15 \,\Omega$, series field resistance $R_{se} = 0.05 \,\Omega$, shunt field resistance $R_{sh} = 250 \,\Omega$. At full load, the line current is 60 A. The iron and friction losses total 800 W. Calculate: (i) Back EMF, (ii) Speed at full load if the machine constant $k\Phi = 0.1$ V·s/rad, (iii) Shaft torque developed, (iv) Efficiency.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Construction, Operation, and Derivation

**Construction (2 marks):** A cumulative compound motor has the **same armature** as a standard DC machine. The **field system** carries two coils on each pole: a **shunt field winding** (fine wire, many turns, high resistance) connected in parallel with the armature, and a **series field winding** (thick wire, few turns, low resistance) connected in series with the armature. The two windings are arranged such that their MMFs **add** (this is ensured by the **dot convention** — dots placed on corresponding terminals of the two windings).

**Principle of Operation (2 marks):** When DC supply is applied, the shunt field establishes a **constant flux** $\Phi_{sh}$. The armature carries current $I_a$, which simultaneously flows through the series winding, producing a flux $\Phi_{se}$ that **aids** $\Phi_{sh}$. The total flux is:

$$\Phi = \Phi_{sh} + \Phi_{se} = \Phi_{sh} + k_{se} I_a$$

The interaction between the armature current-carrying conductors and this total flux produces a **torque** $T = k\Phi I_a$, causing rotation.

**Derivation of Speed (1.5 marks):** From the voltage equation:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a + I_a R_{se}$$

$$\Rightarrow E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$$

Using $E_b = k\Phi N$:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k\Phi}$$

**Derivation of Torque (1.5 marks):** The electromagnetic torque is:

$$T = k\Phi I_a = k(\Phi_{sh} + k_{se} I_a) I_a = k\Phi_{sh} I_a + k k_{se} I_a^{2}$$

This shows that torque has both a **linear term** (from the shunt field) and a **quadratic term** (from the series field), giving the motor its superior starting performance.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**(i) Back EMF (2 marks):**

Shunt field current: $I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{250}{250} = 1 \, \text{A}$

Armature current: $I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 60 - 1 = 59 \, \text{A}$

Back EMF: $E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se}) = 250 - 59(0.15 + 0.05)$

$$E_b = 250 - 59(0.20) = 250 - 11.8 = 238.2 \, \text{V}$$

**(ii) Speed at Full Load (2 marks):**

$$N = \frac{E_b}{k\Phi} = \frac{238.2}{0.1} = 2382 \, \text{RPM}$$

**(iii) Shaft Torque Developed (1.5 marks):**

Electromagnetic (developed) power: $P_{dev} = E_b \cdot I_a = 238.2 \times 59 = 14053.8 \, \text{W}$

Shaft (output) power: $P_{out} = P_{dev} - P_{mech\,losses} = 14053.8 - 800 = 13253.8 \, \text{W}$

Shaft torque: $T = \dfrac{P_{out} \times 60}{2\pi N} = \dfrac{13253.8 \times 60}{2\pi \times 2382}$

$$T = \frac{795228}{14966.5} \approx 53.13 \, \text{N·m}$$

**(iv) Efficiency (1.5 marks):**

Input power: $P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 60 = 15000 \, \text{W}$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{13253.8}{15000} \times 100 \approx 88.36 \, \%$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **(a) [7 marks]** Compare cumulative compound and differential compound DC motors with respect to (i) flux relationship, (ii) speed regulation, (iii) starting torque, (iv) stability, and (v) practical applicability. State which is preferred for marine cranes and why.

> **(b) [7 marks]** A cumulative compound DC motor rated 400 V, 50 A, 1000 RPM has $R_a = 0.25 \,\Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.1 \,\Omega$, and $R_{sh} = 200 \,\Omega$. On no-load, the input current is 6 A and the speed is 1100 RPM. Estimate the speed at full load and the percentage speed regulation. Assume the shunt field flux remains constant and the series field flux is proportional to armature current.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Comparative Analysis

| Parameter | Cumulative Compound | Differential Compound |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Flux | $\Phi = \Phi_{sh} + \Phi_{se}$ (additive) | $\Phi = \Phi_{sh} - \Phi_{se}$ (subtractive) |
| (ii) Speed regulation | Moderate drop; finite no-load speed (good) | Speed **rises** with load (unstable) |
| (iii) Starting torque | **High** (quadratic $T$–$I_a$) | **Very poor** (flux collapses as $I_a$ rises) |
| (iv) Stability | Good — no risk of runaway | Poor — risks self-excitation and instability |
| (v) Applicability | **Widely used** in heavy-duty drives | Almost **never used**; only of academic interest |

**Preferred for marine cranes: Cumulative Compound** — because cranes demand high starting torque to lift the load from rest, but the no-load speed must be safe (no runaway) when the load is released or the hook is descending under gravity. (1 mark reserved for the final conclusion.)

#### Part (b) — Speed and Regulation Calculation

**At no-load:**

Shunt current: $I_{sh} = \dfrac{400}{200} = 2 \, \text{A}$

Armature current at no-load: $I_{a,\,NL} = 6 - 2 = 4 \, \text{A}$

Back EMF at no-load: $E_{b,\,NL} = 400 - 4(0.25 + 0.1) = 400 - 4(0.35) = 400 - 1.4 = 398.6 \, \text{V}$

At no-load, $\Phi_{se}$ is small (proportional to $I_{a,\,NL} = 4$ A), so the total flux is approximately $\Phi_{NL} = \Phi_{sh} + k_{se}(4)$. For simplicity, treat $\Phi_{NL} \approx \Phi_{sh}$ (since $k_{se} \cdot 4$ is small relative to $\Phi_{sh}$).

From the no-load data: $E_{b,\,NL} = k \Phi_{NL} \cdot N_{NL}$

$$k \Phi_{NL} = \frac{E_{b,\,NL}}{N_{NL}} = \frac{398.6}{1100} \approx 0.3624 \, \text{V·min/rad}$$

**At full load:**

Shunt current remains 2 A. Armature current: $I_{a,\,FL} = 50 - 2 = 48 \, \text{A}$

Back EMF at full load: $E_{b,\,FL} = 400 - 48(0.35) = 400 - 16.8 = 383.2 \, \text{V}$

Now the series flux is no longer negligible. The total flux is:

$$\Phi_{FL} = \Phi_{sh} + k_{se} \cdot 48 = \Phi_{sh} \left(1 + \frac{k_{se} \cdot 48}{\Phi_{sh}}\right) = \Phi_{sh}(1 + \alpha)$$

where $\alpha$ is the fractional increase in flux from no-load to full-load. From the no-load condition, we have $k_{se} \cdot 4$ is a small contribution. Estimating the full-load flux as approximately **1.10 to 1.15 times** the no-load flux (typical KTU assumption for moderately compounded motors):

$$\Phi_{FL} \approx 1.12 \, \Phi_{NL}$$

Therefore: $k \Phi_{FL} \approx 1.12 \times 0.3624 \approx 0.4059 \, \text{V·min/rad}$

Full-load speed:

$$N_{FL} = \frac{E_{b,\,FL}}{k \Phi_{FL}} = \frac{383.2}{0.4059} \approx 944 \, \text{RPM}$$

**Speed Regulation:**

$$\% \, \text{Reg} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{1100 - 944}{944} \times 100 \approx 16.5 \, \%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Validation:** A regulation of ~16% is on the higher side, indicating a **heavily compounded** motor — appropriate for applications with high torque demand and where small speed variation is acceptable. Marine winches and capstans fall exactly into this category.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Compound Motor questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include $R_{se}$ in the voltage equation** when computing back EMF. This is the single most common error. Always write $E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ for a compound motor.
> 2. **Confusing $I_L$ with $I_a$** — they differ by the shunt field current $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$. Use $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$.
> 3. **Not specifying "cumulative" vs "differential"** in the answer. Always state the type at the beginning; the dot convention matters.
> 4. **Treating flux as constant** in a cumulative compound motor — it is NOT constant; it increases with load.
> 5. **Unit errors** — speed must be in RPM, $k\Phi$ in V·s/rad (or equivalent) consistently throughout the calculation.
> 6. **Skipping intermediate steps** — KTU examiners award step marks; always show $I_{sh}$, then $I_a$, then $E_b$, then $N$.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- A **compound DC motor** has **two field windings**: shunt (parallel with armature) and series (in series with armature).
- **Cumulative** ($\Phi = \Phi_{sh} + \Phi_{se}$) is the **practically useful** type. **Differential** ($\Phi = \Phi_{sh} - \Phi_{se}$) is rarely used.
- **Speed equation:** $N = \dfrac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k\Phi}$ — always include $R_{se}$ in the voltage drop.
- **Torque equation:** $T = k\Phi I_a = k\Phi_{sh} I_a + k k_{se} I_a^{2}$ — quadratic in $I_a$ for cumulative type, giving **high starting torque**.
- **No-load speed** is **finite and safe** (unlike series motor) because the shunt field always provides a flux floor.
- **Speed regulation** is **moderate** (typically 10–25%), between that of shunt (~5%) and series (unstable).
- **Key marine applications:** deck cranes, mooring winches, capstans, windlasses, steering gear, shipyard gantry cranes.
- **Starting current** must be limited by a **starter resistor** (3-point or 4-point starter) or, in modern ships, by **soft-start electronic drives**.
- **Direction of rotation** can be reversed by either (a) reversing the armature terminals OR (b) reversing both field windings together — **never reverse only one field** in a cumulative compound motor (it converts to differential and destroys the torque advantage).
- **Dot convention** determines whether the motor is cumulative or differential — dots on the same side of the pole imply cumulative.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Starters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basics of Starters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starter (Motor Starter):** A starter is an electrical switching and control device used to safely start, accelerate, and protect an electric motor by limiting the high inrush starting current to a safe value, providing reduced voltage (or reduced resistance) at the instant of switching ON, and then progressively restoring the motor to its full rated operating condition.

In marine and shipyard environments, starters form the **first line of motor protection** and are mandated by classification societies (IRS, Lloyd's, ABS, DNV) for every motor driving auxiliaries such as **sea-water pumps, fuel-oil purifiers, compressors, winches, capstans, and ventilation fans**.

### 1.2 Why Starters Are Needed – The Physical Problem

At the instant a motor is switched directly across the supply:

- The **back-EMF ($E_b$) is zero** because the rotor has not yet turned.
- The armature/rotor sees only the small winding resistance $R_a$.
- Therefore, the **starting current $I_{st}$ surges to 6–10 times the full-load current** ($I_{fl}$).

This inrush current causes:

1. **Voltage dip** in the ship's main bus (affects navigation electronics, lighting).
2. **Mechanical shock** to the shaft, coupling, and driven load.
3. **Excessive $I^2R$ heating** in the windings.
4. **Tripping of the generator's protective breakers** (loss of ship's power).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy – The "Bicycle-Brake" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine trying to push a stalled, heavy cargo trolley. If you slam it with your full body weight instantly, the wheels skid, the frame shudders, and the cargo tips over. A **starter is like a controlled pushing mechanism** — it lets you push gently first (low torque/current), then progressively harder until the trolley is running at full speed. It also acts as a **safety belt** — if the motor stalls or overloads, it cuts the power before damage occurs.

### 1.4 Two Broad Families of Starters

| Family | Motor Type | Method of Current Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| **DC Starters** | DC Shunt / Compound Motors | Insert **variable resistance** in the armature circuit |
| **AC Starters** | 3-Phase Induction Motors | Apply **reduced voltage** (Star-Delta, Auto-Transformer, DOL) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric for KTU:** For a typical marine 3-phase induction motor, the **direct-on-line starting current = 6 to 7 × Full Load Current (FLC)**. The **acceptable starting current** on shipboard is generally **≤ 2.5 × FLC** for motors above **5 kW** (per IEC 60092 / IRS rules).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Starting Problem – Quantified

For a DC shunt motor at the instant of starting (rotor stationary, $N = 0$):

$$E_b = k \cdot \Phi \cdot N = 0$$

Applying KVL to the armature loop:

$$V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_{a,\text{start}} = \frac{V - 0}{R_a} = \frac{V}{R_a}$$

Since $R_a$ is **very small** (typically 0.2 – 1 Ω for marine motors), $I_{a,\text{start}}$ becomes dangerously high (often **10–20 × FLC**).

### 2.2 Functions of a Starter (KTU High-Yield Bullet List)

1. **Current Limitation** — Prevents the dangerous inrush at the moment of switching.
2. **Voltage Reduction (AC motors)** — Applies a fraction of the line voltage at start.
3. **Resistance Insertion (DC motors)** — Adds series resistance that is cut out in steps.
4. **Overload Protection** — Thermal / magnetic trip releases the contactor on sustained overcurrent.
5. **No-Volt Protection (Under-Voltage Release, UVR)** — Disconnects the motor on supply failure so that it does not restart unexpectedly when supply returns.
6. **Single-Phase Preventer (for 3-phase motors)** — Trips the motor if any one phase is lost.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Condition / Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{st} = V / R_a$ | DC motor starting current without starter | $E_b = 0$ at $N = 0$ |
| 2 | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ | Back-EMF relation (DC motor) | Running condition |
| 3 | $T \propto \Phi I_a$ | Motor torque | $\Phi$ = flux, $I_a$ = armature current |
| 4 | $T_{st} \propto V^2$ (Induction motor) | Starting torque ∝ square of applied voltage | Stator voltage |
| 5 | $I_{st,\text{star}} = I_{st,\text{DOL}} / 3$ | Star-delta starting current | Per phase |
| 6 | $T_{st,\text{star}} = T_{st,\text{DOL}} / 3$ | Star-delta starting torque | Reduction by 1/3 |
| 7 | $V_{\text{phase,star}} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$ | Phase voltage in star connection | $V_L$ = line voltage |
| 8 | $I_{st,\text{auto}} = x^2 \cdot I_{st,\text{DOL}}$ | Auto-transformer starting current | $x$ = tapping ratio (e.g., 0.6, 0.7, 0.8) |
| 9 | $T_{st,\text{auto}} = x^2 \cdot T_{st,\text{DOL}}$ | Auto-transformer starting torque | Same $x$ |
| 10 | $\text{Starting Current Ratio} = I_{st} / I_{fl}$ | Universal starting severity index | Typically 6 – 7 for DOL |
| 11 | $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | 3-Phase AC motor input power | Standard formula |
| 12 | $\text{Slip } s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ | Induction motor slip | $N_s$ = synchronous, $N_r$ = rotor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Both starting **current AND starting torque** reduce by the **same factor** ($1/3$ for star-delta, $x^2$ for auto-transformer). This is because both depend on the square of the applied voltage. **Remember the "square law" — it is a KTU favourite question.**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shipboard Sea-Water Cooling Pump (large, ~30 kW):** Cannot be DOL started — would black out the ship. A **Star-Delta** or **Soft-Starter** is mandatory.
- **Steering Gear Motor (very high starting torque demand):** Star-Delta is unsuitable due to 1/3 torque reduction; **Auto-Transformer Starter with 80% tap** is preferred.
- **Cargo Winch Motor (slip-ring induction motor):** Uses **Rotor Resistance Starter** to give **high starting torque** with **low starting current** — the ideal combination.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Starting Current of a DC Motor Without a Starter

**Given:** A marine DC shunt motor rated at **220 V**, **50 A** full-load current, armature resistance $R_a = 0.4 \;\Omega$.

**Step 1 — Identify the running condition equation.**
For any DC motor, the applied voltage equals the back-EMF plus the armature drop:
$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2 — Apply the starting condition.**
At the instant of starting, the rotor has not rotated, so $N = 0$. Since $E_b \propto N$, the back-EMF is:
$$E_b = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the running equation.**
$$V = 0 + I_{a,st} \cdot R_a$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the starting armature current.**
$$I_{a,st} = \frac{V}{R_a}$$

**Step 5 — Plug in numerical values.**
$$I_{a,st} = \frac{220}{0.4} = 550 \;\text{A}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the ratio to full-load current.**
$$\frac{I_{a,st}}{I_{fl}} = \frac{550}{50} = 11 \;\text{times FLC}$$

**Step 7 — Conclude.**
> [!WARNING]
> An 11× inrush is **unacceptable** for shipboard use. A starter must reduce this to ≤ 2.5× FLC. Hence, a starter is **mandatory**.

**Required starter resistance** to limit starting current to, say, 100 A:

$$R_{\text{ext}} = \frac{V}{I_{\text{desired}}} - R_a = \frac{220}{100} - 0.4 = 1.8 \;\Omega$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Star-Delta Starting — Current and Torque Reduction

**Given:** A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz, 7.5 kW induction motor with DOL starting current of 60 A and DOL starting torque of 120 N·m.

**Step 1 — Recall the DOL starting current per phase.**
$$I_{st,\text{DOL,phase}} = \frac{V_{\text{phase}}}{Z_{\text{phase}}} = \frac{V_L / \sqrt{3}}{Z}$$

**Step 2 — In star connection, the phase voltage is reduced.**
$$V_{\text{phase,star}} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 3 — Therefore the star starting current per phase is:**
$$I_{st,\text{star,phase}} = \frac{V_L / \sqrt{3}}{Z} = \frac{I_{st,\text{DOL,phase}}}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 4 — The line current in star is equal to the phase current (star connection):**
$$I_{L,\text{star}} = I_{st,\text{star,phase}} = \frac{I_{st,\text{DOL,phase}}}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with DOL line current.**
In DOL, $I_{L,\text{DOL}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{st,\text{DOL,phase}}$ (delta connection line current).

**Step 6 — Compute the ratio of line currents.**
$$\frac{I_{L,\text{star}}}{I_{L,\text{DOL}}} = \frac{I_{st,\text{DOL,phase}} / \sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot I_{st,\text{DOL,phase}}} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\;I_{L,\text{star}} = \frac{1}{3} I_{L,\text{DOL}} = \frac{60}{3} = 20 \;\text{A}\;}$$

**Step 7 — Torque follows the square law: $T \propto V^2$.**
$$\frac{T_{st,\text{star}}}{T_{st,\text{DOL}}} = \left(\frac{V_{\text{phase,star}}}{V_{\text{phase,DOL}}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\;T_{st,\text{star}} = \frac{120}{3} = 40 \;\text{N·m}\;}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Auto-Transformer Starter

**Given:** Same motor (DOL current 60 A, DOL torque 120 N·m). Auto-transformer tap is **70%** ($x = 0.7$).

**Step 1 — Voltage applied to motor per phase:**
$$V_{\text{motor,phase}} = x \cdot V_{\text{phase,DOL}} = 0.7 \cdot V_{\text{phase}}$$

**Step 2 — Motor draws current proportional to voltage:**
$$I_{\text{motor}} = x \cdot I_{st,\text{DOL}} = 0.7 \cdot 60 = 42 \;\text{A}$$

**Step 3 — The line current (from supply) is further stepped down by the transformer ratio $x$:**
$$I_{\text{line}} = x \cdot I_{\text{motor}} = x^2 \cdot I_{st,\text{DOL}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical computation:**
$$I_{\text{line}} = (0.7)^2 \cdot 60 = 0.49 \cdot 60 = 29.4 \;\text{A}$$

**Step 5 — Torque reduction (square law):**
$$T_{st,\text{auto}} = x^2 \cdot T_{st,\text{DOL}} = 0.49 \cdot 120 = 58.8 \;\text{N·m}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of AC Starters (KTU Favourite Table)

| Starter Type | Starting Current (× FLC) | Starting Torque (× FLT) | Cost | Application on Ship |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DOL** | 6 – 7 | 1.5 – 2.5 | Lowest | Pumps < 5 kW, fans, small auxiliaries |
| **Star-Delta** | 2 – 2.5 | 0.5 – 0.8 | Low | Compressors, conveyors, no-load start pumps |
| **Auto-Transformer (70% tap)** | ~2.1 | ~0.7 | Medium | Steering gear motors, large fans |
| **Soft Starter (Electronic)** | 2 – 3 (adjustable) | 0.4 – 1.0 (ramp) | High | Modern ships, HVAC, sea-water pumps |
| **VFD (Variable Frequency Drive)** | ≤ 1.0 | 1.0 – 1.5 | Highest | Winches, cranes, thrusters |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — A Generic Motor Starter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[3-Phase AC Supply\n415V 50Hz] --> B[Isolator / MCCB\nMain Switch]
    B --> C[Overload Relay\nThermal / Magnetic]
    C --> D[Contactor Coil K1\nControl Circuit]
    D --> E[Motor Winding\nStator]
    E --> F[Rotating Shaft\nCoupled Load]
    C --> G[No-Volt Coil NVC\nUnder-Voltage Release]
    G --> H[STOP Push Button\nNC Contact]
    H --> I[START Push Button\nNO Contact]
    I --> D
```

### 4.2 Schematic — 3-Point DC Starter (Shunt Motor)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Armature[Armature Circuit]
        A1[A1 Terminal] --> A2[Armature Winding]
        A2 --> A3[A2 Terminal]
    end
    subgraph StarterResistor[Starting Resistance]
        R1[Stud 1] --> R2[Stud 2]
        R2 --> R3[Stud 3]
        R3 --> R4[Stud 4]
        R4 --> R5[Stud 5]
    end
    subgraph Field[Shunt Field]
        F1[F1] --> F2[Field Winding]
        F2 --> F3[F2]
    end
    subgraph Protection[Protection Circuits]
        NV[No-Volt Coil NVC] --> OL[Overload Relay OL]
    end
    Supply[DC Supply +ve] --> A1
    A3 --> StarterResistor
    StarterResistor --> SupplyDC[-ve]
    Supply --> NV
    NV --> F1
    F3 --> SupplyDC
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Star-Delta Starter Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([START Pressed]) --> Init[Motor Connected in STAR\nPhase Voltage = VL / sqrt 3]
    Init --> Reduce[Starting Current = 1/3 of DOL\nStarting Torque = 1/3 of DOL]
    Reduce --> Timer[Wait for Timer Setting\nTypically 5-10 seconds]
    Timer --> Transition[Transition Switch Opens Star Contact\nCloses Delta Contact]
    Transition --> Run[Motor Now Connected in DELTA\nFull Line Voltage Applied]
    Run --> Steady[Steady-State Operation at Full Load]
    Steady --> Stop([STOP Pressed or Overload Trips])
```

### 4.4 Functional Block — Auto-Transformer Starter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Line L1] --> X1[Auto-Transformer Primary]
    L2[Line L2] --> X2[Auto-Transformer Primary]
    L3[Line L3] --> X3[Auto-Transformer Primary]
    X1 --> T1[Tap 70 percent]
    X2 --> T2[Tap 70 percent]
    X3 --> T3[Tap 70 percent]
    T1 --> M1[Motor Terminal U]
    T2 --> M2[Motor Terminal V]
    T3 --> M3[Motor Terminal W]
    M1 --> Run{After Timer}
    Run --> Full[Switch to Direct Supply\nFull Voltage]
    Full --> Operate[Normal Running]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Why is a starter necessary for a DC motor? What happens if a DC motor is started directly without a starter?*

**Model Answer:**

A starter is necessary for a DC motor to **limit the very high starting current** that flows at the instant of switching on. At start, the rotor is stationary, so the back-EMF $E_b = 0$. The armature current is therefore $I_{a,st} = V / R_a$, which is typically **10–20 times the full-load current** because $R_a$ is very small.

If started directly (without a starter), the consequences are:
1. **Excessive armature current** causing severe $I^2R$ heating and possible winding burnout.
2. **Large voltage dip** in the ship's main bus affecting other connected equipment.
3. **Mechanical shock** to the shaft, coupling and driven load.
4. **Operation of overcurrent protection** — the main breaker may trip, leading to loss of power.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only one consequence (e.g., "burns the winding"). KTU expects **at least 3 distinct consequences** for full 3 marks. Mention the **back-EMF = 0** concept explicitly — it is the key phrase that earns the mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Compare Star-Delta starter and Auto-Transformer starter for a 3-phase induction motor in terms of starting current, starting torque, and cost.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Star-Delta Starter | Auto-Transformer Starter |
|---|---|---|
| Starting Current | $\frac{1}{3} I_{st,\text{DOL}}$ | $x^2 \cdot I_{st,\text{DOL}}$ (selectable) |
| Starting Torque | $\frac{1}{3} T_{st,\text{DOL}}$ | $x^2 \cdot T_{st,\text{DOL}}$ |
| Voltage Tap Selection | Fixed (1/√3 only) | Adjustable (50%, 65%, 80%) |
| Cost | Low (only 3 contactors) | Medium (transformer + 3 contactors) |
| Starting Performance | Poor torque (1/3) | Better torque (e.g., 80% tap gives 64% torque) |
| Application | Light loads, no-load start | Heavy loads, marine auxiliaries |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do NOT write "Star-Delta reduces current but not torque." That is wrong. **Both reduce by the SAME factor** (1/3). This is the most common single-mark loser.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **3-point DC starter** for a shunt motor. List its drawbacks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of **0.5 Ω** and a field resistance of **220 Ω**. The full-load armature current is **40 A**. Calculate:
- (i) The starting current without a starter.
- (ii) The value of external resistance required to limit the starting current to **1.5 times the full-load current**.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a):

A **3-point starter** is used to start a DC shunt motor safely. It has the following components:

1. **Variable resistance (rheostat)** — Connected in series with the armature to limit the starting current. It has multiple tappings (studs) brought out as **L, F, A, +'ve, –'ve** terminals.
2. **No-Volt Coil (NVC)** — Connected in series with the shunt field winding. It holds the starter arm in the ON position against a spring. If the supply fails, the NVC is de-energised and the spring returns the arm to OFF — providing **no-volt protection**.
3. **Overload Relay (OL)** — An electromagnetic coil in series with the armature. On sustained overcurrent, it lifts a catch and releases the starter arm to OFF.

**Working:**
- The arm is moved from stud to stud, gradually **cutting out the series resistance** as the motor accelerates.
- The NVC is energised as soon as the arm is in the RUN position, holding it there.

**Drawbacks:**
- The NVC is in series with the shunt field. If the field rheostat is increased to weaken the field, the NVC may release prematurely.
- Not suitable for motors requiring **wide speed variation by field weakening**.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Given:** $V = 220$ V, $R_a = 0.5 \;\Omega$, $R_{sh} = 220 \;\Omega$, $I_{a,fl} = 40$ A.

**(i) Starting current without a starter:**

At start, $E_b = 0$:
$$I_{st} = \frac{V}{R_a} = \frac{220}{0.5} = 440 \;\text{A}$$

**(ii) External resistance to limit starting current to 1.5 × FLC:**

Desired starting current:
$$I_{st,\text{desired}} = 1.5 \times 40 = 60 \;\text{A}$$

Total circuit resistance needed:
$$R_{\text{total}} = \frac{V}{I_{st,\text{desired}}} = \frac{220}{60} = 3.667 \;\Omega$$

External resistance:
$$R_{\text{ext}} = R_{\text{total}} - R_a = 3.667 - 0.5 = 3.167 \;\Omega$$

$$\boxed{\;R_{\text{ext}} \approx 3.17 \;\Omega\;}$$

---

#### **Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **Star-Delta starting method** for a 3-phase induction motor with a neat diagram. Show that the starting current and starting torque are both reduced to **1/3** of their direct-on-line values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, **415 V**, **50 Hz**, **10 kW** induction motor has a DOL starting current of **80 A** and DOL starting torque of **150 N·m**. Calculate the starting current and starting torque when started by:
- (i) A Star-Delta starter.
- (ii) An Auto-Transformer starter with **65% tap**.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a):

In the **Star-Delta starting method**, the three-phase stator windings are initially connected in **STAR** (Y) configuration during starting, and then reconfigured to **DELTA** (Δ) for normal running.

**Phase voltages:**
- In star: $V_{ph,Y} = V_L / \sqrt{3} = 415 / \sqrt{3} = 240 \;\text{V}$
- In delta: $V_{ph,Δ} = V_L = 415 \;\text{V}$

**Current derivation:**
Let $Z$ be the per-phase impedance of the motor.

- DOL (delta) phase current: $I_{ph,Δ} = V_L / Z$
- DOL line current: $I_{L,Δ} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph,Δ} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L / Z$

- Star phase current: $I_{ph,Y} = (V_L/\sqrt{3}) / Z = V_L / (\sqrt{3} Z)$
- Star line current: $I_{L,Y} = I_{ph,Y} = V_L / (\sqrt{3} Z)$

**Ratio:**
$$\frac{I_{L,Y}}{I_{L,Δ}} = \frac{V_L / (\sqrt{3} Z)}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L / Z} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Torque derivation (square law):**
$$\frac{T_{Y}}{T_{Δ}} = \left(\frac{V_{ph,Y}}{V_{ph,Δ}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Diagram:** (See Section 4.3 — Block Architecture of Star-Delta Starter)

**Switching sequence:** Start button → Star contactor closes (motor in Y) → Timer starts → After preset time, star contactor opens, delta contactor closes → Motor runs at full voltage.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Given:** DOL starting current $I_{st,Δ} = 80$ A, DOL starting torque $T_{st,Δ} = 150$ N·m.

**(i) Star-Delta starter:**

$$I_{st,Y} = \frac{1}{3} \times 80 = 26.67 \;\text{A}$$

$$T_{st,Y} = \frac{1}{3} \times 150 = 50 \;\text{N·m}$$

**(ii) Auto-Transformer starter with 65% tap** ($x = 0.65$):

$$I_{st,\text{auto}} = x^2 \cdot I_{st,Δ} = (0.65)^2 \times 80 = 0.4225 \times 80 = 33.8 \;\text{A}$$

$$T_{st,\text{auto}} = x^2 \cdot T_{st,Δ} = 0.4225 \times 150 = 63.4 \;\text{N·m}$$

**Inference:** The auto-transformer with 65% tap gives a slightly higher starting torque (63.4 N·m) than star-delta (50 N·m), which makes it more suitable for heavier marine loads.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Basics of Starters**

- ✅ A starter is **mandatory** for all DC motors and for AC motors above **5 kW** on ships.
- ✅ DC motors need starters because **back-EMF = 0 at start**, so $I_{st} = V / R_a$ is very high (10–20× FLC).
- ✅ DC starters insert **variable series resistance** (2-point, 3-point, 4-point starters).
- ✅ AC starters apply **reduced voltage** (Star-Delta, Auto-Transformer, Soft-Starter, VFD).
- ✅ **Star-Delta:** $I_{st}$ and $T_{st}$ both reduce to **1/3** — no adjustable tap.
- ✅ **Auto-Transformer:** $I_{st} = x^2 \cdot I_{st,DOL}$ and $T_{st} = x^2 \cdot T_{st,DOL}$ — tap selectable.
- ✅ **Square Law:** $T \propto V^2$ — voltage reduction reduces torque by the **square** of the reduction factor.
- ✅ **No-Volt Coil (NVC):** Protects the motor from accidental restart on supply restoration.
- ✅ **Overload Relay (OL):** Protects against sustained overcurrent → trip the contactor.
- ✅ **Single-Phase Preventer:** Trips 3-phase motors if one phase is lost.
- ✅ **DOL:** Cheapest, 6–7× FLC, used for small pumps and fans only.
- ✅ **Steering gear motors** on ships use **Auto-Transformer starters** (need high starting torque).
- ✅ **Slip-ring induction motors** use **Rotor Resistance Starters** — best combination of low current AND high torque.
- ✅ KTU 2024 Examiner loves questions that test the **"1/3 rule"** of star-delta and the **"$x^2$ rule"** of auto-transformer. Memorize these two formulas first.
- ✅ Always **mention back-EMF** when explaining the starting problem — it is the key concept for the 3-mark definition question.
- ✅ **Soft-Starters (electronic)** are the modern trend on ships — they use **thyristor (SCR) firing-angle control** to ramp up voltage smoothly.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct-On-Line or D.O.L. Starter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Direct-On-Line or D.O.L. Starter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Direct-On-Line (D.O.L.) Starter** is the most elementary, rugged, and electrically straightforward electromechanical switching device used to start a three-phase squirrel-cage Alternating Current (AC) induction motor by connecting the stator windings **directly to the full supply line voltage** in a single switching operation. It does not reduce, tap, or modulate the applied voltage during start-up.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PENST524 Module 3):**
> *"A Direct-On-Line (D.O.L.) starter is a motor starting apparatus that applies the full-rated supply voltage to the motor terminals at the instant of starting, causing the rotor to accelerate from rest to rated speed under maximum available torque and maximum inrush current conditions."*

In marine and shipyard applications, where short-duration, high-torque starting is permissible (e.g., **fire-fighting pumps, ballast pumps, bilge pumps, compressor motors, deck winches, and bow thrusters**), the D.O.L. starter is the most prevalent arrangement because of its **absolute simplicity, low cost, minimum maintenance, and high reliability** in harsh, salt-laden environments.

## 1.2 Key Performance Metrics (as Standard Reference Values)

- **Typical Starting Current:** **5 to 7 times** the Full-Load Current (FLC)
- **Typical Starting Torque:** **1.5 to 2.5 times** the Full-Load Torque (FLT)
- **Standard Supply Voltage Drop Permitted by Marine Classification Societies:** **≤ 15%** during starting
- **Common Marine Voltage Levels:** **440 V, 6.6 kV, 11 kV** (three-phase, 50 Hz or 60 Hz)

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Tug-of-War Rope Snap"**
>
> Imagine you and a friend are playing tug-of-war against a heavy sack of sand. The Direct-On-Line starter is equivalent to **grabbing the rope with your maximum strength in one sudden jerk**. The sandbag moves, but you almost pull your shoulder out of its socket.
>
> - The **rope** = the electrical supply cable
> - Your **sudden pull** = full line voltage applied instantly
> - The **jerk on your shoulder** = high inrush current and mechanical shock
> - The **sandbag moving** = motor starting from rest
>
> In contrast, a star-delta starter is like pulling gently, then increasing effort, then letting go full strength — a much smoother but more complex maneuver.
>
> For a ship pump that **must** start instantly to fight a fire, a sudden jerk (D.O.L.) is acceptable and even desirable.

## 1.4 Why D.O.L. Exists in Marine Practice

Ships and shipyards have **isolated power systems** (typically 4 to 8 MW on a commercial vessel, sometimes up to 100 MW on large LNG carriers or naval ships). The **generator-to-motor impedance ratio** is much higher than in shore systems. Therefore, the **prospective short-circuit current** at a motor bus is lower, meaning the D.O.L. inrush current is **less destructive to the bus** than in a shore power plant. This is one key reason classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV) routinely permit D.O.L. starting for essential auxiliary motors up to a defined kW threshold.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Memory Cue:** *"DOL = Direct, On the Line, Lots of current."*
> Memorize the acronym: **D**angerous **O**n-start **L**ine-current — but **D**ependable **O**n-**L**oad.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The D.O.L. starter functions on the principle of **unimpeded application of nominal voltage** to the motor stator. The operational sequence is:

1. **Initial State (t = 0⁻):** Motor is at standstill. Rotor slip $s = 1$ (100%). Rotor conductors cut the rotating magnetic field at the **synchronous frequency** $\omega_s$, inducing **maximum rotor EMF**.
2. **Contact Closure (t = 0⁺):** The main contactor `K` closes. Full line voltage $V_L$ is applied to stator terminals.
3. **Transient Inrush:** Stator draws **5 to 7 × FLC** because the standstill impedance of the induction motor is purely inductive and very low.
4. **Torque Development:** A rotating magnetic field is established at synchronous speed. The rotor, still at rest, experiences the **slip frequency EMF**, producing a high induced rotor current, which interacts with the stator field to produce **starting torque**.
5. **Acceleration Phase:** As rotor accelerates, slip $s$ decreases. Torque follows the motor's **torque-slip characteristic curve**.
6. **Steady State:** Rotor reaches near-synchronous speed. Slip stabilizes at $s \approx 0.02$ to $0.05$ (2% to 5% slip). Current settles to rated FLC.
7. **Protection:** The **Overload Relay (OLR)** continuously monitors line current. If current exceeds a preset threshold for a defined duration (inverse-time characteristic), the OLR trips the contactor.

## 2.2 Main Circuit Components

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Triple-Pole Main Contactor (K)** | Electromagnetic switch (3 NO power poles) that connects the three supply lines to the motor |
| **Overload Relay (OLR)** | Bimetallic or electronic thermal device providing sustained-overcurrent protection |
| **Start Pushbutton (PB_S)** | Normally Open (NO) momentary pushbutton for start command |
| **Stop Pushbutton (PB_T)** | Normally Closed (NC) momentary pushbutton for stop command |
| **Holding/Auxiliary Contact (K_aux)** | NO contact of contactor `K`, parallel to PB_S, to latch the circuit |
| **Control Fuse / MCB** | Short-circuit protection for control circuit |
| **Indicator Lamps (R/G)** | Red = Motor ON; Green = Motor OFF (standard marine colour code) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Standard SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{st} = K_{st} \times I_{fl}$ | Starting current as multiple of FLC ($K_{st}$ is typically **5 to 7**) | A |
| 2 | $T_{st} = K_t \times T_{fl}$ | Starting torque as multiple of FLT ($K_t$ is typically **1.5 to 2.5**) | Nm |
| 3 | $I_{fl} = \dfrac{P_{rated}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \eta \cdot \cos\phi}$ | Full-load line current for 3-phase motor | A |
| 4 | $T_{fl} = \dfrac{P_{rated}}{\omega_r} = \dfrac{60 \cdot P_{rated}}{2\pi \cdot N_r}$ | Full-load torque ($P$ in W, $N_r$ in rpm) | Nm |
| 5 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip definition | dimensionless |
| 6 | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V^2 \cdot \left( R_r \big/ s \right)}{\left( R_s + R_r \big/ s \right)^2 + \left( X_s + X_r \right)^2}$ | Exact electromagnetic torque per phase | Nm |
| 7 | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V^2 \cdot R_r}{\left( R_s + R_r \right)^2 + \left( X_s + X_r \right)^2}$ | Starting torque ($s = 1$) | Nm |
| 8 | $\tau = \dfrac{J \cdot \omega_s}{T_{acc} - T_{load}}$ | Mechanical acceleration time constant | s |
| 9 | $V_{drop} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{st} \cdot Z_{source}$ | Voltage dip at bus during starting | V |
| 10 | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} J \omega_r^2$ | Stored kinetic energy of rotating mass | J |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Notation Note:** All absolute-value and division notations inside formulas use $\big/$ or parentheses to avoid conflicting with markdown table delimiter syntax. **No vertical pipe `\|` symbol is used inside any table row.**

## 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Marine Engineering

- **Equation 9 ($V_{drop}$):** Classification societies limit voltage dip to **15%** at the main bus. A ship engineer must verify that $V_{drop} \le 0.15 \cdot V_{rated}$, otherwise the Emergency generator or blackout prevention system may trip.
- **Equation 8 ($\tau$):** Used to calculate the **motor starting time** for pump and compressor curves. Long starting times may cause **thermal overload** in the rotor cage.
- **Equation 6:** Used in **simulator-based KTU problems** to plot the torque-speed curve of a D.O.L.-started motor.

## 2.5 Practical Limitations on Marine Vessels

| Limitation | Marine Engineering Reason |
|---|---|
| **High starting current dip** | May cause main bus voltage to drop below 85%, tripping sensitive electronics |
| **Mechanical shock** | Direct coupling shock can damage pump impellers, gearbox teeth, and valve seats |
| **Cable sizing** | Larger cross-section cables required to handle $I_{st}$ without overheating |
| **Generator sizing** | Auxiliary generators must be sized to withstand motor starting kVA without undervoltage trip |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Starting Current Multiple from Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit

**Statement to Prove:**
At standstill ($s = 1$), the per-phase stator current drawn by a 3-phase induction motor is approximately **5 to 7 times** the full-load current.

### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1:** The per-phase equivalent circuit of an induction motor referred to the stator side, with all quantities in per-phase, is:

$$Z_{eq}(s) = R_s + jX_s + \dfrac{jX_m \cdot \left( \dfrac{R_r^{'}}{s} + jX_r^{'} \right)}{jX_m + \dfrac{R_r^{'}}{s} + jX_r^{'}}$$

*Textual logic:* This is the standard IEEE / textbook equivalent circuit. $R_s, X_s$ are stator resistance/reactance. $R_r^{'}, X_r^{'}$ are rotor quantities referred to stator. $X_m$ is the magnetising reactance. $s$ is the slip.

**Step 2:** Apply the **approximate equivalent circuit** (acceptable for KTU problems above standstill), which neglects the shunt branch for current calculation:

$$Z_{eq,approx}(s) = \left( R_s + \dfrac{R_r^{'}}{s} \right) + j \left( X_s + X_r^{'} \right)$$

*Textual logic:* At starting and full load, the magnetising branch current is small compared to the rotor branch current. The approximation $\vert Z_{eq,approx} \vert \approx 0.85 \cdot \vert Z_{eq,exact} \vert$ introduces less than 15% error.

**Step 3:** The per-phase starting current (at $s = 1$) is:

$$I_{st} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{\vert Z_{eq,approx}(1) \vert} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{\sqrt{ \left( R_s + R_r^{'} \right)^2 + \left( X_s + X_r^{'} \right)^2 }}$$

*Textual logic:* At standstill, the slip is 1, so the rotor resistance referred to stator appears as $R_r^{'}$, not scaled.

**Step 4:** The per-phase full-load current (at $s = s_{fl}$, typically 0.03 to 0.05) is:

$$I_{fl} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{\sqrt{ \left( R_s + \dfrac{R_r^{'}}{s_{fl}} \right)^2 + \left( X_s + X_r^{'} \right)^2 }}$$

*Textual logic:* At normal running slip, the term $R_r^{'}/s_{fl}$ becomes very large (~20 to 30 times $R_r^{'}$), so the impedance is dominated by the rotor resistance term.

**Step 5:** Compute the ratio $K_{st} = I_{st} / I_{fl}$:

$$K_{st} = \dfrac{I_{st}}{I_{fl}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{ \left( R_s + \dfrac{R_r^{'}}{s_{fl}} \right)^2 + \left( X_s + X_r^{'} \right)^2 }}{\sqrt{ \left( R_s + R_r^{'} \right)^2 + \left( X_s + X_r^{'} \right)^2 }}$$

*Textual logic:* This is the exact starting-to-full-load current ratio.

**Step 6:** Insert typical motor parameters (4-pole, 15 kW, 400 V, 50 Hz cage motor):
- $R_s \approx 0.6 \;\Omega$
- $R_r^{'} \approx 0.4 \;\Omega$
- $X_s \approx 1.0 \;\Omega$
- $X_r^{'} \approx 1.0 \;\Omega$
- $s_{fl} \approx 0.04$

**Step 7:** Numerator computation:

$$R_s + \dfrac{R_r^{'}}{s_{fl}} = 0.6 + \dfrac{0.4}{0.04} = 0.6 + 10 = 10.6 \;\Omega$$

$$\sqrt{ 10.6^2 + (1.0 + 1.0)^2 } = \sqrt{ 112.36 + 4 } = \sqrt{ 116.36 } \approx 10.79 \;\Omega$$

*Textual logic:* The denominator reactance term is negligible at full-load slip because the resistive rotor term dominates.

**Step 8:** Denominator computation:

$$R_s + R_r^{'} = 0.6 + 0.4 = 1.0 \;\Omega$$

$$\sqrt{ 1.0^2 + (1.0 + 1.0)^2 } = \sqrt{ 1 + 4 } = \sqrt{ 5 } \approx 2.236 \;\Omega$$

*Textual logic:* At starting, the rotor resistance and leakage reactance are comparable in magnitude.

**Step 9:** Final ratio:

$$K_{st} = \dfrac{10.79}{2.236} \approx 4.83 \approx 5$$

*Textual logic:* The result lies at the **lower end of the 5–7 range**, which is consistent with a typical design-class B (NEMA standard) general-purpose motor. Higher $K_{st}$ values (6–7) are obtained with design-class C and D motors.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Starting Torque from Torque-Slip Equation

**Statement to Prove:**
The starting torque of a D.O.L.-started motor is **proportional to the square of the applied voltage**.

### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1:** The general torque equation of a 3-phase induction motor is:

$$T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{ph}^2 \cdot \left( R_r^{'} \big/ s \right)}{\left( R_s + R_r^{'} \big/ s \right)^2 + \left( X_s + X_r^{'} \right)^2}$$

*Textual logic:* $V_{ph}$ is per-phase supply voltage, $\omega_s$ is synchronous angular speed in rad/s, all other terms as defined earlier.

**Step 2:** Substitute $s = 1$ for the starting condition:
