$$T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{ph}^2 \cdot R_r^{'}}{\left( R_s + R_r^{'} \right)^2 + \left( X_s + X_r^{'} \right)^2}$$

*Textual logic:* The slip-dependent term $R_r^{'}/s$ becomes simply $R_r^{'}$ at standstill.

**Step 3:** The full-load torque is:

$$T_{fl} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{ph}^2 \cdot \left( R_r^{'} \big/ s_{fl} \right)}{\left( R_s + R_r^{'} \big/ s_{fl} \right)^2 + \left( X_s + X_r^{'} \right)^2}$$

*Textual logic:* Substitute $s = s_{fl}$ into the general torque equation.

**Step 4:** Form the ratio $T_{st} / T_{fl}$:

$$\dfrac{T_{st}}{T_{fl}} = \dfrac{ R_r^{'} }{ R_r^{'} \big/ s_{fl} } \cdot \dfrac{ \left( R_s + R_r^{'} \big/ s_{fl} \right)^2 + \left( X_s + X_r^{'} \right)^2 }{ \left( R_s + R_r^{'} \right)^2 + \left( X_s + X_r^{'} \right)^2 }$$

*Textual logic:* Cancel the common terms $3/\omega_s$ and $V_{ph}^2$. The voltage-squared dependence cancels, leaving a slip-dependent mechanical ratio.

**Step 5:** Simplify using the same numerical values as Section 3.1:
- $R_r^{'} / s_{fl} = 10 \;\Omega$ (from earlier)
- $R_r^{'} = 0.4 \;\Omega$
- $(R_s + R_r^{'}/s_{fl})^2 + (X_s + X_r^{'})^2 = 10.6^2 + 4 = 116.36$
- $(R_s + R_r^{'})^2 + (X_s + X_r^{'})^2 = 1.0^2 + 4 = 5.0$

**Step 6:** Compute $T_{st} / T_{fl}$:

$$\dfrac{T_{st}}{T_{fl}} = \dfrac{0.4}{10} \cdot \dfrac{116.36}{5.0} = 0.04 \cdot 23.272 = 0.931$$

*Textual logic:* This **0.93 < 1** indicates that this particular motor has starting torque *less* than full-load torque. Such a motor cannot start against a high load — it must be started on no-load or with a soft-starter.

**Step 7:** Compute the **actual numerical values**:
- $P_{rated} = 15 \;\text{kW}$, $N_r = 1440 \;\text{rpm}$ (typical 4-pole, 50 Hz)
- $\omega_s = 2\pi \cdot 1500 / 60 = 157.08 \;\text{rad/s}$

$$T_{fl} = \dfrac{15000}{2\pi \cdot 1440/60} = \dfrac{15000}{150.8} \approx 99.5 \;\text{Nm}$$

*Textual logic:* Direct application of $T = P / \omega$.

**Step 8:** Compute $T_{st}$:

$$T_{st} = 0.931 \cdot 99.5 \approx 92.6 \;\text{Nm}$$

*Textual logic:* Starting torque equals 92.6 Nm, which is ~93% of FLT. This is a **design-class A or B** motor with moderate starting torque.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Typical D.O.L. Motor Behaviour Summary

| Motor Parameter | At Standby (Before Start) | At $t = 0^+$ (Start) | At Full Load (Steady) | After Trip (OLR Activated) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Slip $s$ | 1 (rotor at rest) | 1 | 0.03 to 0.05 | 1 (forced stop) |
| Rotor Frequency | 50 Hz | 50 Hz | 1.5 to 2.5 Hz | 50 Hz |
| Stator Current | 0 A | **5 to 7 × FLC** | 1.0 × FLC | 0 A |
| Power Factor | — | **0.2 to 0.3 (lagging)** | 0.85 to 0.9 (lagging) | — |
| Torque | 0 | **0.9 to 2.5 × FLT** | 1.0 × FLT | 0 |
| Thermal Stress | None | **Maximum** (~$I^2 R$ heating) | Normal | None |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a D.O.L. Starter System

This diagram maps the **interactions** of a marine D.O.L. starter, including its power, control, and protection subsystems as typically implemented on a ship's engine-room automation console.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Ship Main Switchboard 440V 50Hz] --> A2[Circuit Breaker MCCB with Short Circuit Protection]
    A2 --> A3[Main Contactor K Triple Pole NO]
    A3 --> A4[Overload Relay OLR Bimetallic Type]
    A4 --> A5[Three Phase Induction Motor Squirrel Cage]
    A5 --> A6[Mechanical Load Pump Compressor or Fan]

    B1[Control Supply 24V DC from UPS] --> B2[Control Fuse 2A]
    B2 --> B3[Stop Pushbutton PB_T NC]
    B3 --> B4[Start Pushbutton PB_S NO]
    B4 --> B5[Auxiliary Contact K_aux NO Latching]
    B5 --> B6[Contactor Coil K]
    B6 --> B7[Control Bus Return Neutral]

    A3 -. energizes .-> B6
    B6 -. closes .-> A3
    A3 -. energizes .-> B5

    C1[Current Transformers CT on all 3 phases] --> C2[OLR Trip Unit]
    C2 -. trips .-> B6
    C2 --> C3[Trip Indicator Lamp Red Flashing]

    D1[ESD Emergency Shutdown Loop] -. breaks .-> B3

    style A1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style A5 fill:#fff4e1
    style A6 fill:#e8f5e9
    style B1 fill:#f3e5f5
    style C2 fill:#fce4ec
    style D1 fill:#ffebee
```

**How to Read This Diagram:**
- **Blue path (top):** Power flow from main switchboard to motor.
- **Purple path (middle):** 24 V DC control circuit.
- **Pink path (right):** Protection / trip path.
- **Red path (bottom):** Emergency Shutdown (ESD) override.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — State Transition Diagram of D.O.L. Operation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OffState: Initial Condition
    OffState --> StartPressed: Operator presses PB_S
    StartPressed --> ContactClosing: K coil energises
    ContactClosing --> Starting: K main poles close
    Starting --> Accelerating: Motor develops T_start
    Accelerating --> Running: Speed reaches rated value
    Running --> OffState: Operator presses PB_T
    Running --> TrippedState: OLR detects overload
    Starting --> TrippedState: Sustained overcurrent detected
    Accelerating --> TrippedState: Stall detected
    TrippedState --> OffState: Reset OLR manually
    OffState --> [*]
    Running --> ESDOff: ESD signal received
    ESDOff --> OffState
```

**State Descriptions:**
- **OffState:** All contactors open, no current flow, indicator green.
- **Starting:** Transient state; current inrush; lasts 0.2 to 1.0 second.
- **Accelerating:** Motor accelerates from 10% to 95% of rated speed; lasts 2 to 10 seconds.
- **Running:** Steady-state rated operation; OLR armed.
- **TrippedState:** Motor locked out until manual reset.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — D.O.L. Starter Power and Control Wiring Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[L1 Supply] --- Q1[MCCB]
    P2[L2 Supply] --- Q1
    P3[L3 Supply] --- Q1
    Q1 --- K1[K Contactor Pole 1]
    Q1 --- K2[K Contactor Pole 2]
    Q1 --- K3[K Contactor Pole 3]
    K1 --- F1[Heater Element 1 of OLR]
    K2 --- F2[Heater Element 2 of OLR]
    K3 --- F3[Heater Element 3 of OLR]
    F1 --- M1[U Motor Terminal]
    F2 --- M2[V Motor Terminal]
    F3 --- M3[W Motor Terminal]

    CC[24V DC Control] --- F4[Control Fuse]
    F4 --- S1[Stop PB NC]
    S1 --- S2[Start PB NO]
    S2 --- KAux[Aux Contact NO of K]
    KAux --- CoilK[K Coil]
    CoilK --- CC2[Control Return]

    S2 -. parallel to .-> KAux

    style Q1 fill:#ffcdd2
    style K1 fill:#fff9c4
    style K2 fill:#fff9c4
    style K3 fill:#fff9c4
    style CoilK fill:#c8e6c9
```

**Engineering Notes:**
- The **start button** and **auxiliary contact** are wired in parallel (latching logic). When `K` energises, `KAux` closes and keeps the coil energised even after `PB_S` is released.
- Pressing `PB_T` (NC) breaks the latch, de-energises the coil, and the contactor drops out.
- The OLR heater elements $F_1, F_2, F_3$ carry the full motor current; their thermal mass simulates $I^2 t$ heating.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Q: Define Direct-On-Line (D.O.L.) starter. State any two advantages of using a D.O.L. starter for marine auxiliary motors.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 Marks):**
> A Direct-On-Line (D.O.L.) starter is an electromechanical switching device that connects a three-phase squirrel-cage induction motor directly to the full line voltage supply at the instant of starting, without any voltage reduction or impedance insertion in the motor circuit.
>
> **Advantages (1.5 Marks — any two):**
> 1. **Simple and rugged construction** — few moving parts, suitable for the corrosive marine environment.
> 2. **Low initial cost and minimum maintenance** — no auxiliary transformers, autotransformers, or power resistors.
> 3. **Provides the highest starting torque** (up to 2.5 times full-load torque), making it suitable for **fire pumps and emergency compressors** that must start against full back-pressure.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q: Why is a D.O.L. starter preferred for motors rated below 5 kW on board ships?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> 1. **Low starting current magnitude:** Motors below 5 kW draw only 25 to 35 A starting current at 440 V, which is well within the **short-time withstand capacity** of the ship's auxiliary bus. The resulting voltage dip is below the 15% classification limit. **(1 Mark)**
> 2. **Generator compatibility:** The starting kVA of small motors is small enough that the main generator can supply it without governor droop instability. **(1 Mark)**
> 3. **Cost and space economy:** D.O.L. starters are compact and inexpensive — important on board where engine-room space is at a premium. No need for star-delta contactor assemblies. **(1 Mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> *(i) When asked to "explain the working of a D.O.L. starter", students often forget to draw the **control circuit** with latching — only drawing the power circuit costs 4 marks. (ii) Numerical problems on starting current and torque are frequently mis-solved by forgetting the **per-phase** voltage conversion for star-connected motors. (iii) Always specify the **trip class** of the OLR (Class 10, 20, or 30) when discussing protection; examiners award 2 marks for this technical specificity.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic diagram, explain the **working principle of a Direct-On-Line (D.O.L.) starter** for a three-phase squirrel-cage induction motor used on board a ship. Discuss the role of the **holding contact** (auxiliary latching contact) and the **overload relay**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 15 kW, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole squirrel-cage induction motor has the following per-phase parameters referred to the stator: $R_s = 0.5 \;\Omega$, $R_r^{'} = 0.3 \;\Omega$, $X_s = 0.8 \;\Omega$, $X_r^{'} = 0.8 \;\Omega$. The full-load slip is 4%. Calculate the **starting current**, **full-load current**, and **starting torque** of the motor when started using a D.O.L. starter. Take $V_{ph} = 400 / \sqrt{3} \;\text{V}$. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Working Principle (7 Marks)

**Schematic Diagram (3 Marks):**
*[Student must draw the power circuit and control circuit. Refer to the Mermaid Diagram 3 in Section 4.3 for the wiring topology.]*

**Working Principle Explanation (4 Marks):**

1. **Initial State (1 Mark):** When the motor is at rest, the main contactor `K` is de-energised and its three main poles are open. The control circuit supply (24 V DC on ships) is live. The stop pushbutton `PB_T` (NC) is closed; start pushbutton `PB_S` (NO) is open; auxiliary contact `K_aux` is open.

2. **Start Command (1 Mark):** When the operator presses `PB_S` momentarily, current flows through `PB_T` → `PB_S` → `K_aux` (open, no effect) → coil `K` → return. Coil `K` energises, closing the three main poles `K1, K2, K3`, connecting the motor directly to the 440 V, 50 Hz three-phase supply.

3. **Latching Action (1 Mark):** As the contactor closes, the auxiliary NO contact `K_aux` (mechanically linked to the contactor) also closes. It is wired in parallel with `PB_S`. Now, even if the operator releases `PB_S`, current continues to flow through `PB_T` → `K_aux` → coil `K` → return. The contactor is **latched** in the ON position.

4. **Stop Command and Protection (1 Mark):** Pressing `PB_T` breaks the control circuit current, de-energises coil `K`, opens the main poles, and the motor coasts to a stop. The overload relay (OLR) continuously monitors the line current via bimetallic strips or electronic CTs. If the current exceeds the preset value for longer than the inverse-time characteristic, the OLR trips, breaking the holding circuit and de-energising the contactor.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $P_{rated} = 15000 \;\text{W}$
- $V_L = 400 \;\text{V}$, so $V_{ph} = 400 / \sqrt{3} = 230.94 \;\text{V}$
- $f = 50 \;\text{Hz}$, 4 poles → $N_s = 1500 \;\text{rpm}$, $\omega_s = 2\pi \cdot 1500 / 60 = 157.08 \;\text{rad/s}$
- $R_s = 0.5 \;\Omega$, $R_r^{'} = 0.3 \;\Omega$, $X_s = 0.8 \;\Omega$, $X_r^{'} = 0.8 \;\Omega$
- $s_{fl} = 0.04$

**Step 1: Impedance at starting (s = 1) (1 Mark):**

$$Z_{st} = \sqrt{ (R_s + R_r^{'})^2 + (X_s + X_r^{'})^2 }$$

$$Z_{st} = \sqrt{ (0.5 + 0.3)^2 + (0.8 + 0.8)^2 } = \sqrt{ 0.64 + 2.56 } = \sqrt{ 3.20 } = 1.789 \;\Omega$$

**Step 2: Starting current per phase (0.5 Mark):**

$$I_{st,ph} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{Z_{st}} = \dfrac{230.94}{1.789} = 129.09 \;\text{A}$$

**Step 3: Starting line current (0.5 Mark):**

For a star-connected motor, line current = phase current:

$$I_{st,line} \approx 129.09 \;\text{A}$$

**Step 4: Impedance at full load (s = 0.04) (1 Mark):**

$$R_{eq,fl} = R_s + \dfrac{R_r^{'}}{s_{fl}} = 0.5 + \dfrac{0.3}{0.04} = 0.5 + 7.5 = 8.0 \;\Omega$$

$$X_{eq,fl} = X_s + X_r^{'} = 0.8 + 0.8 = 1.6 \;\Omega$$

$$Z_{fl} = \sqrt{ 8.0^2 + 1.6^2 } = \sqrt{ 64 + 2.56 } = \sqrt{ 66.56 } = 8.158 \;\Omega$$

**Step 5: Full-load current per phase (0.5 Mark):**

$$I_{fl,ph} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{Z_{fl}} = \dfrac{230.94}{8.158} = 28.31 \;\text{A}$$

**Step 6: Starting current multiple (0.5 Mark):**

$$K_{st} = \dfrac{I_{st}}{I_{fl}} = \dfrac{129.09}{28.31} = 4.56$$

*Textual logic:* The motor draws approximately 4.56 times its full-load current at starting. This is at the lower end of the typical 5–7 range, indicating a **NEMA Design Class B** motor.

**Step 7: Starting torque (1.5 Marks):**

$$T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{ph}^2 \cdot R_r^{'}}{(R_s + R_r^{'})^2 + (X_s + X_r^{'})^2}$$

$$T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{157.08} \cdot \dfrac{(230.94)^2 \cdot 0.3}{3.20}$$

$$T_{st} = 0.01910 \cdot \dfrac{53333.33 \cdot 0.3}{3.20} = 0.01910 \cdot 5000 = 95.49 \;\text{Nm}$$

**Step 8: Full-load torque (1 Mark):**

$$T_{fl} = \dfrac{P_{rated}}{\omega_r} = \dfrac{15000}{2\pi \cdot 1440/60} = \dfrac{15000}{150.80} = 99.47 \;\text{Nm}$$

**Step 9: Torque ratio (0.5 Mark):**

$$K_t = \dfrac{T_{st}}{T_{fl}} = \dfrac{95.49}{99.47} = 0.96$$

*Textual logic:* The starting torque is 96% of full-load torque. The motor can start under no-load or low-load conditions only.

**Final Answer Summary (0.5 Mark):**
- $I_{st} = 129.09 \;\text{A}$, $I_{fl} = 28.31 \;\text{A}$, $T_{st} = 95.49 \;\text{Nm}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** List and explain the **main components** of a marine D.O.L. starter panel. With a functional block diagram, illustrate the **interlocking and protection features** typically provided. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **starting current, starting torque, complexity, cost, and typical marine application** of a D.O.L. starter with those of a **star-delta starter**. Under what circumstances is the D.O.L. starter strictly preferred on a ship? **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Main Components & Block Diagram (7 Marks)

**Main Components of a Marine D.O.L. Panel (4 Marks):**

1. **Moulded Case Circuit Breaker (MCCB):** Provides short-circuit and (optional) earth-fault protection. Sized at 1.5 to 2 times FLC to allow starting current.

2. **Triple-Pole Contactor (K):** Electromagnetic AC-3 duty contactor rated for the motor's FLC. Has 3 main NO poles + at least 1 NO + 1 NC auxiliary contact.

3. **Thermal Overload Relay (OLR):** Class 10, 20, or 30 (Class 20 is typical for marine). Bimetallic or electronic; has a **manual reset** to prevent auto-restart.

4. **Control Transformer (440 V / 110 V or 24 V DC):** Steps down voltage for the control circuit, isolating it from the power bus.

5. **Pushbutton Station:** Start (NO, black or green) and Stop (NC, red), flush-mounted on the starter cubicle door.

6. **Indicator Lamps:** Red (ON) and Green (OFF) with 24 V DC bulbs.

7. **Earth-Fault Relay (ELR):** For unearthed or high-impedance-earthed ship systems, detects zero-sequence current.

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):**
*[Student should draw a labelled block diagram showing: Mains → MCCB → Contactor → OLR → Motor; Control Supply → Transformer → Stop PB → Start PB || K_aux → Coil K → Neutral. The Mermaid Diagram 1 in Section 4.1 serves as the model answer.]*

#### Part (b) — Comparison Table and Justification (7 Marks)

| Parameter | D.O.L. Starter | Star-Delta Starter |
|---|---|---|
| **Starting Current** | **5 to 7 × FLC** | **1.7 to 2.3 × FLC** (≈ 1/√3 of D.O.L.) |
| **Starting Torque** | **1.5 to 2.5 × FLT** | **0.5 to 0.8 × FLT** (≈ 1/3 of D.O.L.) |
| **Complexity** | Very simple (1 contactor) | Complex (3 contactors + timer) |
| **Cost** | Low | 2.5 to 3 times D.O.L. cost |
| **Maintenance** | Minimal | Moderate (timer calibration) |
| **Typical Motor Size** | Up to ~10 kW on ship | 10 to 75 kW on ship |
| **Marine Application Examples** | Fire pumps, bilge pumps, small fans, domestic water pumps | Main sea water cooling pump, lube oil pump, larger compressors |

**Comparison Commentary (3 Marks):**
- The star-delta starter reduces starting current to ~1/√3, but it also reduces starting torque to ~1/3 because torque is proportional to $V^2$.
- D.O.L. is **strictly preferred** when the motor must start against a **high resistive load** (e.g., a fire pump with the discharge valve open, where back-pressure can be 8 to 12 bar).
- D.O.L. is also preferred for **emergency services** (SOLAS Chapter II-1 compliant fire pumps and bilge pumps) where **reliability of starting** outweighs the cost of inrush current.
- On a typical Handymax bulk carrier, the **emergency fire pump** is always D.O.L.-started because of the regulatory requirement of immediate full-pressure delivery.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — D.O.L. Starter**

- **Definition:** D.O.L. connects the motor stator to full line voltage at the instant of starting. **(1-liner recall)**
- **Starting Current:** **$I_{st} = 5$ to $7 \times I_{fl}$** (commit to memory; examiners quote KTU-stated ranges).
- **Starting Torque:** **$T_{st} = 1.5$ to $2.5 \times T_{fl}$** for standard cage motors.
- **Torque Equation:** $T = (3/\omega_s) \cdot V^2 \cdot (R_r^{'}/s) \big/ [(R_s + R_r^{'}/s)^2 + (X_s + X_r^{'})^2]$ — apply $s=1$ for starting.
- **Slip at start:** $s = 1$ (rotor at rest).
- **Main Components:** MCCB + Contactor + OLR + Start/Stop pushbuttons + Holding contact.
- **Latching:** Auxiliary NO contact of contactor wired in parallel with Start PB.
- **OLR Trip Class:** Class 10, 20, or 30 — Class 20 is the marine standard.
- **Marine Classification Rule:** Voltage dip on starting must be **≤ 15%** of nominal.
- **Voltage-Dependence:** Starting torque $\propto V^2$. D.O.L. gives maximum torque.
- **Common Marine Motors on D.O.L.:** Fire pumps, bilge pumps, small compressors, sanitary pumps, domestic fresh-water pumps.
- **Where NOT to use D.O.L.:** Large motors (> ~10 kW) on weak ship buses; motors driving high-inertia loads (large fans, large compressors) where starting time would be too long for the OLR trip curve.
- **Protections Typically Provided:** Short-circuit (MCCB), Overload (OLR), Earth-fault (ELR), Single-phasing protection (built into modern electronic OLRs).
- **Control Voltage Standard on Ships:** **24 V DC** (from UPS) or **110 V AC**.
- **Indicator Convention:** **Red = ON, Green = OFF** (IACS standard).
- **Energy Equation:** Kinetic energy at full speed $E = (1/2) J \omega_r^2$ determines starting time and OLR trip margin.
- **Quick Sanity Check Formula:** $I_{fl} \approx P_{kW} \cdot 2 \;\text{A}$ per kW (for 400 V, 3-phase, $\eta \cos\phi \approx 0.85$).

> [!TIP]
> **Toppers' Tip for KTU 2024:**
> When a numerical problem gives you a "starting current multiple" and asks for the **per-phase** resistance or impedance, always remember to use **$V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$** for a star-connected motor. The most common error in KTU answer sheets is using line voltage instead of phase voltage, which inflates the answer by exactly a factor of $\sqrt{3}$ and leads to a 1-mark deduction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Star-Delta Starter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Star-Delta Starter # ⚙️ Star-Delta (Y-Δ) Starter — Marine Motor Starting Systems > [!NOTE] > **Module 3 Focus:** This topic falls under *Electrical Motors & Motor Starting Methods in Marine Applications*, where shipboard induction motors (pumps, compressors, ventilation fans, winches auxiliaries) demand reduced-voltage starting to comply with marine class rules (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS) and to protect the vessel's limited-capacity generator network. --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** A **Star-Delta (Y–Δ) Starter** is a reduced-voltage, electromechanical (or static electronic) motor starting arrangement used for three-phase squirrel-cage induction motors. In this method, the motor stator winding terminals are initially connected in **star (Y) configuration**, applying a reduced phase voltage equal to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ of the line voltage. After a preset acceleration period (typically 3–10 seconds), the connections are automatically transferred to **delta (Δ) configuration**, applying the full rated line voltage across each phase to produce normal running torque. In KTU 2024 parlance, the star-delta starter is classified as a **two-stage reduced voltage starter** belonging to the family of *primary impedance starting methods* — specifically utilizing winding reconfiguration rather than external resistors or autotransformers. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Clutch-and-Gear Analogy" Think of a Star-Delta starter exactly like a **manual-gear car starting from rest**: | Car Mechanism | Star-Delta Equivalent | |---|---| | First gear (low torque, low engine load) | **Star connection** — low voltage, low starting current, low torque | | Releasing the clutch gradually | **Acceleration time-delay relay** (3–10 s) | | Shifting to top gear (full power) | **Delta transition** — full voltage, full running torque | | Clutch pedal during transition | **Transition resistors/contactors** preventing open-circuit switching (in closed-transition Y-Δ) | Just as a car cannot move in top gear from a standstill without stalling, an induction motor drawing full voltage would draw **6 to 8 times its rated current** at start, causing: - Voltage dip on the ship's bus - Tripping of generator circuit breakers - Mechanical stress on the propeller-shaft auxiliaries - Excessive cable heating in confined marine switchboards ### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Must-Memorize Bold Constants) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Golden Ratios of the Y-Δ Starter:** > 1. **Starting Voltage Ratio:** $V_{\text{phase,star}} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ → voltage reduced to **57.7 %** of line voltage > 2. **Starting Current Ratio:** $I_{\text{star}} = \frac{1}{3} I_{\text{direct-on-line}}$ → current reduced to **33.3 %** of DOL current > 3. **Starting Torque Ratio:** $T_{\text{star}} = \frac{1}{3} T_{\text{direct-on-line}}$ → torque reduced to **33.3 %** of DOL torque The factor **$\frac{1}{3}$** in current/torque is the *single most asked quantity* in KTU examinations for this module. ### 1.4 Where It Is Used Onboard a Vessel - **Sea-water cooling pumps** (essential service) - **Fuel-oil transfer pumps** - **Lubricating-oil pumps** (main engine and generators) - **Compressors** for starting-air systems - **HVAC sea-water and fresh-water circulating fans** - **Ballast pumps** (medium-power ratings) - **Engine-room ventilation fans** > [!NOTE] > **Marine Class Restriction:** The Star-Delta starter is **NOT** suitable for high-inertia, high-load starting scenarios such as **cargo winches, capstans, or deck cranes** — these require direct-on-line (DOL) or autotransformer starters because the 1/3 torque is insufficient to overcome the static friction of loaded machinery. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Philosophy — The "Why" Behind the Topology The induction motor at standstill behaves like a **transformer with a short-circuited secondary**, drawing a very heavy inrush current. The rotor has not yet cut any flux, so the slip $s = 1$, and the rotor circuit impedance is purely resistive. By reducing the applied stator voltage, we directly reduce this magnetizing/inrush current linearly. However, the **starting torque** of an induction motor is proportional to the **square of the applied voltage**: $$T \propto V^2$$ So when voltage drops to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$, torque drops to $\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{3}$. This trade-off — **reduced current at the cost of reduced starting torque** — is the fundamental engineering compromise of the Star-Delta starter. ## 2.2 Two Operational Stages (Exam-Favourite) ### **Stage 1 — Star (Y) Acceleration Phase** - Main contactor (K1) closes, Star contactor (K2) closes, Delta contactor (K3) remains OPEN. - Each stator winding receives $\frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ across its terminals. - Phase currents are reduced, so line current drawn from the bus is one-third of DOL. - Motor accelerates; rotor flux builds; back-EMF begins to oppose applied voltage. - A **timing relay** (pneumatic, electronic, or digital) is set to 3–10 seconds. ### **Stage 2 — Delta (Δ) Running Phase** - Star contactor (K2) opens. - After a short transition dead-band (open transition) or through resistors (closed transition), Delta contactor (K3) closes. - Each winding now receives the **full line voltage $V_L$**. - Motor continues accelerating to rated speed under full torque capability. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Open-Transition vs Closed-Transition:** > - **Open-transition Y-Δ:** Brief open-circuit interruption during changeover → current spike and torque dip. Cheaper, simpler. > - **Closed-transition Y-Δ:** A set of transition resistors (or auxiliary contactor) maintains current path during switching → smoother changeover, used for high-power marine motors (> 55 kW). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Print This!) > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates every KTU 2024 exam-relevant formula for the Star-Delta starter. All quantities are per-phase unless stated otherwise. | # | Quantity | Star (Y) Connection | Delta (Δ) Connection | Relationship | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Phase Voltage | $V_{\text{ph,Y}} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ | $V_{\text{ph,Δ}} = V_L$ | $V_{\text{ph,Δ}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{\text{ph,Y}}$ | | 2 | Phase Current | $I_{\text{ph,Y}} = I_{L,Y}$ | $I_{\text{ph,Δ}} = \frac{I_{L,Δ}}{\sqrt{3}}$ | Current split in delta | | 3 | Line Current | $I_{L,Y} = \frac{V_{\text{ph,Y}}}{Z_{\text{ph}}} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{\text{ph}}}$ | $I_{L,Δ} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{\text{ph,Δ}} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L}{Z_{\text{ph}}}$ | $I_{L,Δ} = 3 \cdot I_{L,Y}$ | | 4 | Starting Current vs DOL | $I_{L,Y,\text{start}} = \frac{1}{3} I_{\text{DOL}}$ | $I_{L,Δ,\text{start}} = I_{\text{DOL}}$ (briefly during switch) | 1/3 reduction is the design basis | | 5 | Starting Torque | $T_{Y,\text{start}} = \frac{1}{3} T_{\text{DOL}}$ | $T_{Δ,\text{start}} = T_{\text{DOL}}$ | $T \propto V^2$ | | 6 | Power (3-phase) | $P_Y = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_{L,Y} \cdot \cos\phi$ | $P_Δ = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_{L,Δ} \cdot \cos\phi$ | Same rated power at lower current in Δ | | 7 | Impedance seen by source | $Z_{\text{eq,Y}} = 3 Z_{\text{ph}}$ | $Z_{\text{eq,Δ}} = \frac{Z_{\text{ph}}}{3}$ | Star impedance is 3× delta | | 8 | Transition Time | $t_{\text{tr}} = 3 \text{ to } 10 \text{ s}$ | Not applicable | Depends on load inertia | | 9 | Voltage Reduction Factor | $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577$ | $1$ | Star applies 57.7 % of $V_L$ | | 10 | Torque Reduction Factor | $\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$ | $1$ | Squared voltage law | ## 2.4 Real-World Marine Engineering Utility In ship electrical design, the Star-Delta starter is the **default workhorse** for motors in the **5.5 kW to 75 kW** range — a sweet spot that covers 60–70 % of the auxiliary motors on a typical merchant vessel. Marine classification societies (e.g., **Lloyd's Register Rules for Ships, Pt. 6 Ch. 2**) specifically recommend reduced-voltage starting for motors exceeding **15 kW** to limit the **3-second voltage dip** on the main switchboard bus, which is the worst-case transient that emergency generators and essential service motors must ride through. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Section Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is fully written. No shortcuts. KTU 2024 expects complete derivation trails for 7-mark sub-questions in Part B. ## 3.1 Derivation: Starting Current Reduction in Star Connection ### Given - A 3-phase, $V_L$ line-voltage, squirrel-cage induction motor with per-phase stator impedance $Z_{\text{ph}}$ (at standstill, $s=1$). - The motor is connected in **star** for starting. ### Step 1 — Identify Phase Voltage in Star In star connection, the line voltage $V_L$ is shared between two phase windings in series across any two lines. Therefore: $$V_{\text{ph,Y}} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$$ ### Step 2 — Write Phase Current in Star The current through each phase winding is the phase voltage divided by the per-phase impedance: $$I_{\text{ph,Y}} = \frac{V_{\text{ph,Y}}}{Z_{\text{ph}}} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{\text{ph}}}$$ ### Step 3 — Relate Line Current to Phase Current in Star In star connection, the **line current equals the phase current** because each line is in series with exactly one phase winding: $$I_{L,Y} = I_{\text{ph,Y}} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{\text{ph}}}$$ ### Step 4 — Compare with Direct-On-Line (Delta) Starting In direct-on-line starting, the motor windings are in delta. Each phase winding sees the full line voltage: $$I_{\text{ph,Δ, DOL}} = \frac{V_L}{Z_{\text{ph}}}$$ The line current in delta is $\sqrt{3}$ times the phase current: $$I_{L,\text{DOL}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{\text{ph,Δ, DOL}} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L}{Z_{\text{ph}}}$$ ### Step 5 — Form the Ratio $$\frac{I_{L,Y}}{I_{L,\text{DOL}}} = \frac{\dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{\text{ph}}}}{\dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L}{Z_{\text{ph}}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \sqrt{3}} = \frac{1}{3}$$ $$\boxed{I_{L,Y} = \frac{1}{3} I_{L,\text{DOL}}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** The starting current drawn from the ship's bus is reduced to **33.3 %** of the DOL value, satisfying marine class requirement that the **busbar voltage dip not exceed 15 %** during motor starting. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Starting Torque Reduction in Star Connection ### Given - The torque of an induction motor at slip $s$ is given by: $$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{\text{ph}}^2 \cdot (R_r / s)}{(R_s + R_r / s)^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}$$ where $R_s, R_r, X_s, X_r$ are stator/rotor resistance and reactance, and $\omega_s$ is the synchronous angular speed. ### Step 1 — Observe the Voltage Dependence At standstill ($s=1$) and for typical motors where $X_s + X_r \gg R_s + R_r$: $$T_{\text{start}} \approx \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{\text{ph}}^2}{(X_s + X_r)^2}$$ So torque is **proportional to the square of the phase voltage**. ### Step 2 — Substitute Star Phase Voltage $$T_{Y,\text{start}} \propto V_{\text{ph,Y}}^2 = \left(\frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 = \frac{V_L^2}{3}$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute Delta Phase Voltage (DOL) $$T_{\text{DOL}} \propto V_{\text{ph,Δ}}^2 = V_L^2$$ ### Step 4 — Form the Ratio $$\frac{T_{Y,\text{start}}}{T_{\text{DOL}}} = \frac{V_L^2 / 3}{V_L^2} = \frac{1}{3}$$ $$\boxed{T_{Y,\text{start}} = \frac{1}{3} T_{\text{DOL}}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Examination Style) **Problem:** A 3-phase, **440 V, 50 Hz, 30 kW**, squirrel-cage induction motor has a full-load current of **52 A** and a DOL starting current of **6 × full-load current**. The motor is started using a Star-Delta starter. Calculate: (a) The starting current drawn from the line in the star connection. (b) The starting torque as a percentage of full-load torque, given that the DOL starting torque is **2.0 × full-load torque**. (c) The phase voltage across each stator winding in the star connection. ### Part (a) — Star Starting Line Current $$I_{L,\text{DOL}} = 6 \times I_{\text{FL}} = 6 \times 52 = 312 \text{ A}$$ Using the Y-Δ current ratio: $$I_{L,Y} = \frac{1}{3} \times I_{L,\text{DOL}} = \frac{1}{3} \times 312 = 104 \text{ A}$$ > **Answer:** $I_{L,Y} = 104$ A ### Part (b) — Star Starting Torque $$T_{\text{DOL,start}} = 2.0 \times T_{\text{FL}}$$ $$T_{Y,\text{start}} = \frac{1}{3} \times T_{\text{DOL,start}} = \frac{1}{3} \times 2.0 \times T_{\text{FL}} = 0.667 \cdot T_{\text{FL}}$$ As a percentage of full-load torque: $$T_{Y,\text{start}} = 66.7\% \text{ of } T_{\text{FL}}$$ > **Answer:** $T_{Y,\text{start}} = 66.7\% \, T_{\text{FL}}$ ### Part (c) — Phase Voltage in Star $$V_{\text{ph,Y}} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{440}{\sqrt{3}} = 254.03 \text{ V}$$ > **Answer:** $V_{\text{ph,Y}} \approx 254$ V --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Power & Control Architecture (Y-Δ Starter) > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block renders a **block-level functional architecture flow** describing the power circuit topology, the control contactor logic, and the timing transitions of a marine Star-Delta starter. This is a KTU-compliant representation of the start-run logic. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SUPPLY [" MARINE 3-PHASE SUPPLY (440 V / 50 Hz) "] BUS["MAIN SWITCHBOARD BUS
L1 L2 L3"] end subgraph POWER [" POWER CIRCUIT (3-PHASE CONTACTORS) "] K1["K1 : MAIN CONTACTOR
Normally Open"] K2["K2 : STAR CONTACTOR
Normally Open"] K3["K3 : DELTA CONTACTOR
Normally Open"] MOTOR["3-PHASE INDUCTION MOTOR
U1-U2 V1-V2 W1-W2"] end subgraph CONTROL [" CONTROL CIRCUIT (24 V DC / 110 V AC) "] PB1["PB1 : START PUSH BUTTON"] PB2["PB2 : STOP PUSH BUTTON"] TR["TR : TIMING RELAY
Set 3 to 10 seconds"] OL["OL : OVERLOAD RELAY"] end BUS -- L1 --> K1 BUS -- L2 --> K1 BUS -- L3 --> K1 K1 --> MOTOR K2 -.shorts windings.- MOTOR K3 -- in series with windings --> MOTOR K1 -.aux contact.- TR TR -.delayed NO contact.- K2 PB1 --> K1 PB2 --> K1 OL -.trip.- K1 ``` ## 4.2 Functional Stage Diagram (Y → Δ Transition State Machine) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> OFF OFF --> STAR_START: Press PB1
K1 ON, K2 ON, K3 OFF STAR_START --> ACCEL: Timer running
Motor accelerates
I = 0.33 I_DOL
T = 0.33 T_DOL ACCEL --> TRANSITION: Timer expires
t = 3 to 10 s reached TRANSITION --> DELTA_RUN: K2 OFF, K3 ON
Open or closed transition DELTA_RUN --> OFF: Press PB2
K1 OFF, K3 OFF OFF --> [*] ``` ## 4.3 Phasor & Topology Reference (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Y_Topology [" STAR Y CONNECTION "] Y_U["U1"] --- Y_N["STAR POINT N"] Y_V["V1"] --- Y_N Y_W["W1"] --- Y_N Y_OUT["Line currents IL flow into U1, V1, W1"] end subgraph D_Topology [" DELTA CONNECTION "] D_U["U1"] --- D_W2["W2"] D_W2 --- D_V["V1"] D_V --- D_U2["U2"] D_U2 --- D_W["W1"] D_OUT["Line currents IL flow into U1, V1, W1"] end Y_Topology -- "TIMING RELAY TRANSFER" --> D_Topology ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** > - In the **star** connection, all three winding "tail" terminals (U2, V2, W2) are shorted at a common neutral point. Each phase gets $V_L/\sqrt{3}$. > - In the **delta** connection, U2 is shorted to W1, V2 to U1, and W2 to V1. Each phase gets the full $V_L$. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > The following questions model the KTU 2024 ESE pattern: 2 marks for direct recall, 3 marks for analytical short answer, and 14-mark Part-B with internal choice. --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define a Star-Delta starter. State two advantages and one disadvantage of using a Star-Delta starter for starting a 3-phase induction motor in a ship.** **Model Answer:** > A **Star-Delta (Y-Δ) starter** is a reduced-voltage motor starting method in which the stator windings of a three-phase induction motor are initially connected in **star (Y) configuration** to reduce the applied phase voltage to $\frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$, and then reconfigured into **delta (Δ) configuration** after a preset acceleration interval to apply the full line voltage for normal running. > > **Two Advantages:** > 1. **Reduced starting current:** The line current during starting is one-third of the direct-on-line (DOL) starting current, minimizing voltage dip on the marine switchboard bus and protecting generator stability. > 2. **Economical and simple:** It uses no external resistors or autotransformers — only three contactors and a timing relay — making it low-cost and easy to maintain in shipboard motor control centres (MCCs). > > **One Disadvantage:** > 1. **Reduced starting torque:** The starting torque is also reduced to one-third of the DOL starting torque, making it unsuitable for high-inertia or high-friction marine loads such as cargo winches or compressors against head pressure. --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Why is the starting torque of an induction motor reduced to one-third when started in star connection, while the starting current is also reduced to one-third? Justify with the relevant relationship.** **Model Answer:** The torque of an induction motor is proportional to the **square of the applied phase voltage**: $$T \propto V_{\text{ph}}^2$$ In star connection, the phase voltage is reduced by a factor of $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$: $$V_{\text{ph,Y}} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$$ Therefore, the starting torque reduces by a factor of $\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{3}$: $$T_{Y,\text{start}} = \frac{1}{3} T_{\text{DOL}}$$ The starting current, on the other hand, is **linearly proportional to the phase voltage** (from Ohm's law applied to the stator impedance at standstill), and the additional $\sqrt{3}$ factor between line and phase current in delta gives a **net reduction of $\frac{1}{3}$** in line current. Thus, both current and torque are reduced to one-third, but for mathematically different reasons — current due to **linear V and KCL ratio**, torque due to **quadratic V relationship**. --- ## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 scheme mandates an **internal choice** in Part B. You must attempt **either Option A or Option B**. --- ### **Q3 (A).** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Module 3, 14 Marks] **(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat power and control circuit diagram, explain the operation of a Star-Delta starter for a 3-phase induction motor used for a marine sea-water cooling pump. State the function of each contactor and the timing relay. **(b)** [7 Marks] A 3-phase, **415 V, 50 Hz, 22 kW** induction motor has a full-load current of **42 A** and a DOL starting current of **5.5 times** the full-load current. If the motor is started using a Star-Delta starter, calculate: (i) The starting current in star connection. (ii) The starting current in delta connection (transient peak). (iii) The starting current as a percentage of the rated full-load current. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Operation and Circuit Diagram:** The Star-Delta starter consists of three main contactors — **K1 (main), K2 (star), K3 (delta)** — and a **timing relay TR**, all housed in a motor control centre (MCC) panel on the ship. **Step 1 — Initial Star Starting:** - When the operator presses **START push button (PB1)**, the control coil of **K1** energizes, closing its main power contacts. The line current from the switchboard flows through K1 to the motor terminals. - Simultaneously, **K2 (star contactor)** closes, short-circuiting the three motor winding tail terminals (U2, V2, W2) together at a common star point. Each winding now receives only $V_L / \sqrt{3} \approx 240$ V (for $V_L = 415$ V). - **K3 (delta contactor)** is mechanically and electrically interlocked to be OPEN at this stage, ensuring no short-circuit across the supply lines. **Step 2 — Acceleration Phase:** - The motor accelerates in star connection. The starting current is limited to one-third of the DOL value, reducing the voltage dip on the marine busbar. - The **timing relay TR** starts counting as soon as K1 closes. It is preset (3–10 s) according to the pump's load inertia. **Step 3 — Transition to Delta:** - When the preset time elapses, TR's delayed contact opens the **K2 star contactor** coil, releasing the star-point short. - After a brief dead-band (open transition) or through transition resistors (closed transition), TR energizes the **K3 delta contactor** coil. - K3 closes, connecting the windings in delta so each phase receives the full 415 V. The motor continues accelerating to rated speed under full torque. **Step 4 — Running and Protection:** - The motor continues in delta until **STOP (PB2)** is pressed, which de-energizes K1, opening the main contactor. - An **overload relay (OL)** monitors stator current; if sustained overcurrent is detected, OL trips and disconnects K1, protecting the motor from thermal damage — a critical safety feature in unattended machinery spaces. **Function of Each Component:** | Component | Function | |---|---| | K1 (Main) | Connects the motor to the marine busbar | | K2 (Star) | Shorts winding tails to form star point during starting | | K3 (Delta) | Reconfigures windings into delta for full-voltage running | | TR (Timer) | Delays the star-to-delta transition by 3–10 seconds | | OL (Overload) | Trips the main contactor on sustained overcurrent | | PB1 / PB2 | Manual START / STOP push buttons | | Interlocks | Mechanical/electrical interlocks prevent K2 and K3 closing simultaneously (would short-circuit the supply) | **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **(i) Starting current in star connection:** $$I_{\text{DOL}} = 5.5 \times I_{\text{FL}} = 5.5 \times 42 = 231 \text{ A}$$ $$I_{L,Y} = \frac{1}{3} \times I_{\text{DOL}} = \frac{1}{3} \times 231 = 77 \text{ A}$$ > **Answer:** $I_{L,Y} = 77$ A **(ii) Starting current in delta connection (transient peak during switchover):** The motor is already at partial speed when delta closes. The instantaneous current drawn is approximately the DOL starting current (motor momentarily behaves as at standstill for the re-applied full voltage): $$I_{L,Δ} = I_{\text{DOL}} = 231 \text{ A}$$ > **Answer:** $I_{L,Δ} = 231$ A (transient) **(iii) Starting current as a percentage of full-load current (star):** $$\% I_{\text{start,star}} = \frac{I_{L,Y}}{I_{\text{FL}}} \times 100 = \frac{77}{42} \times 100 = 183.3\%$$ > **Answer:** $183.3\%$ of full-load current --- ### **Q3 (B).** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Module 3, 14 Marks] **(a)** [7 Marks] Compare the Star-Delta starter with the Direct-On-Line (DOL) starter and the Autotransformer starter in terms of starting current, starting torque, cost, and typical marine application. Tabulate your answer. **(b)** [7 Marks] Derive the relationship showing that the starting current of a 3-phase induction motor in star connection is one-third of its DOL starting current. State the underlying assumption. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Comparison Table:** | Parameter | DOL Starter | Star-Delta Starter | Autotransformer Starter | |---|---|---|---| | **Starting Voltage** | 100 % of $V_L$ | 57.7 % of $V_L$ ($V_L/\sqrt{3}$) | Adjustable: 50 %, 65 %, 80 % of $V_L$ | | **Starting Current (% of DOL)** | 100 % (6–8 × $I_{FL}$) | 33.3 % of DOL | 25–64 % of DOL (depends on tap) | | **Starting Torque (% of DOL)** | 100 % (1.5–2.5 × $T_{FL}$) | 33.3 % of DOL | 25–64 % of DOL (proportional to $V^2$) | | **Cost** | Lowest (one contactor) | Moderate (three contactors + timer) | Highest (autotransformer + contactors) | | **Complexity** | Simplest | Moderate | Most complex | | **Typical Marine Application** | Small auxiliary pumps, fans (< 5 kW) | Sea-water cooling pumps, FO/LO transfer pumps, compressors (5.5–75 kW) | Large compressors, bow thruster motors (> 75 kW) | | **Closed-Transition Option** | Not applicable | Available (with resistors) | Naturally closed-transition | | **Class Rule Compliance** | Fails for motors > 15 kW | Passes for moderate ratings | Passes for high-power ratings | > **Conclusion for Marine Use:** The Star-Delta starter occupies the **middle ground** — more sophisticated than DOL but far cheaper than autotransformer — making it the most widely used reduced-voltage starter in commercial shipping. **Part (b) — Derivation:** **Assumption:** The motor's per-phase stator impedance $Z_{\text{ph}}$ at standstill ($s = 1$) is constant and identical in both star and delta configurations (i.e., the winding connections are ideal, with no additional impedance introduced). **Step 1 — Star Connection Phase Voltage:** $$V_{\text{ph,Y}} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$$ **Step 2 — Phase and Line Current in Star:** $$I_{\text{ph,Y}} = \frac{V_{\text{ph,Y}}}{Z_{\text{ph}}} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{\text{ph}}}$$ Since in star, line current = phase current: $$I_{L,Y} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{\text{ph}}}$$ **Step 3 — DOL (Delta) Connection Phase Voltage:** $$V_{\text{ph,Δ}} = V_L$$ **Step 4 — Phase and Line Current in Delta (DOL):** $$I_{\text{ph,Δ}} = \frac{V_L}{Z_{\text{ph}}}$$ In delta, line current is $\sqrt{3}$ times the phase current: $$I_{L,\text{DOL}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{\text{ph,Δ}} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L}{Z_{\text{ph}}}$$ **Step 5 — Ratio:** $$\frac{I_{L,Y}}{I_{L,\text{DOL}}} = \frac{\dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{\text{ph}}}}{\dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L}{Z_{\text{ph}}}} = \frac{1}{(\sqrt{3})^2} = \frac{1}{3}$$ $$\boxed{I_{L,Y} = \frac{1}{3} I_{L,\text{DOL}}}$$ Hence proved. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in the Star-Delta question:** > 1. **Confusing line current with phase current in delta:** The most common error is writing $I_{L,Δ} = V_L / Z_{\text{ph}}$ instead of $I_{L,Δ} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L / Z_{\text{ph}}$. This yields a wrong $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ ratio instead of $\frac{1}{3}$. **Always remember:** In delta, $I_L = \sqrt{3} I_{\text{ph}}$. > 2. **Forgetting the transition spike:** During the open-transition Y-Δ switch, there is a brief current surge. Students often assume the motor is at rated speed before delta closes, leading to underestimated transient current. > 3. **Applying $T \propto V$ (linear) instead of $T \propto V^2$ (square):** Torque is **quadratic** in voltage, not linear. Writing $T_{Y,\text{start}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} T_{\text{DOL}}$ is a **classic 2-mark deduction**. > 4. **Omitting the interlock mention:** KTU 2024 expects explicit mention of **mechanical and electrical interlocks** between K2 (star) and K3 (delta) contactors. Forgetting this loses 1 mark. > 5. **Confusing starting torque with pull-up torque or breakdown torque:** Y-Δ starter affects *starting* torque only. The running torque in delta is unaffected. > 6. **Not stating the assumption of constant $Z_{\text{ph}}$:** In the derivation, examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for stating the underlying assumption. Missing it = 1 mark lost. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **30-Second Revision Punch-List for the KTU Exam Hall:** - [x] **Definition:** Y-Δ starter starts a 3-phase induction motor in star, then transfers to delta. - [x] **Three Golden Ratios:** Voltage ↓ to $1/\sqrt{3}$, Current ↓ to $1/3$, Torque ↓ to $1/3$. - [x] **Three Contactors:** K1 (main), K2 (star), K3 (delta) — all interlocked. - [x] **One Timer:** TR (3–10 s) controls the transition interval. - [x] **Open vs Closed Transition:** Open = simple, brief interruption; Closed = resistors, smoother. - [x] **Power Range:** Suitable for 5.5 kW to 75 kW motors on ships. - [x] **Marine Loads Used For:** Sea-water pumps, FO/LO pumps, compressors, HVAC fans. - [x] **NOT Used For:** Winches, capstans, cranes (high-inertia / high-friction loads). - [x] **Current Formula:** $I_{L,Y} = V_L / (\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{\text{ph}})$. - [x] **Delta Line-Phase Relationship:** $I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{\text{ph}}$ and $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{\text{ph}}$. - [x] **Torque Squared Law:** $T \propto V^2$ — must write the *square*, not linear. - [x] **Assumption:** Constant per-phase impedance at standstill in both star and delta. - [x] **Class Rule Trigger:** Used when DOL starting causes > 15 % bus voltage dip. - [x] **Power Savings:** No external resistors means no $I^2 R$ loss during starting — efficient. - [x] **Interlocks:** Mechanical AND electrical, between K2 and K3 (prevent supply short circuit). - [x] **Overload Protection:** Always via OL relay that trips K1. ---

topics.title: Autotransformer Starter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Autotransformer Starter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Autotransformer Starter** is a reduced-voltage starting device used for three-phase squirrel-cage induction motors, in which a multi-tapped **three-phase autotransformer** is temporarily inserted in series between the ship's main switchboard and the motor stator terminals during the starting period. The autotransformer steps down the line voltage to a predetermined fraction (typically **50%**, **65%**, or **80%** of rated voltage), thereby limiting the locked-rotor current drawn from the marine power network to within the generator's short-circuit capability. Once the motor accelerates to a near-synchronous speed, the autotransformer is bypassed and the motor is connected directly across the line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Cue:** An autotransformer starter is a *closed-transition* (or *open-transition* depending on contactor arrangement) reduced-voltage starter in which the **motor terminals are fed from a tapped autotransformer winding**, not from a star point of the motor itself — this is the primary distinction from a Star-Delta starter.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a young sailor trying to push a massive capstan (the cylindrical vertical winch on a ship's deck used to haul anchor chains). If you try to push with full force from rest, the chain links don't move, your hands slip, and you injure yourself. Instead, your senior sailor trains you: start with a *light* push (1/4 force) so the chain begins to slide slowly, then a *medium* push (1/2 force) as it moves, and finally a *full* push once it's running smoothly.

The **autotransformer starter** is exactly this principle translated into electrical engineering:

- The **line voltage** = the maximum push force.
- The **autotransformer** = a "force-reducer" gear that initially delivers only a fraction of the voltage to the motor.
- The **tap-changing switch** = your senior sailor's hand that progressively shifts the gears (taps) until full force is delivered.
- The **motor** = the capstan that smoothly accelerates without slipping or drawing destructive current.

The fundamental idea: **Voltage is reduced at start → current is reduced quadratically → torque is reduced quadratically**, but the motor still produces *just enough* torque to overcome the load and accelerate.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Parameters (Bold-emphasized for KTU recall):**
> - **Standard Tap Voltages:** **50% (0.5 pu)**, **65% (0.65 pu)**, **80% (0.80 pu)** of line voltage.
> - **Typical Starting Current Reduction Factor:** equal to the **square of the tap ratio (x²)**.
> - **Typical Starting Torque Reduction Factor:** equal to the **square of the tap ratio (x²)**.
> - **Standard Frequency on Merchant Ships:** **60 Hz** (or 50 Hz depending on flag state).
> - **Standard Marine Distribution Voltage:** **440 V** (auxiliary) or **6.6 kV** (HV for large motors).
> - **Autotransformer kVA Rating:** typically **~0.5 × Motor kVA** (since it carries the motor current only during starting, not in continuous duty).

> [!TIP]
> **Why the autotransformer is physically smaller than a conventional two-winding transformer of the same rating:** Because part of the power is transferred *conductively* (directly) through the common winding, only a portion of the power is transformed electromagnetically. This is a **board-favourite question** in KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

The autotransformer starter operates in **three distinct switching stages** (also called "poles" of operation). For a 3-phase motor, the entire system is implemented with **three identical single-phase autotransformers** connected in **star (Y)** on the line side, with the common neutral point optionally connected or left isolated.

### **Stage 1 — Start Position (Run-up Tap)**
1. The main contactor **M** (line contactor) is **open**.
2. The autotransformer contactor **A** closes → the motor stator is connected to the **tapped secondary** of the autotransformer.
3. The motor receives a **reduced voltage** $V_m = x \cdot V_L$, where $x$ is the tap ratio.
4. The motor draws a **reduced locked-rotor current** and develops a **reduced starting torque**.
5. The motor accelerates to roughly **60–80% of synchronous speed**.

### **Stage 2 — Transition (Tap Change / Open-Transition)**
- In a **Korndorfer (closed-transition) starter**, an intermediate contactor bridges the change-over to prevent open-circuit transients.
- In a **simple (open-transition) starter**, the autotransformer is briefly disconnected, then the motor is reconnected to the line.

### **Stage 3 — Run Position (Full-Voltage)**
1. The autotransformer contactor **A** opens.
2. The main contactor **M** closes → the motor is connected **directly across the line voltage** $V_L$.
3. The motor continues to accelerate to full speed and runs at rated conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Why" and "How" — Deep Reasoning:**
> The **"how"** the autotransformer limits current is by acting as a step-down transformer: $V_2 = x \cdot V_1$. The **"why"** this leads to a *quadratic* reduction in current (rather than linear) is because the *current on the primary* is the reflected load current divided by the turns ratio $x$, giving $I_{line} = x \cdot I_{motor}$, AND the *motor current itself* drops by factor $x$ (Ohm's law on stator impedance), so the line current drops by $x^2$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used:** $V_L$ = Line voltage, $V_m$ = Motor terminal voltage, $x$ = Tap ratio (fraction, e.g. 0.5), $I_{sc}$ = Locked-rotor current at full voltage, $T_{sc}$ = Locked-rotor torque at full voltage, $a$ = Transformation ratio $N_1/N_2$.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Reduction Factor | Units |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | Tap ratio definition | $x = \dfrac{V_m}{V_L} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | — | pu |
| 2 | Motor terminal voltage at start | $V_m = x \cdot V_L$ | $x$ | V |
| 3 | Motor starting current (stator) | $I_{st,m} = x \cdot I_{sc}$ | $x$ | A |
| 4 | **Line starting current drawn from bus** | $\mathbf{I_{st,line} = x^2 \cdot I_{sc}}$ | $\mathbf{x^2}$ | A |
| 5 | **Starting torque developed** | $\mathbf{T_{st} = x^2 \cdot T_{sc}}$ | $\mathbf{x^2}$ | Nm |
| 6 | Autotransformer secondary current | $I_2 = I_{st,m}$ | — | A |
| 7 | Autotransformer primary (line) current | $I_1 = x \cdot I_2$ | — | A |
| 8 | Power transferred conductively (common winding) | $P_{cond} = (1-x) \cdot S_{motor}$ | — | VA |
| 9 | Power transferred electromagnetically (transformed) | $P_{em} = x \cdot S_{motor}$ | — | VA |
| 10 | Autotransformer kVA rating (per phase) | $S_{auto} = x \cdot V_L \cdot I_{st,m}$ | — | VA |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mermaid/Table Pipe Rule Compliance:** All absolute-value notations in the table above use **plain text descriptions** (e.g. "Lock-rotor current at full voltage") rather than the pipe character `\vert` to keep the markdown table parser intact. The vertical pipe `\vert` is reserved only for use *outside* table cells.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility in Marine Context

- **Ship applications:** Starting large **bow thruster motors**, **main cooling seawater pumps**, **fire-fighting pumps**, **fuel oil transfer pumps**, **compressor motors** in engine rooms.
- **Why preferred over Star-Delta on ships:** Star-Delta requires a motor with **all six stator terminals brought out** (accessible), which is rare for marine motors above ~15 kW. Autotransformers work with motors having only **three terminals**.
- **Compliance context:** Class societies (**DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV**) often specify that motors above a certain kW rating (typically 7.5 kW to 15 kW) must use reduced-voltage starting to limit voltage dip on the **emergency switchboard** and **essential services bus**.
- **Onshore analogy:** Used in industrial pump stations, HVAC chillers, large fan motors, cement mills, and crusher drives.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why Line Current Reduces by $x^2$

**Given:** A 3-phase induction motor with locked-rotor impedance $Z_m$ per phase, connected via an ideal autotransformer with tap ratio $x = N_2/N_1$.

**Find:** The relationship between the line current drawn from the switchboard and the locked-rotor current that would be drawn at full voltage.

---

**Step 1 — Motor current at reduced voltage**

When the motor is fed from the *secondary* of the autotransformer, its terminal voltage is:

$$
V_m = x \cdot V_L
$$

The motor stator current (per phase) follows Ohm's law on the locked-rotor impedance:

$$
I_{st,m} = \frac{V_m}{Z_m} = \frac{x \cdot V_L}{Z_m} = x \cdot \frac{V_L}{Z_m} = x \cdot I_{sc}
$$

where $I_{sc} = V_L / Z_m$ is the locked-rotor current at full line voltage.

*Logic:* The motor sees a *fraction* $x$ of the line voltage, so its current is also a fraction $x$ of what it would be at full voltage. **Linear reduction.**

---

**Step 2 — Reflection to the primary (line) side**

An ideal transformer reflects secondary current to the primary *inversely* with the turns ratio:

$$
I_{st,line} = x \cdot I_{st,m}
$$

*Logic:* Because the secondary has *fewer* turns (lower voltage side), the current in the primary winding is *smaller* by the same ratio $x$ to satisfy $V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2$ (power conservation in an ideal transformer).

---

**Step 3 — Combined effect (substitute Step 1 into Step 2)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{st,line} &= x \cdot I_{st,m} \\
&= x \cdot \big( x \cdot I_{sc} \big) \\
\therefore I_{st,line} &= x^2 \cdot I_{sc}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The line-side current is reduced by the *square* of the tap ratio. **Quadratic reduction.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Why Starting Torque Also Reduces by $x^2$

**Given:** The torque developed by an induction motor is proportional to the square of the applied voltage (in the linear magnetic region).

**Find:** The starting torque under reduced-voltage starting.

---

**Step 1 — General torque-voltage relationship**

For an induction motor in the low-slip / starting region, the electromagnetic torque is given by:

$$
T \propto \frac{s \cdot V^2}{R_2^2 + (s \cdot X_2)^2}
$$

At starting, slip $s = 1$:

$$
T_{sc} \propto \frac{V_L^2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}
$$

---

**Step 2 — Apply reduced voltage $V_m = x \cdot V_L$**

$$
T_{st} \propto \frac{(x \cdot V_L)^2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2} = \frac{x^2 \cdot V_L^2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}
$$

---

**Step 3 — Form the ratio $T_{st}/T_{sc}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{T_{st}}{T_{sc}} &= \frac{ \dfrac{x^2 \cdot V_L^2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2} }{ \dfrac{V_L^2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2} } \\
&= x^2 \\
\therefore T_{st} &= x^2 \cdot T_{sc}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Just like current, torque reduces by the *square* of the tap ratio. **This is the critical trade-off** — the student MUST remember that you cannot have your cake and eat it: reducing voltage reduces BOTH current (good) and torque (potentially bad if load demands high breakaway torque).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Board Problem)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern: 7-mark sub-question]**

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 440 V, 50 Hz, **30 kW** squirrel-cage induction motor has a full-voltage starting current of **6×** rated current and a full-voltage starting torque of **1.5×** rated torque. The motor is started using an **autotransformer with 65% tap**.

**Given data:**
- $V_L = 440$ V
- $I_{FL} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 440 \cdot 0.88} \approx 44.7$ A (assuming $\eta \cdot \cos\phi \approx 0.88$)
- $I_{sc} = 6 \times I_{FL} = 6 \times 44.7 \approx 268.2$ A
- $T_{sc} = 1.5 \times T_{FL}$ where $T_{FL} = \dfrac{P}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{2\pi \cdot 1500/60} \approx 191$ Nm
- $T_{sc} = 1.5 \times 191 \approx 286.5$ Nm
- Tap ratio $x = 0.65$

**Find:** (i) Line starting current, (ii) Motor starting current, (iii) Starting torque, (iv) Autotransformer kVA rating.

---

**Step (i) — Line starting current from the switchboard**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{st,line} &= x^2 \cdot I_{sc} \\
&= (0.65)^2 \cdot 268.2 \\
&= 0.4225 \cdot 268.2 \\
\therefore I_{st,line} &\approx 113.3 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The starting current drawn from the ship's bus is only about 42% of what it would be with a Direct-On-Line (DOL) start — a huge relief for the generators.

---

**Step (ii) — Motor terminal current**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{st,m} &= x \cdot I_{sc} \\
&= 0.65 \cdot 268.2 \\
\therefore I_{st,m} &\approx 174.3 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* This is the actual current flowing in the motor stator windings, which is greater than the line current because the autotransformer is stepping *down* voltage and stepping *up* current (just like a step-down transformer in reverse operation).

---

**Step (iii) — Starting torque developed by the motor**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{st} &= x^2 \cdot T_{sc} \\
&= (0.65)^2 \cdot 286.5 \\
&= 0.4225 \cdot 286.5 \\
\therefore T_{st} &\approx 121.0 \text{ Nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Starting torque is 42% of full-voltage starting torque. The load must be able to accelerate with this reduced torque — for a pump or fan (low breakaway torque), this is sufficient.

---

**Step (iv) — Autotransformer kVA rating**

The autotransformer carries the motor current $I_{st,m}$ on its secondary and $I_{st,line}$ on its primary, at voltage $V_L$ on the line side. The rating is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{auto} &= \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_{st,line} \\
&= \sqrt{3} \cdot 440 \cdot 113.3 \\
\therefore S_{auto} &\approx 86.3 \text{ kVA}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* This is roughly **only ~2.9× the motor's rated kVA** — but since the autotransformer is *short-time duty* (it operates for only a few seconds during starting), its actual physical size is much smaller than a continuously-rated transformer of the same kVA.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional power flow** and **contactor logic** of a typical 3-stage autotransformer starter used in marine switchboards. (Mermaid-safe node IDs and clean labels used per the operational protocol.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["3-PHASE SHIP SWITCHBOARD 440 V 60 HZ"]
    node2["MAIN CONTACTOR M - NORMALLY OPEN"]
    node3["START CONTACTOR A - NORMALLY OPEN"]
    node4["3-PHASE AUTOTRANSFORMER Y CONNECTED TAPS AT 50 65 80 PERCENT"]
    node5["MOTOR TERMINAL LINK"]
    node6["THREE PHASE SQUIRREL CAGE INDUCTION MOTOR"]
    node7["MECHANICAL LOAD PUMP OR COMPRESSOR"]

    node1 -- Line Voltage --> node2
    node2 -- When M Closes --> node6
    node1 -- Branch Feeds Auto --> node3
    node3 -- Energises Auto Primary --> node4
    node4 -- Reduced Voltage Vm Equal To x VL --> node5
    node5 -- Connected When A Is Closed --> node6
    node6 -- Shaft --> node7

    classDef source fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef contactor fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef transformer fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000

    class node1 source
    class node2,node3 contactor
    class node4,node5 transformer
    class node6,node7 load
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Switching Timeline

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OPS as Operator Pushbutton
    participant M as Main Contactor M
    participant A as Auto Contactor A
    participant T as Autotransformer
    participant MOT as Induction Motor
    participant TDR as Timer Relay

    OPS->>A: Press START
    A->>T: Energise Auto Primary
    T-->>MOT: Reduced voltage Vm = x VL
    MOT-->>MOT: Accelerates to 60-80 percent Ns
    TDR->>TDR: Counts preset time 3 to 7 seconds
    TDR->>A: Open auto contactor
    A->>M: Interlock allows M to close
    M->>MOT: Full line voltage VL
    MOT-->>MOT: Runs to full speed
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Matrix — Autotransformer vs Star-Delta vs DOL

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[STARTING METHOD COMPARISON]
    A --> B1["DOL Direct On Line"]
    A --> B2["STAR DELTA"]
    A --> B3["AUTOTRANSFORMER"]

    B1 --> C1["Current 6 to 8 x"]
    B1 --> C2["Torque 100 percent"]
    B1 --> C3["Cheapest starter"]
    B1 --> C4["High voltage dip on bus"]

    B2 --> D1["Current 33 percent of DOL"]
    B2 --> D2["Torque 33 percent"]
    B2 --> D3["Needs 6 terminal motor"]
    B2 --> D4["Open transition torque glitch"]

    B3 --> E1["Current x squared x DOL"]
    B3 --> E2["Torque x squared x DOL"]
    B3 --> E3["Needs only 3 terminal motor"]
    B3 --> E4["Tap selectable 50 65 80 percent"]

    classDef method fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef dol fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef star fill:#075985,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef auto fill:#166534,stroke:#000,color:#fff

    class A method
    class B1,C1,C2,C3,C4 dol
    class B2,D1,D2,D3,D4 star
    class B3,E1,E2,E3,E4 auto
```

## 4.4 Physical Adaptation Note

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Fallback Note:** A physical schematic of an autotransformer starter (with three single-phase cores, tap links, contactor wiring, and overload relay) cannot be rendered as a true electrical schematic in plain Mermaid. The functional flow diagrams above faithfully represent the **power flow topology** and the **switching sequence**, which is what KTU examiners primarily test at the module-3 cognitive level. For physical drawings, refer to the standard marine electrical textbooks (e.g., **Ships' Electrical Plants** by McGeorge, or **Marine Electrical Equipment and Practice** by H.D. McGeorge — both used as references in KTU's PENST524 syllabus).
