| **Main propulsion alternator** | Holds 6.6 kV bus during crane/winch load transients |
| **Harbour generator (shipyard)** | Compensates for long cable runs and welding-machine inrush |
| **Emergency diesel generator** | Auto-restores voltage after blackout-start within 10 s (SOLAS) |
| **Shaft generator / PTO** | Compensates for shaft-speed variation ±2% |
| **DP (Dynamic Positioning) class 2/3 vessels** | Maintains power quality during sudden thruster load changes |

---

### 2.5 Advanced Variants Beyond Plain Thyristor Bridges

> [!TIP]
> Modern shipboard SAVRs also employ two power-electronic cousins — frequently asked in KTU as a 7-mark contrast question:

**(a) Thyristor-Controlled Reactor (TCR):** A thyristor-switched inductor in shunt with the bus. By varying α, the **reactive current drawn is varied**, effectively acting as a continuously-variable susceptance. Used in **Static Var Compensators (SVCs)** for very large shipyard supplies.

**(b) Thyristor-Switched Capacitor (TSC):** Capacitor banks are switched ON/OFF in discrete steps via anti-parallel thyristor pairs. Used for **coarse voltage correction** in shore-power connections.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Field Voltage of a 1-φ Full-Controlled Bridge

**Given:** Single-phase AC supply $v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ feeding a fully-controlled bridge with highly inductive load (rotor field) such that load current is **continuous and ripple-free** ($I_f$ = constant).

**Step 1 — Identify the conduction interval.**
Thyristors T1 and T2 conduct from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\pi + \alpha$. Thyristors T3 and T4 conduct from $\pi + \alpha$ to $2\pi + \alpha$. Because the load is inductive, conduction extends slightly beyond $\pi$ (overlap μ), but for the **average voltage derivation we assume μ ≈ 0** (a standard KTU assumption for steady-state analysis).

**Step 2 — Write the output voltage expression over one conduction half-cycle.**

$$v_o(\omega t) = V_m \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{for } \alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi + \alpha$$

**Step 3 — Compute the average over a full period $T = 2\pi/\omega$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_f &= \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) \\
&= \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\Big[-\cos(\omega t)\Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} \\
&= \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\Big[-\cos(\pi + \alpha) + \cos(\alpha)\Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the trigonometric identity $\cos(\pi + \alpha) = -\cos\alpha$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_f &= \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\Big[-(-\cos\alpha) + \cos\alpha\Big] \\
&= \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\Big[2\cos\alpha\Big] \\
&= \frac{V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on sign convention:** Some textbooks write the bridge with opposite polarity giving $E_f = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$ for a **semi-controlled** (asymmetric / half-controlled) bridge. For a **fully-controlled bridge** with continuous current: $E_f = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$. Always state your bridge type in the KTU exam.

**Step 5 — Numerical sanity check.**
At $\alpha = 0°$: $E_f = V_m/\pi \approx 0.318\,V_m$ (maximum, equivalent to an uncontrolled rectifier with continuous current). At $\alpha = 90°$: $E_f = 0$ (zero average, the bridge is at the inversion limit). At $\alpha > 90°$: $E_f < 0$ → **inverter mode** (used for **field suppression / de-excitation** during fault clearing).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Terminal Voltage from Synchronous Machine Phasor Equation

**Given:** A cylindrical-rotor marine alternator with synchronous reactance $X_s$, armature resistance $R_a \approx 0$ (typical assumption for $X_s/R_a > 50$ in large alternators), supplying a load current $I_a$ at power-factor angle $\phi$.

**Step 1 — Write the phasor equation per phase.**

$$\bar{E}_f = \bar{V}_t + \bar{I}_a(R_a + jX_s)$$

**Step 2 — Set $R_a \approx 0$ and substitute $\bar{I}_a = I_a \angle -\phi$ (lagging PF).**

$$\bar{E}_f = \bar{V}_t + jX_s I_a \angle -\phi = V_t \angle 0 + jX_s I_a(\cos\phi - j\sin\phi)$$

**Step 3 — Expand using $j \cdot (\cos\phi - j\sin\phi) = j\cos\phi + \sin\phi$.**

$$\bar{E}_f = (V_t + X_s I_a \sin\phi) + j(X_s I_a \cos\phi)$$

**Step 4 — Take the magnitude.**

$$E_f = \sqrt{(V_t + X_s I_a \sin\phi)^2 + (X_s I_a \cos\phi)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the inner squares.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_f^2 &= V_t^2 + 2V_t X_s I_a \sin\phi + X_s^2 I_a^2 \sin^2\phi + X_s^2 I_a^2 \cos^2\phi \\
&= V_t^2 + 2V_t X_s I_a \sin\phi + X_s^2 I_a^2(\sin^2\phi + \cos^2\phi) \\
&= V_t^2 + 2V_t X_s I_a \sin\phi + X_s^2 I_a^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $V_t$ in terms of $E_f$ (the AVR-controlled quantity).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_t^2 + 2V_t X_s I_a \sin\phi + X_s^2 I_a^2 - E_f^2 &= 0 \\
V_t &= \frac{-2X_s I_a \sin\phi + \sqrt{4X_s^2 I_a^2 \sin^2\phi - 4(X_s^2 I_a^2 - E_f^2)}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$V_t = -X_s I_a \sin\phi + \sqrt{E_f^2 - X_s^2 I_a^2 \cos^2\phi}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **fundamental control equation**: the AVR adjusts $E_f$ (via firing angle α) so that $V_t$ remains constant. As $I_a$ increases, $X_s I_a \sin\phi$ increases, demanding a higher $E_f$ → α is reduced (firing advanced) → field current increases → terminal voltage restored.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Commutation Overlap Angle μ

**Given:** AC source inductance $L_s$ per phase (transformer leakage), load current $I_f$ continuous.

**Step 1 — During commutation from thyristor T1 to T3, both devices conduct. The commutating voltage is $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, and the difference supports $L_s \dfrac{di}{dt}$.**

$$\frac{V_m}{2}\sin(\omega t) = 2L_s\frac{di}{dt} \quad \text{(per phase, single-phase equivalent)}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from $\alpha$ to $\alpha + \mu$ (overlap period).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\alpha}^{\alpha+\mu} \frac{V_m}{2}\sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) &= \int_{I_f}^{I_f - 0} 2L_s\, di \quad \text{(effective drop to incoming device)}\\
\frac{V_m}{2}\big[-\cos(\omega t)\big]_{\alpha}^{\alpha+\mu} &= -2L_s \cdot I_f \\
\frac{V_m}{2}\big[\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha + \mu)\big] &= 2L_s I_f \cdot \omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $X_s = \omega L_s$ and solve for $\mu$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos(\alpha + \mu) &= \cos\alpha - \frac{2 X_s I_f}{V_m} \\
\mu &= \cos^{-1}\!\left(\cos\alpha - \frac{2 X_s I_f}{V_m}\right) - \alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 hot tip:** If the question says "highly inductive source" and gives $X_s$ and $I_f$, examiners expect you to compute $\mu$ and then write the **corrected average voltage** $E_f = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha - \dfrac{X_s I_f}{\pi}$ (the overlap subtracts an extra $X_s I_f/\pi$ term). Many students forget this and lose 2 marks.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Behaviour of SAVR Under Common Marine Transients

| Disturbance | $\Delta I_a$ | AVR Response | Final $E_f$ | Time to settle |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **No-load → Full-load (resistive, PF=1)** | Step up | α decreases (earlier firing) | Rises to maintain $V_t$ | ~0.1–0.3 s |
| **Induction motor start (PF≈0.3 lag)** | 6× inrush | α hits lower limit (10°) | Field ceiling reached | Voltage dip 10–15%, recovers in 1–1.5 s |
| **Sudden load rejection** | Step down | α increases toward 150° (inverter mode briefly) | Brief over-excitation suppressed | Over-voltage < 5%, recovers in 0.5 s |
| **Symmetric short circuit** | Up to 10× | Forced α = 10° (field forcing) | Ceiling for 0.5–5 s | Sustained fault current for breaker coordination |
| **Prime-mover speed dip** | $f$ changes | Indirect — $V_t = f(\Phi, f, N)$ | Auto-compensated by α | ~0.2 s |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Closed-Loop SAVR Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ship Bus 440V / 6.6kV] -->|PT step-down| B[Potential Transformer]
    B --> C[Precision Rectifier]
    C --> D[DC Reference Vref Comparator]
    R[Zener Reference Vref] --> D
    D -->|Error Ve| E[PI Error Amplifier Kp Ki]
    E --> F[Ramp Comparator Firing Circuit]
    S[AC Sync from PT secondary] --> F
    F -->|Gate pulse at angle alpha| G[Thyristor Full Bridge]
    P[Exciter AC supply] --> G
    G -->|Variable DC Ef| H[Generator Rotor Field Winding]
    H -.->|Coupling| A
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Thyristor Bridge Internals (1-φ Full-Controlled)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AC[AC Supply Vs Vm sin wt] --> T1[SCR T1]
    AC --> T2[SCR T2]
    T1 -->|anode| J1[Junction Positive DC]
    T2 -->|cathode| J1
    J1 --> Lf[Field Winding Lf Rf]
    Lf --> J2[Junction Negative DC]
    J2 --> T3[SCR T3]
    J2 --> T4[SCR T4]
    T1 -.->|gate pulse at alpha| GP1[Gate Driver 1]
    T3 -.->|gate pulse at pi plus alpha| GP3[Gate Driver 3]
    T2 -.->|gate pulse at pi plus alpha| GP2[Gate Driver 2]
    T4 -.->|gate pulse at two pi plus alpha| GP4[Gate Driver 4]
    GP1 --> CTL[Firing Control from PI]
    GP2 --> CTL
    GP3 --> CTL
    GP4 --> CTL
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Power Flow in Marine Generating Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DMG[Diesel Engine Prime Mover] -->|Mechanical shaft| GEN[Synchronous Generator Stator Rotor]
    EXC[Exciter AC / Brushless PMG] -->|AC supply| THY[Thyristor Bridge in SAVR]
    AVR[Static Automatic Voltage Regulator Control Electronics] -->|Gate pulses alpha| THY
    THY -->|DC field current If| GEN
    GEN -->|3-phase AC 440V| MSB[Main Switchboard Bus]
    MSB --> L1[Propulsion Motor Drives]
    MSB --> L2[Winch Crane Bow Thruster]
    MSB --> L3[Hotel Load Lighting HVAC]
    MSB --> L4[Emergency Board via ATS]
    MSB -.->|VT feedback via PT| AVR
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Signal-Waveform Diagram — Firing Angle Control Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Waveforms
        V[Vs Vm sin wt supply voltage]
        G1[Gate pulse T1 T3 at omega t equals alpha]
        G2[Gate pulse T2 T4 at omega t equals pi plus alpha]
        E[Output Ef Vm sin wt during conduction only]
        R[Average Ef equals Vm cos alpha divided by pi]
    end
    V --> G1
    V --> G2
    G1 --> E
    G2 --> E
    E --> R
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation:** The gate pulses `G1` and `G2` occur **later** along the time axis when the AVR demands lower field voltage. The shaded portion of `E` shrinks as α increases. The DC average `R` reduces monotonically from $+V_m/\pi$ at $\alpha = 0$ to $-V_m/\pi$ at $\alpha = 180°$ (full inversion).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
*Define a static automatic voltage regulator (SAVR) for a ship's alternator. Why is the term "static" used, and what is its main advantage over the older electromechanical carbon-pile type?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A SAVR is a **closed-loop, solid-state electronic controller** that automatically maintains the **terminal voltage of a marine synchronous generator** within prescribed limits (typically ±2.5%) by controlling the **excitation current** supplied to the rotor field winding. (1 mark)
- The term **"static"** denotes **absence of any moving parts**; regulation is achieved through controlled conduction of **thyristors (SCRs)** in the excitation bridge. (1 mark)
- **Advantage over carbon-pile type:** Response time is **< 50 ms** (vs. 200–500 ms for carbon pile), no mechanical wear, **higher reliability** in shipboard vibration, lower maintenance, and the ability to deliver **field forcing** during faults. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*State the relationship between the thyristor firing angle (α) and the average field voltage ($E_f$) of a single-phase fully-controlled bridge supplying a highly inductive load. What happens when α > 90°?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- For a 1-φ fully-controlled bridge with continuous load current: $E_f = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$. (1 mark)
- The relationship is **inverse-cosine** — as α **increases** from 0° to 90°, $E_f$ **decreases monotonically** from $V_m/\pi$ to 0. (1 mark)
- When **α > 90°**, $\cos\alpha$ becomes negative → $E_f$ is **negative** (DC output reverses polarity). This is the **inverter mode**, used deliberately for **field suppression (de-excitation)** during fault clearing and load rejection on ships. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Modified]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **functional blocks of a thyristor-based static AVR** used for a ship's synchronous generator. Explain how the **error signal** is processed by the PI controller to generate the **firing angle α** for the thyristor bridge. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1500 kVA, 440 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz ship's alternator has a **synchronous reactance $X_s = 0.6 \,\Omega$ per phase** (star-connected). The AVR supplies the rotor field via a **1-φ fully-controlled thyristor bridge** fed from a **230 V AC, 50 Hz exciter**. The armature resistance is negligible. Compute:
   (i) The **field voltage $E_f$** required at **full-load, 0.8 power factor lagging**, when the line current is the rated value. **(4 marks)**
   (ii) The **firing angle α** for the bridge under this condition. **(3 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Functional Block Diagram & PI Action — 7 marks**

- **Block 1 — Voltage Sensing:** A PT on the generator terminals steps 440 V down to 110 V; a precision full-wave rectifier gives $V_{fb} = K_{PT}\,V_t$. (1 mark)
- **Block 2 — Reference Comparator:** A temperature-compensated Zener reference $V_{ref}$ is subtracted from $V_{fb}$ to produce the error $V_e = V_{ref} - K_{PT} V_t$. (1 mark)
- **Block 3 — PI Amplifier:** $V_e$ is fed to a PI controller. Its output is the **firing-angle command**:

$$\alpha(t) = K_p V_e(t) + K_i \int_0^t V_e(\tau)\,d\tau$$

The proportional term gives **fast dynamic response**; the integral term eliminates **steady-state offset**. (2 marks)

- **Block 4 — Ramp/Phase-Shift Circuit:** A sawtooth ramp synchronised to the AC supply is generated. When the ramp voltage equals the PI output, a comparator triggers a **monostable** that produces a **gate pulse** at angle α to the SCRs. (1 mark)
- **Block 5 — Thyristor Power Bridge:** The 1-φ full bridge rectifies the 230 V exciter AC into variable DC $E_f$ delivered to the rotor. (1 mark)
- **Feedback closure:** The increased/decreased $E_f$ changes the rotor flux → changes the generator internal EMF → restores $V_t$ to set value → $V_e \to 0$. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often **omit the feedback line** in the block diagram — this is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. Always close the loop with an arrow from the **sensed output** back to the **comparator**. Also, do NOT confuse the **firing angle α** with the **overlap angle μ**; they are different quantities.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation — 7 marks**

**Given data:**
- $S = 1500$ kVA, $V_L = 440$ V (line), 3-φ, 50 Hz
- $X_s = 0.6\ \Omega$/phase (star), $R_a \approx 0$
- Full-load PF = 0.8 lagging
- Exciter: 1-φ, 230 V, 50 Hz → 1-φ fully-controlled bridge
- $V_{m,\text{exc}} = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27$ V

**Step (i) — Find rated line current.**

$$I_L = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L} = \frac{1500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 440} = \frac{1\,500\,000}{762.10} = 1968.2\ \text{A}$$

Per-phase (star) current: $I_a = I_L = 1968.2$ A. (1 mark)

**Step (ii) — Compute per-phase terminal voltage.**

$$V_{t,\text{ph}} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{440}{1.732} = 254.0\ \text{V}$$

**Step (iii) — Compute field voltage $E_f$ (line-to-neutral internal EMF) using the phasor relation derived in Section 3.2:**

$$E_f = \sqrt{V_t^2 + 2V_t X_s I_a \sin\phi + X_s^2 I_a^2}$$

With $\cos\phi = 0.8 \Rightarrow \sin\phi = 0.6$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_f &= \sqrt{(254.0)^2 + 2(254.0)(0.6)(1968.2)(0.6) + (0.6)^2(1968.2)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{64\,516 + 359\,996 + 1\,394\,503} \\
&= \sqrt{1\,819\,015} \\
&\approx 1348.7\ \text{V per phase}
\end{aligned}
$$

(1 mark each for substitution, computation, final value = 3 marks)

**Step (iv) — Compute firing angle α for the 1-φ bridge (Section 3.1):**

$$E_f = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\alpha = \frac{E_f \cdot \pi}{V_m}$$

$$V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27\ \text{V}$$

$$\cos\alpha = \frac{1348.7 \times \pi}{325.27} = \frac{4237.6}{325.27} = 13.03$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This gives $|\cos\alpha| > 1$ — physically impossible!** The problem is that the 230 V exciter is far too small to produce the **per-phase internal EMF** of 1348 V. In practice, the AVR does **not** directly produce $E_f$ from one bridge; it produces the **rotor field voltage** of the *exciter* (typically tens of volts DC), which in turn drives the main generator's rotating exciter, which finally supplies the rotor. The KTU problem is checking whether the student **recognises this hierarchy**.

**Corrected interpretation (2 marks):**
The "field voltage" the bridge must deliver is the **rotor field voltage of the exciter** — typically **40–80 V DC** for a 1500 kVA alternator. Taking a realistic $E_f = 60$ V:

$$\cos\alpha = \frac{60 \times \pi}{325.27} = \frac{188.5}{325.27} = 0.5795$$

$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}(0.5795) = 54.6°$$

(1 mark for the correct interpretation, 1 mark for final α value)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, Modified]**
**(a)** Explain with a **neat sketch** the operation of a **1-φ fully-controlled thyristor bridge** feeding the highly inductive field winding of a marine alternator. Sketch the **supply voltage, output voltage, and thyristor current** waveforms for firing angle $\alpha = 60°$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship's static AVR uses a **3-phase fully-controlled thyristor bridge** excited from a **415 V, 50 Hz** exciter alternator (line-to-line RMS). The bridge supplies a field winding of resistance $R_f = 8\ \Omega$ and inductance $L_f = 4$ H (continuous current assumed). If the AVR demands a **field current $I_f = 25$ A** at a **firing angle α = 45°**, compute:
   (i) The **average field voltage $E_f$**. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) The **RMS line current** drawn from the exciter. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) Whether the **field time constant $\tau_f$** is fast enough for the regulator to respond to a **step load** within statutory limits. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Operation & Waveform Description — 7 marks**

Refer to the **Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2** for the topology. Description:

- The 1-φ AC supply feeds a bridge of **four thyristors T1, T2, T3, T4** in anti-series pairs across the DC load (rotor field). (1 mark)
- T1 and T2 are forward-conducting in the **positive half cycle starting at α**; T3 and T4 conduct in the **negative half cycle starting at π + α**. (1 mark)
- Because the load is highly inductive, current is **continuous and ripple-free** ($I_f$ = constant). (1 mark)
- Output voltage $v_o = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ during conduction intervals, and **0** during non-conduction intervals. (1 mark)
- The average $E_f = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ decreases as α increases. (1 mark)
- For α = 60°: $E_f = V_m/\pi \times \cos 60° = V_m/(2\pi) = 0.159\,V_m$ (positive). (1 mark)
- **Waveform sketch** (verbal description since text-only): Supply is a sine; output voltage is a "chopped" sine starting at α; thyristor current is a rectangular pulse of height $I_f$ lasting π radians per thyristor per cycle. (1 mark)

---

**(b) Numerical Computation — 7 marks**

**Given:** 3-φ bridge, $V_{LL} = 415$ V (RMS), $f = 50$ Hz, $R_f = 8\ \Omega$, $L_f = 4$ H, $I_f = 25$ A, $\alpha = 45°$.

**Step (i) — Average field voltage $E_f$.**

Peak line-to-line voltage: $V_{m,LL} = \sqrt{2} \times 415 = 586.9$ V.

For a 3-φ fully-controlled bridge (continuous current):

$$E_f = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_{m,LL}}{2\pi}\cos\alpha$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor in the denominator is **$2\pi$** (not $\pi$ as in 1-φ), because the average is taken over **2π/3** segments repeated six times per cycle; standard textbook form is $E_f = \dfrac{3 V_{m,L-L,\text{peak}}}{\pi}\cos\alpha \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ which simplifies to the expression above. (Equivalent forms are acceptable.)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_f &= \frac{3 \times 1.732 \times 586.9}{2\pi} \cos 45° \\
&= \frac{3049.3}{6.2832} \times 0.7071 \\
&= 485.3 \times 0.7071 \\
&\approx 343.2\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

(3 marks: 1 formula, 1 substitution, 1 answer)

**Step (ii) — RMS line current.**

For a 3-φ fully-controlled bridge with continuous current, the **thyristor RMS current** is:

$$I_{TRMS} = \frac{I_f}{\sqrt{3}}$$

The **line RMS current** is related to thyristor RMS by the square root of the number of conducting devices per half-cycle. For a 3-φ bridge:

$$I_{L,RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \cdot I_f = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \times 25 = 0.8165 \times 25 \approx 20.4\ \text{A}$$

(2 marks: 1 formula, 1 answer)

**Step (iii) — Field time constant & statutory check.**

$$\tau_f = \frac{L_f}{R_f} = \frac{4}{8} = 0.5\ \text{s}$$

For a first-order response, the field current reaches **95%** of its demanded value in $3\tau_f = 1.5$ s. (1 mark)

The **statutory transient recovery** is 1.5 s (per IEC 60092-301). The bare field winding alone is **at the edge of compliance**; in practice, the **PI controller's proportional path** provides instantaneous partial response, and the closed-loop bandwidth (from the table in Section 2.3) is much higher. Hence the **closed-loop system meets the standard** even though the open-loop field winding is borderline. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is to write the 3-φ bridge formula as $E_f = \dfrac{3V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ where $V_m$ is the **phase** peak — that gives an answer **off by a factor of $\sqrt{3}$**. Always clarify: is $V_m$ line-to-line peak or phase peak? Use the form that matches the RMS value you computed.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- [x] **SAVR = Solid-state + Automatic + Voltage + Regulator** — zero moving parts, uses thyristors to control excitation.
- [x] **Steady-state tolerance on ships:** **±2.5%** of nominal bus voltage (statutory).
- [x] **1-φ full-bridge average voltage:** $E_f = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ for continuous current.
- [x] **3-φ full-bridge average voltage:** $E_f = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_{m,LL}}{2\pi}\cos\alpha$ — remember the **2π** in the denominator.
- [x] **Inverter mode (α > 90°):** Used for **field suppression / fast de-excitation** — a critical shipboard safety function.
- [x] **PI controller** (not P) is the standard — integral action removes steady-state error demanded by classification societies.
- [x] **Firing angle α** is measured from the **natural commutation point** (the instant a diode would start conducting).
- [x] **Commutation overlap μ** reduces the effective $E_f$ by an extra $\dfrac{X_s I_f}{\pi}$ — include this when source inductance is non-negligible.
- [x] **Terminal voltage equation** for cylindrical rotor ($R_a \approx 0$): $E_f = \sqrt{V_t^2 + 2V_t X_s I_a \sin\phi + X_s^2 I_a^2}$.
- [x] **Field-forcing ceiling** during faults: α is driven to its **minimum limit (~10°)** to maximise excitation and sustain fault current for **selective breaker coordination**.
- [x] **Main blocks** in order: **PT → Rectifier → Comparator (Vref) → PI Amplifier → Ramp/Firing Circuit → Thyristor Bridge → Rotor Field → (back to PT)**.
- [x] **Marine-specific extras**: ambient 45°C/55°C derating, vibration-resistant mounting, IP44 enclosure, EMC compliance to IEC 60945.
- [x] **Brushless variant**: SAVR controls the **stationary field of the pilot exciter** (PMG), not the main rotor — eliminates sliprings.
- [x] **KTU 2024 favourite trick question:** "What if the load becomes **leading PF (capacitive)**?" — answer: AVR must **reduce** $E_f$ (increase α) because the leading current **naturally** boosts terminal voltage. The regulator handles this automatically.
- [x] **Open-loop field time constant** $\tau_f$ typically 0.3 – 2 s; the **closed-loop PI** overcomes this and reduces effective response to < 50 ms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of kW Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effect of kW Loading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **kW Loading** in marine electrical engineering refers to the magnitude of **real (active) power** drawn from the ship's generating set (auxiliary diesel generator or shaft generator) expressed in **kilowatts (kW)**. It is the portion of electrical loading that performs useful mechanical work at the load — the kW component of the apparent power $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$.

Mathematically, the kW load on a three-phase ship's alternator is given by:

$$P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$$

where $V_L$ is the line voltage, $I_L$ is the line current, and $\cos\phi$ is the load power factor. The remaining component, **kVAr (reactive loading)**, is handled separately by the Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR) and excitation system.

In a marine diesel generator, the **kW load is controlled by the fuel rack position**, whereas the **kVAr is controlled by the excitation current** — a critical distinction in shipboard operations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine a **water tap connected to a pump (the engine) supplying a tank (the alternator + load)**. The *flow rate of water* is the **kW load** — it represents actual work being done. If you open the tap wider (increase kW load), the pump must work harder, burns more fuel, gets warmer, and the exhaust becomes hotter. The *water pressure* in the system is the **voltage (V)**, and is held nearly constant by the AVR. This is why a marine engineer measures kW loading with a **kW meter**, not an ammeter.

A ship is rarely at full load. It moves through distinct operational states — **berthing, manoeuvring, sea passage, cargo working, and dynamic positioning (DP)** — each with very different kW demands on the main and auxiliary generators. Understanding the effect of these varying kW loads on fuel, heat, emissions, and component life is essential for marine engineers preparing for Class 4/Class 5 MEO competency exams and KTU ESE assessments.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Bold)

- **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)**: **g/kWh** or **kg/kWh** (typically 180–220 g/kWh for modern marine 4-stroke auxiliary engines).
- **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)**: Same as SFC, applied to the brake power output.
- **CV (Calorific Value) of Marine Diesel Oil (MDO)**: **≈ 42,700 kJ/kg** (≈ 10,200 kcal/kg).
- **Standard reference temperature**: **15 °C / 288 K** (ISO 3046).
- **Class society normal load condition**: **100% MCR (Maximum Continuous Rating)**.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Ship Operations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> On a ship, the **fuel bill is the single largest operating expense** — over 50% of voyage cost. The way kW loading is managed directly affects:
> - **Specific Fuel Oil Consumption (SFOC)** and bunker cost.
> - **Cylinder liner wear** and **scavenge air fire risk** at sustained low loads.
> - **Wet-stacking** when the engine runs below ~30% load for long periods.
> - **Emissions compliance** (IMO MARPOL Annex VI NOx Tier limits).
> - **EEDI / EEXI / CII** carbon intensity indicators — directly tied to kW load profile.
> </br>

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of kW Loading — What Happens Inside the Engine

When the kW load on a marine diesel generator is increased (or decreased), the engine governor automatically adjusts the **fuel rack position** to maintain nominal RPM (e.g., 720 rpm for a 4-stroke auxiliary). This change cascades through every subsystem:

### A. Combustion & Mechanical Subsystem Effects
1. **Fuel rack opens** — more fuel injected per cycle.
2. **Cylinder peak pressure** ($P_{max}$) rises — typically 80–120 bar at full load in 4-stroke marine engines.
3. **Rate of pressure rise (RPRR)** increases — affects mechanical noise and bearing loads.
4. **Mean Effective Pressure (MEP)** rises in proportion to kW load.
5. **Piston ring tension** works against higher gas pressure — increased wear.
6. **Crankcase oil pressure** must rise to support heavier bearings.
7. **Torsional vibration amplitudes** in the shaft line change with load.

### B. Thermal Subsystem Effects
1. **Jacket cooling water outlet temperature** rises (designed ΔT ≈ 10–12 °C at MCR).
2. **Lubricating oil temperature** rises (typical outlet 65–75 °C at MCR).
3. **Exhaust gas temperature (EGT)** rises — at the turbocharger inlet, from ~280 °C (no load) to ~400–500 °C (full load).
4. **Scavenge air temperature** rises (after-cooler outlet target 45–55 °C).

### C. Air-Handling & Turbocharger Effects
1. **Turbocharger speed** rises sharply — a doubling of kW load can triple turbo RPM.
2. **Boost pressure** rises from ~0.2 bar (naturally aspirated idle) to ~2.5–3.5 bar at MCR.
3. **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)** narrows — exhaust smoke darkens at very high load.

### D. Performance & Efficiency Effects
1. **SFC vs. load** follows a **U-shaped curve** (see Willans line analysis below).
2. **Brake thermal efficiency** peaks around **80–85% MCR**.
3. **Mechanical efficiency** improves as friction-to-power ratio falls with load.
4. **Volumetric efficiency** may dip at very high load due to heat-soak.

## 2.2 The Willans Line — Linear Approximation of Fuel–Power Relationship

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Willans Line** is the most useful tool to predict fuel rate at any kW load. It is a **straight-line approximation** between *fuel mass flow rate ($\dot{m}_f$)* and *brake power ($P$)*, valid for steady-state loading between ~25% and 100% MCR.

$$\dot{m}_f = a \cdot P + b$$

where:
- $\dot{m}_f$ = fuel mass flow rate (kg/h)
- $P$ = brake power output (kW)
- $a$ = slope of Willans line (kg/kWh) — represents the *incremental* SFC
- $b$ = intercept on the fuel-rate axis (kg/h) — represents the *no-load* fuel rate

Converting fuel rate to SFC:

$$\text{SFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{P} = a + \frac{b}{P}$$

This shows SFC **decreases hyperbolically** with P in the simple Willans model. The minimum theoretical SFC approaches **a** as $P \to \infty$. In practice, mechanical/thermal losses cause SFC to rise again beyond ~85% MCR, producing the actual **U-curve**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Active (Real) Power | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | kW | Three-phase balanced load |
| 2 | Apparent Power | $S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L$ | kVA | Generator rating reference |
| 3 | Reactive Power | $Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\phi$ | kVAr | Supplied/absorbed by AVR |
| 4 | Specific Fuel Consumption | $\text{SFC} = \dot{m}_f \div P$ | kg/kWh | Or g/kWh |
| 5 | Willans Line Equation | $\dot{m}_f = a \cdot P + b$ | kg/h | Linear fuel-power fit |
| 6 | SFC from Willans Line | $\text{SFC} = a + b \div P$ | kg/kWh | Hyperbolic decrease |
| 7 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = 3600 \cdot P \div (\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV})$ | dimensionless | CV in kJ/kg |
| 8 | Heat to Jacket Water | $Q_j = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_w \cdot \Delta T$ | kW | $c_w = 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K) |
| 9 | Heat to Lub Oil | $Q_o = \dot{m}_o \cdot c_o \cdot \Delta T$ | kW | $c_o \approx 1.9$ kJ/(kg·K) |
| 10 | Load Factor | $\text{LF} = P_{actual} \div P_{rated}$ | fraction | 0 to 1.0 (or %) |
| 11 | Brake Mean Effective Pressure | $\text{BMEP} = 60 \cdot P \div (L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot K)$ | bar | $K=1$ (2-stroke), $K=0.5$ (4-stroke) |
| 12 | Heat Balance | $Q_{in} = Q_{eff} + Q_{cool} + Q_{exh} + Q_{loss}$ | kW or kJ/h | $Q_{in} = \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV}$ |
| 13 | Shaft Torque | $T = 9550 \cdot P \div N$ | N·m | $P$ in kW, $N$ in rpm |
| 14 | Air-Fuel Ratio (typical) | $\text{AFR} \approx 14.5:1$ to $18:1$ | — | Mass basis, marine diesel |
| 15 | Excess Air | $\lambda = \text{Actual AFR} \div \text{Stoich. AFR}$ | — | $\lambda \approx 1.8$–$2.5$ at MCR |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
> 1. **Fuel Planning (Bunker Optimization):** Chief Engineers use Willans-line constants to forecast voyage fuel consumption when operating with one generator in standby.
> 2. **Condition Monitoring:** Tracking SFC drift over time is the most sensitive indicator of engine degradation (nozzle wear, air-cooler fouling, turbocharger loss).
> 3. **EEXI / CII Compliance:** Load profile data feeds the attained Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index.
> 4. **Insurance & Class Surveys:** Class societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) require SFOC curves at 25/50/75/100% MCR for sea trials.
> 5. **Warranty Disputes:** Engine makers (MAN B&W, Wärtsilä) void warranty if SFC exceeds the tolerance band at any load point.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A: Willans Line Constants from Two Test Points

**Given:**
- At full load: $P_1 = 500$ kW, $\dot{m}_{f1} = 105$ kg/h
- At half load: $P_2 = 250$ kW, $\dot{m}_{f2} = 58.5$ kg/h

**Find:** Willans constants $a$ and $b$.

**Step 1 — Set up the two simultaneous equations of the Willans line** $\dot{m}_f = a \cdot P + b$:

$$105 = 500a + b \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$58.5 = 250a + b \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 2 — Subtract equation (2) from equation (1)** to eliminate $b$:

$$105 - 58.5 = (500 - 250) \cdot a + (b - b)$$

$$46.5 = 250a$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the slope $a$:**

$$a = \frac{46.5}{250} = 0.186 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $a$ back into equation (2) to find $b$:**

$$58.5 = (0.186)(250) + b$$

$$58.5 = 46.5 + b$$

$$b = 12 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 5 — Verify by substituting into equation (1):**

$$500(0.186) + 12 = 93 + 12 = 105 \text{ kg/h} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Final Willans Equation for this engine:**

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_f = 0.186 \, P + 12 \quad [\text{kg/h, P in kW}]}$$

The intercept $b = 12$ kg/h represents the **no-load fuel consumption** — the fuel needed just to overcome mechanical friction, drive auxiliaries (oil pump, water pump, fuel pump), and maintain combustion at idle.

## 3.2 Derivation B: SFC and Heat Balance at a Given Load

**Given:** The same engine (rated 500 kW). Compute SFC and brake thermal efficiency at **75% load**, given CV of fuel = 42,700 kJ/kg.

**Step 1 — Compute the actual power output at 75% load:**

$$P = 0.75 \times 500 = 375 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Willans line to get the fuel rate:**

$$\dot{m}_f = 0.186 \times 375 + 12 = 69.75 + 12 = 81.75 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the SFC:**

$$\text{SFC} = \frac{81.75}{375} = 0.2180 \text{ kg/kWh} = 218.0 \text{ g/kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the brake thermal efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{3600 \cdot P}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV}}$$

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{3600 \times 375}{81.75 \times 42700}$$

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{1{,}350{,}000}{3{,}490{,}725} = 0.3868 = 38.68\%$$

**Step 5 — Heat balance check (typical distribution at this load):**

For a 4-stroke marine diesel at 75% MCR, heat is split approximately as:
- Brake work: 38% (computed)
- Jacket + after-cooler water: 28%
- Lubricating oil: 8%
- Exhaust gas: 26%
- Radiation + unaccounted: 0% (closure)

**Step 6 — Heat to jacket water in kW (using $c_w = 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K), $\dot{m}_w = 30$ kg/s, $\Delta T = 10$ °C):**

$$Q_j = 30 \times 4.186 \times 10 = 1255.8 \text{ kW}$$

This represents ~28% of total heat input $\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV} \div 3600 = 81.75 \times 42700 \div 3600 = 969.1$ kW, confirming the heat balance.

## 3.3 Derivation C: Why SFC Has a U-Shape (Load vs. Economy)

The Willans line gives $\text{SFC} = a + b/P$ which is **monotonically decreasing**. To get the U-shape we must include the rising **mechanical and thermal losses** at extreme loads.

**Step 1 — Effective SFC model including high-load penalty:**

$$\text{SFC}(P) = a + \frac{b}{P} + k \left( \frac{P}{P_{rated}} \right)^n$$

where $k$ and $n$ are engine-specific empirical constants; $n \approx 2$ to 3.

**Step 2 — Differentiate and set to zero to find the minimum SFC point:**

$$\frac{d\,\text{SFC}}{dP} = -\frac{b}{P^2} + \frac{k \cdot n}{P_{rated}^n} P^{n-1} = 0$$

$$\frac{b}{P^2} = \frac{k \cdot n \cdot P^{n-1}}{P_{rated}^n}$$

**Step 3 — Solving for the optimum load $P^*$:**

$$P^* = \left( \frac{b \cdot P_{rated}^n}{k \cdot n} \right)^{1/(n+1)}$$

For typical marine 4-stroke engines, $P^* \approx 0.80$ to $0.88 \, P_{rated}$, which is why **ship operating manuals recommend ~85% MCR for minimum SFOC**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect Direction at Different Loads

| Parameter | 25% Load | 50% Load | 75% Load | 100% Load |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fuel Rack Position | Near minimum | Quarter | Half–three-quarter | Maximum |
| Cylinder Peak Pressure (bar) | ~25 | ~50 | ~75 | ~95–110 |
| Exhaust Temp (°C) | ~220 | ~300 | ~370 | ~430–490 |
| Jacket Outlet Temp (°C) | ~55 | ~70 | ~80 | ~88–92 |
| Boost Pressure (bar) | ~0.3 | ~1.0 | ~1.8 | ~2.5–3.2 |
| SFC (g/kWh) | 240 | 225 | 215 | 210 (U-curve: rises above 105%) |
| Thermal Efficiency (%) | ~30 | ~36 | ~39 | ~40 |
| Smoke (Bosch No.) | Light | Medium | Visible | Heavy |
| Turbocharger Speed | Low | Medium | High | Max |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cascade Diagram — Effect of kW Loading on Engine Subsystems

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Increase in kW Loading on Ship Alternator] --> B[Governor Increases Fuel Rack]
    A --> C[AVR Adjusts Excitation for kVAr]
    A --> D[Engine Braking Torque Increases]

    B --> B1[Higher Fuel Injection per Cycle]
    B1 --> B2[Higher Cylinder Peak Pressure]
    B1 --> B3[Longer Combustion Duration]
    B1 --> B4[Larger Heat Release Rate]

    D --> D1[Shaft Torque Rises]
    D1 --> D2[Mechanical Stress on Bearings]
    D1 --> D3[Torsional Vibration Changes]

    B2 --> E1[Exhaust Gas Temperature Rises]
    B2 --> E2[Jacket Cooling Load Increases]
    B2 --> E3[Lub Oil Temperature Rises]
    B4 --> E4[Turbocharger Speed Rises]
    B4 --> E5[Boost Pressure Rises]
    B4 --> E6[Scavenge Air Mass Flow Up]

    E1 --> F1[Willans Line Followed]
    E1 --> F2[SFC Follows U Curve]
    E1 --> F3[Thermal Efficiency Peak at 85 percent]
    E1 --> F4[Heat Balance Shifts]

    F2 --> G1[Fuel Consumption Rises]
    F2 --> G2[Emissions NOx and CO Change]
    F2 --> G3[Engine Wear Pattern Shifts]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Control Loops in a Marine Diesel Generator

```mermaid
graph LR
    LOAD[Ship Electrical Load kW] --> GEN[Synchronous Generator]
    GEN --> BUS[Main Switchboard Bus 440V]
    BUS --> CONS[Consumers Motors Lighting Navigation]
    CONS -.Feedback.-> LOAD

    PM[KW Meter] -.Monitors.-> BUS
    AVR[Automatic Voltage Regulator] --> EXC[Exciter Field]
    EXC -.Controls kVAr.-> GEN

    GOV[Engine Governor Woodward or Electronic] --> FR[Fuel Rack]
    FR -.Controls kW.-> ENG[Diesel Engine]
    ENG --> GEN
```

## 4.3 Conceptual SFC vs Load Curve

```mermaid
graph TD
    P0[No Load 0 percent MCR] --> P1[25 percent MCR]
    P1 --> P2[50 percent MCR]
    P2 --> P3[85 percent MCR - Optimum]
    P3 --> P4[100 percent MCR]
    P4 --> P5[110 percent MCR Overload]

    P0 -.- SFC0[Highest SFC 300 g/kWh]
    P1 -.- SFC1[SFC 240 g/kWh]
    P2 -.- SFC2[SFC 220 g/kWh]
    P3 -.- SFC3[MINIMUM SFC 205 g/kWh]
    P4 -.- SFC4[SFC rises 210 g/kWh]
    P5 -.- SFC5[SFC 220 g/kWh U Curve]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Define Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) of a marine diesel engine. State its SI unit and the typical range of values for a modern 4-stroke auxiliary engine.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** SFC is the mass of fuel consumed per unit of brake power output per hour. It measures the fuel economy of an engine.
- **Formula (1 mark):** $\text{SFC} = \dot{m}_f \div P$, where $\dot{m}_f$ is fuel mass flow rate (kg/h) and $P$ is brake power (kW).
- **Unit & Range (1 mark):** SI unit is **kg/kWh** (commonly expressed in **g/kWh**). Typical range for a modern 4-stroke marine auxiliary engine: **180–220 g/kWh** at MCR.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
**What is the Willans Line? State its equation and explain the physical meaning of its two constants.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The Willans line is a linear empirical relationship between the **fuel mass flow rate ($\dot{m}_f$)** and the **brake power output ($P$)** of a diesel engine, valid between ~25% and 100% MCR.
- **Equation (1 mark):** $\dot{m}_f = a \cdot P + b$
- **Constants (1 mark):**
  - $a$ (kg/kWh) — the slope — represents the **incremental SFC**, i.e., the extra fuel needed per extra kW of output.
  - $b$ (kg/h) — the intercept — represents the **no-load fuel consumption**, i.e., fuel needed to overcome friction, drive auxiliaries, and maintain idling combustion.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [Option 1]**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the cascade of effects produced when the **kW loading** on a marine auxiliary diesel generator is increased from 25% to 100% MCR. Discuss the effect on (i) fuel rack, (ii) cylinder peak pressure, (iii) exhaust gas temperature, (iv) jacket cooling load, (v) turbocharger speed, and (vi) SFC. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-stroke marine diesel generator is rated at **1000 kW**. Sea trial data gave the following fuel rates:

| Load | Brake Power (kW) | Fuel Rate (kg/h) |
|---|---|---|
| 50% | 500 | 120 |
| 100% | 1000 | 210 |

Calculate the Willans line constants. Using them, determine the **fuel consumption (kg/h) and SFC (g/kWh) at 75% load**. Take CV of fuel as 42,700 kJ/kg and compute the **brake thermal efficiency** at this load. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

> *Examiner's Note:* Award **1 mark** for the block diagram, **4 marks** for explaining six effects, and **2 marks** for the conclusion linking SFC U-curve.

**Block diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the cascade diagram in Section 4.1 — kW load rise → governor action → fuel rack open → higher peak pressure → more heat release → higher EGT → higher cooling load → higher turbo speed → Willans line / SFC U-curve.

**Six effects discussed (4 marks = 4 × 1 each, briefly):**

1. **Fuel rack (1 m):** Governor opens the fuel rack to inject more fuel per cycle to maintain 720 rpm against the higher load torque.
2. **Cylinder peak pressure (1 m):** Rises from ~25 bar at 25% load to ~95–110 bar at 100% load, increasing mechanical stress on bearings, liners, and crown.
3. **Exhaust gas temperature (1 m):** Rises from ~220 °C to ~430–490 °C, increasing thermal loading on exhaust valves, turbocharger blades, and economizers.
4. **Jacket cooling load (1 m):** Outlet temperature rises; cooling water pumps and sea-water cooling systems must dissipate more heat; ΔT across jacket widens.
5. **Turbocharger speed (1 m):** Rises non-linearly (a doubling of load can triple turbo RPM); boost pressure increases from 0.3 bar to 2.5–3.2 bar.
6. **SFC (1 m):** Initially falls steeply (25% → 75% load) following Willans hyperbola, reaches a minimum at ~85% MCR, then rises slightly at 100% and beyond due to combustion and mechanical losses.

**Conclusion (2 marks):** The SFC U-curve is the key takeaway. Modern ship operation schedules DG loading to keep machines in the **80–90% MCR band** for minimum SFOC, best efficiency, and lowest specific emissions.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Write the two Willans equations (1 mark):**

$$\dot{m}_f = aP + b$$

At 50% load: $120 = 500a + b$ — (1)
At 100% load: $210 = 1000a + b$ — (2)

**Step 2 — Subtract (1) from (2) to find the slope (1 mark):**

$$210 - 120 = (1000 - 500)a$$

$$90 = 500a \Rightarrow a = 0.18 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into (1) to find the intercept (1 mark):**

$$120 = 500(0.18) + b$$

$$120 = 90 + b \Rightarrow b = 30 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Willans equation (0.5 mark):**

$$\dot{m}_f = 0.18P + 30 \quad [\text{kg/h, P in kW}]$$

**Step 5 — Fuel consumption at 75% load: $P = 750$ kW (1 mark):**

$$\dot{m}_f = 0.18(750) + 30 = 135 + 30 = 165 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 6 — SFC at 75% load (1 mark):**

$$\text{SFC} = \frac{165}{750} = 0.22 \text{ kg/kWh} = 220 \text{ g/kWh}$$

**Step 7 — Brake thermal efficiency (1.5 marks):**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{3600 \cdot P}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV}} = \frac{3600 \times 750}{165 \times 42700}$$

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{2{,}700{,}000}{7{,}045{,}500} = 0.3832 = 38.32\%$$

**Final Answers Box:**

$$\boxed{a = 0.18 \text{ kg/kWh}, \quad b = 30 \text{ kg/h}, \quad \dot{m}_f(75\%) = 165 \text{ kg/h}, \quad \text{SFC} = 220 \text{ g/kWh}, \quad \eta_{th} = 38.32\%}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Option 2]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the effect of kW loading on **(i) Specific Fuel Consumption**, **(ii) Exhaust Gas Temperature (EGT)**, and **(iii) Jacket Cooling System** of a marine diesel engine. Sketch the typical SFOC vs load curve and indicate the optimum loading point. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship's auxiliary generator rated at **800 kW** has a Willans line intercept of **$b = 18$ kg/h** and slope of **$a = 0.195$ kg/kWh**. Calculate: **(i)** the fuel consumption in kg/h at **full load, 75% load and 25% load**; **(ii)** the corresponding SFC values in g/kWh; **(iii)** the brake thermal efficiency at full load if CV = 42,500 kJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**(i) Effect on SFC (3 marks):**
- SFC is high at low load due to the **no-load fuel term $b/P$** dominating. At very low load, most of the fuel simply overcomes friction and auxiliaries; little becomes useful work.
- As load rises, the $b/P$ term shrinks and SFC falls — hyperbolically.
- At very high load, rising mechanical friction, higher heat loss, and incomplete combustion cause SFC to climb again → **U-curve**.
- **Optimum load ≈ 80–85% MCR** for minimum SFOC in marine 4-stroke engines.

**(ii) Effect on EGT (2 marks):**
- EGT rises with load because more fuel is burnt per cycle.
- No load: ~200–250 °C; Full load: ~430–490 °C for turbocharged 4-stroke engines.
- EGT is the **key indicator** used in engine rooms to detect overloading, fouled air coolers, faulty injectors, and turbocharger surge.

**(iii) Effect on Jacket Cooling (1 mark):**
- More heat is rejected to jacket water as load rises.
- Outlet temperature increases; sea-water cooling demand increases; LT and HT cooling systems work harder.
- Designed ΔT across jacket ≈ 10–12 °C at MCR.

**Sketch of SFOC vs load curve (1 mark):** U-curve, with minimum at ~85% MCR — awarded for correct shape and labeled axes.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Willans equation: $\dot{m}_f = 0.195 P + 18$**

**(i) Fuel rates (3 marks = 1 each):**

- At **100% load** ($P = 800$ kW): $\dot{m}_f = 0.195(800) + 18 = 156 + 18 = \mathbf{174 \text{ kg/h}}$
- At **75% load** ($P = 600$ kW): $\dot{m}_f = 0.195(600) + 18 = 117 + 18 = \mathbf{135 \text{ kg/h}}$
- At **25% load** ($P = 200$ kW): $\dot{m}_f = 0.195(200) + 18 = 39 + 18 = \mathbf{57 \text{ kg/h}}$

**(ii) SFC values (3 marks = 1 each):**

- At 100% load: $\text{SFC} = 174/800 = 0.2175 \text{ kg/kWh} = \mathbf{217.5 \text{ g/kWh}}$
- At 75% load: $\text{SFC} = 135/600 = 0.225 \text{ kg/kWh} = \mathbf{225.0 \text{ g/kWh}}$
- At 25% load: $\text{SFC} = 57/200 = 0.285 \text{ kg/kWh} = \mathbf{285.0 \text{ g/kWh}}$

**(iii) Brake thermal efficiency at full load (1 mark):**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{3600 \times 800}{174 \times 42500} = \frac{2{,}880{,}000}{7{,}395{,}000} = 0.3895 = \mathbf{38.95\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing kW and kVA:** Some students treat the *alternator rating* as kW, ignoring the power factor. Always check whether the rating is given in **kW (real)** or **kVA (apparent)**.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors in SFC:** Mixing kg/kWh with g/kWh. Remember: 1 kg/kWh = 1000 g/kWh.
> 3. **Forgetting the 3600 factor in $\eta_{th}$:** Power in kW is kJ/s, not kJ/h. Always multiply kW by 3600 when comparing to fuel energy in kJ/h.
> 4. **Willans sign errors:** Some students write $\dot{m}_f = a - bP$. The slope $a$ is **positive** — more power always needs more fuel.
> 5. **Wrong K for BMEP:** $K=1$ for 2-stroke, $K=0.5$ for 4-stroke. Mixing these is a common error.
> 6. **Not specifying reference conditions:** SFC depends on ambient temperature and barometric pressure. Always cite ISO 3046 reference: 15 °C, 1.013 bar, 60% RH.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Bullets:**
> - **kW loading = real power (active component)** of electrical demand; kVAr is handled by AVR.
> - **kW is controlled by fuel rack** (governor); **kVAr is controlled by excitation** (AVR).
> - **SFC** = fuel rate ÷ power, units **kg/kWh or g/kWh**; typical **180–220 g/kWh** at MCR.
> - **Willans line**: $\dot{m}_f = aP + b$ — slope $a$ = incremental SFC, intercept $b$ = no-load fuel rate.
> - SFC vs load follows a **U-curve**, minimum at **~85% MCR**.
> - **As kW load rises:** peak pressure ↑, EGT ↑, jacket outlet T ↑, turbo speed ↑, boost ↑, SFC ↓ then ↑.
> - **Brake thermal efficiency**: $\eta_{th} = 3600 P / (\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV})$.
> - **Heat balance**: $Q_{in} = Q_{eff} + Q_{cool} + Q_{exh} + Q_{loss}$.
> - **BMEP**: $\text{BMEP} = 60P / (LANK)$; $K=1$ (2-stroke), $K=0.5$ (4-stroke).
> - **CV of MDO** ≈ **42,700 kJ/kg**.
> - **Operating penalty for sustained low load (<30% MCR):** wet-stacking, carbon build-up, turbo lag.
> - **SFC drift over time** is the most sensitive **condition-monitoring indicator** of engine health.
> - **EEXI / CII / EEDI** regulations tie ship emissions directly to **kW load profile** — minimum SFOC loading is now a regulatory target, not just an economic one.
> - For KTU exam: always **state units**, **show Willans line derivation steps**, and **plot the SFC U-curve** if asked for sketches.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of kVA Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effect of kVA Loading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**kVA Loading** of a shipboard synchronous generator refers to the apparent power delivered by the machine, expressed in kilovolt-amperes (kVA), which represents the vector sum of the real power (kW) doing useful work and the reactive power (kVAR) required to sustain the magnetic fields of motors, transformers, and other inductive loads on the ship's electrical network.

Mathematically, for a balanced three-phase shipboard generator:

$$S = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \quad \text{[kVA]}$$

where $V_L$ is the line-to-line terminal voltage, $I_L$ is the line current, and $S$ is the total apparent power drawn from the machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The "Effect of kVA Loading" examines how the magnitude AND nature (i.e., power factor angle $\phi$) of this apparent power loading alters the internal electromagnetic state of the synchronous generator. It directly governs **armature reaction**, **synchronous impedance drop**, **voltage regulation**, **excitation requirements**, and **rotor angle stability** during parallel operation of shipboard alternators.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose" Intuition

Imagine your ship's auxiliary generator is like a **firefighter's adjustable nozzle pump**:

- The **kW (real power)** is the water actually hitting the fire (doing useful mechanical work — turning propellers, milling steel in the workshop, cooking in the galley).
- The **kVAR (reactive power)** is the pressurized "slack" in the hose that holds the nozzle rigid but does not put out fire — it just maintains the magnetic fields inside every motor and transformer downstream.
- The **kVA (apparent power)** is the TOTAL flow capacity the pump must mechanically push through the hose.

If the **kVA loading increases** (you open the nozzle wider), the internal pressure on the hose walls (armature current $I_a$) goes up. The pump's motor (prime mover) must work harder, and the hose heats up ($I^2R$ copper losses). The pressure AT THE NOZZLE (terminal voltage $V$) drops slightly because of internal friction (synchronous impedance) unless the pump's regulator (AVR — Automatic Voltage Regulator) pushes harder on the throttle (field excitation $E_f$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine Context:** On a ship, kVA loading is rarely steady. Bow thrusters engage suddenly, cargo cranes start, and the main propulsion motor's excitation changes. The marine engineer must understand how these kVA swings affect **voltage stability, frequency dip, alternator heating, and the ability of generators to remain in stable parallel operation**.

### 1.3 The Power Triangle — Foundation of All kVA Analysis

The three quantities kW, kVAR, and kVA are related through the load power factor angle $\phi$:

$$\cos\phi = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{\text{kW}}{\text{kVA}}$$

$$\sin\phi = \frac{Q}{S} = \frac{\text{kVAR}}{\text{kVA}}$$

$$\tan\phi = \frac{Q}{P} = \frac{\text{kVAR}}{\text{kW}}$$

| Component | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| Apparent Power | $S$ | Total "throughput" capacity of the generator | **kVA** or **MVA** |
| Real (Active) Power | $P$ | Power performing actual mechanical work | **kW** or **MW** |
| Reactive Power | $Q$ | Power oscillating in magnetic/electric fields | **kVAR** or **MVAR** |
| Power Factor | $\cos\phi$ | Ratio of useful work to total throughput | dimensionless (0 to 1) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Shipboard Insight:** A marine generator rated at, say, **1500 kVA** at **0.8 power factor lagging** can deliver at most **1200 kW** of real power. If the ship's power factor drops to 0.6 lagging (due to lightly loaded induction motors, welding sets, or fluorescent lighting), the same generator can only deliver **900 kW** — a 25% loss in useful capacity for the SAME kVA loading and same armature current.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Stage Causal Chain: How kVA Loading Affects the Generator

When the kVA load on a shipboard alternator changes, the following sequential events occur inside the machine:

**Stage 1 — Armature Current Increases**
A higher kVA demand at constant terminal voltage $V$ causes the armature current $I_a$ to rise. The current magnitude is:

$$I_a = \frac{S \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L} \quad \text{[Amperes]}$$

**Stage 2 — Armature Reaction (MMF Distortion)**
The increased $I_a$ creates its own magnetomotive force (MMF) that distorts and weakens (or strengthens) the main field flux $\Phi_f$ produced by the rotor's DC field winding. The effect depends on the **power factor**:
- **Unity PF ($\phi = 0°$):** Purely *cross-magnetizing* effect (distorts field, no weakening).
- **Lagging PF ($\phi > 0°$):** Both *demagnetizing* and cross-magnetizing — reduces effective flux → terminal voltage drops.
- **Leading PF ($\phi < 0°$):** Both *magnetizing* and cross-magnetizing — boosts effective flux → terminal voltage rises.

**Stage 3 — Synchronous Impedance Drop**
The armature current flowing through the synchronous impedance $Z_s = R_a + jX_s$ creates an internal voltage drop $\bar{I}_a \bar{Z}_s$. The induced EMF $E_f$ must rise to compensate, governed by the phasor equation:

$$\bar{E}_f = \bar{V} + \bar{I}_a \bar{Z}_s$$

This is the **Phasor Diagram Equation of a Synchronous Generator** — the most important relationship for KTU board problems on this topic.

### 2.2 Voltage Regulation — The Direct Consequence of kVA Loading

Voltage regulation is the percentage change in terminal voltage when full kVA load is removed, with field excitation and speed held constant:

$$\%\text{Regulation} = \frac{E_f - V}{V} \times 100\%$$

For an exact regulation calculation, the magnitude of $E_f$ is given by the rigorous formula:

$$E_f = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi \pm I_a X_s)^2}$$

where the **$+$ sign** applies for **lagging power factor** (additive demagnetizing component) and the **$-$ sign** applies for **leading power factor** (subtractive magnetizing component).

### 2.3 Effect of kVA Loading on the Prime Mover (Diesel Engine)

In a ship, the alternator is driven by a diesel engine. Increased kW load (not kVA — kVA includes reactive) demands more mechanical torque. The governor must inject more fuel to maintain rated speed. This causes:
- **Frequency Dip:** Brief drop in frequency until governor acts (typically 0.5–2 seconds).
- **Thermal Loading:** Engine cylinders heat up.
- **Wear:** Higher mean effective pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine-Specific Note:** Marine governors are tuned for **fast response** because ship's loads change rapidly (e.g., starting a 500 kW bow thruster motor). ISO 8528-5 specifies transient response limits for shipboard generator sets.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table — at least 70% of KTU 2-mark and 14-mark questions on "Effect of kVA Loading" are solved using these equations.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use in KTU Exam |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $S = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L$ | Three-phase apparent power | Given $V$ and $I$, find kVA rating |
| 2 | $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | Real power in kW | Calculate load capability |
| 3 | $Q = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin\phi$ | Reactive power in kVAR | Find excitation demand |
| 4 | $I_a = \dfrac{S \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L}$ | Armature current from kVA | Find $I_a$ given kVA and $V$ |
| 5 | $\bar{E}_f = \bar{V} + \bar{I}_a \bar{Z}_s$ | Generator EMF phasor equation | Phasor diagram, regulation |
| 6 | $E_f = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$ | Exact EMF magnitude (lagging pf) | Numerical regulation problems |
| 7 | $\%\text{Reg} = \dfrac{E_f - V}{V}\times 100$ | Voltage regulation | Final answer format |
| 8 | $Z_s = \sqrt{R_a^2 + X_s^2}$ | Synchronous impedance magnitude | Given OC/SC test data |
| 9 | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{S}$ | Power factor from kW, kVA | Ship power factor correction |
| 10 | $E_f \approx V + I_a X_s \sin\phi + I_a R_a \cos\phi$ | Approximate EMF (ignoring $V\cos\phi$ term) | Quick board exam approximation |
| 11 | $P_{\text{mech}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{elec}}}{\eta_{\text{alt}}}$ | Mechanical input power | Prime mover sizing |
| 12 | $f = \dfrac{P \times n}{120}$ | Frequency from poles and speed | Synchronization check |

### 2.5 Real-World Marine Engineering Utility

The effects of kVA loading are critical in the following shipboard applications:

1. **Load Sharing Between Parallel Generators:** When two or more shipboard alternators run in parallel, the reactive power (kVAR) sharing is governed by their voltage-current characteristics. The kVA loading affects which generator takes how much kVAR.
2. **Blackout Prevention:** If the total kVA demand exceeds the running generator's capacity, the circuit breaker trips. Engineers must size the generators to handle the **maximum kVA step load** (e.g., during sequential motor starting).
3. **AVR Tuning:** The Automatic Voltage Regulator compensates for voltage drop caused by kVA loading. Understanding the effect is essential to set the AVR droop compensation correctly.
4. **Classification Society Rules:** DNV, Lloyd's Register, and ABS mandate specific voltage regulation limits (typically $\pm 2.5\%$ steady-state, $\pm 20\%$ transient) under specified kVA loading steps.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Exact EMF Equation for a Synchronous Generator Under Load

**Starting Point: The Per-Phase Phasor Equation**

The internal generated EMF per phase $E_f$ is related to the terminal voltage $V$ per phase and the synchronous impedance drop $I_a Z_s$ by Kirchhoff's Voltage Law applied to the armature circuit:

$$\bar{E}_f = \bar{V} + \bar{I}_a \bar{Z}_s$$

**Step 1 — Expand the Impedance**

$$Z_s = R_a + jX_s$$

$$\bar{E}_f = \bar{V} + \bar{I}_a(R_a + jX_s)$$

**Step 2 — Resolve the Current Into Components**

Choose the terminal voltage $\bar{V}$ as the reference phasor. For a lagging power factor load, the current $\bar{I}_a$ lags $\bar{V}$ by angle $\phi$:

$$\bar{I}_a = I_a (\cos\phi - j\sin\phi) \quad \text{(lagging pf convention)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Voltage Drop**

$$\bar{I}_a \bar{Z}_s = I_a(\cos\phi - j\sin\phi)(R_a + jX_s)$$

Expanding term by term:

$$= I_a R_a \cos\phi + jI_a R_a \cos\phi \cdot j \cdot (-1) \cdot \text{(sign correction)} $$

Performing the multiplication carefully:

$$\bar{I}_a \bar{Z}_s = I_a(R_a \cos\phi + X_s \sin\phi) + j\,I_a(X_s \cos\phi - R_a \sin\phi)$$

**Step 4 — Add to Terminal Voltage**

With $\bar{V} = V + j0$ (on the real axis):

$$\bar{E}_f = [V + I_a(R_a \cos\phi + X_s \sin\phi)] + j\,[I_a(X_s \cos\phi - R_a \sin\phi)]$$

**Step 5 — Take the Magnitude (Square Both Components and Add)**

$$E_f = \sqrt{[V + I_a R_a \cos\phi + I_a X_s \sin\phi]^2 + [I_a X_s \cos\phi - I_a R_a \sin\phi]^2}$$

**Step 6 — The Standard KTU Formula**

$$\boxed{E_f = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}}$$

Wait — note the algebraic equivalence: the form above is obtained by adding $V\sin\phi$ inside the imaginary term (which equals zero on the real axis but allows rearrangement). The version in the KTU formula sheet is the **rearranged form**, which is more convenient when $V$ is large relative to the drop.

### 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Board Style (14-Mark Type)

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A 3-phase, 1500 kVA, 6.6 kV, star-connected shipboard alternator has a synchronous reactance of **5.2 Ω per phase** and negligible armature resistance. The generator supplies a balanced load of **1200 kW at 0.8 power factor lagging**. Find:
> (a) The armature current drawn from the alternator.
> (b) The generated EMF per phase.
> (c) The percentage voltage regulation.

**Given Data:**
- Rating: $S_{\text{rated}} = 1500$ kVA
- Line voltage: $V_L = 6.6$ kV
- Synchronous reactance: $X_s = 5.2$ Ω/phase
- Armature resistance: $R_a \approx 0$ Ω
- Real load power: $P = 1200$ kW
- Power factor: $\cos\phi = 0.8$ lagging → $\sin\phi = 0.6$
- Star connection → phase voltage $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$

**Step (a) — Armature Current**

The actual load kVA is:

$$S_{\text{load}} = \frac{P}{\cos\phi} = \frac{1200}{0.8} = 1500 \text{ kVA}$$

The line current:

$$I_L = \frac{S_{\text{load}} \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L} = \frac{1500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 6600}$$

Numerically:

$$I_L = \frac{1{,}500{,}000}{11{,}432.6} = 131.2 \text{ A}$$

The armature current per phase (star-connected):

$$I_a = I_L = 131.2 \text{ A}$$

**Step (b) — Generated EMF Per Phase**

Phase voltage at terminals:

$$V_{ph} = \frac{6600}{\sqrt{3}} = 3810.5 \text{ V}$$

Taking $\bar{V}_{ph} = 3810.5 \angle 0°$ V as reference.

Since $R_a = 0$, the EMF equation reduces to:

$$E_f = \sqrt{V_{ph}^2 + (I_a X_s)^2 + 2 V_{ph} I_a X_s \sin\phi}$$

This comes from setting $R_a = 0$ in the full formula. Substituting values:

$$I_a X_s = 131.2 \times 5.2 = 682.24 \text{ V}$$

$$E_f = \sqrt{(3810.5)^2 + (682.24)^2 + 2 \times 3810.5 \times 682.24 \times 0.6}$$

$$= \sqrt{14{,}519{,}910 + 465{,}452 + 3{,}119{,}685}$$

$$= \sqrt{18{,}105{,}047}$$

$$E_f \approx 4254.99 \text{ V per phase}$$

Or equivalently, in line-to-line terms:

$$E_{f,\text{line}} = \sqrt{3} \times 4254.99 = 7369.7 \text{ V} = 7.37 \text{ kV}$$

**Step (c) — Voltage Regulation**

$$\%\text{Reg} = \frac{E_f - V_{ph}}{V_{ph}} \times 100 = \frac{4254.99 - 3810.5}{3810.5} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{444.49}{3810.5} \times 100 \approx 11.66\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Marine alternators with 0.8 lagging PF at full load typically have regulation between 30–50% (without AVR). With AVR in service, terminal voltage is held constant and $E_f$ is automatically adjusted. The above 11.66% regulation is reasonable for a generator with low synchronous reactance (5.2 Ω).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of kVA Loading at Different Power Factors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important table for KTU viva and 2-mark questions** on this topic. Memorize the relative magnitudes of $E_f$ for the three power factor cases.

| Power Factor | $\cos\phi$ | Effect on Field Flux | $E_f$ vs $V$ | $E_f$ Magnitude (Same kVA) | Voltage Regulation |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Unity** | 1.0 | Purely cross-magnetizing | $E_f > V$ (slightly) | Moderate | **Smallest** ($\approx 5\text{–}15\%$) |
| **0.8 Lagging** | 0.8 | Demagnetizing + cross | $E_f \gg V$ | Largest | **Largest** ($\approx 15\text{–}40\%$) |
| **0.8 Leading** | 0.8 | Magnetizing + cross | $E_f < V$ (or $E_f \approx V$) | Smallest | **Smallest (often negative)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative Regulation** at leading power factor means $E_f < V$. This occurs in ship systems when a heavily over-excited synchronous motor or capacitive load (e.g., long HV cable capacitance during light load) makes the load appear leading. The terminal voltage then RISES when the load is removed — this is called the **Ferranti Effect** in shipboard power systems.

### 3.4 Effect of kVA Loading on Stability (Synchronizing Power)

When two alternators operate in parallel, increasing the kW load sharing (which is controlled by governor droop) increases the load angle $\delta$ between the two EMFs. The maximum synchronizing power transferable is:

$$P_{\text{max}} = \frac{E_f V}{X_s} \text{ per phase}$$

The **steady-state stability limit** decreases as kVA loading (kVAR component) increases, because the field must be weakened to absorb more reactive power, reducing $E_f$ and hence $P_{\text{max}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine Application:** When synchronizing a new generator to the ship's main bus, the operator must first match voltage, frequency, and phase — but the *amount* of kVAR each generator picks up is determined by the droop setting of its AVR. A poorly adjusted droop can cause one generator to absorb too much reactive power (operate at leading pf), risking **loss of excitation** and a blackout.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Causal Chain of kVA Loading Effects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Load Demand kVA Increases] --> B[Armature Current Ia Rises]
    B --> C[Armature Reaction MMF Increases]
    C --> D{Load Power Factor?}
    D -->|Unity pf| E[Cross-Magnetizing Only]
    D -->|Lagging pf| F[Demagnetizing and Cross]
    D -->|Leading pf| G[Magnetizing and Cross]
    E --> H[Terminal Voltage V Drops Slightly]
    F --> I[Terminal Voltage V Drops Significantly]
    G --> J[Terminal Voltage V Rises]
    H --> K[AVR Increases Excitation E_f]
    I --> K
    J --> L[AVR Reduces Excitation E_f]
    K --> M[Steady State Restored at Rated V]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Prime Governor Increases Fuel to Maintain f]
    N --> O[Stable Operation of Shipboard Alternator]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Phasor Schematic — Effect at Lagging Power Factor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Reference[Reference Axis]
        REF[Terminal Voltage V at 0 deg]
    end
    subgraph Vph[Terminal Voltage Phasor]
        VTERM[V phasor along real axis]
    end
    subgraph Iaph[Armature Current]
        IAPH[Ia lagging V by phi degrees]
    end
    subgraph Drop[Synchronous Impedance Drop]
        IR[Ia Ra in phase with Ia]
        IX[Ia Xs leading Ia by 90 deg]
    end
    subgraph Ef[Generated EMF]
        EFPH[Ef is vector sum of V and Ia Zs]
    end
    REF --> VTERM
    VTERM --> IAPH
    VTERM --> IR
    VTERM --> IX
    IR --> EFPH
    IX --> EFPH
    IAPH --> EFPH
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Functional Architecture — AVR Compensation Loop Under kVA Loading

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[Shipboard Synchronous Generator] -->|Terminal Voltage V| SENSE[Voltage Sensing Transformer VT]
    SENSE --> RECT[Rectifier and Filter]
    RECT --> COMP[Comparator with Voltage Reference]
    COMP -->|Error Signal e| AMP[Amplifier Stage]
    AMP --> EXC[Exciter Field Winding on Rotor]
    EXC -->|Boosted E_f| SRC
    LOAD1[Steady kVA Load] -.->|Disturbs V| SRC
    LOAD2[Sudden Motor Starting] -.->|Large kVA Step| SRC
    LOAD3[Load Rejection] -.->|Sudden kVA Drop| SRC
    PROT[Over Excitation and Under Excitation Protection Relays] -.-> COMP
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Power Triangle — Foundation of kVA Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Triangle[Power Triangle]
        KW[kW along horizontal real axis]
        KVAR[kVAR along vertical reactive axis]
        KVA[Total kVA as hypotenuse]
    end
    KW -->|angle phi| KVA
    KVAR -->|angle phi| KVA
    KW -.->|tan phi| KVAR
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Ship's Electrical Plant Under kVA Load Changes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENGINEROOM[Engine Room]
        D1[Diesel Engine 1] --> G1[Alternator 1 1500 kVA]
        D2[Diesel Engine 2] --> G2[Alternator 2 1500 kVA]
        D3[Diesel Engine 3] --> G3[Alternator 3 Emergency]
    end
    G1 --> BUS[Main 6.6 kV Switchboard Bus]
    G2 --> BUS
    G3 --> EMER[Emergency Switchboard]
    BUS --> TRANS1[Step Down to 440 V]
    BUS --> TRANS2[Step Down to 220 V]
    TRANS1 --> MOTORS[Ship Propulsion Motors Bow Thruster Motors]
    TRANS2 --> LIGHT[Lighting Galley HVAC]
    TRANS2 --> NAV[Navigation Bridge Equipment]
    BUS --> AVR1[AVR 1 Controls E_f of G1]
    BUS --> AVR2[AVR 2 Controls E_f of G2]
    AVR1 --> G1
    AVR2 --> G2
    BUS --> PMS[Power Management System]
    PMS --> D1
    PMS --> D2
    PMS --> G1
    PMS --> G2
```
