## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*Define the term 'kVA loading' of a shipboard synchronous generator. How does it differ from 'kW loading'?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
kVA loading refers to the total apparent power delivered by the generator, given by $S = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L$. It includes both the real (kW) and reactive (kVAR) components. kW loading, on the other hand, is the real power doing useful work: $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$. **The key difference is that kVA loading includes the magnetizing requirement of inductive loads (kVAR), while kW loading represents only the mechanical output of the prime mover.** For the same armature current, a lower power factor means the generator can deliver less kW for the same kVA.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*State the effect of leading power factor load on the terminal voltage of a shipboard alternator.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
At leading power factor, the armature reaction has a **magnetizing component** (in addition to cross-magnetizing). This magnetizing effect boosts the main field flux, which causes the terminal voltage to **rise** as kVA loading increases. The induced EMF $E_f$ required is smaller than at unity or lagging power factor. The voltage regulation may even become **negative**, meaning the terminal voltage is higher under load than at no-load — a phenomenon common on ships with long HV cables and lightly loaded synchronous motor drives.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the EMF equation of a 3-phase synchronous generator supplying a lagging power factor load, starting from the basic phasor relation $\bar{E}_f = \bar{V} + \bar{I}_a \bar{Z}_s$. Clearly state the meaning of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 750 kVA, 3.3 kV, 3-phase, star-connected shipboard alternator has armature resistance of **0.4 Ω/phase** and synchronous reactance of **3.8 Ω/phase**. It supplies a balanced load of **600 kW at 0.8 power factor lagging**. Calculate:
  (i) The armature current per phase. **(3 marks)**
  (ii) The generated EMF per phase. **(2.5 marks)**
  (iii) The percentage voltage regulation. **(1.5 marks)**

##### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting with the per-phase KVL equation:

$$\bar{E}_f = \bar{V} + \bar{I}_a (R_a + jX_s) \quad \cdots (1)$$

Let $\bar{V} = V\angle 0°$ (reference), and for lagging pf, $\bar{I}_a = I_a \angle -\phi$.

Writing (1) in component form by expanding $R_a + jX_s$:

$$E_f = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$$

Each term has the following meaning:
- $V\cos\phi$ : in-phase component of terminal voltage.
- $I_a R_a$ : ohmic drop in armature winding.
- $V\sin\phi$ : quadrature component of terminal voltage.
- $I_a X_s$ : reactive drop across synchronous reactance.
- The **$+$** sign in the imaginary term arises because the current's lagging nature causes $I_a X_s$ to add to the quadrature component of $V$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step evaluation to be shown in exam** (see Section 3.1 of these notes for full expansion). The final boxed formula carries **7 marks** if all intermediate steps are written.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $S_{\text{rated}} = 750$ kVA, $V_L = 3.3$ kV, star-connected
- $R_a = 0.4$ Ω, $X_s = 3.8$ Ω
- $P = 600$ kW, $\cos\phi = 0.8$ lagging, $\sin\phi = 0.6$

**(i) Armature current (3 marks):**

Phase voltage:

$$V_{ph} = \frac{3300}{\sqrt{3}} = 1905.3 \text{ V}$$

Load apparent power:

$$S_{\text{load}} = \frac{P}{\cos\phi} = \frac{600}{0.8} = 750 \text{ kVA}$$

Armature current:

$$I_a = \frac{S \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L} = \frac{750{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \times 3300} = 131.2 \text{ A}$$

**(ii) Generated EMF per phase (2.5 marks):**

Using the standard formula:

$$E_f = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$$

Computing each term:

$$V\cos\phi = 1905.3 \times 0.8 = 1524.2 \text{ V}$$
$$I_a R_a = 131.2 \times 0.4 = 52.48 \text{ V}$$
$$V\sin\phi = 1905.3 \times 0.6 = 1143.2 \text{ V}$$
$$I_a X_s = 131.2 \times 3.8 = 498.56 \text{ V}$$

Sum of components:

$$\text{Real part} = 1524.2 + 52.48 = 1576.68$$
$$\text{Imag part} = 1143.2 + 498.56 = 1641.76$$

$$E_f = \sqrt{(1576.68)^2 + (1641.76)^2} = \sqrt{2{,}486{,}400 + 2{,}695{,}615}$$

$$E_f = \sqrt{5{,}182{,}015} \approx 2276.4 \text{ V per phase}$$

**(iii) Voltage regulation (1.5 marks):**

$$\%\text{Reg} = \frac{2276.4 - 1905.3}{1905.3} \times 100 = \frac{371.1}{1905.3} \times 100 \approx 19.48\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A very common mistake students make is to use **line voltage** (3.3 kV) instead of **phase voltage** (1.905 kV) in the EMF formula. The equation $E_f = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$ is a **PER-PHASE** equation. Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ division from $V_L$ to $V_{ph}$ will give you a regulation value that is exactly $\sqrt{3}$ times too large, and you will lose **3 full marks**. Also, students often forget to convert kW/kVA values into per-phase (they are total 3-phase — do not divide by 3). The current formula is correctly applied with total 3-phase S and line voltage.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain with the help of phasor diagrams how the terminal voltage of a shipboard synchronous generator is affected by kVA loading at **(i) unity power factor**, **(ii) lagging power factor**, and **(iii) leading power factor**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 500 kVA, 3.3 kV, 3-phase, star-connected marine alternator has a synchronous impedance of **4.5 Ω/phase** (resistance negligible). The generator supplies full load at **(i) 0.8 power factor lagging** and **(ii) 0.8 power factor leading**. Compute the percentage voltage regulation in each case and comment on the results. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution Outline to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Phasor Analysis (7 marks):**

For each case, draw the phasor diagram with $\bar{V}$ as reference, $\bar{I}_a$ at angle $\pm\phi$, the drop $I_a X_s$ perpendicular to $\bar{I}_a$, and the resultant $\bar{E}_f$ as the vector sum.

(i) **Unity PF:** $E_f = \sqrt{V^2 + (I_a X_s)^2}$. Small regulation (5–15%).
(ii) **Lagging PF:** $E_f = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$. Largest regulation (15–40%). Demagnetizing effect.
(iii) **Leading PF:** $E_f = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi)^2 + (V\sin\phi - I_a X_s)^2}$. Smallest, often negative regulation. Magnetizing effect.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

$V_{ph} = 3300/\sqrt{3} = 1905.3$ V. $I_a = 500{,}000 / (\sqrt{3} \times 3300) = 87.5$ A.

$I_a X_s = 87.5 \times 4.5 = 393.75$ V.

**Case (i) — Lagging pf (0.8):**
$$E_f = \sqrt{(1905.3 \times 0.8)^2 + (1905.3 \times 0.6 + 393.75)^2}$$
$$= \sqrt{(1524.2)^2 + (1143.2 + 393.75)^2} = \sqrt{2{,}323{,}178 + 2{,}364{,}185}$$
$$= \sqrt{4{,}687{,}363} = 2165.0 \text{ V}$$
$$\%\text{Reg}_{\text{lag}} = \frac{2165.0 - 1905.3}{1905.3} \times 100 = 13.63\%$$

**Case (ii) — Leading pf (0.8):**
$$E_f = \sqrt{(1524.2)^2 + (1143.2 - 393.75)^2} = \sqrt{2{,}323{,}178 + 561{,}479}$$
$$= \sqrt{2{,}884{,}657} = 1698.4 \text{ V}$$
$$\%\text{Reg}_{\text{lead}} = \frac{1698.4 - 1905.3}{1905.3} \times 100 = -10.86\%$$

**Comment:** At leading pf, the regulation is **negative**, confirming the magnetizing effect of leading power factor loads. On a ship, this often occurs during navigation with light load and long cable runs, and is one of the conditions the AVR must handle.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following 12 points the night before the KTU exam:**

- [ ] **kVA loading** is the total apparent power: $S = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L$, comprising kW (real) and kVAR (reactive).
- [ ] Increasing kVA loading at constant $V$ → armature current $I_a$ increases → armature reaction intensifies.
- [ ] **Lagging pf load** causes **demagnetizing + cross-magnetizing** armature reaction → highest $E_f$ → largest (positive) voltage regulation.
- [ ] **Leading pf load** causes **magnetizing + cross-magnetizing** armature reaction → lowest $E_f$ → smallest or **negative** voltage regulation.
- [ ] **Unity pf load** causes **pure cross-magnetizing** effect → moderate regulation.
- [ ] The fundamental equation is $\bar{E}_f = \bar{V} + \bar{I}_a (R_a + jX_s)$ — KTU favourite 2-mark question.
- [ ] The exact magnitude form (for lagging pf) is $E_f = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$ — must be applied with **per-phase** values.
- [ ] **Voltage regulation** = $(E_f - V)/V \times 100\%$; it tells you how much the terminal voltage changes between no-load and full-load at constant field.
- [ ] Marine alternators with AVR have regulation maintained within $\pm 2.5\%$ steady-state; without AVR, regulation can be 20–40%.
- [ ] kVA loading affects the **prime mover** (fuel demand) and **excitation system** (AVR demand) separately — kW affects fuel, kVAR affects excitation.
- [ ] In **parallel operation**, kW sharing is governed by **governor droop** and kVAR sharing by **AVR droop**.
- [ ] **Stability limit** $P_{\text{max}} = E_f V / X_s$ per phase decreases as kVAR loading increases — the field weakens to absorb more reactive power, reducing $E_f$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner's Tip:** In the KTU board exam, ALWAYS draw the phasor diagram in your answer, even for numerical problems. Examiners award **partial marks** for a correct phasor diagram even if your final EMF magnitude is wrong. Label all four phasors: $\bar{V}$, $\bar{I}_a$, $\bar{I}_a R_a$, $\bar{I}_a X_s$, and $\bar{E}_f$. This single habit can boost your score from a 6 to a 9 on a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# MODULE 4 — BASICS OF MARINE ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Marine electrical engineering* refers to the specialised branch of electrical engineering that deals with the **generation, distribution, control, protection, and utilisation** of electrical energy onboard seagoing vessels, offshore platforms, and shipyard installations. The system must comply with classification society rules (Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, IRS) and international conventions such as **SOLAS** (Safety of Life at Sea) and **MARPOL** (Marine Pollution).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Power Plant" View
Think of a modern merchant ship (bulk carrier, container vessel, or oil tanker) as a **self-propelled, floating, miniature power utility** — almost exactly like a small city of about 200–500 inhabitants that moves through the ocean. Onshore, a city receives electricity from a distant national grid through transmission lines. On a ship, however, **the "power station" sits inside the engine room** itself. Diesel generators (or sometimes steam/gas turbines) spin synchronously to produce electrical energy, which is then routed through a network of switchboards, transformers, and cables to drive everything from the bow thruster motor to the cabin reading lamp.

Just as a city grid has voltages stepped up for transmission and down for household use, a ship uses different voltage levels for different consumer categories — heavy propulsion motors may operate at **6.6 kV**, while cabin lights and navigation instruments run on **220 V** or **24 V DC**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standardised Marine Parameters (must memorise for KTU exams):**
> - Standard shipboard AC frequency: **60 Hz** (some naval/civilian ships use **50 Hz**)
> - Standard shipboard AC voltages: **440 V, 3.3 kV, 6.6 kV, 11 kV** (three-phase)
> - Standard DC battery voltages: **24 V, 110 V, 220 V**
> - Emergency power activation time after main failure: **≤ 45 seconds** (SOLAS Ch. II-1)

### Why Are Marine Systems "Special"?
Unlike a shore-based factory, a ship electrical system must:
1. **Withstand** constant rolling, pitching, vibration, humidity, and salt-laden air.
2. **Operate** with limited space, hence very high power density per cubic metre.
3. **Maintain** uninterrupted power for safety-critical equipment (steering gear, fire pumps, navigation).
4. **Synchronise** multiple generators that swing in unison with the ship's motion.
5. **Isolate faults** instantly to prevent total blackout — even one engine room fire or flooding must not disable all power.

---

## 1.2 Taxonomy of a Marine Electrical Network

A complete shipboard electrical installation is conventionally divided into four interconnected sub-systems:

| Sub-System | Primary Function | Typical Components |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Generation** | Convert mechanical energy → electrical energy | Diesel Generators (DG), Shaft Generators, Emergency Generators |
| **Power Distribution** | Route energy from sources to consumers | Main Switchboard (MSB), Emergency Switchboard (ESB), Section Boards, Bus-Tie Breakers |
| **Power Utilisation** | Convert electrical energy → useful work | Propulsion Motors, Winch Motors, HVAC Compressors, Galley Equipment |
| **Control, Protection & Monitoring** | Sense, command, isolate, alarm | Protective Relays, AVR, Governor, PMS (Power Management System), IAS (Integrated Automation System) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** If a KTU question says *"Explain the layout of a ship's electrical system"*, the expected answer must explicitly cover all **four** of the above blocks in the correct order. Skipping "Protection & Monitoring" is the #1 cause of losing 2–3 marks in 14-mark questions.

---

## 1.3 Standard Shipboard Voltage & Frequency Hierarchy

Marine classification societies publish strict rules on permissible voltage levels to standardise global shipbuilding. The most widely adopted tiers are:

```
TIER-1  :  6.6 kV / 11 kV        →  Large propulsion motors (≥ 5 MW)
TIER-2  :  440 V (line-to-line)  →  3-phase, ship-wide distribution
TIER-3  :  220 V (line-to-line)  →  3-phase, hotel & workshop loads
TIER-4  :  110 V (line-to-neutral) →  Single-phase lighting
TIER-5  :  24 V / 110 V DC       →  Batteries, control, emergency
```

The relationship between these tiers is established through **transformers** (e.g., a 6.6 kV / 440 V step-down transformer feeds the auxiliary switchboard from the high-voltage main bus).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOLAS Regulation II-1/40–45** mandates that for vessels whose main propulsion is rated ≥ **3 MW**, the electrical power systems must be designed so that essential services continue even with any one generator out of service. This is called the **"n+1 redundancy rule"** in ship design.

# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fundamental Electrical Quantities in Marine Context

A ship is a self-contained micro-grid. The **same five basic quantities** that govern terrestrial power systems also govern marine systems — but the *boundary conditions* differ dramatically (vibration, salt-fog, sloshing fluids, space constraints).

The five foundational quantities and their marine interpretations are:

| # | Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Marine Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Voltage | $V$ or $E$ | Volt (V) | Determines cable insulation class & safety clearances |
| 2 | Current | $I$ | Ampere (A) | Determines bus-bar sizing & breaker rating |
| 3 | Resistance | $R$ | Ohm ($\Omega$) | Affects cable voltage drop (must be ≤ 7% per IEC 60092) |
| 4 | Power (Active) | $P$ | Watt (W) | Determines generator loading (kW, MW) |
| 5 | Power (Reactive) | $Q$ | VAR (var) | Determines generator kVAr rating & PF correction |
| 6 | Frequency | $f$ | Hertz (Hz) | Fixed at 60 Hz; deviating > ±5% trips protection |
| 7 | Power Factor | $\cos\phi$ | Dimensionless | Typically maintained **0.8 lagging** for ship systems |

---

## 2.2 The Three-Phase Power Triangle (HIGH-YIELD KTU TOPIC)

Almost all shipboard generation and major consumption is **three-phase, three-wire (or four-wire)**. The instantaneous powers sum to a constant value, eliminating torque pulsations in motors — a major reason ships prefer three-phase over single-phase.

For a balanced three-phase system:

$$P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$$

$$Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\phi$$

$$S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L$$

And the power triangle relationship:

$$S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$$

Where:
- $V_L$ = Line-to-line voltage (e.g., 440 V)
- $I_L$ = Line current
- $\cos\phi$ = Power factor (typically 0.8 lagging for ship auxiliaries)
- $P$ = Active (real) power in Watts
- $Q$ = Reactive power in VAR
- $S$ = Apparent power in VA

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 440 V and not 415 V (the Indian onshore standard)?** Marine classification societies standardised on **440 V at 60 Hz** historically because 60 Hz machines are physically smaller and lighter for the same kW rating — critical when every cubic metre of a ship costs money in displacement tonnage. A 60 Hz transformer core, for instance, requires **~17% fewer turns** than an equivalent 50 Hz core, saving copper and iron weight.

---

## 2.3 Synchronous Generator EMF Equation (the "E" of every DG)

The heart of every ship's main switchboard is the **synchronous generator** (alternator). Its induced EMF per phase is governed by:

$$E = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \phi \cdot K_w$$

Where:
- $E$ = RMS induced EMF per phase (V)
- $f$ = Frequency (Hz) → **60 Hz** on most ships
- $N$ = Number of turns per phase in the armature winding
- $\phi$ = Flux per pole (Weber, Wb)
- $K_w$ = Winding factor = $K_d \cdot K_p$ (distribution × pitch factor), typically 0.95–0.98
- $4.44$ = Form factor constant for sinusoidal flux

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Hook:** Imagine $E$ as a "muscle measurement" of the generator. To keep $E$ steady when the load suddenly increases (e.g., a bow thruster starting), the **Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR)** senses the dip and increases the **DC excitation current** on the rotor, which boosts $\phi$ — restoring $E$ to its rated value within milliseconds. This is why ships require *very fast* AVRs compared to land systems: the loads change violently (cranes, winches, pumps cycling on/off).

---

## 2.4 Synchronous Speed & Slip (Motors on Board)

Ships use both **synchronous motors** (e.g., for large fixed-speed compressors) and **squirrel-cage induction motors** (for pumps, fans, winches). The synchronous speed is:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{P}$$

For a 60 Hz, 4-pole motor:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 60}{4} = 1800 \text{ RPM}$$

Induction motors always run slightly slower than $N_s$ due to slip:

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

$$N_r = N_s (1 - s)$$

Where $s$ is typically **0.02 to 0.05** (2–5%) under load.

---

## 2.5 KTU FORMULA CHEAT SHEET (MEMORISE THIS TABLE)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Marine Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | 3-φ active power | kW, MW |
| 2 | $S = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L$ | 3-φ apparent power | kVA, MVA |
| 3 | $Q = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin\phi$ | 3-φ reactive power | kVAr, MVAr |
| 4 | $E = 4.44\,f\,N\,\phi\,K_w$ | Generator EMF/phase | 254 V (for 440 V LL) |
| 5 | $N_s = 120f/P$ | Synchronous speed | 1800 RPM (60 Hz, 4-pole) |
| 6 | $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ | Slip of induction motor | 0.02–0.05 |
| 7 | $V_{drop} = I \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)$ | Cable voltage drop | ≤ 7% (per IEC 60092) |
| 8 | $I_{sc} = V / Z_{th}$ | Short-circuit current | 10–20× full load |
| 9 | $f_{res} = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$ | Resonant frequency | Critical for harmonic filters |
| 10 | $\eta = P_{out}/P_{in} \times 100\%$ | Generator/Motor efficiency | 95–98% (large M/G) |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU answer sheets, **always state units explicitly** after every numerical value. A bare "1800" is worth 0 marks; "1800 RPM" or "1800 revolutions per minute" is worth full marks.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of These Concepts in Marine Industry

The formulas above are not academic — they are the **daily working language** of marine electrical engineers. A few real applications:

1. **Generator Sizing:** To select a diesel generator rating, the engineer computes the **Maximum Demand (MD)** of all consumers plus a 10–15% diversity allowance, then adds a 20% safety margin. The result is the DG rating in kVA.
2. **Cable Sizing:** Voltage drop ($\leq 7\%$) and short-circuit withstand (I²t rating) are the two governing constraints when choosing cross-sectional area of marine power cables (e.g., **IEC 60092-350**).
3. **Power Factor Correction:** Shore-side regulations and onboard battery chargers can impose reactive power limits; engineers install **capacitor banks** at the main switchboard to maintain $\cos\phi \geq 0.85$ lagging.
4. **Synchronising:** When two DGs run in parallel, they must match in **voltage, frequency, and phase sequence** before the bus-tie breaker closes — the **synchroscope** measures all three.

# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Three-Phase Active Power Formula

We start from the fundamental definition of single-phase instantaneous power for a balanced sinusoidal system.

**Step 1 — Write the instantaneous phase voltages:**

$$v_a = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$

$$v_b = V_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$

$$v_c = V_m \sin(\omega t + 120°)$$

**Step 2 — Write the corresponding phase currents (lagging by angle $\phi$):**

$$i_a = I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

$$i_b = I_m \sin(\omega t - 120° - \phi)$$

$$i_c = I_m \sin(\omega t + 120° - \phi)$$

**Step 3 — Compute total instantaneous power as the sum of all three phase powers:**

$$p(t) = v_a i_a + v_b i_b + v_c i_c$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and expand using trigonometric identities.** Applying the identity $\sin A \sin B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$ and noting that the three $2\omega t$ terms are phase-shifted by 120° and **sum to zero**, we obtain:

$$p(t) = \frac{3}{2} V_m I_m \cos\phi = \text{constant}$$

**Step 5 — Convert peak values to RMS.** Since $V_m = \sqrt{2}\,V_{ph}$ and $I_m = \sqrt{2}\,I_{ph}$:

$$p(t) = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \sqrt{2}\,V_{ph} \cdot \sqrt{2}\,I_{ph} \cdot \cos\phi = 3 V_{ph} I_{ph} \cos\phi$$

**Step 6 — Convert phase quantities to line quantities.** For a star (Y) connected system: $V_L = \sqrt{3}\,V_{ph}$ and $I_L = I_{ph}$. Substituting:

$$\boxed{P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi}$$

This is the **most-tested formula** in KTU Marine Electrical papers for the "Basics" module.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Marine Switchboard Loading

**Problem Statement:**
A bulk carrier's auxiliary switchboard is supplied at **440 V, 3-phase, 60 Hz**. The connected load consists of:
- Steering gear motor: 75 kW, $\cos\phi = 0.85$ lagging
- Ballast pump motor: 110 kW, $\cos\phi = 0.88$ lagging
- HVAC compressors: 60 kW, $\cos\phi = 0.90$ lagging
- Galley + lighting: 40 kW, $\cos\phi = 0.95$ lagging

Assume all loads operate simultaneously (diversity factor = 1.0). Determine the **(a)** total active power, **(b)** total reactive power, **(c)** apparent power, **(d)** overall power factor, and **(e)** line current drawn from the switchboard.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a) — Total Active Power** (simple arithmetic sum since all are kW values):

$$P_{total} = 75 + 110 + 60 + 40 = 285 \text{ kW}$$

**Part (b) — Total Reactive Power.** We use $Q = P \tan\phi$ for each load:

$$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.85) = 31.79° \Rightarrow \tan\phi_1 = 0.6197$$

$$\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.88) = 28.36° \Rightarrow \tan\phi_2 = 0.5396$$

$$\phi_3 = \cos^{-1}(0.90) = 25.84° \Rightarrow \tan\phi_3 = 0.4843$$

$$\phi_4 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19° \Rightarrow \tan\phi_4 = 0.3287$$

Now compute each $Q$:

$$Q_1 = 75 \times 0.6197 = 46.48 \text{ kVAr}$$

$$Q_2 = 110 \times 0.5396 = 59.36 \text{ kVAr}$$

$$Q_3 = 60 \times 0.4843 = 29.06 \text{ kVAr}$$

$$Q_4 = 40 \times 0.3287 = 13.15 \text{ kVAr}$$

Summing:

$$Q_{total} = 46.48 + 59.36 + 29.06 + 13.15 = 148.05 \text{ kVAr}$$

**Part (c) — Apparent Power** using the Pythagorean identity:

$$S_{total} = \sqrt{P_{total}^2 + Q_{total}^2} = \sqrt{285^2 + 148.05^2}$$

$$S_{total} = \sqrt{81225 + 21918.8} = \sqrt{103143.8} = 321.16 \text{ kVA}$$

**Part (d) — Overall Power Factor:**

$$\cos\phi_{overall} = \frac{P_{total}}{S_{total}} = \frac{285}{321.16} = 0.8874 \text{ lagging}$$

**Part (e) — Line Current** using $I_L = S / (\sqrt{3}\,V_L)$:

$$I_L = \frac{321.16 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 440} = \frac{321160}{762.10} = 421.39 \text{ A}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Judgment:** The total apparent power of **321 kVA** would require the shipyard to install at least one **400 kVA** generator (next standard size above MD), or two 250 kVA generators operating in parallel. This is a typical aux-load for a medium-sized bulk carrier.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Synchronous Generator EMF Equation

Consider one phase of a three-phase synchronous generator armature winding rotating at $N$ RPM in a magnetic field with $\phi$ Webbers per pole.

**Step 1 — Time period of one revolution:**

$$T = \frac{60}{N} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2 — Time to traverse one pole-pair (one electrical cycle):**

For a $P$-pole machine, one revolution contains $P/2$ electrical cycles. So one cycle lasts:

$$T_{cycle} = \frac{60}{N} \times \frac{2}{P} = \frac{120}{N \cdot P} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency:**

$$f = \frac{1}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{N \cdot P}{120} \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Average EMF in one conductor cutting $\phi$ Webbers per half-cycle:**

$$E_{avg/conductor} = \frac{2 \phi}{T_{cycle}/2} = 4 f \phi$$

**Step 5 — For $N$ series turns per phase (2 conductors per turn):**

$$E_{avg/phase} = 4 f \phi N$$

**Step 6 — Apply the form factor (RMS/Average = 1.11 for sinusoid) and winding factor $K_w$:**

$$E_{rms/phase} = 1.11 \times 4 f \phi N \times K_w = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, N \, K_w$$

$$\boxed{E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi \, K_w}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Frequency and Voltage Across Common Ship Types

| Vessel Type | Propulsion Power | Bus Voltage | Frequency | Generators | Typical Configuration |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Small fishing boat | < 250 kW | 220 V | 50/60 Hz | 1 × 100 kVA | Single DG, no paralleling |
| Yacht / Patrol boat | 250 kW – 1 MW | 440 V | 60 Hz | 2 × 200 kVA | 2 DGs, 1 in standby |
| Bulk Carrier (Handysize) | 5–8 MW | 440 V | 60 Hz | 3 × 800 kVA | 3 DGs, 2 running + 1 standby |
| Container Ship (Post-Panamax) | 25–40 MW | 6.6 kV | 60 Hz | 4 × 4 MVA | HV switchboard, 3 running + 1 |
| LNG Carrier | 20–30 MW | 6.6 kV | 60 Hz | 4 × 3.5 MVA | Dual-fuel DFDE, sophisticated PMS |
| Cruise Liner | 40–80 MW | 11 kV | 60 Hz | 6 × 12 MVA | HV ring main, extensive redundancy |
| Aircraft Carrier (Naval) | 100+ MW | 13.8 kV | 60 Hz | 8+ × 20 MVA | Zonal electrical distribution |

# STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Top-Level Block Diagram — Shipboard Electrical Network

The figure below depicts the **hierarchical architecture** of a modern ship's electrical plant, from prime movers to end consumers. The arrows indicate power flow direction; the dashed arrows indicate control / monitoring signals.

```mermaid
graph TD
    M1[Diesel Engine DG1] -->|Mechanical Shaft| G1[Synchronous Generator 1 MVA]
    M2[Diesel Engine DG2] -->|Mechanical Shaft| G2[Synchronous Generator 2 MVA]
    M3[Diesel Engine DG3] -->|Mechanical Shaft| G3[Synchronous Generator 3 MVA]
    M4[Emergency Diesel] -->|Mechanical Shaft| GE[Emergency Generator 200 kVA]
    SH[Shaft Generator from Main Engine] -->|Variable Freq| RFC[Rotating Freq Converter] -->|60 Hz| G4[Synchronous Generator]

    G1 --> MSB
    G2 --> MSB
    G3 --> MSB
    G4 --> MSB
    MSB[Main Switchboard 6.6 kV / 440 V] --> TR1[Step-down Transformer 6.6/0.44 kV]
    TR1 --> ASB[Auxiliary Switchboard 440 V]
    ASB --> SB1[Section Board 1 Accommodation]
    ASB --> SB2[Section Board 2 Engine Room]
    ASB --> SB3[Section Board 3 Deck Machinery]
    ASB --> SB4[Section Board 4 Galley HVAC]
    MSB --> ESB[Emergency Switchboard]
    GE --> ESB
    ASB -->|Feed on blackout| ESB

    ESB --> EBAT[Emergency Batteries 24V / 110V DC]
    EBAT --> NAV[Navigation Radars GPS AIS]
    EBAT --> COM[Communication GMDSS]
    EBAT --> FIRE[Fire Detection Sprinkler Pumps]
    EBAT --> STER[Steering Gear Motor]
    EBAT --> LIFE[Life-saving Appliances]

    ASB --> PM1[Propulsion Motor M Drive]
    ASB --> BT1[Bow Thruster Motor]
    ASB --> CP1[Cargo Pump Motor]
    ASB --> WIN1[Winch Crane Motor]
    ASB --> CP2[Compressor Pump Motor]
    ASB --> FAN1[Ventilation Fan Motor]
    ASB --> GAL1[Galley Laundry Loads]

    MSB --> PMS[Power Management System SCADA]
    PMS -->|Control| M1
    PMS -->|Control| M2
    PMS -->|Control| M3
    PMS -->|Sync Breaker| MSB
    PMS -->|Alarm IAS| CR[Control Room Operator]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The diagram has three logical "rivers" of power. The **top river** is generation (M1→G1 etc.). The **middle river** is distribution (MSB → ASB → Section Boards). The **bottom river** is utilisation (motor + battery loads). The right-side "tap" is the emergency segregation, which is legally required to come alive within **45 seconds** of main bus failure.

---

## 4.2 Single-Line Diagram — Simplified Main Switchboard Topology

A single-line diagram (SLD) is the universal language of shipyard electrical design. Below is a simplified SLD of a typical bulk carrier.

```mermaid
graph LR
    DG1A[DG 1 800 kVA]:::gen -->|ACB1| BUS
    DG2A[DG 2 800 kVA]:::gen -->|ACB2| BUS
    DG3A[DG 3 800 kVA]:::gen -->|ACB3| BUS
    BUS{Bus Bar 440 V}:::bus
    BUS -->|BTB| BUSTIE
    BUSTIE{Bus-Tie Breaker}:::bus
    BUSTIE --> ESSB[Emergency Switchboard]
    DGEM[Emergency DG 250 kVA]:::gen -->|ACB-E| ESSB
    BUS -->|TR1| TRHV[HV Switchboard 6.6 kV]:::bus
    TRHV -->|T1| TRHV2[HV Bus]
    TRHV2 -->|PMM| PROPM[Propulsion Motor 4 MW]:::load
    BUS -->|F1| FEED1[Feed to Auxiliaries]
    FEED1 --> COMPRESSOR[Compressors Pumps Fans]:::load
    FEED1 --> NAVLIGHT[Navigation Lights]:::load
    FEED1 --> GALLEY[Galley HVAC Loads]:::load
    ESSB -->|B1| BATT[Battery Bank 24V DC]:::load
    ESSB -->|F2| STEER[Steering Gear]:::load
    ESSB -->|F3| FIREP[Fire Pump]:::load

    classDef gen fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bus fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef load fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

**Legend:**
- 🟡 **Yellow boxes** → Generators (sources of power)
- 🔵 **Blue boxes/bus bars** → Conductors carrying the main current
- 🟢 **Green boxes** → Consumer loads (sinks of power)
- The `ACB` labels stand for **Air Circuit Breaker** — the principal switching device.
- The `BTB` is the **Bus-Tie Breaker**, which can split the main bus into two halves for fault isolation.

---

## 4.3 Synchronising Sequence Flowchart

Before two generators can be paralleled, three conditions must be met: **same voltage, same frequency, same phase sequence** (and in-phase).

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DG 1 Running on Bus] --> B[DG 2 to be Paralleled]
    B --> C{Engineer adjusts DG2 speed via governor}
    C -->|Speed too high| D[Decrease fuel rack]
    C -->|Speed too low| E[Increase fuel rack]
    D --> F[Monitor Synchroscope]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Frequency match within ±0.1 Hz?}
    G -->|No| C
    G -->|Yes| H{Voltage match within ±5%?}
    H -->|No| I[Adjust AVR excitation]
    I --> F
    H -->|Yes| J{Phase angle approaching 0 deg?}
    J -->|No| F
    J -->|Yes| K[Wait for synchroscope pointer at 12 o'clock position]
    K --> L[Close ACB at exactly zero phase angle]
    L --> M[Both DGs now in parallel load sharing]
    M --> N[Governor droop ensures P share]
    M --> O[AVR droop ensures Q share]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Synchroscope reading" interpretation:** When the synchroscope pointer rotates *slowly clockwise* (≈ 3–5 RPM), DG2 is **slightly faster** than DG1 — this is the safe direction. If it rotates *anti-clockwise*, DG2 is slower and must NOT be connected. Closing the breaker with anti-clockwise rotation can cause catastrophic mechanical damage.

# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define "marine electrical system" and list the four sub-systems into which it is conventionally divided.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A marine electrical system is the integrated network of generating, distributing, controlling, and utilising electrical energy onboard a seagoing vessel, designed to comply with classification society rules (Lloyd's, DNV, ABS) and international conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL).

The four sub-systems are:
1. **Power Generation** — diesel generators, shaft generators, emergency generators.
2. **Power Distribution** — main switchboard, emergency switchboard, section boards, bus-tie breakers.
3. **Power Utilisation** — propulsion motors, aux motors, galley, HVAC, navigation.
4. **Control, Protection & Monitoring** — relays, AVR, governor, PMS, IAS.

*(1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the four items, 1 mark for a brief example.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Why is 60 Hz preferred over 50 Hz for shipboard electrical systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Reduced weight & size:** For the same kW rating, 60 Hz transformers and motors require **~17% fewer turns** and **smaller magnetic cores**, saving precious displacement tonnage. *(1 mark)*
2. **Standardisation:** Most classification societies (ABS, Lloyd's) historically standardised on 60 Hz, allowing global spare-parts interchangeability. *(1 mark)*
3. **Higher synchronous speed:** A 60 Hz 4-pole motor runs at 1800 RPM vs 1500 RPM for 50 Hz, giving more compact, higher-power machinery — useful for shipboard winches and pumps. *(1 mark)*

---
