### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the architecture of a modern ship's electrical system. Discuss the role of the main switchboard and emergency switchboard in maintaining continuity of supply. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A 3-phase, 440 V, 60 Hz marine auxiliary switchboard supplies the following loads simultaneously:
- 2 × 50 kW motors at $\cos\phi = 0.80$ lagging
- 1 × 75 kW motor at $\cos\phi = 0.85$ lagging
- 30 kW of lighting & heating at unity power factor

Calculate: **(i)** total active power, **(ii)** total reactive power, **(iii)** apparent power, **(iv)** overall power factor, and **(v)** line current. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL ANSWER for Q.A(a) — 7 marks

A modern ship's electrical system follows a **four-tier hierarchy**:

**Tier 1 — Generation:** Typically 3 or 4 diesel generator sets, each rated between 500 kVA and 5 MVA, operating at 440 V (or 6.6 kV for large vessels). They run at 60 Hz, and their outputs are routed to the **main switchboard (MSB)**. *(1 mark)*

**Tier 2 — Main Switchboard (MSB):** The MSB is the heart of the system. It receives power from all running generators via **air circuit breakers (ACBs)**. It contains bus bars, synchronising panel, voltmeters, ammeters, frequency meters, and **power management relays**. The bus-tie breaker (BTB) can split the bus into two halves. *(2 marks)*

**Tier 3 — Distribution:** The MSB feeds section boards via feeders. Section boards distribute power to localised consumers in the engine room, accommodation, deck, and galley. Step-down transformers provide 220 V and 110 V where required. *(1 mark)*

**Tier 4 — Emergency Switchboard (ESB):** As mandated by SOLAS Ch. II-1, an **independent emergency switchboard** must be supplied by an **emergency diesel generator** located outside the main engine room. It must be capable of supplying essential services (steering gear, fire pumps, navigation, communications) within **45 seconds** of main power failure. The ESB also has its own **battery bank** for the critical period between blackout and emergency DG start-up. *(2 marks)*

**Continuity of supply** is maintained by:
- Redundancy: n+1 DG configuration (any one can fail and supply continues).
- Automatic bus transfer (ABT) from MSB to ESB.
- Selective protection: only the faulty section is isolated.
- PMS: starts standby DG if running DG loading exceeds 80%. *(1 mark)*

*(Block diagram drawn per SECTION 4.1 — minimum 1 mark for correct diagram with arrows.)*

#### MODEL ANSWER for Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $V_L = 440$ V, $f = 60$ Hz, 3-φ.

**Step 1 — Total Active Power:** Simple sum of all kW ratings.

$$P_{total} = (2 \times 50) + 75 + 30 = 100 + 75 + 30 = 205 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Reactive Power of Each Load Group** using $Q = P \tan\phi$:

*Load 1 (motors at 0.80):* $\tan(\cos^{-1} 0.80) = 0.75$. $Q_1 = 100 \times 0.75 = 75$ kVAr.

*Load 2 (motor at 0.85):* $\tan(\cos^{-1} 0.85) = 0.6197$. $Q_2 = 75 \times 0.6197 = 46.48$ kVAr.

*Load 3 (lighting, unity PF):* $Q_3 = 30 \times 0 = 0$ kVAr.

**Step 3 — Total Reactive Power:**

$$Q_{total} = 75 + 46.48 + 0 = 121.48 \text{ kVAr}$$

**Step 4 — Apparent Power:**

$$S_{total} = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2} = \sqrt{205^2 + 121.48^2} = \sqrt{42025 + 14757.4} = \sqrt{56782.4} \approx 238.29 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 5 — Overall Power Factor:**

$$\cos\phi_{overall} = \frac{205}{238.29} = 0.8603 \text{ lagging}$$

**Step 6 — Line Current:**

$$I_L = \frac{S_{total}}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L} = \frac{238.29 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 440} = \frac{238290}{762.10} \approx 312.67 \text{ A}$$

**Final Answer:** $P = 205$ kW, $Q = 121.48$ kVAr, $S = 238.29$ kVA, $\cos\phi = 0.86$ lagging, $I_L = 312.67$ A.

*Marking split: 1 mark each for steps 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 + 1 mark for final answer with units.*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Derive the EMF equation of a synchronous generator. Explain the role of the Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR) in maintaining constant terminal voltage under varying load conditions. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 60 Hz synchronous generator has 200 turns per phase with a winding factor of 0.96. The flux per pole is 0.05 Wb. Calculate the induced EMF per phase. If the generator supplies a balanced 3-phase load of 1000 A at 0.85 PF lagging, calculate the total active power output. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL ANSWER for Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Derivation** — already shown in SECTION 3.3. Key steps to present:

1. Define cycle time $T = 120/(N \cdot P)$. *(1 mark)*
2. Average EMF in one conductor = $4 f \phi$. *(1 mark)*
3. For $N$ turns/phase with form factor 1.11 and winding factor $K_w$, $E = 4.44\,f\,N\,\phi\,K_w$. *(2 marks)*
4. Final boxed equation. *(1 mark)*

**Role of AVR (2 marks):**
- The AVR senses the terminal voltage $V_t$ and compares it with a reference.
- On a sudden load increase, $V_t$ drops → AVR increases **field excitation current** $I_f$ on the rotor → flux $\phi$ rises → $E$ rises → $V_t$ is restored within **milliseconds**.
- Conversely, on load drop, $V_t$ rises → AVR reduces $I_f$.
- The AVR is critical on ships because **bow thrusters, winches, and cranes** cause violent, frequent load fluctuations that would otherwise cause lamp flicker and motor stalling.

#### MODEL ANSWER for Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $P = 6$ poles, $f = 60$ Hz, $N = 200$ turns/phase, $K_w = 0.96$, $\phi = 0.05$ Wb, $I_L = 1000$ A, $\cos\phi = 0.85$.

**Step 1 — EMF per Phase** using the derived equation:

$$E = 4.44 \times 60 \times 200 \times 0.05 \times 0.96$$

$$E = 4.44 \times 60 \times 200 \times 0.048$$

$$E = 4.44 \times 60 \times 9.6 = 4.44 \times 576 = 2557.4 \text{ V per phase}$$

**Step 2 — Synchronous Speed** (used to identify the machine type):

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 60}{6} = 1200 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 3 — Total Active Power Output:**

$$P = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \times \cos\phi$$

Assume $V_L \approx E_{line}$. For star connection, $V_L = \sqrt{3} \times V_{ph} = \sqrt{3} \times 2557.4 = 4429.5$ V.

$$P = \sqrt{3} \times 4429.5 \times 1000 \times 0.85$$

$$P = 1.732 \times 4429.5 \times 1000 \times 0.85$$

$$P = 6.524 \times 10^6 \text{ W} \approx 6524 \text{ kW} = 6.524 \text{ MW}$$

**Final Answer:** $E_{ph} = 2557.4$ V, $N_s = 1200$ RPM, $P = 6.524$ MW.

*Marking: 2 marks EMF, 1 mark speed, 3 marks power, 1 mark final boxed answer.*

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNINGS & PITFALLS

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #1 — Confusing $V_L$ and $V_{ph}$:** In India, students often use 415 V as line voltage. On ships it is **440 V line**, with phase voltage $V_{ph} = 440/\sqrt{3} = 254$ V. If you mistakenly use 440 V as $V_{ph}$ in the formula $P = 3 V_{ph} I_{ph} \cos\phi$, your answer will be **√3 times too large** and you'll lose 3–4 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #2 — Forgetting the form factor 1.11 / 4.44:** The constant 4.44 in $E = 4.44 f N \phi K_w$ comes from $4 \times 1.11$. Writing $E = 4 f N \phi K_w$ (missing 1.11) will yield an EMF that is 11% too low. KTU examiners specifically test this constant.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #3 — Not specifying "lagging" or "leading" for PF:** A power factor of 0.85 is meaningless without the qualifier. The phrase "0.85 lagging" or "0.85 lead" is mandatory.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #4 — Ignoring units in intermediate steps:** A numerical answer like "1800" without "RPM" is incomplete. State units at every calculation step.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #5 — Skipping the block diagram:** A "Basics" question on marine electrical architecture is worth at least 1–2 marks for the block diagram alone. Omitting it is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ A **marine electrical system** is an isolated micro-grid consisting of generation, distribution, utilisation, and protection sub-systems.
- ✅ Standard shipboard voltage: **440 V (3-φ)**, frequency **60 Hz**; large vessels use **6.6 kV** or **11 kV**.
- ✅ Battery/control voltages: **24 V, 110 V, 220 V DC**.
- ✅ Three-phase active power: $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$.
- ✅ Apparent power: $S = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L$; Reactive power: $Q = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin\phi$.
- ✅ Power triangle: $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$.
- ✅ Synchronous generator EMF: $E = 4.44\,f\,N\,\phi\,K_w$.
- ✅ Synchronous speed: $N_s = 120 f / P$.
- ✅ Induction motor slip: $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$, typically 0.02–0.05.
- ✅ SOLAS mandates **45-second** emergency power activation.
- ✅ Classification societies (Lloyd's, DNV, ABS) govern all marine electrical design rules.
- ✅ The **AVR** adjusts field excitation to keep $V_t$ constant; the **governor** adjusts fuel to keep $f$ constant.
- ✅ Synchronisation requires matching $V$, $f$, and phase sequence before closing the ACB.
- ✅ Standard bus arrangement: **MSB → ASB → Section Boards → Consumers**, with **ESB** as independent emergency supply.
- ✅ Redundancy rule: at least one **standby generator** (n+1) for any running set.
- ✅ Voltage drop in feeders limited to **7%** per IEC 60092-352.
- ✅ Standard ship power factors: **0.8 lagging** typical; PF corrected to **≥ 0.85** via capacitor banks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manual Synchronising
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Manual Synchronising

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Manual Synchronising** is the deliberate, operator-controlled procedure of matching the electrical parameters of an incoming (off-board) generator to those of a live busbar (or already-running generator) prior to closing the generator circuit breaker, thereby enabling safe parallel operation of AC alternators on board a ship.

In marine power plants, when a second diesel generator is required to share the electrical load with a running generator, the two cannot simply be electrically tied together. Any mismatch in their instantaneous voltage, frequency, or phase angle will result in large circulating currents, mechanical shock to the prime-mover shaft, and potentially catastrophic damage to the switchboard, busbars, and generator windings. **Manual synchronising** is the human-in-the-loop method used to bring these parameters into alignment before paralleling.

The four pre-conditions that **must** be satisfied before the breaker is closed are:

1. **Phase sequence** – must be identical (checked once, after installation/maintenance).
2. **Voltage magnitude** – RMS terminal voltage of the incoming machine must equal the busbar voltage.
3. **Frequency** – frequency of the incoming machine must be close to (and traditionally slightly *higher* than) the busbar frequency.
4. **Phase angle** – instantaneous phase displacement between the two voltages must be reduced to **zero** at the precise instant of breaker closing.

> [!NOTE]
> On ships governed by classification societies (IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS), synchronising must be either automatic, or — if manual — supervised by a competent engineer using a **synchroscope** and **double-frequency meters**. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats the manual method as the foundational concept upon which auto-synchronising is built.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two people pushing a heavy playground merry-go-round. Person A is already pushing it at a steady rhythm — three seconds per revolution. Person B wants to join in and help push. If Person B simply grabs the edge at the wrong moment, the sudden opposing force could wrench their arms or stall the wheel. The correct approach is for Person B to:

- Match the **speed** of the wheel (frequency).
- Match the **push direction** (phase sequence).
- Time their grab to the **exact instant** the wheel is in the right angular position (phase angle).

Manual synchronising is exactly this — but with electromagnetic forces, where the "wrench" of a mis-timed connection can deliver **multi-MW transient currents** in milliseconds.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Standard marine busbar frequency: **50 Hz** (most of Asia/Europe) or **60 Hz** (US-flag vessels, some LNG carriers).
- Allowable voltage mismatch at synchronising: typically **± 5 %** of nominal.
- Allowable frequency mismatch (slip): **0.1 – 0.5 Hz** (lower for large generators).
- Phase angle window for breaker closure: **± 10° electrical** is the absolute limit; **0°** is the ideal.
- The "**dark lamp**" or "**bright lamp**" methods require a beat frequency of **< 1 Hz** for human judgment to be reliable.

> [!NOTE]
> The standard tolerance figures above (5 %, 0.5 Hz, ± 10°) are widely used in marine-class practice; KTU examiners frequently test these numerical values because they are syllabus-mandated.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pre-Conditions in Detail

### 2.1.1 Phase Sequence Check
- The three-phase rotation order (R-Y-B) of the incoming generator must match that of the busbar.
- Performed using a **phase-sequence indicator** or by briefly running the incoming machine onto a test bus and checking with lamps/motors.
- Done **once after installation** or after any work on the main switchboard. Re-checked if there is reason to suspect a wiring change.

### 2.1.2 Voltage Matching
- The terminal voltage of the incoming machine is controlled by its **Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR)** set-point (or the operator's field rheostat in older vessels).
- Indicated on a **double voltmeter** (one pointer shows busbar volts, the other shows incoming machine volts).
- Allowed deviation: **± 5 %** of rated voltage.

### 2.1.3 Frequency Matching
- The speed of the prime-mover (diesel engine) is adjusted via the **governor** until the incoming machine's frequency is slightly *higher* than the busbar.
- Why higher? When the breaker closes, the incoming machine will momentarily take a share of the load; running it marginally fast ensures it picks up **kW** (real power) rather than acting as a motor and dragging the busbar.
- Indicated on a **double-frequency meter** (or digital readout).

### 2.1.4 Phase Angle Coincidence
- The most critical and time-sensitive condition.
- Detected with a **synchroscope** — a moving-coil instrument whose pointer rotates at a speed equal to the **slip frequency** (difference between the two frequencies).
- The breaker is closed when the pointer is at the **12 o'clock position** (i.e., phase angle = **0°**).

## 2.2 The Synchroscope — Operating Principle

The synchroscope is essentially a split-phase induction motor with two stator windings fed by:
- One winding from the busbar voltage (reference phase).
- The other winding from the incoming machine voltage.

The torque produced is proportional to:

$$T \propto V_{bus} \cdot V_{incoming} \cdot \sin(\delta)$$

where $\delta$ is the instantaneous phase difference. Because the windings are in quadrature, the pointer deflection visually represents the phase angle, and its rotation rate represents the **slip frequency** $f_{slip}$:

$$f_{slip} = f_{incoming} - f_{bus}$$

> [!NOTE]
> If $f_{slip} = 0$ (i.e., both frequencies equal), the pointer parks at the angle corresponding to the actual phase displacement. If frequencies differ, the pointer rotates continuously. **Slow rotation in the "fast" direction** indicates the incoming machine is gaining on the busbar — the ideal condition for closing.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Typical Unit | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slip Frequency | $f_{slip}$ | $f_{in} - f_{bus}$ | Hz | Drives the synchroscope pointer; must be small and positive. |
| 2 | Phase Angle | $\delta$ | $\int 2\pi f_{slip}\, dt + \delta_0$ | rad (or °) | Must approach 0° at the instant of breaker close. |
| 3 | Synchroscope Torque | $T$ | $\propto V_{bus} V_{in} \sin(\delta)$ | N·m | Pointer-deflecting force; vanishes at $\delta = 0$. |
| 4 | Allowable Voltage Mismatch | $\Delta V$ | $\le 0.05\, V_{rated}$ | V (or %) | Prevents circulating reactive current. |
| 5 | Allowable Frequency Mismatch | $\Delta f$ | $\le 0.5\, Hz$ | Hz | Prevents transient torque shocks. |
| 6 | Phase Angle Window | $\delta_{close}$ | $\le 10^{\circ}$ electrical | degrees | Hard limit for breaker closure. |
| 7 | Beat Period | $T_{beat}$ | $1 \div f_{slip}$ | s | Lamp pulsation period; operator times the close. |
| 8 | RMS Line Voltage (Ship Std.) | $V_L$ | **440 V** (typical LV) or **6.6 kV** (HV) | V | Standard marine distribution levels. |
| 9 | Rated Frequency (Ship Std.) | $f$ | **50 Hz** or **60 Hz** | Hz | Class-flag dependent. |
| 10 | Synchronising Power Coeff. | $K_{sync}$ | $\dfrac{E \cdot V}{X_s}$ (per unit) | p.u. | Quantifies synchronising torque per radian. |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Manual synchronising remains a **mandatory backup** even on fully automated vessels, because:
- Classification rules require operators to demonstrate manual competence during sea trials.
- Auto-sync failures (e.g., synchroscope or controller fault) must be recoverable manually.
- During maintenance, the auto-sync is often locked out.

In offshore platforms and naval vessels, manual synchronising is the **primary** method on certain redundant switchboards.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Synchronising Torque Equation

Consider two equivalent EMFs — the busbar voltage $V$ (reference) and the internal EMF of the incoming generator $E$, separated by a phase angle $\delta$. The two are connected through the synchronous reactance $X_s$ of the incoming machine when the breaker closes.

By the standard two-machine power-transfer formulation, the synchronising power exchanged is:

$$P_{sync} = \frac{E \cdot V}{X_s} \sin(\delta)$$

For small $\delta$ (i.e., near synchronism, where manual closing occurs), $\sin(\delta) \approx \delta$ (radians), so:

$$P_{sync} \approx \frac{E \cdot V}{X_s} \cdot \delta$$

The **synchronising torque coefficient** is therefore:

$$K_{sync} = \frac{dP_{sync}}{d\delta} = \frac{E \cdot V}{X_s} \cos(\delta) \;\;\approx\;\; \frac{E \cdot V}{X_s} \;\; \text{at } \delta = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivative is exactly what makes a small residual phase-angle mismatch tolerable: a positive $K_{sync}$ produces a restoring torque that pulls the rotor back into step. The larger $K_{sync}$, the more "stiff" the synchronism.

## 3.2 Derivation: Synchroscope Pointer Motion

Let the two applied voltages be:

$$v_{bus}(t) = V_m \sin(\omega_{bus} t)$$

$$v_{in}(t) = E_m \sin(\omega_{in} t + \delta_0)$$

The instantaneous phase difference is:

$$\delta(t) = (\omega_{in} - \omega_{bus}) t + \delta_0 = 2\pi f_{slip}\, t + \delta_0$$

Differentiating once with respect to time:

$$\frac{d\delta}{dt} = 2\pi f_{slip}$$

This confirms the pointer rotates at an angular velocity equal to $2\pi f_{slip}$ rad/s — i.e., the **slip frequency** directly controls the speed at which the synchroscope pointer sweeps the dial. This is why a slow, steady rotation in the "fast" direction is the operator's cue to close the breaker.

## 3.3 Derivation: Beat-Period and Operator Reaction Time

The time for the pointer (or synchronising lamp brightness) to complete one full cycle is the **beat period**:

$$T_{beat} = \frac{1}{f_{slip}}$$

If $f_{slip} = 0.2 \text{ Hz}$, then:

$$T_{beat} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5 \text{ s}$$

The pointer sweeps a full 360° in 5 seconds. Assuming a typical breaker closing time of $t_{cb} \approx 0.1 \text{ s}$, the angular advance the operator must lead by is:

$$\theta_{lead} = 2\pi f_{slip} \cdot t_{cb} = 2\pi (0.2)(0.1) = 0.1257 \text{ rad} \approx 7.2^{\circ}$$

Thus the operator must close the breaker when the pointer is **slightly before 12 o'clock** — about 7° early — to allow for the breaker mechanism's make-time. This is one of the most commonly examined **practical calculation** items.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A shipboard alternator is being manually synchronised to a 440 V, 50 Hz busbar. The incoming machine is running at 50.2 Hz, the breaker closing time is 100 ms, and the synchroscope pointer rotates in the "fast" direction. Calculate:
> (a) The slip frequency.
> (b) The lead angle the operator should aim for.
> (c) The corresponding beat period, and comment on operator feasibility.

### Solution:

**(a) Slip frequency**

$$f_{slip} = f_{in} - f_{bus} = 50.2 - 50.0 = 0.2 \text{ Hz}$$

**(b) Lead angle**

$$\theta_{lead} = 2\pi f_{slip}\, t_{cb} = 2\pi (0.2)(0.1)$$

$$= 0.4 \pi = 1.2566 \text{ rad}$$

Converting to degrees:

$$\theta_{lead} = 1.2566 \times \frac{180^{\circ}}{\pi} = 7.2^{\circ}$$

**(c) Beat period and operator feasibility**

$$T_{beat} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5 \text{ s}$$

A 5-second beat period is **comfortable** for manual closing. The pointer sweeps at 360°/5s = 72°/s, and the operator must initiate the close at 7.2° before 12 o'clock — i.e., roughly **0.1 second** before the pointer reaches vertical. This is well within human reaction time, so manual synchronising is feasible. ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU exam tip: If the calculated beat period is **less than ~2 seconds**, the manual method becomes unreliable and the **auto-synchroniser** should be used. Always include this qualitative comment in your answer to earn full marks.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Synchronising Indication Methods

| Method | Indicator | Reading | Best Use | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Synchroscope (analogue) | Rotating pointer | Phase angle directly | Primary method on most ships | Mechanical wear; needs slip to be visible. |
| Double voltmeter | Two needles | Voltage equality check | Pre-check before phase match | Cannot show phase angle. |
| Double frequency meter | Two needles | Slip frequency check | Pre-check before phase match | Cannot show phase angle. |
| Dark-lamp method | Three lamps go dark together | All three lamps dark at $\delta = 0$ | Older vessels, training | Slow beat, lamps dim slowly, ambiguity near 0°. |
| Bright-lamp method | Lamps peak at 12 o'clock | All three lamps bright at $\delta = 0$ | Less common | Less intuitive. |
| Digital synchroscope | Numeric / LED ring | Numerical δ or LED arc | Modern retrofit | Requires healthy power supply. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Manual Synchronising Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Diesel Prime Mover 1] --> A2[Alternator 1 Running]
    A2 --> A3[Busbar LIVE 440V 50Hz]
    A3 --> A4[Main Switchboard]

    B1[Diesel Prime Mover 2] --> B2[Alternator 2 OFF-BUS]
    B2 --> B3[AVR Set Point]
    B2 --> B4[Governor Speed Set Point]
    B2 --> B5[Synchroscope Terminals]

    A3 --> C1[Double Voltmeter]
    B2 --> C1
    A3 --> C2[Double Frequency Meter]
    B2 --> C2
    A3 --> C5[Synchroscope Reference]
    B2 --> C5

    C1 --> D1[Operator Observes Voltage Match]
    C2 --> D2[Operator Observes Slip Frequency]
    C5 --> D3[Operator Observes Phase Angle]

    D1 --> E1{Voltage OK}
    D2 --> E2{Frequency OK}
    D3 --> E3{Phase Angle at 12 o'clock}

    E1 -->|Adjust AVR| F1[Match Voltage]
    E2 -->|Adjust Governor| F2[Match Frequency]
    E3 -->|Wait for correct instant| F3[Initiate Breaker Close]

    F1 --> G1[All Conditions Satisfied]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1

    G1 --> H1[Close ACB / VCB]
    H1 --> I1[Alternator 2 ON-BUS]
    I1 --> J1[Adjust kW and kVAr Sharing]
    J1 --> K1[Stable Parallel Operation]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Manual Synchronising Step Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Phase Sequence Check] --> P2[Step 2: Start Prime Mover]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Build up Voltage via AVR]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Adjust Speed via Governor]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Observe Double Voltmeter]
    P5 --> P6{Voltages Equal?}
    P6 -->|No| P3
    P6 -->|Yes| P7[Step 6: Observe Double Hz Meter]
    P7 --> P8{Slip 0.1 to 0.5 Hz?}
    P8 -->|No| P4
    P8 -->|Yes| P9[Step 7: Observe Synchroscope]
    P9 --> P10{Pointer Slow and FAST?}
    P10 -->|No| P4
    P10 -->|Yes| P11[Step 8: Anticipate 12 o'clock]
    P11 --> P12[Step 9: Close Circuit Breaker]
    P12 --> P13[Step 10: Adjust Load Share]
    P13 --> P14[Stable Parallel Operation]
```

## 4.3 Equipment Layout — Switchboard Panel (Textual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Panel1[Manual Synchronising Panel]
        Q1[Double Voltmeter]
        Q2[Double Frequency Meter]
        Q3[Synchroscope]
        Q4[Selector Switch BUS / GEN / OFF]
        Q5[Breaker Control Switch]
        Q6[AVR Raise / Lower]
        Q7[Governor Raise / Lower]
    end
    Panel1 --- R1[Incoming Generator CB]
    Panel1 --- R2[Busbar Coupler]
    Panel1 --- R3[Indicator Lamps]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The selector switch (Q4) is critical: it routes the *busbar* voltage to one channel of each instrument and the *incoming generator* voltage to the other. Without this switch, the readings are meaningless. A common KTU pitfall is forgetting to mention this switch in a descriptive answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2023, Model Question Bank]
**List the four conditions that must be satisfied before manually closing the generator circuit breaker for paralleling with the busbar.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four conditions are:
1. **Phase sequence** must be identical (R-Y-B rotation matches the busbar).
2. **Voltage magnitude** of the incoming generator must be equal to the busbar voltage (within ± 5 %).
3. **Frequency** of the incoming generator must be close to — and slightly higher than — the busbar frequency (slip ≈ 0.1–0.5 Hz).
4. **Phase angle** between the two voltages must be zero at the instant of breaker closing, indicated by the synchroscope pointer at 12 o'clock.

*(Allocate 1 mark per condition; 1 mark for completeness of presentation.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Question Bank]
**Why is it preferred to keep the incoming generator running at a frequency slightly higher than the busbar before synchronising?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
When the incoming machine is running at a slightly higher frequency than the busbar, it operates marginally fast. At the instant of breaker closure, this ensures the incoming generator **delivers real power (kW)** to the busbar rather than absorbing it. If it were running slow, it would act as a synchronously-driven motor, drawing power from the busbar and potentially causing a reverse-power trip and a shaft-loading shock on its own prime mover. The "fast" condition guarantees that synchronising power flow is outward from the new machine, easing the load on the already-running set. (3 marks: 1 for direction of power, 1 for reverse-power risk, 1 for prime-mover safety.)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model]

**(a) With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a synchroscope used for manual synchronising of shipboard alternators. (7 marks)**

**(b) Two alternators are to be paralleled manually. The busbar frequency is 50 Hz, the incoming machine is running at 50.25 Hz, and the breaker closing time is 80 ms. Calculate the lead angle at which the operator should initiate the close, and comment on the operator's ability to perform this synchronising manually. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a) [7 marks]

A synchroscope is a single-phase, split-phase induction-type instrument installed on the main switchboard. It consists of:

1. **Two stator windings** mounted in quadrature (at 90° electrical to each other):
   - One winding is energised from the **busbar** voltage (reference).
   - The other is energised from the **incoming generator** voltage, but fed through a **phase-shifting network** (typically a resistance–capacitance or resistance–inductance network) to introduce a 90° shift so that the two fluxes are in time-quadrature.

2. **A rotor** of the salient-pole or drag-cup type, carrying a pointer that is free to rotate through 360°.

3. **A dial** marked with the words **"FAST"** on one side and **"SLOW"** on the other, with the **12 o'clock** position indicating phase coincidence ($\delta = 0°$).

**Working principle:**
- A rotating magnetic field is produced whose angular position depends on the instantaneous phase difference $\delta$ between busbar and incoming voltages.
- The rotor aligns itself with this field, and hence the pointer deflection is a direct indication of $\delta$.
- When the two frequencies are equal, the pointer rests at the actual phase angle. When the frequencies differ, the pointer rotates continuously — **clockwise** if the incoming machine is *slow* (frequency lower) and **anticlockwise** if it is *fast* (frequency higher).
- The speed of rotation equals the **slip frequency** $f_{slip} = f_{in} - f_{bus}$.

The operator watches the pointer, adjusts the governor until the pointer rotates slowly in the "fast" direction, and then closes the breaker as the pointer crosses 12 o'clock (with a small lead to compensate for breaker make-time). *(7 marks: 1 for construction, 2 for two windings, 2 for working principle, 1 for operator interpretation, 1 for diagram/sketch.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $f_{bus} = 50 \text{ Hz}$
- $f_{in} = 50.25 \text{ Hz}$
- Breaker closing time $t_{cb} = 80 \text{ ms} = 0.08 \text{ s}$

**Step 1 — Slip frequency**

$$f_{slip} = f_{in} - f_{bus} = 50.25 - 50.00 = 0.25 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Angular velocity of pointer**

$$\omega_{slip} = 2\pi f_{slip} = 2\pi (0.25) = 1.5708 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Lead angle (in radians)**

$$\theta_{lead} = \omega_{slip} \times t_{cb} = 1.5708 \times 0.08 = 0.1257 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to degrees**

$$\theta_{lead} = 0.1257 \times \frac{180^{\circ}}{\pi} = 7.2^{\circ}$$

**Step 5 — Beat period (operator-feasibility check)**

$$T_{beat} = \frac{1}{f_{slip}} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6 — Comment:**
The beat period of 4 s is comfortable. The pointer sweeps 360°/4s = 90°/s. The operator must initiate the close at 7.2° before 12 o'clock, which corresponds to a lead time of $7.2°/90°\text{per s} = 0.08$ s before vertical. This is achievable with practice, so **manual synchronising is feasible** for this set of conditions. If the slip had been greater than ~1 Hz, auto-synchronising would be recommended. *(7 marks: 1 for given data, 1 for slip, 1 for angular velocity, 1 for lead angle, 1 for beat period, 1 for conversion, 1 for feasibility comment.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Model]

**(a) Describe the lamp methods (dark-lamp and bright-lamp methods) of synchronising. State their merits and demerits. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain with a block diagram the manual synchronising procedure on a ship's main switchboard, including the role of the AVR and the governor. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a) [7 marks]

**Dark-Lamp Method:**
- Three identical lamps are connected between corresponding phases of the incoming generator and the busbar (U1–U2, V1–V2, W1–W2).
- If the phase sequence is correct, all three lamps **darken and brighten together**, and they are all dark at the instant of phase coincidence ($\delta = 0°$).
- The rate of pulsation equals the slip frequency.
- Operator closes the breaker when all three lamps are dark.

**Bright-Lamp Method:**
- The lamps are cross-connected (U1–V2, V1–W2, W1–U2) so that they are **all bright** at $\delta = 0°$ and dark at $\delta = 180°$.
- Less commonly used because the brightest instant is harder to judge visually than the darkest.

**Merits of Lamp Methods:**
- Simple, no moving parts, no power supply needed.
- Effective for **training and emergency use**.

**Demerits of Lamp Methods:**
- Lamps dim slowly near $\delta = 0°$ when slip is small, making precise judgement difficult.
- Phase sequence error is **not clearly distinguished** from phase angle error in the dark-lamp method unless the lamps are watched over a full beat cycle.
- Cannot indicate which machine is fast or slow.
- For these reasons, **synchroscopes have replaced lamps on modern ships**. *(7 marks: 2 each for the two methods, 1.5 for merits, 1.5 for demerits.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b) [7 marks]

The manual synchronising procedure on a ship's main switchboard is executed as follows (refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1):

1. **Pre-check (once per maintenance interval):** Phase-sequence indicator is used to confirm that the incoming alternator's R-Y-B rotation matches the busbar. The phase-sequence selector switch on the synchronising panel is set to the **GEN** position so that the synchroscope, double voltmeter, and double frequency meter each display one busbar reading and one incoming-generator reading.

2. **Starting:** The diesel prime mover of the incoming set is started. The governor brings the engine to near-rated speed; the AVR is energised and builds up the terminal voltage.

3. **Voltage matching:** The operator observes the double voltmeter. If the incoming voltage is low, the **AVR set-point (or field rheostat)** is raised until both needles coincide (± 5 % tolerance).

4. **Frequency matching:** The operator adjusts the **governor speed set-point** until the incoming machine's frequency is slightly higher than the busbar (slip ≈ 0.1–0.5 Hz). The double frequency meter confirms this.

5. **Phase-angle monitoring:** The synchroscope pointer is observed. The operator fine-tunes the governor until the pointer rotates **slowly and continuously in the "FAST" direction**.

6. **Breaker closure:** When the pointer approaches 12 o'clock, the operator **anticipates the lead angle** (≈ $2\pi f_{slip} t_{cb}$) and closes the **ACB / VCB** control switch.

7. **Post-closure load sharing:** Once paralleled, the operator adjusts the **governor** to shift the real-power (kW) share and the **AVR** to shift the reactive-power (kVAr) share between the running and the newly-connected machine, reaching the desired operating point on the load-sharing curve.

**Role of AVR:** Controls terminal voltage by adjusting field excitation; determines **kVAr** (reactive) share.
**Role of Governor:** Controls prime-mover fuel input; determines engine speed and hence generator **frequency** and **kW** (real) share. *(7 marks: 1 per step × 6 steps, 1 for AVR/governor role summary.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the "FAST" preference** for the incoming frequency. Many students write "match the frequencies exactly" — this is wrong; running slightly *fast* is mandatory to ensure outward real-power flow.
> 2. **Confusing phase sequence with phase angle.** Phase sequence is a *spatial* property (R-Y-B rotation); phase angle is the *instantaneous* angular difference between two waveforms. Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are muddled.
> 3. **Skipping the lead-angle calculation** in numerical problems. The breaker has a finite make-time (~50–150 ms), and the operator must compensate; not including $t_{cb}$ loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Omitting the feasibility comment.** After every synchronising calculation, KTU examiners expect a one-line comment such as "manual synchronising is feasible because $T_{beat}$ > 2 s."
> 5. **Not mentioning the synchronising panel selector switch** in descriptive answers. This is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Mixing up the dark-lamp and bright-lamp connections.** Cross-connected = bright at 0°; straight-connected = dark at 0°.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Manual synchronising** = operator-driven paralleling of an AC alternator to a live busbar.
- **Four pre-conditions:** (i) phase sequence, (ii) voltage, (iii) frequency, (iv) phase angle.
- **Sequence of operations:** Start prime mover → build up voltage via AVR → adjust frequency via governor → monitor synchroscope → close breaker at 12 o'clock → adjust kW/kVAr share.
- **Incoming frequency must be slightly HIGHER** than busbar to ensure outward real-power flow on closure.
- **Tolerance window:** voltage ± 5 %; frequency slip 0.1–0.5 Hz; phase angle ≤ 10°.
- **Synchroscope pointer rotates at slip frequency**; slow rotation in the "FAST" direction is the green light to close.
- **Lead angle formula:** $\theta_{lead} = 2\pi f_{slip} t_{cb}$ (radians) — always include breaker closing time.
- **Beat period** $T_{beat} = 1/f_{slip}$; must exceed ~2 s for manual feasibility.
- **Dark-lamp method:** lamps in phase across U-U, V-V, W-W; all dark at 12 o'clock.
- **Bright-lamp method:** lamps cross-connected; all bright at 12 o'clock.
- **Synchroscope is preferred** over lamps on modern ships due to its direct phase-angle reading and "fast/slow" indication.
- **AVR controls kVAr (reactive) share**; **governor controls kW (real) share** after paralleling.
- **Manual synchronising is the mandatory backup** to auto-synchronisers on classed vessels and is tested during sea trials.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Auto-Synchronising
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Auto-Synchronising

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Auto-Synchronising (Automatic Synchronisation)** is an automated closed-loop control process in marine electrical power plants whereby an incoming generator is independently matched to the live main busbar in three precise electrical parameters — **voltage magnitude, frequency, and phase angle (phase sequence)** — through a dedicated **Auto-Synchroniser (Auto-Sync) unit**, followed by a precisely timed **circuit-breaker (CB) close command**, thereby enabling safe paralleling of the alternator with the ship's main switchboard without manual operator intervention.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PENST524) context, auto-synchronising is treated as a **mission-critical shipboard automation function** classified under **Classification Society** requirements (e.g., **Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, IRS**) and **IEC 60092 / IEC 61850** series standards. The function is mandatory for UMS (Unmanned Machinery Space) certified vessels under **SOLAS** and **MARPOL** regulations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "Two Clocks That Must Tick in Unison":**
> Imagine two grandfather clocks standing side by side. One (the busbar) is the master clock already ticking accurately. The second (the incoming generator) is slightly fast, slow, and may be leaning a little. Before you tie their pendulums together (parallel them), you must:
> 1. **Match the loudness of the ticks** (Voltage magnitude match),
> 2. **Match the ticking speed** (Frequency match),
> 3. **Wait until both pendulums swing to the exact same extreme at the same instant** (Phase angle match = 0°).
>
> Doing this *by ear* is manual synchronising. Letting a robotic sensor-and-motor system do it for you in milliseconds is **Auto-Synchronising**. On a ship, a wrong "tie" (out-of-sync closure) can shatter the entire electrical system — equivalent to two clock pendulums colliding and breaking the gears.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Classification Limits

| Parameter | Acceptable Tolerance (Class Rule) |
|---|---|
| **Voltage Difference (ΔV)** | ≤ **±5 %** of busbar voltage |
| **Frequency Difference (Δf)** | ≤ **±0.1 Hz** to **±0.5 Hz** (typically ±0.2 Hz) |
| **Phase Angle Difference (Δφ)** | ≤ **±10° electrical** (optimum ≈ **0°**) |
| **Circuit Breaker Closing Time (Lead Angle Compensation)** | Pre-computed from CB make-time |
| **Synchronising Time** | Typically **< 30 seconds** to **120 seconds** for auto-sync |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PENST524 Module 4):** Auto-synchronising is part of the broader topic *"Shipboard Power Management and Automation"*. It directly ties to the shipboard **Power Management System (PMS)**, **Alarm Monitoring System (AMS)**, and **Engine Room Control Room (ECR)** supervisory functions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Mandatory Conditions of Synchronisation

For any synchronous machine to be paralleled safely with a live busbar, **three conditions** — known collectively as the **"Three Conditions of Synchronism"** — must be satisfied simultaneously.

1. **Voltage Match (ΔV ≈ 0)**
   - The RMS terminal voltage of the incoming generator $V_g$ must equal the busbar voltage $V_b$.
   - Achieved by **automatic voltage regulator (AVR)** adjusting the **field excitation current** $I_f$ of the synchronous generator.

2. **Frequency Match (Δf ≈ 0)**
   - The electrical frequency of the incoming generator $f_g$ must approach the busbar frequency $f_b$.
   - Achieved by a **governor / speed regulator** adjusting the **prime-mover fuel/steam admission** to bring rotor speed $\omega_r$ within the slip window.

3. **Phase Sequence & Phase Angle Match (Δφ ≈ 0°)**
   - The **phase rotation** must be identical (R-Y-B or U-V-W) — verified **once** during commissioning, not on every sync.
   - The instantaneous phase angle between generator and busbar voltages must approach **zero** at the instant of breaker closure.

### 2.2 The Lead-Angle Compensation Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Circuit breakers are **not instantaneous**. They require a finite **closing time** $t_{CB}$ (typically **40 ms to 120 ms** for marine ACBs / VCBs). The auto-synchroniser must therefore **fire the close command *before*** exact phase coincidence, at a pre-computed **lead angle** $\phi_{lead}$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | rpm, where $f$ = Hz, $P$ = number of poles |
| 2 | Slip Frequency (Beat Frequency) | $f_{slip} = f_g - f_b$ | Hz |
| 3 | Slip Cycle Period | $T_{slip} = \dfrac{1}{f_{slip}}$ | seconds |
| 4 | Phase Angle Rate of Change | $\dfrac{d\phi}{dt} = 2\pi \, f_{slip}$ | rad/s |
| 5 | Lead Angle (Pre-closure) | $\phi_{lead} = 2\pi \, f_{slip} \cdot t_{CB}$ | radians / electrical degrees |
| 6 | Beat Voltage (Phasor Difference) | $V_{beat} = \sqrt{V_g^2 + V_b^2 - 2 V_g V_b \cos\phi}$ | Volts (Voltmeter across CB) |
| 7 | Closing Command Window | $\phi \in [-\phi_{lead}, +\phi_{lead}]$ at $V_{beat} \le V_{set}$ | degrees |
| 8 | Real Power after Paralleling | $P = \dfrac{V_g V_b}{X_s} \sin\delta$ | Watts (per-phase) |
| 9 | Reactive Power after Paralleling | $Q = \dfrac{V_g^2 - V_g V_b \cos\delta}{X_s}$ | VAR (per-phase) |
| 10 | Synchronising Power Coefficient | $P_{sync} = \dfrac{dP}{d\delta}\bigg\vert_{\delta=0} = \dfrac{V_g V_b}{X_s}$ | W per radian |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Safeguard:** The vertical pipe $\vert$ used in conditionals is written as `\vert` only inside the table above to prevent markdown parsing errors. All equations are rendered in standard LaTeX.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

Auto-synchronising is the **backbone of all UMS-class vessels** (container ships, LNG carriers, naval frigates, cruise liners). It enables:
- **Load sharing** between generators (kW and kVAr).
- **Blackout recovery** — auto-start and auto-sync of standby generator within **30 s** (Class rule).
- **Fuel optimisation** — PMS sheds inefficient DG sets and brings online optimal ones via auto-sync.
- **Shaft Generator (Shaft Gen / PTO)** paralleling with aux. diesel generators on a moving ship where shaft RPM varies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Lead-Angle Compensation Equation

The breaker close command cannot be issued at the **exact** zero-phase-crossing because the breaker contacts physically move over a finite duration. We must mathematically determine the *advance* phase angle required.

**Step 1 — Define the variables.**
Let $f_g$ be the generator frequency and $f_b$ the busbar frequency. Define the slip frequency:

$$
f_{slip} = f_g - f_b
$$

**Step 2 — Express the phase angle drift.**
Since the rotating phasor of the generator advances relative to the busbar phasor at the slip rate, the angular velocity difference is:

$$
\omega_{slip} = 2\pi \, f_{slip}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the phase swept during CB closing time.**
If the breaker takes $t_{CB}$ seconds to physically close, the phase angle that will elapse between the *command* and the *actual contact touch* is:

$$
\phi_{lead} = \omega_{slip} \cdot t_{CB} = 2\pi \, f_{slip} \cdot t_{CB}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert radians to electrical degrees (KTU standard).**
Using $1 \text{ rad} = \dfrac{180}{\pi}$ degrees:

$$
\phi_{lead}^{\circ} = f_{slip} \cdot t_{CB} \cdot 360
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example.**
A marine ACB has a closing time $t_{CB} = 80 \text{ ms} = 0.08 \text{ s}$. The measured slip frequency is $f_{slip} = 0.25 \text{ Hz}$. Compute the lead angle.

$$
\phi_{lead}^{\circ} = 0.25 \times 0.08 \times 360
$$

$$
\phi_{lead}^{\circ} = 0.02 \times 360 = 7.2^{\circ}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The auto-synchroniser must issue the close command when the incoming generator is **7.2 electrical degrees AHEAD** of the busbar phasor. By the time the breaker contacts actually meet (80 ms later), the phasors will be coincident at 0°.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Beat Voltage (Synchroscope Phasor Equation)

A traditional **synchroscope** is a two-coil instrument that displays the vector difference between $V_g$ and $V_b$. The mathematical expression for the magnitude of the phasor difference is:

$$
V_{beat} = \sqrt{V_g^2 + V_b^2 - 2 V_g V_b \cos\phi}
$$

**Derivation Steps (parallelogram law of phasors):**

**Step 1 — Represent both voltages as rotating phasors.**
Take $V_b$ as the reference phasor on the real axis, and $V_g$ as leading (or lagging) by phase angle $\phi$.

$$
V_b = V_b \angle 0^{\circ}
$$

$$
V_g = V_g \angle \phi
$$

**Step 2 — Form the phasor difference (ΔV across open CB contacts).**

$$
\Delta V = V_g - V_b
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the magnitude using the law of cosines.**
Treating $V_g$ and $V_b$ as two sides of a triangle with included angle $\phi$, the third side (opposite to $\phi$) is:

$$
\vert \Delta V \vert = \sqrt{V_g^2 + V_b^2 - 2 V_g V_b \cos\phi}
$$

**Step 4 — Limit-case verification.**
- If $V_g = V_b$ and $\phi = 0^{\circ}$: $\vert \Delta V \vert = \sqrt{2V^2 - 2V^2} = 0$ ✓ (safe to close).
- If $V_g = V_b$ and $\phi = 180^{\circ}$: $\vert \Delta V \vert = 2V$ ✗ (anti-phase — destructive closure).

### 3.3 Closed-Loop Control Block (Analytical Form)

The auto-synchroniser is a **multi-variable feedback controller**. The governing Laplace-domain error signals are:

$$
e_V(t) = V_b - V_g(t)
$$

$$
e_f(t) = f_b - f_g(t)
$$

$$
e_\phi(t) = \phi_{ref} - \phi_{meas}(t)
$$

These three errors drive three independent PID controllers (AVR for $e_V$, Governor for $e_f$, Breaker Driver for $e_\phi$). The synchroniser uses **permissive interlocks** — the breaker is allowed to close **only if and only if**:

$$
\vert e_V \vert \le \Delta V_{max} \quad \wedge \quad \vert e_f \vert \le \Delta f_{max} \quad \wedge \quad \vert e_\phi \vert \le \phi_{lead}
$$

This is a classic **AND-gated permissive logic**, implemented in the PLC of the Auto-Sync unit (e.g., **DEIF MASC 100/200**, **ABB UNITROL**, **Caterpillar EMCP**, **Siemens SIMATIC**).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Auto-Synchronising Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** A Mermaid *block-level functional architecture flow* is used in lieu of a physical drawing to satisfy the KTU schematic representation requirement while remaining Mermaid-compliant (no special characters inside node labels, no reserved keywords as IDs).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[BUSBAR VT SECONDARY] --> B1[Vbus Measurement Module]
    A2[GENERATOR VT SECONDARY] --> B2[Vgen Measurement Module]
    A3[BUSBAR FREQUENCY TRANSDUCER] --> C1[Fbus Measurement Module]
    A4[GENERATOR FREQUENCY TRANSDUCER] --> C2[Fgen Measurement Module]
    A5[BREAKER AUX CONTACT] --> D1[CB Status Feedback]

    B1 --> E1[AUTO SYNC PROCESSOR PLC]
    B2 --> E1
    C1 --> E1
    C2 --> E1
    D1 --> E1

    E1 --> F1[AVR REFERENCE COMMAND]
    E1 --> F2[GOVERNOR SPEED REFERENCE COMMAND]
    E1 --> F3[BREAKER CLOSE COIL DRIVER]

    F1 --> G1[EXCITER AVR LOOP]
    F2 --> G2[PRIME MOVER GOVERNOR]
    F3 --> G3[MAIN CIRCUIT BREAKER]

    G1 --> A2
    G2 --> A4
    G3 --> H1[PARALLELED GENERATOR ON BUS]

    E1 --> I1[PERMISSIVE LOGIC GATE]
    I1 -->|DeltaV OK DeltaF OK DeltaPhi OK| F3
    I1 -->|OUT OF WINDOW| J1[BLOCK CLOSE ALARM ECR AMS]
```

### 4.2 Sequence-of-Operations Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[START AUTO SYNC COMMAND] --> S2{Generator Breaker Open?}
    S2 -->|NO| S3[Abort Raise Alarm]
    S2 -->|YES| S4[Measure Vbus Vgen Fbus Fgen]
    S4 --> S5{DeltaV within plus minus 5 percent?}
    S5 -->|NO| S6[Adjust AVR Field Current]
    S6 --> S4
    S5 -->|YES| S7{DeltaF within plus minus 0.2 Hz?}
    S7 -->|NO| S8[Adjust Governor Fuel Ramping]
    S8 --> S4
    S7 -->|YES| S9[Compute Lead Angle phi_lead]
    S9 --> S10{Phi at zero crossing within plus minus phi_lead?}
    S10 -->|NO| S11[Wait Next Slip Cycle]
    S11 --> S10
    S10 -->|YES| S12[Issue CLOSE Command to Breaker]
    S12 --> S13[Confirm CB Status Closed]
    S13 --> S14[Activate Load Sharing P and Q]
    S14 --> S15[End Auto Sync Sequence]
```

### 4.3 Permissive Interlock Logic (Truth Table)

| ΔV OK? | Δf OK? | Δφ OK? | Breaker Close Permission | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ✗ | — | — | **DENIED** | Voltage mismatch — AVR active |
| ✓ | ✗ | — | **DENIED** | Frequency mismatch — Governor active |
| ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | **DENIED** | Phase not in lead-angle window — Waiting |
| ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | **GRANTED** | Synchronising pulse issued to CB |
| ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (transient) | **LATCHED** | Breaker driving toward closure |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define auto-synchronising. List the three conditions of synchronism that must be satisfied before closing the generator circuit breaker.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Auto-synchronising is the automated process of paralleling a synchronous generator to a live busbar using an Auto-Sync controller, without operator intervention.
The three conditions are:
1. **Voltage match:** $V_g \approx V_b$ (within ±5%).
2. **Frequency match:** $f_g \approx f_b$ (within ±0.2 Hz).
3. **Phase angle match:** $\phi \approx 0°$ electrical, with lead-angle compensation applied for breaker closing time.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the purpose of "lead-angle compensation" in an auto-synchroniser?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Lead-angle compensation accounts for the **finite closing time** $t_{CB}$ of the circuit breaker. Since the breaker cannot close instantaneously, the auto-sync unit issues the **close command in advance**, when the phase angle is $\phi_{lead} = 360 \cdot f_{slip} \cdot t_{CB}$ electrical degrees **ahead** of the busbar, so that actual contact coincidence occurs at zero phase difference, avoiding damaging inrush transients.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse "phase sequence" with "phase angle"** — phase sequence is checked once at commissioning; phase angle is checked every sync.
> - **Always state the Class rule tolerance** (ΔV = ±5%, Δf = ±0.2 Hz) in your answer or lose 1 mark.
> - **Draw the block diagram / flowchart** — diagrams carry 3–4 marks in a 14-mark question. Skipping them is the #1 reason students fail Part B.
> - **Show the lead-angle formula derivation**, not just the formula. Examiners award method marks.
> - **Units are mandatory** — write Hz, V, °, ms.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Auto-Sync Principles & Lead Angle*

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **functional operation of an auto-synchroniser** in a ship's power plant. Describe how the three permissives (ΔV, Δf, Δφ) are evaluated before issuing the breaker close command. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A marine **vacuum circuit breaker (VCB)** has a closing time of **60 ms**. The incoming generator frequency is **50.3 Hz** and the busbar frequency is **50.0 Hz**. Calculate the **lead angle** at which the auto-synchroniser must issue the close command. Also compute the **slip cycle period** $T_{slip}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks):**
The auto-synchroniser consists of:
1. **Measurement modules** — VT and frequency transducers sense busbar and generator voltage/frequency.
2. **Auto-Sync Processor (PLC/Microcontroller)** — computes error signals $e_V, e_f, e_\phi$.
3. **AVR control output** — adjusts excitation to reduce $e_V$.
4. **Governor control output** — adjusts fuel admission to reduce $e_f$.
5. **Permissive AND-gate logic** — confirms all three permissives are within tolerance.
6. **Breaker close driver** — issues a pulse to the CB close coil at the correct lead angle.
7. **CB status feedback** — confirms closure; aborts and alarms if breaker fails to close.
*(Full marks awarded for diagram + explanation of each block + permissive logic.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute slip frequency.**

$$
f_{slip} = f_g - f_b = 50.3 - 50.0 = 0.3 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute slip cycle period.**

$$
T_{slip} = \frac{1}{f_{slip}} = \frac{1}{0.3} = 3.333 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply lead-angle formula.**

$$
\phi_{lead}^{\circ} = f_{slip} \cdot t_{CB} \cdot 360
$$

$$
\phi_{lead}^{\circ} = 0.3 \times 0.06 \times 360
$$

$$
\phi_{lead}^{\circ} = 0.018 \times 360 = 6.48^{\circ}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- **Lead angle = 6.48° electrical (ahead of busbar)**
- **Slip cycle period $T_{slip}$ = 3.33 seconds**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Marine PMS Integration & Blackout Recovery*

**(a)** Describe the role of the **Power Management System (PMS)** in coordinating auto-synchronising during **normal load sharing** and **post-blackout auto-restart**. Mention the relevant **Classification Society rules** (DNV / Lloyd's / ABS) that govern auto-sync functionality on UMS vessels. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two identical 1000 kW marine diesel generators are paralleled through an auto-synchroniser. After synchronising, Generator 1 carries 600 kW and Generator 2 carries 400 kW (total 1000 kW). The governor droop is set to 4%. Calculate the **frequency drop** of the system from no-load to full load. Comment on how the **isochronous vs. droop mode** affects the auto-sync process. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PMS Role in Auto-Sync (7 marks):**
- During **normal operation**, PMS continuously monitors busbar load (kW). When a running generator exceeds a pre-set threshold (e.g., 80% of rated kW), PMS automatically **starts** the standby generator, **ramps it up**, and **auto-syncs** it to share load — extending engine life and improving fuel efficiency.
- During a **blackout**, PMS executes a **Blackout Recovery Sequence**: the main breaker trips, all consumers are shed, the standby DG is **auto-started** (within 30 s as per DNV Pt.4 Ch.8 / Lloyd's Rules), and once voltage and frequency stabilise, it is **auto-synchronised** to the dead busbar, followed by sequential **load restoration** in priority blocks.
- **Classification rules:** DNV Pt.4 Ch.8 §4.6, Lloyd's Register Rules for Ships Pt.6 Ch.2, ABS Rules Pt.4 Ch.3 §4 — all mandate auto-sync for UMS notation vessels.

**(b) Droop Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total load.**

$$
P_{total} = 600 + 400 = 1000 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Frequency drop formula using droop percentage.**

$$
\Delta f = \text{Droop} (\%) \times \frac{P_{load}}{P_{rated}} \times f_{nom}
$$

For one generator carrying the full 1000 kW (worst case):

$$
\Delta f = 4\% \times \frac{1000}{1000} \times 50 = 0.04 \times 50 = 2 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — For 2 paralleled generators sharing load (each carrying 500 kW ideally):**

$$
\Delta f = 0.04 \times \frac{500}{1000} \times 50 = 1 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment on droop vs. isochronous:**
- **Droop mode:** Speed decreases as load increases — allows multiple generators to share load proportionally without "fighting". **Preferred for marine** (paralleled sets).
- **Isochronous mode:** Constant frequency regardless of load — used for **single generator on bus** (e.g., during blackout recovery before sync). Auto-sync brings the second machine onto the bus, then the system reverts to droop for stable load sharing.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Three conditions of synchronism:** Voltage match, Frequency match, Phase angle match.
- ✅ **Phase sequence** is verified **once at commissioning**, NOT every sync.
- ✅ **Lead-angle formula:** $\phi_{lead}^{\circ} = f_{slip} \cdot t_{CB} \cdot 360$.
- ✅ **Slip frequency formula:** $f_{slip} = f_g - f_b$.
- ✅ **Slip period formula:** $T_{slip} = \dfrac{1}{f_{slip}}$.
- ✅ **Beat voltage:** $V_{beat} = \sqrt{V_g^2 + V_b^2 - 2 V_g V_b \cos\phi}$.
- ✅ **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}$ (very high-yield formula).
- ✅ **Standard tolerances:** ΔV = ±5%, Δf = ±0.2 Hz, Δφ = ±10° (optimum 0°).
- ✅ **Droop mode** for paralleled generators; **isochronous** for solo generator.
- ✅ **Blackout recovery** time limit: **30 seconds** (DNV / Lloyd's Class).
- ✅ **Permissive AND-logic** — all three conditions MUST be satisfied simultaneously.
- ✅ **Real & reactive power equations** after synchronising: $P = \dfrac{V_g V_b}{X_s}\sin\delta$, $Q = \dfrac{V_g^2 - V_g V_b \cos\delta}{X_s}$.
- ✅ **Auto-synchroniser vendors to remember (KTU-favourite brands):** DEIF, ABB, Woodward, Caterpillar EMCP, Siemens.
- ✅ **Diagram is compulsory** in any 14-mark question — always include the block diagram / flowchart.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Parallel Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Academic Definition

**Parallel Operation** in marine electrical engineering refers to the deliberate electrical interconnection of two or more AC synchronous generators (alternators) or DC generators on a common bus-bar (main switchboard) so that they jointly supply the connected shipboard load while maintaining a common voltage, frequency, and phase sequence. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, this is termed **"Generator Paralleling"** or **"Synchronization"** and forms the backbone of any ship's **Integrated Electric Power Plant (IEPP)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** *Parallel operation of alternators, conditions for synchronization, methods of synchronization (dark lamp, bright lamp, synchroscope), load sharing, governor characteristics, and circulating currents between alternators.*

The conditions for successful parallel operation of two alternators (Generator 1 → G1, and Generator 2 → G2) feeding a common load are formally known as the **"Three Golden Conditions of Synchronization"**:

1. **Identical Terminal Voltage Magnitude** → $|E_1| = |E_2|$ (EMFs must be equal in RMS value).
2. **Identical Frequency** → $f_1 = f_2$ (i.e., identical electrical angular velocity, $\omega_1 = \omega_2$).
3. **Identical Phase Sequence and Zero Phase Angle Difference** → The phase angle between the two EMFs at the instant of switching must be $\delta = 0^{\circ}$ (in-phase condition), and they must have the same phase rotation (R-Y-B).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine Engineering Context:** In a typical merchant ship (Bulk Carrier, Tanker, Container Vessel), the auxiliary engine room houses **3 to 4 diesel generator sets** (e.g., 3 × 1500 kW + 1 emergency). At berth, usually **one generator** feeds the entire ship; however, during **maneuvering (SOP – Stationary Operations Period)** and **sea passage**, **two or three generators** are run in parallel to handle bow thrusters, cargo pumps, and propulsion auxiliary loads. Hence, the engineer officer MUST master parallel operation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine two water pumps trying to push water into a **single common pipe**:
- If Pump A pushes **harder** (higher pressure = higher voltage) than Pump B, water will flow *backwards* into Pump B — this is the **"circulating current"** that damages alternators.
- If Pump A pumps **faster** (higher frequency) than Pump B, the two water columns will **slap** against each other — this is the **"phase angle difference"** that causes violent mechanical torque transients.
- The two pumps must push at the **exact same rhythm and same force** for the water to merge smoothly — this is **"synchronization"**.

Once the pumps are merged, the **load (water demand)** must be **shared equitably**. If Pump A is asked to deliver 60% of the water while Pump B delivers 40%, that is **load sharing**, controlled by the **governor** (engine throttle) of each prime mover.

> [!NOTE]
> **Shipboard Intuition:** Think of the main switchboard as a "marriage altar" — generators must be perfectly compatible (voltage, frequency, phase) before they can be "married" in parallel. The **synchroscope** and **governor** act as the "matchmaker" and the "load negotiator."

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Marine Alternator Ratings (Typical):**
> - **Frequency:** $\mathbf{f = 60\ Hz}$ (most common globally) or $\mathbf{50\ Hz}$ (Europe/Asia grids).
> - **Voltage:** $\mathbf{450\ V}$, $\mathbf{6.6\ kV}$, or $\mathbf{11\ kV}$ (LV/MV marine).
> - **Power Factor:** $\mathbf{0.8\ lagging}$ (typical inductive shipboard load).
> - **Speed:** $\mathbf{1800\ RPM}$ (4-pole, 60 Hz) or $\mathbf{1500\ RPM}$ (4-pole, 50 Hz).
> - **Synchronous Reactance:** $\mathbf{X_s \approx 1.5\ to\ 2.5\ pu}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Logic of Paralleling

The act of bringing two alternators onto a common bus-bar is broken down into five logical KTU-exam-ready steps:

### Step 1: Pre-Paralleling Verification
- Confirm the incoming generator (G2) is **running at rated speed** (no-load rated frequency).
- Check **insulation resistance** ($> 1\ M\Omega$) using the **megger**.
- Verify **excitation system** is functional (AVR in auto mode).
- Confirm the **circuit breaker** of G2 is **OPEN** (mechanically locked out).

### Step 2: Voltage Matching
- Adjust the **field excitation** (AVR voltage reference) of G2 until its terminal voltage equals the bus-bar voltage of G1.
- Measurement is via the **voltmeter** of the synchroscope panel.
- Acceptable tolerance: $\pm 5\%$ of rated voltage.

### Step 3: Frequency Matching
- Adjust the **governor speed droop setting** of G2's prime mover until its frequency is **slightly above** bus frequency.
- This "slightly higher" frequency creates a small positive **slip frequency** ($f_{slip} = f_2 - f_1 \approx +0.1\ to\ +0.5\ Hz$) so that G2 takes load smoothly upon closing the breaker.

### Step 4: Phase Sequence and Phase Angle Check
- Verify the **phase rotation** (R-Y-B) of G2 matches the bus using a **phase sequence indicator**.
- Bring the phase angle $\delta$ between the two voltages to **zero** (in-phase) using the **synchroscope** (a split-phase electrodynamometer instrument).

### Step 5: Breaker Closure and Load Transfer
- At the **moment synchroscope pointer crosses 12 o'clock (in-phase position)**, close G2's circuit breaker.
- Immediately adjust both governors so that load is shared in proportion to their **kW ratings**.

## 2.2 Conditions for Synchronization — The "3 V's and 1 F"

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic — "V-V-F-S"** = **Voltage equal, Voltage (phase) sequence same, Frequency equal, (Phase) angle zero.**

| S.No | Condition | Practical Consequence if Violated |
|------|-----------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | $\|V_{G2}\| = \|V_{bus}\|$ | **Circulating reactive current** flows between machines |
| 2 | Phase sequence RYB same | **Reverse rotation, severe short circuit** — catastrophic |
| 3 | $f_{G2} = f_{bus}$ | **Beating**, mechanical torsional stress, hunt |
| 4 | Phase angle $\delta = 0$ | **Huge inrush current** ($\approx 10\times$ rated) at out-of-phase closure |

## 2.3 Synchronizing Methods (KTU High-Yield)

### Method 1: Two-Bright-One-Dark Lamp Method (3-Lamp Method)
- Three lamps are connected across the **open breaker poles** between the two alternators.
- Lamps will **dark** (all three off) when all three phases are in phase simultaneously.
- Drawback: Cannot distinguish "fast" from "slow" frequency — only the rate of dark-bright cycles.

### Method 2: One-Bright-Two-Dark Lamp Method
- One lamp is connected with a **reversed connection** to its phase.
- Lamps light in a **rotating sequence** indicating fast or slow frequency.

### Method 3: Synchroscope Method (Most Common on Ships)
- An **electrodynamometer instrument** with two pairs of coils:
  - **Fixed coils** carry current proportional to bus-bar voltage.
  - **Moving coil** carries current proportional to incoming generator voltage.
- The pointer **rotates clockwise** (fast) or **counter-clockwise** (slow) depending on slip frequency.
- Closure at the **12 o'clock position** ($\delta = 0$).

### Method 4: Automatic Synchronizer (Modern Ships)
- Microprocessor-based devices (e.g., Woodward SPM-D, DEIF CAS) automatically adjust governor and AVR and close the breaker when all conditions are met within tolerance.
- Mandatory on **UMS (Unmanned Machinery Space)** vessels per SOLAS and Class rules.

## 2.4 Load Sharing Between Parallel Alternators

After synchronization, the real power ($P$) is shared based on **governor droop characteristics** and reactive power ($Q$) is shared based on **AVR voltage droop**.

### 2.4.1 Real Power ($kW$) Sharing — Governed by Prime Mover Droop

$$P_{G1} + P_{G2} = P_{total\ load}$$

$$P_{G1} : P_{G2} = \text{Rated } kW_1 : \text{Rated } kW_2$$

**Governor Droop** is defined as:

$$D\% = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100\%$$

where $N_{NL}$ = no-load speed, $N_{FL}$ = full-load speed. Typical marine droop = **4% to 5%**.

### 2.4.2 Reactive Power ($kVAr$) Sharing — Governed by AVR Droop

$$Q_{G1} + Q_{G2} = Q_{total\ load}$$

**Voltage Droop** is defined as:

$$V_{droop}\% = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$$

Typical marine AVR droop = **3% to 5%**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \cdot f}{P}$ | $N_s$ in RPM, $f$ in Hz, $P$ = number of poles |
| 2 | Slip Frequency | $f_{slip} = f_2 - f_1$ | Hz; should be $\approx +0.1$ to $+0.5$ Hz |
| 3 | Power Angle | $P = \dfrac{\vert E \vert \cdot \vert V \vert}{X_s} \sin \delta$ | Synchronizing power equation |
| 4 | Reactive Power | $Q = \dfrac{\vert E \vert \cdot \vert V \vert}{X_s} \cos \delta - \dfrac{\vert V \vert^2}{X_s}$ | VAr output of alternator |
| 5 | Governor Droop | $D\% = \dfrac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100$ | Percentage droop |
| 6 | Voltage Droop | $V_d\% = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$ | Percentage droop |
| 7 | Circulating Current | $I_{circ} = \dfrac{\vert E_1 - E_2 \vert}{2X_s}$ | RMS amps, $X_s$ per phase |
| 8 | Synchronizing Power | $P_{syn} = \dfrac{dP}{d\delta} = \dfrac{\vert E \vert \vert V \vert}{X_s} \cos\delta$ | Restoring torque coefficient (W/elect. rad) |
| 9 | Load Sharing Ratio | $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \dfrac{kW_1}{kW_2}$ (at same droop) | Proportional to rating |
| 10 | Induced EMF | $E = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \Phi \cdot k_w$ | Transformer EMF equation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Reminder:** All electrical quantities in SI (Volts, Amps, Watts, VAr, VA). Angles $\delta$ in **electrical radians** for derivations, **degrees** for practical phasor diagrams.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ships (this course):** Main switchboard paralleling of 3–4 diesel generators for redundancy and load management.
- **Power Plants:** Multiple turbo-generators feed a common grid.
- **Microgrids & Islanded Systems:** Parallel inverter operation with droop control.
- **Aircraft APUs:** Auxiliary Power Units paralleled with main engine-driven generators.
- **Hybrid Marine (LNG, Hybrid Ferries):** Shaft generators, fuel cells, and battery banks parallel with diesel generators — all governed by **ISO 16315** and **DNV GL rules**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Synchronizing Power Coefficient

**Statement:** Show that the synchronizing power coefficient of a cylindrical rotor alternator connected to an infinite bus is $P_{syn} = \dfrac{\vert E \vert \cdot \vert V \vert}{X_s} \cos\delta$.

### Given
- Cylindrical rotor synchronous machine.
- Internal EMF $E$ (phase value), terminal voltage $V$ (infinite bus).
- Synchronous reactance $X_s$.
- Power angle $\delta$ between $E$ and $V$.

### Derivation

Step 1 — **Phasor representation.** The current delivered by the alternator to the bus is given by Ohm's law for the synchronous generator:

$$
I_a = \frac{E\angle\delta - V\angle 0^{\circ}}{j X_s}
$$

Step 2 — **Rationalize the complex denominator.** Multiplying numerator and denominator by $-j$:

$$
I_a = -j \, \frac{(E\angle\delta - V\angle 0^{\circ})}{X_s}
$$

Step 3 — **Convert to rectangular form.** Writing $E\angle\delta = E\cos\delta + jE\sin\delta$:

$$
I_a = -j \, \frac{(E\cos\delta - V) + j E\sin\delta}{X_s}
$$

$$
I_a = \frac{-j (E\cos\delta - V) + (E\sin\delta)}{X_s}
$$

$$
I_a = \frac{E\sin\delta}{X_s} \; - \; j \, \frac{(E\cos\delta - V)}{X_s}
$$

Step 4 — **Compute complex power per phase.** The complex power delivered is $S = V \cdot I_a^{\*}$ (using the conjugate of $I_a$):

$$
S = V \angle 0^{\circ} \cdot \left[ \frac{E\sin\delta}{X_s} + j \, \frac{(E\cos\delta - V)}{X_s} \right]
$$

$$
S = \frac{V E \sin\delta}{X_s} + j \, \frac{V(E\cos\delta - V)}{X_s}
$$

Step 5 — **Extract real (active) power.**

$$
P = \Re\{S\} = \frac{V \cdot E}{X_s} \sin\delta
$$

Step 6 — **Differentiate to obtain synchronizing power coefficient.** This represents the **restoring torque per unit electrical radian of angular displacement**:

$$
P_{syn} = \frac{dP}{d\delta} = \frac{d}{d\delta}\left[ \frac{V \cdot E}{X_s} \sin\delta \right]
$$

$$
P_{syn} = \frac{V \cdot E}{X_s} \cos\delta
$$

**Hence proved.** The synchronizing power coefficient per phase is $\dfrac{V \cdot E}{X_s} \cos\delta$, and for a 3-phase machine, the total is $3 \times \dfrac{V \cdot E}{X_s} \cos\delta$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning:** $P_{syn}$ acts as the "electrical spring stiffness" that keeps the rotor locked in synchronism with the bus. A larger $P_{syn}$ means greater stability against disturbances.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Circulating Current Between Two Alternators

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the circulating current that flows between two alternators connected in parallel, both having synchronous reactance $X_{s1}$ and $X_{s2}$ respectively, when their EMFs are $E_1\angle\delta_1$ and $E_2\angle\delta_2$ with a common terminal voltage $V$.

### Given
- Two alternators G1 (EMF $E_1$, reactance $X_{s1}$) and G2 (EMF $E_2$, reactance $X_{s2}$).
- Common bus voltage $V$.
- $E_1 \approx E_2 = E$ (machines equally excited, small imbalance).

### Derivation

Step 1 — **Apply KVL to each machine loop.**

For G1, the current $I_1$ flowing **out** of G1 towards the bus:

$$
E_1\angle\delta_1 - V\angle 0^{\circ} = j X_{s1} \cdot I_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_1 = \frac{E_1\angle\delta_1 - V}{j X_{s1}}
$$

For G2, the current $I_2$ flowing **out** of G2 towards the bus:

$$
I_2 = \frac{E_2\angle\delta_2 - V}{j X_{s2}}
$$

Step 2 — **Net current into bus = load current.** By KCL, $I_1 + I_2 = I_{load}$. This is the **load current**, the useful part.

Step 3 — **Isolate the circulating component.** The circulating current $I_{circ}$ is the part that flows **internally between the two machines without reaching the load**. It exists purely due to the EMF difference $E_1 - E_2$.

If we momentarily ignore the load and set $I_{load} = 0$, then $I_1 = -I_2 = I_{circ}$:

$$
I_{circ} = \frac{E_1\angle\delta_1 - E_2\angle\delta_2}{j(X_{s1} + X_{s2})}
$$

Step 4 — **Simplify for small differences.** For a practical paralleling transient where $E_1 \approx E_2$ in magnitude and $\delta_1 \approx \delta_2$:

$$
I_{circ} \approx \frac{\vert E_1 - E_2 \vert}{X_{s1} + X_{s2}}
$$

Step 5 — **Symmetric case** ($X_{s1} = X_{s2} = X_s$):

$$
I_{circ} = \frac{\vert E_1 - E_2 \vert}{2 X_s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine Engineering Insight:** Circulating current is purely **reactive** (since $X_s$ dominates over $R_a$). It does no real work but overheats armature windings. It is minimized by ensuring equal excitation and zero phase angle at the instant of breaker closure.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Load Sharing with Equal Governor Droop

**Statement:** Show that two alternators with ratings $kW_1$ and $kW_2$ and identical governor droop $D\%$ share the load in proportion to their ratings.

### Derivation

Step 1 — **Governor's droop line equation.** For an alternator driven by a governor of droop $D$:

$$
f = f_{NL} - \left(\frac{D}{100}\right) \cdot f_{NL} \cdot \left(\frac{P}{P_{rated}}\right)
$$

Step 2 — **At common bus frequency** $f_{bus}$, both generators operate at the **same frequency** (rigid electrical coupling of parallel operation):

$$
f_{bus} = f_{NL} \cdot \left[1 - \frac{D_1}{100} \cdot \frac{P_1}{P_{1,rated}}\right] = f_{NL} \cdot \left[1 - \frac{D_2}{100} \cdot \frac{P_2}{P_{2,rated}}\right]
$$

Step 3 — **For equal droop** $D_1 = D_2 = D$:

$$
\frac{P_1}{P_{1,rated}} = \frac{P_2}{P_{2,rated}}
$$

$$
\therefore \quad \frac{P_1}{P_2} = \frac{P_{1,rated}}{P_{2,rated}} = \frac{kW_1}{kW_2}
$$

**Hence proved.** The load is shared in the **ratio of the rated kW capacities** when droop is identical.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Two 3-phase alternators are to be paralleled. G1 rating = 1000 kVA, $X_{s1} = 1.2\ \Omega$; G2 rating = 1500 kVA, $X_{s2} = 1.0\ \Omega$. At the moment of closing the breaker, $E_1 = 420\ V$ (phase), $E_2 = 415\ V$ (phase), and they are exactly in phase ($\delta = 0^{\circ}$). Find the circulating current.

### Solution

Step 1 — Identify the EMF difference (magnitude):

$$
\vert E_1 - E_2 \vert = \vert 420 - 415 \vert = 5\ V
$$

Step 2 — Sum of synchronous reactances:

$$
X_{s1} + X_{s2} = 1.2 + 1.0 = 2.2\ \Omega
$$

Step 3 — Compute circulating current:

$$
I_{circ} = \frac{\vert E_1 - E_2 \vert}{X_{s1} + X_{s2}} = \frac{5}{2.2} \approx 2.27\ A \text{ (per phase)}
$$

Step 4 — For 3-phase:

$$
I_{circ,3\phi} = 2.27\ A \text{ (per phase, line current equal in star connection)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 5V mismatch in a 415V system produced only 2.27 A circulating current, which is well within tolerable limits. A 10% mismatch (≈42 V) would produce $42/2.2 \approx 19.1\ A$ — far more dangerous. **This is why AVR fine-tuning before sync is critical.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Parallel Operation Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Prime Mover G1 - Diesel Engine 1] --> B[Shaft Coupling]
    B --> C[Alternator G1 - Synchronous Generator 1]
    C --> D[AVR G1 - Excitation Control]
    C --> E[Main Switchboard Bus Bar]
    F[Prime Mover G2 - Diesel Engine 2] --> G[Shaft Coupling 2]
    G --> H[Alternator G2 - Synchronous Generator 2]
    H --> I[AVR G2 - Excitation Control]
    H --> E
    E --> J[Shipboard Load - Motors Lighting Pumps]
    E --> K[Synchroscope Panel]
    K --> L[Manual or Auto Synchronizer]
    L --> M[Circuit Breaker G2]
    D --> N[Governor G1 - kW Load Share]
    I --> O[Governor G2 - kW Load Share]
    N --> A
    O --> F
```

## 4.2 Synchronization Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start - G2 Running at Rated Speed] --> B{Voltage Equal Check}
    B -- No --> C[Adjust AVR of G2] --> B
    B -- Yes --> D{Frequency Equal Check}
    D -- No --> E[Adjust Governor of G2] --> D
    D -- Yes --> F{Phase Sequence Match}
    F -- No --> G[STOP - Reverse Two Phases of G2]
    F -- Yes --> H{Phase Angle Delta Zero}
    H -- Fast --> I[Reduce G2 Speed Slightly]
    H -- Slow --> J[Increase G2 Speed Slightly]
    I --> H
    J --> H
    H -- At 12 O Clock Position --> K[Close Circuit Breaker of G2]
    K --> L[Adjust Both Governors for Load Share]
    L --> M[Parallel Operation Established]
    M --> N[Continuous Monitoring kW kVAr PF]
```

## 4.3 Phasor Diagram (Mermaid Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference V - Bus Bar] --> B[Phasor V along X axis]
    C[Induced EMF E1] --> D[Phasor E1 at angle delta]
    E[Induced EMF E2] --> F[Phasor E2 at angle delta with E1]
    B -.-> G[Circulating current Icirc perpendicular to delta E]
    D -.-> G
    F -.-> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The phasor diagram shows $V$ as the reference, with both alternator EMFs $E_1$ and $E_2$ leading $V$ by the power angle $\delta$. The small vector difference $\Delta E = E_1 - E_2$ drives the circulating current $I_{circ}$ which lags the difference by nearly 90° (reactive in nature).

## 4.4 Load Sharing Block Diagram (Droop Control)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1[Prime Mover G1]
        G1[Governor with Droop D1] --> E1[Engine 1]
    end
    subgraph P2[Prime Mover G2]
        G2[Governor with Droop D2] --> E2[Engine 2]
    end
    E1 --> A1[Alternator 1]
    E2 --> A2[Alternator 2]
    A1 --> BUS[Common Bus Bar]
    A2 --> BUS
    BUS --> LD[Total Ship Load Ptotal]
    A1 -. kW Share P1 .-> SH[Load Share Controller]
    A2 -. kW Share P2 .-> SH
    SH -. Droop Adjustment Signal .-> G1
    SH -. Droop Adjustment Signal .-> G2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
