# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**  
**State any three conditions that must be satisfied before two 3-phase alternators can be connected in parallel.**

**Model Answer:**

The three essential conditions (also called the *conditions of synchronization*) are:

1. **Equal Terminal Voltage:** The RMS magnitude of the incoming alternator's terminal voltage must equal the bus-bar voltage. Practically, $|V_{G2}| = |V_{bus}|$ within ±5%.

2. **Equal Frequency:** The frequency of the incoming machine must match the bus-bar frequency, i.e., $f_{G2} = f_{bus}$. In practice, a slight positive slip of +0.1 to +0.5 Hz is maintained.

3. **Identical Phase Sequence and Zero Phase Angle:** Both machines must have the same phase rotation (R-Y-B), and the phase angle between their voltages must be zero ($\delta = 0$) at the instant of breaker closure.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**  
**What is the function of a synchroscope? Name two alternative methods of synchronizing alternators.**

**Model Answer:**

A **synchroscope** is an electrodynamometer instrument used to indicate the instantaneous phase angle between the voltage of the incoming alternator and the bus-bar voltage. Its pointer rotates:
- **Clockwise (FAST):** When the incoming generator runs faster than the bus.
- **Counter-clockwise (SLOW):** When it runs slower.
- **At 12 o'clock (IN-PHASE):** When the two voltages are in phase, indicating the correct instant to close the breaker.

**Two alternative synchronizing methods:**
1. **Three-lamp method (two bright – one dark lamp method):** Lamps connected across the breaker poles; lamps darken in a rotating sequence, indicating phase and slip.
2. **Dark-bright lamp method (or one-bright-two-dark):** Lamps are cross-connected such that the lighting pattern indicates fast/slow.

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024]**

**Q. (a)** [7 Marks] With neat diagrams, explain the **three-lamp method** of synchronizing two 3-phase alternators. How does it differ from the **synchroscope method**?

**Q. (b)** [7 Marks] Two alternators rated 1500 kW and 1000 kW operate in parallel. The governor droop characteristics are 4% and 5% respectively. If the total load is 2000 kW, find the load shared by each machine. Assume infinite bus-bar.

---

#### Model Solution for Q. (a) — Three-Lamp Method

**Working Principle:**  
Three incandescent lamps $L_1, L_2, L_3$ are connected between the corresponding phases of the incoming alternator and the bus-bar, across the **poles of the yet-to-be-closed circuit breaker** of G2.

- If the phase sequence of G2 matches the bus, the lamps will brighten and darken **simultaneously** (in unison) at a rate equal to the **slip frequency** $(f_{G2} - f_{bus})$.
- At the instant all three lamps are **equally dim** (or fully dark in the "dark-lamp" variant), the voltages are in **phase opposition** or **phase coincidence** depending on the connection.
- The operator adjusts G2's governor until the **slip is very low** (lamps flicker slowly) and closes the breaker at the dark moment.

**Difference from Synchroscope Method:**

| Aspect | 3-Lamp Method | Synchroscope Method |
|--------|---------------|---------------------|
| Indication | Visual flicker of lamps | Rotating pointer |
| Distinguishes Fast/Slow | No (in straight connection) | Yes (CW = fast, CCW = slow) |
| Accuracy | Moderate | High |
| Cost | Low | Moderate |
| UMS Compliance | Not acceptable | Acceptable (with auto-sync) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Mention that on modern ships, the **automatic synchronizer** is preferred as it requires no human intervention, fulfilling **UMS (Unmanned Machinery Space)** class notation requirements.

#### Model Solution for Q. (b) — Load Sharing Calculation

**Given:**
- $P_{1,rated} = 1500\ kW$, $D_1 = 4\%$
- $P_{2,rated} = 1000\ kW$, $D_2 = 5\%$
- $P_{total} = 2000\ kW$

**Step 1 — Use the common frequency equation.**

For parallel operation at common bus frequency $f_{bus}$:

$$
f_{bus} = f_{NL}\left[1 - \frac{D_1}{100} \cdot \frac{P_1}{P_{1,rated}}\right] = f_{NL}\left[1 - \frac{D_2}{100} \cdot \frac{P_2}{P_{2,rated}}\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Equate the two expressions.**

$$
\frac{4}{100} \cdot \frac{P_1}{1500} = \frac{5}{100} \cdot \frac{P_2}{1000}
$$

$$
\frac{4 P_1}{150000} = \frac{5 P_2}{100000}
$$

$$
4 P_1 \times 100000 = 5 P_2 \times 150000
$$

$$
400000 P_1 = 750000 P_2
$$

$$
P_1 = \frac{750000}{400000} P_2 = 1.875 P_2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply total load constraint.**

$$
P_1 + P_2 = 2000
$$

$$
1.875 P_2 + P_2 = 2000
$$

$$
2.875 P_2 = 2000
$$

$$
\boxed{P_2 = 695.65\ kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_1 = 2000 - 695.65 = 1304.35\ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify each machine's loading percentage:**

- G1: $\dfrac{1304.35}{1500} \times 100 = 86.96\%$
- G2: $\dfrac{695.65}{1000} \times 100 = 69.57\%$

Both machines are within rated capacity ✓.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024]**

**Q. (a)** [7 Marks] Derive the **synchronizing power coefficient** of a synchronous machine connected to an infinite bus-bar. State its physical significance.

**Q. (b)** [7 Marks] With a neat sketch, explain the working of a **synchroscope**. State its advantages over the lamp method.

---

#### Model Solution for Q. (a) — Synchronizing Power Coefficient

**Step 1:** Current delivered by synchronous machine to infinite bus:

$$
I_a = \frac{E\angle\delta - V\angle 0^{\circ}}{j X_s}
$$

**Step 2:** Rationalize denominator (multiply by $-j$):

$$
I_a = -j \cdot \frac{(E\cos\delta - V) + j E\sin\delta}{X_s}
$$

$$
I_a = \frac{E\sin\delta}{X_s} - j \cdot \frac{(E\cos\delta - V)}{X_s}
$$

**Step 3:** Complex power per phase:

$$
S = V \cdot I_a^{*} = V \left[ \frac{E\sin\delta}{X_s} + j \cdot \frac{(E\cos\delta - V)}{X_s} \right]
$$

**Step 4:** Real power (active):

$$
P = \frac{V \cdot E}{X_s} \sin\delta
$$

**Step 5:** Differentiate to find synchronizing power:

$$
P_{syn} = \frac{dP}{d\delta} = \frac{V \cdot E}{X_s} \cos\delta
$$

For 3-phase: $P_{syn,3\phi} = \dfrac{3 V E}{X_s} \cos\delta$

**Physical Significance:**
- Represents the **restoring torque** per unit electrical radian of angular disturbance.
- Acts as an **electrical spring** keeping the rotor in synchronism.
- Higher $P_{syn}$ → greater **steady-state and transient stability**.
- At $\delta = 0$ (ideal sync), $P_{syn}$ is maximum: $\dfrac{VE}{X_s}$.
- At $\delta = 90°$ (steady-state stability limit), $P_{syn} = 0$ — the machine loses synchronism.

#### Model Solution for Q. (b) — Synchroscope Working

**Construction:** It is an **electrodynamometer-type instrument** with:

- **Two fixed coils** (stator) — connected in **series with a resistor**, across the **incoming generator terminals** (or bus).
- **One moving coil** (rotor) — connected in **series with a capacitor** across the **bus-bar** (or vice versa).
- A pointer attached to the moving coil, with a graduated dial (0° at 12 o'clock, ±90° at 9 and 3 o'clock).

**Working Principle:**

The torque on the moving coil is proportional to the **phase angle** between the fixed-coil flux and the moving-coil flux.

$$
T \propto \cos(\omega t - \delta)
$$

The pointer deflects and stays at the angle where instantaneous torque is zero — i.e., the **actual phase difference** between the two voltages.

- If the incoming alternator frequency is **higher** → the angle $\delta$ continuously changes in one direction → pointer rotates **clockwise** (FAST).
- If **lower** → pointer rotates **counter-clockwise** (SLOW).
- When pointer is at **12 o'clock** → voltages are in phase → close the breaker.

**Advantages over Lamp Method:**

1. **Directional indication:** Clearly shows whether G2 is fast or slow.
2. **Higher accuracy:** Indicates exact phase angle continuously.
3. **Operator-friendly:** Single glance reading; no flicker interpretation needed.
4. **Can be integrated** with auto-synchronizers for UMS operation.
5. **Suitable for high-voltage** main switchboards where lamps are impractical.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Do NOT confuse "frequency matching" with "phase angle matching"** — they are separate conditions. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if students state them as one.
> - **Governor droop vs. Voltage droop** — students often interchange them. Remember: **kW (real power) → Governor (engine); kVAr (reactive) → AVR (excitation).**
> - **Synchronizing power ≠ Synchronous power** — synchronizing power is the *derivative* of the power-angle curve (a coefficient), not the power itself.
> - **Forgetting the factor 3** in 3-phase totals is a common 1-mark loss.
> - **Not drawing a phasor diagram** in synchronization questions costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Parallel operation = synchronization** of two or more generators on a common bus-bar.
- [x] **Three Golden Conditions:** Equal Voltage, Equal Frequency, Zero Phase Angle (with same phase sequence).
- [x] **Synchronizing Methods:** (i) 3-Lamp (ii) Synchroscope (iii) Auto-synchronizer.
- [x] **Breaker closure at $\delta = 0$**, with positive slip of +0.1 to +0.5 Hz for smooth load pickup.
- [x] **Real power (kW) sharing** is controlled by the **governor droop** of the prime mover.
- [x] **Reactive power (kVAr) sharing** is controlled by the **AVR voltage droop**.
- [x] **Load sharing ratio** equals **rating ratio** when droops are equal.
- [x] **Governor droop** = $(N_{NL} - N_{FL})/N_{FL} \times 100\%$ (typical: 4–5%).
- [x] **Synchronous speed** $N_s = 120f/P$.
- [x] **Synchronizing power** $P_{syn} = (VE/X_s)\cos\delta$ per phase, $= 3(VE/X_s)\cos\delta$ for 3-phase.
- [x] **Circulating current** $I_{circ} = \vert E_1 - E_2 \vert / (X_{s1} + X_{s2})$ — purely reactive if $R_a \ll X_s$.
- [x] **Active power depends on $\delta$** (power angle) — small changes in load change $\delta$ only.
- [x] **Reactive power depends on $|E|$** (excitation) — small changes in excitation change VAr share.
- [x] **Marine context:** 3–4 diesel alternators paralleled on main switchboard; UMS class requires auto-sync.
- [x] **Modern ships** use **microprocessor auto-synchronizers** (Woodward, DEIF) for safe, unattended paralleling.
- [x] **Out-of-phase paralleling** → massive fault current (10× rated), catastrophic mechanical shock — **NEVER bypass the synchroscope**.
- [x] **After sync, immediately trim both governors** to the desired load share per the chief engineer's standing orders.
- [x] **Reverse power trip** is mandatory protection: $P_{reverse} > 5–15\%$ of rated for $> 5$ seconds → trip breaker.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** Always draw a **phasor diagram** in any synchronization or load-sharing question — it instantly demonstrates your conceptual clarity and earns easy 1–2 marks. Use **block diagrams** liberally in 14-mark questions to break up text and show systems thinking.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Sharing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Load Sharing # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Load Sharing? **Load Sharing** in the context of shipboard electrical power plants is defined as the *controlled division of total electrical power demand among multiple synchronous generators operating in parallel* on the same bus, so that each machine carries a *predetermined, stable, and proportionate share* of the active (kW) and reactive (kVAR) power. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):** > *Load sharing is the technique by which two or more alternators running in synchronism share the connected load in a fixed ratio, governed by their droop characteristics, ensuring stability of frequency, voltage, and prime-mover loading.* In a modern vessel, the **Auxiliary Generators (Aux. Gen.)**, **Emergency Generators**, and **Harbour Generators** are routinely operated in parallel to: 1. Provide redundancy (N+1 or N+2 configuration). 2. Improve overall plant efficiency at part-load. 3. Meet the high in-rush currents of large motors (e.g., bow thrusters, cargo pumps). --- ## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Bucket Brigade" Imagine a group of friends carrying a heavy water bucket along a row of cups. - One person trying to carry the entire bucket alone will spill it, get tired, and stagger (this is an overloaded single alternator — frequency drops, voltage collapses). - When **two people** hold the same handle from opposite sides, they must *coordinate* their push and pull so that no one drops the bucket. - The *person who pushes harder* carries more weight — this corresponds to the **governor droop** setting in alternators. - The *person who angles their hand upward more* tilts the bucket — this is analogous to **reactive power (kVAR) sharing** through **excitation control**. **Key Intuition:** Just as the brigade members must communicate and match their efforts, parallel alternators share load based on their **governer speed-droop** (for kW) and **voltage regulator droop** (for kVAR). --- ## 1.3 Why Parallel Operation? (The "Why" Behind Load Sharing) > [!NOTE] > **Fundamental Necessities of Paralleling Synchronous Generators (KTU High-Yield):** > 1. **Continuity of Supply** — Fault on one machine does not black out the ship. > 2. **Economic Loading** — Generators operate near rated capacity (typically 70–85%) for best Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC). > 3. **Maintenance Flexibility** — One unit can be serviced while others feed the switchboard. > 4. **Peak Load Handling** — Cargo handling, dynamic positioning, and combat systems demand several MWs. > 5. **Reduced Spare Capacity** — N units replace one oversized unit at lower capital cost. --- ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Metric | Standard Value on Merchant / Naval Ships | |---|---| | **Generator frequency** | **50 Hz** (or 60 Hz on US/UK carriers) | | **Nominal bus voltage** | **440 V** or **6.6 kV** (HV on LNG/LPG carriers) | | **Allowable frequency deviation** | **± 2.5 %** steady, **± 10 %** transient | | **Allowable voltage deviation** | **± 2.5 %** steady, **± 20 %** transient | | **Governor speed droop** | **3 % – 5 %** typically (adjustable 0–10 %) | | **Cross-current compensation coil resistance** | 0.5 Ω – 2 Ω in compound circuits | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Load Sharing Load sharing has **two orthogonal components** that must be controlled *independently*: ### Pillar 1 — Active Power (kW) Sharing - Controlled by the **Prime Mover Governor** (mechanical input torque). - Affects the **frequency** of the bus. - Distributed according to the **Speed-Droop Characteristics** of each engine. ### Pillar 2 — Reactive Power (kVAr) Sharing - Controlled by the **Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR)** (field excitation). - Affects the **terminal voltage** of the bus. - Distributed according to the **Voltage-Droop Characteristics** of each AVR. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Orthogonality Theorem (Examination Favourite):** > *Active power (P) and reactive power (Q) cross-couple only through the second-order terms of the swing equation. For slow steady-state load sharing, P and Q can be treated as decoupled — a 90° phase shift exists between their control actions.* --- ## 2.2 Speed-Droop (Governor Characteristic) The **speed-droop** (or *speed regulation*) of a prime mover is defined as: > *The drop in steady-state speed when full rated load is applied, expressed as a percentage of no-load speed.* **Mathematical Form:** $$D \;=\; \frac{N_{nl} \;-\; N_{fl}}{N_{nl}} \times 100\ \%$$ Where: - $N_{nl}$ = No-load (idle) speed in **rpm** - $N_{fl}$ = Full-load speed in **rpm** - $D$ = Speed-droop in **%** **Effect of Droop on Load Division (Step-by-Step Logic):** 1. Each alternator has a near-linear droop line on the *frequency-vs-load* graph. 2. When two alternators with **equal droop** are paralleled, the operating frequency settles at the geometric mean — both carry **50 % of the load**. 3. When droops are **unequal**, the alternator with the **smaller (steeper) droop** picks up a *larger share* of any new kW load. 4. The **no-load frequencies** are pre-set by the operator via the **speeder gear / load-set motor** until the bus wattmeter (or PMS controller) reads the desired ratio. > [!TIP] > **Real-World Naval Example:** A DDG (Destroyer) operating in 'Cruise 2' mode runs **4 × 2.5 MW** generators in parallel. The PMS raises the speed-set of all four governors in *synchronised increments* — the load ramps up uniformly to 8 MW (2 MW per unit) without any manual intervention. --- ## 2.3 Voltage-Droop (AVR Characteristic) A similar droop concept applies to the **AVR**: $$V_{droop} \;=\; \frac{V_{nl} \;-\; V_{fl}}{V_{nl}} \times 100\ \%$$ Generators with **steeper voltage droop** absorb **less reactive load**; those with **flatter droop** absorb **more kVAr**. Classically regulated alternators use a **reactive-current compensator (RCC)** or **quadrature droop** to achieve a 4–6 % droop on the AVR reference, ensuring stable kVAr division. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) | # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $D = \dfrac{N_{nl} - N_{fl}}{N_{nl}} \times 100$ | Speed droop | % | Governor characteristic | | 2 | $P_1 \cdot D_1 = P_2 \cdot D_2$ | Load-share product | kW·% | Equal-droop division check | | 3 | $f_{bus} = f_{nl,1} - \dfrac{P_1}{P_{rated,1}} \cdot D_1 \cdot f_n$ | Parallel operating frequency | Hz | Steady-state bus frequency | | 4 | $I_{load,1} = \dfrac{P_{total}}{2 V \cos\phi}$ | Per-unit current | A | Two-machine equal share | | 5 | $V_{droop} = \dfrac{V_{nl} - V_{fl}}{V_{nl}} \times 100$ | Voltage droop | % | AVR kVAr division | | 6 | $Q = \dfrac{V}{X_s}(E_f \sin\delta)$ | Reactive power transfer | kVAr | Internal angle δ | | 7 | $P = \dfrac{V \cdot E_f}{X_s}\sin\delta$ | Power-angle equation | kW | Synchronising power | | 8 | $S_{sync} = \dfrac{dP}{d\delta}\bigg\lvert_{\delta=0} = \dfrac{V \cdot E_f}{X_s}$ | Synchronising torque coefficient | pu/rad | Stability margin | | 9 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{S_{sync}}{2 H}}$ | Natural frequency of swing | rad/s | Transient stability | | 10 | $\tau_{sync} = \dfrac{H}{\pi f} \cdot \dfrac{\delta_{max} \times \pi}{180}$ | Critical clearing time | s | Breaker setting | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Note on Pipes:** In all the formulas above, vertical bars are written using `\vert` (e.g., $\lvert x \rvert$) or `\big\vert` so the markdown table does not break. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Load Sharing | Industry / Domain | Application of Load Sharing | |---|---| | **Merchant Shipping** | Parallel Aux. Generators on bulk carriers, tankers, container ships. | | **Naval Combat Systems** | N+1 redundant generation for radar, weapon systems, and combat management. | | **Dynamic Positioning (DP)** | DP-3 class vessels need at least 3 generator groups; load sharing is automatic via Power Management System (PMS). | | **Offshore Platforms** | Parallel diesel/gas generator sets with PMS. | | **Hybrid / Electric Propulsion** | Shaft generators and diesel generators operate in parallel with shore power during cold-ironing. | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Load Division Between Two Parallel Alternators (Active Power) Consider two alternators **G1** and **G2** operating in parallel on a common bus of voltage $V$ and frequency $f$. ### Given: - G1 rating: $P_{r1}$ kW, droop $D_1$ %, no-load frequency $f_{1,nl}$ - G2 rating: $P_{r2}$ kW, droop $D_2$ %, no-load frequency $f_{2,nl}$ - Total load: $P_L$ kW (to be divided) ### Step 1 — Write the droop line equations The droop line of G1 (frequency decreases as load increases): $$f_1 \;=\; f_{1,nl} \;-\; \left(\dfrac{f_{1,nl} \cdot D_1}{100}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_{r1}}\right)$$ The droop line of G2 (frequency decreases as load increases): $$f_2 \;=\; f_{2,nl} \;-\; \left(\dfrac{f_{2,nl} \cdot D_2}{100}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_{r2}}\right)$$ ### Step 2 — Apply the paralleling condition (same frequency) In parallel operation, $f_1 = f_2 = f_{bus}$. Therefore: $$f_{1,nl} - \left(\dfrac{f_{1,nl} \cdot D_1}{100}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_{r1}}\right) \;=\; f_{2,nl} - \left(\dfrac{f_{2,nl} \cdot D_2}{100}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_{r2}}\right)$$ ### Step 3 — Use load conservation By Kirchhoff's law applied to the active power balance: $$P_1 + P_2 \;=\; P_L$$ ### Step 4 — Solve simultaneously Solving the two-equation system yields the classic KTU relation: $$\boxed{\;P_1 \;=\; \dfrac{P_L \cdot (D_2 / P_{r2})}{(D_1 / P_{r1}) + (D_2 / P_{r2})}\;}$$ $$\boxed{\;P_2 \;=\; \dfrac{P_L \cdot (D_1 / P_{r1})}{(D_1 / P_{r1}) + (D_2 / P_{r2})}\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** *The alternator with the **lower** value of $D/P_r$ (i.e., stiffer characteristic) carries the **larger** share of the load. This is the exact opposite intuition of a series-current divider because droop is a "negative" resistance slope.* ### Step 5 — Numerical Example (Worked Out) **Given:** - $P_{r1} = P_{r2} = 1000$ kW - $D_1 = 4$ %, $D_2 = 5$ % - $P_L = 1500$ kW - $f_{1,nl} = f_{2,nl} = 51$ Hz **Solution:** $$\dfrac{D_1}{P_{r1}} = \dfrac{4}{1000} = 0.004$$ $$\dfrac{D_2}{P_{r2}} = \dfrac{5}{1000} = 0.005$$ $$P_1 = \dfrac{1500 \times 0.005}{0.004 + 0.005} = \dfrac{7.5}{0.009} = 833.33 \text{ kW}$$ $$P_2 = \dfrac{1500 \times 0.004}{0.004 + 0.005} = \dfrac{6.0}{0.009} = 666.67 \text{ kW}$$ $$P_1 + P_2 = 833.33 + 666.67 = 1500 \text{ kW} \;\; \checkmark$$ $$f_{bus} = 51 - \left(\dfrac{51 \times 4}{100}\right) \times \left(\dfrac{833.33}{1000}\right) = 51 - 1.700 = 49.30 \text{ Hz}$$ The drop in frequency is acceptable for a transient state. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reactive Load Sharing Between Two Alternators Reactive power division follows the same droop concept applied to the AVR. ### Step 1 — Voltage droop equations $$V_1 \;=\; V_{1,nl} \;-\; \left(\dfrac{V_{1,nl} \cdot V_{droop,1}}{100}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_{r1}}\right)$$ $$V_2 \;=\; V_{2,nl} \;-\; \left(\dfrac{V_{2,nl} \cdot V_{droop,2}}{100}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_{r2}}\right)$$ ### Step 2 — Apply $V_1 = V_2 = V_{bus}$ and $Q_1 + Q_2 = Q_L$ The general solution is: $$Q_1 = \dfrac{Q_L \cdot (V_{droop,2} / Q_{r2})}{(V_{droop,1} / Q_{r1}) + (V_{droop,2} / Q_{r2})}$$ ### Step 3 — Cross-coupling term A small coupling exists because a change in $\delta$ (power angle) also changes $Q$. The full $P$–$Q$ relationship for a salient-pole machine is: $$P = \dfrac{E_f V}{X_d}\sin\delta + \dfrac{V^2}{2}\left(\dfrac{1}{X_q}-\dfrac{1}{X_d}\right)\sin 2\delta$$ $$Q = \dfrac{E_f V}{X_d}\cos\delta - \dfrac{V^2}{2}\left(\dfrac{1}{X_q}+\dfrac{1}{X_d}\right) + \dfrac{V^2}{2}\left(\dfrac{1}{X_q}-\dfrac{1}{X_d}\right)\cos 2\delta$$ For a *cylindrical rotor* (turbo-alternator) $X_d = X_q = X_s$, simplifying to: $$P = \dfrac{E_f V}{X_s}\sin\delta, \qquad Q = \dfrac{E_f V}{X_s}\cos\delta - \dfrac{V^2}{X_s}$$ > [!TIP] > **Examination Trick:** *Most KTU problems are stated on cylindrical-rotor marine alternators — use the simplified $P$–$Q$ equations. Only when salient-pole is explicitly mentioned (rare on ships) should you use the $2\delta$ terms.* --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Synchronising Power and Steady-State Stability Limit ### Step 1 — Recap the power-angle equation $$P_e = \dfrac{E_f V}{X_s}\sin\delta$$ ### Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $\delta$ $$\dfrac{dP_e}{d\delta} = \dfrac{E_f V}{X_s}\cos\delta$$ ### Step 3 — Find the synchronising torque coefficient At the steady-state operating angle $\delta_0$: $$S_{sync} = \left.\dfrac{dP_e}{d\delta}\right|_{\delta = \delta_0} = \dfrac{E_f V}{X_s}\cos\delta_0$$ ### Step 4 — Maximum transferable power (steady-state stability limit) The condition $\frac{dP_e}{d\delta} = 0$ gives the **critical angle**: $$\delta_{max} = 90^{\circ} \;(\text{cylindrical rotor})$$ $$P_{max} = \dfrac{E_f V}{X_s}$$ ### Step 5 — Operating margin Stability margin in %: $$M_{stab} = \dfrac{P_{max} - P_{op}}{P_{max}} \times 100\ \%$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** *Always quote $\delta$ in degrees, but use **radians** in the derivative. A 1° error in $\delta$ causes a ~5 % error in $P_{max}$. In KTU valuation, half a mark is lost per inconsistent unit.* --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Variable Matters | Variable | Physical Meaning | Effect if Increased | |---|---|---| | $E_f$ (EMF) | Internal induced voltage (field-dependent) | $\uparrow P_{max}$, $\uparrow Q$ capability | | $V$ (Terminal) | Bus voltage | $\uparrow P_{max}$, $\uparrow$ stability | | $X_s$ (Sync reactance) | Armature reaction & leakage | $\downarrow P_{max}$, $\downarrow$ stability | | $\delta$ (Torque angle) | Rotor-to-stator electrical separation | $\uparrow P$ (up to $90°$) | | $H$ (Inertia const.) | $\dfrac{1}{2} J \omega^2 / S_{base}$ | $\uparrow$ transient stability | | $D_{gov}$ (Droop) | Slope of speed-load line | $\downarrow$ load sensitivity | | $D_{AVR}$ (Volt droop) | Slope of V–Q line | $\downarrow$ kVAr sensitivity | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Shipboard Load Sharing Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Diesel Engine 1] --> B1[Governor 1
Droop 4 percent] A --> C1[Alternator 1
1000 kW] B1 --> C1 C1 --> D1[CB 1] D1 --> E[Main Switchboard Bus
440 V 50 Hz] F[Diesel Engine 2] --> B2[Governor 2
Droop 5 percent] F --> C2[Alternator 2
1000 kW] B2 --> C2 C2 --> D2[CB 2] D2 --> E G[Diesel Engine 3] --> B3[Governor 3
Droop 4 percent] G --> C3[Alternator 3
1000 kW] B3 --> C3 C3 --> D3[CB 3] D3 --> E E --> H[Power Management System PMS] H --> B1 H --> B2 H --> B3 E --> I[Ship Service Loads
Pumps Fans Lighting] E --> J[Propulsion Drive Bus
2 MW] E --> K[Combat System Feed
Naval Vessels] E --> L[Emergency Board
via Bus Tie] ``` > [!NOTE] > *Node labels intentionally avoid the reserved mermaid keywords `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, and `style`. All identifiers are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters.* --- ## 4.2 Flowchart — Sequence of Paralleling & Load Sharing ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start G1 G2 Bring to Rated Speed Voltage] --> B[Check Phase Sequence L1 L2 L3 Match] B --> C[Check Voltage Difference less than 5 percent] C --> D[Check Frequency Difference less than 0.5 Hz] D --> E[Check Phase Angle less than 10 deg] E --> F{All Conditions Met} F -->|No| G[Adjust Governor or AVR] G --> D F -->|Yes| H[Close CB 2 Synchroscope at 12 O Clock] H --> I[Synchronising Breaker Closes] I --> J[Gradually Raise Load Set of G2] J --> K[Simultaneously Lower Load Set of G1] K --> L{Target Load Sharing Achieved} L -->|No| M[Fine Tune Governor Speeder Motors] M --> L L -->|Yes| N[Steady State Parallel Operation] N --> O[Monitor kW kVAr Frequency Voltage] O --> P{Any Trip Condition} P -->|Yes| Q[Open CB Trip Generator] Q --> R[Auto Load Shedding via PMS] P -->|No| O ``` --- ## 4.3 Schematic — Governor Droop Characteristics ```mermaid graph LR A[Frequency Hz
Y axis] --> B[Droop Line G1
D1 equals 4 percent] A --> C[Droop Line G2
D2 equals 5 percent] B --> D[Operating Point P1 P2
Bus Frequency 49.3 Hz] C --> D D --> E[Total Load PL equals 1500 kW
on X axis] ``` **Reading the schematic:** - The two droop lines intersect at the *common bus frequency* (49.3 Hz in our example). - The horizontal line drawn at the intersection point shows the *individual kW share* of each generator. - The **steeper line (G2)** has its intercept at a *higher* no-load frequency — but parallel operation *forces* both to operate at the common bus frequency, redistributing load automatically. --- ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Active vs Reactive Power Decoupling ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Total Load P total
Q total] --> B{Orthogonal Control} B --> C[Active kW Path
via Governor] B --> D[Reactive kVAr Path
via AVR] C --> E[Speed Droop
4 to 5 percent] E --> F[Frequency f
50 Hz Nominal] D --> G[Voltage Droop
4 to 6 percent] G --> H[Terminal Voltage V
440 V Nominal] F --> I[Stable Parallel Bus] H --> I ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define speed-droop of a governor. Why is droop deliberately introduced in the governors of alternators meant for parallel operation?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Speed-droop of a governor is defined as the percentage drop in steady-state speed from no-load to full-load, expressed as a fraction of the no-load speed. $$D = \frac{N_{nl} - N_{fl}}{N_{nl}} \times 100\ \%$$ **Droop is deliberately introduced for three reasons:** 1. *Stable load sharing* — without droop, any minor difference in governor characteristic would cause one machine to grab all the load. 2. *Negative feedback for stability* — when a generator picks up extra load, its speed drops, signalling the governor to add fuel. 3. *Preventing sustained hunting* — pure isochronous (zero-droop) governors on paralleled machines lead to power oscillations. **(1 mark for formula, 2 marks for the three reasons.)** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Distinguish between active power sharing and reactive power sharing in paralleled alternators. Mention the controlling device for each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Active Power (kW) | Reactive Power (kVAr) | |---|---|---| | **Controlling Device** | Prime-mover **Governor** | **AVR / Exciter** | | **Variable Changed** | Mechanical input (fuel) | Field current (excitation) | | **Bus Quantity Affected** | **Frequency** | **Terminal Voltage** | | **Indicator** | Frequency meter / kW meter | Voltmeter / kVAr meter | | **Droop Quantity** | Speed droop (4–5 %) | Voltage droop (4–6 %) | **(1.5 marks for distinction table, 1.5 marks for controller identification.)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **Two alternators rated 1500 kW and 1000 kW are to operate in parallel. Their governor droops are 4 % and 5 % respectively, and the no-load frequencies are set at 51 Hz on both. Determine (a) the load shared by each machine and the bus frequency when the total load is 2000 kW. (b) If a third identical machine to G1 (1500 kW, 4 % droop) is added with the same no-load frequency, find the new load distribution and bus frequency for the same 2000 kW total load.** --- #### **Solution:** **Part (a) — Two Machines in Parallel** **Step 1:** Compute droop slopes. $$\text{Slope}_1 = \frac{D_1}{P_{r1}} = \frac{4}{1500} = 2.667 \times 10^{-3} \; \%/\text{kW}$$ $$\text{Slope}_2 = \frac{D_2}{P_{r2}} = \frac{5}{1000} = 5.000 \times 10^{-3} \; \%/\text{kW}$$ **Step 2:** Apply load-division formulas (from §3.1). $$P_1 = \frac{P_L \cdot (D_2/P_{r2})}{(D_1/P_{r1}) + (D_2/P_{r2})} = \frac{2000 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3}}{2.667 \times 10^{-3} + 5.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$P_1 = \frac{10.0}{7.667 \times 10^{-3}} = 1304.35 \text{ kW}$$ $$P_2 = P_L - P_1 = 2000 - 1304.35 = 695.65 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3:** Compute bus frequency using G1's droop line. $$f_{bus} = f_{nl} - \frac{f_{nl} \cdot D_1}{100} \cdot \frac{P_1}{P_{r1}}$$ $$f_{bus} = 51 - \frac{51 \times 4}{100} \times \frac{1304.35}{1500} = 51 - 1.7735 = 49.23 \text{ Hz}$$ **Verification using G2:** $$f_{bus} = 51 - \frac{51 \times 5}{100} \times \frac{695.65}{1000} = 51 - 1.7735 = 49.23 \text{ Hz} \;\checkmark$$ **Part (a) Result:** $P_1 = 1304.35$ kW, $P_2 = 695.65$ kW, $f_{bus} = 49.23$ Hz. **(7 marks)** --- **Part (b) — Third Machine Added** **Step 1:** Updated parallel system with G3 (1500 kW, 4 % droop, same as G1). $$D_1/P_{r1} = D_3/P_{r3} = 4/1500 = 2.667 \times 10^{-3} \; \%/\text{kW}$$ **Step 2:** Combined conductance of G1 and G3 (identical). $$G_{13} = 2 \times 2.667 \times 10^{-3} = 5.333 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 3:** Re-apply load-division with G13 and G2. $$P_{13} = \frac{2000 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3}}{5.333 \times 10^{-3} + 5.0 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{10.0}{10.333 \times 10^{-3}} = 967.74 \text{ kW}$$ $$P_2 = 2000 - 967.74 = 1032.26 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4:** By symmetry, G1 = G3. $$P_1 = P_3 = \frac{967.74}{2} = 483.87 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5:** New bus frequency. $$f_{bus} = 51 - \frac{51 \times 4}{100} \times \frac{483.87}{1500} = 51 - 0.6581 = 50.34 \text{ Hz}$$ **Part (b) Result:** $P_1 = P_3 = 483.87$ kW, $P_2 = 1032.26$ kW, $f_{bus} = 50.34$ Hz. **(7 marks)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Loss Points:** > 1. *Forgetting to express droop in absolute terms (multiply by $f_{nl}/100$)* → loses 1 mark. > 2. *Using $f_{fl}$ instead of $f_{nl}$ in the formula* → loses 1 mark. > 3. *Skipping verification using G2's droop line* → loses 0.5 mark (no consistency check). > 4. *Not verifying $P_1 + P_2 + P_3 = P_L$* → loses 0.5 mark. > 5. *Mixing up droop direction (smaller droop = larger share)* → loses 1 mark. --- ### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a) Explain with a neat sketch the procedure of synchronising a 3-phase alternator to an existing live bus. List the four essential conditions that must be satisfied before closing the circuit breaker. (7 marks)** **(b) A 2 MVA, 11 kV, 3-phase, 50 Hz star-connected cylindrical-rotor alternator is operating in parallel with another identical machine. The per-phase synchronous reactance is 10 Ω. If the excitation of G1 is increased by 20 % while the load angle remains 25°, calculate the synchronising power, the new load angle, and comment on the stability margin. Assume $E_f$ = 1.0 pu initially, and $V$ = 1.0 pu. (7 marks)** --- #### **Solution:** **Part (a) — Synchronising Procedure** **Step 1 — The Four Conditions (KTU Mandatory):** 1. **Same phase sequence** — RYB of incoming matches RYB of bus. 2. **Same voltage magnitude** — $\lvert V_{bus} - V_{incoming} \rvert \leq 5\%$. 3. **Same frequency** — $\lvert f_{bus} - f_{incoming} \rvert \leq 0.5$ Hz. 4. **Same phase angle** — $\lvert \delta \rvert \leq 10°$ at the instant of breaker closure (synchroscope at 12 o'clock position). **Step 2 — Procedure:** 1. Start the prime mover; bring the alternator to rated speed via governor. 2. Adjust the AVR field rheostat to match the incoming terminal voltage to the bus voltage. 3. Verify phase sequence using a phase-sequence indicator. 4. Observe the **synchroscope** (a rotating dial indicating phase difference). 5. Adjust the speeder motor so that the synchroscope rotates *slowly clockwise* (incoming faster than bus by 0.1–0.3 Hz). 6. At the moment the synchroscope needle points to the **12 o'clock position** (phase angle = 0°), close the breaker. 7. After synchronisation, raise the load-set of the incoming machine and lower the load-set of the running machine to the desired kW ratio. **Step 3 — Sketch (described verbally for KTU answer sheet):** - Draw a synchronoscope with three lamps (dark-bright-dark) or a rotating-coil type. - Show the bus PT (potential transformer) and incoming PT. - Show the breaker in series with the synchronising check relay (25 sync-check). **(7 marks: 2 marks for the four conditions, 3 marks for the procedure, 2 marks for the sketch.)** --- **Part (b) — Synchronising Power and Stability** **Step 1:** Initial condition — $E_{f1} = 1.0$ pu, $V = 1.0$ pu, $\delta_1 = 25°$, $X_s = 10$ Ω. Synchronising power coefficient at $\delta_1$: $$S_{sync,1} = \frac{E_{f1} V}{X_s} \cos\delta_1 = \frac{1.0 \times 1.0}{10} \cos 25° = 0.1 \times 0.9063 = 0.0906 \text{ pu/rad}$$ **Step 2:** New excitation $E_{f2} = 1.2 \times E_{f1} = 1.2$ pu. Assuming real power transfer is constant (shaft input unchanged): $$P_e = \frac{E_{f1} V}{X_s}\sin\delta_1 = \frac{E_{f2} V}{X_s}\sin\delta_2$$ $$\frac{1.0}{10}\sin 25° = \frac{1.2}{10}\sin\delta_2$$ $$\sin\delta_2 = \frac{\sin 25°}{1.2} = \frac{0.4226}{1.2} = 0.3522$$ $$\delta_2 = \sin^{-1}(0.3522) = 20.62°$$ **Step 3:** New synchronising power coefficient: $$S_{sync,2} = \frac{E_{f2} V}{X_s}\cos\delta_2 = \frac{1.2 \times 1.0}{10} \times \cos 20.62°$$ $$S_{sync,2} = 0.12 \times 0.9359 = 0.1123 \text{ pu/rad}$$ **Step 4:** Maximum transferable power (stability limit). $$P_{max,1} = \frac{E_{f1} V}{X_s} = \frac{1.0 \times 1.0}{10} = 0.10 \text{ pu} \quad (\delta = 90°)$$ $$P_{max,2} = \frac{E_{f2} V}{X_s} = \frac{1.2 \times 1.0}{10} = 0.12 \text{ pu} \quad (\delta = 90°)$$ **Step 5:** Actual power transferred. $$P_e = 0.10 \times \sin 25° = 0.04226 \text{ pu}$$ **Step 6:** Stability margin. $$M_{stab,1} = \frac{P_{max,1} - P_e}{P_{max,1}} \times 100 = \frac{0.10 - 0.04226}{0.10} \times 100 = 57.74\ \%$$ $$M_{stab,2} = \frac{P_{max,2} - P_e}{P_{max,2}} \times 100 = \frac{0.12 - 0.04226}{0.12} \times 100 = 64.78\ \%$$ **Conclusion:** Increasing excitation by 20 % **reduces the load angle from 25° to 20.62°** and **increases the stability margin from 57.74 % to 64.78 %.** The system becomes *more stable* because excitation boost raises the ceiling of transferable power. **(7 marks: 2 marks for synchronising power, 2 marks for new $\delta$, 1 mark for stability margin, 2 marks for the conclusion.)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Loss Points:** > 1. *Forgetting to convert 25° to radians when differentiating* — answer is for steady state, so degrees are fine, but mention explicitly. > 2. *Treating $X_s$ as per-unit on the machine's own base* — if $X_s = 10\ \Omega$ on a 2 MVA / 11 kV base, the student must convert to pu. (Common base conversion error: 0.5 mark lost.) > 3. *Not stating the assumption of constant real power* — 1 mark penalty. > 4. *Confusing the two stability margins* — quote both initial and final. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorise for KTU 2024 ESE):** > > ☐ **Droop definition:** $D = (N_{nl} - N_{fl}) / N_{nl} \times 100\ \%$. > > ☐ **Droop direction:** *Smaller droop → larger load share* (counter-intuitive — practise one numerical). > > ☐ **Two-axis decoupling:** P ↔ Governor ↔ Frequency; Q ↔ AVR ↔ Voltage. > > ☐ **Speed-droop typical value:** 3–5 % for marine governors. > > ☐ **Voltage-droop typical value:** 4–6 % (via quadrature droop compensator). > > ☐ **Power-angle equation:** $P = (E_f V / X_s) \sin\delta$ for cylindrical rotor. > > ☐ **Maximum power limit:** $P_{max} = E_f V / X_s$ at $\delta = 90°$. > > ☐ **Synchronising torque:** $dP/d\delta = (E_f V / X_s) \cos\delta$ — *decreases* as $\delta$ increases. > > ☐ **Four synchronising conditions:** Phase sequence, voltage, frequency, phase angle. > > ☐ **Standard bus:** 440 V, 50 Hz, 3-phase, 3-wire insulated neutral (or 4-wire with neutral). > > ☐ **AVR term:** "Quadrature droop" — current transformer in the AVR senses reactive component. > > ☐ **PMS function:** Power Management System automates load-dependent start/stop, load sharing, and load shedding for DP-class vessels. > > ☐ **Exam trap:** Always cross-verify your $P_1$ and $P_2$ by recomputing $f_{bus}$ from both droop lines. > > ☐ **Memorise units:** Hz for frequency, V for voltage, kW for P, kVAr for Q, pu for normalised calculations, s for time constants. > > ☐ **Real-world anchor:** Reference at least one ship type (e.g., VLCC, DDG, Aframax) in descriptive answers for higher marks.

topics.title: Speed Droop and Power Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Speed Droop and Power Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Speed Droop** (also called **Speed Regulation** or **Governor Droop**) is a fundamental characteristic of a prime-mover governor that defines the intentional, controlled drop in rotational speed of an alternator as its electrical load increases from no-load to full-load. It is mathematically expressed as a percentage of the rated (nominal) no-load speed.

$$n_{droop} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{NL}} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $N_{NL}$ = Steady-state no-load speed (rpm)
- $N_{FL}$ = Steady-state full-load speed (rpm)
- The speed drop is typically between **2.5%** and **5%** for marine governor systems (as per IEC 60092 / IACS UR M3 requirements).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Speed droop is the *enabling mechanism* for stable load sharing between alternators operating in parallel on a ship's main switchboard. Without it, two or more generators connected in parallel would continuously "fight" each other for the load, causing severe frequency instability and mechanical stress.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Friends Carrying a Table" Intuition

Imagine two friends carrying a heavy table together:
- **Friend A** has *muscle memory*: when the table gets heavier, he automatically bears more weight without complaining — this is like a generator with **isochronous (zero droop) governor**. If both friends are exactly equal, the load on each is unpredictable, and they constantly argue.
- **Friend B** has a *flexible back*: when the load increases, he bends down slightly (speed drops) and gracefully accepts more weight — this is **speed droop**. Now, if Friend B also bends slightly, they both share the load in a stable, predictable manner.

The "bending" (speed drop) is the droop. The governor's droop characteristic is what allows **two or more generators to "know" how much load to take** without explicit communication, based purely on the common bus-bar frequency.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value (Marine) | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Rated Frequency | **50 Hz** or **60 Hz** | Hertz (Hz) |
| Synchronous Speed (4-pole, 50 Hz) | **1500** | rpm |
| Synchronous Speed (4-pole, 60 Hz) | **1800** | rpm |
| Typical Governor Droop | **2.5 – 5** | % |
| Permissible Steady-State Frequency Band (Class) | $\pm 5$ | % |
| Transient Frequency Deviation | $\pm 10$ | % |

> [!NOTE]
> In ships, the prime mover is **almost always a diesel engine** (turbocharged, 4-stroke, medium-speed for auxiliaries) or a **steam/gas turbine** for naval/advanced vessels. The alternator is a **synchronous machine with brushless excitation** (per IEEE 45 / IEC 60092-301).

---

## 1.4 Types of Governor Characteristics

| Type | Droop Value | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Isochronous (Flat)** | **0 %** | Single-generator islanded operation, or one master in droop with others in isochronous |
| **Droop (Sloped)** | **2.5 – 5 %** | Parallel operation of two or more generators (marine standard) |
| **Composite / Variable** | Adjustable | Modern electronic governors (DEGO/Electrohydraulic) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics Behind Speed Droop

The speed droop characteristic is implemented **mechanically or electronically** within the governor. Here's the operational logic:

1. **Speed Sensing:** The governor continuously senses alternator speed via a centrifugal flyweight mechanism, a magnetic pickup, or a shaft-mounted encoder.
2. **Load Detection:** As electrical load increases, the electromagnetic counter-torque on the prime mover (diesel) shaft increases.
3. **Fuel Correction Attempt:** The governor commands more fuel to maintain speed, but due to the *droop setting* (a deliberate feedback gain adjustment), it intentionally allows a small speed reduction proportional to load.
4. **Steady-State Equilibrium:** A new equilibrium speed $N_{FL}$ is reached, lower than $N_{NL}$, such that:

$$N = N_{NL} - k \cdot P$$

Where $k$ is the droop constant (rpm per kW) and $P$ is the active power output.

## 2.2 Load Sharing Between Parallel Generators

When **Generator A** (droop $D_A$) and **Generator B** (droop $D_B$) operate in parallel on a common bus of frequency $f_{bus}$:

$$P_A = P_{A,rated} \cdot \frac{N_{NL,A} - N_{bus}}{N_{NL,A} - N_{FL,A}}$$

$$P_B = P_{B,rated} \cdot \frac{N_{NL,B} - N_{bus}}{N_{NL,B} - N_{FL,B}}$$

Because $N_{bus}$ is **identical for both** (they are rigidly coupled electrically), the load division is **inversely proportional to droop**. Therefore:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Board Exam Rule:** Generators with *equal droop* share load in proportion to their **ratings**. Generators with *unequal droop* share load inversely proportional to droop (lower droop = takes more load).

## 2.3 Power Generation in Ships — System Architecture

A modern vessel's electrical plant typically comprises:

| Source | Prime Mover | Power Range | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Main Diesel Generators (D/G)** | 4-stroke diesel | 0.5 – 6 MW each | Normal sea-going service |
| **Shaft Generator (SG)** | Main propulsion shaft | 1 – 25 MW | Cruising economy mode |
| **Auxiliary/Turbo Generator** | Steam/GT exhaust | 0.5 – 3 MW | Waste heat recovery |
| **Emergency Generator (EDG)** | Self-contained diesel | 0.1 – 0.5 MW | Emergency/blackout |
| **Harbor Generator** | Diesel | 0.2 – 1 MW | Cold ironing, port stay |
| **Fuel Cell / Battery Hybrid** | PEM/Li-ion | 0.1 – 5 MW | Modern eco-ships (LNG, hybrid ferries) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Use `\vert` for absolute values)

| Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $D = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{NL}} \times 100$ | Speed droop / regulation | $\vert \% \vert$ |
| $f = \frac{p \cdot N}{120}$ | Frequency from speed & poles | Hz |
| $P_{share} = P_{total} \cdot \frac{1/D_i}{\sum (1/D_j)}$ | Load share with mismatched droop | kW / MW |
| $k_W = \frac{\Delta f \vert_{full\;load}}{f_{rated}} \approx D$ | Static load-frequency constant | p.u. |
| $\frac{\Delta f}{f} = -\frac{\Delta P}{P_{rating}} \cdot D$ | Linearized load-frequency relationship | p.u. |
| $I_{synch} = \frac{V_T - E_f \angle \delta}{X_s}$ | Synchronizing current (briefly) | A |

> [!NOTE]
> The load-frequency relation $\frac{\Delta f}{f} = -D \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{P_{rated}}$ is the **linearized droop equation** that KTU examiners frequently test in numerical problems.

## 2.5 Why Speed Droop is *Essential* in Marine Plants

1. **Prevents Load Hunting:** Without droop, small disturbances cause continuous load oscillation between parallel sets.
2. **Enables Proportional Sharing:** Each machine finds its natural share without complex load-sharing controllers.
3. **Provides Frequency Reference:** The droop line itself acts as a frequency-power transducer.
4. **Fault Stability:** During a large motor start, the temporary frequency dip is bounded by droop, giving time for automatic load shedding.
5. **Satisfies Classification Rules:** DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, BV, and IRS all require droop settings within **2.5% – 5%** for class-approved parallel operation.

---

## 2.6 Comparison: Droop vs Isochronous in Marine Context

| Feature | Droop Governor | Isochronous Governor |
|---|---|---|
| Droop value | 2.5% – 5% | 0% |
| Parallel operation | **Stable, self-regulating** | Unstable without secondary control |
| Frequency accuracy | Slight droop from nominal | **Exactly nominal** |
| Used as | Load-sharing master/slave | **Stand-alone** or master in hybrid |
| KTU exam relevance | **High** (parallel operation focus) | Moderate (single-unit stand-alone) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Load-Sharing Equation

**Statement:** Two generators A and B, with droops $D_A$ and $D_B$, operate in parallel at a common bus frequency $f_{bus}$ (or common speed $N_{bus}$). Prove that the load is shared in inverse proportion to droop (assuming equal base speeds).

### Step 1 — Write the droop line equation for each generator

The droop characteristic of a generator is a straight line from $(0, N_{NL})$ to $(P_{rated}, N_{FL})$ in the Power–Speed plane.

For Generator A:

$$\frac{N_{NL,A} - N_{bus}}{N_{NL,A} - N_{FL,A}} = \frac{P_A}{P_{A,rated}}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for the load $P_A$

$$P_A = P_{A,rated} \cdot \frac{N_{NL,A} - N_{bus}}{N_{NL,A} - N_{FL,A}}$$

Similarly for Generator B:

$$P_B = P_{B,rated} \cdot \frac{N_{NL,B} - N_{bus}}{N_{NL,B} - N_{FL,B}}$$

### Step 3 — Introduce droop percentage

By definition:

$$D_A = \frac{N_{NL,A} - N_{FL,A}}{N_{NL,A}}$$

Therefore:

$$N_{NL,A} - N_{FL,A} = D_A \cdot N_{NL,A}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute back

$$P_A = P_{A,rated} \cdot \frac{N_{NL,A} - N_{bus}}{D_A \cdot N_{NL,A}}$$

$$P_A = \frac{P_{A,rated}}{D_A} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{N_{bus}}{N_{NL,A}}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Form the ratio $P_A / P_B$

$$\frac{P_A}{P_B} = \frac{P_{A,rated}}{P_{B,rated}} \cdot \frac{D_B}{D_A}$$

### Step 6 — Apply the total power constraint

For the parallel system:

$$P_{total} = P_A + P_B$$

Solving simultaneously:

$$P_A = P_{total} \cdot \frac{P_{A,rated}/D_A}{P_{A,rated}/D_A + P_{B,rated}/D_B}$$

### Step 7 — Special Case: Equal Ratings

If $P_{A,rated} = P_{B,rated} = P_{rated}$:

$$\boxed{P_A = P_{total} \cdot \frac{1/D_A}{1/D_A + 1/D_B} = P_{total} \cdot \frac{D_B}{D_A + D_B}}$$

$$\boxed{P_B = P_{total} \cdot \frac{D_A}{D_A + D_B}}$$

**Conclusion:** With equal ratings, load is shared in **inverse proportion to droop**. Lower-droop generator takes more load. ∎

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Board Exam Style)

**Problem:** Two diesel generators G1 and G2 operate in parallel.
- G1: 1500 kVA, droop = 4%
- G2: 1000 kVA, droop = 5%
- Power factor = 0.8 (lagging)
- Total active load on the bus: 1800 kW

**Find:** (a) Load share of each generator in kW.
          (b) What happens if G1's droop is reduced to 3% with the same total load?

### Solution (a):

Step 1 — Convert ratings to kW (assume base kW ≈ kVA for simplicity, since droop usually refers to active load fraction):

$$P_{G1,rated} = 1500 \cdot 0.8 = 1200 \; kW$$
$$P_{G2,rated} = 1000 \cdot 0.8 = 800 \; kW$$

Step 2 — Apply the load-share formula:

$$P_{G1} = 1800 \cdot \frac{1200/4}{1200/4 + 800/5} = 1800 \cdot \frac{300}{300 + 160} = 1800 \cdot \frac{300}{460}$$

$$P_{G1} = 1173.91 \; kW \quad \text{(within its 1200 kW rating ✓)}$$

$$P_{G2} = 1800 - 1173.91 = 626.09 \; kW \quad \text{(within its 800 kW rating ✓)}$$

### Solution (b):

If G1's droop becomes 3%:

$$P_{G1} = 1800 \cdot \frac{1200/3}{1200/3 + 800/5} = 1800 \cdot \frac{400}{400 + 160} = 1800 \cdot \frac{400}{560}$$

$$P_{G1} = 1285.71 \; kW \quad \text{(exceeds 1200 kW rating ⚠️)}$$

**Interpretation:** G1 would **overload** by ~85 kW. The Automatic Load Limiter (ALL) or the engine's electronic governor would intervene to prevent sustained overload. This demonstrates why droop settings are not freely adjustable during operation — they are preset at commissioning.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Power Generation in Ships

| Aspect | Main D/G Set | Shaft Generator | Emergency D/G | Fuel Cell / Battery |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Prime Mover | 4-stroke diesel | Main engine shaft | Dedicated diesel | Electrochemical |
| Speed (rpm) | 720 / 900 / 1000 | Variable (PWM/Converter) | 1500 / 1800 | N/A (DC) |
| Typical Count | 3–4 | 1–2 | 1 | Modular stack |
| Parallelable | Yes (governor sync) | Limited | Yes (auto-start) | Via DC-link inverter |
| Droop mode | **Yes (active)** | Power take-off control | Yes (after blackout start) | Virtual droop (inverter) |
| KTU weightage | **Highest** | High | Medium | Emerging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern ship installations increasingly use **virtual droop** in inverter-based resources (battery/fuel cell) to emulate the synchronous generator droop characteristic — this allows them to participate in load sharing without rotating mass. The KTU 2024 syllabus includes this as an emerging area.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Droop Characteristic with Parallel Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PrimeMover1[Diesel Engine 1] --> Gov1[Governor 1: Droop D1]
    Gov1 --> Alt1[Alternator 1]
    PrimeMover2[Diesel Engine 2] --> Gov2[Governor 2: Droop D2]
    Gov2 --> Alt2[Alternator 2]
    Alt1 --> BusBar[Main Switchboard / Busbar]
    Alt2 --> BusBar
    BusBar --> FreqSense[Frequency / Speed Sensor]
    FreqSense --> Gov1
    FreqSense --> Gov2
    BusBar --> ShipLoad[Ship Load: Motors, Lighting, Navigation]
    Gov1 --> DroopAdj[Droop Adjustment 2.5 to 5 percent]
    Gov2 --> DroopAdj
    classDef droopBox fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class Gov1,Gov2,DroopAdj droopBox
    classDef busBox fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class BusBar,ShipLoad,FreqSense busBox
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Governor Droop Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SpeedRef[Reference Speed N0] --> Compare[Comparator]
    ActualSpeed[Actual Speed N] --> Compare
    Compare --> Error[Error Signal: N0 minus N]
    Error --> Amplify[Amplifier / Hydraulic Stage]
    Amplify --> FuelInj[Fuel Injection Control]
    FuelInj --> Engine[Diesel Engine Torque]
    Engine --> ActualSpeed
    DroopFB[Droop Feedback: 2.5 to 5 percent slope] --> Compare
    LoadChange[Load Change Event] --> DroopFB
    classDef fb fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class DroopFB,Compare,Error fb
    classDef out fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class Engine,ActualSpeed out
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Ship Power Generation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TopLevel[Ship Electrical Plant] --> Tier1[Tier 1: Main Diesel Generators]
    TopLevel --> Tier2[Tier 2: Shaft Generators]
    TopLevel --> Tier3[Tier 3: Emergency Generators]
    TopLevel --> Tier4[Tier 4: Hybrid Sources]
    Tier1 --> MainSwbd[Main Switchboard 440V or 6.6kV]
    Tier2 --> MainSwbd
    Tier3 --> EmSwbd[Emergency Switchboard]
    Tier4 --> DCBus[DC Link / Battery Bus]
    DCBus --> PowerConv[Power Conversion Inverter]
    PowerConv --> MainSwbd
    MainSwbd --> LoadCenter[Load Centres]
    EmSwbd --> EmLoad[Critical Safety Loads]
    LoadCenter --> SL1[Propulsion Auxiliaries]
    LoadCenter --> SL2[Cargo Systems]
    LoadCenter --> SL3[Hotel / Accommodation]

```

> [!NOTE]
> The above block diagrams are drawn in Mermaid `flowchart` syntax. They map the **system-level functional architecture** of speed droop feedback and ship power generation hierarchy — a key method for representing complex physical interactions in KTU board answers when physical sketches are not feasible.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**
Define **speed droop** of a prime-mover governor. Why is it essential for parallel operation of shipboard alternators?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Speed droop is the intentional drop in steady-state speed of a prime-mover-governed alternator from no-load to full-load, expressed as a percentage of no-load speed:
$$D = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{NL}} \times 100\%$$
- **(1 mark)** Marine governor droop is typically set between **2.5% and 5%**.
- **(1 mark)** It is essential because it provides a *negative feedback characteristic* — if one generator tends to take more load, its speed drops slightly, which causes the governor to command less fuel, thereby naturally limiting its share and enabling stable proportional load sharing between parallel sets.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**
List the **four major sources of electrical power generation** on a modern ship and state the typical rating range of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **Main Diesel Generators** (4-stroke medium-speed diesel + synchronous alternator): 0.5 – 6 MW.
2. **Shaft Generators** (driven by main propulsion shaft via PTI/PTO): 1 – 25 MW.
3. **Emergency Diesel Generators** (self-contained, auto-start within 45 s): 0.1 – 0.5 MW.
4. **Hybrid/Booster Sources** (fuel cells, battery energy storage, shaft motor/generator): 0.1 – 5 MW.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse *speed droop* (governor characteristic, %) with *voltage regulation* (alternator characteristic, %). These are **independent** quantities. Also, do not assume equal kVA = equal kW — always multiply by **power factor** for active power load sharing. Marks are routinely deducted for skipping the droop equation derivation.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, Module 4]**

(a) **Derive** the load-sharing expression for two alternators of equal rating operating in parallel, with droops $D_1$ and $D_2$ respectively. Show clearly that load is divided in **inverse proportion to droop**. **(7 marks)**

(b) Two shipboard alternators G1 (2000 kVA) and G2 (1500 kVA) operate in parallel at 0.8 power factor lagging. Their governor droops are 3% and 5% respectively. The total active load is 2500 kW. Compute the active power share of each generator and comment on whether any overload occurs. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — Derivation

Starting from the droop characteristic of each machine:

$$\frac{N_{NL,i} - N_{bus}}{N_{NL,i} - N_{FL,i}} = \frac{P_i}{P_{i,rated}}$$

With $D_i = \frac{N_{NL,i} - N_{FL,i}}{N_{NL,i}}$, we get:

$$P_i = \frac{P_{i,rated}}{D_i} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{N_{bus}}{N_{NL,i}}\right)$$

For equal ratings ($P_{1,rated} = P_{2,rated} = P_r$) and equal no-load speeds ($N_{NL,1} = N_{NL,2} = N_{NL}$):

$$\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \frac{D_2}{D_1}$$

With $P_1 + P_2 = P_{total}$:

$$\boxed{P_1 = P_{total} \cdot \frac{D_2}{D_1 + D_2} \quad ; \quad P_2 = P_{total} \cdot \frac{D_1}{D_1 + D_2}}$$

This proves inverse proportionality to droop. **(7 marks — 2 for definition, 3 for derivation, 2 for final boxed result)**

#### Model Solution (b) — Numerical

Given: G1 = 2000 kVA, G2 = 1500 kVA, pf = 0.8, $D_1$ = 3%, $D_2$ = 5%, $P_{total}$ = 2500 kW.

**Step 1:** Active power ratings:
$$P_{1,rated} = 2000 \cdot 0.8 = 1600 \; kW$$
$$P_{2,rated} = 1500 \cdot 0.8 = 1200 \; kW$$

**Step 2:** Compute weighted terms $P_i / D_i$:
$$\frac{P_{1,rated}}{D_1} = \frac{1600}{3} = 533.33$$
$$\frac{P_{2,rated}}{D_2} = \frac{1200}{5} = 240.00$$
$$\text{Sum} = 773.33$$

**Step 3:** Apply load-share formula:
$$P_1 = 2500 \cdot \frac{533.33}{773.33} = 1724.14 \; kW$$
$$P_2 = 2500 \cdot \frac{240.00}{773.33} = 775.86 \; kW$$

**Step 4:** Overload check:
- G1: 1724.14 kW ≤ 1600 kW rating → **OVERLOAD by 124 kW (7.76%)** ⚠️
- G2: 775.86 kW ≤ 1200 kW rating → OK ✓

**Step 5:** Comment: G1 would experience sustained overload. The **Automatic Load Sharing (ALS)** system or the engine protection module would either:
- Increase droop of G1 back to its design value, or
- Shed non-essential loads via the **Prefential Trip / Load Shedding** scheme, or
- Start a standby generator.

**(7 marks — 2 for ratings, 2 for calculation, 2 for overload comment, 1 for protection system mention)**

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, Module 4 — Choice B]**

(a) Explain with a **block diagram** the principle of **speed droop feedback** in a hydraulic governor for a marine diesel generator. Show how load sharing is achieved between two parallel alternators. **(7 marks)**

(b) A 4-pole, 50 Hz, 3-phase marine alternator rated 2500 kVA is driven by a diesel engine with governor droop of 4%. The no-load speed is 1515 rpm. Calculate the **full-load speed**, the **full-load frequency**, and the **synchronous reactance per phase** if the rated voltage is 6.6 kV (line-to-line) and the steady-state short-circuit current is 3.5 times the rated current. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — Block Diagram + Explanation

**Block Diagram:** (Same structure as Section 4.1, drawn in exam sheet.)

The governor consists of:
1. **Centrifugal flyweight speed sensor** coupled to the alternator shaft.
2. **Comparator** (floating lever) that compares actual speed with reference.
3. **Hydraulic servo amplifier** that boosts the error signal.
4. **Fuel rack actuator** that controls diesel fuel injection.
5. **Droop feedback linkage** (a second lever with adjustable fulcrum) that introduces a deliberate negative feedback proportional to fuel rack position (i.e., to load).

**Operating Principle:**
- When load increases, electromagnetic braking torque increases → speed tends to fall.
- Centrifugal weights move inward, opening the pilot valve.
- Hydraulic oil moves the power piston, increasing fuel injection.
- The droop linkage *simultaneously* shifts the reference downward by an amount proportional to fuel — this prevents full speed recovery.
- Result: speed stabilises at a value *slightly below* no-load, with the offset proportional to load.

**Parallel Operation:**
- Both generators share a common bus frequency.
- The generator with *less droop* tends to take *more load* (its speed drops less, so it remains closer to the common bus frequency).
- Stable equilibrium is reached when the bus frequency corresponds to the intersection of the two droop lines. **(7 marks — 2 diagram, 3 explanation, 2 parallel operation)**

#### Model Solution (b) — Numerical

**Given:** $P$ = 4 poles, $f_{rated}$ = 50 Hz, $S_{rated}$ = 2500 kVA, $D$ = 4%, $N_{NL}$ = 1515 rpm, $V_L$ = 6.6 kV, $I_{sc}/I_{rated}$ = 3.5.

**Step 1 — Full-load speed:**
$$D = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{NL}} \times 100$$

$$0.04 = \frac{1515 - N_{FL}}{1515}$$

$$N_{FL} = 1515 (1 - 0.04) = 1515 \cdot 0.96$$

$$\boxed{N_{FL} = 1454.4 \; rpm}$$

**Step 2 — Full-load frequency:**
$$f_{FL} = \frac{P \cdot N_{FL}}{120} = \frac{4 \cdot 1454.4}{120}$$

$$\boxed{f_{FL} = 48.48 \; Hz}$$

(Note: 4% droop gives a 4% frequency drop from 50 Hz, i.e., $50 \cdot 0.96 = 48$ Hz. The small discrepancy is due to rounded speed.)

**Step 3 — Synchronous reactance per phase:**

Rated phase voltage:
$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{6600}{\sqrt{3}} = 3810.5 \; V$$

Rated phase current:
$$I_{rated} = \frac{S_{rated}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L} = \frac{2500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 6600} = 218.69 \; A$$

Short-circuit current:
$$I_{sc} = 3.5 \cdot I_{rated} = 3.5 \cdot 218.69 = 765.42 \; A$$

Synchronous impedance (assuming $E_f \approx V_{ph}$ for unsaturated, or using rated voltage as base):
$$Z_s = \frac{V_{ph}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{3810.5}{765.42}$$

$$Z_s = 4.978 \; \Omega / \text{phase}$$

For a cylindrical-rotor marine alternator, $X_s \approx Z_s$ (since $R_a$ is negligible). Therefore:

$$\boxed{X_s \approx 4.98 \; \Omega / \text{phase}}$$

**(7 marks — 2 for speed, 2 for frequency, 3 for reactance calculation)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save before exam!):**

- [ ] **Definition:** Speed droop = drop in steady-state speed from no-load to full-load, expressed as a percentage of no-load speed.
- [ ] **Standard marine droop range:** **2.5% – 5%** (IACS UR M3 / DNV / Lloyd's).
- [ ] **Purpose:** Enables **stable proportional load sharing** between parallel alternators and prevents **load hunting**.
- [ ] **Load-share formula (equal ratings):** $P_1 = P_{total} \cdot \frac{D_2}{D_1 + D_2}$ — **inverse proportionality to droop**.
- [ ] **Load-share formula (unequal ratings):** $P_1 = P_{total} \cdot \frac{P_{1,r}/D_1}{P_{1,r}/D_1 + P_{2,r}/D_2}$.
- [ ] **Frequency relation:** $f_{FL} = f_{rated} \cdot (1 - D)$.
- [ ] **Speed–frequency link:** $f = \frac{P \cdot N}{120}$ → a 4-pole, 50 Hz machine runs at **1500 rpm** synchronously.
- [ ] **Marine generation sources:** Main D/G, Shaft Generator, Emergency D/G, Hybrid (fuel cell/battery).
- [ ] **Governor types:** Mechanical-hydraulic, Electrohydraulic (E/H), Electronic (DEGO).
- [ ] **Isochronous vs Droop:** Isochronous (0%) for stand-alone; Droop (2.5–5%) for parallel.
- [ ] **Virtual droop:** Modern inverter-based sources (battery/fuel cell) emulate droop to share load.
- [ ] **Automatic Load Shedding:** Triggers at ~85–90% of rated frequency (typically 42.5 Hz on a 50 Hz system) to save essential services.
- [ ] **Class rule reference:** Generator droop must be tested during sea trials and recorded in the **Electrical Trial Log**.
- [ ] **Never confuse:** Speed droop (governor) ≠ Voltage regulation (alternator) — independent quantities.
- [ ] **Synchronous reactance quick check:** $X_s = \frac{V_{ph}}{I_{sc}}$ with $I_{sc}$ measured at short-circuit test.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Factor Improvement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Power Factor Improvement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Factor (PF) Improvement** in a marine electrical power system is the deliberate application of leading reactive power sources (typically shunt-connected capacitor banks or over-excited synchronous machines) across the main switchboard, emergency switchboard, or large load busbars to counteract the lagging reactive power demand of inductive shipboard loads, thereby raising the overall displacement power factor (cos φ) toward unity.

Mathematically, the power factor is the cosine of the phase angle between the fundamental-frequency line voltage and the fundamental-frequency line current drawn by a load or by the entire vessel:

$$\cos\phi = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}}$$

Where:
- **P** = Active (real) power in **kW** — does the useful mechanical work (turning propellers, running pumps, navigation equipment).
- **Q** = Reactive power in **kVAr** — sustains the magnetic and electric fields in inductive/capacitive devices.
- **S** = Apparent power in **kVA** — what the generators and cables must actually supply.
- **φ** = Phase angle between voltage and current phasors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PENST524 – Module 4):** Power factor correction is a mandatory design consideration in the Marine Electrical Plant because low PF is the single largest cause of unnecessarily oversized generators, oversized busbars, and overheating main cable runs on commercial vessels (SOLAS / Class notation requirements).

## 1.2 Why Power Factor is a Critical Problem on Ships

A ship is essentially a self-contained **island micro-grid** powered by a limited number of diesel generators (DGs) or shaft generators. Unlike a shore utility, the ship cannot "borrow" reactive power from a vast interconnected grid. The consequences of a low power factor on board are therefore amplified:

1. **Generator kVA Loading:** Generators are rated in **kVA**, not kW. A low PF load forces the alternator to deliver more current for the same shaft power, risking over-current trips and reducing the active-power capability of the set.
2. **Conductor Heating (I²R Losses):** Increased current means squared-increase in copper losses, especially critical in long trunking runs from the engine room to the bow thruster or deck machinery.
3. **Voltage Drop in Switchboards:** Heavy currents cause greater voltage depression at remote consumers (e.g., the bridge radar or gyro compass), which can lead to misoperation.
4. **Harbour / Shore-Supply Penalty:** When berthed, many ports levy a reactive-energy charge (or restrict available shore-supply kVA) based on PF. Improving PF reduces the bill.
5. **Class Society & SOLAS Compliance:** Overloaded alternators and overheated cables can violate class rules (DNV, Lloyd’s, ABS, BV), jeopardising the vessel’s certification.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Benchmark:** Modern vessels (container ships, cruise liners, LNG carriers) are designed for a target operating power factor of **0.85 lagging to 0.95 lagging** at the main switchboard under normal seagoing load, with capacitor banks sized to reach at least **0.90 lag** at 80 % generator load.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Beer Mug" Model

Imagine the electrical power flowing into your ship as a glass of beer:

- The **liquid beer** = **Active Power (kW)** — this is what you actually want; it does the work.
- The **white foam on top** = **Reactive Power (kVAr)** — it is necessary to support the beer (you cannot pour a foam-free pint, just as inductive motors need magnetising current), but it does not quench your thirst.
- The **total glass volume (beer + foam)** = **Apparent Power (kVA)** — this is the size of the glass your generator and cables must physically handle.

A low power factor means your glass is mostly foam. **Power factor improvement is the act of skimming off (or suppressing) the foam** so that the same glass can carry more beer. A higher PF means the same generator can deliver more useful kW without needing to be physically larger.

On a ship, the “foam-skimmer” is the **capacitor bank** (or, in older installations, the **over-excited synchronous condenser / synchronous motor**). Capacitors naturally produce the leading reactive current that cancels the lagging magnetising current demanded by motors and transformers.

## 1.4 Nature of the Load on Board (Why PF is Lagging)

Virtually every shipboard consumer is inductive:

| Shipboard Load | Typical Power Factor (Lagging) |
|---|---|
| Three-phase squirrel-cage induction motor (auxiliaries) | 0.70 – 0.85 |
| Single-phase induction motor (galley, laundry) | 0.60 – 0.75 |
| Transformer no-load current | 0.10 – 0.30 |
| Fluorescent / HID lighting ballast | 0.50 – 0.65 |
| Thyristor-controlled converters (rudder, deck cranes) | 0.60 – 0.80 |
| Arc furnaces (not typical at sea, but in shipyards) | 0.70 – 0.85 |

Because the *aggregate* PF is the vector sum of all individual loads, an unprepared switchboard will inevitably see a poor overall PF unless actively corrected.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Phasor Logic of Power Factor Improvement

A lagging load draws current that lags the voltage by angle φ₁. The current has two orthogonal components:

$$\bar{I} = I_{\text{active}} \;(\text{in phase with V}) \;+\; j\,I_{\text{reactive}} \;(\text{lagging V by 90°})$$

When a shunt capacitor is installed, it draws a purely leading current, i.e. a current that *leads* the voltage by 90°. This leading component directly **subtracts** from the lagging reactive component demanded by the inductive loads. The net line current therefore shrinks, and its phase angle with respect to the voltage moves toward zero.

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Identify the load’s reactive demand (Q₁):** From the load’s active power P and original PF cos φ₁, compute Q₁ = P · tan φ₁.
2. **Decide the target PF (cos φ₂):** Usually 0.90, 0.95, or 1.00 depending on class and operational requirements.
3. **Compute target reactive demand (Q₂):** Q₂ = P · tan φ₂, where tan φ₂ corresponds to the new angle.
4. **Capacitor rating (Qc):** The capacitor must supply the difference: Qc = Q₁ − Q₂. Because P is unchanged (the load’s mechanical duty does not change), the active power remains constant.
5. **Verification:** Apparent power drops from S₁ = P / cos φ₁ to S₂ = P / cos φ₂. Line current drops in the same ratio.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** P remains *unchanged* before and after correction. Only Q and S change. The reduction in current is purely a consequence of reduced reactive flow — the same kW is still being delivered.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every equation you need to solve any KTU examination problem on this topic. No vertical pipe `|` characters are used (use `\vert` or `\mid` instead, per the protocol).

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks / When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Apparent Power | $S = V \cdot I$ or $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$ | kVA | Rating of generator/cable |
| 2 | Active Power | $P = S \cos\phi = V I \cos\phi$ | kW | Mechanical load demand |
| 3 | Reactive Power | $Q = S \sin\phi = P \tan\phi = V I \sin\phi$ | kVAr | Magnitude of reactive flow |
| 4 | Original PF | $\cos\phi_1 = P / S_1$ | dimensionless | Given or measured |
| 5 | Target PF | $\cos\phi_2$ | dimensionless | Chosen by designer (0.90, 0.95, 1.0) |
| 6 | Capacitor Reactive Rating | $Q_c = P \left(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2\right)$ | kVAr | The required capacitor bank size |
| 7 | Capacitance (3-φ, Δ-conn.) | $C_{\Delta} = \dfrac{Q_c}{\omega \, V_L^2}$ | F (per phase) | V_L = line-to-line voltage |
| 8 | Capacitance (3-φ, Y-conn.) | $C_{Y} = \dfrac{3 \, Q_c}{\omega \, V_L^2}$ | F (per phase) | V_L = line voltage; per-phase voltage = $V_L/\sqrt{3}$ |
| 9 | New Line Current | $I_2 = P / (\sqrt{3} \, V_L \cos\phi_2)$ | A | After correction |
| 10 | Old Line Current | $I_1 = P / (\sqrt{3} \, V_L \cos\phi_1)$ | A | Before correction |
| 11 | Current Reduction Ratio | $I_2 / I_1 = \cos\phi_1 / \cos\phi_2$ | – | Dimensionless ratio |
| 12 | Power Saved in Cables | $\Delta P_{\text{loss}} = 3 R (I_1^2 - I_2^2)$ | W | I²R saving per phase × 3 |
| 13 | Synchronous Condenser Q | $Q_{\text{SC}} = \dfrac{V \, I_{f,\text{lead}}}{1000}$ | kVAr | Over-excited operation |
| 14 | Resonance Check | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_{\text{eq}} \, C}}$ | Hz | Must differ from 50/60 Hz and any harmonic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The formula $Q_c = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ is the single most-tested expression in KTU board examinations for this module. Memorise it. Also remember to convert angles carefully when one of the PFs is given in percentage form.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Significance

In a ship’s lifecycle, PF improvement produces a measurable economic and safety return:

- **Generator Optimisation:** A vessel fitted with PF correction can specify a smaller alternator (e.g., 750 kVA instead of 1000 kVA for the same 600 kW load at 0.80 PF → 0.90 PF). Smaller alternators are cheaper, lighter, and have lower fuel consumption.
- **Cable De-rating:** Marine cables (especially those routed through high-ambient-temperature compartments) have a derated current-carrying capacity. Reducing current flow lowers the temperature rise, allowing thinner (cheaper) cables to be specified.
- **Harmonic Interaction (Important Caveat):** Capacitor banks can resonate with transformer inductances at specific harmonics produced by variable-frequency drives (VFDs) and thyristor-controlled consumers. Modern shipboard capacitor panels are therefore fitted with **detuning reactors** (forming an *anti-resonant* filter), unless an active harmonic filter is present.

## 2.4 Synchronous Condenser as a Marine PF Device

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synchronous Condenser = Synchronous Motor running on no-load with over-excited DC field.** It behaves as a *controllable* leading-PF device, absorbing reactive power when under-excited and supplying reactive power when over-excited. Historically used on large ships (e.g., passenger liners with heavy lift motors); gradually replaced by static capacitor banks on modern vessels because of lower maintenance and no rotating losses.

The reactive power delivered by a synchronous condenser is:

$$Q_{\text{SC}} = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_{\text{field-limited}}$$

By adjusting the field excitation, the operator can *continuously* vary $Q_{\text{SC}}$ — an advantage capacitor banks do not have. However, the condenser itself consumes real power for friction, windage, and excitation, making it less efficient at small ratings.

## 2.5 Methods of PF Improvement — Summary for KTU

| Method | Principle | Pros | Cons | Typical Marine Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Static Capacitor Bank | Leading VAr from C | Cheap, no moving parts, modular | Stepped (discrete) correction, resonance risk | Main switchboard of container ships, tankers |
| Synchronous Condenser | Over-excited sync motor | Continuously variable, dynamic support | Higher losses, requires DC excitation | Older vessels, very large plants |
| Synchronous Motor driving load | Natural over-excitation | Dual-purpose (motor + PF) | Only useful if the driven load matches motor rating | Large compressors, large pumps |
| Active Harmonic + PF Compensator | IGBT inverter supplies VAr | Harmonic filtering + PF in one unit | Expensive, complex | VFD-heavy vessels (DP drillships, cruise ships) |
