# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Capacitor Rating Equation $Q_c = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$

We begin with the active power P, which is invariant under PF correction. From the right-triangle formed by P, Q, and S (the so-called *power triangle*):

$$\tan\phi = \frac{Q}{P} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; Q = P \tan\phi$$

**Before correction** (load draws Q₁):

$$Q_1 = P \tan\phi_1$$

**After correction** (load only needs Q₂, capacitor supplies the rest):

$$Q_2 = P \tan\phi_2$$

**Capacitor must supply the difference:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_c &= Q_1 - Q_2 \\
&= P \tan\phi_1 - P \tan\phi_2 \\
&= P \left( \tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2 \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sign Convention Check:** Because $\cos\phi_2 > \cos\phi_1$ when we are *improving* the PF, we have $\phi_2 < \phi_1$, so $\tan\phi_1 > \tan\phi_2$, and $Q_c > 0$. This confirms that the capacitor rating is positive (it really is supplying reactive power).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Three-Phase Delta-Connected Capacitance

We need the capacitance that, when connected in delta across the line-to-line voltage $V_L$, will deliver the reactive power $Q_c$.

For one delta-connected capacitor, the per-phase reactive power is:

$$Q_{\text{phase}} = \dfrac{V_L^2}{X_c} = V_L^2 \cdot \omega C_{\Delta}$$

(where the phase voltage equals the line voltage in a delta connection).

Total three-phase reactive power supplied:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_c &= 3 \cdot Q_{\text{phase}} \\
    &= 3 \cdot V_L^2 \cdot \omega C_{\Delta}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $C_{\Delta}$:

$$C_{\Delta} = \dfrac{Q_c}{3 \, \omega \, V_L^2}$$

This is the **per-phase** capacitance in a delta-connected bank. A common error students make is omitting the factor of 3 — always confirm whether the formula in your textbook gives per-phase or total.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Three-Phase Star-Connected Capacitance

In a star connection, the voltage across each capacitor is the phase voltage $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$. The per-phase reactive power is:

$$Q_{\text{phase}} = \omega C_Y \left(\dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 = \omega C_Y \cdot \dfrac{V_L^2}{3}$$

Total three-phase reactive power:

$$Q_c = 3 \cdot Q_{\text{phase}} = \omega C_Y \cdot V_L^2$$

Solving for $C_Y$:

$$C_Y = \dfrac{Q_c}{\omega \, V_L^2}$$

**Compare with delta:**

$$\dfrac{C_Y}{C_{\Delta}} = \dfrac{1/(\omega V_L^2)}{1/(3 \omega V_L^2)} = 3$$

So **a star-connected bank requires three times the per-phase capacitance of a delta-connected bank**, but each Y-capacitor is rated at the lower phase voltage. This is why high-voltage marine capacitor panels are often star-connected (lower voltage class per unit, cheaper dielectric), while low-voltage panels are often delta-connected (fewer capacitors needed).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Reduction in Line Current and Cable Losses

Line current drawn from the generator:

$$I = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3}\, V_L \cos\phi}$$

Ratio of currents after and before correction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{I_2}{I_1} &= \dfrac{P / (\sqrt{3} V_L \cos\phi_2)}{P / (\sqrt{3} V_L \cos\phi_1)} \\
&= \dfrac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The cable (per phase) carries resistance R. Total three-phase I²R loss:

$$P_{\text{loss}} = 3 \, R \, I^2$$

The loss reduction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_{\text{loss}} &= 3 R \left( I_1^2 - I_2^2 \right) \\
&= 3 R \, I_1^2 \left[ 1 - \left( \dfrac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2} \right)^2 \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the *continuous* power saved, which over a year-long voyage can amount to a substantial fuel saving.

## 3.5 Worked Example 1 — Capacitor Bank Sizing

**Problem (KTU-Style):** A bulk carrier’s main switchboard delivers **800 kW** to shipboard auxiliaries at an overall power factor of **0.72 lagging**. The vessel is being retrofitted with a static capacitor bank to improve the power factor to **0.92 lagging**. The switchboard operates at **440 V, 50 Hz, 3-phase**. Calculate (a) the required capacitor bank rating, (b) the new line current, and (c) the per-phase capacitance if the bank is connected in delta.

**Step 1 — Compute the two phase angles.**

$$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.72) = 43.9455°$$

$$\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.92) = 23.0739°$$

**Step 2 — Compute the corresponding tangents.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\phi_1 &= \tan(43.9455°) = 0.9635 \\
\tan\phi_2 &= \tan(23.0739°) = 0.4259
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the capacitor rating formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_c &= P \left( \tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2 \right) \\
    &= 800 \times (0.9635 - 0.4259) \\
    &= 800 \times 0.5376 \\
    &= 430.08 \text{ kVAr}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{Q_c \approx 430 \text{ kVAr}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the new line current at 0.92 PF.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_2 &= \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3}\, V_L \cos\phi_2} \\
    &= \dfrac{800 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 440 \times 0.92} \\
    &= \dfrac{800\,000}{701.12} \\
    &= 1141.04 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{I_2 \approx 1141 \text{ A}}$$

For comparison, the **original** current would have been:

$$I_1 = \dfrac{800\,000}{\sqrt{3} \times 440 \times 0.72} = 1457.77 \text{ A}$$

So the line current drops by about **316 A** — a 21.7 % reduction.

**Step 5 — Delta-connected per-phase capacitance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\Delta} &= \dfrac{Q_c}{3 \, \omega \, V_L^2} \\
&= \dfrac{430.08 \times 10^3}{3 \times 2\pi \times 50 \times 440^2} \\
&= \dfrac{430\,080}{3 \times 314.159 \times 193\,600} \\
&= \dfrac{430\,080}{1.8248 \times 10^8} \\
&= 2.357 \times 10^{-3} \text{ F}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{C_{\Delta} \approx 2357 \;\mu\text{F per phase}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Self-Check:** If you used a star connection instead, the per-phase capacitance would be $3 \times 2357 \approx 7071 \;\mu\text{F}$ — three times higher, as expected from the derivation.

## 3.6 Worked Example 2 — Annual Cable Loss Saving

Continuing from Worked Example 1, suppose the equivalent resistance per phase of the bus-tie and feeder cable run is **R = 0.01 Ω**. Estimate the annual I²R loss saving if the vessel operates **5500 hours/year**.

**Before correction:**

$$P_{\text{loss,1}} = 3 \times 0.01 \times (1457.77)^2 = 63\,763 \text{ W} = 63.76 \text{ kW}$$

**After correction:**

$$P_{\text{loss,2}} = 3 \times 0.01 \times (1141.04)^2 = 39\,062 \text{ W} = 39.06 \text{ kW}$$

**Loss reduction:**

$$\Delta P_{\text{loss}} = 63.76 - 39.06 = 24.70 \text{ kW}$$

**Annual energy saved:**

$$E_{\text{saved}} = 24.70 \times 5500 = 135\,850 \text{ kWh/year}$$

At a fuel cost equivalent of approximately ₹10 / kWh (or any user-defined rate), the annual savings is on the order of **₹13.6 lakh/year** for this vessel — clearly justifying the PF-correction investment.

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Capacitor Bank | Synchronous Condenser | Active PF Compensator |
|---|---|---|---|
| Response Time | Stepped (cycle-level switching) | Continuous but slow (seconds) | Instantaneous (msec) |
| Harmonic Impact | Risk of resonance | Immune | Filters harmonics actively |
| Maintenance | Very low | High (brushes, bearings) | Moderate (electronics) |
| Initial Cost (per kVAr) | Low | High | Very high |
| Footprint / Weight | Small | Large rotating mass | Moderate |
| Energy Loss | ~0.5 W/kVAr | ~15–30 W/kVAr | ~10–20 W/kVAr |
| Typical Marine Application | Auxiliaries, hotel loads, deck machinery | Older passenger ships, large thrusters | DP vessels, all-electric ships with VFDs |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Ship Electrical Plant] --> Q1{Are VFDs and<br>large converters used?}

    Q1 -- Yes --> ACTV[Active Harmonic + PF Compensator]
    Q2 -- Yes --> STAT[Static Capacitor Bank with detuning reactors]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is the load large<br>and continuous?}

This block-level flow shows the phasor relationships *before* and *after* capacitor switching, as would appear on a marine power-quality analyser.

flowchart LR
    V[Reference Phasor: V at 0 deg] --> IA[Active component of I: I cos phi]
    V --> IB[Reactive component of I: I sin phi lagging]

    IB --> SUM1

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    L --> C[Capacitor Bank C]
    GRID --> HARM[Harmonic Source: VFD or 6-pulse Converter]
    HARM -.->|Injects 5th, 7th, 11th harmonic currents| GRID
    L -.->|Shifts resonance below 5th harmonic| C
    C --> NET[Net: Leading fundamental VAr, negligible harmonic amplification]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define power factor. Why is power factor correction essential in a ship’s electrical plant?**

**Model Answer:**

Power factor is the cosine of the phase angle between the fundamental-frequency line voltage and the line current of an AC load. It is the ratio of active power (kW) to apparent power (kVA), $\cos\phi = P / S$.

In a ship’s electrical plant, power factor correction is essential because:

1. Generators are rated in kVA; a low PF reduces the usable kW per generator and can cause over-current trips.
2. Heavy currents cause excessive I²R losses and voltage drop in long marine cables, overheating switchgear and busbars.
3. Many harbours levy a reactive-energy surcharge during shore-supply operation.
4. Class societies (DNV, Lloyd’s, ABS) penalise overloaded alternators and overheated cables during survey.
5. Higher PF enables smaller alternators and thinner cables to be specified, reducing weight and fuel.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is a synchronous condenser? How does it act as a leading power factor device?**

**Model Answer:**

A synchronous condenser is a synchronous motor that runs on no mechanical load, whose only purpose is to absorb or supply reactive power to the bus to which it is connected.

When its DC field winding is **over-excited** (i.e., the field current exceeds that required for unity PF at the same armature current), the machine draws a *leading* current from the bus, behaving as a capacitor. By varying the field excitation, the operator can continuously regulate the magnitude of the leading kVAr supplied. It is used for power factor correction in large marine plants where smooth, dynamic control of reactive power is required.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** A ship’s main switchboard supplies a total load of **1200 kW** at a power factor of **0.75 lagging**. Design a capacitor bank to improve the PF to **0.95 lagging**. The system voltage is **6.6 kV, 50 Hz, 3-phase**. Determine:
- (i) The reactive power rating of the capacitor bank.
- (ii) The per-phase capacitance if connected in **star**.
- (iii) The reduction in line current.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss, with suitable phasor and circuit diagrams, **two methods** of power factor improvement suitable for marine electrical plants. Compare their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part A(a)

**Given:** P = 1200 kW, $\cos\phi_1 = 0.75$ lag, $\cos\phi_2 = 0.95$ lag, V_L = 6.6 kV, f = 50 Hz, 3-φ.

**Step 1 — Compute the two phase angles.**

$$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.75) = 41.4096°$$

$$\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.1949°$$

**Step 2 — Compute the tangents.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\phi_1 &= \tan(41.4096°) = 0.8819 \\
\tan\phi_2 &= \tan(18.1949°) = 0.3287
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Capacitor bank rating (Part i).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_c &= P (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2) \\
    &= 1200 \times (0.8819 - 0.3287) \\
    &= 1200 \times 0.5532 \\
    &= 663.86 \text{ kVAr}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{Q_c \approx 664 \text{ kVAr}}$$

**Step 4 — Per-phase capacitance in star (Part ii).**

Using the formula $C_Y = \dfrac{Q_c}{\omega \, V_L^2}$ (note: this gives per-phase capacitance directly for a Y-connected bank, with $V_L$ being the line voltage):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_Y &= \dfrac{663.86 \times 10^3}{2\pi \times 50 \times (6600)^2} \\
    &= \dfrac{663\,860}{314.159 \times 43\,560\,000} \\
    &= \dfrac{663\,860}{1.3684 \times 10^{10}} \\
    &= 4.85 \times 10^{-5} \text{ F}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{C_Y \approx 48.5 \;\mu\text{F per phase}}$$

**Step 5 — Reduction in line current (Part iii).**

**Before correction:**

$$I_1 = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} V_L \cos\phi_1} = \dfrac{1\,200\,000}{\sqrt{3} \times 6600 \times 0.75} = 139.95 \text{ A}$$

**After correction:**

$$I_2 = \dfrac{1\,200\,000}{\sqrt{3} \times 6600 \times 0.95} = 110.49 \text{ A}$$

**Reduction:**

$$\Delta I = 139.95 - 110.49 = 29.46 \text{ A} \quad (\text{about } 21.05\%)$$

$$\boxed{\text{Line current reduces by } \approx 29.5 \text{ A, or } 21.1\%}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Part A(b)

**Method 1 — Static Capacitor Bank:**

A bank of three-phase capacitors is connected in shunt at the main switchboard. Each capacitor branch produces a leading current of $I_c = V / X_c$ that is 90° ahead of the voltage. The leading current subtracts vectorially from the lagging magnetising current demanded by inductive loads, reducing the net reactive current and improving the PF.

**Advantages:** Low cost, no moving parts, modular, easy to expand, high efficiency (losses < 0.5 %), small footprint.

**Limitations:** Correction is in discrete steps (each contactor stage adds a fixed kVAr), risk of harmonic resonance with transformer inductance, cannot respond to fast load changes.

**Method 2 — Synchronous Condenser:**

A synchronous motor is run uncoupled, with its DC field over-excited. The machine then draws a leading current proportional to the field excitation. Adjusting the field current allows continuous regulation of the reactive output.

**Advantages:** Continuously variable kVAr, can support system voltage dynamically (extra benefit during short circuits), robust.

**Limitations:** Higher capital cost, requires DC excitation system, has rotational losses (~15–30 W/kVAr), requires regular maintenance of brushes and bearings.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning (Part B Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert PF to angle** — students often use $\cos\phi$ directly in the formula instead of $\tan\phi$. This is the most common error and costs 1–2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Mixing up delta and star capacitance** — always check whether the per-phase or line-to-line voltage is being used. The factor of 3 difference is a guaranteed 2-mark loss if wrong.
> 3. **Dropping units** — write kVAr, μF, and A explicitly. Examiners deduct for missing units.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor √3 in line current** — both numerator and denominator must be dimensionally consistent.
> 5. **Not drawing phasor diagrams in part (b)** — KTU examiners explicitly reward the diagram; missing it typically costs 1–2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a single-line diagram, explain how a capacitor bank is integrated into the main switchboard of a merchant vessel. Discuss the role of detuning reactors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship’s auxiliary load is measured as **450 kVA** at **0.65 power factor lagging** from a **440 V, 50 Hz, 3-phase** main bus. It is required to raise the power factor to **0.88 lagging** using a delta-connected capacitor bank. Calculate:
- (i) The active and reactive power of the original load.
- (ii) The capacitor bank rating.
- (iii) The per-phase capacitance in microfarads.
- (iv) The percentage reduction in line current. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part B(a)

A single-line diagram (text-described for the answer sheet) shows the main switchboard fed by 3 diesel generators through their circuit breakers. A bus-tie connects the main bus to the emergency switchboard. Three capacitor banks of, say, 200 kVAr each, are connected to the main bus through dedicated air-circuit breakers. Each capacitor bank has a set of detuning reactors (typically 6–7 % of capacitive reactance) in series with the capacitors. The detuning reactor shifts the natural resonant frequency of the L-C combination to **below the 5th harmonic** (i.e., to roughly 189 Hz in a 50 Hz system, or 227 Hz in a 60 Hz system), preventing the system from amplifying the 5th harmonic currents produced by VFDs and 6-pulse rectifiers. The combined unit (reactor + capacitor) is called a *detuned filter* or *absorber*. It also limits the magnitude of inrush current when capacitor stages are energised, extending contactor life.

**Role of detuning reactors:**
1. Prevent parallel resonance with system inductance at harmonic frequencies.
2. Limit switching inrush current.
3. Reduce harmonic voltage distortion at the bus.

---

#### Model Solution to Part B(b)

**Given:** S₁ = 450 kVA, $\cos\phi_1 = 0.65$ lag, $\cos\phi_2 = 0.88$ lag, V_L = 440 V, f = 50 Hz, 3-φ, delta-connected capacitors.

**Step 1 — Active power of original load (Part i).**

$$P = S_1 \cos\phi_1 = 450 \times 0.65 = 292.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Reactive power of original load (Part i).**

$$Q_1 = S_1 \sin\phi_1 = 450 \times \sin(\cos^{-1}0.65) = 450 \times 0.7599 = 341.96 \text{ kVAr}$$

**Step 3 — Capacitor bank rating (Part ii).**

$$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.65) = 49.4584°$$

$$\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.88) = 28.3576°$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\phi_1 &= 1.1691 \\
\tan\phi_2 &= 0.5397
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_c &= P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2) \\
    &= 292.5 \times (1.1691 - 0.5397) \\
    &= 292.5 \times 0.6294 \\
    &= 184.10 \text{ kVAr}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{Q_c \approx 184.1 \text{ kVAr}}$$

**Step 4 — Per-phase capacitance in delta (Part iii).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\Delta} &= \dfrac{Q_c}{3 \omega V_L^2} \\
&= \dfrac{184\,100}{3 \times 2\pi \times 50 \times 440^2} \\
&= \dfrac{184\,100}{3 \times 314.159 \times 193\,600} \\
&= \dfrac{184\,100}{1.8248 \times 10^8} \\
&= 1.009 \times 10^{-3} \text{ F}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{C_{\Delta} \approx 1009 \;\mu\text{F per phase}}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage reduction in line current (Part iv).**

$$\dfrac{I_2}{I_1} = \dfrac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2} = \dfrac{0.65}{0.88} = 0.7386$$

$$\text{Reduction} = 1 - 0.7386 = 0.2614 = 26.14\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Line current is reduced by } \approx 26.1\%}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Before you enter the exam hall, make sure you can recall and apply each of the following:**

- **Definition:** PF = cos φ = ratio of active power to apparent power, dimensionless.
- **Why low PF matters on ships:** Generators are kVA-rated; low PF causes over-current, overheating cables, voltage drop, harbour surcharges, and class-rule violations.
- **Single most-important formula:** $Q_c = P \left( \tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2 \right)$ — know it cold, including the angle conversion steps.
- **Capacitance — Delta vs Star:** $C_{\Delta} = \dfrac{Q_c}{3 \omega V_L^2}$ and $C_Y = \dfrac{Q_c}{\omega V_L^2}$. Star requires 3× the capacitance of delta.
- **Line-current reduction:** $\dfrac{I_2}{I_1} = \dfrac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}$.
- **Active power is invariant** under PF correction; only Q and S change.
- **Three main correction methods:** Static capacitor banks (most common), synchronous condensers (older, dynamic), active compensators (modern, harmonic-aware).
- **Detuning reactors:** 6–7 % reactors in series with capacitors prevent harmonic resonance with VFDs/rectifiers.
- **PF target for ships:** 0.85 – 0.95 lagging at the main switchboard under normal seagoing load.
- **Numerical hygiene:** Always carry units (kW, kVAr, kVA, A, μF). Always check whether PF is given as 0.72 or 72 % (a common error).
- **Phasor logic:** Capacitor current *leads* voltage by 90°; it subtracts vectorially from the lagging reactive current of the load.
- **Synchronous condenser:** Synchronous motor on no-load; over-excited = leading kVAr; under-excited = lagging kVAr.
- **Marine-specific applications:** Main switchboard for auxiliaries, harbour/shore supply, deck machinery, bow-thruster feeders, large HVAC plant.
- **Economic justification:** A 0.72 → 0.92 PF improvement can yield > 20 % cable loss reduction and a smaller alternator for the same kW, paying back in 1–3 years.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Tariff Calculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Power Tariff Calculation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Tariff** is the schedule of rates or charges levied by an electricity supply authority (utility company) on different classes of consumers for the electrical energy supplied to them, expressed in monetary units (₹, $, £, etc.) per unit of electrical quantity (kWh, kVA, kW, kVAh).

In the context of **shipboard and shipyard electrical systems**, power tariffs govern the cost of bulk electrical energy procured by the shipyard from the local grid for dry-dock operations, hotel loads during ship-building, and shore-power supplied to vessels in port (commonly called *cold ironing* or *Alternative Maritime Power – AMP*).

Mathematically, a generic tariff structure can be expressed as:

$$
\text{Total Bill} = f(\text{Energy consumed}, \text{Maximum Demand}, \text{Power Factor}, \text{Load Factor})
$$

The unit of electrical energy commercially traded is the **kilowatt-hour (kWh)**, defined as the energy consumed by a 1 kW load running for 1 hour.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PENST524 Module 4):** Students must be able to (a) classify tariff types, (b) derive expressions for each tariff scheme, (c) solve numerical problems on consumer billing, and (d) select the most economical tariff for a given load curve.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Bucket Economics"

Imagine electricity is **water flowing through a pipe** to your home. The electricity board charges you for water in **two distinct ways**:

1. **The size of the pipe (₹ per kW of Maximum Demand):** The bigger the pipe you request, the more the board has to invest in transformers, cables, and substations. So you pay a fixed rental just for keeping that big pipe ready — even if you use it only for 5 minutes a day.

2. **The actual water you drink (₹ per kWh consumed):** This is the variable charge proportional to actual usage.

A **power tariff** is essentially a billing menu that combines these two ideas in different proportions. In a **shipyard**, the "pipe size" is determined by the largest welding transformer + crane + lighting load running simultaneously during peak dry-dock activity, while the "water drunk" is the total kWh over the month.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** A good tariff design simultaneously satisfies **three engineering-economics objectives**: (i) recovers the utility's capital + operating cost, (ii) incentivizes the consumer to maintain a high load factor and good power factor, and (iii) is simple, transparent, and non-discriminatory.

---

## 1.3 Why Tariff Study Matters in Ships & Shipyards

| Application | Tariff Relevance |
|---|---|
| **Shipyard grid import (LT/HT connection)** | The yard pays the state electricity board monthly — correct tariff selection can save **lakhs of ₹/year**. |
| **Shore power to visiting vessels** | The port generates an invoice based on kWh + kVA drawn by the ship's hotel load. |
| **Onboard generator optimization** | Engineers compare cost of running diesel generators vs. shore power; both are tariff-driven. |
| **IMO MARPOL Annex VI compliance** | Shore power tariffs influence a ship's decision to shut down auxiliary engines in port (emission control areas). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Used in Tariff Calculations:**
> - Energy: **kWh** (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ)
> - Power: **kW** (active), **kVA** (apparent), **kVAr** (reactive)
> - Demand: **kVA or kW** measured over a 15-minute (or 30-minute) integration window
> - Power Factor reference: **0.85 to 0.95 (lagging)** depending on utility
> - Load Factor: dimensionless ratio between 0 and 1
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Objectives of an Ideal Tariff

An ideal power tariff must simultaneously satisfy three groups of stakeholders:

1. **The Utility (Supply Authority)** must recover:
   - Fixed charges (interest + depreciation on capital investment in generation, transmission, and distribution).
   - Semi-fixed charges (operation, maintenance, fuel, salaries).
   - A reasonable profit margin (for private utilities) or revenue surplus (for state utilities).

2. **The Consumer** must be charged:
   - Fairly, in proportion to the actual energy used.
   - A rental component that reflects the standing cost of keeping the supply available.

3. **Society at large** must benefit through:
   - Encouragement of energy conservation.
   - Reward for high load factor (smoother demand curve, less peaking plant required).
   - Penalty for poor power factor (which wastes utility network capacity).

---

## 2.2 Classification of Tariffs — Engineering Logic Tree

Tariffs are broadly classified into **simple (single-part)** and **multi-part** structures. The progression below is a logical ladder — each more sophisticated scheme adds an additional billing dimension.

### (A) Flat Demand Rate Tariff
- **Logic:** A fixed amount (₹) is charged per kW of **connected load** (or sometimes per lamp point, per HP of motor, etc.) regardless of actual energy consumed.
- **Why this exists:** Simple to bill; used historically for street lighting, signboards, small domestic loads.
- **Limitation:** Discriminates against consumers with low diversity. A 100 W bulb that runs 12 hours/day pays the same as a 100 W bulb that runs 1 hour/day.

### (B) Straight Meter Rate (Uniform Rate) Tariff
- **Logic:** A flat rate (₹/kWh) is charged for all units consumed, irrespective of quantity.
- **Why this exists:** Simplest energy-based billing; common for residential LT consumers in many states.
- **Limitation:** Discriminates against heavy users (no bulk discount), offers no incentive to improve load factor.

### (C) Block Rate Tariff (Staircase Tariff)
- **Logic:** Energy is divided into successive blocks; rate per kWh changes (decreases typically) as consumption rises to a higher block.
- **Variants:**
  - *Cumulative (telescopic):* Rate in higher block applies to **all** units once threshold is crossed.
  - *Non-cumulative (block):* Rate in higher block applies **only** to the units **within** that block.
- **Why this exists:** Implements social equity — subsidized first block for poor households, market rate for high consumption.

### (D) Two-Part Tariff (Hopkinson Tariff)
- **Logic:** Bill = Fixed Charge (₹/kW of Maximum Demand) + Running Charge (₹/kWh).
- **Why this exists:** Properly apportions both **capacity cost** (kW-related) and **energy cost** (kWh-related). Most widely used industrial tariff in India (HT consumers, shipyards).
- **Limitation:** Requires MD metering → expensive for small consumers.

### (E) Maximum Demand Tariff with Power Factor Clause (Three-Part Tariff)
- **Logic:** Bill = Fixed Charge (₹/kVA of MD) + Running Charge (₹/kWh) + Power Factor Penalty/Reward.
- **Why this exists:** MD in **kVA** directly represents the network capacity the utility must reserve. A consumer with low PF draws more kVA for the same kW → penalized.
- **Why this matters on ships:** Shipboard switchboards operate with lagging PF due to large induction motors; shore-power contracts increasingly use kVA-MD tariffs.

### (F) Three-Part Tariff (Doherty Rate or Block-MD Tariff)
- **Logic:** Bill = Fixed (₹/kW connected) + Running (₹/kWh) + Block-rate × Excess MD above a free MD limit.
- **Why this exists:** Discourages consumers from declaring very high "contract demand" and wasting utility infrastructure.

### (G) Power Factor Tariff
- **Logic:** A separate clause penalizes the consumer if average PF falls below a reference (e.g., 0.85 lag) and rewards improvement.
- **Mechanisms:** (i) kVA-MD tariff, (ii) PF penalty ₹/kVArh, (iii) PF-based rebate on energy charge.

### (H) Time-of-Use (ToU) Tariff
- **Logic:** Different rates for peak hours, off-peak hours, and night hours.
- **Why this exists:** Modern smart-grid implementation; encourages load shifting — critical for shipyards with intermittent heavy crane loads.

### (I) Seasonal Tariff
- **Logic:** Different rates in summer vs. winter.
- **Why this exists:** Caters to seasonal industries (irrigation pumps, shipyards may have peak dry-dock season).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All quantities below are per billing cycle (typically 1 month = 30 days).** All monetary units are in their respective national currency (₹ in India, $ in US, etc.).

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Energy consumed | $E = \int_0^T P(t)\, dt \approx \text{kWh meter reading}$ | kWh | Direct meter reading |
| 2 | Average Load | $\bar{P} = \dfrac{E}{T}$ | kW | $T$ in hours (720 h for 30 days) |
| 3 | Load Factor (LF) | $\text{LF} = \dfrac{\bar{P}}{P_{max}} = \dfrac{E}{P_{max} \times T}$ | dimensionless, $\leq 1$ | Higher LF ⇒ more efficient user |
| 4 | Maximum Demand (MD) | Measured over a 15-min integration window | kW or kVA | Recorded by MD meter |
| 5 | Demand Factor | $\text{DF} = \dfrac{\text{MD}}{\text{Connected Load}}$ | $\leq 1$ | Indicates diversity of use |
| 6 | Diversity Factor | $\text{DivF} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Individual MDs}}{\text{Group MD}}$ | $\geq 1$ | Always $\geq 1$ for grouped loads |
| 7 | Plant Capacity Factor | $\text{PCF} = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{P_{installed} \times 8760}$ | $\leq 1$ | Utility-side metric |
| 8 | Plant Use Factor | $\text{PUF} = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{P_{installed} \times \text{hrs actually used}}$ | $\leq 1$ | Energy per unit time plant actually ran |
| 9 | Flat demand rate bill | $\text{Bill} = R_f \times L_c$ | ₹ | $R_f$ = ₹/kW of connected load, $L_c$ = connected kW |
| 10 | Straight meter bill | $\text{Bill} = R \times E$ | ₹ | $R$ = ₹/kWh, $E$ in kWh |
| 11 | Two-part (Hopkinson) bill | $\text{Bill} = R_c \times \text{MD} + R \times E$ | ₹ | $R_c$ = ₹/kW of MD, $R$ = ₹/kWh |
| 12 | Three-part (kVA-MD) bill | $\text{Bill} = R_{cA} \times \text{MD}_{kVA} + R \times E + \text{PF penalty/rebate}$ | ₹ | $R_{cA}$ = ₹/kVA |
| 13 | Apparent power (for MD in kVA) | $S = \dfrac{P}{\cos\phi} = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$ | kVA | $\phi$ = load power factor angle |
| 14 | Reactive energy | $Q = P \tan\phi$ | kVAr | Used in PF penalty clauses |
| 15 | Power factor (avg) | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{E_{kWh}}{\sqrt{E_{kWh}^2 + E_{kVArh}^2}}$ | dimensionless | From kWh + kVArh meters |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse **kW** (active) with **kVA** (apparent). When a problem says "MD is 100 kVA at PF 0.8", the active component is $P = 100 \times 0.8 = 80$ kW. The tariff in kVA-MD is applied to **100 kVA**, not 80 kW.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **shipyards**, the HT (High Tension) industrial tariff is almost always a **three-part tariff** with the following structure (typical Indian example):

1. **Demand charge:** ₹ 250 per kVA of billing demand per month.
2. **Energy charge:** ₹ 7.50 per kWh.
3. **Power factor incentive/disincentive:** ±0.5% of energy charge for every 0.01 PF above/below 0.90.

This structure pushes shipyard engineers to:
- Install **power factor correction capacitor banks** (raising PF from 0.75 → 0.95 → ~25% reduction in kVA MD → ~25% reduction in demand charge).
- **Stagger welding and crane operations** (improving load factor → better utilization of contracted demand).
- **Schedule heavy load tests during off-peak hours** (ToU optimization).

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU numerical problems:** Always carry a **minimum of 4 significant figures** in intermediate calculations. The final monetary answer is typically rounded to the nearest ₹. Always state the units clearly in the final boxed answer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Load Factor from Energy and Maximum Demand

**Starting premise:** Load factor is the ratio of average load to peak (maximum) load over a specified period.

By definition, the average load is the total energy divided by the duration:

$$
\bar{P} = \frac{E}{T}
$$

where $E$ is energy in kWh and $T$ is the period in hours.

The maximum demand $P_{max}$ is the highest instantaneous (or 15-min integrated) load observed in the period.

By the definition of load factor:

$$
\text{LF} \equiv \frac{\bar{P}}{P_{max}}
$$

Substituting the expression for $\bar{P}$:

$$
\text{LF} = \frac{E / T}{P_{max}} = \frac{E}{P_{max} \cdot T}
$$

**Explanatory text row:** The numerator $E$ is the total kWh consumed; the denominator $P_{max} \cdot T$ is what would have been consumed **if** the consumer had run their maximum demand continuously for the entire period $T$. The ratio is therefore the fraction of theoretical-maximum energy that was actually used.

For a 30-day month, $T = 30 \times 24 = 720$ h. Hence:

$$
\text{LF} = \frac{E_{kWh}}{P_{max,kW} \times 720}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Apparent-Energy Relationship

**Starting premise:** Apparent power (kVA) is the vector sum of active power (kW) and reactive power (kVAr) in an AC circuit.

In a single-phase AC circuit, instantaneous values are:

$$
s(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t) \quad [\text{VA}]
$$

When averaged over a cycle for sinusoidal steady state:

$$
S = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \quad [\text{VA}]
$$

By the Pythagorean identity of AC power:

$$
S^2 = P^2 + Q^2
$$

**Explanatory text row:** Active power does the useful mechanical/thermal work; reactive power oscillates between source and load (does no net work) but occupies network capacity. Therefore the utility must size transformers, cables, and switchgear for the **larger** quantity $S$, not the smaller $P$.

Dividing by $\cos\phi$ to extract the active component:

$$
P = S \cos\phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad S = \frac{P}{\cos\phi}
$$

Integrating over time to get energy:

$$
E_{kVAh} = \int S\, dt, \quad E_{kWh} = \int P\, dt, \quad E_{kVArh} = \int Q\, dt
$$

Therefore:

$$
E_{kVAh} = \frac{E_{kWh}}{\cos\phi} = \sqrt{E_{kWh}^2 + E_{kVArh}^2}
$$

**Explanatory text row:** This identity is the foundation of the kVA-MD tariff — utility meters often record kWh and kVArh independently, and the kVAh is computed from these two readings to bill the consumer for the true network burden.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Three-Part Tariff Equation

**Starting premise:** The total monthly bill under a three-part tariff is the sum of three distinct cost components.

**Component 1 — Capacity / Demand Charge:**

The utility must keep generating + transmission capacity equal to the consumer's maximum demand. The fixed cost per kW (or kVA) of installed capacity is recovered here. Let $R_A$ be the rate in ₹/kVA per month and $MD$ the recorded maximum demand in kVA. Then:

$$
C_1 = R_A \times \text{MD}_{kVA}
$$

**Component 2 — Energy / Running Charge:**

The variable cost (fuel, water, O&M) is proportional to energy delivered. Let $R$ be the rate in ₹/kWh. Then:

$$
C_2 = R \times E_{kWh}
$$

**Component 3 — Power Factor Penalty / Rebate:**

A consumer with power factor $\cos\phi$ lower than the reference $\cos\phi_{ref}$ causes additional line losses. The penalty is structured as a percentage adjustment to the energy bill, or as a direct ₹/kVArh charge. For the percentage form:

$$
C_3 = \begin{cases} +\, k \cdot (\cos\phi_{ref} - \cos\phi) \cdot C_2 & \text{if } \cos\phi < \cos\phi_{ref} \quad (\text{penalty}) \\[4pt] -\, k \cdot (\cos\phi - \cos\phi_{ref}) \cdot C_2 & \text{if } \cos\phi > \cos\phi_{ref} \quad (\text{rebate}) \end{cases}
$$

where $k$ is the penalty coefficient (commonly 0.5% to 2% of energy charge per 0.01 PF deviation).

**Total Bill:**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Total Bill} = R_A \cdot \text{MD}_{kVA} + R \cdot E_{kWh} + C_3\;}
$$

**Explanatory text row:** Equation is the **universal framework** for any industrial tariff in India and most international ports. The first term punishes sustained peak demand, the second term punishes wasteful total consumption, the third term punishes reactive power wastefulness — together they drive the consumer toward a smooth, high-PF, high-LF load profile.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Three-Part Tariff for a Shipyard (KTU Style Numerical)

**Problem Statement:**
A shipyard has the following monthly data:
- Maximum demand (recorded): $\text{MD} = 800$ kVA
- Energy consumed: $E = 3,60,000$ kWh
- Average power factor: $\cos\phi = 0.80$ lagging

Tariff schedule (typical Indian HT industrial):
- Demand charge: ₹ 300 per kVA per month
- Energy charge: ₹ 8.00 per kWh
- PF penalty/rebate: ±1% of energy charge for every 0.01 deviation from reference PF of 0.90

**Find:** (a) Total monthly bill, (b) effective cost per kWh, (c) what the bill would be if PF were corrected to 0.95.

**Solution — Part (a):**

Compute demand charge:

$$
C_1 = 300 \times 800 = 2,40,000 \;\text{₹}
$$

Compute energy charge:

$$
C_2 = 8.00 \times 3,60,000 = 28,80,000 \;\text{₹}
$$

Compute PF penalty:
Deviation: $0.90 - 0.80 = 0.10$ ⇒ $0.10 / 0.01 = 10$ steps
Penalty = $10 \times 1\% \times C_2 = 0.10 \times 28,80,000 = 2,88,000$ ₹

$$
\text{Total Bill} = 2,40,000 + 28,80,000 + 2,88,000 = \boxed{34,08,000 \;\text{₹}}
$$

**Solution — Part (b): Effective ₹/kWh**

$$
\text{Effective rate} = \frac{34,08,000}{3,60,000} = \boxed{9.47 \;\text{₹/kWh}}
$$

**Solution — Part (c): PF corrected to 0.95 (above 0.90 reference)**

Demand charge with reduced kVA: New MD in kVA at the same kW:

$$
P_{kW} = 800 \times 0.80 = 640 \text{ kW}
$$

New kVA at PF = 0.95:

$$
\text{New MD}_{kVA} = \frac{640}{0.95} \approx 673.68 \text{ kVA}
$$

$$
C_1' = 300 \times 673.68 = 2,02,105 \;\text{₹}
$$

Energy charge unchanged (same kWh):

$$
C_2' = 28,80,000 \;\text{₹}
$$

PF rebate: $(0.95 - 0.90)/0.01 = 5$ steps
Rebate = $5 \times 1\% \times 28,80,000 = 1,44,000$ ₹

$$
\text{Total Bill}' = 2,02,105 + 28,80,000 - 1,44,000 = 29,38,105 \;\text{₹}
$$

**Savings:**

$$
\Delta = 34,08,000 - 29,38,105 = 4,69,895 \;\text{₹/month} \approx 56.4 \text{ lakhs/year}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** Power factor correction from 0.80 to 0.95 saved the shipyard **~₹56 lakhs/year** purely on the electricity bill, *before* counting the avoided infrastructure upgrade (smaller transformer, cables, switchgear). This is why every modern shipyard switchboard includes an **automatic power factor correction (APFC) panel**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Tariff for a Given Load Profile

| Consumer Profile | Recommended Tariff | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Domestic (low, steady consumption) | Flat rate / telescopic block rate | Simplicity; no MD meter needed |
| Commercial (variable, lighting + AC) | Two-part (kW-MD + kWh) | Captures midday peak from AC load |
| Industrial continuous (3-shift) | Two-part (kW-MD) | High LF → MD charge dominates; kW-MD is fair |
| Industrial with welding / furnace (low PF) | Three-part kVA-MD with PF clause | Penalizes reactive power waste |
| Shipyard (intermittent heavy cranes) | ToU + three-part | ToU rewards off-peak testing; PF clause protects utility |
| Large port with cold-ironing supply | Custom negotiated + ToU | High contractual value, bilateral contract |
| Small workshop (single-phase) | Flat demand rate | No need for MD metering |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Power Tariff System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Utility Generation Plant] --> B[Transmission Network 220 kV]
    B --> C[Substation 66/11 kV]
    C --> D[Shipyard HT Consumer Point 11 kV]
    D --> E[Step-down Transformer 11kV / 433V]
    E --> F[Main LT Switchboard]
    F --> G[MD Meter kVA / kW]
    F --> H[kWh Energy Meter]
    F --> I[kVArh Reactive Meter]
    G --> J[Three-part Tariff Billing Engine]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Demand Charge Component]
    J --> L[Energy Charge Component]
    J --> M[Power Factor Penalty Component]
    K --> N[Total Monthly Bill]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Invoice to Shipyard]
    N --> P[Cost Optimization Feedback]
    P --> Q[Install APFC Capacitor Bank]
    P --> R[Reschedule Crane Operations]
    P --> S[Load Shedding Strategy]
```

## 4.2 Tariff Classification Tree (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Power Tariff Classification]
    T --> S1[Simple Tariffs]
    T --> S2[Multi-part Tariffs]

    S1 --> F1[Flat Demand Rate]
    S1 --> F2[Straight Meter Rate]
    S1 --> F3[Block Rate Tariff]

    S2 --> M1[Two-part Hopkinson]
    S2 --> M2[Three-part kVA-MD]
    S2 --> M3[Three-part Doherty]
    S2 --> M4[Power Factor Tariff]
    S2 --> M5[Time of Use Tariff]
    S2 --> M6[Seasonal Tariff]

    F3 --> B1[Cumulative Telescopic]
    F3 --> B2[Non-cumulative Block]

    M2 --> P1[Fixed Component]
    M2 --> P2[Running Component]
    M2 --> P3[PF Adjustment]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Which Tariff is Most Economical?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Consumer Load Data Available] --> B{Continuous 3-shift Operation?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Two-part kW-MD]
    B -- No --> D{Power Factor Consistently < 0.85?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use Three-part kVA-MD]
    D -- No --> F{Load Varies Sharply by Time of Day?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use ToU Tariff]
    F -- No --> H{Predominantly Seasonal Use?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Use Seasonal Tariff]
    H -- No --> J[Use Block Rate Tariff]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Power Factor Correction Impact on Tariff

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Load at PF 0.80] --> B[Active P 640 kW]
    A --> C[Reactive Q 480 kVAr]
    A --> D[Apparent S 800 kVA]
    D --> E[Bill includes kVA-MD 800 × Rate]
    F[APFC Capacitor Bank] --> G[Provides 240 kVAr]
    G --> H[New Q 240 kVAr]
    H --> I[New S 673.7 kVA]
    I --> J[Bill includes kVA-MD 673.7 × Rate]
    J --> K[Net Monthly Savings]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Style]**

### Q1. Define "Power Tariff". List any **four** objectives that an ideal tariff must satisfy.

**Model Answer:**
A **power tariff** is the rate or schedule of rates charged by an electricity supply undertaking to its consumers for the energy supplied, expressed in monetary units per unit of energy (typically ₹/kWh), sometimes combined with a demand charge (₹/kW or ₹/kVA).

**Four objectives of an ideal tariff:**
1. **Recovery of cost** — both fixed (capital) and running (fuel, O&M) costs of the utility.
2. **Fair to consumer** — charge proportional to actual usage and demand.
3. **Incentive to consumer** — rewards high load factor and good power factor.
4. **Simple and unambiguous** — easy to compute, understand, and audit.

---

### Q2. What is the difference between a **Two-Part Tariff** and a **Three-Part Tariff**? When is each preferred?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Two-Part Tariff | Three-Part Tariff |
|---|---|---|
| **Components** | Fixed (₹/kW MD) + Running (₹/kWh) | Fixed (₹/kVA MD) + Running (₹/kWh) + PF adjustment |
| **MD unit** | kW (active only) | kVA (apparent — includes reactive burden) |
| **PF consideration** | None explicitly | Explicit penalty/reward clause |
| **Preferred for** | Continuous-process industries with unity/near-unity PF | Industries with low PF (welding, induction furnaces, shipyards) |
| **Equation** | $B = R_c \cdot \text{MD}_{kW} + R \cdot E$ | $B = R_A \cdot \text{MD}_{kVA} + R \cdot E \pm C_3$ |

The three-part tariff is **fairer to the utility** because it bills the consumer for the full network burden (kVA) including reactive component.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Model Question, May 2024 Scheme]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**OR**

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

*(Students answer either A or B as per the KTU choice pattern.)*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

A shipyard has a contract maximum demand of **1500 kVA** and supplies the following loads on a typical day:

- 08:00–17:00: 900 kW at 0.85 PF
- 17:00–22:00: 400 kW at 0.80 PF
- 22:00–08:00: 200 kW at 0.90 PF

**Tariff:**
- Demand charge: ₹ 250 per kVA per month
- Energy charge: ₹ 7.50 per kWh
- PF penalty: 1% of energy charge for every 0.01 below reference PF of 0.90

**(a)** Calculate the **total energy consumed per day** and the **average load factor** for the day. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the **total monthly bill** (assume 30 days), and the **average cost per kWh**. Compare with the bill that would result if power factor were improved to unity throughout. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

Energy in each block:
- Block 1 (9 h × 900 kW) = 8100 kWh
- Block 2 (5 h × 400 kW) = 2000 kWh
- Block 3 (10 h × 200 kW) = 2000 kWh

$$
E_{daily} = 8100 + 2000 + 2000 = 12{,}100 \text{ kWh}
$$

Maximum demand in kW = 900 kW (during 08:00–17:00 block).

$$
P_{max} = 900 \text{ kW}
$$

Average load:

$$
\bar{P} = \frac{12{,}100}{24} = 504.17 \text{ kW}
$$

Load factor:

$$
\text{LF} = \frac{504.17}{900} = 0.5602 \approx \boxed{56.02\%}
$$

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Total monthly energy:

$$
E_{month} = 12{,}100 \times 30 = 3{,}63{,}000 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 2:** Determine kVA at peak (worst-case for MD charge). At 08:00–17:00: $S = 900 / 0.85 = 1058.8$ kVA. At 17:00–22:00: $S = 400 / 0.80 = 500$ kVA. At 22:00–08:00: $S = 200 / 0.90 = 222.2$ kVA.

Recorded MD = max = **1058.8 kVA** (taken as ≤ contracted 1500 kVA — OK).

**Step 3:** Compute demand charge:

$$
C_1 = 250 \times 1058.8 = 2{,}64{,}706 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute energy charge:

$$
C_2 = 7.50 \times 3{,}63{,}000 = 27{,}22{,}500 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute PF penalty. Lowest PF in the day is 0.80; the **average PF** for the day must be computed via the ratio $E_{kWh} / E_{kVAh}$.

For each block, compute kVAh:
- Block 1: 1058.8 × 9 = 9529.4 kVAh
- Block 2: 500 × 5 = 2500 kVAh
- Block 3: 222.2 × 10 = 2222.2 kVAh

Total kVAh per day = 14,251.6 kVAh

Average PF for the day:

$$
\cos\phi_{avg} = \frac{12{,}100}{14{,}251.6} = 0.8490
$$

Deviation: $0.90 - 0.849 = 0.051$ ⇒ 5.1 steps below reference.

Penalty:

$$
C_3 = 0.01 \times 5.1 \times 27{,}22{,}500 = 1{,}38{,}848 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 6:** Total bill:

$$
\boxed{\;B = 2{,}64{,}706 + 27{,}22{,}500 + 1{,}38{,}848 = 31{,}26{,}054 \text{ ₹/month}\;}
$$

**Step 7:** Average cost per kWh:

$$
\text{Rate}_{eff} = \frac{31{,}26{,}054}{3{,}63{,}000} = \boxed{8.61 \text{ ₹/kWh}}
$$

**Step 8 — Comparison at unity PF:**

If PF = 1.0 throughout, $S = P$ (no kVAr component). MD = 900 kVA (= 900 kW).

$$
C_1' = 250 \times 900 = 2{,}25{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
C_2' = 27{,}22{,}500 \text{ ₹ (unchanged kWh)}
$$

$$
C_3' = 0 \text{ (no penalty at unity PF)}
$$

$$
B' = 2{,}25{,}000 + 27{,}22{,}500 = 29{,}47{,}500 \text{ ₹/month}
$$

**Monthly savings = ₹ 1,78,554 ⇒ Annual savings ≈ ₹ 21.4 lakhs** by PF correction alone.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common error (–2 marks):** Using MD = 1500 kVA (contracted demand) instead of the *recorded* MD of 1058.8 kVA. The demand charge is on the **recorded/actual** MD, not the contract value.
> - **Common error (–2 marks):** Using the *worst PF* (0.80) directly in the penalty clause instead of the **average PF** computed from energy. The penalty is applied on the energy-weighted average.
> - **Common error (–1 mark):** Forgetting to multiply the daily energy by 30 for monthly billing. Always state the basis (daily/monthly) explicitly in your answer.
> - **Mark distribution reminder:** Full step-by-step working for each numerical sub-part = full marks. Jumping directly to the answer = 0 marks even if the number is correct.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

A small ship repair workshop consumes **50,000 kWh per month** with a **maximum demand of 200 kVA** at an **average power factor of 0.85 lag**. The electricity board offers two tariff options:

**Option 1 (Two-part Hopkinson):**
- Demand charge: ₹ 200 per kW of MD
- Energy charge: ₹ 6.00 per kWh

**Option 2 (Three-part kVA-MD):**
- Demand charge: ₹ 180 per kVA of MD
- Energy charge: ₹ 6.50 per kWh
- PF penalty: 0.5% of energy charge for every 0.01 below 0.90 PF

**(a)** Compute the monthly bill under **both options** and identify the cheaper one. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** At what minimum average PF would Option 2 become more economical than Option 1? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1: Compute active (kW) MD from kVA MD:**

$$
P_{MD} = 200 \times 0.85 = 170 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2: Bill under Option 1 (Hopkinson, kW-MD):**

$$
C_1^{(1)} = 200 \times 170 = 34{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
C_2^{(1)} = 6.00 \times 50{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
B_1 = 34{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 = \boxed{3{,}34{,}000 \text{ ₹/month}}
$$

**Step 3: Bill under Option 2 (kVA-MD with PF penalty):**

$$
C_1^{(2)} = 180 \times 200 = 36{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
C_2^{(2)} = 6.50 \times 50{,}000 = 3{,}25{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

PF penalty: deviation = 0.90 - 0.85 = 0.05 ⇒ 5 steps ⇒ 5 × 0.5% = 2.5%

$$
C_3^{(2)} = 0.025 \times 3{,}25{,}000 = 8{,}125 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
B_2 = 36{,}000 + 3{,}25{,}000 + 8{,}125 = \boxed{3{,}69{,}125 \text{ ₹/month}}
$$

**Step 4: Comparison:** $B_1 = 3,34,000$ ₹ < $B_2 = 3,69,125$ ₹.

**⇒ Option 1 (Hopkinson) is cheaper by ₹ 35,125 per month.**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

Let $\cos\phi$ = unknown PF. The kW MD remains $P_{MD} = 170$ kW (load active power unchanged). The kVA MD varies as $200 / \cos\phi \times 0.85 = 170 / \cos\phi$.

Under **Option 1** (independent of PF):

$$
B_1 = 34{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}34{,}000 \text{ ₹ (constant)}
$$

Under **Option 2** (with PF = $\cos\phi$):

$$
C_1^{(2)} = 180 \times \frac{170}{\cos\phi} = \frac{30{,}600}{\cos\phi} \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
C_2^{(2)} = 3{,}25{,}000 \text{ ₹ (energy unchanged)}
$$

PF deviation = $0.90 - \cos\phi$ (only if $\cos\phi < 0.90$). Let us first solve without worrying about rebate.

For $\cos\phi \geq 0.90$ — no penalty:

$$
B_2 = \frac{30{,}600}{\cos\phi} + 3{,}25{,}000
$$

Setting $B_2 = B_1$:

$$
\frac{30{,}600}{\cos\phi} + 3{,}25{,}000 = 3{,}34{,}000
$$

$$
\frac{30{,}600}{\cos\phi} = 9{,}000
$$

$$
\cos\phi = \frac{30{,}600}{9{,}000} = 3.4
$$

Since cosine cannot exceed 1, **no PF in the valid range $[0.90, 1.00]$** can make Option 2 cheaper than Option 1 under this assumption.

**Now for $\cos\phi < 0.90$ — penalty applies:**

Penalty steps = $(0.90 - \cos\phi)/0.01$; penalty % = $0.005 \times (0.90 - \cos\phi)/0.01 = 0.5 \times (0.90 - \cos\phi)$;

Penalty ₹ = $0.5 \times (0.90 - \cos\phi) \times 3{,}25{,}000$.

Full bill:

$$
B_2 = \frac{30{,}600}{\cos\phi} + 3{,}25{,}000 \times [1 + 0.5(0.90 - \cos\phi)]
$$

Setting $B_2 = B_1 = 3{,}34{,}000$ and solving numerically:

Try $\cos\phi = 0.85$ → $B_2 = 36,000 + 3,25,000 \times [1 + 0.5 \times 0.05] = 36,000 + 3,25,000 \times 1.025 = 3,69,125$ — too high.

Try $\cos\phi = 0.99$ → $B_2 = 30,909 + 3,25,000 \times [1 - 0.045] = 30,909 + 3,10,375 = 3,41,284$ — still > 3,34,000.

Try $\cos\phi = 1.0$ (with rebate) → $B_2 = 30,600 + 3,25,000 \times [1 - 0.05] = 30,600 + 3,08,750 = 3,39,350$ — still > 3,34,000.

Even at PF = 1.0, Option 2 yields ₹ 3,39,350 > Option 1's ₹ 3,34,000.

**Conclusion:** Under the given rate structure, **Option 1 (Hopkinson) is always more economical** than Option 2 for this consumer, regardless of PF. The crossover PF would require $\cos\phi > 1.0$, which is physically impossible.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> - **–2 marks:** Confusing kW MD (170 kW) with kVA MD (200 kVA) when applying the demand charge. Always re-check: "₹ per kW" → use kW; "₹ per kVA" → use kVA.
> - **–2 marks:** Applying the PF penalty to $C_1$ (demand charge) instead of $C_2$ (energy charge). The penalty clause is **always on the energy charge** in standard tariffs.
> - **–1 mark:** Skipping the validity check on cosine value (must lie in [0, 1]). If the equation gives an impossible answer, state so explicitly with reasoning.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this checklist — these are the items most frequently tested in KTU 2024 scheme ESE papers for PENST524.**

- [x] **Definition:** A power tariff is the rate schedule (₹/kWh, ₹/kW, ₹/kVA) charged by the utility; it recovers fixed + variable costs.
- [x] **Three-part tariff equation:** $B = R_A \cdot \text{MD}_{kVA} + R \cdot E \pm C_3(\text{PF})$.
- [x] **Two-part tariff equation:** $B = R_c \cdot \text{MD}_{kW} + R \cdot E$.
- [x] **Load Factor:** $\text{LF} = \dfrac{E}{P_{max} \cdot T}$. For 30-day month, $T = 720$ h.
- [x] **kW ↔ kVA conversion:** $S = P / \cos\phi$ (active ↔ apparent).
- [x] **Energy ↔ kVAh:** $E_{kVAh} = \sqrt{E_{kWh}^2 + E_{kVArh}^2}$.
- [x] **PF penalty is on the energy charge**, not on the demand charge.
- [x] **MD is recorded**, not contracted; contracted demand is the *upper ceiling* and over-draw attracts heavy penalty.
- [x] **PF correction always pays back**: every 0.05 improvement in PF reduces kVA-MD charge by ~5–10% and eliminates PF penalty.
- [x] **Shipyards use three-part kVA-MD tariffs** because of large welding/crane loads with low lagging PF.
- [x] **ToU tariffs** penalize daytime peaks and reward off-peak usage — relevant to shipyards with intermittent crane duty.
- [x] **Flat rate tariff** is the simplest and is used for residential/lighting consumers.
- [x] **Block rate tariff** has two variants: cumulative (telescopic) and non-cumulative (block-wise). Question wording decides.
- [x] **To convert a "Two-part kW" bill to "Three-part kVA", multiply kW by 1/PF.**
- [x] **Final answer must carry units (₹/kWh, ₹, kVA, kWh)** — KTU examiners deduct marks for unit-less final values.
- [x] **For multiple-load-block problems, sum kWh and kVAh across all blocks separately**, then compute the average PF from the totals.

> **End of Section 5 — Power Tariff Calculation Notes for PENST524 Module 4**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Essential Regulations and Main Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Essential Regulations and Main Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of marine electrical engineering, **Essential Regulations** refer to the codified set of rules, codes, and standards issued by international bodies (IMO, IEC) and Classification Societies (Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, Bureau Veritas) that govern the design, installation, and operation of electrical systems aboard seagoing vessels. **Main Loads** are the aggregate electrical power-consuming devices and machinery connected to a ship's main switchboard, classified by their operational priority as *essential services*, *emergency services*, and *non-essential services*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 4):** Students must understand the demarcation between statutory regulations (SOLAS, MARPOL, IEC 60092) and class society rules, and be able to perform a quantitative **load analysis** to size main generators, emergency generators, and main switchboard busbars.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a ship as a **floating city**. Just like a city has hospitals, streetlights, and water pumps that *must* keep running during a blackout, a ship has a strict priority list for every electrical consumer. Essential regulations are the "building codes" of this floating city, while the main loads are the "appliances."

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Consider a hospital with a normal power grid, a backup generator, and a UPS. The "essential loads" in a ship (e.g., steering gear, fire pumps, navigation lights) behave exactly like the operating theater, ICU, and life-support equipment of that hospital. Classification societies act as the "hospital accreditation board," verifying that the electrical system can handle worst-case blackout scenarios.

### 1.3 Regulatory Bodies – The Three Pillars of Marine Electrical Compliance

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compliance Hierarchy} = \{ &\text{International (IMO/SOLAS)}, \\ 
&\text{Statutory (Flag State / IEC 60092)}, \\ 
&\text{Class (Lloyd's, DNV, ABS, BV)} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Tier | Body | Jurisdiction | Example Document |
|------|------|--------------|------------------|
| 1 | **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** | Global treaty-based | SOLAS Chapter II-1, Reg. 40–45 |
| 2 | **International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)** | Global standard | IEC 60092 Series |
| 3 | **Classification Societies** | Ship registration & insurance | Lloyd's Register Rules, DNV Pt.4 Ch.8 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Standard Metric:** The standard reference voltage levels for shipboard AC systems are **440 V, 6.6 kV, and 11 kV**, with a nominal frequency of **60 Hz** (or **50 Hz** for vessels trading in Europe). The standard ambient design temperature is **45 °C** with seawater at **32 °C**.

### 1.4 Definition of "Main Load" Categories

A **Main Load** is any device drawing power from the *Main Switchboard (MSB)*. They are classified by KTU/Class rules into:

1. **Essential Services (Continuous)**: Required for normal operation at all times (e.g., steering gear pumps, lubricating oil pumps, navigation equipment, lighting). Cannot be shed.
2. **Essential Services (Non-Continuous)**: Required for vessel safety but not simultaneously (e.g., fire pumps, ballast pumps). Subject to *permitted short-term shedding*.
3. **Non-Essential Services**: Comfort and commercial loads (e.g., HVAC, galley, cabin sockets). Can be shed during a blackout to protect the generator.
4. **Emergency Services**: Powered from the *Emergency Switchboard (ESB)* via the Emergency Generator (e.g., emergency lighting, fire detection, life-boat davit power). Must function for **3 hours minimum** (SOLAS) for cargo ships and **36 hours** for passenger ships.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Load Categorization

The classification of loads is not arbitrary; it is governed by a logical safety hierarchy:

- **Step 1 – Identify Statutory Loads:** The first filter is the *SOLAS convention*. Any equipment listed in SOLAS Chapters II-1, II-2, III, IV, V is automatically "essential."
- **Step 2 – Apply Class Rules:** The chosen Classification Society refines SOLAS into ship-class-specific requirements (e.g., the number of fire pumps based on vessel GT).
- **Step 3 – Apply Demand Factor:** The theoretical sum of all connected loads is *never* equal to the running load. A **Demand Factor** $k_d$ is applied per equipment type.
- **Step 4 – Apply Diversity/Coincidence Factor:** Not all loads run simultaneously. A **Coincidence Factor** $k_c$ accounts for statistical overlap.
- **Step 5 – Apply Service Factor:** Motor-driven loads have a starting surge. A **Service Factor** ensures transformers and generators are not sized only on steady-state.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Demand Factor

The **Why:** A ship does not need *all* its motors at full load at the *same instant*. The **How:** Each equipment group is assigned a fractional coefficient based on historical operational data. For example, a 100 kW compressor may only draw 75 kW during normal operation, giving a demand factor of 0.75.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (Lloyd's Register):** The total generator capacity must exceed the **maximum demanded load** (after applying demand factors) by a margin of typically **10–15%** to allow for sudden load additions and to prevent prolonged operation at >90% of rated capacity.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table provides the complete set of KTU-grade formulas for load analysis on ships. All symbols and their units are listed below.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Total Connected Load | $P_{TC} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{n,i}$ | **kW** – sum of nameplate ratings |
| 2 | Demand Load | $P_{D} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{n,i} \cdot k_{d,i}$ | **kW** – applies demand factor per load |
| 3 | Coincident Demand | $P_{CD} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} P_{D,j} \cdot k_{c,j}$ | **kW** – applies coincidence factor per group |
| 4 | Maximum Demand | $P_{MD} = P_{CD} + P_{margin}$ | **kW** – includes 10–15% margin |
| 5 | Demand Factor | $k_d = \dfrac{P_{actual}}{P_{rated}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le k_d \le 1$ |
| 6 | Diversity Factor | $k_{div} = \dfrac{\sum P_{individual}}{P_{max\,simultaneous}}$ | Dimensionless, $k_{div} \ge 1$ |
| 7 | Coincidence Factor | $k_c = \dfrac{1}{k_{div}}$ | Dimensionless, $k_c \le 1$ |
| 8 | Load Factor | $k_{lf} = \dfrac{P_{average}}{P_{peak}}$ | Dimensionless – average over 24h |
| 9 | Service Factor (Motor) | $SF = \dfrac{P_{max\,op}}{P_{rated}}$ | Typically **1.15** for 1 hour |
| 10 | Generator Sizing (N+1) | $N_{gen} = \lceil P_{MD} / P_{gen} \rceil + 1$ | Redundancy for sea-going vessels |
| 11 | Emergency Load (SOLAS) | $E_{LD} = \sum P_{essential\,emer} \cdot k_d$ | **3 h battery** or **3 h generator** backup |
| 12 | Transformer Sizing | $S_T = \dfrac{P_{D}}{\cos\phi \cdot \eta_T}$ | **kVA** – $\cos\phi \approx 0.8$ lagging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe Symbol Rule:** In all calculations involving absolute values or parallel symbols, use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX (e.g., $\vert V_L \vert = 440$ V) and **never** the raw ASCII pipe `|` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

These formulas are not academic exercises. In a shipyard, the **Shipyard Project Engineer** uses this exact load analysis to:

1. Select the **Main Generator** rating and number (e.g., 4 × 2500 kW).
2. Select the **Main Switchboard** busbar rating (e.g., 6300 A).
3. Determine the **Emergency Generator** rating (e.g., 250 kW, 3-hour endurance).
4. Calculate the **Battery Bank** for emergency lighting (e.g., 1200 Ah @ 24 V).
5. Determine the **shedding priority** to prevent generator overload during a black-out.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Nameplate Rating to Generator Sizing

This exhaustive walk-through demonstrates how a marine electrical engineer converts raw equipment nameplate data into a final generator selection.

**Problem Setup:** A 50,000 DWT bulk carrier has the following motor inventory on the main switchboard. Determine the number and rating of main generators.

| Group | Equipment | Rating (kW) | Quantity | Demand Factor $k_d$ | Coincidence $k_c$ |
|-------|-----------|-------------|----------|---------------------|-------------------|
| A | Steering gear pumps | 75 | 2 | 0.85 | 1.00 |
| B | Main seawater cooling pumps | 110 | 2 | 0.80 | 0.80 |
| C | Fuel oil purifiers | 55 | 2 | 0.75 | 0.50 |
| D | Compressed air compressors | 60 | 2 | 0.70 | 0.60 |
| E | Deck auxiliaries (winches) | 90 | 2 | 0.50 | 0.40 |
| F | HVAC & Galley | 200 | 1 | 0.60 | 1.00 |
| G | Navigation & Communication | 25 | 1 | 1.00 | 1.00 |
| H | Lighting (general) | 120 | 1 | 0.90 | 1.00 |

**Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Total Connected Load per group} \\
P_{TC,A} &= 2 \times 75 = 150 \text{ kW} \\
P_{TC,B} &= 2 \times 110 = 220 \text{ kW} \\
P_{TC,C} &= 2 \times 55 = 110 \text{ kW} \\
P_{TC,D} &= 2 \times 60 = 120 \text{ kW} \\
P_{TC,E} &= 2 \times 90 = 180 \text{ kW} \\
P_{TC,F} &= 1 \times 200 = 200 \text{ kW} \\
P_{TC,G} &= 1 \times 25 = 25 \text{ kW} \\
P_{TC,H} &= 1 \times 120 = 120 \text{ kW} \\
P_{TC,total} &= 150+220+110+120+180+200+25+120 \\
&= 1125 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2: Apply Demand Factor per group} \\
P_{D,A} &= 150 \times 0.85 = 127.5 \text{ kW} \\
P_{D,B} &= 220 \times 0.80 = 176.0 \text{ kW} \\
P_{D,C} &= 110 \times 0.75 = 82.5 \text{ kW} \\
P_{D,D} &= 120 \times 0.70 = 84.0 \text{ kW} \\
P_{D,E} &= 180 \times 0.50 = 90.0 \text{ kW} \\
P_{D,F} &= 200 \times 0.60 = 120.0 \text{ kW} \\
P_{D,G} &= 25 \times 1.00 = 25.0 \text{ kW} \\
P_{D,H} &= 120 \times 0.90 = 108.0 \text{ kW} \\
P_{D,total} &= 813.0 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3: Apply Coincidence Factor per group} \\
P_{CD,A} &= 127.5 \times 1.00 = 127.5 \text{ kW} \\
P_{CD,B} &= 176.0 \times 0.80 = 140.8 \text{ kW} \\
P_{CD,C} &= 82.5 \times 0.50 = 41.25 \text{ kW} \\
P_{CD,D} &= 84.0 \times 0.60 = 50.4 \text{ kW} \\
P_{CD,E} &= 90.0 \times 0.40 = 36.0 \text{ kW} \\
P_{CD,F} &= 120.0 \times 1.00 = 120.0 \text{ kW} \\
P_{CD,G} &= 25.0 \times 1.00 = 25.0 \text{ kW} \\
P_{CD,H} &= 108.0 \times 1.00 = 108.0 \text{ kW} \\
P_{CD,total} &= 648.95 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 4: Apply Generator Sizing Margin (10%)} \\
P_{MD} &= P_{CD,total} \times 1.10 = 648.95 \times 1.10 \\
&= 713.85 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 5: Generator Selection (N+1 redundancy)} \\
\text{If we select 500 kW generators:} \\
N_{req} &= \left\lceil \frac{713.85}{500} \right\rceil = 2 \text{ generators for running} \\
N_{total} &= N_{req} + 1 = 3 \text{ generators (incl. 1 standby)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Three 500 kW diesel generators (with one as a sea-going standby) are selected, providing 1500 kW total installed capacity against 713.85 kW maximum demand, giving a comfortable **52% utilization** at peak.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Demand Factors by Equipment Type

| Equipment Category | Typical $k_d$ | Notes |
|--------------------|---------------|-------|
| Steering gear (running pump) | **0.80 – 0.90** | Continuously running in confined waters |
| Bilge & ballast pumps | **0.40 – 0.60** | Intermittent duty |
| Air conditioning compressors | **0.65 – 0.75** | Thermostatically controlled |
| Galley equipment | **0.50 – 0.70** | Cooking peaks not always aligned |
| Deck winches & cranes | **0.30 – 0.50** | Highly intermittent |
| Navigation electronics | **0.95 – 1.00** | Near-constant draw |
| Battery chargers (running) | **0.70 – 0.85** | Float + equalize cycles |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Coincidence Factor < 1?** The probability of every motor in a group drawing peak power at the same millisecond is statistically negligible. For non-safety loads (e.g., galley + HVAC), $k_c$ can be as low as **0.4**, but for safety systems (e.g., fire pumps), $k_c$ approaches **1.0** because they must all function during an emergency.

### 3.3 Emergency Load Derivation (SOLAS Ch. II-1 Reg. 43)

The emergency source of power must supply the following for **3 hours** (cargo ships) or **36 hours** (passenger ships):

$$
P_{emer} = P_{emer\_lighting} + P_{emer\_nav} + P_{fire\,pumps} + P_{emer\,stbd}
$$

Where:
- $P_{emer\_lighting}$: Emergency lighting at exits, muster stations, embarkation (typically 15–25% of total lighting).
- $P_{emer\_nav}$: Full navigation radar, GPS, GMDSS communication.
- $P_{fire\,pumps}$: One fire pump (mandatory) of 40–80 kW.
- $P_{emer\,stbd}$: Steering gear motor (emergency override).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Class Rule (Lloyd's):** If the emergency source is a *generator*, it must start automatically within **45 seconds** of a main power failure. If a *battery*, it must instantly carry the emergency load without voltage drop exceeding **12%** of nominal.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Marine Power Distribution Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    mainGen1[Main DG 1 - 500 kW]
    mainGen2[Main DG 2 - 500 kW]
    mainGen3[Main DG 3 - 500 kW - Standby]
    
    mainGen1 --> MSB[Main Switchboard 440V]
    mainGen2 --> MSB
    mainGen3 --> MSB
    
    MSB --> Essential[Essential Loads - Non-S sheddable]
    MSB --> SemiEssential[Semi-Essential Loads - Short-term sheddable]
    MSB --> NonEssential[Non-Essential Loads - Comfort services]
    
    Essential --> Steering[Steering Gear Pumps]
    Essential --> NavLights[Navigation Lights]
    Essential --> LubOil[Lub Oil Pumps]
    
    SemiEssential --> FirePump[Fire Pump]
    SemiEssential --> Ballast[Ballast Pump]
    SemiEssential --> Compressor[Air Compressor]
    
    NonEssential --> HVAC[HVAC System]
    NonEssential --> Galley[Galley Equipment]
    NonEssential --> CabinSockets[Cabin Sockets]
    
    MSB -.Power Supply.-> ESB
    ESB[Emergency Switchboard]
    ESB --> EmerGen[Emergency DG 250 kW]
    ESB --> BatteryBank[Battery Bank 24V DC]
    
    ESB --> EmerLight[Emergency Lighting]
    ESB --> FireDet[Fire Detection System]
    ESB --> GMDSS[GMDSS Communication]
```

### 4.2 Flowchart: Load Shedding Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Vessel Operating - All Gens Online]) --> Monitor{Generator Load > 90 percent rated}
    Monitor -- No --> Normal([Continue Normal Operation])
    Monitor -- Yes --> Stage1[Stage 1 - Shed HVAC non-critical zones]
    Stage1 --> Recheck1{Load < 85 percent}
    Recheck1 -- No --> Stage2[Stage 2 - Shed Galley heavy loads]
    Stage2 --> Recheck2{Load < 85 percent}
    Recheck2 -- No --> Stage3[Stage 3 - Shed Deck machinery]
    Stage3 --> Recheck3{Load < 85 percent}
    Recheck3 -- No --> Alarm[Class Alarm - Trip non-essential feeder]
    Recheck3 -- Yes --> Stable([System Stabilized])
    Recheck2 -- Yes --> Stable
    Recheck1 -- Yes --> Stable
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Essential vs Non-Essential Service Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SOLAS[SOLAS Mandatory Services]
        S1[Steering Gear]
        S2[Fire Pumps]
        S3[Emergency Lighting]
        S4[Lifeboat Power]
    end
    
    subgraph Class[Class Required Services]
        C1[Bilge Pumps]
        C2[Ballast Pumps]
        C3[Navigation Radar]
        C4[Main Lub Oil Pumps]
    end
    
    subgraph Commercial[Commercial / Comfort]
        M1[HVAC]
        M2[Galley]
        M3[Cabin Power]
        M4[Entertainment]
    end
    
    MSB[Main Switchboard] --> SOLAS
    MSB --> Class
    MSB --> Commercial
    
    ESB[Emergency Switchboard] --> S1
    ESB --> S2
    ESB --> S3
    ESB --> S4
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Load Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Step 1: Compile Equipment List] --> B[Step 2: Sum Nameplate Ratings Pn]
    B --> C[Step 3: Apply Demand Factor kd]
    C --> D[Step 4: Apply Coincidence Factor kc]
    D --> E[Step 5: Add 10 to 15 percent margin]
    E --> F[Step 6: Determine number of running generators]
    F --> G[Step 7: Add N+1 standby generator]
    G --> H[Step 8: Select Main Switchboard rating]
    H --> I[Step 9: Validate against Class Rules]
    I --> J[Final Generator and Switchboard Selection]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Essential Services" in a ship's electrical system and give **two** examples.*

**Model Answer:**
As per SOLAS Chapter II-1 and major classification society rules, *Essential Services* are the electrical loads required for the safe navigation, maneuvering, and operation of the vessel under all conditions, including emergencies. They must remain energized at all times and are not subject to load shedding.

**Two Examples:**
1. **Steering gear pumps** – required for vessel course control.
2. **Main lubricating oil pumps** for the propulsion prime mover – required to prevent catastrophic engine seizure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** If the student lists "air conditioning" as essential, it is a definitive mark-loser. The examiner is testing for the *statutory* category, not the *comfort* category.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of the **Emergency Switchboard (ESB)** in a ship, and which regulation governs its autonomy duration?*

**Model Answer:**
The **Emergency Switchboard (ESB)** is a secondary distribution board fed by the Emergency Generator (or battery bank). It supplies only those loads essential for safety during a main power blackout (e.g., emergency lighting, fire detection, GMDSS radio, one fire pump). The autonomy duration is governed by **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 43**, which mandates a minimum of **3 hours** for cargo ships and **36 hours** for passenger ships carrying more than 36 passengers.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) – Module 4 Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule (ESE Pattern):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split as Part (a) = **7 marks** and Part (b) = **7 marks**. Students must attempt **one** of the two choices OR all if specifically required by the question paper pattern. Both questions below are valid 14-mark parallel choices.

---

## ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Paper]**
A 30,000 DWT oil tanker is fitted with the following main switchboard consumers. Using suitable demand and coincidence factors:

| S.No | Consumer | Installed Power (kW) | Quantity |
|------|----------|----------------------|----------|
| 1 | Cargo oil pump motors | 350 | 3 |
| 2 | Ballast pump motors | 180 | 2 |
| 3 | Main sea water cooling pumps | 90 | 2 |
| 4 | Air conditioning compressors | 75 | 2 |
| 5 | Galley and pantry | 60 | 1 |
| 6 | Navigation and communication | 35 | 1 |
| 7 | General lighting | 110 | 1 |

**Part (a) – 7 Marks:**
Calculate the **maximum demand** on the main switchboard using the following assumed factors: Demand factor $k_d = 0.75$ for cargo and ballast pumps, $0.70$ for cooling pumps, $0.65$ for HVAC, $0.60$ for galley, $1.00$ for navigation, and $0.90$ for lighting. Coincidence factor $k_c = 1.00$ for all groups.

**Part (b) – 7 Marks:**
Determine the **number and rating of main generators** required, assuming a 10% margin and that each generator is identical.

---

### **Model Solution – Question A**

**Part (a) Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{TC,1} &= 3 \times 350 = 1050 \text{ kW (Cargo pumps)} \\
P_{TC,2} &= 2 \times 180 = 360 \text{ kW (Ballast)} \\
P_{TC,3} &= 2 \times 90 = 180 \text{ kW (Cooling)} \\
P_{TC,4} &= 2 \times 75 = 150 \text{ kW (HVAC)} \\
P_{TC,5} &= 1 \times 60 = 60 \text{ kW (Galley)} \\
P_{TC,6} &= 1 \times 35 = 35 \text{ kW (Nav)} \\
P_{TC,7} &= 1 \times 110 = 110 \text{ kW (Lighting)} \\
P_{TC,total} &= 1945 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_D \text{ (after demand factor):} \\
P_{D,1} &= 1050 \times 0.75 = 787.5 \text{ kW} \\
P_{D,2} &= 360 \times 0.75 = 270.0 \text{ kW} \\
P_{D,3} &= 180 \times 0.70 = 126.0 \text{ kW} \\
P_{D,4} &= 150 \times 0.65 = 97.5 \text{ kW} \\
P_{D,5} &= 60 \times 0.60 = 36.0 \text{ kW} \\
P_{D,6} &= 35 \times 1.00 = 35.0 \text{ kW} \\
P_{D,7} &= 110 \times 0.90 = 99.0 \text{ kW} \\
P_{D,total} &= 1451.0 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $k_c = 1.00$ for all groups, the **coincident demand equals demand load**:

$$
P_{CD} = 1451.0 \text{ kW}
$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{MD} &= P_{CD} \times 1.10 = 1451.0 \times 1.10 \\
&= 1596.1 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

If we select generators of rating $P_{gen} = 800$ kW:

$$
N_{req} = \left\lceil \frac{1596.1}{800} \right\rceil = 2 \text{ running generators}
$$

With N+1 redundancy (one standby for sea passage):

$$
N_{total} = 3 \text{ generators of } 800 \text{ kW each}
$$

**Total installed capacity** = $3 \times 800 = 2400$ kW, which gives a utilization of:

$$
k_{util} = \frac{1596.1}{2400} = 66.5\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Lose 2 Marks):** Students frequently forget the **N+1 rule** and quote only 2 generators. For a sea-going vessel under any major class, **at least one** running generator must be available as a hot standby with auto-start. Failure to include this is a direct non-compliance with SOLAS Reg. 41.

---

## ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Replica]**
**(a) – 7 Marks:**
Explain in detail the **classification of ship's electrical loads** with reference to SOLAS and a classification society (Lloyd's Register or DNV). Provide **at least three** examples for each category.

**(b) – 7 Marks:**
Describe the **load shedding scheme** used on a modern ship, including the priority levels and the protection logic for main generators.

---

### **Model Solution – Question B**

**Part (a) Model Answer:**

The classification of marine electrical loads is hierarchical and safety-driven. The principal categories are:

1. **Essential Services (Continuous)** – Cannot be interrupted.
   - *Example 1:* Steering gear pumps (SOLAS Reg. 29).
   - *Example 2:* Main lubricating oil pump for the propulsion engine.
   - *Example 3:* Navigation lights (COLREGs).

2. **Essential Services (Non-Continuous)** – Required for safety but may be shed briefly.
   - *Example 1:* Fire-fighting pumps (SOLAS Reg. 10).
   - *Example 2:* Bilge pumps (SOLAS Reg. 21).
   - *Example 3:* Fuel oil purifiers (Class rule for main engine supply).

3. **Non-Essential Services** – Comfort and commercial; shed first during overload.
   - *Example 1:* Air conditioning and ventilation (HVAC).
   - *Example 2:* Galley cooking equipment.
   - *Example 3:* Cabin power sockets and entertainment systems.

4. **Emergency Services** – Powered from ESB; operate during blackout.
   - *Example 1:* Emergency lighting (muster stations, escape routes).
   - *Example 2:* GMDSS communication equipment.
   - *Example 3:* Lifeboat davit winches.

SOLAS Chapter II-1 (Regulations 40–45) defines the boundary between essential and emergency services. **Lloyd's Register Rules and Regulations for Ships (Part 6, Chapter 2)** refines these definitions and specifies the number of redundant consumers.

**Part (b) Model Answer:**

A modern vessel employs a **Tiered Load Shedding Scheme (TLSS)** governed by the Power Management System (PMS):

- **Tier 1 (Trigger: Generator > 90% rated load):** Shed all non-essential services (HVAC, galley, cabin sockets). Restore the breaker only when load falls below 85%.
- **Tier 2 (Trigger: Generator > 95%):** Shed semi-essential services (deck auxiliaries, non-critical pumps). Auto-start the standby generator.
- **Tier 3 (Trigger: Generator > 100% / reverse power):** Trip the non-essential feeder circuit breaker and sound a Class alarm at the bridge. If overloading persists for >30 seconds, the heaviest motor is automatically started on a "soft-start" to avoid further surge.
- **Protection Logic:** Each generator is protected by a **Reverse Power Relay (RPR)** set at 5–10% of rated, an **Overcurrent Relay (OCR)** with IDMT characteristic, and a **Preferential Trip Relay** that sheds non-essential feeders in a pre-programmed priority order.

The PMS monitors frequency and active power. If frequency drops below 1% of nominal (e.g., 59.4 Hz on a 60 Hz system), the shedding logic activates within 0.5 seconds to prevent generator stall.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Lose 2 Marks):** A common mistake is to describe load shedding in terms of "switching off lights." Examiners expect PMS logic, threshold percentages, and the auto-start of the standby generator. Hand-wavy answers without numbers (90%, 95%, 100%) lose significant marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam Day**

- **Regulatory Hierarchy (in order of precedence):** IMO/SOLAS → Flag State → Class Society → IEC 60092.
- **SOLAS Ch. II-1 Reg. 40–45** governs main electrical installation; **Reg. 43** governs emergency power.
