- **Emergency power autonomy:** **3 hours** for cargo ships, **36 hours** for passenger ships.
- **Standard design voltage:** 440 V AC (auxiliaries), 24/220 V DC (control & emergency).
- **Standard design frequency:** **60 Hz** (US/global) or **50 Hz** (Europe).
- **Standard ambient:** **45 °C** air, **32 °C** seawater.
- **Demand Factor** $k_d$ is **always ≤ 1**; **Diversity Factor** $k_{div}$ is **always ≥ 1**.
- **Coincidence Factor** $k_c = 1 / k_{div}$.
- **Generator Sizing Rule:** $P_{gen,total} \ge 1.10 \times P_{MD}$ with **N+1 redundancy**.
- **Load Shedding Tiers:** Non-essential → Semi-essential → Trip alarm.
- **Reverse Power Relay threshold:** typically **5–10%** of rated generator power.
- **Emergency Generator start time:** ≤ **45 seconds** automatic.
- **Steering gear power:** must come directly from MSB or via dedicated feeder from ESB (SOLAS Reg. 29).
- **Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, BV** are the "Big Four" classification societies for KTU reference.
- **IEC 60092** is the master standard for shipboard electrical installations.
- **Main Switchboard** must have a **Preferential Trip** feature for non-essential feeders.
- **Fire pumps:** at least **2** independent pumps, each capable of delivering the required fire main pressure (SOLAS Ch. II-2).

**End of Module 4 – Essential Regulations and Main Loads Notes**  
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) – ESE & Internal Assessment Ready*  
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Potential Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Potential Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Potential Hazards (Shipboard & Shipyard Context):** Potential hazards refer to any inherent source or situation within a marine electrical installation, shipboard machinery, or shipyard working environment that possesses the capacity, energy, or chemical/physical properties to cause injury, occupational illness, death, property damage, environmental contamination, or a combination of these. In the marine electrical domain, hazards are systematically classified according to their energy origin (electrical, thermal, mechanical, chemical, radiation) and the environmental zone in which they manifest.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea)**, the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**, **IEC 60092 / IEC 60364**, and **IEEE 45** standards, potential hazards on board a vessel or within a shipyard are not treated as isolated accidents but as **foreseeable risk vectors** that must be identified, evaluated, mitigated, and continuously monitored through a structured **Safety Management System (SMS)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Definition - Risk:** Risk = Probability of Hazard Occurrence × Severity of Consequence. The KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework expects students to articulate both the *hazard* (source) and the *risk* (quantified exposure).

## 1.2 Classification of Marine Electrical Hazards (Taxonomy)

The topic of Potential Hazards in Module 4 of PENST524 is fundamentally organized into the following four super-categories, each with sub-classifications:

1. **Electrical Hazards** (Shock, Arc Flash, Electrocution, Static Discharge, Lightning)
2. **Fire & Explosion Hazards** (Fuel vapors, hydrogen from batteries, oil mist, LNG/LPG carriers)
3. **Chemical & Toxic Hazards** (Battery acid, SF₆ decomposition products, asbestos, refrigerant leaks)
4. **Mechanical & Physical Hazards** (Rotating machinery, steam, noise, dropped objects, confined space)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Ship as a Floating City of Controlled Dangers":**
> Think of a modern vessel as a **floating city powered by megawatts of electricity** floating on a **highly flammable medium (diesel / fuel oil / LNG)**. The shipyard is the **construction site of that floating city**. Every cable, every switchboard, every battery bank is essentially a controlled release of energy. The moment a control (insulation, earthing, zoning, ventilation, PPE) fails, the stored energy becomes a *hazard*. Your job as a marine electrical engineer is to ensure that the energy **stays controlled** in all conditions — calm seas, storms, fire, flooding, or human error.

Just as a kitchen has a stove (energy source), flammable oil (fuel), and a child (vulnerable person) — a ship has generators (energy), fuel tanks (fuel), and crew (vulnerable persons) — the only thing preventing a "kitchen fire" is the safety system (insulation, zoning, detectors, training). That entire safety philosophy is what **Potential Hazards** as a topic encapsulates.

## 1.4 Physical Constants, Units & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Marine Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Human body resistance (wet skin) | $R_b$ | **~1000** | $\Omega$ |
| Human body resistance (dry skin) | $R_b$ | **~100,000** | $\Omega$ |
| Let-go threshold current (AC) | $I_{LG}$ | **10–20** | mA |
| Ventricular fibrillation threshold (AC, 1s) | $I_{VF}$ | **100** | mA |
| DC shock threshold | $I_{DC}$ | **~300** | mA |
| Minimum ignition energy (hydrogen) | MIE | **0.019** | mJ |
| Minimum ignition energy (gasoline vapor) | MIE | **0.25** | mJ |
| Flash point of diesel | $T_{fp}$ | **>55** | °C |
| Auto-ignition of hydrogen | $T_{ai}$ | **500** | °C |
| Standard shipboard voltage (LV) | $V$ | **440 / 220** | V AC |
| High voltage shipboard (HV, modern vessels) | $V$ | **6.6 / 11** | kV AC |
| Arc flash incident energy threshold | $E_{AF}$ | **1.2** | cal/cm² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The body current rule of thumb** $I = \frac{V}{R_b}$ is the single most important equation on this topic. At 440 V across wet skin (1000 $\Omega$), the current is **440 mA** — well above the **100 mA** ventricular fibrillation threshold. This is why **440 V is lethal in a marine environment** where humidity, sweat, and salt are omnipresent.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchy of Electrical Hazards

Marine electrical hazards are best understood as a **four-tier pyramid of severity**, from nuisance to catastrophic:

### Tier 1 — Static Electricity & Minor Leakage
- Triboelectric charging of fuel during transfer
- Static on synthetic ropes and clothing
- **Energy level:** $\mu$J to mJ range
- **Mitigation:** Bonding cables between ship and shore, antistatic additives in fuel, conductive footwear, humidification

### Tier 2 — Electric Shock (Direct Contact)
- Touching a live conductor (phase) while body is grounded
- Contact with a faulty enclosure that has become energized
- **Equation:** $I_{body} = \dfrac{V_{touch}}{R_b + Z_{ground}}$

### Tier 3 — Arc Flash
- A short-circuit fault that ionizes air and creates a plasma channel
- **Incident Energy:** $E = \dfrac{4.184 \cdot V \cdot I_{arc} \cdot t}{d^2}$ (in cal/cm²)
- Temperatures can reach **35,000 °F (≈19,400 °C)** — hotter than the sun's surface

### Tier 4 — Explosion & Fire
- Ignition of flammable vapors (hydrocarbon fuels, hydrogen, paint fumes) by an electrical spark
- Battery room hydrogen explosion (4% to 75% H₂ in air is explosive)
- Catastrophic failure of HV switchgear

## 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Electric Shock — The Physiological Mechanism

A current passing through the human body produces four primary effects, each governed by a current threshold:

1. **Perception threshold:** $\approx 1$ mA AC, $\approx 5$ mA DC
2. **Muscular contraction (let-go) threshold:** $\approx 10$ mA AC, $\approx 30$ mA DC
3. **Respiratory muscle paralysis:** $\approx 30$ mA AC
4. **Ventricular fibrillation:** $\approx 100$ mA AC (for exposure $\geq 0.2$ s)
5. **Severe burns / cardiac arrest:** $> 1$ A

The governing relationship is Ohm's Law applied to the body:

$$I_{body} = \frac{V_{touch}}{Z_{body} + Z_{ground}}$$

Where $Z_{body}$ depends heavily on skin condition, contact area, and path through the body (hand-to-hand ≈ 1000 $\Omega$, hand-to-foot ≈ 500 $\Omega$).

### 2.2.2 Arc Flash Physics

When a fault current $I_{arc}$ flows across an ionized air gap, the arc is sustained because the air is now a plasma. The incident energy at working distance $d$ for an arc duration $t$ is approximated by:

$$E = \frac{V_{arc} \cdot I_{arc} \cdot t \cdot k}{d^2}$$

Where $k = 4.184$ is the unit conversion factor from Joules to calories. The IEEE 1584 standard refines this with empirical correction factors for enclosure size, electrode configuration, and grounding.

### 2.2.3 Hazardous Area Classification (Zone System)

Following **IEC 60079 / ATEX / SOLAS** framework:

| Zone | Definition | Typical Shipboard Location |
|---|---|---|
| **Zone 0** | Flammable atmosphere continuously present | Inside fuel tanks, cargo tanks of oil/product carriers |
| **Zone 1** | Likely to occur in normal operation | Battery rooms, paint stores, paint lockers, cofferdams adjacent to tanks |
| **Zone 2** | Not likely in normal operation, briefly if so | Engine room vicinity, deck near vents, bilges with fuel piping |
| **Non-Hazardous** | No flammable atmosphere expected | Accommodation spaces, navigation bridge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **type of electrical equipment** allowed in each zone is strictly prescribed: Zone 0 requires **Ex ia** (intrinsically safe), Zone 1 requires **Ex d** (flameproof) or **Ex e** (increased safety), Zone 2 accepts **Ex n** (non-sparking) or **Ex i**.

### 2.2.4 Ingress Protection (IP) Code — IEC 60529

The IP rating is a two-digit code:
- **First digit (0–6):** Solid particle protection
- **Second digit (0–9):** Liquid ingress protection

**Examples relevant to ships:**
- **IP 22:** Engine room auxiliary equipment (drip-proof)
- **IP 44:** Deck machinery (splash-proof)
- **IP 56:** Deck-exposed lighting (powerful jets)
- **IP 67:** Submersible pump motors (temporary immersion)
- **IP 68:** Underwater sensors (continuous immersion)

### 2.2.5 Battery Room Hazards

Lead-acid batteries (still the dominant shipboard technology) generate hydrogen during charging:

$$2H_2SO_4 + 2Pb \xrightarrow{\text{discharge}} 2PbSO_4 + 2H_2O$$
$$2PbSO_4 + 2H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{charge}} 2Pb + 2H_2SO_4 + O_2$$
$$2H_2 + O_2 \longrightarrow 2H_2O + \text{Energy (explosion)}$$

Hydrogen is explosive between **4%** and **75%** by volume in air. For this reason:
- Battery rooms are **Zone 1** classified
- Forced mechanical ventilation (≥ 6 air changes/hour) is mandatory
- All fittings are **flameproof (Ex d)**
- **No smoking / no naked flames** — ever
- Eye-wash and emergency shower within **10 m**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the High-Yield Cheat Sheet for KTU End Semester Exam — memorize before attempting the paper.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Body current on shock | $I = V / R_b$ | A | Electric shock calculation |
| 2 | Arc flash incident energy | $E = \dfrac{4.184 \cdot V \cdot I_{arc} \cdot t}{d^2}$ | cal/cm² | PPE category selection |
| 3 | Step potential | $V_{step} = I_{f} \cdot R_{s}$ | V | Earth fault at shore power |
| 4 | Touch potential | $V_{touch} = I_{f} \cdot R_{t}$ | V | Operator contact during fault |
| 5 | Hydrogen LEL | $4\%$ by volume | — | Battery room safety |
| 6 | Hydrogen UEL | $75\%$ by volume | — | Battery room safety |
| 7 | Let-go threshold | $I_{LG} = 10$ | mA | AC shock severity |
| 8 | Fibrillation threshold | $I_{VF} = 100$ | mA | AC shock severity |
| 9 | Ohm's law (general) | $V = I \cdot R$ | V | All circuits |
| 10 | Power dissipated as heat | $P = I^2 \cdot R$ | W | Fire initiation by overload |
| 11 | Energy in capacitor | $E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ | J | Stored energy hazards |
| 12 | Min. ignition energy | $MIE_{H_2} = 0.019$ | mJ | Explosion risk assessment |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These theoretical models drive:
- **PPE Category Selection** (IEEE 1584 / NFPA 70E) for shipyard workers
- **Earthing System Design** (IT, TN, TT systems on ships — typically **IT** for safer first-fault behavior)
- **Hazardous Area Classification Drawings** (mandatory for class approval by IACS members)
- **SOLAS Chapter II-1** electrical safety compliance
- **Classification Society Rules** (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, BV, IRS) for ship construction
- **Risk Assessment** under ISO 12100 and ISO 31000 adopted by major shipyards

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Body Current During an Electrical Shock on Board

**Problem:** A crew member wearing wet boots (body resistance including ground path $Z_b = 1000 \; \Omega$) touches a single-phase 220 V AC live conductor on a deck winch whose metal enclosure has lost its earth connection. The ship's neutral is solidly grounded. Calculate the body current and classify the hazard severity.

### Step 1 — Identify the Circuit Topology

The circuit formed is:

$$\text{Line} \rightarrow \text{Body} \rightarrow \text{Boots/Skin} \rightarrow \text{Deck} \rightarrow \text{Ship's Hull} \rightarrow \text{Neutral} \rightarrow \text{Source}$$

This is a series circuit. The supply voltage is the 220 V AC line-to-neutral voltage of the standard shipboard auxiliary system.

### Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law for AC

For AC circuits, we use impedance $Z$ instead of pure resistance $R$:

$$I_{body} = \frac{V_{touch}}{Z_{body}}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values

We are told $V_{touch} = 220$ V (touch voltage is the full line-to-neutral voltage because the enclosure is not earthed). The body impedance under wet conditions is $Z_{body} = 1000 \; \Omega$.

$$I_{body} = \frac{220 \; V}{1000 \; \Omega}$$

### Step 4 — Perform the Division

We convert 220 V to millivolts for clarity:

$$220 \; V = 220{,}000 \; mV$$

$$I_{body} = \frac{220{,}000 \; mV}{1000 \; \Omega} = 220 \; mA$$

### Step 5 — Classify the Hazard

Compare $I_{body} = 220 \; mA$ with the physiological thresholds:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{perception} &= 1 \; mA \quad (\text{trivial}) \\
I_{let\text{-}go} &= 10 \; mA \quad (\text{muscle lock}) \\
I_{fibrillation} &= 100 \; mA \quad (\text{heart arrest}) \\
I_{body,calculated} &= 220 \; mA
\end{aligned}$$

Since $220 \; mA > 100 \; mA$, the current exceeds the ventricular fibrillation threshold by a factor of 2.2. **Result: Lethal hazard — the crew member is almost certain to suffer cardiac fibrillation within 0.2 s of contact.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always state the *comparison* explicitly. Marks are lost when students just compute the number and forget to interpret against the threshold.

## 3.2 Derivation: Arc Flash Incident Energy on a Main Switchboard

**Problem:** A 440 V, three-phase shipboard main switchboard experiences a three-phase bolted fault current of $I_{arc} = 25$ kA. The upstream breaker clears the fault in $t = 0.5$ s. A technician is working at a distance of $d = 0.45$ m (18 inches, the standard working distance per IEEE 1584). Calculate the incident energy and select the PPE category.

### Step 1 — Recall the Arc Flash Energy Equation

The simplified IEEE 1584-style approximation for incident energy at the working distance is:

$$E = \frac{4.184 \cdot V_{LL} \cdot I_{arc} \cdot t}{d^2}$$

Where the factor $4.184$ converts Joules to calories (cal/cm² requires energy per unit area, and 1 calorie = 4.184 J).

### Step 2 — Convert Units to SI Base

$$V_{LL} = 440 \; V$$
$$I_{arc} = 25 \; kA = 25{,}000 \; A$$
$$t = 0.5 \; s$$
$$d = 0.45 \; m$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into the Equation

$$E = \frac{4.184 \cdot 440 \cdot 25{,}000 \cdot 0.5}{(0.45)^2}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Numerator

First compute the product $440 \cdot 25{,}000 \cdot 0.5$:

$$440 \cdot 25{,}000 = 11{,}000{,}000$$

$$11{,}000{,}000 \cdot 0.5 = 5{,}500{,}000$$

Now multiply by $4.184$:

$$4.184 \cdot 5{,}500{,}000 = 23{,}012{,}000$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate the Denominator

$$(0.45)^2 = 0.2025$$

### Step 6 — Final Division

$$E = \frac{23{,}012{,}000}{0.2025} = 113{,}640{,}740 \; J/m^2$$

Wait — we must convert J/m² to cal/cm². Recall that 1 cal/cm² = 41,868 J/m² (since 1 cal = 4.184 J, and 1 m² = 10,000 cm², so 1 cal/cm² = 4.184 × 10,000 = 41,840 J/m²).

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{113{,}640{,}740 \; J/m^2}{41{,}840 \; J/(cal/cm^2)} \\
E &\approx 2{,}716 \; cal/cm^2
\end{aligned}$$

This is an **astronomically high** value. In real marine practice, the upstream breaker clearing time is much shorter (e.g., 0.05 s for instantaneous trips), giving:

$$E \approx 272 \; cal/cm^2 \rightarrow \text{still Category 4 (Dangerous)}$$

### Step 7 — Select PPE

NFPA 70E / IEEE 1584 PPE Categories:

| Category | Incident Energy (cal/cm²) | PPE Required |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $\leq 1.2$ | Standard work clothing |
| 1 | $1.2 - 4$ | Arc-rated 4 cal/cm² suit |
| 2 | $4 - 8$ | Arc-rated 8 cal/cm² suit |
| 3 | $8 - 25$ | Arc-rated 25 cal/cm² suit + hood |
| 4 | $25 - 40$ | Arc-rated 40 cal/cm² suit + hood |
| **Dangerous** | $> 40$ | Do not work energized; de-energize first |

**Conclusion:** At $E \approx 272$ cal/cm², the situation is *beyond Category 4 — the switchboard MUST be de-energized before any work is performed.* No PPE on earth can protect against this.

> [!WARNING]
> **Real-world lesson:** This is exactly why **LOTO (Lockout-Tagout)** and **PTW (Permit-to-Work)** systems are sacred on ships. A 440 V 25 kA fault is no joke — it can vaporize copper, destroy switchboards, and kill.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Hazard Scenarios on Ships

| Scenario | Hazard Source | Energy Level | Mitigation | Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Engine room oil mist + hot surface | Fire | Thermal | Flameproof fittings, oil mist detector | SOLAS Reg II-1/40 |
| Fuel transfer (bunkering) | Static electricity | $\mu$J | Bonding wire between ship and barge | ISGOTT, IEC 60079-32-1 |
| Battery room charging | H₂ explosion | mJ | Forced ventilation, Ex d fittings | SOLAS Reg II-1/45 |
| HV switchboard 6.6 kV | Arc flash | MJ | Remote racking, arc-flash detection | IEEE 1584, NFPA 70E |
| Deck winch in rain | Electric shock | Lethal | IP56 enclosure, RCD (30 mA) | IEC 60364, IEEE 45 |
| Paint locker | Flammable vapor | mJ | Ex ia equipment, no electricals | SOLAS, IEC 60079 |
| LNG carrier cargo area | Cryogenic + flammable | High | Inert gas, no electricals in tanks | IGF Code |
| Shipyard welding near fuel tank | Spark ignition | High | Gas-free certificate, hot-work permit | ISGOTT |
| Rotating generator | Mechanical entrapment | Kinetic | Guards, insulation of couplings | SOLAS Reg II-1/43 |
| Corrosion in salt environment | Insulation failure | Slow | Cathodic protection, regular megger testing | IEEE 45 |

## 3.4 Derivation: Hydrogen Concentration in a Battery Room

**Problem:** A battery room has volume $V = 30$ m³. Eight lead-acid cells are being charged at 400 A total. Calculate whether the hydrogen concentration will exceed 4% (LEL) within 1 hour if ventilation is OFF.

### Step 1 — Hydrogen Generation Rate

The empirical formula for hydrogen evolution from lead-acid batteries is:

$$V_{H_2} = 0.418 \cdot I \cdot n \cdot t$$

Where:
- $V_{H_2}$ = volume of H₂ in liters
- $I$ = charging current in Amperes
- $n$ = number of cells
- $t$ = time in hours
- $0.418$ = empirical constant (liters H₂ per Ah per cell)

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$V_{H_2} = 0.418 \cdot 400 \cdot 8 \cdot 1 = 1337.6 \; L$$

### Step 3 — Compute Volume Fraction

Room volume = 30 m³ = 30,000 L.

$$\text{Concentration} = \frac{1337.6}{30{,}000} = 0.0446 = 4.46\%$$

### Step 4 — Compare with LEL

Since $4.46\% > 4\%$ (LEL), the room is **EXPLOSIVE within 1 hour** without ventilation.

### Step 5 — Ventilation Requirement

Standard SOLAS requires at least **6 air changes per hour** = 180,000 L/h. After 1 hour with ventilation, the H₂ would be diluted to:

$$C = \frac{1337.6 \text{ L}}{180{,}000 \text{ L/h} \cdot 1 \text{ h} + 30{,}000} \approx 0.64\%$$

This is well below LEL — **safe**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This derivation is a classic KTU exam question.** Memorize the $0.418$ factor and the $4\%$ LEL.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Format: KTU 2024 ESE Part A — 2-mark conceptual + 1-mark key term. Direct, no choices. Average answer length: 80–120 words.

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain any three potential electrical hazards that may be encountered on board a ship.**

**Model Answer:**
The three principal potential electrical hazards on board a ship are:

1. **Electric Shock:** Caused by direct or indirect contact with a live conductor. The body acts as a current path to the ship's hull (which is grounded via the neutral). Even 220 V AC is lethal under wet or perspiring conditions, with body currents exceeding the 10 mA let-go threshold.

2. **Arc Flash:** A short-circuit fault that creates a sustained plasma arc with temperatures up to 35,000 °F (≈ 19,400 °C), generating intense heat, pressure wave (~2000 lbf/ft²), toxic copper vapor, and sound (≈ 140 dB). It can destroy switchgear and cause fatal burns.

3. **Static Electricity:** Generated during fuel transfer (bunkering) due to triboelectric charging of hydrocarbon liquids. The accumulated charge can discharge as a spark, igniting fuel vapors. Mitigated by bonding cables between ship and shore.

*(3 marks — 1 per hazard with one-line explanation each)*

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the meaning of the term "Ingress Protection (IP)" as applied to marine electrical equipment. Give two examples of IP ratings used on ships and the equipment they are typically applied to.**

**Model Answer:**
**Ingress Protection (IP)** is a two-digit coding system defined by **IEC 60529** that classifies the degree of protection an enclosure provides against intrusion of solid foreign objects (first digit, 0–6) and liquids (second digit, 0–9).

**Examples relevant to marine use:**

1. **IP 22** — Drip-proof. Used for **engine room auxiliary equipment** such as control panels where falling water drops are expected but not heavy jets.

2. **IP 56** — Dust-protected and protected against powerful water jets. Used for **deck-exposed lighting fixtures and junction boxes** that must withstand wave splash and rain.

3. *(Optional 3rd example)* **IP 67** — Temporary immersion. Used for **submersible bilge pump motors** and underwater sensors.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 each for two IP examples with application)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks per module with internal choice. Each sub-part is 7 marks. Provide a detailed model answer for each.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Discuss in detail the physiological effects of electric shock on the human body. Explain the various threshold current values and their significance. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Physiological Effects of Electric Shock:**

When current passes through the human body, it interacts with the nervous and muscular systems, producing effects that depend on the magnitude, frequency, duration, and path of the current.

**Threshold Current Values and Their Significance:**

| Threshold | Current (AC 50/60 Hz) | Current (DC) | Physiological Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Perception | **1 mA** | **5 mA** | Slight tingling sensation; first detectable feeling |
| Maximum Let-Go | **10 mA** | **30 mA** | Muscles contract; victim cannot release conductor |
| Respiratory Paralysis | **30 mA** | **80 mA** | Breathing muscles lock; asphyxiation if prolonged |
| Ventricular Fibrillation | **100 mA** (≥ 0.2 s) | **300 mA** | Disorganized heart rhythm; usually fatal without defibrillation |
| Myocardial Sustained Contraction | **> 5 A** | **> 5 A** | Heart "clamps"; may return to normal rhythm if current removed |
| Severe Burns | **> 5 A** | **> 5 A** | Tissue destruction at contact points and along current path |

**Significance of Each Threshold:**

1. **1 mA Perception Threshold:** Below this, the shock is not consciously felt. Important for designing safe touch voltages in dry environments (limit: 50 V AC).

2. **10 mA Let-Go Threshold:** Above this, the victim cannot voluntarily release the conductor. This is the most critical threshold for handheld tools, e.g., a 220 V hand drill on deck. Residual Current Devices (RCDs) for personnel protection are set at **30 mA** to ensure they trip *before* the let-go threshold is exceeded for the full duration.

3. **30 mA Respiratory Threshold:** At this level, sustained exposure causes suffocation even if the heart continues. This is why RCDs at 30 mA are mandatory for socket-outlets on ships.

4. **100 mA Fibrillation Threshold:** This is the threshold above which the heart enters a chaotic rhythm that does not pump blood. Survival is rare without immediate defibrillation. Note that the threshold is **time-dependent** — for exposures below 0.2 s, higher currents can be tolerated.

5. **Burns and Tissue Damage:** At very high currents, Joule heating ($P = I^2 R$) causes thermal necrosis at entry and exit points (the "Joule burn" pattern).

**Path Dependency:**

- Hand-to-foot path passes current through the heart → highest risk
- Hand-to-hand path also crosses the heart
- Foot-to-foot (step potential) is least dangerous to the heart but can cause falls from height (e.g., from a mast)

**Conclusion:** The body behaves as a non-linear impedance ($Z_b$ varies with voltage and frequency). The KTU-expected formula is $I = V/Z_b$, and at standard shipboard 440 V with wet skin ($Z_b \approx 1000 \; \Omega$), the current is $440$ mA — well into the lethal zone.

*(7 marks — 2 for table of thresholds, 3 for explanation, 1 for path dependency, 1 for conclusion)*

**(b) What is meant by Hazardous Area Classification on ships? Describe the Zone 0, Zone 1, and Zone 2 classifications with suitable shipboard examples. Explain the type of electrical equipment permitted in each zone. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Hazardous area classification is a systematic zoning of shipboard spaces based on the **probability of presence of a flammable atmosphere** (gas, vapor, mist, or dust) that could be ignited by electrical equipment. It is governed by **IEC 60079**, **ATEX 2014/34/EU**, and classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS).

**The Three Zones (per IEC 60079-10-1):**

**Zone 0:** An area in which an **explosive gas-air mixture is continuously present, or present for long periods**.
- *Shipboard Examples:* Interior of fuel tanks, cargo tanks of oil tankers, bilge wells containing fuel, cofferdams with permanent fuel vapor presence.
- *Equipment Permitted:* **Ex ia (Intrinsically Safe)** — equipment in which the energy in any circuit (both normal and fault conditions) is below the Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE) of the surrounding atmosphere. No sparking or thermal effects capable of causing ignition are possible.

**Zone 1:** An area in which an **explosive gas-air mixture is likely to occur in normal operation**.
- *Shipboard Examples:* Battery rooms (during charging), paint lockers, spaces adjacent to fuel tanks, pump rooms of product carriers, areas near tank vents.
- *Equipment Permitted:* **Ex d (Flameproof Enclosure)** — containment type that can withstand internal explosion without propagating; or **Ex e (Increased Safety)** — no arcs, sparks, or hot surfaces in normal service; or **Ex p (Pressurized)** — purged with clean air to prevent ingress.

**Zone 2:** An area in which an **explosive gas-air mixture is not likely to occur in normal operation, and if it does, only for a short period**.
- *Shipboard Examples:* Engine room, machinery spaces, areas within 1.5 m of fuel tank vents, weather decks near bunker stations, holds of LNG carriers outside the cargo area.
- *Equipment Permitted:* **Ex n (Non-Sparking)** — also called "Type n" or "Ex nA"; provides a level of protection during normal operation. Standard industrial equipment of good build quality with no arcs is acceptable; not required to be certified for Zone 1.

**Summary Table:**

| Zone | Atmosphere | Examples | Equipment Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Continuous | Fuel tanks, cofferdams | Ex ia |
| 1 | Likely in normal op | Battery rooms, paint lockers | Ex d, Ex e, Ex p |
| 2 | Brief / abnormal | Engine room, deck near vents | Ex n, Ex i, standard |

**Key Principle:** The classification drives every design choice — from cable entry (Ex-rated glands), to lighting (Ex d fluorescent), to switches (Ex e rotary), to even the certification marking on nameplates. The classification drawing must be approved by the classification society *before* construction.

*(7 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for zones + examples, 2 for equipment types, 2 for summary table and principle)*

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a) Explain in detail the various fire and explosion hazards associated with battery rooms on ships. Discuss the safety measures required under SOLAS for battery room installations. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Fire and Explosion Hazards in Battery Rooms:**

Lead-acid and nickel-cadmium batteries (used for emergency power, UPS, and engine starting) generate **hydrogen gas (H₂)** during the charging process due to electrolysis of water in the electrolyte. Hydrogen has uniquely dangerous properties:

1. **Wide Explosive Range:** Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) = **4%** by volume in air; Upper Explosive Limit (UEL) = **75%** by volume. This wide range makes hydrogen one of the most easily ignited gases.

2. **Low Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE):** Only **0.019 mJ** — a static discharge of just touching a doorknob can ignite it.

3. **Invisible Flame:** Hydrogen burns with a pale blue flame that is nearly invisible in daylight.

4. **Buoyancy:** Hydrogen rises rapidly to the ceiling, where it can accumulate in pockets if ventilation is poor.

5. **High Flame Speed:** Detonation can occur in confined spaces, producing destructive overpressures.

**Other Battery Hazards:**
- **Sulfuric acid splashes** (from lead-acid) causing severe chemical burns
- **Thermal runaway** in lithium-ion batteries (modern installations), releasing flammable electrolyte vapors
- **Corrosion** of cable terminations and terminals
- **Electric shock** from exposed terminals (battery voltage is typically 24 V or 110/220 V DC for shipboard banks)

**SOLAS Safety Measures for Battery Rooms (Regulation II-1/45 & Chapter II-2):**

1. **Ventilation:** **Forced mechanical ventilation** providing at least **6 air changes per hour**, with exhaust taken from the **highest point** of the room (where hydrogen accumulates). Ventilation must be continuous during charging and on standby.

2. **Equipment Certification:** All electrical fittings (lights, switches, fans) must be of a **certified safe type** suitable for **Zone 1** classification — typically **Ex d (flameproof)** or **Ex e (increased safety)**.

3. **No Naked Flames:** Strict prohibition of smoking, open flames, or sparking equipment. Prominent signage required.

4. **Cable Installation:** Cables must be **armored** (e.g., XLPE/SWA/PVC) with **gastight gland entries**. Mineral-insulated cables (MICC) are preferred for the highest safety level.

5. **Structural Requirements:** Battery rooms must be **gas-tight** from adjacent accommodation spaces, with **self-closing fire-rated doors** (A-60 class).

6. **Emergency Equipment:** **Eye-wash stations** and **emergency showers** must be located within 10 m of the room, but **outside** the hazardous area.

7. **Detection:** **Hydrogen detectors** set to alarm at 25% of LEL (i.e., 1% H₂) and to trip the battery charger at 50% of LEL (i.e., 2% H₂) are increasingly required by classification societies.

8. **Bonding and Earthing:** All metallic parts must be bonded to the ship's hull to prevent static accumulation.

9. **Maintenance:** Electrolyte levels, specific gravity, and cell voltages must be logged daily; terminals must be kept clean and tight to prevent hot spots.

**Conclusion:** The combination of low MIE, wide explosive range, and continuous generation makes battery rooms one of the highest-risk spaces on a ship. The SOLAS-prescribed layered safety (ventilation + Ex equipment + detection + structural isolation) is non-negotiable.

*(7 marks — 2 for hazard properties, 3 for SOLAS measures, 1 for detection, 1 for conclusion)*

**(b) What is an Arc Flash? With a neat diagram, describe the phenomenon of an arc flash. Explain the hazards caused by arc flash and the PPE categories used to protect personnel. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Arc Flash:**
An **arc flash** is a sustained electric discharge through ionized air (plasma) that occurs when a sufficient potential difference exists across a gap, typically due to a short-circuit fault, insulation failure, or accidental contact with energized conductors. The resulting plasma channel can reach temperatures of **35,000 °F (≈ 19,400 °C)**, nearly four times the temperature of the sun's surface.

**Diagram of Arc Flash Phenomenon:**

```
   [Phase A]      [Phase B]      [Phase C]
       |              |              |
       ●━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━●     ← Arc Plasma Channel
                      ║
                [Operator at d]
                  ⇣ Incident Energy
```

The arc fault occurs between phases or phase-to-ground. The operator at working distance $d$ receives the incident energy $E$.

**Hazards Caused by Arc Flash:**

1. **Thermal Burns (Primary Hazard):** The intense radiant heat can cause fatal second- and third-degree burns even at distances of several meters. The threshold for a curable (second-degree) burn is **1.2 cal/cm²**.

2. **Pressure Wave (Arc Blast):** The rapid expansion of ionized air and vaporized metal produces a pressure wave of up to **2000 lbf/ft²**, capable of throwing a worker across a room, collapsing lungs, and rupturing eardrums.

3. **Sound Blast:** Levels up to **140 dB** — equivalent to a jet engine at close range — causing permanent hearing loss.

4. **Toxic Vapors:** Vaporized copper, insulation materials, and switchboard components produce toxic fumes (CuO, dioxins, PFAS) that can cause respiratory damage and metal-fume fever.

5. **High-Intensity Light (UV/IR):** The arc emits extreme UV and IR radiation, capable of causing **arc eye** (photokeratitis) — a painful but temporary blindness from corneal burn.

6. **Molten Metal Spray:** Copper and aluminum conductors vaporize and recondense as fine molten droplets, embedding in skin and igniting clothing.

7. **Equipment Destruction:** Switchboards, cables, and surrounding structures are often completely destroyed, with repair costs running into millions of dollars.

**PPE Categories (per NFPA 70E / IEEE 1584):**

| Category | Incident Energy (cal/cm²) | PPE Required |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $\leq 1.2$ | Standard work clothing |
| 1 | $1.2$ – $4$ | Arc-rated 4 cal/cm² suit, face shield |
| 2 | $4$ – $8$ | Arc-rated 8 cal/cm² suit, hood, gloves |
| 3 | $8$ – $25$ | Arc-rated 25 cal/cm² suit + hood |
| 4 | $25$ – $40$ | Arc-rated 40 cal/cm² suit + hood |
| Dangerous | $> 40$ | Do not work energized |

**Best Practice on Ships:**
- **Energized work is prohibited** above 40 cal/cm²
- **Arc flash study** (incident energy calculation) is mandatory before any work on LV switchboards
- **Remote racking** devices keep operators outside the arc-flash boundary
- **Arc-flash detection relays** (e.g., ABB Arc Guard, Schneider Vamp) trip the breaker in < 4 ms

**Conclusion:** Arc flash is one of the most catastrophic electrical hazards on a ship, where switchboards are often located in narrow engine rooms with limited escape routes. Mitigation is achieved through proper PPE, faster protection, and a strict **Permit-to-Work** culture.

*(7 marks — 1 for definition + diagram, 3 for hazards, 2 for PPE table, 1 for best practice)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Examinations (Module 4 — Potential Hazards):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the comparison with the threshold:** Many students compute $I = V/R$ correctly but fail to write the conclusion — e.g., "440 mA > 100 mA fibrillation threshold → LETHAL." Examiners *specifically* look for this comparison. **You lose 1 mark without it.**
> 
> 2. **Confusing LEL with UEL:** Hydrogen LEL is **4%**, UEL is **75%**. Mixing these up is a fatal conceptual error. The LEL is what triggers alarms (typically at 25% of LEL = 1% H₂).
> 
> 3. **Mixing up IP digits:** Students often swap the first and second digits. **First digit = Solids, Second digit = Liquids.** A mnemonic: "**S**olids come **f**irst in the alphabet." IP 56 means **5** = dust-protected, **6** = powerful water jets.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting units in arc flash calculation:** The answer must be in **cal/cm²**, not J/m². The conversion factor is 4.184 × 10,000 = 41,840. Examiners check units strictly.
> 
> 5. **Naming zones but not the equipment type:** Simply stating "Zone 0, Zone 1, Zone 2" is incomplete. KTU expects you to name the **type of equipment** permitted — Ex ia, Ex d, Ex e, Ex n respectively. Marks are awarded only when both are paired.
> 
> 6. **Neglecting the hydrogen derivation constant 0.418:** In the $V_{H_2} = 0.418 \cdot I \cdot n \cdot t$ formula, the constant 0.418 must be stated explicitly and the units (liters H₂ per Ah per cell) must be mentioned.
> 
> 7. **Confusing arc flash with electric shock:** Arc flash is a *plasma event* with thermal, pressure, light, and acoustic effects. Electric shock is *current through the body*. They are *not* the same thing. Examiners deduct marks for using them interchangeably.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this page the night before the exam — it covers the highest-frequency points in KTU 2024 Module 4.**

- [ ] **Body current formula:** $I = V / R_b$. At 440 V + wet skin ($R_b = 1000 \; \Omega$), $I = 440$ mA — lethal.
- [ ] **Five physiological thresholds (AC):** 1, 10, 30, 100, 5000 mA — Perception, Let-go, Respiratory paralysis, Fibrillation, Burns.
- [ ] **Arc flash temperature:** 35,000 °F ≈ 19,400 °C. Pressure: 2000 lbf/ft². Sound: 140 dB.
- [ ] **Arc flash PPE threshold:** 1.2 cal/cm². Above 40 cal/cm² — do not work energized.
- [ ] **Hydrogen LEL = 4%, UEL = 75%, MIE = 0.019 mJ.** Battery rooms are Zone 1.
- [ ] **Ventilation in battery rooms:** 6 air changes per hour minimum; exhaust at high level.
- [ ] **Zone 0 → Ex ia**, **Zone 1 → Ex d or Ex e or Ex p**, **Zone 2 → Ex n**.
- [ ] **IP rating rule:** First digit = solids, second digit = liquids. IP 22 (engine room), IP 56 (deck), IP 67 (submersible).
- [ ] **Hydrogen generation formula:** $V_{H_2} = 0.418 \cdot I \cdot n \cdot t$ liters.
- [ ] **Minimum ignition energies:** Hydrogen 0.019 mJ, gasoline 0.25 mJ, methane 0.28 mJ, propane 0.26 mJ.
- [ ] **SOLAS Reg II-1/45:** Battery room safety (ventilation, Ex equipment, doors, no naked flames).
- [ ] **SOLAS Reg II-1/40:** Fire prevention in machinery spaces.
- [ ] **IEEE 1584:** Standard for arc flash hazard calculation.
- [ ] **IEEE 45:** Recommended Practice for Electrical Installations on Shipboard.
- [ ] **IEC 60079:** Explosive atmospheres (the Ex zoning series).
- [ ] **IEC 60529:** Degrees of protection provided by enclosures (IP Code).
- [ ] **NFPA 70E:** Standard for Electrical Safety in the Workplace (PPE selection).
- [ ] **ISGOTT:** International Safety Guide for Oil Tankers and Terminals (bunkering, gas-free, hot work).
- [ ] **IACS:** International Association of Classification Societies — unified rules for hazardous area classification.
- [ ] **LOTO:** Lockout-Tagout — the cardinal rule for safe electrical work.
- [ ] **IT earthing system** is the default on ships — first fault does not trip, but is detected by insulation monitor (safer for personnel).
- [ ] **Bunkering hazards:** Static electricity — bond ship to shore before any transfer.
- [ ] **Arc-flash boundary:** The distance at which incident energy = 1.2 cal/cm² (second-degree burn threshold). No one may enter unprotected inside this boundary.
- [ ] **Step potential:** Voltage developed between feet of a person standing near an earth fault. Important in shipyard shore-power fault scenarios.
- [ ] **Touch potential:** Voltage between an energized object and the feet of a person in contact with it. Limited by equipotential bonding.
- [ ] **Permit-to-Work (PTW):** A formal written authorization for non-routine work in hazardous areas (hot work, cold work, enclosed space entry, electrical isolation).
- [ ] **Confined Space Entry** on ships (e.g., cargo tanks, cofferdams, double bottoms) requires gas testing (LEL < 1%, O₂ 21%, no H₂S) and a stand-by person at the entry.
- [ ] **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- [ ] **Five common shipboard hot spots for hazards:** Engine room, battery room, paint locker, bunker station, switchboard room.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:** This module is a *knowledge + application* module. Marks are awarded for: (1) Correct numerical evaluation with units, (2) Comparison against threshold/standard, (3) Real-world marine examples, and (4) Reference to the correct SOLAS/IEC/IEEE clause. Memorize the *numbers* (thresholds, constants), the *formulas* (body current, hydrogen generation, arc energy), and the *zone definitions*. You will score well.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maritime Labour/Industry Recommendations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST524 - ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN SHIPS AND SHIPYARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maritime Labour/Industry Recommendations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Maritime Labour/Industry Recommendations** refer to the comprehensive set of international conventions, codes, regulations, and industry standards established by global maritime governing bodies to govern the working conditions, safety protocols, training requirements, and technical operational standards for seafarers, shipboard personnel, and shipyard workers — particularly those engaged with electrical systems, installations, and maintenance activities on board vessels and in shipbuilding/repair facilities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Maritime Labour/Industry Recommendations encompass the ILO Maritime Labour Convention (MLC 2006), IMO conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW), Classification Society Rules, and industry codes that govern electrical system design, installation, operation, maintenance, and the human workforce handling these systems in ships and shipyards."

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Maritime Governance

The maritime regulatory framework rests on **three principal international bodies** that influence electrical systems and the workforce handling them:

| Body | Full Name | Scope |
|------|-----------|-------|
| **ILO** | International Labour Organization | Seafarer welfare, working conditions, contracts |
| **IMO** | International Maritime Organization | Ship safety, design, construction, equipment |
| **IEC/IEEE** | International Electrotechnical Commission / Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers | Technical electrical standards |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Maritime "Rule Book"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Ship as a Floating City**
>
> Imagine a ship as a **self-contained floating city** with its own power plant, hospital, hotel, and factory. Just as a city has building codes, electrical codes, labor laws, and safety inspectors, a ship must follow a **layered set of rules**:
>
> - **The Constitution** → IMO Conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL) — defines what a ship MUST have
> - **The Labor Law** → ILO MLC 2006 — defines how workers MUST be treated
> - **The Electrical Code** → IEC 60092 / IEEE 45 — defines HOW electrical work is done
> - **The Quality Inspector** → Classification Societies (DNV, LR, ABS) — verifies compliance
>
> For an **electrical engineer on a ship**, all four layers apply simultaneously. A single cable run must satisfy SOLAS fire safety, MLC crew safety, IEC technical specs, AND DNV/LR class rules.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters for KTU Exam

> [!WARNING]
> **High-Weightage Alert:**
> Maritime Labour/Industry Recommendations is a **frequently asked topic** in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) under Module 4. Students often lose marks by:
> - Confusing IMO with ILO roles
> - Missing specific MLC 2006 titles
> - Forgetting the difference between **Convention** (legally binding) and **Recommendation** (advisory)

---

**Key Constants and Standards Referenced:**

- **MLC 2006** — Entry into force: **20 August 2013**
- **STCW** — Manila Amendments entered force: **1 January 2012**
- **SOLAS** — Consolidated edition updated periodically; current: **2024 Consolidated Edition**
- **Standard Maritime Working Hours**: **14 hours/day max**, **72 hours/week max** (MLC Reg 2.3)
- **Minimum Crew Rest Hours**: **10 hours/24 hrs**, **77 hours/7 days** (STCW Manila)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ILO Maritime Labour Convention (MLC) 2006

### 2.1.1 Background and Significance

The **MLC 2006** is often called the **"Seafarers' Bill of Rights."** It consolidated **68 existing ILO maritime labour instruments** into a single, coherent, and globally applicable convention. It represents the **fourth pillar** of the international maritime regulatory regime, alongside SOLAS, MARPOL, and STCW.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MLC 2006 — Key Facts:**
> - Adopted: **23 February 2006**
> - Entry into force: **20 August 2013** (after 30 ratifications representing 33% of world tonnage)
> - Often called: **"The Seafarers' Bill of Rights"**
> - Ratified by **100+ countries** representing over **90% of world shipping tonnage**

### 2.1.2 Structure: Five Titles of MLC 2006

The MLC 2006 is divided into **five major Titles**, each addressing a different aspect of seafarer welfare:

| Title | Subject | Key Areas |
|-------|---------|-----------|
| **Title 1** | **Minimum requirements for seafarers to work on a ship** | Minimum age (16), medical fitness, training, recruitment |
| **Title 2** | **Conditions of employment** | Wages, hours of work/rest, leave, repatriation |
| **Title 3** | **Accommodation, recreational facilities, food, and catering** | Living standards, mess rooms, ventilation, lighting |
| **Title 4** | **Health protection, medical care, welfare, and social security** | On-board medical care, occupational safety, accident prevention |
| **Title 5** | **Compliance and enforcement** | Flag state, port state, labour-supply state responsibilities |

> [!NOTE]
> **Relevance to Electrical Engineers (KTU Focus):**
> - **Title 2** mandates **safe working hours** for engineers
> - **Title 3** specifies **electrical lighting standards** in accommodation
> - **Title 4** is the **most critical** — it mandates **safe working conditions for electrical maintenance**, including PPE, lockout/tagout, and accident prevention procedures

### 2.1.3 The "Convention + Code + Recommendation" Triad

MLC 2006 is unique in using a **three-tier structure** to give flexibility to ratifying states:

```
MLC 2006
   ├── Convention Articles (Legally binding, mandatory)
   ├── Code (Implementation rules)
   │     ├── Part A (Mandatory standards)
   │     └── Part B (Non-mandatory guidelines)
   └── Recommendation (Advisory, supplementary)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention vs. Code vs. Recommendation:**
> - **Convention Articles** = Mandatory in principle; countries accepting must implement
> - **Code Part A** = Mandatory minimum standards for all ratifying countries
> - **Code Part B** = Implementation guidance; "should" rather than "shall"
> - **Recommendation** = Advisory only, not legally binding

## 2.2 IMO Conventions and Recommendations for Electrical Systems

### 2.2.1 SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) — Electrical Provisions

The **SOLAS Convention** is the most important international treaty concerning merchant ship safety. Electrical provisions are primarily in **Chapter II-1** (Construction — Subdivision and Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations) and **Chapter II-2** (Fire Protection, Fire Detection, Fire Extinction).

**Key SOLAS Electrical Requirements (Chapter II-1, Part D — Electrical Installations):**

| Regulation | Requirement |
|------------|-------------|
| **Reg II-1/40** | General electrical installation requirements |
| **Reg II-1/41** | Main source of electrical power (generators, capacity) |
| **Reg II-1/42** | Emergency source of electrical power (batteries, emergency generator) |
| **Reg II-1/43** | Emergency lighting |
| **Reg II-1/44** | Starting arrangements for emergency generator |
| **Reg II-1/45** | Precautions against shock, fire, and other hazards |

### 2.2.2 SOLAS Emergency Power Time Requirements

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Formula: Emergency Battery Capacity**
>
> $$C_{battery} = \frac{P_{load} \times t_{autonomy}}{V_{system} \times \eta_{inverter}}$$
>
> Where:
> - $C_{battery}$ = Battery capacity in **Ampere-hours (Ah)**
> - $P_{load}$ = Total emergency load in **Watts (W)**
> - $t_{autonomy}$ = Required autonomy in **hours (h)**
> - $V_{system}$ = Battery bus voltage in **Volts (V)**
> - $\eta_{inverter}$ = Inverter efficiency (typically **0.85 to 0.95**)
>
> **SOLAS-mandated autonomy durations:**
> - Cargo/ passenger ships: **36 hours** minimum
> - Periodically unattended machinery spaces: **6 hours** for transitional source

### 2.2.3 STCW (Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping)

The **STCW Convention and Code** sets the qualification standards for masters, officers, and watchkeeping ratings. For electrical engineers, the relevant provisions are:

**STCW Manila Amendments (2010) — Electrical/Engineering Personnel:**

| Role | Required Certification | Key Competencies |
|------|------------------------|------------------|
| **Chief Engineer** | Certificate of Competency (CoC) — Management Level | Management of electrical/electronic/control engineering systems |
| **Second/Third Engineer** | CoC — Operational Level | Operation, surveillance, maintenance of electrical systems |
| **Electro-Technical Officer (ETO)** | CoC per STCW Reg III/6 | Specialized electrical/electronic maintenance |

> [!NOTE]
> **STCW Working Hours — Rest Periods (Regulation VIII/1):**
> - Maximum working hours: **14 hours in any 24-hour period**
> - Maximum working hours: **72 hours in any 7-day period**
> - Minimum rest hours: **10 hours in any 24-hour period**
> - Minimum rest hours: **77 hours in any 7-day period**
> - Rest hours may be divided into **no more than 2 periods**, one of which must be **at least 6 consecutive hours**

## 2.3 Classification Society Rules for Electrical Systems

**Classification Societies** are non-governmental organizations that establish and apply technical standards for ship design, construction, and survey. They are critical for industry recommendations.

### 2.3.1 Major Classification Societies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IACS — International Association of Classification Societies:**
> Founded **1968**, with **12 member societies** representing **~90% of world tonnage**.

| Abbreviation | Society | Country |
|--------------|---------|---------|
| **DNV** | Det Norske Veritas | Norway (merged with Germanischer Lloyd in 2013) |
| **LR** | Lloyd's Register | UK |
| **ABS** | American Bureau of Shipping | USA |
| **BV** | Bureau Veritas | France |
| **CCS** | China Classification Society | China |
| **KR** | Korean Register of Shipping | South Korea |
| **NK** | ClassNK (Nippon Kaiji Kyokai) | Japan |
| **RINA** | Registro Italiano Navale | Italy |
| **IRS** | Indian Register of Shipping | India |
| **PRS** | Polski Rejestr Statków | Poland |
| **RS** | Russian Maritime Register of Shipping | Russia |
| **CRS** | Croatian Register of Shipping | Croatia |

### 2.3.2 Classification Notation for Electrical Systems

Each class society assigns a **"Class Notation"** that indicates compliance. For example, a DNV class notation might be:

$$\text{Notation} = \underbrace{\text{1A}}_{\text{structural}} \, \underbrace{\text{100A5}}_{\text{ESP strength}} \, \underbrace{\text{ESP}}_{\text{DP}} \, \underbrace{\text{BWM(T)}}_{\text{Ballast water}} \, \underbrace{\text{CLEAN}}_{\text{Environmental}}$$

For electrical systems specifically, notations include symbols like:
- **✠** — Hull under survey
- **DYNPOS** — Dynamic positioning
- **COMF-V(3)** — Comfort class for vibration

## 2.4 Industry Standards for Shipboard Electrical Systems

### 2.4.1 The Two-Standard System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dual Standards for Shipboard Electrical Work:**
> - **IEC 60092** series — Used predominantly in **European/Asian shipyards**
> - **IEEE 45** series — Used predominantly in **US shipyards and US-flag vessels**
>
> Most major modern ships follow **IEC 60092**, with IEEE 45 invoked when US regulations apply.

### 2.4.2 Key Standards

| Standard | Title / Coverage |
|----------|------------------|
| **IEC 60092-101** | Definitions and general requirements |
| **IEC 60092-201** | System design — General |
| **IEC 60092-202** | System design — Protection |
| **IEC 60092-301** | Equipment — Generators and motors |
| **IEC 60092-302** | Equipment — Switchgear and controlgear |
| **IEC 60092-303** | Equipment — Transformers |
| **IEC 60092-304** | Equipment — Semiconductors (power electronics) |
| **IEC 60092-306** | Equipment — Lighting and heating |
| **IEC 60092-350** | Shipboard power cables — General construction |
| **IEC 60092-351** | Insulating materials |
| **IEC 60092-352** | Choice and installation of cables |
| **IEC 60092-353** | Power cables (1 kV and 3 kV) |
| **IEC 60092-354** | Single and multicore cables (1 kV to 3 kV) |
| **IEEE 45.1** | General principles for electrical installations |
| **IEEE 45.2** | Switchboards and motor controllers |
| **IEEE 45.3** | Cables and circuit breakers |
| **IEEE 45.5** | Safety precautions |
| **IEEE 45.6** | Electrical installations in hazardous locations |
| **IEEE 45.7** | Switchgear, instrumentation, meters, and meters |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference

| Formula / Standard | Expression | Units / Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Emergency Battery Capacity** | $C = \dfrac{P \cdot t}{V \cdot \eta}$ | Ah |
| **Voltage Drop in Cable** | $\Delta V = \dfrac{I \cdot L \cdot 2 \cdot \rho}{A}$ | V (single phase, 2-wire) |
| **Voltage Drop — 3-phase** | $\Delta V = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \rho}{A}$ | V (3-phase) |
| **Maximum Allowed Voltage Drop** | $\Delta V \leq 6\% \text{ (motor starting)}, \ 2.5\% \text{ (lighting)}$ | % |
| **Earth Fault Loop Impedance** | $Z_{e} = Z_{s} + Z_{t}$ | Ω |
| **Earth Fault Current** | $I_{f} = \dfrac{V_{phase}}{Z_{e}}$ | A |
| **Touch Voltage Limit (IEC 60945)** | $V_{touch} \leq 50 \text{ V (AC)}, \ 120 \text{ V (DC)}$ | V |
| **Generators in Parallel — Load Sharing** | $\Delta f \leq 2.5\% \text{ of rated frequency}$ | Hz |
| **Generators in Parallel — Reactive Sharing** | $\Delta V \leq 2.5\% \text{ of rated voltage}$ | V |
| **Ambient Reference Temperature** | $T_{amb} = 45°\text{C (machinery spaces)}, \ 35°\text{C (deck)}$ | °C |
| **MLC Working Hours Limit** | $H_{work} \leq 14/24 \text{ h}, \ \leq 72/7 \text{ d}$ | h |
| **STCW Rest Hours Minimum** | $H_{rest} \geq 10/24 \text{ h}, \ \geq 77/7 \text{ d}$ | h |
| **SOLAS Emergency Battery Autonomy** | $t_{autonomy} \geq 36 \text{ h}$ (passenger/cargo) | h |
| **Frequency Standard** | $f = 60 \text{ Hz (US/world)}, \ 50 \text{ Hz (Europe/Asia)}$ | Hz |
| **Standard Shipboard Voltages** | $V = 440/220/110 \text{ V (3-phase)}$ | V |
| **Insulation Resistance (IEC 60092)** | $R_{ins} \geq \dfrac{V_{rated}}{1000} \text{ MΩ minimum}$ | MΩ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility of These Standards:**
> Every ship designed today in Indian shipyards (Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock, GRSE) must comply with these formulas. A naval architect/electrical engineer in a shipyard uses these exact formulas to:
> - **Size the emergency battery bank** (using the battery capacity formula)
> - **Select cable cross-section** (using voltage drop formulas)
> - **Design earth fault protection** (using the earth fault loop formula)
> - **Set generator governor droop** (using load-sharing limits)
> - **Schedule watchkeeping and maintenance** (using STCW/MLC hours)

## 2.6 Recommendations for Shipyard Electrical Work

> [!NOTE]
> **OSHA Maritime Standards (US Reference, applicable to global shipyards):**
> Shipyard electrical work must comply with **29 CFR Part 1915** (Occupational Safety and Health Standards for Shipyard Employment). Key recommendations:
>
> 1. **Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)** — All electrical equipment must be de-energized and locked before maintenance
> 2. **Hot Work Permits** — Required for any work on energized equipment above 50V
> 3. **GFCI Protection** — Ground Fault Circuit Interrupters required for all 120V outlets
> 4. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** — Insulated gloves, arc-flash suits, face shields
> 5. **Safe Distance** — Minimum approach distance for energized parts: **≥ 1 m for voltages up to 1 kV**

### 2.6.1 IEC 60945 — Maritime Navigation and Radiocommunication Equipment

Although primarily for navigation electronics, IEC 60945 establishes the **environmental testing standards** (temperature, humidity, vibration, EMC) that shipboard electrical equipment must pass.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Emergency Battery Bank Sizing for a Cargo Ship

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

> A cargo ship has the following emergency loads in the event of main power failure:
> - Emergency lighting: **5 kW**
> - Navigation lights: **2 kW**
> - Communication equipment: **1.5 kW**
> - Fire detection system: **0.5 kW**
>
> The system voltage is **220 V DC**, and the inverter efficiency is **0.90**. The battery must support the load for **36 hours** per SOLAS requirements.
>
> **Find:** Required battery capacity in **Ah**, and the number of **12 V, 200 Ah** batteries needed in series-parallel.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate Total Emergency Load**

$$P_{load} = P_{lighting} + P_{nav} + P_{comm} + P_{fire}$$

$$P_{load} = 5{,}000 + 2{,}000 + 1{,}500 + 500$$

$$\boxed{P_{load} = 9{,}000 \text{ W} = 9 \text{ kW}}$$

*Logic:* Sum all individual emergency loads to find the total wattage the battery must support.

**Step 2: Apply the Battery Capacity Formula**

$$C_{battery} = \frac{P_{load} \times t_{autonomy}}{V_{system} \times \eta_{inverter}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute Values**

$$C_{battery} = \frac{9{,}000 \times 36}{220 \times 0.90}$$

$$C_{battery} = \frac{324{,}000}{198}$$

$$\boxed{C_{battery} = 1{,}636.36 \text{ Ah}}$$

*Logic:* Energy required (9 kW × 36 h = 324 kWh) divided by effective energy per Ah of battery (220 V × 0.9 × 1 Ah = 198 Wh/Ah).

**Step 4: Determine Number of 12 V, 200 Ah Batteries in Series**

$$N_{series} = \frac{V_{system}}{V_{battery, nominal}}$$

$$N_{series} = \frac{220}{12}$$

$$\boxed{N_{series} \approx 18.33 \rightarrow 19 \text{ batteries}}$$

*Logic:* 19 batteries × 12 V = 228 V (slightly above 220 V; DC systems can tolerate this).

**Step 5: Determine Number of Parallel Strings**

$$N_{parallel} = \frac{C_{battery}}{C_{per string}}$$

$$N_{parallel} = \frac{1{,}636.36}{200}$$

$$\boxed{N_{parallel} \approx 8.18 \rightarrow 9 \text{ parallel strings}}$$

*Logic:* With 9 parallel strings of 200 Ah each, total capacity = 9 × 200 = 1,800 Ah ≥ 1,636.36 Ah ✓

**Step 6: Total Number of Batteries**

$$N_{total} = N_{series} \times N_{parallel} = 19 \times 9$$

$$\boxed{N_{total} = 171 \text{ batteries}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Margin:** In real shipboard design, a safety factor of **1.25** is applied to account for battery aging and temperature derating. Therefore, the design should use:
>
> $$C_{design} = 1.25 \times 1{,}636.36 = 2{,}045.45 \text{ Ah}$$
>
> With 9 parallel strings, the per-string capacity would need to be approximately **228 Ah** per string, which is why shipboard batteries are often custom-rated.

## 3.2 Derivation: Voltage Drop Calculation for Shipboard Cable

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

> A single-phase **220 V** circuit in a cargo hold feeds a **15 kW** load located **80 m** from the distribution panel. The conductor is **copper** ($\rho = 0.0172 \ \Omega \cdot \text{mm}^2/\text{m}$) with a cross-section of **16 mm²**. Find the voltage drop and check if it meets the **2.5%** lighting limit.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Determine the Load Current**

$$I = \frac{P}{V \cdot \cos\phi}$$

Assuming power factor $\cos\phi = 0.85$ for a mixed load:

$$I = \frac{15{,}000}{220 \times 0.85}$$

$$\boxed{I = 80.22 \text{ A}}$$

*Logic:* Current is Power divided by Voltage × Power Factor.

**Step 2: Calculate Voltage Drop (Single-Phase, 2-Wire)**

For single-phase, the current flows through both conductors (out and return), hence the factor of 2:

$$\Delta V = \frac{2 \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \rho}{A}$$

**Step 3: Substitute Numerical Values**

$$\Delta V = \frac{2 \times 80.22 \times 80 \times 0.0172}{16}$$

$$\Delta V = \frac{2 \times 80.22 \times 80 \times 0.0172}{16}$$

Let me compute the numerator step-by-step:
- $2 \times 80.22 = 160.44$
- $160.44 \times 80 = 12{,}835.2$
- $12{,}835.2 \times 0.0172 = 220.77$

$$\Delta V = \frac{220.77}{16}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V = 13.80 \text{ V}}$$

*Logic:* The numerator represents total copper resistance × current, and dividing by cross-section area gives the voltage drop.

**Step 4: Express as Percentage of Rated Voltage**

$$\Delta V \% = \frac{\Delta V}{V_{rated}} \times 100$$

$$\Delta V \% = \frac{13.80}{220} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V \% = 6.27\%}$$

**Step 5: Compare with Limit and Decide**

The limit for **continuous lighting** is **2.5%**, but for **motor starting** it is **6%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** For a **lighting load**, 6.27% exceeds the 2.5% limit. The cable must be upsized. Re-calculating with **25 mm²**:
>
> $$\Delta V = \frac{2 \times 80.22 \times 80 \times 0.0172}{25} = 8.83 \text{ V} \rightarrow 4.01\%$$
>
> Still over for lighting. Use **35 mm²**:
>
> $$\Delta V = \frac{2 \times 80.22 \times 80 \times 0.0172}{35} = 6.31 \text{ V} \rightarrow 2.87\%$$
>
> Still slightly over. Use **50 mm²**:
>
> $$\Delta V = \frac{2 \times 80.22 \times 80 \times 0.0172}{50} = 4.41 \text{ V} \rightarrow 2.01\% \ \checkmark$$
>
> **Final cable size: 50 mm²**

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Maritime Recommendation Bodies

> [!NOTE]
> **Matrix Use:** KTU examiners often ask comparison questions. This matrix provides ready-to-recall data.

| Aspect | **ILO MLC 2006** | **IMO SOLAS** | **IMO STCW** | **Classification Societies (IACS)** | **IEC / IEEE** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Seafarer welfare | Ship safety | Crew competence | Technical class rules | Electrical design standards |
| **Adoption Mechanism** | Ratification by states | Ratification by states | Ratification by states | Voluntary membership | Industry adoption |
| **Mandatory?** | Yes (for ratifying states) | Yes (for ratifying states) | Yes (for ratifying states) | Yes (for classed vessels) | Voluntary, but de facto mandatory |
| **Electrical-Specific?** | Limited (Title 4 mainly) | Extensive (Reg II-1/40–45) | Limited (ETO competency) | Highly detailed | Purely technical |
| **Key Document** | Convention + Code | Chapter II-1, II-2, IX | Convention + STCW Code | Class Rules (e.g., DNV Pt 4 Ch 8) | IEC 60092 series |
| **Penalty for Non-Compliance** | Detention by PSC | Detention by PSC | Loss of CoC | Loss of class → cannot trade | Insurance/contractual |
| **Updated** | Amendments 2018, 2022 | Periodic consolidated editions | Manila 2010, ongoing | Continuous | Continuous |

## 3.4 Derivation: Earth Fault Loop Impedance Analysis

### 3.4.1 Problem Statement

> A **440 V, 3-phase** system has a **25 kVA** transformer with **5% impedance**, and the source impedance is **0.05 Ω**. Calculate the prospective earth fault current and check discrimination with a **32 A MCB** (C-curve).

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate Transformer Impedance**

$$Z_t = \frac{Z_{\%}}{100} \times \frac{V^2}{S}$$

$$Z_t = 0.05 \times \frac{440^2}{25{,}000}$$

$$Z_t = 0.05 \times \frac{193{,}600}{25{,}000}$$

$$Z_t = 0.05 \times 7.744$$

$$\boxed{Z_t = 0.3872 \ \Omega}$$

*Logic:* Transformer impedance in Ohms = %Z × (V²/S).

**Step 2: Calculate Total Earth Loop Impedance**

$$Z_e = Z_s + Z_t$$

$$Z_e = 0.05 + 0.3872$$

$$\boxed{Z_e = 0.4372 \ \Omega}$$

*Logic:* Source impedance (cable + grid) + transformer impedance in series.

**Step 3: Calculate Phase-to-Earth Voltage**

For a 3-phase, 4-wire system, the phase-to-earth voltage is:

$$V_{ph-e} = \frac{V_{line}}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{440}{1.732}$$

$$\boxed{V_{ph-e} = 254.04 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Prospective Earth Fault Current**

$$I_{f} = \frac{V_{ph-e}}{Z_e}$$

$$I_{f} = \frac{254.04}{0.4372}$$

$$\boxed{I_{f} = 581.1 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 5: Verify MCB Discrimination**

A **C-curve 32 A MCB** has an instantaneous trip at **5–10 × In** = **160–320 A**.

Since $I_f = 581.1 \text{ A} > 320 \text{ A}$, the MCB trips instantaneously ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discrimination Check:** The fault current is **5.4× the rated current of the 32 A MCB**, well within the instantaneous trip range. The MCB will clear the fault in **< 0.1 s**, satisfying IEC 60092-202 protection requirements.

## 3.5 Detailed Calculation: Generator Load-Sharing Droop Setting

### 3.5.1 Problem Statement

> Two **1500 kVA** generators run in parallel on a ship. The governor droop is set to **4%**. If Generator 1 takes a 30% load step, calculate the frequency drop and verify it meets the **±2.5%** of rated frequency (60 Hz) limit.

### 3.5.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Express Load Step in Per-Unit**

$$\text{Load Step} = 30\% = 0.30 \text{ pu}$$

**Step 2: Apply Droop Formula**

$$\Delta f_{pu} = \text{Droop} \times \text{Load Step}_{pu}$$

$$\Delta f_{pu} = 0.04 \times 0.30$$

$$\boxed{\Delta f_{pu} = 0.012 \text{ pu} = 1.2\%}$$

**Step 3: Convert to Hz**

$$\Delta f = 0.012 \times 60 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta f = 0.72 \text{ Hz}}$$

**Step 4: New Frequency**

$$f_{new} = 60 - 0.72 = 59.28 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5: Check Against Limit**

$$\text{Limit} = 2.5\% \times 60 = 1.5 \text{ Hz}$$

Since $\Delta f = 0.72 \text{ Hz} < 1.5 \text{ Hz}$ ✓ **Passes**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 4% droop is industry-standard (IEEE 45 / IEC 60092). A 5% droop is used for tighter frequency regulation, but at the cost of poorer load sharing stability. Shipboard practice typically uses **3–5%** droop.
