# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**"Briefly explain the MLC 2006 and list its five titles."**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

The **Maritime Labour Convention (MLC) 2006**, adopted by the **International Labour Organization (ILO)** on **23 February 2006** and in force since **20 August 2013**, is often called the **"Seafarers' Bill of Rights."** It consolidates 68 previous ILO maritime instruments into a single binding convention covering working and living conditions of seafarers worldwide.

**Five Titles of MLC 2006:**

1. **Title 1** — Minimum requirements for seafarers to work on a ship (minimum age, medical fitness, training)
2. **Title 2** — Conditions of employment (wages, hours of work/rest, leave, repatriation)
3. **Title 3** — Accommodation, recreational facilities, food, and catering
4. **Title 4** — Health protection, medical care, welfare, and social security
5. **Title 5** — Compliance and enforcement (flag, port, and labour-supply state responsibilities)

*(1 mark for definition + entry into force; 1.5 marks for listing titles with brief explanation; 0.5 mark for concluding remark on significance)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**"Differentiate between SOLAS Chapter II-1 and Chapter II-2 with respect to electrical safety."**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

| Aspect | **SOLAS Chapter II-1** | **SOLAS Chapter II-2** |
|---|---|---|
| **Title** | Construction — Subdivision, Stability, Machinery, and **Electrical Installations** | Construction — **Fire Protection**, Fire Detection, Fire Extinction |
| **Focus on Electrical** | **Primary** — Reg II-1/40 to 45 cover generators, switchboards, emergency power, shock prevention | **Secondary** — Covers electrical fire prevention, fire-detection systems, hazardous area equipment |
| **Key Requirements** | Main + emergency sources, autonomy, earthing, insulation monitoring | Flame-retardant cables, fire-resistant penetrations, intrinsically safe equipment in hazardous zones |
| **Relevance to Electrical Engineer** | **High** — Directly governs design and installation | **Medium-High** — Governs material selection and routing |

*(1 mark each for distinguishing the two chapters on three key parameters)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice

### 🔷 Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q. (a)** Discuss in detail the **International Labour Organization (ILO) Maritime Labour Convention (MLC) 2006**. Explain its **five titles**, the **"Convention + Code + Recommendation" structure**, and how **Title 4** specifically addresses the **occupational safety of electrical engineers** on board ships. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**1. Background of MLC 2006 (2 marks)**

- Adopted at the **94th session of the International Labour Conference** on **23 February 2006**.
- Entered into force on **20 August 2013** — the **second ILO Convention** to enter into force using the **tacit amendment procedure** (the first was the Maritime Labour Convention itself).
- Often called **"The Seafarers' Bill of Rights"**.
- Consolidates **68 previous ILO maritime instruments**.
- Ratified by **100+ countries** representing **>90% of world tonnage**.

**2. Convention + Code + Recommendation Structure (2 marks)**

- **Convention Articles**: Legally binding, set out the **core principles**.
- **Code Part A**: **Mandatory standards** that all ratifying states must enforce.
- **Code Part B**: **Non-mandatory guidelines** for implementation.
- **Recommendation**: **Supplementary advice** on how to give effect to the Code.

> The unique two-tier approach (Convention Articles = mandatory; Code split into A = mandatory and B = voluntary) gives **flexibility** while ensuring **uniform minimum standards**.

**3. The Five Titles (2 marks)**

- **Title 1** – Minimum requirements to work on a ship (age ≥16, medical certificate, training)
- **Title 2** – Conditions of employment (wages ≤ 14 h/24 h, ≤ 72 h/7 d; rest ≥ 10 h/24 h, ≥ 77 h/7 d)
- **Title 3** – Accommodation, recreational facilities, food, and catering
- **Title 4** – Health protection, medical care, welfare, and social security
- **Title 5** – Compliance and enforcement (flag, port, labour-supply state)

**4. Title 4 and Electrical Engineer Occupational Safety (1 mark)**

Title 4 (Reg 4.3 — Health and Safety and Accident Prevention) is the **most directly relevant** to electrical engineers. It mandates:

- **Safe working environment** for all seafarers, including those handling electrical equipment
- **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** — insulated gloves, arc-flash suits, safety boots
- **Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) procedures** before any maintenance on energized equipment
- **Periodic safety training** in electrical hazards and emergency response
- **Investigation and reporting** of all electrical accidents
- **Risk assessment** for shipboard electrical work

---

**Q. (b)** With the aid of a **neat sketch**, describe the **SOLAS requirements for the emergency source of electrical power** on board a cargo ship. Include the **autonomy duration**, **starting time**, and **location** of the emergency source. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**1. SOLAS Reg II-1/42 Requirements (3 marks)**

The emergency source of electrical power must:

- Be located **above the freeboard deck**, preferably in the **emergency generator room**
- Be independent of the **main source of electrical power** (i.e., not in the same compartment)
- Be capable of starting automatically and supplying the emergency loads within **45 seconds** of main power failure
- Provide power for a minimum of **36 hours** for cargo ships
- Have a **transitional source of emergency power** (typically a battery) with **6 hours** autonomy that can supply critical loads immediately until the emergency generator comes online

**2. Sketch: Emergency Power System Architecture (2 marks)**

```
[Main Generator 1] ──┐
                    ├──► [Main Switchboard] ──► [Main Bus]
[Main Generator 2] ──┘         │
                               │ (Interconnector)
                               ▼
                    [Emergency Switchboard] ◄── [Emergency Generator]
                               │                    (Automatic Start)
                               │
                               ▼
                    [Emergency Battery Bank]
                     (220 V DC, 36 h, 6 h transitional)
                               │
              ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐
              ▼                ▼                ▼
      [Emergency         [Steering Gear    [Navigation
       Lighting]          Motor]            Equipment]
              ▼                ▼                ▼
      [Fire Pumps]    [Fire Detection]   [Communication
                                              Systems]
```

**3. Autonomy, Starting, and Location (2 marks)**

| Parameter | Requirement | SOLAS Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Autonomy duration** | **36 hours** (cargo ships); passenger ships per Reg II-1/42.2.2 | Reg II-1/42 |
| **Starting time** | **≤ 45 seconds** of power failure | Reg II-1/44 |
| **Location** | Above freeboard deck; outside machinery casings | Reg II-1/42 |
| **Transitional source** | Battery with 6 h autonomy, 1/3 hour for UMS ships | Reg II-1/42.3.1 |
| **Loads supplied** | Emergency lighting, navigation, communication, fire pumps, steering gear | Reg II-1/43 |

---

### 🔷 Question B: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]

**Q. (a)** Explain the role of **Classification Societies** in the maritime industry. List the **12 member societies of IACS** and describe the **class assignment process** for a new ship. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**1. Role of Classification Societies (2 marks)**

Classification societies are **independent, non-governmental organizations** that:

- Establish and apply **technical standards** for the design, construction, and maintenance of ships
- Conduct **surveys and inspections** during construction and throughout the ship's life
- Issue **class certificates** confirming compliance with their rules
- Help ensure **safety at sea**, **prevention of pollution**, and **asset protection**
- Some are also authorized as **Recognized Organizations (ROs)** by flag states to survey on their behalf

**2. IACS Member Societies (2 marks)** — The **12 current members** of the International Association of Classification Societies (founded 1968) are:

1. **ABS** — American Bureau of Shipping (USA)
2. **BV** — Bureau Veritas (France)
3. **CCS** — China Classification Society (China)
4. **CRS** — Croatian Register of Shipping (Croatia)
5. **DNV** — Det Norske Veritas (Norway, merged with GL in 2013)
6. **IRS** — Indian Register of Shipping (India)
7. **KR** — Korean Register of Shipping (South Korea)
8. **LR** — Lloyd's Register (UK)
9. **NK** — ClassNK (Japan)
10. **PRS** — Polski Rejestr Statków (Poland)
11. **RINA** — Registro Italiano Navale (Italy)
12. **RS** — Russian Maritime Register of Shipping (Russia)

Together, IACS members classify approximately **90% of world tonnage**.

**3. Class Assignment Process (3 marks)**

- **Step 1 — Design Review**: The shipyard submits design plans (lines, structure, machinery, electrical) to the chosen class society. Plans are reviewed against the society's rules.
- **Step 2 — Approval**: Approved drawings are returned with a **"Approved" stamp**. Modifications may be required.
- **Step 3 — Construction Survey**: Class surveyors attend the shipyard during construction, inspect materials, witness tests, and verify workmanship.
- **Step 4 — Sea Trials**: After launch, the class society witnesses sea trials, including **inclining experiment**, **speed/power trials**, **steering gear tests**, and **electrical load tests**.
- **Step 5 — Class Certificate Issued**: If all surveys are satisfactory, the society issues a **Class Certificate** and the ship is "in class."
- **Step 6 — Continuing Surveys**: To maintain class, the ship undergoes **annual surveys**, **intermediate surveys** (every 2.5 years), and **special surveys** (every 5 years for hull, every 4 years for machinery).

> Loss of class means the ship **cannot trade** and insurance coverage is void.

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss the **STCW Convention and Code** with reference to the **Electro-Technical Officer (ETO)**. Explain the **STCW watchkeeping hours** and **rest period requirements** for marine engineers. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**1. STCW Background (1.5 marks)**

- **Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping (STCW)** Convention adopted by **IMO in 1978**, in force since **1984**.
- **Major revision (Manila Amendments)** adopted in **2010**, in force from **1 January 2012**.
- Establishes **minimum qualification standards** for masters, officers, and watchkeeping ratings.

**2. Electro-Technical Officer (ETO) (2.5 marks)**

The **ETO** is a specialized role introduced in the **Manila Amendments** under **STCW Regulation III/6**.

**Eligibility and Competencies:**

- **Minimum age**: 18 years
- **Sea service**: 12 months of approved seagoing service in the engine department, OR 36 months with completed ETO training program
- **Education**: Completion of an approved ETO training program covering:
  - Marine electrical and electronic systems
  - Control engineering
  - High-voltage installations (above 1000 V)
  - Safety and emergency procedures
- **Certificate of Competency (CoC)**: Issued by the flag state after meeting all requirements

**Duties of an ETO:**

- Maintenance and repair of all electrical/electronic equipment
- Operation of generators, switchboards, and emergency power systems
- Troubleshooting PLC, automation, and control systems
- Compliance with SOLAS, class, and ISM Code requirements
- Management of high-voltage systems on modern vessels

**3. STCW Watchkeeping Hours and Rest (3 marks)**

Per **STCW Regulation VIII/1** and **STCW Code Section A-VIII/1**:

| Parameter | Limit |
|---|---|
| **Maximum working hours** | **14 hours** in any 24-hour period |
| **Maximum working hours** | **72 hours** in any 7-day period |
| **Minimum rest hours** | **10 hours** in any 24-hour period |
| **Minimum rest hours** | **77 hours** in any 7-day period |
| **Rest period division** | Maximum **2 periods**, one of which must be **at least 6 consecutive hours** |

**Watchkeeping Schedule Example (4 hours on / 8 hours off):**

$$\text{4 h (00:00–04:00)} \rightarrow \text{8 h rest} \rightarrow \text{4 h (12:00–16:00)} \rightarrow \text{8 h rest}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Record Keeping Mandatory:** The Master must ensure watch schedules are **posted in conspicuous locations** and **records maintained** for inspection by Port State Control.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Conflating IMO and ILO**: ILO = labour/welfare; IMO = safety/pollution. Many students mix these up, costing **2–3 marks** per question.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the difference between MLC Code Part A and Part B**: Part A is **mandatory**, Part B is **non-mandatory**. Confusing these is a frequent error.
>
> 3. **Wrong STCW vs. MLC hours**: Some students mix the **STCW rest hours** (10 h/24 h, 77 h/7 d) with **MLC working hours** (14 h/24 h, 72 h/7 d). Note: both are essentially the same limits stated from opposite perspectives.
>
> 4. **Missing SOLAS autonomy values**: Students forget that the **SOLAS emergency battery autonomy is 36 hours**, not 24 or 48.
>
> 5. **Not naming the 12 IACS members**: Examiners expect specific names, not "DNV, LR, ABS" alone. You should be able to list at least 8–10.
>
> 6. **Confusing IEEE 45 with IEC 60092**: IEEE 45 is US-oriented; IEC 60092 is European/global. State explicitly which is used in your context.
>
> 7. **Skipping units in formulas**: Voltage drop without units (V) or battery capacity without (Ah) costs 0.5 mark per instance.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Maritime Labour/Industry Recommendations**

### 🏛️ Regulatory Bodies
- ☐ **ILO** = Labour; **IMO** = Safety; **IACS** = Class; **IEC/IEEE** = Technical
- ☐ MLC 2006 in force: **20 August 2013**
- ☐ STCW Manila Amendments in force: **1 January 2012**
- ☐ SOLAS = Chapter II-1 (electrical), Chapter II-2 (fire), Chapter IX (ISM)

### 📋 MLC 2006 — Five Titles
- ☐ **T1**: Minimum requirements (age, medical, training)
- ☐ **T2**: Conditions of employment (wages, hours)
- ☐ **T3**: Accommodation and catering
- ☐ **T4**: Health and safety (most relevant to electrical engineers)
- ☐ **T5**: Compliance and enforcement (Flag/Port/Labour Supply States)

### ⏰ Working Hours Limits
- ☐ **Work ≤ 14 h/24 h, ≤ 72 h/7 d**
- ☐ **Rest ≥ 10 h/24 h, ≥ 77 h/7 d**
- ☐ Rest split into max **2 periods**, one **≥ 6 h** consecutive

### ⚡ SOLAS Electrical Key Requirements
- ☐ Emergency battery autonomy: **36 hours**
- ☐ Emergency generator starting: **≤ 45 seconds**
- ☐ Transitional battery: **6 hours** (1/3 h for UMS)
- ☐ Main source: **minimum 2 generators**
- ☐ Reg II-1/40 to 45 cover all electrical provisions

### 🔋 Key Formulas to Memorize
- ☐ Battery capacity: $C = \dfrac{P \cdot t}{V \cdot \eta}$
- ☐ Voltage drop (1-phase): $\Delta V = \dfrac{2 I L \rho}{A}$
- ☐ Voltage drop (3-phase): $\Delta V = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} I L \rho}{A}$
- ☐ Earth fault current: $I_f = \dfrac{V_{ph-e}}{Z_e}$
- ☐ Generator droop: $\Delta f = \text{Droop} \times \text{Load Step}$
- ☐ Touch voltage limit: ≤ **50 V AC** / **120 V DC** (IEC 60945)
- ☐ Voltage drop limits: **6% motor**, **2.5% lighting**
- ☐ Frequency standard: **50/60 Hz**; Common voltages: **440/220/110 V**

### 🏢 IACS — 12 Member Societies
- ☐ Memorize: **DNV, LR, ABS, BV, CCS, ClassNK, KR, RINA, IRS, PRS, RS, CRS**
- ☐ IACS founded: **1968**; classifies **~90% world tonnage**

### 🛠️ Dual Standards
- ☐ **IEC 60092** (series 101–354) = European/global
- ☐ **IEEE 45** (subsections 45.1–45.7) = American
- ☐ Modern ships: **IEC dominant**; US-flagged: **IEEE 45**

### ⚙️ ETO (Electro-Technical Officer)
- ☐ STCW Reg **III/6**
- ☐ Age ≥ 18; Sea service 12 mo (with training) or 36 mo (without)
- ☐ Specialized in **HV systems, automation, control**

### 🛡️ Shipyard Safety
- ☐ **LOTO** (Lockout/Tagout) mandatory
- ☐ **PPE**: Insulated gloves, arc-flash suit, face shield
- ☐ **GFCI** for all 120 V outlets
- ☐ Safe approach distance: **≥ 1 m** for ≤ 1 kV
- ☐ **29 CFR Part 1915** (US OSHA maritime standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For the 14-mark question, always structure your answer with a **diagram + 3 key points + conclusion**. Examiners reward **structured answers** with **clear section breaks**. Use **bullet points** and **tables** liberally. Always state **units** in numerical answers.
